title: Aux origines de la guerre en Ukraine, vingt ans de propagande russe dans le Donbass
text:
Russian speakers in Ukraine, threatened with "genocide." An entire country under the yoke of "Nazism." Listening to the Kremlin's propaganda, repeated incessantly by Russian television, justifying the invasion of their neighbor, one might think that Moscow's propaganda has the weight of a T-90 tank or a "Grad" missile. Who could believe this absurd rhetoric? And then, we remember that eighteen years ago, during one of our reports in Ukraine, a young resident warned us: "When you are constantly told that black is white and white is black, you end up believing it." In this month of March 2022, where the Russian army is currently tormenting a democratic country pretending to save it from dictatorship, we recall the prophetic words of a Ukrainian who knew well that lies are nails that just require skillful hammer blows. Our memories also bring back our reports in eastern Ukraine, in Donetsk, in the Donbass, where, in a way, it all began, where Vladimir Putin prepared his territorial conquest of Ukraine through indoctrination and brainwashing. The region, now a separatist enclave, is waiting to vote for its annexation to Russia, as Crimea did in 2014. Despite having voted 86% in favor of Ukraine's independence in 1991. To reach this point, minds have been patiently manipulated, paranoia carefully instilled, and hatred of Kiev meticulously nurtured. And today, it is evident for a large part of the population in Donetsk that their city has been saved from genocide thanks to Vladimir Putin, that the Donbass has survived the Nazi peril thanks to Russian assistance, and that its future, its survival, depends solely on Moscow. Black is white; white is black: Orwellian theory in a chemically pure version. How did we come to this inversion of facts? How did Donbass become the laboratory for what the Kremlin wants to impose on the rest of the country? A crucial first challenge December 2004. In Kiev, the "Orange Revolution," the color chosen by pro-Western Ukrainians, aimed to dismantle the last remnants of the communist system and definitively turn the country towards Europe. The people are on the move. Well, on the move is a way of speaking. In reality, they are standing still in front of the Rada, the Parliament. Tens of thousands of Viktor Yushchenko's supporters stand day and night in freezing cold weather in front of the building to contest the official results of the presidential runoff. They stamp their feet and warm themselves by chanting the three syllables of their country "U-" "krai-" "ne," and the three syllables of their favorite's name: "Yu-" "shchen-" "ko." They claim the victory of this miracle worker who emerged with a pockmarked face from an attempted poisoning by Russian secret services. Ballot stuffing had nevertheless given the pro-Russian Prime Minister and candidate, Viktor Yanukovych, the victory. The result will soon be invalidated and Viktor Yushchenko announced as the winner of a new second round, but in the meantime, on Independence Square (Maidan) and on Khreschatyk Avenue leading to it, protesters from all over the country have pitched tents and are keeping warm around braziers. They talk about their dreams of Europe. They fear only one thing: a descent of miners from the Donbass, located 700 kilometers and ten hours away from the capital. Rumors regularly suggest they are on their way. Buses transporting them have been seen. The fear is palpable. And it may seem justified: since independence in 1991, their violent outbreaks, pickaxes and iron bars in hand, to demand higher wages or a change of regime, have punctuated the country's political life. These miners at the vanguard of the proletariat have always been pampered by the communist regime, glorified by propaganda, like Stakhanov, who died in 1977, a hero of planned productivity. But their courageous strikes, starting in 1989, hastened the fall of Gorbachev and accelerated the disintegration of the Soviet Union. This is to say how admired they were in Kiev, these workers, before being feared and now reviled. The intellectual and pro-Western youth, whether speaking Ukrainian or Russian (often both), now show a certain disdain towards them. They now consider them only as a relic of the past, a hindrance to the country's democratic development. The black mines of the Zasyadko galleries. Finally, this time, the Donbass miners did not come to Kiev. So, we went to them. We took an overnight train to Donetsk, a rickety and overheated carriage, an old cantankerous lady in a worn-out uniform of the railway company serving passengers black and sweet tea from an antique samovar. After fourteen hours of travel, the omnibus arrived in the Donbass. At the French Alliance, we found not one but three interpreters, delighted to practice their language skills with a native French speaker. They explained that French visitors were so rare to venture here. How to describe Donetsk at the beginning of the 21st century? A modern downtown rump, with luxury shops and fine restaurants. Young women outrageously made up, stepping out in fur from anthracite Hummers, with tinted windows, driven by unfriendly tough guys in their unchanging black leather coats. Just a few kilometers away, the time of a half-dozen stops on a crowded bus spewing indigestible clouds of carbon monoxide, you plunged into the heart of the Soviet Union. A backdrop of endless mines and giant steel factories, headframes and chimneys laid out as far as the eye can see on the horizon, amidst dilapidated buildings exuding poverty. A fossilized legacy from a time when the city, after many other names in history, was called Stalino, in homage, of course, to the dictator. Black is the heavy soil of Ukraine, black is the smoke from the factories, black is the snow soiled on the sidewalks. Black, above all, are the faces of the miners and metallurgists. Here they are, these tough guys who made Kiev tremble. We stand at the shift change in front of the Zasyadko mine, one of the largest in the coal basin and one of the most dangerous in the world. It then records four to five deaths per million tons of coal extracted. In the galleries of Zasyadko, gas is everywhere. But silicosis, heart attacks, and collapses also kill as surely as firedamp. Miners come and go, greet each other, talk. In Donetsk, they recognize each other among a thousand by the black line that surrounds their eyes, coal dust that resists all scrubbing. Those who come up from the depths agree to talk about their lives. They are asked their age. They are 30 or 40 years old. They are given ten to twenty years more. Downgrading and disenchantment. They tell their story, fatalistically and in Russian, these proletarians once revered, now abandoned. The constant danger, the exhausting work for the equivalent of 150 euros per month. The in-house unions whose only role, as in the good old days of communism, is to provide a train ticket once a year to a Black Sea resort. These miners, whose numbers have been divided by three since the early 1980s, know they are anachronisms, an anomaly for Ukrainians who dream of being a modern nation. They return, and tenfold, the contempt to the people of Kiev. Lazy people who live off the workers of the Donbass. They have the time and the right to dream there. Here, we work. The Prime Minister, Viktor Yanukovych, has their votes. This former communist apparatchik has the merit of being from the area. He was governor of Donetsk before making a career in the capital. Above all, he knows how to maintain the resentment and nostalgia of this faded aristocracy of the working class. If the population of the Donbass massively voted for independence in 1991, thirteen years later, it is disenchantment, downgrading, the end of hope for better days and finally decent pay. On the contrary, everything has gone from bad to worse. The situation of the workers has continued to deteriorate, as witnesses from Zasyadko recount, to the point that they were sometimes only paid in food vouchers. In 1998 already, in Kiev, there was this economic malaise. Old women selling their meager possessions in a market of misery, unable to receive their pensions. A former Bolshoi dancer, widow of a World War II hero, forced to sell tickets in a hut at the entrance of the football stadium to survive. The military medals of the USSR that were sold by weight of metal... And so many other poignant, almost unimaginable scenes. The grab of the oligarchs In the Donbass as in the rest of Ukraine, the fault largely lay with the oligarchs of the region who, at the time of the disintegration of the USSR, had seized the factories for a pittance. At the forefront was Rinat Akhmetov, a son of a miner from Donetsk who became the richest man in Ukraine. There was also Ioukhym Zviahilsky, owner of the Zasiadko mine and... former prime minister of the new independent state. They and a few others, satiated after gorging themselves, had agreed to give back a little of what they had plundered. With the prices of coal and steel rising, salaries had been paid again and - a little - increased. People were no longer starving. That was something. Rinat Akhmetov also maintained local pride by distributing almost free tickets to the stadium to support the local football club, the renowned Shakhtar ("miner" in Slavic) Donetsk, the only one that challenged Dynamo Kiev in the championship. The miners of Zasiadko were not completely fooled by the generosity of these exploiters, but still voted for them and for the Party of Regions, that of Viktor Yanukovych, which sported the color blue. They did it without illusions. Let's say that, mafia for mafia, these profiteers were hated less than the others, the oligarchs and politicians of Kiev who lived there at the expense of the workers of the Donbass. So, in 2004, on Lenin Square, at the foot of the Bolshevik leader's statue, at the end of Pushkin Avenue, the "blue" protesters praised as a well-learned lesson the record of Prime Minister Yanukovych. Like Vladimir Putin had done on the other side of the border, the locals were grateful to the local boy for repaying the salaries, however meager they were. The babushkas also praised him for reinstating pensions, even though they were meager, as they had lost their husbands, who had died prematurely in the mine or had left too soon because of their lungs. Far from the "orange revolution" The inhabitants of Donetsk willingly recounted their history, woven with labor interspersed with wars. When they traced their family tree, they didn't have to go far to find ancestries on the other side of the border. Russification had been intense in the region during the Soviet Union era. The Kremlin, already wary of Ukraine's independence aspirations, kept sending Russian workers here, attracted by the promise of better wages and living conditions. But an article from Le Monde in 1959 stated that 68% of the population still claimed Ukrainian origin. Despite their distrust of the leeches in Kiev, most of them still felt Ukrainian in 2004. They were simply emotionally, culturally, and familially connected to the neighboring country. Russia was just there, a few kilometers away. The European Union, on the other hand, seemed like a world away. The orange color that dominated in Kiev in 2004 was banned from the streets of Donetsk. Pro-Westerners had to be sought in secret, appointments had to be made with a thousand precautions. They lived in fear of the arrival of the undemocratic Hummer drivers, known for their swift methods. Only one coal miners' union had taken a stand for the "orange revolution." But the miners had immediately returned their union cards, whether under pressure or not was unknown. Their leader was threatened, and a delegate was brutally beaten. An "orange" activist finally joined us in a café. Maxime carried a bag with Mickey Mouse printed on it. Inside, he carried a Kalashnikov. He had lost count of the intimidations. He never slept in the same place for two nights. With a vigilant gaze, he raged at seeing his compatriots with their thinking stuck in the 20th century, and at the same time excused them. He said they were prisoners of a past revisited and mythified by local propaganda and Russian-language television. Looking towards Russia Ten years later, in 2014, we returned to Donetsk. Kiev was rising up again. The population of the capital had taken over Maidan Square. After being defeated by Yushchenko at the end of 2004, Viktor Yanukovych had returned to power, with massive support from Donbass but also due to divisions within the pro-Western camp. He became prime minister again in 2006, then president in 2010. Under pressure from Vladimir Putin, he eventually broke off the process of rapprochement with the European Union and talks with NATO. Ukraine turned its back on Europe and looked towards Russia. This U-turn led to protests that forced the president to flee the capital in February 2014 and seek refuge on the other side of the border. We returned to Donetsk in early March, this time by plane, landing at a brand new and oversized international airport. The whole city seems to have beautified. The city center has expanded. The buildings have been freshly renovated. Franchised shops, electronics stores, and trendy cafes have sprung up. But modernity has not reached the Zasyadko mine. It remains in its Soviet state. Just like the DMZ steel complex, burying its immense maze of buildings and all the neighboring buildings under the soot from its chimneys. At the entrance of Zasyadko as well as at DMZ, the miners and steelworkers say that for them, nothing has really changed. The end of the month is still just as difficult. Death still lurks in the mines and around the blast furnaces. In 2007, a gas explosion once again killed nearly a hundred miners in Zasiadko. Their eyes are still circled with the same coal line, but their faces are even harder when Kiev is mentioned. They grumble that the capital has abandoned them. Even Viktor Yanukovych no longer finds favor in their eyes. "He did nothing for us," assures one miner. They rage against the corrupt elites of Kiev. They no longer trust Ukrainian media. The workers are now tuned in to the propaganda tap of Russian channels that victimize them at will. Like in the heyday of the USSR, our interlocutors denounce the "fascists" and "hooligans" of Kiev, echoing the Moscow media's tune. They are convinced that life is much better in Russia than in Ukraine. When asked if he feels Russian or Ukrainian, a worker from DMZ responds, "I don't do politics," before slipping away. Power takeover During the 2012 European Football Championship, the Donbass Arena, the new and magnificent stadium offered by Ukrainian oligarch Rinat Akhmetov to Shakhtar Donetsk, hosted a match between Ukraine and France. In the stands, the supporters were adorned in the yellow and blue colors of the host country. The national anthem was sung at the top of their lungs, even though, the atmosphere dampened by the French goals, a handful of supporters began chanting "Russia" in the stadium. Two years later, this wave of unanimity is a distant memory, almost a mirage. The Maidan protests in 2014 were felt in Donetsk as a coup. A sentiment amplified by Russian propaganda. In Crimea, pro-Moscow forces have just taken over the local Parliament, a prelude to the annexation of the peninsula. In Donetsk, they do the same. When you arrive on the scene, the Russian flag flies over the huge building of the regional administration on Arkema Avenue. A small crowd is there, supporting this power grab. People of all ages and backgrounds continue to denounce the "thugs" of Kiev. They refer to history, revisited, to war, reinterpreted, to call for resistance against those who are labeled both "anarchists" and "fascists." The protesters wear on their chest or as armbands, no longer the blue of Yanukovych's Party of Regions, now demonetized, but the orange and black colors of the Order of St. George, a Russian military distinction. The belligerent intention is displayed. A former soldier of the Foreign Legion, happy to serve as our guide and to speak French, even if only roughly, leads us into these places whose doors are firmly held by mismatched militants. In the corridors, amid a great mess, a few Berkut, the equivalent of riot police, are sitting on the steps, quite passive and idle. Some three hundred pro-Russian militants occupy the offices. Inside the regional Parliament hall, the only journalists are Russian. Their cameras eagerly film the activists sitting on the red armchairs chanting "Ru-" "ssia", while speakers take turns at the podium expressing their unwavering attachment to the powerful neighbor. Their leader is named Pavel Gubarev. He is a computer scientist who came out of nowhere, leading an equally obscure "People's Militia of Donbass." He self-proclaims himself as governor and calls for a referendum on the region's future. His deputy, Denis Pushilin, received less than 1% of the votes in the previous year's legislative elections. 
But this commotion is limited to a few streets around the building. Beyond that, there is a strange calm. People go about their business as if none of this concerns them. 
At the same time, in Donetsk University, one of the oldest and most brilliant strongholds of Slavic culture, Sviatoslav Vakarchuk is welcomed. The leader of Okean Elzy, a rock band adored throughout Ukraine, the singer was elected as a pro-Western deputy before resigning, disgusted by the corruption and scheming in Kiev's political life. In the large amphitheater, this man from Lviv, near the Polish border, speaks about the future and democracy in front of a thousand young people. "For twenty-three years that Ukraine has been independent, people still want the same thing: a normal life. But this simple hope is being stolen from them," he summarizes. The audience sings the Ukrainian national anthem at the end. The students we talk to have their eyes set on Kiev and further towards Europe. But they also worry about the prospect of possibly abandoning the Russian language as the second official language, in favor of Ukrainian. They see this as a humiliation and a definite disadvantage. The bill will be withdrawn, but too late: the damage is done, and propaganda can denounce the attempt to eradicate Russian in Ukraine. 
In Donetsk, on Pushkin Avenue and Lenin Square, supporters of Ukraine gather every evening under the national colors. A counter-demonstration quickly forms in front of the regional Parliament, waving the Russian flag. Thugs escalate the tension, insult and push the pro-Ukrainian protesters, first bombarding them with eggs, flour, and then with punches. It is impossible to ask questions to these agitators rumored to come from Russia. The courage of the protesters facing these strongmen is admirable. But their forceful intervention marks the end of peaceful demonstrations. The next day, a young Ukrainian woman with the soul of a heroine, draped in blue and yellow, is violently confronted in front of the Parliament. The Iron Curtain If he succeeded in Crimea, the coup seems to have failed in Donetsk, as it did in Kharkiv, Dnipropetrovsk, Odessa, and Mariupol. The Parliament on Arkema Street is retaken by loyalists. The Ukrainian flag is raised back to its peak. Pavel Gubarev is arrested and imprisoned for sedition. But, on a platform set up outside the Parliament, pro-Russian speakers openly call for military support from Vladimir Putin this time. They collect signatures for petitions to that effect. The Kremlin will not stop fanning the flames. On April 7, 2014, the situation turns around. The pro-Russians retake the Parliament and then control of the entire region. A referendum for self-determination, not recognized by the international community, decides in early May on the region's independence. Kiev refuses and tries to regain control militarily. The Kremlin arms the separatists, already in the name of fighting against the Nazis and genocidal forces in Kiev. The war in Donbass begins, a new stage desired by Vladimir Putin to destabilize Ukraine. While Europe turns a blind eye, the conflict will result in 13,000 deaths and immense destruction. An Iron Curtain descends on Donbass. Kremlin propaganda wins the battle of minds before engaging in the battle of weapons. No dissenting voices are allowed. Living conditions have not improved for miners and metalworkers, but who would dare to complain? For the young generation of Donetsk, 2014 marked the end of illusions of a normal life, as desired by the singer Sviatoslav Vakarchuk. Many of those we met have since left the region. They have sought refuge in Kiev or emigrated to Europe. The "People's Republic of Donetsk," under Russian control and support, has emptied part of its population, especially the young and educated. It was quickly mocked as a retirement home of the USSR. But the Ukrainians were probably wrong to mock. The now President Denis Pushilin, who yesterday represented only himself, was by Putin's side when he signed the recognition of the "Republic of Donetsk" on February 21, 2022. And now, more than ever convinced that they are the victims of genocide and fighting against Kiev's Nazism, the separatists actively participate, alongside Russian troops, in the conquest of the rest of Ukraine.
lead: Vladimir Putin waged a war of minds in eastern Ukraine that paved the way for a war of weapons. Our journalist delved back into his reporting notebooks to recount the beginnings of the Russian invasion.

title: En France, la double peine de la diaspora tchétchène
text: To accompany the green tea, there are still a few "Russian candies" left. With a neat hair and beard, Chamil Albakov points to the chocolates scattered on the long meeting table. The co-founder of the Assembly of Chechens in Europe (ATE), created in October 2020, receives guests in the offices of the association, squeezed between a garage, a road haulage company, and a tattoo salon on the outskirts of Strasbourg. On the wall, the outline of the European continent, the colors of independent Chechnya, and the name of the association, in Cyrillic and English, overlap on the same flag. This father of five children is about to give a French lesson voluntarily to two young members of the association - one of the many roles of this 36-year-old civil engineer, who arrived in France in 2002. Today, his commitment to the ATE occupies him several hours a week. Sometimes entire nights and weekends. "We try to reassure the diaspora, but with what? We have no means, we only have words." Influential figures On Saturday, January 8, the ATE organized a demonstration in Strasbourg, in front of the Council of Europe, to denounce the kidnappings of relatives of opponents on Chechen soil by the dictatorial regime of President Ramzan Kadyrov, a protege of Vladimir Putin. For two hours, 300 people from all over Europe held up signs saying "Chechen Lives Matter," a reference to the American Black Lives Matter movement. But Chamil Albakov laments that the gathering did not even elicit "a tweet of support" from a politician, despite the presence of influential figures from the diaspora, such as the anti-regime blogger Toumso Abdourakhmanov, who has been granted political asylum in Sweden. Sitting on a wooden chair in these premises lent by a local Turkish NGO, the Council for Justice, Equality, and Peace (Cojep), the thirty-something man sighs: "If our actions have no effect, at some point, everyone will lose hope." As the presidential election in France approaches, in a period where security, immigration, and Islam take up a lot of space in the debates, members of the Chechen diaspora say they are very "concerned" about the election. But they have a hard time making themselves heard. The situation is not improving. Mostly Muslims, the 60,000 Chechens in France are regularly stigmatized by political figures. The leader of La France insoumise, Jean-Luc Mélenchon, mentioned "a problem with the Chechen community" on the LCI channel. This was a few days after the attack on Professor Samuel Paty on October 16, 2020, in Conflans-Sainte-Honorine, committed by Abdoullakh Anzorov, an 18-year-old of Chechen origin raised in France. Since then, the presidential candidate has apologized for his remarks. A few years earlier, in 2014, the controversial figure Eric Zemmour denounced on RTL "the gangs of Chechens, Roma, Kosovars, North Africans, Africans who rob, assault, or plunder." Accused of inciting hatred and racial defamation, the justice system acquitted him in June 2016. And this year, the situation is not improving, Chamil Albakov laments: "We are being used as scapegoats during this campaign." The now far-right presidential candidate Eric Zemmour repeated his 2014 attack almost word for word in his book, "France Has Not Yet Said Its Last Word" (Rubempré, 2021). Chamil Albakov also deplores the increasing number of Chechens being expelled from French territory. Since the end of 2020, the NGO Chechnya Committee has documented more than a dozen cases, whereas they were rare before. Most of the procedures are now justified by the fight against terrorism, but this organization notes threats to public order that are often vaguely defined and incidents that sometimes date back several years. Lawyer Lucie Simon has lost count of the phone calls from Chechens seeking her help regarding the withdrawal of refugee status or deportation from French territory to Moscow. At least "once a week," she specifies. She denounces the stigmatization of an entire community: "It's the era of suspicion." 150,000 refugees in Europe In France, Chechens say they suffer from multiple stereotypes. Since several young people left for Syria to join the Islamic State in the mid-2010s, they have been associated with terrorism. They are also often imagined to be practitioners of combat sports, especially MMA, elevated to the status of a national sport by President Kadyrov. To combat these stereotypes, the ATE is trying to mobilize the diaspora. The organization now brings together dozens of members, spread across 40 associations in 10 countries. An ambitious union representing a total of 150,000 refugees across all of Europe. Two wars of independence against Russia, from 1994 to 1996 and then from 1999 to 2000, forced the Chechens into exile. These two conflicts resulted in tens of thousands of civilian casualties. With the support of Russian President Vladimir Putin, Ramzan Kadyrov became the President of the Chechen Republic, which is part of the Russian Federation, in 2007. The transformation of the narrow Caucasus territory into a ruthless dictatorship has driven tens of thousands more people to flee abroad. This loosely structured community prefers to remain discreet. But now, the ATE refuses to remain "enclosed in a bubble." They no longer have much of a choice. The first time Heda Inderbaeva, 30 years old, decides to speak to BFM-TV, the young member of the ATE's communication team timidly addresses the camera. "No one wanted to do it," she explains today, at a Parisian café terrace. It was in June 2020, after violence in the Grésilles neighborhood in Dijon. The assault on a young Chechen triggers clashes involving dozens of people, including some members of the diaspora who came from abroad to settle scores. At the time, the community was wrongly accused of fighting to take control of the drug trafficking. Several arrests were made, followed by indictments for "participation in a gathering with a weapon by a person intentionally concealing their face." Heda Inderbaeva, "annoyed and tired," tries to set the record straight and explain to continuous news channels that it is not about drug trafficking. Summary of the programs The young woman, who arrived in Cahors in Occitanie at the age of 10 and whose father, an entrepreneur, was killed during the first war of independence, gradually becomes the "press attaché" for the Chechens. After the assassination of Samuel Paty, she is once again sought after. In April 2021, Heda Inderbaeva leaves the Parisian communication agency where she was employed ("My boss asked me to stop representing the Chechens and I gradually distanced myself," she confides), and then creates her own company. The Chechen diaspora then officially becomes one of her clients: she works for the ATE and other associations. For this presidential campaign, Heda Inderbaeva has set herself a goal: to interest her compatriots in French politics. She plans to organize a debate on the daily lives of Chechens in low-income neighborhoods in Strasbourg in March. Young people will improvise a public meeting during which they will play different roles: NGO leader, neighborhood representative, political leader... Each will then explain which candidate they would like to support. This is a way, according to her, to motivate these young people to join political parties. "There is already a young woman who has just joined Les Républicains," she points out. She also plans to send a summary of each candidate's programs and a voting advice written by her to the diaspora and its thousands of potential voters through social networks. For now, the thirty-year-old is leaning towards Jean-Luc Mélenchon. "But everything can change," she hesitates. The EELV candidate, Yannick Jadot, gained points after his remarks on France 2 on February 17: "I remind you that Vladimir Putin, he gassed the Syrian populations, he massacred the Chechens, he invaded Crimea and poisons his political opponents." During the 2021 regional elections in Nice, where the Chechen community is very present, she had suggested not to vote for Thierry Mariani (RN) or Renaud Muselier (LR). Temporary flag Members of the diaspora seem to trust this communicator, including the first to come or the most prestigious, such as Saïd-Emin Ibragimov. This man, soon to be 76 years old, was a deputy minister under the USSR in the Chechen territory, then Minister of Information and Transport of the Republic of Ichkeria, which is independent Chechnya, which existed from 1991 to 2000. He arrived in France in 2001, after a stop in Istanbul, where he was the victim of a knife attack, never claimed. Jacques Chirac, then head of state, reportedly personally offered him political asylum. A significant support that, he says, allowed him to actively denounce the atrocities committed in Chechnya in the name of the scattered diaspora in Europe. It is also to be closer to the "human rights organizations", potentially listening to his stories of wars and despair, that Ibragimov settled in the city of European institutions. Several thousand Chechens decided to join him there, making Bas-Rhin one of the departments where the diaspora is most present. The septuagenarian receives guests at his home, in a discreet tower in Ostwald, in the nearby suburbs. A single light bulb still allows the chandelier in the living room to function, partially illuminating a floral wallpaper and the temporary flag of the Republic of Ichkeria - red and green with a wolf in the center. Today, in his native country, this emblem is banned by the authorities. On the other side of the wall is the bedroom: a "laboratory" where Saïd-Emin Ibragimov constantly works on evidence of Russian abuses against the Chechen people. Left for dead Trained as an international law jurist in Moscow, he has made it his mission to alert public opinion. But he has always struggled to attract attention. After a first forty-two-day hunger strike in 2001 to denounce "crimes against humanity committed during the last war," the politician repeated the strike in 2002, 2004, and 2006. "In total, I spent more than three hundred days without eating," he calculates, almost proud behind his rectangular glasses with smoked lenses. "They asked me to sign papers and apologize to Vladimir Putin and the Kadyrov clan, on camera." Saïd-Emin Ibragimov, former minister under independent Chechnya In 2009, Ibragimov and three other Chechen opponents on French soil are targeted by a terrorist plot, according to a report from the Central Directorate of Interior Intelligence (DCRI) consulted by M Le magazine. But the attack is thwarted. In July 2014, he filed a complaint against Vladimir Putin with the International Criminal Court (ICC) for crimes committed during the Second Chechen War. A few weeks later, the former minister was the victim of an assassination attempt. That summer, he was fishing along the Ill, the river that runs through Strasbourg, when suddenly he was knocked unconscious. When he came to, he was blindfolded and recognized the "typically Muscovite" accent of his captors, he points out today. His son Baïsangour, a student in Paris, helps him overcome these traumatic memories by recalling the death threats first, then the tortures... "They asked me to sign papers and apologize to Vladimir Putin and the Kadyrov clan, on camera, and then to explain that there had been no genocide in Chechnya, contrary to what I claim." About ten days after the incident, the former political leader showed his wounds and multiple bruises to the American magazine Time. Everything is detailed in his medical file from Hautepierre hospital, of which he shows a copy. The sixty-year-old man was eventually thrown into a hole, "covered with branches and leaves," in the middle of the forest. Left for dead. After managing to get out of the pit, he filed a complaint at a Strasbourg police station, and an investigation was launched. However, after two years of proceedings, no suspects were arrested and the case was closed. Targeted killings of opponents Today, the Chechen diaspora lives in fear of reprisals. Everyone remembers the targeted killings of opponents on European soil: Zelimkhan Khangochvili, shot in the head twice in Berlin in August 2019; Imran Aliev, killed with 137 stab wounds in Lille in February 2020; Mamikhan Oumarov, found dead in a parking lot on the outskirts of Vienna in July 2020... The authoritarian regime of Ramzan Kadyrov acts as if it has a "kind of long arm," capable of killing its citizens, even in Europe, analyzes Anne Le Huérou, lecturer in Russian and post-Soviet studies at the University of Paris-Nanterre. "We almost canceled the demonstration on January 8," admits Chamil Albakov, aware of the risks of any act of opposition to President Ramzan Kadyrov. This summer, back in Chechnya for the holidays, Heda Inderbaeva was "interrogated" twice. At the airport first, by a man in a police uniform. Then in Grozny, the capital, by an "FSB agent." "Regime supporters have infiltrated the diaspora," she asserts. And, as many fear that this may also be the case within associations, a portion of the refugee Chechens prefer to stay away from any attempt at organization. "The rest of the political class listens politely to me before moving on." Raphaël Glucksmann, Member of the European Parliament L'ATE does not give up, however. To be better heard, they have sent numerous letters to Emmanuel Macron, and then to his Minister of the Interior, Gérald Darmanin. They proposed to set up a meeting to discuss insecurity in certain popular neighborhoods and prevention against radicalization within the diaspora. But these dozens of letters have received, at best, only a response promising to be "attentive to [these] concerns." In the office of the Minister of the Interior, Gérald Darmanin, it is believed that this association cannot be the sole interlocutor of the Chechen community. "Politicians don't care," regrets one of its few supporters, European Parliament member Raphaël Glucksmann. He discovered the situation in this Caucasus territory through his father, the philosopher André Glucksmann, a committed advocate for the Chechen people during the wars against Russia. But the parliamentarian laments, "The rest of the political class listens politely to me before moving on." Hoping to still attract the attention of France in the midst of an election campaign and international institutions, Saïd-Emin Ibragimov planned a new demonstration on February 23 in Strasbourg. It ultimately could not take place. Nevertheless, he attended a small gathering at Place de la République in Paris. A few hours before Vladimir Putin's entry into war, Saïd-Emin Ibragimov took the opportunity to strongly condemn Russia's attitude towards Ukraine.
lead: They say they are victims of both stigmatization within French society and the terror imposed by the dictator Ramzan Kadyrov in their home country and on their adopted land.

title: Jean-Yves Le Drian dans « Questions politiques » : en Ukraine, « nous sommes devant un mur, une logique de sièges »
text: Every Sunday, from noon to 1 pm, Le Monde partners with France Inter to host and broadcast the program "Political Questions," presented by Thomas Snegaroff, with Carine Bécard and Alexandra Bensaid (France Inter), Jeff Wittenberg (France TV), and Françoise Fressoz (Le Monde). The guest on Sunday, March 13th, was Jean-Yves Le Drian, Minister for Europe and Foreign Affairs. The war in Ukraine will be long because Vladimir Putin is "stuck in his maximalism" despite the gradual "suffocation" of Russia caused by Western sanctions, he stated. "We are facing a wall, a logic of sieges that risks lasting a long time, so much so that (...) I think the worst is yet to come," continued the French Foreign Minister, who described himself as "pessimistic" as several Ukrainian cities are surrounded and bombarded by the Russian army. "This war will be long unless we reach the point where it is no longer possible for Vladimir Putin, given the extent of the consequences of the sanctions we are implementing," defended Jean-Yves Le Drian.
lead: The Minister of Europe and Foreign Affairs was a guest on France Inter, France Télévisions, and "Le Monde" this Sunday, from noon to 1 p.m.
