title: « Munichois », « va-t-en-guerre » : le conflit en Ukraine réveille les vieux clivages de la gauche
text: It is now evident that the war in Ukraine has provided the left with a new opportunity to highlight its differences. On one side, there is Jean-Luc Mélenchon, and on the other side, Yannick Jadot and Anne Hidalgo. In the demonstrations in support of the Ukrainian people, each marches separately. While Olivier Faure, Raphaël Glucksmann, Yannick Jadot, and Christiane Taubira have been photographed side by side, the deputies of La France insoumise avoid posing for family photos. "What a stupid thing war is!" For his major Parisian rally on Thursday, March 10, Fabien Roussel chose the words of Prévert to also emphasize messages of peace, calling for "setting aside quarrels" to demand a ceasefire in Ukraine. However, the left is far from agreeing on the means to make peace; it even disputes the pacifist legacy. European Parliament member Raphaël Glucksmann questioned on March 6 on France 5: "We can all be for peace, but how do we achieve peace, and what is peace? Refusing to give the Ukrainian resistance the means to defend themselves is not being a pacifist; it is letting those who wage war win." The term "Munichois" - in reference to the Munich agreements of 1938, signed by France and the United Kingdom with Nazi Germany and Mussolini's Italy to avoid war but resulting in Hitler's annexation of the Sudetenland and the dismantling of Czechoslovakia - was quickly used in Yannick Jadot's camp to describe Jean-Luc Mélenchon's position. "Warmonger," Mélenchon retorted, believing that supplying weapons to Ukraine risks a warlike escalation. They also argue over the legacy of Jean Jaurès, a pacifist assassinated on the eve of World War I. The invectives reached a peak this week, following Jean-Luc Mélenchon's "peace" rally on Sunday, where he reiterated his desire to leave NATO and his preference for national sovereignty over the idea of European defense. "All his grand speeches on peace mask his complacency and capitulations to Putin," Yannick Jadot reacted on Sud Radio. "Can you imagine Jean Jaurès defending the bombing of civilian populations in Syria?" This was in reference to Jean-Luc Mélenchon's recent statement that Vladimir Putin had "solved the problem in Syria." Each claims Jaurès for themselves. "The reference to Jaurès is almost a recurring theme," notes historian Roger Martelli. "It has always been ambiguous. Jaurès is the one, in the communist reading, who died because he was a pacifist and would never have accepted that the left be engulfed in the sacred union at the outbreak of World War I. On the socialist side, Jaurès is a man of peace but also of the new army, who would have accepted participation in the sacred union." Each has their own Jaurès, then. As pointed out by the La France insoumise deputy from Seine-Saint-Denis, Alexis Corbière, quoting Trotsky, "great men know how to die at the right moment" and leave their legacy in ambiguity. Behind the founder of L’Humanité, another figure with a less dazzling legacy emerges in peace arguments: Léon Blum, and his choice of non-intervention in the Spanish Civil War (1936-1939). No one on the left identifies as "Blumist" today. But when Yannick Jadot refuses Jean-Luc Mélenchon the legacy of Jaurès, he implicitly sends him back to that camp, or even to a much darker history, that of Munich, or even that of the left of integral pacifism and anti-Bolshevism which provided figures for collaboration. "Peace at the cost of capitulation is not peace. Peace is inseparable from the defense of democracy and freedom," emphasizes the entourage of the ecological candidate. But analogies have obvious limits: to the pacifism of the first half of the 20th century is added the balance of terror in place during the Cold War. Everyone agrees on this, even if they place the cursor of the war machine differently. "We can support someone who calls for help but certainly not engage militarily. It is out of the question to end up with French planes in Ukraine," warns, for example, the Socialist senator for French citizens abroad, Hélène Conway Mouret, who advises Anne Hidalgo on these issues. "I am wary of Jadot's bellicosity, which is only due to one thing, distancing himself from what we are doing," retorts Alexis Corbière. Frédéric Dabi, director of opinions at the polling institute IFOP, looks at the left's campaign against war. While the Russian invasion further complicates the opposition's ability to make itself heard, "Jean-Luc Mélenchon is the one who expresses peace the most. The other candidates address the issue more indirectly, Yannick Jadot makes it an ecological issue by calling for an embargo and moving away from fossil fuels to stop financing war crimes, Anne Hidalgo takes on her role as mayor of Paris to welcome refugees and position herself on the moral ground..." Far from extinguishing the campaign, the war in Ukraine forces everyone to choose new paths.
lead: Tensions have risen sharply since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, between the left-wing candidates in the presidential election, with the ecologist Yannick Jadot and the "unsubmissive" Jean-Luc Mélenchon at the forefront.

title: Ukraine : la Russie entrouvre la porte à un apaisement avec les Occidentaux
text: A portion of Russian troops deployed in border regions of Ukraine have started to return to their bases after completing military exercises, Russian agencies reported on Tuesday, February 15, citing the Ministry of Defense. According to Interfax, units deployed in the southern and western regions, on the eastern border of Ukraine, have finished their maneuvers, although other military exercises are ongoing in the rest of the country. "Units from the Southern and Western military districts, having completed their tasks, have already started to board rail and road transports and today they will begin to return to their garrisons," said Russian Defense Ministry spokesperson Igor Konashenkov, without providing further details. The return of Russian soldiers to their bases is seen as a sign of de-escalation, as Moscow and Western countries engage in diplomatic exchanges to find a way out of the crisis surrounding Ukraine. On Monday, February 14, Russian Defense Minister Sergei Shoigu gave an initial indication of a possible easing of tensions with the West, stating that other maneuvers, organized simultaneously in Belarus, "are coming to an end." "Exercises are taking place, some have finished. Others are still ongoing, given their scale," added the minister. On Tuesday, Kiev authorities welcomed these announcements, stating that Ukraine and the West have prevented a Russian "escalation." This initial de-escalation comes as, on Monday, Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov, breaking from his previous offensive statements, expressed a desire to give diplomacy a new "chance." The same day, German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, visiting Kiev, urged Moscow to consider the "dialogue offers" from Western countries. "There is always a chance" The Russian foreign minister made these remarks during a consultation with President Vladimir Putin, who, true to his habit, remained mostly silent. The staged event, with both men seated at a very long table, Covid-19 protocol in place, was televised to convey the message, albeit ambiguously. "Our options are far from exhausted," explained Sergei Lavrov. The foreign minister emphasized: there is "always a chance" to reach a compromise. Pointing out that Moscow is awaiting responses from Western countries to its demands for "security guarantees," including assurance that Ukraine will never join NATO, he noted that the United States has put forward "constructive" proposals to reduce military risks, but the responses from the European Union and NATO are not satisfactory. Sergei Lavrov also warned: "We have already warned more than once that we would not allow endless negotiations on issues that require a solution today." Western countries have so far deemed Russian demands unacceptable, but have proposed increased dialogue on other topics, including arms control. The Russian army, which has deployed around 125,000 men along the Ukrainian border - a ninth of its total forces - has also been active in the north with exercises in Belarus, as well as in the south with maneuvers in the Black Sea. During his discussions at the Kremlin with French President Emmanuel Macron on Monday, February 7, Vladimir Putin reportedly assured that Russia would withdraw its troops and weapons after the exercises in Belarus. This information was provided by the Elysée, but has not been fully confirmed by the Kremlin. Diplomacy of uncertainty Defense Minister Sergei Shoigu and President Vladimir Putin in Moscow on February 14. SPUTNIK / VIA REUTERS "There remains a crucial opportunity for diplomacy," British Prime Minister Boris Johnson and U.S. President Joe Biden said during a phone call on Monday. But the situation remains tense: the Pentagon stated that Moscow reinforced its military presence along the Ukrainian borders over the weekend, and its spokesperson warned that "military action [could] occur at any time." On Monday, before Moscow sent its signs of de-escalation, French Foreign Minister Jean-Yves Le Drian had stated that "all the elements" were in place for a "strong" Russian military offensive, while also assuring that "there is no indication today" that Vladimir Putin had made a decision. After listening to his two ministers, the Kremlin leader did not provide any indication of his mindset. "Good," he simply replied to Sergey Lavrov regarding the "chances" of compromise with the Western countries. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz was expected in Moscow on Tuesday. He was to meet with President Vladimir Putin. This would be an opportunity for the Kremlin leader to confirm the de-escalation gestures made by his defense and foreign ministers. Or to once again play it cool, or at least remain evasive, as an expert in the diplomacy of uncertainty. Especially as, alongside this partial troop withdrawal, the State Duma (the lower house of the Russian Parliament) is preparing to vote on whether or not to recognize the Donbass, a region in eastern Ukraine that fell into the hands of pro-Russian rebels in 2014. A symbolic gesture that could serve as a reminder that Moscow continues to exert pressure on Kiev.
lead: Moscow announced that some of the military forces deployed near the Ukrainian border for weeks have started to return to their bases. The departure of Russian soldiers is seen as a sign of de-escalation.

title: Crise ukrainienne : Valérie Pécresse et Marine Le Pen accusent Emmanuel Macron d’avoir « fait de la communication »
text: Marine Le Pen denounced, on Tuesday, February 22, the attitude of Emmanuel Macron, whose services had announced in the night from Sunday to Monday a "principle" agreement between Vladimir Putin and Joe Biden for a meeting aimed at a diplomatic resolution of the conflict between them in Ukraine. On Monday evening, Mr. Putin crossed the red line established by the Americans and their allies, including France, by recognizing the independence of the separatist territories in Ukraine and ordering a Russian military intervention in these same territories. "Emmanuel Macron sought to use this diplomatic sequence to support a campaign entry," said Marine Le Pen, the candidate of the National Rally (RN) for the Elysée, on RTL. "I saw photos of Emmanuel Macron unshaven, head in hands, it's all very artistic, but it's not serious to communicate on these subjects," she lamented. "That's why, due to this failure, he postponed his campaign entry," added Ms. Le Pen. "We even wondered if he was really the President of the Republic in this sequence or if he was trying to be a Nobel Peace Prize winner." "It's not me saying this, but his Twitter account. Communication all the time, in all circumstances, I find it very questionable," she mocked. The RN candidate refers to the photos taken by the official photographer of the Elysée published on Instagram, which ended up on Twitter, without being relayed by the official account of the President of the Republic. In a tweet, Yannick Jadot asks the President of the Republic to receive "all the candidates and parties represented to discuss the situation." The Green candidate also called for a gathering this Tuesday at 5 p.m. in front of the Russian Embassy in Paris to show his "solidarity with the Ukrainian people." A topic "that goes far beyond Emmanuel Macron" Tuesday morning on France Inter, responding to an accusation from Clément Beaune about François Fillon's "complicity" with Vladimir Putin, Valérie Pécresse also criticized Emmanuel Macron's attitude: "I am in the opposition but I show dignity and I do not attack the President of the Republic, even when I feel he is using an international cause for electoral purposes." On France Inter, earlier on Tuesday morning, the president of the La République en marche group in the National Assembly, Christophe Castaner, regretted that "President Putin has broken all the commitments he had made," including participating in a summit with President Joe Biden, as announced by the Elysée on Sunday night. "Reducing this to Emmanuel Macron is having very nationalistic intentions." "We are discussing a topic that goes far beyond Emmanuel Macron," he said, noting that he currently holds the presidency of the Council of the European Union for six months.
lead: The announcement from the Elysée on Monday morning of an upcoming meeting between Vladimir Putin and Joe Biden, at the initiative of Emmanuel Macron, was made obsolete as of last night by the Russian president's about-face.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : Valérie Pécresse appelle à dépasser le « réflexe légitimiste » favorable à Emmanuel Macron
text: Valérie Pécresse sought, on Sunday, March 13, to make another point of view on French policy towards Ukraine more clearly heard. The candidate of the Republicans in the presidential election called on the French to go beyond, in the first round of the presidential election on April 10, the "legitimacy reflex" encouraged, according to her, by the Russian threat at the borders of the European Union, which she believes favors Emmanuel Macron. As a guest on the "Grand Rendez-vous" of Europe 1, Les Echos, and CNews, Valérie Pécresse notably mentioned a "gap" between the summit of European leaders in Versailles on March 10 and 11, and the expectations of Kiev. She mentioned a meeting of the European People's Party (right-wing) held at the same time, with Ukrainian political leaders who reportedly testified that the arms deliveries promised by Brussels had "still not arrived." She defended herself against wanting to rewrite recent French-Russian diplomacy history while questioning the actions of the President. She emphasized that the United States "had foreseen" the invasion in Ukraine. "We didn't believe it," she lamented. Le Monde reported on March 4 that different Western intelligence services had a different interpretation of the signals sent by Moscow between the fall of 2021 and the beginning of the invasion on February 24 - the United States and the United Kingdom considering the offensive a certainty, while the Europeans believed the diplomatic path was still open. She also recalled that she had suggested a "peace conference in Europe" that would have clearly stated that "Ukraine's entry into NATO was not current," in order to appease the President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin. Macron "too late in his awareness" The right-wing candidate mentioned what she believed were two "precursors" of the invasion: the deployment of the Russian Wagner militia in the Sahel, which came to "destabilize French interests," and the "migratory blackmail" organized at the Polish border in the summer of 2021 by Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko, an ally of Vladimir Putin - Brussels had accused Minsk of granting visas to many migrants to destabilize the European Union. According to Valérie Pécresse, the French-Russian dialogue was not "sufficiently supported" between the Macron-Putin meetings in Versailles in 2017 and at Fort de Brégançon in 2019, and in 2021, when the hegemonic desire of the Kremlin leader was "increasingly felt." In essence, she criticized the President for being "too late in realizing the danger posed by Vladimir Putin." Ms. Pécresse also criticized the President on domestic policy issues, such as the "stagnant" purchasing power and the "laissez-faire" approach to migration. "When there is a war, there is a legitimist reflex, one thinks that there is a captain leading the operation," she noted, adding: "I believe we should not be afraid to change captains on April 11 [the day after the first round] because I have the experience, I have a very solid government team, I have alliances with major parties, with the great leaders of this world." Particularly, according to her, in the European Parliament and the European Commission.
lead: The Republican candidate for the 2022 presidential election, who believes "we must not be afraid to change the captain," criticized the diplomacy conducted by the head of state in recent months on Sunday.

title: Comment la guerre en Ukraine a ébranlé l’ordre mondial
text: On Tuesday, March 8, two American citizens, including an executive in the oil industry, were released from Venezuelan jails. Far from the battles on the snowy lands of Eastern Europe, this seemingly insignificant event reflects the global impact of the shock of the Russian invasion of Ukraine on February 24. These releases are a sign of a thaw between Washington and Caracas, where an official American delegation had been received at the presidential palace the previous weekend for talks described by Nicolas Maduro as "respectful, cordial, and very diplomatic." Why Venezuela? The Maduro regime, which the Trump administration unsuccessfully tried to overthrow, is Moscow's main ally in Latin America. It is also a major oil producer, whose oil was refined in the United States before the country shifted towards Chavista anti-imperialism and its economy collapsed. With the war in Ukraine and the oil embargo on Russia declared on March 8 by the Biden administration, barriers are falling. The oil market chessboard is being reconsidered, with Venezuela being one of the pieces to move. In just two weeks, the return of war in Europe has shifted geopolitical lines even in the American subcontinent. Nothing is yet decided on the ground: even though the Ukrainian resistance has managed to slow the Russian army's advance, the outcome of the conflict remains uncertain. It is also too early to say how far the shockwave will reach. But its impact is already comparable to that of the September 11 attacks, or even the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz and French President Emmanuel Macron have both referred to "the new era" that Russia has just ushered in for Europe. This new era marks the end of the three decades of the post-Cold War era, which began with the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991. What will this era be made of? Historian Mary Elise Sarotte, an expert on the Cold War and post-Cold War era, is pessimistic: in a text published by The New York Times, she envisions a "period of immense hostility with Moscow," especially problematic since the codes of the Cold War have disappeared. Conflicts in the 20th century were characterized by the concern of the "two superpowers" to avoid direct confrontation; rules and communication channels framed this modus vivendi. "The lack of scruples of President Putin," writes Mary Elise Sarotte, "calls all of this into question." Most arms control treaties have become obsolete or have been abandoned, while new weapons have emerged without any regulation. Resuming lengthy negotiations with a Moscow regime capable of attacking a country overnight with such violence seems very difficult. "Back in the USSR" But who can bet today that it is with Vladimir Putin that one will have to deal? His offensive targets Ukraine but also backfires against Russia. Its economy is brought to its knees by Western sanctions. The master of the Kremlin destroys two countries at once. His ignorance of what Ukraine is, the errors in his invasion plan, and his underestimation of the Western reaction tarnish his image as a tactician and raise doubts about his ability to surround himself. His determination to prevent any popular dissent about the war has led him to take increasingly repressive measures, turning Russia into a prison from which intellectual elites desperately seek to escape. To prevent dissent at the top, he staged a spectacle that will go down in history as a model of dictatorial exercise, convening his security council and then the oligarchs, under the watchful eye of millions of viewers, on February 21 and 24. The fate of his inner circle, now made complicit in his decision to invade Ukraine, is now tied to that of the president. There is no peaceful alternative imaginable at the top of the state. It's "Back in the USSR," without the mechanisms that allowed the Politburo to remove the number one and replace him. Vladimir Putin had everything planned to stay in power until 2036; if that is still his intention, it promises him many years locked in his bunker. The unprecedented scope of Western sanctions, financial, commercial, economic, and technological, deeply and durably compromises the progress of the Russian economy and its innovation capabilities. The cost of the war in Ukraine, maintaining the forces engaged, will burden its budget. Is there an outcome to this conflict that is favorable to Mr. Putin? If he manages to establish a puppet regime in Kiev, at the cost of even bloodier battles, the occupation of Ukraine will ruin him, while Western sanctions continue to suffocate his economy. If the Russian army eventually retreats, controlling only part of Ukraine, this defeat will be his. "I am curious to see how he will celebrate this year on May 9th," the traditionally grand commemoration of victory in the "Great Patriotic War" in 1945, pondered Andrei Grachev, former adviser to Mikhail Gorbachev, in Paris. Will a weakened Russia be able to maintain its grip on Belarus, virtually annexed since its president, Alexander Lukashenko, called for help to quell the popular uprising in 2020? And on Kazakhstan, which also called for help to quell another revolt in January? Vladimir Putin launched an offensive on Ukraine to prevent it from joining what he sees as the Western camp and to stop its eastward expansion. The upheavals he has triggered could have the opposite effect: reducing the sphere of influence he claims for Russia. Not only is Ukraine seeking to join the European Union (EU), but it is followed by the small Moldova, which has so far been cautious in its European aspirations so as not to provoke Moscow, and by Georgia: three former Soviet republics. A chasm of uncertainty has opened up in front of Russia, which still shares 90% of the world's nuclear arsenal with the United States. European awakening In the West, this crisis has also transformed the European Union, but for the better. To provoke a European awakening, to awaken the "sleeping beauty," it took an armed aggression led at its border by a nuclear power, a permanent member of the UN Security Council. Unprecedented since World War II. Europe was indeed asleep. "The old concepts of tank battles on European soil are outdated," explained British Prime Minister Boris Johnson to his MPs in the fall. However, starting in November 2021, the United States, and the United Kingdom in echo, repeated that Russia was preparing to invade Ukraine. Incredulous, neither the EU nor the Ukrainian authorities took them seriously. When they finally admitted that preparations were underway, they convinced themselves that, if there was aggression, it would be limited. "They will not attack Kiev," assured a Ukrainian official to European colleagues on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference over the weekend of February 18, which preceded the start of the war. The awakening is as brutal as the entry of Russian tanks into Ukraine. Shaken, the Europeans stand united with the United States and NATO. At an unprecedented speed and with exemplary unity, they break with fourteen years of complacency towards Mr. Putin, since the invasion of Georgia in 2008, then the annexation of Crimea and the intervention in Donbass in 2014. They decide on massive sanctions against Russia and take the step of military aid to Ukraine. In a matter of days, "soft power" gives way to "hard power." An airlift is set up to deliver tons of weapons, equipment, armored vehicles, and anti-tank missiles to Ukraine. The most dramatic shift occurs in Germany. Suddenly, the country opens its eyes to its military weakness and its energy dependence on Russia. On February 22, after months of hesitation, Berlin finally gives up on the German-Russian gas pipeline Nord Stream 2. On the 27th, Chancellor Scholz delivers a speech in the Bundestag, immediately dubbed historic: defense spending will be increased to 2% of GDP; a fund of 100 billion euros will be allocated to the Bundeswehr to finance investments and armament projects. The dividends of peace will no longer enrich Germany; it must spend. Just two months after Chancellor Angela Merkel's departure, "demerkelization" is underway. How far will it go? The already precarious plan for the German energy transition will be called into question? What will the German army do with these 100 billion euros? Will the critical examination of Berlin's Russian policy extend to its mercantilist policy towards China? "We know what Germany no longer wants to do; we do not yet know what it will do," nuances a European diplomat. Multiple dynamics are shaking Europe. The expansion to the east of the EU is potentially explosive, while in the Western Balkans, other complicated candidates are becoming impatient. Another dynamic is the weight gained by the eastern countries of the Union, particularly Poland, which is getting its revenge with the exemplary reception of over 1 million Ukrainian refugees in ten days. And which sees the opportunity to assert its European identity, despite the conflict with Brussels over the rule of law. Gathered in Versailles on March 10 and 11, the heads of state and government of the Twenty-Seven are tackling this new hostile environment. With war, the "European sovereignty" and "strategic autonomy" advocated by Paris and Brussels take on a very concrete meaning. Unlike the Cold War, during which economic systems were separated, Europe now depends on Russia for its gas supply. Paradox: since February 24, while seeking to strangle the Russian economy, Europeans are paying Moscow $700 million a day for their gas and oil. The decision, made in Versailles, to end this dependence by 2027 will have major consequences on their economies. New sanctions have been decided against Moscow, but the step of allowing Kiev to join the EU has not been taken. Another European change of course, in terms of defense, Denmark has announced, following Germany, a significant increase in its military budget and will ask its citizens in a referendum on June 1 whether they want to join the EU's security policy: "In a historic era, historic decisions," said Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen. Finland and Sweden are also considering whether to join NATO. Resurrected NATO It's another feat by Putin: he has resurrected the Atlantic Alliance, two years after Mr. Macron's diagnosis of "brain death." At the Munich conference on February 19, its Secretary-General, Jens Stoltenberg, promises: "If the Kremlin's goal is to have fewer NATO forces on the Russian borders, it will only get more NATO." Forgotten are the digressions on the organization's role in Asia or elsewhere. It is returning to its core mission, the collective defense of Europe. Its cooperation with the EU has reached an unprecedented level. Thousands of soldiers are being sent as part of "reassurance" to strengthen the eastern flank of the Alliance in the Baltic countries, Poland, and Romania. Because this is the limit of NATO, set by President Joe Biden even before the start of the Russian offensive: Ukraine is not a member state, so NATO will not send troops there. However, Article 5 of its Charter, which states that an attack on one member is an attack on all, is "sacrosanct." Thus, according to Marc Pierini of the Carnegie think tank, "Moscow is conducting its campaign with complete military impunity, since NATO powers have clearly stated that they will not deploy troops on the ground, and with complete political impunity, as Moscow has veto power in the UN Security Council." Two documents will help measure the extent of the transformations in how Europe will conceive its defense: the EU's "strategic compass," revised in light of the Russian war, which is expected to be adopted by the member states at the end of March; and NATO's "strategic concept," under study for two years, which will be submitted at the Madrid summit in June. In any case, the invasion of Ukraine renders the founding NATO-Russia act of 1997 obsolete, under which the Alliance committed not to station permanent troops in the former Soviet satellite countries. Meanwhile, Turkey, a troublemaker in NATO, is trying to navigate as skillfully as possible between Russia, with which it wants to preserve its energy and economic ties, and Ukraine, to which it provides highly effective Bayraktar TB2 drones. But the longer the crisis lasts, the more uncomfortable the exercise will be for President Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Choices will need to be made. The US Mistake The question of American leadership, starkly raised during Donald Trump's presidency and already during Barack Obama's, is on the minds of all Europeans. While there is recognition of the close level of consultation between Washington and its allies, in contrast to the missteps of the Afghanistan withdrawal and the clumsiness of Aukus, the Australian-British-American pact, in 2021, there are still questions about American strategy – not to mention uncertainties about the strength of the Biden administration's majority, with the midterm elections in November. Fully focused on China, the United States has also misjudged Russia, seeing it as a disruptive power rather than aggressive, downplaying the significance of the annexation of Crimea and Putin's expansionist drift since 2008. Joe Biden, who was vice president from 2009 to 2017, contributed to this stance. Wrongly, he thought he had resolved the Russian issue after a meeting with Vladimir Putin in Geneva in July 2021, at the beginning of his term. Unlike Obama, Joe Biden was shaped by the Cold War. Will this influence his view of Russia? Will containment strategy return against Moscow, and if so, how will it be reconciled with his policy toward China? Will there be a "Biden doctrine" like there was a Truman doctrine, formulated in 1947, the foundation of American policy during the Cold War? The "foreign policy for the middle class" advocated by Joe Biden during his campaign is shattered by these questions. Washington must now manage not one but two adversaries, in two different geographical areas. Return of the nuclear weapon Moreover, the West will have to revise its "strategic grammar," notes Elie Tenenbaum in a report from IFRI. In this crisis, the Western countries have left Moscow with the "control of escalation," a "key concept in deterrence, which systematically benefits the one who has it." Thus, explains the researcher, Russia has left itself the possibility to escalate the conflict, while NATO has set its limits from the beginning. The "nuclear posturing," which is part of this "escalation" by Moscow, signals the return of this weapon in geopolitical discourse. While during the Cold War, nuclear weapons were not meant to be used, their use is now being brandished as a possibility. On February 27, Belarus amended its Constitution following a referendum as fictitious as the reelection of its president in 2020. This text includes two major changes: there is no longer mention of the principle of neutrality of the Belarusian state, nor of its commitment not to be a nuclear power. In other words, the door is open to the deployment of Russian nuclear weapons on its territory. Since the beginning of the offensive, the Russian president has been waving the nuclear threat. In the early hours, he warned countries opposing it that they would face consequences "like they have never seen before," and then put the Russian nuclear forces on "alert" on February 27. The stakes of deterrence are thus back in the spotlight, even though, as Olivier Schmitt, a professor at the Center for War Studies at the University of Southern Denmark, points out, "the modernization of arsenals and nuclear doctrines has continued uninterrupted since the end of the Cold War. However, due to the return of competition between major powers and the collapse of major arms control agreements, deterrence is becoming central again as a mode of regulating strategic interactions." And the warning resonates all the way to Asia. Beijing-Moscow, a friendship "without limits" Pariah in the eyes of international law, Russia was condemned on March 2 by the vote of 141 states (against 5 votes against and 35 abstentions) at the United Nations General Assembly. Europeans welcomed China's abstention, but these votes reveal deep divisions. If they foreshadow the balance of power in the future world order, they are bad omens for the Western countries. According to sources in European capitals, India's abstention can be explained by its dependence on arms supplies from Russia. However, this reason alone cannot explain the country's non-alignment, as it also participates in other alliances with the United States, Japan, and Australia. In Munich and Paris in February, India's Foreign Minister, Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, explained how his country had incorporated the "American retreat" into its foreign policy and intended to pursue an autonomous path. China, on the other hand, is engaging in a remarkable balancing act that should not deceive anyone. The fate of the Russo-Chinese relationship was sealed on February 4th when Vladimir Putin visited Beijing for the Winter Olympics opening. In a joint statement, President Xi Jinping and his Russian counterpart celebrated the "boundless" friendship between the two countries; China took a stance against NATO expansion. During this rare outing of Russia since 2020, did the Russian leader discuss his plans when over 100,000 of his soldiers were already amassed at the Ukrainian border? In any case, he waited until the end of the Games on February 20th to initiate hostilities. Western sanctions, the decoupling of the Russian and European economies, and the fragmentation of globalization will logically favor a rapprochement between Russia and China, with China seeking to assist Moscow by integrating it into the parallel international payment system it is trying to establish. Moreover, according to Pierre Noël, an expert on energy issues at Columbia University, "in the medium term, the impact of the Ukrainian crisis on global oil and gas markets will largely depend on the cooperation between Russia and China." For gas, he says, Russia has already begun redirecting its exports: by 2025, its pipeline exports to China will be close to what Moscow currently sells to Germany. Therefore, China and other emerging economies in Asia will determine the extent of Russia's energy isolation. "Their interest is to limit, as much as possible, the global increase in energy prices, thus helping Russia maintain its exports. The United States will pressure the countries of Southeast Asia, but India will not be dictated to," Pierre Noël continues. For China, there are obvious strategic considerations. "The most likely scenario in the medium term is not an energy isolation of Russia, but a shift of Russian dependence from Europe to Asia." Far from Ukraine, China now has even more cards to play. This is the ultimate test of this geopolitical challenge.
lead: In two weeks, the shockwave of the Russian invasion has shifted geopolitical dynamics. Europe is rearming, Putin is plunging Russia into uncertainty, and new fractures are emerging outside the Western bloc.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : Wikipedia, menacée de blocage en Russie, poursuit sa documentation du conflit
text: When you click on the "discussion" tab of the "Russian invasion of Ukraine (2022)" page on Wikipedia, a veritable barrage of warnings appears. On several pages, about ten boxes warn the internet user, notably that it is useless to dispute the title of the page to replace it with "Russian special operation in Ukraine" – the term officially used by Moscow. The community has decided, it is indeed an "invasion". This choice – and others – has attracted threats of blocking Wikipedia in Russia. Already in the crosshairs of Roskomnadzor, the Internet regulator in Russia, Wikipedia remains, for now, accessible, even as a new series of laws adopted in early March provide for very heavy penalties for any "defamation" against the Russian army or dissemination of "false information". Contributors active on pages related to the invasion face significant risks. "If you live in Russia or Belarus, DO NOT SIGN your messages and DO NOT SHARE personal information," a pinned message on the discussion page reminds. The threat is extremely concrete: on March 11, Mark Bernstein, an important contributor to the Russian-language Wikipedia, was arrested by the Belarusian police in Minsk. The day before, his identity and personal information had been disseminated on a pro-Russian Telegram channel, where he was accused of spreading "false information" about the army via Wikipedia. He was sentenced to fifteen days in prison for refusing to comply, but also faces a much heavier sentence, up to fifteen years of detention, for violating the new Russian law. Global interest If political repression also affects contributors to the online encyclopedia, it may be because it is one of the main resources, in Ukraine as in Russia, for internet users seeking to understand current events. The main article in Russian dedicated to the invasion has been viewed more than ten million times since its creation; on February 24, the day the invasion began, the twenty most read articles on the Russian-language version of the encyclopedia were related to the conflict and its protagonists – and eighteen for the English version, according to figures compiled by Pierre-Yves Beaudouin, administrator of the association Wikimedia France. "It can happen that a subject completely dominates readings in a country, but it is much rarer for it to occur so internationally and simultaneously," he explains to Le Monde. In the conflict, Wikipedia occupies a special place: there is a version of the encyclopedia in Ukrainian, but it remains less developed than its Russian counterpart; the vast majority of Ukrainians speak Russian, and many of them use the Russian-language version on a daily basis. Bombardments and mobilization have had direct consequences on the Ukrainian version, where the number of new contributions dropped drastically with the beginning of the conflict. The "Ukraine" page on the Ukrainian version of Wikipedia. "Wikipedia in Ukrainian is a small community, which also cannot rely on a very large and active diaspora, like Russia," analyzes Mr. Beaudouin. With the war, it quickly became overwhelmed, especially since, unlike the French version of the encyclopedia, in the Russian and Ukrainian versions, "modifications must be approved by a moderator before being published online." Ukrainian administrators have issued a call for help to cope; they see the encyclopedia as a means to document Ukrainian culture, which Vladimir Putin regularly denies the existence of. Local contributors have launched a "month of informational resistance to the Russian invasion," aiming to counter possible attempts at misinformation on Ukrainian Wikipedia. Fear of a blockage In the Russian version of the encyclopedia, and despite sometimes fierce debates, the main page dedicated to the Russian invasion is very similar, in substance, to those in French or English. While the official Russian viewpoint logically occupies more space there, one can find photos of civilian buildings destroyed by the Russian army, details of international condemnations, and Vladimir Putin's description of Ukraine as a "Nazi state" is labeled as a "false image." In the discussion page, where contributors debate the terms to use and the modifications to make, the debate over the name to give to the invasion was quickly settled, but very specific questions remain. Are articles from Voice of America, a media outlet funded by the U.S. State Department, reliable sources? How to continue communicating between contributors if Wikipedia is blocked in Russia in the future? And most importantly, how to debate constructively if no one can sign their messages anymore for fear of being arrested, and if one can no longer identify whether they are speaking to a newcomer in the discussion or to someone they were already debating with before? In France and the French-speaking world, discussions on the Russian invasion have remained relatively calm. Here too, fears of disinformation maneuvers may have made tempers flare, but none have been detected; debates have mainly focused on the status given to certain sources close to the Russian government. "The use of RT and Sputnik as sources was already discouraged, and the conflict has also led to discussions about other Russian sources accused of not respecting journalistic ethics," notes Jules, one of the French administrators of the encyclopedia. "But the debates are much less violent than what we have seen during the pandemic on pages dedicated to vaccination, for example." However, many contributors are concerned about the consequences of a potential block of the encyclopedia in Russia, even though for states, blocking Wikipedia often proves to be counterproductive. China is the only major country where the encyclopedia is completely inaccessible, and "the pages in Mandarin are mostly written by residents of Taiwan," notes Pierre-Yves Beaudouin. A side effect of the block that apparently bothers Beijing: last year, and after a lengthy investigation, a group of Chinese-speaking users responsible for a series of dishonest edits promoting China's positions was banned from the encyclopedia. Turkey, which censored access to the encyclopedia for three years, finally restored access in 2020.
lead: An important Belarusian contributor to the collaborative encyclopedia was arrested on March 11th.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : « L’Europe n’a pas vécu situation plus dangereuse depuis des décennies »
text: Article. The war has started in Ukraine. The choices of the United States and the European Union (EU) are rich in lessons and heavy with questions for the future. What is the purpose of Western unity if it does not prevent war? The fact that the war has started is first and foremost a failure of deterrence through sanctions. Recalling in various statements the principles of American action, President Joe Biden had drawn a red line: no direct military intervention. At the same time, he had outlined four priorities: choosing diplomacy and sanctions; redeploying American troops to the territories of the Baltic States, members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO); unity of the "Western" response or the international community through new sanctions aimed at "punishing and inflicting pain" on the Russian government; a desire to limit the negative consequences for American businesses and consumers (the House of Representatives elections will be held in eight months). The timing, often interpreted as slowness bordering on weakness, actually confirmed the American choice to let the Europeans, specifically the EU - and primarily Berlin with the Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline - lead the response to Vladimir Putin. A second package of sanctions is expected to follow, ready on the American side, but it could jeopardize transatlantic unity. Anti-interventionist sentiment American choices should not come as a surprise. Joe Biden is implementing what three successive presidents have constantly emphasized: the United States is no longer the world's policeman, Europe is a secondary theater in their priorities, and Europeans must take more responsibility for their security. The reactions of the American public and political leaders so far show a lack of rallying around the president and the strength of anti-interventionist or isolationist sentiment among Americans. The Russian bombings definitively bury the post-Cold War era as well as the European security architecture inherited from the Cold War and extended in the 1990s. The Ukrainian president bitterly recalled the appeasement policy of the French and British leaders towards Nazism in 1938, and its poor results... The real rupture on the American side was in Donald Trump's statements about a NATO declared "obsolete" and which he had come close to withdrawing the United States from in 2017. Putin had no reason to interrupt his American admirer at that time. With Trump gone, the Russian president is testing what remains of that legacy with a president weakened from within by accusations of illegitimacy made by his predecessor and now the dominant position of the Republican Party. The Russian head of state is also fueling the new divisions that Donald Trump and his "pro-Russian populist anti-system international" have exacerbated in public opinion, in the United States and elsewhere. Joe Biden has reiterated that the United States will uphold its commitment to its NATO allies: Will Vladimir Putin go as far as testing this? Europe has not faced a more dangerous situation in decades. American choices have shifted the responsibility to Europe, confirming to the latter that the United States is no longer the world's policeman, nor even Europe's, and that now is the time to become a geopolitical player. But what can a player do who does not want to fight, whose defense ambitions have not materialized in twenty years, and who is not even sure what economic consequences they are willing to bear, depending on the state of public opinion and their electoral deadlines? In Munich, just days before the start of Russian bombings in Ukrainian cities, the transatlantic community gathered for the grand event of the Security Conference. Munich, the symbol was strong. Amid celebrations of "renewed unity," Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky bitterly recalled the appeasement policy of the French and British leaders towards Nazism in 1938, and its poor results. A week later, Russian military operations proved him right. They also show, for the countries bordering Ukraine and Russia, that NATO is anything but obsolete in the absence of a credible European defense. American politicians, commentators, and media know how to highlight Joe Biden's domestic imperatives, and his press conference on February 22 illustrated them perfectly. They all forget to apply this to Vladimir Putin as well. Yet he too was addressing his population, prepared by his rewriting of history, invoking an imaginary "genocide" in progress, like former Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic who, in 1989, also claimed a risk of genocide against the Serbian population. Weakening of European cohesion But the second audience that Vladimir Putin was addressing, of course, was the international opinion: weakening internal cohesion and the resolve of European leaders has long been at the heart of the Russian strategy and fuels the "anti-system" vision that often aligns with Moscow's arguments. Debates in the United States and France, two countries in election campaigns, show that this strategy is working perfectly. Joe Biden's priority is to maintain a united "Western" (transatlantic) front, while one of Vladimir Putin's objectives is to divide it and dismantle the West. This is also what is currently at stake, under the watchful eye of Beijing. Does the West invoked by Joe Biden still exist? And above all, how is it defined, what does it defend, and at what cost? The internal divisions and contradictory visions of what defines and constitutes the West have so far focused on domestic, national issues, but their international consequences are now coming to light. Donald Trump, who has once again expressed support for Vladimir Putin, and Trumpism, which now defines the Republican Party and could return to power in Congress in a few months – and to the presidency in three years – defend an alternative vision of the West, which is also found in Europe, sometimes among its leadership. From the perspective of the United States, this conflict also crystallizes this new decisive fracture in foreign policy, which some characterize as pitting globalists against nationalists, but which also sheds light on the value placed on international cooperation and respect for peoples.
lead: The Russian bombings in Ukraine definitively bury the security architecture inherited from the Cold War, confirming to Europe that the United States are "no longer the world's policemen," analyzes historian Maya Kandel in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: Guerre en Ukraine : « Face au tyran enferré dans la logique du mal, la résistance ordinaire des héros de Vassili Grossman »
text:
Tribune. For now, voices of prominent witnesses are still reaching us from Ukraine, decrypting from within the tragedy unfolding on this land since the Russian invasion. How long will these voices be able to reach us? One thing is certain: even if exchanges were to stop tomorrow, there are Ukrainians who will be impossible to silence. Their voices cry out to us from beyond the grave, where the Stalinist lackeys, who so captivate Putin, thought they had buried them. This is the case with Vassili Grossman (1905-1964), one of the greatest Russian-language novelists of the Soviet era - through which he lived the horrors - who speaks today closest to our current reality, as the same infernal machine that distorts reality, twists language, and strives to destroy bodies and minds, operates in Russia and Ukraine, then and now. Let us recall this pivotal moment in Grossman's history. After years before the war, during which he turned a blind eye to the compromises demanded by the regime to be a recognized writer, the shock of truth came. It was the weeks spent covering the siege of Stalingrad as a war correspondent for the newspaper Red Star. It was entering the camps of Treblinka and Majdanek in Poland alongside the troops of the Red Army. It was the discovery of the murder of his mother and almost the entire Jewish population of the country by the Nazis. He could no longer ignore the gaping chasm between the "ruthless truth of war" and the lies of official propaganda. He had to acknowledge the dangerous truth: Nazism and Stalinism were in fact monstrous twin brothers. A "stopped" book The same land, saturated with the blood of Jews exterminated by the Nazi Einsatzgruppen, was where, a few years earlier, the great famine organized by Stalin (in 1932-1933) had reached a peak of horror, killing several million peasants whom the tyrant had first made pariahs to be eliminated. A reality destined for absolute censorship by the KGB. Any resemblance to our current reality is obviously not coincidental. However, Grossman broke the taboo. In his monumental novel Life and Fate, written in the late 1950s, as well as in his unfinished short story Everything Flows, he demonstrates the kinship of the two tyrannies. Naturally, the manuscript of Life and Fate was confiscated, the book "stopped" as one would do to a criminal, and condemned, according to Communist Party leader Mikhail Suslov (1902-1982), to obscurity for two hundred and fifty years. A prophecy that proved wrong, as in April 2007, a university symposium on Grossman's work was held at the Mohyla University in Kiev by the philosopher Constantin Sigov, a passionate European in the spirit of Maidan, a cultural mediator, who is now, for us, amidst the bombs in Kiev, the tireless analyst of the European tragedy unfolding in Ukraine. Here is the text translated into English:

Now, Grossman's words come back to us like a boomerang. Firstly, shedding harsh light on the imposture, which Putin claims as his justification by declaring his intention to "denazify" Ukraine, when in fact, as a loyal agent of Soviet-style totalitarianism, he is the one perpetuating the practices of pure Nazi barbarity, seizing a neighboring country like a bandit and leading it to ruin. The recent bombing near Babi Yar is a striking symbol: in this ravine on the outskirts of Kiev, where 33,771 Jewish men, women, and children were massacred on September 29 and 30, 1941 by the Nazis, the mad leader of the Kremlin now leaves his own mark of criminality. A signature of the perversion that led him to dissolve Memorial International in December 2021, the organization that worked to preserve the lost memory of millions of Gulag victims... as if one decided to erase Auschwitz from maps and history. But to invite Vassili Grossman to decipher this staggering moment is also to see another truth supported, the other side of what is currently happening in Europe. It is to train oneself to resist the evil's blackmail, to refute the aggressor of Ukraine's claim that the force of tanks, coupled with even worse weapons, is irresistible. The master of the Kremlin only knows this truth. "The history of man is the struggle of evil seeking to crush the tiny seed of humanity," writes Grossman in "Life and Fate." Grossman's work stubbornly contradicts this by being, at the heart of the furnaces of evil, a plea for humanity. By recounting, throughout his pages, alongside horror, the tiny gestures of daily life in which he embodies what he calls "small kindness." This kindness is against the appearances of weakness and insignificance. It is completely inaccessible to the myopic eyes of the tyrant trapped in the logic of evil, who enslaves his public opinion to lies, and who may soon brandish a miserable victory made of devastation and unhappiness, as already seen during the wars in Chechnya and Syria. Yet, the ordinary resistance of this small kindness, like the defense of truth at the cost of life, is what consumes, in our deep history, the defeat of bloodthirsty men. In this situation, let us listen again to Vassili Grossman. His words are golden when they form on the lips of Ikonnikov, the madman in Christ of "Life and Fate": "I saw that it was not man who was powerless in his struggle against evil, I saw that it was evil that was powerless in its struggle against man. The secret of the immortality of kindness lies in its powerlessness. It is invincible." And again: "The history of men is not the struggle of good seeking to overcome evil. The history of man is the struggle of evil seeking to crush the tiny seed of humanity." "But if even now humanity has not been killed in man, then evil will never prevail" (Grossman, Life and Fate, Works, Robert Laffont, 2006, page 346). A truth to be recognized in the current events of these days. Our small, but essential, contribution to the fight for dignity and truth, led on their land by Grossman's heirs.
lead: The work of the great Russian novelist from the Soviet era is a "plea for humanity," which steadfastly contradicts the truth of Vladimir Putin and his tanks, analyzes the academic and writer Anne-Marie Pelletier in an opinion piece for "Le Monde."

title: « Kombinat », ou la poésie des hauts fourneaux de l’Oural
text:
THE "MONDE" REVIEW - A MUST-SEE
Smoke as far as the eye can see. Blast furnaces and factory chimneys as urban landmarks. Working-class pride on full display. Kombinat, it's the Magnitogorski Metallurgical Combine, known as "MMK" for short, the gigantic metallurgical factory in Magnitogorsk, a single-industry city in the Urals. "MMK" is everywhere, even on signs at every street corner urging passersby to "be in a good mood." A hint of Sovietism? Undoubtedly, for better or for worse. And it is precisely this chiaroscuro that gives strength to Gabriel Tejedor's film, Kombinat, presented in November 2020 at the International Jean Rouch Festival. The director, who continues his exploration of the remnants of the Soviet empire started years ago (Rue Mayskaya, La Trace), filmed over several seasons the life of the kombinat and some of its workers - metalworkers who speak candidly about their joys and anxieties. One must be sensitive to the poetry of the blast furnaces and the pastel-colored trams to truly appreciate this very contemplative film. Or one must want to feel the nostalgia for a working-class world that has largely disappeared in our own society... Life in Magnitogorsk is neither good nor bad. It is Russian life in its greatest banality. Children play ice hockey at the foot of the towers; in the summer, they are taken for walks by the lake, attend patriotic concerts, and share kebabs in the gentle evening. Ecology: a lurking anxiety. Work is also a big part of life. Like the metalworker who comes home tired, slurps his soup, and regains his strength by quoting an old Soviet slogan: "Go to work with joy, come back with pride." Then he heads off to his Latin dance class, his face lighting up with life. There is, of course, pride in extracting tons of hot steel from the bowels of the factory every day, but we understand that Sasha is primarily excited for his end-of-year show... This unexpected plot is not the only clash that gives flavor to the film and outlines the reality of modern Magnitogorsk. Take, for example, the celebration organized by the factory in honor of the metalworkers. A child in a pioneer shirt proclaims his determination to follow in his fathers' footsteps, while under the screen, a dancer performs a breakdance routine. A lurking - and new - anxiety runs through the city: ecology. Magnitogorsk, shrouded in smoke, is one of the most polluted cities in Russia. "We want to live a long life, it's not possible here," says a couple who want to move to Novosibirsk. Their concern is mainly for their daughter, who suffers from developmental delays. The doctors say, "Yes, that's how it is here..." Moscow and Putin are far from the Urals, political discussions are rare. Choosing a path other than the factory is already setting oneself apart. This is the case for Sergei, who openly questions the military fervor and respect for the "strength" of Vladimir Putin. "It's Putin who put filters on the chimneys of our factories," he is told! "Yes, but they are broken!" Like a metaphor for what Russia has become.
lead: Swiss director Gabriel Tejedor continues his exploration of the remnants of the Soviet empire in Magnitogorsk, a city entirely dedicated to steel production and one of the most polluted in Russia.

title: Crise en Ukraine : des bombardements dans le Donbass ravivent les tensions et les inquiétudes sur une possible action militaire russe
text: The Donbass region is the scene of new tensions. Bombardments were raging near Stanytsia Louhanska, a city in eastern Ukraine under government control, on Friday, February 18, as witnessed by journalists from Agence France-Presse. The Ukrainian army and pro-Russian separatists are accusing each other of using heavy weapons, a resurgence of violence that fuels fears of a Russian invasion. The Secretary General of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), Jens Stoltenberg, stated on Friday that the Russian military presence at the Ukrainian border represents "the largest concentration of military forces" since the Cold War. He said to the German television channel ZDF, "This is much more than just maneuvers, and Russia is certainly capable, without further warning, of attacking Ukraine." A U.S. official estimated that Russia has 190,000 troops near Ukraine's borders and on its territory, including pro-Russian separatist forces. Previously, 150,000 soldiers were mentioned at the country's borders. Evacuation of civilians from pro-Russian territories In Stanytsia Louhanska, the sound of shells could be heard as the town had already been hit the day before by bombardments that damaged a kindergarten. According to the Ukrainian army, thirty-two shells fell on Stanytsia Louhanska, partially cutting off its electricity. Ukrainian forces have been facing pro-Russian separatists in the east of the country for eight years. Buses were chartered to evacuate residents from the city of Donetsk, held by pro-Russian separatists in Ukraine, on February 18, 2022. ALEXANDER ERMOCHENKO / REUTERS Both sides blamed each other for the clashes: Ukrainian authorities reported twenty ceasefire violations by separatists overnight, while pro-Russian rebels reported twenty-seven shots fired by the Ukrainian army. On Friday afternoon, the leader of the "Donetsk People's Republic," held by pro-Russian separatists and at war with Ukraine, called for the evacuation of civilians to neighboring Russia, accusing Kiev of preparing an invasion after a surge in clashes. The leader of the "Luhansk People's Republic" did the same, urging "all able-bodied men to defend their homeland." The President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin, "ordered [his Minister of Emergency Situations] to urgently go to the Rostov region to organize (...) reception conditions, provide hot meals and everything necessary, including medical care," said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov. Moscow held responsible for recent cyberattacks Washington, on the other hand, sees this as a "cynical" move by Moscow. "It is cynical and cruel to use human beings as pawns to divert attention from the fact that Russia is strengthening its troops in preparation for an attack," a spokesperson for the U.S. State Department said in Munich. The United States and the United Kingdom also accused Moscow on Friday of being "responsible" for the recent cyberattacks that targeted several official Ukrainian military websites and two public banks on Tuesday, attacks for which the Kremlin had denied any responsibility. Washington issued multiple threats on Friday, promising that international sanctions in case of a Ukrainian invasion would make Russia an "outcast." "It would become an outcast to the international community. It will be isolated from global financial markets and deprived of the most sophisticated technological inputs," said Daleep Singh, Deputy National Security Advisor for International Economics at the White House, before President Joe Biden spoke at 10 p.m. Paris time. Furthermore, Washington stated it is "prepared" in the event that Russia uses energy resources as a pressure lever, asserting that the Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline would not become operational if Moscow were to attack Ukraine. "We have taken steps (...) to ensure we have continuous energy supplies and stable energy markets," Mr. Singh supported. However, these threats had already been dismissed by Vladimir Putin on Friday. "Sanctions will be introduced no matter what. Whether there is a reason or not, they will find one, because their goal is to hinder Russia's development," he stated. Washington views them as "false provocations." The United States now fears an attack on Ukraine "in the coming days," while Russia continues to dismiss these accusations. The Russian president mentioned on Friday an "aggravation of the situation" in eastern Ukraine, while U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken denounced "a scenario already in place, which involves creating false provocations, then responding to these provocations, and finally committing a new aggression against Ukraine." French President Emmanuel Macron called for a "cessation" of military actions, deeming the situation on the ground "very concerning." France and Germany urged Russia on Friday to "use its influence" on the separatists in eastern Ukraine "to call for restraint and contribute to de-escalation." Meanwhile, Vladimir Putin met with his Belarusian ally, Alexander Lukashenko, where extensive joint military exercises are ongoing. "All Kiev has to do is sit down at the negotiating table with the representatives [the separatists] of Donbass and agree on political, military, and humanitarian measures to end the conflict," said Mr. Putin after the meeting. On Saturday, the Kremlin leader is set to oversee maneuvers of his "strategic forces," including ballistic and cruise missile tests. Deployment of American troops in Poland Russia claimed on Friday to be making new withdrawals from the Ukrainian border, information that Kiev and Western countries are skeptical about. "This is not happening," said Ukrainian Defense Minister Oleksii Reznikov on Friday in front of lawmakers, stating that Russia has massed 149,000 soldiers at the Ukrainian border. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz agreed, mentioning a "dramatic reality" and calling for the situation to be taken "very seriously." "We are witnessing a massive increase in Russia's military capabilities at the Ukrainian border, maneuvers in Belarus, at sea (...)," he said, reiterating the need for a "dual approach" of preparing sanctions in case of Russian military aggression while trying to find a "diplomatic solution" to the crisis. In response to the reinforcement of Russian troops around Ukraine, the United States is deploying around 4,700 additional soldiers to Poland. These reinforcements bring the American military presence in the country to about 10,000 troops. The American minister also announced the agreement of the U.S. State and Defense Departments for the sale of 250 American Abrams combat tanks to Poland. Poland had announced this transaction last year, but no details had been provided about this purchase yet.
lead: According to the Ukrainian army, shells have fallen on the town of Stanytsia Luhanska. Kiev and the separatists are blaming each other for the clashes, while the United States and the United Kingdom accuse Moscow of seeking a pretext to attack.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : jour 3, les images de la bataille pour Kiev
text: "We will not lay down our arms," assured the Ukrainian president in a video posted on Twitter on Saturday, February 26th. As Russian soldiers have been attempting to besiege Kiev since the beginning of the invasion on Thursday, February 24th, Volodymyr Zelensky called on the population to resist the assaults. "We cannot abandon our capital," he stated. The night of February 25th to 26th was marked by numerous clashes in the city, where the Russians were trying to enter from different points. While part of the population sought refuge in the subway corridors to protect themselves from the bombings, a residential building of about twenty floors was targeted by a missile strike. This "cynical" attack was denounced by the spokesperson of the Ukrainian emergency services.
lead: The Ukrainian capital was subjected to repeated attacks on Saturday, February 26th. Although the Russian Ministry of Defense claimed that the army was only targeting military infrastructure, a residential building was hit by a bombing in the morning.

title: A Paris, dans la manifestation de solidarité avec l’Ukraine : « C’est démentiel d’imaginer vivre une guerre en Europe »
text: "Glory to Ukraine! Glory to the heroes! Glory to the nation! Death to the enemies!" chant hundreds of people gathered on the evening of Thursday, February 24, at Place de la République in Paris. Most of them are Ukrainians who have come to denounce the war declared on them by Russia in the night from Wednesday to Thursday. Earlier in the day, other Ukrainians had already gathered in front of the Russian embassy in the 16th arrondissement. They shouted "Putin, terrorist!" or "Putin, assassin!" while waving blue and yellow flags. According to the Ministry of the Interior, just under 19,000 Ukrainians hold a residence permit in France. Some have been living here for a few months or years, while others have acquired French nationality. They all stay in touch with their families and loved ones back home. Olena Bondar, 30, shows text messages she exchanged with her parents that very morning. Living in Kyiv, they report hearing bombings. "Our children do not want to die," insists Svitlana Stanislavska. Wrapped in her puffer jacket, this building caretaker who has been living in Paris for over twenty years holds a sign that reads "Putin = Hitler." Her son, daughter, and grandchildren live in Ternopil, in western Ukraine. "I cry for my grandchildren. I don't want their fathers to go to war," she repeats, tears in her eyes. "Putin wants to erase a nation" They are all stunned by what is happening and denounce the "madness" of Russian leader Vladimir Putin. "Putin, like Hitler, wants to erase a nation," rages Khrystyna, a 26-year-old Ukrainian who is protesting with her 2-year-old child and her 25-year-old sister. "Since 2014, he has been taking a piece of the country and then another," also says Janna Sobolevska, a 55-year-old security agent. "It's a war that is not recognized, but it has been going on for eight years already," remarks this Ukrainian originally from Kyiv, referring to the annexation of Crimea by Russia in March 2014 and the recognition of the autonomy of the Donetsk and Luhansk regions in Ukrainian Donbass. "I'm not saying it's World War III, but this war bears a strong resemblance to the aggression against Poland in 1939 that triggered World War II," adds Michal Kurela, a Polish engineer who came to show solidarity with the Ukrainians. Some Russians have also taken action, like André, who has been in France for twenty years and feels "ashamed" of the Kremlin. Or Ekaterina Breslava, a 43-year-old lawyer, who wants to show that "not all Russians are on Putin's side." Olga Escoffier, 49, who is also French-Russian, has joined the pro-Ukrainian gatherings. "It's the first time in my life that I protest," she confides. "It was impossible for me to stay at home this morning." "French and Russians came to support at Place de la République, and French people also came to show their support. Like these two colleagues, Margaux and Eglantine, aged 23 and 26, who were touched by a war happening 'in Europe'. The last time they had participated in a gathering was, for one, after the November 13, 2015 attacks, and for the other, after the Orlando (Florida) attack in 2016, which took place in an LGBT nightclub. Farida, 67, finds it 'unbelievable to imagine living through a war in Europe'. 'It's like a reminder of past horrors,' worries this Parisian. 'People don't realize that it could continue beyond Ukraine.' 'There's no reason for Putin to stop if he succeeds in his plan,' also warns Michal Kurela. Olivier Faure, Raphaël Glucksmann, Yannick Jadot, and Christiane Taubira at the rally in support of Ukraine at Place de la République in Paris on February 24, 2022. Laurence Geai for 'Le Monde'. Personalities parade on a stage. Among them is the Ukrainian ambassador to France, Oleh Shamshur, who laments that 'in the heart of Europe, in the 21st century, bombs are falling on our beautiful country.' Also speaking are the Mayor of Paris and presidential candidate, Anne Hidalgo (Socialist Party), her competitors Yannick Jadot (Europe Ecology-The Greens) and Christiane Taubira, and the European Parliament member Raphaël Glucksmann. 'For too many years, there has been complacency,' says Mr. Jadot, who calls for 'exceptional sanctions' and the 'seizure of Russian oligarchs' assets.' 'Nobody cares about sanctions,' dismisses Liliya Gryshuk, 29. 'We need weapons,' adds her friend Janna Sobolevska, who claims that the country's armories are already empty, while Liliya reports that in the countryside, people are grabbing their tools to go to war. 'Ukrainians are very patriotic,' she recalls, as the national anthem plays several times. 'I offered my family to join me in Paris, but my son, grandson, brother, and nephews all tell me they are staying there,' Janna points out. Vasyl Danyshchuk, a 55-year-old Ukrainian, a team leader in construction in France, is in contact with his son. 'He lives in Ivano-Frankivsk, in the east of the country, 2 kilometers from the city's airport that was bombed this morning,' he reports. 'He took his wife and children to another region, but he came back.' On social media, Ukrainians in France are looking for ways to return to Ukraine to provide assistance. 'I will join the armed forces,' promises Dmytro Kalinichenko, a 23-year-old Ukrainian student. Go fight? 'There's nothing else to do.'"
lead: Hundreds of Ukrainians gathered on Thursday to denounce the "madness" of Vladimir Putin and show solidarity with their fellow countrymen.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : comment Kiev remporte la bataille de la communication
text: Facing a militarily superior enemy, Ukraine has at least managed to win an initial battle: the battle of the narrative. Since the early days of the Russian invasion, and partly thanks to Moscow's silence, Kyiv has succeeded in generating worldwide sympathy for the Ukrainian resistance to the aggression of its larger neighbor. By constantly speaking out through frequent videos with an epic and personal tone, President Volodymyr Zelensky has already carved out a legend and a new heroic figure for himself. However, not everything rests on the stature that circumstances have allowed him to adopt. A considerable effort has also been made by the Ukrainian forces to portray this war as the result of the destructive will to power of one man, Vladimir Putin, even if it means using the voices and bodies of prisoners of war for this purpose. Countless videos have been posted online by Ukrainian soldiers and volunteers since the beginning of the conflict, showing soldiers with sometimes youthful faces stating their age, name, and region of origin. They often appear disoriented, explaining that they were lied to and unaware they would be fighting in Ukraine. More strikingly, the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense invited the mothers of captured Russian soldiers to come and collect them in the country. This initiative came after the launch of the website and Telegram channel 200rf.com, which includes photographs of passports and military documents purportedly belonging to soldiers killed by Ukrainian forces, as well as videos featuring prisoners of war. Here too, the aim is to contrast transparency and humanity with a Russian power that only began to acknowledge its losses on Wednesday, March 2. War silenced in Russia The Ukrainian narrative of the ongoing war, delivered in real-time, is not solely the result of a carefully planned strategy within state institutions fighting for survival. It mainly stems from a myriad of anonymous individuals filming and constantly broadcasting their daily acts of resistance through a functioning telephone network. The Ukrainian people showcase their efforts, their collections of empty bottles intended for Molotov cocktails, and ordinary people who have only their words and courage to throw at the faces of the booted and helmeted occupiers. On Wednesday, March 2, while Kharkiv was being bombarded, Ukrainians were able to film hundreds of unarmed people blocking a road leading to the nuclear power plant in the city of Enerhodar to prevent Russian forces from accessing it. Faced with this repeated popular movement captured in images and videos, Moscow offers no counter-narrative, even going as far as denying reality rather than showing it in a favorable light. Officially, in Russia, the war is being kept silent. This horizontal transfer of images and narratives of the ongoing war goes even further. It encourages the production and sharing by internet users worldwide of a popular iconography that has emerged very rapidly since the beginning of the conflict. On Tuesday, Alexis Rapin, a researcher at the University of Quebec in Montreal, pointed out on his Twitter account the emergence of a new "mythology" related to the conflict. Among others, there is Saint Javelin, a representation of Mary Magdalene reminiscent of the aesthetics of Orthodox icons, holding an American anti-tank weapon Javelin in her delicate hands, which has been used very effectively by Ukrainian forces against the Russian invaders. Similarly, the first, unverifiable legends of the war in Ukraine are appropriated by Twitter and Instagram users, such as the "Ghost of Kyiv," a Ukrainian aviation ace who allegedly shot down five enemy aircraft over the capital on the first day of the conflict. Serving as vectors of invisible but global solidarity and support, these icons hold a significant place in people's imaginations, in the background of events that speak for themselves. This is how, since the beginning of the war, Ukraine has been telling its story through images, and the rest of the world has made it their own.
lead: The videos of Ukrainians resisting against the might of the Russian army are going viral around the world.

title: « La Russie n’est pas un pays, ni même un empire, mais une emprise »
text: Tribune. "The Russian soul" is the awareness that everything we think of as solid or somewhat secure in our existence can be erased in an instant, by the arbitrary emergence of violence. Russia is like a storm or a mudslide: it reminds you of the absurdity of your life. This Russian horror has inspired excellent novels and bloody pages of history. Today, this same Russian horror is leading Vladimir Putin to invade Ukraine and threaten the world with a nuclear response. It is important to remember the centuries-old contract between the Russian population and its leaders: "scare us and scare the world." This political pact, their own kind of "Social Contract," was born during the sack of Novgorod, under the reign of Ivan the Terrible, in 1570, and it is still valid. At that time, a certain intellectual and liberal elite had formed in the city of Novgorod. The tsar interpreted this awakening as a challenge to his power and turned the city into a bloodbath. Five centuries later, strength and law, respect and fear are still intertwined concepts in Russia. It is possible that this confusion is the only thing truly uniting the inhabitants of the Kuril Islands with those of Pskov, those of Murmansk with those of the Caucasus, allowing them to be a nation. Russians are not citizens but subjects ruled by fear like a lid. Perhaps because Russia is not strictly a country, nor even an empire spanning a tenth of the world's land, but a grip. Those Russians who are aware of this, among those who do not live abroad, exist in an "internal exile": unable to see their country for what it is, they blindfold themselves. These "internal exiles" live in Moscow or St. Petersburg, rarely in other cities. They never talk about politics because inevitably the conversation will end with a melancholic sigh and a shameful sense of powerlessness. Russian politics has become, over time, impolite. You do not speak of it to avoid discomfort. It is the exclusive domain of a few brutes. Weak support for dissidents Russian dissident artists like the group Voyna ("war" in Russian) or Pussy Riot (English for "riot of the vagina"), who in the past decade have tried to awaken this apathy or false virtue, have received only lukewarm support. They were criticized for their intensity or aesthetics. It was as if, once again, the problem had to be sidestepped.
