In 2010, Voyna painted a 65-meter-high fluorescent phallus on a drawbridge in front of the headquarters of the FSB (the domestic intelligence service) in the heart of St. Petersburg. This performance asserted that the vitality of the Russian people was greater than the repression oppressing it. Its echo was immense but it was ultimately judged as inelegant or simply comical, and it was defused. Once again, fear prevailed. The grip held. To what extent is this disengagement of the Russians in the management of their country complicity? Do the Russians really disapprove of Putin's foreign policy and the terror it inspires? The annexation of Crimea [in 2014] had garnered some support among the population, but this was less due to its legitimacy than to the inaction of other countries. Vladimir Putin was providing Russians with proof that Russia still frightened the world. He was fulfilling his role. Acknowledging the lamentable state of their country and the corruption of its leaders seems like too insurmountable an effort for the Russians. Therefore, annexing or bombing their neighbors to, in a more or less explicit corner of their brain, reassure themselves that their country still counts for something, serves as a stopgap: Russia is still a superpower. Too bad if the life expectancy of Russians resembles that of North Koreans. In the absence of a dignified life, they possess hypersonic missiles and over 6,000 nuclear warheads. Above all, they are more than willing to use them. Why would they hold back? They have much less to lose than their enemies. An enlightening joke circulates during the May 9 parades, when the Soviet victory over Nazi Germany is celebrated with grandeur every year: "Just look at how the losers live, compared to the winners..." There must be a biological law that tends to establish that an unhealthy body, when it has reached the end of its incubation, exports its disease. A cancerous body matures its illness and then distributes its metastases to the rest of the body, like the TOS-1 multiple rocket launchers currently bombarding Ukraine and which the Russians have given a pretty girl's name: Katyusha. A very beautiful song celebrates the beauty and strength of Katyusha. Everyone knows its haunting melody. Few peoples have sung so well about their love for multiple rocket launchers. Plutocratic regime With their heroic resistance, at the cost of their lives, the Ukrainians are currently demonstrating that the Russian army - that is to say, the very concept of Russia, the ability to inspire fear - no longer scares them. This war could precipitate the Putin regime into the abyss because it is clear that, in addition to its military setbacks and the foreseeable entrenchment of the conflict, this regime of plutocrats will have a hard time concealing this truth that no Katyusha can erase: if it still scares the Russians, if it fascinates the majority of Russians, Russia no longer scares the Ukrainians. With hammers and shotguns, they are taking down tanks and helicopters. They have overcome fear. They assert their desire to break free from its deadly grip. What if the Russians followed their example? And if, for the second time in history, Ukraine allowed Russia to (re)emerge? If, after the princes of Kiev, the fierce Ukrainian resistance offered a future to a Russia that seems to lack one? If the battles taking place in Ukraine allowed for a revolution to take place? Twelve years after the symbolic war of Voyna, will a real war awaken the Russian people? Several times, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has addressed the Russians, in Russian, to call for such an uprising. It would be wrong to see his appeals as mere maneuvers. Vladimir Putin has stopped being frightening. He has therefore broken the sacred Russian political pact and the condition for the existence of his tyranny. Failing to be an effective scarecrow, he will lose his throne, like Nicholas II [Emperor of Russia from 1894 to 1917], Khrushchev [leader of the USSR from 1953 to 1964], and Gorbachev [leader of the USSR from 1985 to 1991] before him, and for the same reasons. The big unknown in this conflict is not when Kiev, Kharkiv, or the wonderful Odessa will fall into the hands of the Russian army, but how soon the Russians will take back control of their country and stop exporting the fear that pierces their hearts.
lead: In a column in "Le Monde," writer Arthur Larrue asserts that Russians are not citizens but "subjects who are overshadowed by fear like a lid." He wonders: could the fighting in Ukraine pave the way for a "revolution" in Russia?

title: Guerre en Ukraine : « Est-ce si difficile de dire les choses comme elles sont ? Un dictateur, en mal de reconstitution d’un empire perdu, agresse un pays indépendant »
text: Opinion piece. "It's NATO's fault." The Euro-Atlantic military pact is said to be responsible for the bombings in Ukraine! "The guilty West," as always, chant Vladimir Putin's supporters. They are quick to condemn the bombing of Kiev – unpleasant, indeed – but in the same breath, they denounce the "project of annexing Ukraine" by the malevolent entity NATO - the United States. This is an implicit way of partially excusing the crime committed by Russia against one of its neighbors. Is it so difficult to say things as they are? A dictator, in need of rebuilding a lost empire, the collapsed USSR under its own contradictions, is attacking an independent country. Objective: to break it to subdue it; to terrorize it to subject it again, "as before." Putin, the orchestrator of the assault on Ukraine, belongs to that generation of Russians from the security services, writes the political scientist Ivan Krastev in The New York Times, who have never come to terms with the end of the USSR: "They are not interested in building the future, they want to redo the past." What traumatizes Putin about today's Ukraine is not the distant prospect of its possible membership in NATO, but the country's desire to join "the West" – the democratic and liberal world. The traitor must be brought back "to the east," to the Russian home to which it belongs. Vladimir Putin has written: there is no Ukrainian people. Why see only a figure of speech in the autocrats' words? Missed opportunities Russia has legitimate security interests. Americans and Europeans have opened all possible forums for discussion. The Russian president is not interested. He is carrying out a historic mission to, in his words, "denazify" and "demilitarize" Ukraine. In this madness, what role does NATO play, which, while not ruling out that this country may one day become a member, has never initiated an accession process for it? The years of American hyperpower, those that followed the implosion of the USSR, did not lack missed opportunities to imagine an intelligent security architecture in Europe. This was not a matter for academic debate: this history was written in chaos. From the United States and its allies, there were arrogant and humiliating gestures towards a Russia traumatized by the loss of an empire without which it clearly struggles to live. Those years had their less than glorious moments – from the bombing of Belgrade to the aggression against Iraq. But on the Russian side, a country that has never hesitated to humiliate the many peoples it has subjugated, foresight and wisdom have rarely been present. The first major security question was the dissemination of the thousands of nuclear warheads owned by the USSR and spread across four of its republics: Russia, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, and Belarus. President George H. W. Bush agreed with Mikhail Gorbachev: these weapons must all be handed over to Russia. This was done, with Washington funding the dismantling of these weapons before their transport. Ukraine, which gained its independence in 1991, held the world's third-largest nuclear arsenal. In exchange for giving up its nuclear weapons, Ukraine received massive financial aid from the United States and a guarantee from Moscow of its independence and respect for its borders... Should reunified Germany, in 1990, remain in NATO? Gorbachev is against it: "it would recreate a line of confrontation" in Europe. Chancellor Helmut Kohl and Bush are in favor and pressure the Russians. As a "counterpart," it was decided that no NATO troops or armaments would be permanently deployed in the former East Germany. To this day, this commitment has been upheld. In the negotiations, the statement by U.S. Secretary of State James Baker about not extending the Atlantic pact "to the east" seems to only concern Germany. For a good reason: the USSR and the Soviet bloc still existed. Hurt pride The Warsaw Pact, which bound the countries of Central and Eastern Europe to the USSR after the Second World War, dissolved in 1991. A few months later, it would be the turn of the Soviet Union. In Bill Clinton's administration, which succeeded George H. W. Bush, there was a debate: should the request of these countries to join NATO be accepted? Because the initiative came from Hungary, Poland, and the Czech Republic, not from Washington. Experts and ministers in Clinton's administration were divided. George Kennan, the father of the doctrine of containing the USSR during the Cold War, was firmly against it. It would be a provocative gesture towards Moscow, he said. Eager to spare the hurt pride of the orphans of the Soviet empire, Clinton hesitated, proposed a vague formula, the Partnership for Peace, before giving in to Presidents Vaclav Havel of the Czech Republic, Lech Walesa of Poland, and Arpad Göncz of Hungary, all of whom came to plead with him in Washington. They were wary of the future, they told him, they wanted NATO. Accepted in 1994, the accession of these three countries took place in 1997 – later followed by the Baltic states. However, the organization did not deploy troops to the new members until 2014 and for a specific purpose: to respond to Putin's annexation of Crimea. He is focused on reconstituting Moscow's influence over its near abroad. Not out of fear of NATO, but in a mix of complex feelings, including an unchecked desire for power and a will for revenge on history.
lead: What traumatizes Putin in today's Ukraine is not the distant prospect of its possible membership in NATO, but rather the desire of this country to join "the West."

title: Guerre en Ukraine : nouvelles sanctions occidentales après une première journée d’offensive
text: The Russian invasion has sparked a wave of condemnations from the United States, the European Union, the United Kingdom, and Canada. Deadly fighting between Russians and Ukrainians was taking place on Thursday, February 24, up to the gates of Kiev. The Chernobyl nuclear power plant was taken over following a massive air and ground attack by Vladimir Putin's army against Ukraine, to which the United States and the European Union responded with new sanctions. "This is a premeditated attack," said President Joe Biden in a press conference at the White House, adding that his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin, had rejected all Western initiatives for dialogue. The American president stated that Russian-American relations are now completely severed. Joe Biden aims to make Vladimir Putin "a pariah." Mr. Biden announced a series of new economic sanctions targeting Russian banks, elites, and exports, which he believes will make Vladimir Putin "a pariah on the international stage." The president assured that these sanctions would impose a "severe cost on the Russian economy, both immediately and in the long term." He also announced that four additional Russian banks would be sanctioned and more than half of Russia's technological imports would be cut off. "We are also adding names to the list of sanctioned Russian [elites] and their family members," stated the White House tenant. Personally sanctioning Vladimir Putin is also "on the table," Joe Biden further announced, without providing more details. Cutting Russia off from the Swift interbank network, a crucial component of the global financial system, is also "an option," according to the American president. This request was made earlier in the day by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. However, he emphasized that "currently, this was not [a] position" shared by the Europeans, and stated that the other financial sanctions announced on Thursday by the United States and its allies had "as much impact, if not more impact" than this option. Joe Biden once again reiterated that the United States was not acting alone on this issue. "We will limit Russia's ability to do business in dollars, euros, pounds, and yen," he warned. Joe Biden mentioned that NATO countries would meet on Friday to discuss further measures. The Europeans approve "massive" sanctions against Moscow. The Twenty-Seven approved on Thursday a new set of sanctions against Russia, targeting the finance, energy, and transportation sectors, which will have "massive consequences," according to their statement. Approved by European leaders at the beginning of their summit in Brussels, "in close coordination with the partners and allies" of the European Union (EU), these measures will also cover dual-use goods (both civil and military) and visa issuance, the text specifies, without detailing the sanctions. The President of the Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, detailed the measures, on Thursday night to Friday, following a European Council meeting, with four aspects: financial, energy, air, and technological: Cut off access to major capital markets: "We are now targeting 70% of the Russian banking market, but also key state-owned enterprises, particularly in the defense sector." Ban on Russian oil exports. Ban on the sale of all aircraft, spare parts, and equipment to Russian airlines. Limited access to crucial technologies "such as semiconductors or advanced technologies." Emmanuel Macron announced that French sanctions will accompany European sanctions, targeting "personal assets and individuals." London bans Aeroflot and sanctions Russian banks and businessmen. The United Kingdom, for its part, imposed a new series of sanctions against Russia on Thursday, banning the airline Aeroflot and targeting the banking sector, technology exports, and five businessmen. "Putin (...) will never be able to wash his hands of the blood of Ukraine," said Prime Minister Boris Johnson, announcing "the most significant and severe package of economic sanctions Russia has ever seen." The day before, the first package of British sanctions against Russia had only received criticism: it was considered too mild, targeting only second-tier banks and only three oligarchs - out of dozens who had chosen London as the most welcoming capital to hide their ill-gotten fortunes. Canada sanctions 58 Russian individuals and entities. Canada will sanction "58 individuals and entities" from Russia in response to the Russian army's invasion of Ukraine, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced on Thursday, citing "severe sanctions." The sanctions will target "members of the Russian elite," "major Russian banks," and "members of the Russian Security Council," among others, the Prime Minister specified. "We are halting all export permits to Russia," added Justin Trudeau, stating that the Russian invasion of Ukraine represented a "huge threat to security and peace in the world." Russia does not want to be excluded from the global economic system. Russia does not want to harm the global economic system or be excluded from it, Vladimir Putin said on Thursday, as his invasion of Ukraine has rattled markets and exposed Moscow to devastating sanctions. "Russia continues to participate in the global economy, we are not preparing to harm it," said Vladimir Putin in front of Russian business representatives gathered in the Kremlin. "It seems to me that our partners should understand and not aim to push us out of the system."
lead: The United States, the European Union, the United Kingdom, and Canada have announced several economic sanctions aimed at making Vladimir Putin "a pariah on the international stage."

title: Crise en Ukraine : Pécresse ferme contre la Russie, Le Pen et Zemmour dénoncent la volonté d’expansion de l’OTAN
text: As the crisis between Ukraine and Russia escalated on Saturday, February 19, with several ceasefire violations at the border between the two countries and an acceleration of diplomatic consultations among Western nations, it also entered the French presidential campaign. For the LR candidate, Valérie Pécresse, a "steel-like firmness" is needed in dealing with Russia's Vladimir Putin. "If acts of aggression towards Ukraine continue, drastic sanctions will be necessary. Dialogue is needed, but also a show of strength," she continued on Saturday morning on France Culture. "Waging war on Ukraine is a major mistake, breaking the Minsk agreements is a fault," added the presidential candidate, referring to the 2015 agreements aimed at pacifying the separatist East of Ukraine. But "if Russia withdraws, everything becomes possible," she estimated, including the prospect of a security conference in Europe and the message to Ukraine that its NATO membership, a red line for Moscow, "is not on the agenda." "Diplomacy is not done at the last minute," criticized the candidate of the National Rally, Marine Le Pen, on France Inter. "It requires having long-standing and well-developed relationships with a head of state." She regrets that Emmanuel Macron "flip-flopped on the relations he claimed to want with Vladimir Putin. He thought it would be more useful to align with the European Union or NATO." Le Pen fears that the confrontation between Russia and Ukraine could "go very wrong without either party wishing it, with an event that could tip the situation." For her, the two countries can reach an agreement if several conditions are met: "acknowledging that Ukraine will not join NATO"; "Russia definitively leaving the Donbass region"; "implementing the Minsk agreements"; "recognizing the integration of Crimea into Russia"; and finally, "keeping Ukraine a neutral state internationally." Exchange between Macron and Putin According to Eric Zemmour, "the original fault" of this conflict is "NATO's expansion eastward over the past thirty years," he said Sunday morning on CNews and Europe 1. If elected, the Reconquest! candidate would "do everything to ensure that the Minsk agreements are respected and I will have a treaty signed where Ukraine and NATO must commit to never allowing Ukraine to join" the military institution. For him, this expansion of NATO - an institution he deems "obsolete" and wants to exit from the integrated command - towards Eastern countries after the fall of the USSR is "legitimately seen as aggression by Russia." On the left, the candidate of La France insoumise, Jean-Luc Mélenchon, reminded on France 3 that for him "the French must be non-aligned. That's how we become a credible interlocutor." According to him, "the Russians should not cross the border with Ukraine and the Americans should not seek to annex Ukraine to NATO." Christiane Taubira, on the other hand, praises the action of Emmanuel Macron who is "right to try to pursue discussions to the end, even if they are likely to be unpleasant with the Russian president." Interviewed on BFM-TV at midday on Sunday, the former Minister of Justice regrets, however, "our diplomatic and anticipatory incapacity, our passivity, and a tendency to take Mr. Putin's statements in good faith." "How do we manage international risks? It is managed upstream, the situation between Russia and Ukraine is not a new discovery. We have known the risks for a long time," she laments. As for Emmanuel Macron, who had warned that he would announce his decision on a possible presidential candidacy after the "peak of the current geopolitical crisis is behind us," he exchanged this weekend with the Ukrainian and Russian presidents, Volodymyr Zelensky and Vladimir Putin. Following their meeting on February 7 in Moscow, this discussion between the French and Russian leaders represents "the last possible and necessary efforts to avoid a major conflict in Ukraine," emphasized the Elysée.
lead: With just over a month until the presidential election, the situation at the border between Russia and Ukraine is creeping into the electoral campaign in France.

title: Crise en Ukraine : les craintes d’une action militaire russe à leur comble, les ressortissants français et allemands appelés à quitter le pays
text: Tensions were running high in eastern Ukraine on Saturday, February 19. Fears of a Russian invasion are growing as ceasefire violations on the eastern Ukrainian front have increased "dramatically," according to observers, and Moscow conducted exercises involving heavy missile firings. The United States continues to accuse Moscow of preparing to attack its neighbor. More than 40% of the 150,000 Russian soldiers massed on the borders of Ukraine are now in attack positions, a Pentagon official said on Friday. "War is threatening Europe again. And the risk is far from being eliminated," warned German Chancellor Olaf Scholz on Saturday. In this context, France and Germany have recommended their citizens to leave Ukraine and are urging those in the most war-exposed areas in the east of the country to move away "without delay." The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) also announced on Saturday evening that its personnel in Ukraine had been relocated from Kyiv to Lviv in the west of the country and to Brussels for their safety. "All signs indicate that Russia is planning a full-scale attack on Ukraine," said the organization's chief, Jens Stoltenberg, in an interview broadcast by the German channel ARD. Escalation of clashes between Ukrainian forces and pro-Russian separatists INFOGRAPHIC LE MONDE On the front line in eastern Ukraine, Ukrainian armed forces and pro-Russian separatists accused each other on Saturday of new serious ceasefire violations. Kiev announced that two of its soldiers had been killed in these clashes, the first Ukrainian military deaths in over a month. Observers from the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) reported a "dramatic increase" in ceasefire violations: by Saturday evening, they had recorded more than 1,500 incidents in twenty-four hours. According to them, the number of armed incidents on the front line is now the same as before July 2020, the date of an agreement to strengthen the ceasefire. Mortar shells exploded near Denys Monastyrsky, the Ukrainian interior minister, on Saturday during his visit to the front line. The incident, which caused no injuries, occurred near the village of Novoluhanske in Donetsk territory on the front line with pro-Russian separatists, where the minister and Ukrainian deputies were visiting. Moscow also announced on Saturday that it had opened an investigation after reports from the media of a Ukrainian shot falling on Russian territory near the Ukrainian border, causing no damage or casualties. The separatists of the "People's Republic of Donetsk" (self-proclaimed pro-Russian state in 2014), who accuse Kiev of wanting to retake their territory, have, for their part, described the situation as "critical" on Saturday and announced a "general mobilization," just like their neighbors in the "People's Republic of Luhansk." The day before, they had ordered the evacuation of civilians to neighboring Russia. Nearly 20,000 people left these territories on Saturday, according to local officials. Russian missile firing exercises and American alerts Russian President Vladimir Putin personally supervised "strategic" exercises on Saturday with "hypersonic" missile launches. The Kremlin chief had previously described these new weapons as "invincible" and stated that they could carry nuclear warheads. "The main objective of the exercises is to train strategic offensive forces to inflict a guaranteed defeat on the enemy," said Russian Chief of Staff Valery Gerasimov during a video conference meeting, where Mr. Putin appeared alongside his Belarusian counterpart, Alexander Lukashenko. In response to this new show of force, the United States reiterated their alerts. Vice President Kamala Harris mentioned the threat of NATO reinforcement in Eastern Europe in the event of a Russian attack and threatened Moscow with "severe and swift" economic sanctions. On Saturday, U.S. Defense Secretary Lloyd Austin asserted that the Russians "are deploying and preparing to strike" during a visit to Lithuania. According to him, Moscow's soldiers "are moving to appropriate positions to be able to launch an attack." The day before, U.S. President Joe Biden had stated he was "convinced" that Vladimir Putin had decided to invade Ukraine. However, Germany and Ukraine distanced themselves from these very alarmist statements. "In crisis situations, the worst thing is to presume or try to guess what will happen," said German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock in Munich, emphasizing that "we do not know if an attack has already been decided." "We do not think there is a need to panic," later echoed Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in front of a gathering of diplomats and experts from around the world. Saturday night, during a meeting with French President Emmanuel Macron, Mr. Zelensky also "expressed his willingness not to respond to provocations along the contact line," according to the Elysee Palace. A position confirmed by the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, Oleksii Reznikov, who nevertheless warned that he would not allow civilian populations and his troops to be targeted by gunfire "with impunity," in a message on Facebook. Kiev calls on the West to support the Ukrainian "shield." "For eight years, Ukraine has been a shield. For eight years, Ukraine has held back one of the largest armies in the world, which is stationed at our borders and not at those of the European Union (EU)," Volodymyr Zelensky told his Western allies at the Munich Security Conference in Germany. He also called on them to stop any policy of "appeasement" with Moscow. While Ukraine's accession to NATO is a red line for Russia, Mr. Zelensky demanded a "clear" timetable for his country's entry into the institution on Saturday. The West should "support Ukraine and its defense capabilities," he said. The Kremlin continues to deny any intention of attacking its neighbor, but conditions de-escalation on "guarantees" for its security, such as the withdrawal of NATO military infrastructure from Eastern Europe and assurance that Ukraine will never join the Atlantic Alliance – demands unacceptable to Western countries. In Munich, Mr. Stoltenberg reaffirmed the "unwavering" commitment of NATO members to protect each other. British Prime Minister Boris Johnson, for his part, called on allies to "unity," emphasizing the "shock" to the world that an invasion would represent. Mr. Macron is scheduled to speak with Mr. Putin on the phone at 11 a.m. on Sunday, in an attempt to "still avoid the worst," the Elysée Palace said.
lead: Ceasefire violations in the east of the country are increasing as the United States says they are "convinced" that Vladimir Putin has decided to attack.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : protéger les civils
text: Editorial from "Le Monde". Civilian populations trapped in bombarded cities, massive destruction of residential buildings, maternity wards moved underground, water and communications cut off, food shortages. The unbearable spectacle of the tragedy endured by Ukrainians since the invasion of their country by Russia on February 24 has sparked a strong wave of solidarity in Europe. Demonstrations, collections of essential materials and products, hosting hundreds of thousands of refugees demonstrate a commendable commitment from European civil society. However, in the absence of a halt to the Russian offensive, the urgency is to allow civilians to evacuate war zones. President Vladimir Putin has little respect for humanitarian law, just as he has little respect for international law as a whole. On Saturday, March 5, and then on Sunday, two negotiated ceasefires to allow civilians to flee the besieged and bombarded port city of Mariupol were violated. Each time, the bombings resumed as the populations attempted to leave. Gathering points were targeted, and most of the buses intended for evacuation were destroyed. Monday morning, the Russian Ministry of Defense announced the opening of several humanitarian corridors and the establishment of local ceasefires to evacuate civilians from the Ukrainian capital, Kyiv, as well as the cities of Kharkiv, Mariupol, and Sumy, which are experiencing intense fighting. The evacuation routes planned by Moscow only allow civilians to leave their country for Russia or Belarus, de facto occupied by Russian forces: the alternative offered to them is either to remain under the bombs or to seek refuge in enemy territory. These restrictions are not acceptable. Total war. Rightly so, on Sunday, Emmanuel Macron called on Vladimir Putin to respect international law on the fundamental point of protecting civilians and delivering aid during a telephone conversation, and requested an end to the bombings. By blaming the Ukrainians, to whom he believes it is their responsibility to allow the population to leave the cities, President Putin adds cynicism to inhumanity. The increasingly indiscriminate violence sweeping over Ukraine dramatically recalls the past behavior of the Russian army: the two wars in Chechnya (1994-1996 and 1999-2000) of unprecedented brutality, which resulted in the massive destruction of the capital, Grozny, and the siege of Aleppo during the Syrian civil war in 2016, during which the Russian air force did not hesitate to bomb hospitals. In pursuit of his stated goals - the "denazification" of Ukraine, its "neutralization," and ultimately, the total control of a country he does not consider as such - Vladimir Putin displays his regime's contempt for human life, whether it be that of civilians or his own troops. With the Ukrainians' resistance thwarting his initial Blitzkrieg strategy, the Russian president is now engaged in a total war, in which civilians are the primary victims. Even nuclear power plants do not seem to be safe from military operations. Alongside the destruction, carnage, and exodus - with nearly 2 million people already fleeing their homes - there is now the risk of a massive humanitarian catastrophe.
lead: Moscow announced on Monday, March 7, the establishment of humanitarian corridors that only offer Ukrainian civilians the option to head towards territories occupied by Russian forces. These unacceptable restrictions illustrate Vladimir Putin's disregard for human rights.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : assumer le coût des sanctions contre la Russie
text: Editorial from "Le Monde". Brewing for several weeks as the Russian threat became increasingly imminent, the famous "massive and unprecedented" sanctions promised by Western democracies were imposed on Friday, February 25, in response to the invasion of Ukraine by Russia. They come in two forms: personal measures against President Vladimir Putin, his foreign minister, Sergey Lavrov, and numerous officials whose foreign assets are frozen, and a series of sanctions aimed at hitting the Russian economy. These restrictive measures target the finance, transportation, technology, energy sectors, and visa policies. In the financial sector, they aim to block major Russian banks' access to Western capital markets. However, no consensus has been reached at this stage on the harshest financial sanction and undoubtedly the most effective against Moscow: the expulsion of Russia from the Swift messaging system, used for financial transactions by over 11,000 banks and institutions in 200 countries. Such a decision would significantly penalize Russia because by targeting the payment system for its energy exports, it would deprive the Russian budget of its main resource and complicate the financing of the war effort. Why is there no consensus? Because such a strategy would have painful consequences for Western economies, particularly those of the European Union, Russia's top trading partner, dependent on its gas and oil. The lesson from the sanctions imposed since 2014 in response to the annexation of Crimea by Russia, intervention in Donbass, and the downing of Malaysia Airlines flight is now clear: these measured decisions, with no major negative consequences for European economies, punished Russia but had no effect on its behavior. Personal sanctions are politically symbolic. The truly effective sanctions are those that inevitably come at a cost to the issuing countries. A real economic isolation of Russia will inevitably lead to increased inflation, decreased growth, and disruptions in financial markets. Facing the price of solidarity, the truth must be told to European public opinion. If there is currently a consensus among Western democracies, it is on non-direct intervention of their armed forces in Ukraine, a country with which they are not bound by a defense treaty. There is also consensus that Russia is the aggressor and its invasion of Ukraine not only threatens international law but also the entire security framework in Europe. If we consider this issue to be existential for our democracies, there is, at the very least, an economic price to pay. Thinking that we can influence Russia without sacrifices is an illusion. While the Ukrainian army, leaders, and civilians are heroically resisting the Russian invader, the time has come for European political leaders to clearly face the price of solidarity and prepare public opinion for it. To retreat in the face of the cost of sanctions today is to risk having to pay a much heavier price if Vladimir Putin achieves his goal in Ukraine.
lead: If we consider that the issue of security in Europe is existential for our democracies, there is, at the very least, an economic price to pay. Thinking that we can influence Russia without making sacrifices is an illusion.

title: Crise ukrainienne : Anne Hidalgo critique « l’extrême droite française, de concert avec Jean-Luc Mélenchon, oubliant de condamner l’agression de Poutine »
text: In a solemn tone, Anne Hidalgo denounced, on Wednesday, February 23, "an unprecedented act of aggression towards Ukraine, an allied and friendly country." "When the essential is at stake, our country must face it, armed with the unity of statesmen and women who know how to rise to the occasion," she said at a press conference at her campaign headquarters, before criticizing all those who, in her opinion, did not measure up. In her sights: the far-right candidates for the presidential election Marine Le Pen (National Rally), Eric Zemmour (Reconquest!), and, evidently, the "unsubmissive" candidate, Jean-Luc Mélenchon. "The French far right, along with Jean-Luc Mélenchon, forgetting to condemn Putin's aggression, take turns denouncing the alleged NATO threat to Russian security," she lamented, calling them "accomplices of aggressive nationalists and imperialists." "Pathetic slander," reacted the "unsubmissive" deputy Bastien Lachaud. "Jean-Luc Mélenchon has strongly condemned the violation of Ukrainian sovereignty by Russia. He has repeated a hundred times that he does not support Putin's regime in any way, but Hidalgo doesn't care," he wrote on Twitter, before judging that "the Socialist Party has long been synonymous with lies." Emmanuel Macron also criticized Pointing out that "the specter of war is looming at Europe's doorstep," Anne Hidalgo called for "strengthening a European security and defense policy" in the face of the Ukrainian crisis. Believing that it is "a blatant denial of the principle of sovereignty" not to leave "the door open to all territorial aggressions," she emphasized "the necessary sense of unity, both national and European, that must drive us." The socialist candidate, polling below the symbolic 5% mark in various polls for several months, said she was "in favor" of the initial European sanctions "currently being adopted," which, however, "can only be a prelude to a deep political dialogue between the EU and the Russian Federation, as this crisis may last." She called for a European conference to adopt more advanced economic sanctions. Emmanuel Macron's diplomatic policy was also targeted: "This five-year term has been extremely weak, fragile, with a diplomacy characterized by a pas de deux with Trump, this desire to charm Putin, but without relying on substantive work," she said. "If Europe is in danger today, it is because it has shown only weakness since the beginning of the conflict," also stated the Mayor of Paris, considering that "President Macron's speeches have been nothing but a series of staged events and have only weakened the credibility of his words." "It is more than ever time to strengthen a European security and defense policy based on a mechanism of coordination and common decision-making," she insisted, noting that "it is in times of crisis that Europe is built." Finally, the candidate promised that "international climate action would be the first pillar of [her] foreign policy" if elected, because, in her opinion, "by reducing our energy dependence, we will make peace more sustainable."
lead: Pointing out that "the specter of war is looming at the gates of Europe," the socialist candidate in the presidential election called for "strengthening a European security and defense policy."

title: Réunis à Versailles, les Vingt-Sept tentent de faire front, alors que la guerre s’aggrave en Ukraine
text: The Elysée Palace made it clear that "the location of the summit had been chosen before the outbreak of the war." Nevertheless, the symbolism is nonetheless significant. Because it is at the Palace of Versailles, the very place where European heads of state and government gathered on Thursday, March 10 and Friday, March 11 to confront the Russian offensive in Ukraine, that Emmanuel Macron had received Vladimir Putin, with great pomp, on May 29, 2017. At the time, the newly elected French president hoped to rekindle a relationship with Moscow that had been strained by the annexation of Crimea three years earlier, the destabilization of eastern Ukraine, and Russia's military involvement alongside Damascus. By hosting his European counterparts nearly five years later, Emmanuel Macron undoubtedly remembered those early days with the Russian president and could assess the challenges posed by the invasion of Ukraine. Between the exchange in Madame Victoire's corner office with the Kremlin leader and the dinner with the Twenty-Seven in the Hall of Mirrors, the world has shifted into a new era, and so have the Europeans. "This summit is very important because it comes in the midst of a war, and we must make strong decisions. These decisions must be made in the coming days and weeks," said the head of state. The entire Thursday evening and part of the night were dedicated to the conflict and the numerous questions it raises. The stakes are strategic for the Europeans. As the conflict triggered by Vladimir Putin threatens to persist and destabilize the entire continent, the question is how to influence the course of events and encourage the Russian president to accept a ceasefire, even though that prospect is fading, as acknowledged by Mr. Macron. At the same time, the Europeans have emphasized their willingness to assist Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, without, however, following his request for "immediate" EU membership after heated debates between the east and west of the continent. No consensus for a blockade Like the United States, the Twenty-Seven do not want to go to war against Russia. Therefore, in addition to the military equipment provided to Kyiv, they have the leverage of economic sanctions. On this point, they are pleased to have hastily adopted, in consultation with Washington, massive retaliations, the effects of which are already being felt on the economy and the Russian state: the ruble has lost more than 75% of its value since February 24, J.P. Morgan predicts a 35% drop in GDP in the second half, foreign companies are leaving Russia one after the other, and Moscow is on the brink of default. On Thursday, the Europeans discussed new retaliatory measures in anticipation of an escalation in fighting. The Baltic States and Poland advocated for an embargo on Russian hydrocarbons. "If we stop buying from Russia, it will stop financing the Russian military machine. If we do not strengthen sanctions now, then when?" said Latvian Prime Minister Arturs Krisjanis Karins. He added, "We need to stop importing Russian energy in order to bring Putin back to the negotiating table." "Ukrainians are paying with their blood for European imports of Russian oil and gas," echoed a Polish diplomat. In contrast, Germany and Italy, heavily dependent on Russian gas, have distanced themselves from a potential embargo. Hungary as well. "While condemning the Russian military offensive and also condemning the war, we will not allow Hungarian families to pay the price," said Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban. Due to a lack of consensus on an energy blockade, the Twenty-Seven agreed, however, that they needed to reduce their dependence on Russian hydrocarbons without delay. Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the Commission, even suggested completely eliminating this dependence by 2027, without convincing all member states. Another controversial proposal is for the EU to reduce its dependence on Russian hydrocarbons by two-thirds by the end of the year. "The question is whether Russia will use gas as a weapon: we must prepare for all scenarios," judged Emmanuel Macron. Resilience plan Warsaw once again tried to steer its partners away from their climate agenda, aimed at achieving carbon neutrality by 2050, which is particularly challenging for a country with a coal-intensive economy. Thursday evening, Mateusz Morawiecki emphasized his arguments even more as Poland has welcomed over a million Ukrainian refugees in two weeks: he hopes to obtain concessions from Europeans, both on climate issues and on the European recovery plan, which Brussels has denied due to the rule of law dispute. Beyond the Ukrainian case, at the request of Emmanuel Macron, the Twenty-Seven also wanted to discuss the "dependencies" they face, which this war has made even more visible. Energy, therefore, but also defense, agriculture, and semiconductors. The French president, who has made European sovereignty his mantra during his presidency, hopes to use this crisis to further his ideas. "We must build the sovereignty of our Europe to resist," declared Emmanuel Macron. In addition to the sanctions that will undoubtedly impact European economies, this program requires investments that will need to be financed and are currently far from unanimous. In this context, Paris is discreetly urging its partners to put in place a European resilience plan, which would be financed through common debt, similar to the €750 billion recovery plan implemented during the Covid-19 pandemic. So far, without success. "It's not on the table," says Mark Rutte, the Dutch Prime Minister. Berlin is also not hiding its reservations. "Every two weeks, Paris comes up with an idea for a 2.0 recovery plan. It's like a broken record," exclaims a diplomat from a Northern country. The Netherlands, like Germany, Denmark, and Finland, argue that the European recovery plan and the common budget still offer opportunities of "€500 billion," without needing to create a new instrument. "There's no need to rush to buy new shoes when you already have good ones in the closet," summarizes another diplomat. While the Twenty-Seven want to present a united front against Moscow, Emmanuel Macron did not seek confrontation on Thursday evening. "You should never start the discussion on instruments before clarifying the objectives; this allows taboos and blockages to be lifted," he declared. The President has deferred decisions on energy to the summit on March 24th and 25th, and on defense to an exceptional council at the end of May. The discussions are far from over.
lead: Faced with the intensification of Moscow's offensive, European Union member states are considering a gradual halt to imports of Russian hydrocarbons, but are not ready for an immediate embargo.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : Oleg Sentsov, un artiste au front pour la défense de Kiev
text: It's a checkpoint on a roundabout like so many others. An ordinary checkpoint on the outskirts of Kiev, with barricades, concrete blocks, sandbags, and a casemate for resting and having tea. Fighters are checking the cars passing through this axis that the Russian army could use in its offensive against the Ukrainian capital. Next to the checkpoint, the army has set up an improvised military camp in a house and a furniture store. When they are not on guard duty, this is where the fighters come to grab a few hours of restless sleep. The base hosts a unit of the territorial defense, a troop of hundreds of thousands of volunteers, led by retired officers and veterans, who have joined the Ukrainian army in defense of the country. Oleg Sentsov had been missing since Moscow declared war on Ukraine. The Ukrainian filmmaker, one of the most famous former political prisoners in Putin's Russia, a globally recognized artist and an icon of the Ukrainian democratic revolution, has joined the front lines like many of his compatriots and has cut himself off from the outside world. It is at the "roundabout checkpoint," as he asks to be called without further details to avoid being located by the Russian army, that the territorial defense has stationed Oleg Sentsov. The artist, with no prior military experience, is the deputy commander of Colonel Arkadich's unit, a retired army officer. After a week of holding the checkpoint in the second line, the filmmaker joined a mobile group of the territorial defense that patrols the first front line in the outskirts of Kiev. Engaged in territorial defense, Oleg Sentsov's life, who was working on his next film and writing various scripts, changed, like that of all Ukrainians, on February 24th. "At 5 a.m., they fired the first missiles on Kiev. I was woken up by a friend border guard who told me that Russian tanks had entered Ukraine," the filmmaker recounts during a meeting with Le Monde at the "roundabout checkpoint." "I packed my bags and, fifteen minutes later, I hit the road to take my mother and two children to Lviv [the safe city in western Ukraine]. Then I returned to Kiev the same day and joined the territorial defense to fight Putin and defend my country." Oleg Sentsov is an icon of the democratic movement in Ukraine. In 2014, after participating in the "dignity revolution" at Maidan in Kiev, he returned to his native Crimea invaded by the Russian army and pro-Russian separatists. He was helping Ukrainian soldiers by delivering provisions to military bases. He was arrested with other political opponents by the FSB, the Russian intelligence services, and accused of "terrorism." Sentenced the following year to twenty years in prison after a trial described as "Stalinist" by Amnesty International, he was sent to a penal colony in Siberia. Oleg Sentsov became world-famous. The international film community mobilized, followed by human rights organizations, and then by European and American governments. In 2018, the UN General Assembly adopted a resolution calling for his release, and he received the Sakharov Prize, awarded by the European Parliament to a person who has dedicated their life to defending freedoms – the latest prize, in 2021, was awarded to another political prisoner of Vladimir Putin, the Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny. Sentsov was finally released in 2019 as part of a prisoner exchange between Russia and Ukraine. When he went to Strasbourg after his release to finally receive the Sakharov Prize, Oleg Sentsov called, in his speech, for caution towards the Russian government. He now recounts with a smile having had a side conversation with French President Emmanuel Macron. "Do you believe Putin?" I asked him. "He has never deceived me," Macron replied. "You will see, he will do it in the future," I told him... With a serious face, Sentsov believes that, "today, it is clear, the whole world can see how Putin is waging this war against Ukraine, against civilians." Incredulous until a wake-up "under rocket fire" The artist admits, "I did not believe in this war in recent months." "I only began to understand two weeks before that the war was coming, because there were still too many warning signs. And then, suddenly, on the last day, I no longer believed it again, because it seemed so crazy. Until I woke up under rocket fire..." Oleg Sentsov is an icon of the democratic movement in Ukraine. Kiev, March 10, 2022. LAURENT VAN DER STOCKT FOR "LE MONDE" Oleg Sentsov remained discreet while the crisis was brewing. He just warned, in an interview published on January 26 by Reuters, against "the strategy of chaos and destabilization" of the Russian president. "Putin wants to turn Ukraine into a failed state. And if Ukraine falls, then he will move further west." Sentsov warned that "Crimea, Donbass, Ukraine" but also "Poland, the Baltic countries, Moldova, Georgia, Kazakhstan" are, in Putin's mind, "one and the same war." The artist then joined a call to the leaders of the world from the cultural and scientific community of Ukraine in favor of peace, published on February 14, while Russia had already sent its troops to the country's borders. International pressure had no effect on Moscow, and the invasion began. Oleg Sentsov, who had premiered his latest film, Rhino, in Ukraine seven days before the war, put aside his ongoing scripts, one in Ukrainian for a co-produced dramedy with Germany and Poland, and another in English co-produced with Hollywood. "These projects belong to the past. In two weeks, our lives have changed, everything has changed," notes the artist, who now lives in camouflage in the house next to the "checkpoint roundabout" and only rarely returns home to "take a hot shower." "Ukraine is strong and united like never before." His only public statement since the war broke out was a short text dictated by phone to his assistant, and published on March 3 by the Hollywood site Deadline. He recounts life shifting "in an instant" and announces that he has joined the defense of Kyiv. He denounces "Russian bombs falling on Ukrainian children" and calls for support from "intellectuals and artists who oppose Putin's bloodthirsty regime." While Oleg Sentsov now says he "still doesn't understand why Putin is ruining Ukraine like this, and also ruining Russia," he, like all his comrades, is preparing for "a long war." "It's a war for our survival. Either Ukraine survives and is victorious, or the Putin system survives. There is no other choice," Sentsov says. The only question now is "To be or not to be." Putin wants to kill us, destroy our country." The filmmaker no longer believes in a last-minute negotiation and is preparing, like all Kyiv residents engaged in defending the city, for a Russian army assault. "Even if our president, Volodymyr Zelensky, reached an agreement with Vladimir Putin, the people would not accept it," Sentsov believes. The only possible agreement now would be: firstly, the liberation of our country's territories; secondly, that Putin and his clique be judged for crimes against humanity; and thirdly, that Russia pay for the reconstruction of Ukraine." Oleg Sentsov is confident in "Ukraine's victory, with international support." "Putin has become a problem for the whole world. He threatens to use nuclear weapons, he is a danger." In order to build "a Russian empire," the Kremlin master wants, according to him, to "destroy independent Ukraine." "It's a war against Ukrainian culture, against the Ukrainian mentality, against this taste for freedom," the filmmaker continues. If Ukraine, as a free and independent country, were successful, the Russians would ask Putin why life is so difficult in Russia, a country built on lies." Ukrainian director Oleg Sentsov has been involved in the territorial defense of the Ukrainian army since the first day of the war. In Kiev, on March 10, 2022. For Sentsov, "Putin wants to destroy the new Ukraine, the one that started with Maidan." "Ukraine has changed a lot in eight years," he explains, despite the conflict with Russia in its eastern regions. "And now, with this war, Ukraine is strong and united like never before. Even Russian speakers, who used to be neutral and had a positive feeling towards Russia, are waving Ukrainian flags. Everyone has understood that the future of the country is independence and Europe." "We will fight street by street." Oleg Sentsov shows the room he shares with four other soldiers. His mattress is on the floor in a corner. Next to it is his weapon, and a military helmet with his blood type written on it. Then he joins comrades who have just arrived in front of the "checkpoint at the roundabout." He checks a rifle scope. Colonel Arkadich discreetly confides that the artist is "an excellent deputy commander," despite his very recent integration into the army. "There are no civilians left in Ukraine," adds the officer, "only fighters." The filmmaker looks around. "Just two weeks ago, I was walking not far from here, with my mother and children, peacefully..." This nostalgic observation does not prevent him from being in high spirits. "All my life, I watched Hollywood movies with super-agents fighting evil," he says with a smile. "Today, Putin is the evil and Ukraine doesn't have super-agents, but it has us, the Ukrainian people. And we will fight street by street, all over the country, until liberation." The sun, orange, sets on the horizon. The "checkpoint at the roundabout" prepares for another night on high alert. The Russian army is not far, on the outskirts of Kiev. Oleg Sentsov gets into a Jeep with two other fighters. They head out on a mission to the front line.
lead: Icon of the Maidan revolution, former political prisoner in Russia, 2018 Sakharov Prize winner, the filmmaker, involved in the Ukrainian army, tells "Le Monde" about a life that changes "in an instant", his struggle against Vladimir Putin and for the "survival" of his country.

title: La guerre en Ukraine rattrape les Jeux paralympiques d’hiver
text: Twelve days separate the extinguishing of the Olympic flame and the lighting of the Paralympic flame, on Friday, March 4, in Beijing. Twelve days during which the Olympic truce was shattered. By deciding, in the night from Wednesday, February 23, to Thursday, February 24, to invade Ukraine, Russian President Vladimir Putin caused Russian and Belarusian sports to be banned for their support of Moscow. The Winter Paralympic Games, taking place until March 13, find themselves disrupted by the conflict in Ukraine. "War has now caught up with these Games and behind the scenes, many governments have exerted influence on our event," said Andrew Parsons, the president of the International Paralympic Committee (IPC), in a statement on Thursday, March 3. Less than a day after announcing that Russian and Belarusian athletes could compete "under a neutral flag," the Games organizer reversed course. "Athletes from these two countries will no longer be allowed to participate in the Games," the IPC stated, facing a wave of criticism following its initial decision. "Teams and athletes are now threatening not to participate, jeopardizing the success of the Winter Paralympic Games," explained Andrew Parsons, justifying the IPC's reversal, twenty-four hours before the opening ceremony. The situation in the athletes' village is deteriorating, and ensuring the safety [of the Russian and Belarusian delegations] has become impossible." Since the beginning of the week, the IPC had been under pressure from many actors, both in sports and politics. On Monday, the International Olympic Committee (IOC), in an unprecedented decision breaking with years of promoting the apolitical nature of sports, called on sports organizations to exclude Russia from their competitions. Following the sanctions imposed by the powerful football federations – the European confederation (UEFA) and, especially, the International Federation (FIFA) – all Russian sports found themselves sidelined. The "dilemma" of the organizers A Ukrainian journalist wears a mask that reads "No war in Ukraine" before the 2022 Winter Paralympic Games in Beijing, China, on Wednesday, March 2, 2022. ANDY WONG / AP The International Paralympic Committee is not a sub-chapter of the IOC. "These two institutions do not operate in the same way," explains Patrick Clastres, a sports historian and professor at the University of Lausanne. "The IOC gives instructions to international federations, then relies on them to make decisions. The IPC, on the other hand, does not have this 'filter' of international federations – there are none in parasports – which changes the paradigm." During the Rio de Janeiro Paralympic Games in 2016, the IPC, unable to rely on international federations to recommend excluding Russian athletes, decided to suspend the country from the Paralympic Games. In a statement earlier this week, the IOC highlighted a "dilemma": how to allow Russian athletes to participate when their Ukrainian counterparts "are prevented from doing so due to the attack on their country"? The Ukrainian Paralympic delegation (20 athletes and 9 staff members) managed to reach Beijing "without incident," prompting the IPC to initially choose to adhere to its statutes, where "neutrality is firmly anchored": no exclusion of athletes, but exclusion of symbols (flags, anthems, colors...). The reaction of many delegations and the threat of a boycott forced it to reverse its decision. "The fact that these two delegations are not excluded is incomprehensible. We argue based on rules, while in Ukraine people are being killed without any rules," Friedhelm-Julius Beucher, the president of the German Paralympic Sports Federation, denounced on Wednesday. "This decision sends an absolutely wrong signal." While the IPC emphasized that its choice to allow Russians and Belarusians to compete under a neutral flag was "the most severe penalty we could impose" - as the statutes of the organization do not allow for exclusions - the numerous reactions pushed it to disregard its rules in an attempt to save its competition. On Wednesday, British Sports Minister Nadine Dorries called on the organization to "urgently reconsider" its "bad decision." Beijing in a delicate position This is not the first time that Russia under Vladimir Putin has disregarded the "Olympic Truce." In 2008, the country launched an offensive in Georgia on the day of the opening of the Beijing Summer Olympics. "In 2014, Russia annexed Crimea just after the end of 'its' Sochi Olympic Games," also recalls Simon Chadwick, director of the Eurasian Sports Industry Center at EM Lyon Business School. At that time, too, it did not wait for the Paralympic Games to begin. However, the Russian intervention did not occur during the Olympic Games, which could have greatly embarrassed China. As a strategic partner of Russia, Beijing remains cautious in this crisis. During the United Nations General Assembly resolution on Wednesday, March 2, demanding "that Russia immediately cease using force against Ukraine," the Middle Kingdom once again abstained. "This offensive, in fact, undermines the image of the Paralympic Games and puts Beijing in a delicate position," continues Simon Chadwick. But, according to the researcher, "perhaps in Russia, or even in China, they might say, 'These are just the Paralympic Games.'" A competition much less exposed in the media than its big sister, the Olympics. The Russian delegation, which according to the German Paralympic Federation - a neighbor in the athletes' village - had "exploded with joy when Parsons announced the decision [to allow him to compete]," finds itself forced to leave Beijing. "You are the victims of the actions of your governments," lamented the IPC president addressing the eighty-three athletes from the two countries concerned. "We are sorry." The day before, Andrew Parsons emphasized that "unlike their respective governments, these athletes are not aggressors." Despite the International Paralympic Committee's reversal, it is difficult to imagine the event proceeding smoothly: even in the absence of Russians and Belarusians, many athletes could take advantage of the competition to make their voices heard.
lead: Initially authorized by the International Paralympic Committee to compete "under a neutral flag," Russian and Belarusian athletes were ultimately excluded on Thursday.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : le Guide suprême iranien ménage la Russie
text: Talking about a conflict without mentioning the name of the main belligerent: this is the exercise that the Supreme Leader of Iran, Ali Khamenei, engaged in when discussing the war in Ukraine. During a speech of about forty minutes delivered on March 1st from Tehran - the first since the start of the Russian offensive on February 24th - the country's top authority carefully avoided mentioning even a word related to Russia, a close political ally of Iran. This is despite the fact that Russian military intensifies their bombings in Kyiv and the second-largest city in the country, Kharkiv, is surrounded. Instead, Ali Khamenei mentioned the words "United States" about twenty times and "West" about ten times, blaming them for the current war. "Ukraine has been a victim of the destabilizing policies of the United States. This country has led Ukraine to its current situation by interfering in its internal affairs, organizing rallies against governments, and orchestrating velvet revolutions, coups," supported Ali Khamenei, repeating the official narrative of the Iranian regime that attributes the roots of any conflict or uprising to American and Western interference. Continuing his statement, the Supreme Leader also suggested that the Ukrainian government lacked support from its own population. "The people are the greatest support for governments. If the Ukrainian people had intervened, the situation of the Ukrainian government would have been different. People are not there because they do not believe in the government," said Ali Khamenei, while the Ukrainian army resists and a large number of men and women join volunteer units in Ukraine since the start of the Russian offensive. He also disregarded the growing popularity of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. According to a study published in February by the Razumkov Center, 90% of Ukrainians support their president, compared to 45% before the offensive. Stalled nuclear negotiations If the highest political and religious authority in Iran assured that he was "for the end of the war" in Ukraine, he made no mention of the role Russia could play in the solution he advocates. "The remedy for any crisis lies in resolving the root of the problem. And in Ukraine, the roots are American policies," concluded Ali Khamenei. The remarks of the Iranian Supreme Leader come as negotiations to revive the 2015 nuclear deal on the Iranian nuclear issue (the JCPOA) are ongoing in Vienna, directly involving the United Kingdom, Germany, France, Russia, and China, and indirectly involving the United States. A few days before the start of the conflict in Ukraine, all parties involved were betting on a resolution by the end of February, either reaching an agreement or the failure of the talks. However, today, everything suggests that the negotiations may last much longer. "Indeed, we had reasons to expect that the talks on the JCPOA would be successfully concluded by the end of February. These expectations were not met," warned the Russian ambassador to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), Mikhail Ulyanov, in a message posted on Twitter on February 28. "Regrettable but not dramatic. It was a deadline, not the final date. We still have time to finalize the talks in the coming days." "Despotism and dictatorship" In the nuclear talks, Tehran sees Moscow as a support against the Western camp. This is why Ali Khamenei's statements have spared Russia, to the extent of refusing to mention its role in Ukraine. The same tone is echoed on Iranian public television channels: the war in Ukraine is referred to as a "special military operation," the term used by Vladimir Putin to describe the invasion of the country. Russian officials' press briefings are broadcast live, and information from the Russian Ministry of Defense scrolls across the screen during news broadcasts. The message? In harmony with the Supreme Leader's statements: trusting the West is a mistake, while Russia remains a reliable partner. On social media, one of the few spaces where Iranians can still express themselves freely, many express their aversion to Vladimir Putin and their admiration for the Ukrainian people. "The Islamic Republic of Iran's enthusiasm for Putin is not due to shared interests between the two countries. It is because, as written by a journalist from Tehran who prefers to remain anonymous, the leaders of both countries have a penchant for despotism and dictatorship." On Wednesday, March 2, the United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution "demanding that Russia immediately cease the use of force against Ukraine," in a vote approved by 141 countries, with 5 opposing and 35 abstaining, including Iran.
lead: In a speech delivered on Tuesday, Ali Khamenei blamed the United States and the West for the current war.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : l’utilisation de Google Maps restreinte dans le pays ainsi qu’en Russie
text: In times of war, information is a sensitive issue. Google has decided to freeze its mapping service Google Maps by preventing its users from publishing new information on its Ukrainian and Russian maps. In a statement released on Wednesday, March 2, the American company also announced that it had deleted recent posts: all new locations, comments, as well as photos and videos submitted since the beginning of the invasion. This decision follows suspicions of Google Maps being misused by pro-Russian and pro-Ukrainian supporters. On Tuesday, several Twitter accounts claimed that the Russian army had bombed several locations possibly added to Google Maps by Russian agents, which appeared on the map around February 28. Even though Google expressed doubts about these reports to the American site BuzzFeed, the company decided to delete all new posts as a precaution. Troop positions At the same time, the Russian version of Google Maps was subject to a more clearly confirmed misuse. The profiles of many businesses and institutions saw comments that had no connection to the services they offered: for example, comments such as "Putin is lying to you, there are many deaths on both sides" could be found in reviews of a Moscow fast-food restaurant. In the profile of a pool located in Sochi, there were photos of bombed buildings. According to our findings, this information has indeed disappeared following the measures taken by Google. The idea of communicating through Google Maps profiles had been promoted by a Twitter account claiming to be part of the Anonymous movement, urging people to write comments on Google Maps to "explain what is happening in Ukraine" and thus "inform the Russian civilian society to which Putin is lying." In general, the Russian invasion of Ukraine demonstrates the importance of maps in times of war. Google's mapping service was quickly exploited in the context of the war in Ukraine. From the early hours of the conflict, Google Maps allowed the location of certain troops, whose presence could be suspected by blocked roads appearing on local maps. This is why, on Sunday, February 27, the American company decided to disable the traffic display on these maps, in agreement with local authorities. In general, the Russian invasion of Ukraine demonstrates the importance of maps in times of war. Google Maps, whose Ukrainian map remains accessible although cleared of recent information, remains a valuable tool for journalists seeking to verify the location of buildings targeted by attacks or to validate the authenticity of war videos posted on social media. On March 1st, another mapping tool of Ukrainian origin, Liveuamap, was hit by a cyberattack, causing it to be non-operational for eighteen hours. This service, used by journalists and non-governmental organizations, allows tracking the developments of the conflict in Ukrainian territory.
lead: Due to suspicions of misappropriation, Google has decided to block the publication of new information on the Russian and Ukrainian maps in its mapping service.

title: Bafta 2022 : « The Power of the Dog » triomphe en s’adjugeant les prix du meilleur film et de la meilleure réalisation
text: The dark and psychological western film The Power of the Dog was crowned best film at the Bafta awards, the British film awards, on Sunday, March 13, and earned New Zealander Jane Campion the award for best director. This Netflix production, also nominated for Best Picture at the Oscars on March 28, won over Belfast, Don’t Look Up!, Dune, and Licorice Pizza. With eight nominations, the New Zealander's film entered the ceremony as a strong contender two weeks before the Oscars. Presented at the Venice Film Festival in September, where the director had won an award, the western has since won the Golden Globe for Best Drama and Jane Campion for Best Director. She becomes the third woman to win the Bafta for Best Director, following Chinese director Chloé Zhao (Nomadland) last year, and American director Kathryn Bigelow (The Hurt Locker) in 2009. The New Zealander was absent from the ceremony at the Royal Albert Hall in London, but her producer, Tanya Seghatchian, paid tribute to her, calling her a "visionary who has shaped the past thirty years of cinema." American actor Will Smith won the award for Best Actor for his role in "King Richard" - directed by Reinaldo Marcus Green - where he portrays the father and coach of tennis champions Serena and Venus Williams. The 53-year-old actor, also nominated for an Oscar for Best Actor, won over Adeel Akhtar, Mahershala Ali, Benedict Cumberbatch, Leonardo DiCaprio, and Stephen Graham. English actress Joanna Scanlan, on the other hand, won the award for Best Actress for her portrayal of a wife discovering her late husband's secrets in "After Love," directed by Aleem Khan. The 60-year-old actress beat out Lady Gaga, Alana Haim, Emilia Jones, Renate Reinsve, and Tessa Thompson. A middle finger to Putin After an audience-less awards ceremony last year due to the Covid-19 pandemic, it was the war in Ukraine that marked this 75th edition of the Bafta awards. The Russian invasion "has shocked the world with images and stories telling a truly horrible and heartbreaking situation," said the president of the British Academy, Krishnendu Majumdar, at the beginning of the ceremony. The Bafta and other film academies in Europe stand with Ukraine and "we share the hope for a return to peace," he added. On the red carpet, some stars wore badges or ribbons in the colors of the Ukrainian flag, including actor Benedict Cumberbatch, who said he was "doing what he could" to host Ukrainian refugees, after the British government called on residents to welcome Ukrainians into their homes. Presenting the award for Best Director, Andy Serkis criticized British Home Secretary Priti Patel for her handling of the crisis, sarcastically stating that Patel's latest film "All refugees are welcome but some are more welcome than others is a total hell." Australian actress Rebel Wilson, who was hosting the ceremony, also flipped off Russian President Vladimir Putin. "Luckily, in all sign languages, it's a gesture for Putin," she said while introducing Emilia Jones, who was singing and signing. Emilia Jones was nominated for Best Actress for her portrayal in CODA of a music-loving teenager who is the only hearing member in a deaf family. British actor Stephen Graham wore a ribbon in the colors of Ukraine on the red carpet at the Bafta ceremony in London on March 13, 2022. Disappointment for "Dune" This American remake of the French film "La Famille Bélier" made history at the Baftas, with Troy Kotsur becoming the first deaf actor to win an award for Best Supporting Actor. "I think it's great that you're recognizing my work on the other side of the ocean, it's truly incredible," he reacted while receiving his award, with his interpreter by his side. Disappointment for "Dune," the adaptation of Frank Herbert's science fiction novel by Denis Villeneuve, which did not win any of the top awards. The space opera had received eleven nominations (the most this year) and left London with five awards, including for special effects, original music, and cinematography. Also nominated for Best Film, "Belfast," in which Kenneth Branagh pays tribute to his hometown by tracing the journey of a child from Northern Ireland traumatized by the civil war who finds solace in cinema, won the award for Best British Film. Among other awards, American actress Ariana DeBose won the Bafta for Best Supporting Actress for "West Side Story," while "Drive My Car" by Japanese director Ryusuke Hamaguchi won the award for Best Foreign Language Film. Nominated for Best Film and Best Director, the coming-of-age film "Licorice Pizza" by Paul Thomas Anderson won the award for Best Original Screenplay.
lead: Jane Campion's dark western maintained its status as the favorite at the British film awards ceremony, which was marked this year by messages of support for Ukraine.

title: Volodymyr Zelensky au Parlement canadien : « Je voudrais que vous ressentiez ce que nous ressentons »
text: After speaking before the British House of Commons on March 8 and a few hours before addressing the American Congress, the President of Ukraine, Volodymyr Zelensky, was invited on Tuesday, March 15, to a special session of the Canadian Parliament. On the twentieth day of the war, in a video conference from Kyiv, the Ukrainian leader, dressed in khaki green, delivered an emotional speech lasting about ten minutes to tell Canadian MPs and senators about the reality of the war. "I want you to feel what we feel," he told them. "Since the beginning of this war, 97 children have died," and "every night is a nightmare," he explained, describing bombings on hospitals and daycare centers, explosions at 4 a.m. in residential neighborhoods of cities, fires in nuclear power plants, water and electricity cuts, and hunger now affecting the residents of Mariupol. "Imagine the questions from your children wondering what is happening," "imagine," repeated Volodymyr Zelensky several times, drawing parallels with Canadian cities: "Imagine Vancouver besieged (...), imagine Montreal airport bombed (...), imagine the CN Tower in Toronto hit by Russian bombs, that's our reality." "How many attacks will it take before taking these measures?" These images were meant to support his request for an air exclusion zone in Ukrainian airspace: "You provide us with military and humanitarian aid, you have imposed severe sanctions, but unfortunately, we see that this does not end the war (...). How many attacks will it take before taking these measures?" He added, "We are not asking for much. We ask for real support that will help us prevail." Applauded for several minutes Canadian lawmakers, wearing yellow and blue ribbons as a sign of support, applauded standing up for several minutes after his speech, with some MPs shouting "Glory to Ukraine!" A few minutes earlier, the Canadian Prime Minister, Justin Trudeau, opened this special session by welcoming Volodymyr Zelensky remotely, calling him a "courageous and exceptional leader." "Your courage and the courage of your people inspire us all. You are defending the right of Ukrainians to choose their own future. And in doing so, you are defending the values that are the foundation of all free and democratic countries," he said, without mentioning the request for an air exclusion zone. Last week, during an interview on the English-language public broadcaster CTV, like his NATO partners, he had already ruled out agreeing to the Ukrainian request: "It's heartbreaking," he said, "we cannot do that, as the risk of escalation is too high if we send NATO planes into the skies of Ukraine to shoot down Russian planes." Justin Trudeau therefore contented himself with announcing to the Ukrainian president a new set of sanctions against fifteen other Russian individuals, bringing the total number of people or entities targeted to over 500 since Russia's invasion of Ukraine. "Unwavering support" from Canada In response, Moscow announced that the Canadian Prime Minister, along with 313 Canadian citizens, were also subject to Russian sanctions. "Vladimir Putin's disregard for human life is absolutely unacceptable," Justin Trudeau concluded, assuring Ukraine of Canada's "unwavering support" in the name of their common values, but also in the name of their shared history. He indeed recalled that his country had been built thanks to the significant Ukrainian immigration since the late 19th century. Canada still hosts the second largest Ukrainian diaspora in the world, after the one in Russia, with over 1.3 million Canadians of Ukrainian origin. His predecessor had addressed the House of Commons in September 2014 Volodymyr Zelensky is not the first Ukrainian president to address Canadian parliamentarians. His predecessor, Petro Poroshenko, had addressed the House of Commons in September 2014, a few months after the annexation of Crimea and the beginning of the war in Donbass. "We are engaged in a real battle for our independence and we are paying a very real price for it. Today, Ukraine is paying a high price for defending its convictions, namely democracy and the freedom to choose its future," Mr. Poroshenko had declared to the lawmakers. Nearly eight years later, the current Ukrainian president, leading a country under massive Russian invasion, came to tell Canadians that this price was now being counted in thousands of deaths.
lead: Eight years after his predecessor Petro Poroshenko, the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, addressed the Canadian Parliament live to request the establishment of an air exclusion zone in Ukrainian airspace.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : inquiétudes sur la couverture du conflit par la chaîne russe RT France
text: "Provocation comes from NATO and the Ukrainian army, which put pressure on the Donbass by bombing civilian populations," asserted Emmanuel Leroy on Thursday, February 24th in the morning on RT France. Just a few hours after the Russian attacks, the expert, known for his pro-Vladimir Putin regime stance and former writer for Marine Le Pen, thus presented his own version of the attacks. Sitting beside him on the set, Alexis Poulin added, accusing the United States. "Everything is being done to prevent a reliable partnership with Russia," stated the commentator. While Europe unanimously condemns Vladimir Putin, the Elysée Palace is keeping an eye on RT France and Sputnik, another media outlet in the Kremlin's orbit. During the press conference in Brussels following the European Council meeting, Emmanuel Macron mentioned the necessary "protection" against, among other things, "Russian propaganda outlets on European soil."
