According to our information, "possible sanctions or suspensions" are part of the ongoing discussions, although these decisions could ultimately only be made by the Audiovisual and Digital Communication Regulatory Authority (Arcom, formerly CSA). The executive branch is not alone in its concerns. Centrist senator Laurent Lafon (Val-de-Marne) has requested Arcom to "immediately suspend the broadcast of Russia Today, which is the official Russian propaganda outlet in France." Subsequently, Arcom assured that it was "closely monitoring RT France's compliance with its legal and conventional obligations." This warning was issued while an investigation is already underway. At the end of 2021, the audiovisual regulator was approached by the association Media & Democracy Europe, which complained about RT France's coverage of the Donbass. In its range of tools, Arcom could also quickly suspend the broadcast by directly appealing to the president of the litigation section of the Council of State, a mechanism inserted in the 1986 law, known as the Léotard law, relating to freedom of communication. However, a solid case must be presented. RT France responded to these criticisms by condemning "the political pressure exerted on Arcom which goes against the legal framework aimed at protecting freedom of expression." The channel also claims to have covered the beginning of the Ukrainian crisis "comprehensively, by presenting all points of view." Balancing act Is this really the case? To maintain its television license, RT France is engaging in a delicate balancing act to avoid being accused of misinformation. On Thursday, its journalists reported all information regarding the Russian strikes, including international condemnations. However, the vocabulary chosen was carefully selected: there was no mention of "war" or "invasion" by the Russian army. If journalists tried to maintain a form of neutrality, the guests on set did not spare their efforts to convey the Kremlin's rhetoric. "People wonder if we are going to close, and what future they can give to their career: will they always be associated with RT France," wonders an SNJ official. "The Atlanticist world did not expect an operation of demilitarization and denazification of Ukraine," judged Karine Bechet-Golovko, a law professor at Moscow University who has her place at RT France, and who thus endorsed Mr. Putin's words. "When there are strategic events for Russia, suddenly there is a takeover of news production in Moscow and an alignment of RT subsidiaries on the same language elements," explains researcher Maxime Audinet, author of Russia Today (RT): a media of influence in the service of the Russian state (INA). Frédéric Taddeï's departure had the effect of an explosion. This heavy criticism against RT France plunged its editorial staff into worry. "People wonder if we are going to close, and what future they can give to their career: will they always be associated with the intensified criticisms towards RT France?" asks an SNJ official. Frédéric Taddeï's departure, who had been hosting "Interdit d'interdire" since 2018, had the effect of an explosion. The famous host justified the end of his collaboration by his "loyalty" to France. "Staying does not mean that we are disloyal to France. Because how do we pay our rent?" grumbles a journalist. Following the declaration of war on Ukraine, Jacques de Guillebon, editor-in-chief of the very right-wing magazine L'Incorrect, who had been appearing in a show on the presidential election since the beginning of the season, also ended his collaboration. "I always thought that I would regain my freedom in case of a problem. The very structure of RT means that there can be no debate on the conflict," he explains. The editorial staff of RT France is made up of journalists comfortable with the Kremlin's line, who justify the editorial treatment of the channel by the right to "information pluralism," and others who have found a permanent contract "rather better paid than elsewhere," says one of them, as the media industry recruits few. "We have received several calls to protest in front of the editorial office," continues the SNJ representative. "We are called 'traitors,' 'collaborators,' or 'enemies of the nation.' We now fear isolated acts against employees on site." Maxime Audinet analyzes: "The contradiction will increase between the requirement to comply with the rules of the Arcom and those of the Russian state, which will need all its media apparatus to advance its narrative." In the meantime, Xenia Fedorova, the head of RT France, sent an email to the teams offering to address their concerns. A general assembly, initiated by the management and the unions, and "dedicated to the situation" will be held on Wednesday, March 2. However, censorship of RT France could lead to retaliation. "RT France is not a threat to democracy, given its low viewership. In case of prohibition, the Russian state would immediately expel journalists from RFI or France 24," predicts the researcher. RT France has only 3.7 million visits per month, according to SimilarWeb. The Spanish and Arabic versions perform much better, with 22 million and 31.4 million visits respectively. RT's impact is not limited to France. In the UK, the Office of Communications (Ofcom) is currently examining its case. In Germany, the regulator has just banned it for legal reasons.
lead: According to our information, "possible sanctions or suspensions" are being considered to penalize or suspend the channel, whose many guests spread Kremlin propaganda.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : le président Volodymyr Zelensky appelle les citoyens à se battre et promet des armes
text: Following the military offensive led by Russia against Ukraine, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called on citizens to fight and promised weapons. Mr. Zelensky also urged Ukrainians to donate their blood in hospitals for the wounded soldiers.
lead: In a progress report released on February 24, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky calls on citizens to fight, donate their blood, and promises to provide them with weapons against the Russian offensive.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : la Chine rechigne à endosser le rôle de médiateur
text: China does not currently intend to play a leading role in resolving what it continues to call "the Ukrainian crisis." This is evidenced by the - lengthy - statement published by Beijing after the telephone conversation that President Xi Jinping had on Tuesday, March 8, with French President Emmanuel Macron and German Chancellor Olaf Scholz. Xi had only discussed the issue with one foreign leader before: Vladimir Putin, on February 25, the day after the start of the "special military operation," as Moscow refers to the war it is currently waging in Ukraine. Two phone calls in thirteen days and none for the Ukrainian president. Meanwhile, U.S. President Joe Biden has had seven phone conversations with European leaders and met with Finnish President Sauli Niinistö at the White House. According to the statement, Xi "deeply regrets the return of war to Europe." China has offered its assistance, but it largely relies on "humanitarian corridors" which are often, according to Europeans, a trap set by Vladimir Putin for civilian populations. Xi Jinping reiterated China's consistent position since the beginning of the conflict: "the sovereignty and territorial integrity of all countries must be respected" - without condemning the invasion of Ukraine - and "the legitimate security concerns of all countries must be taken seriously." While "China appreciates the mediation efforts made by France and Germany," it clearly does not intend to take the lead, as confirmed by the statement from the Elysée: "President Xi expressed his support for the actions of France and Germany for a ceasefire." China is even less inclined to take a prominent role as it disagrees on several points with the Europeans. For example, it believes it is "necessary to call for the utmost restraint to avoid a major humanitarian crisis," indirectly criticizing Western arms deliveries to Ukraine. More explicitly, Beijing is concerned about the consequences of Western sanctions, without questioning the war, as they "will have impacts on the stability of finance, energy, transportation, and supply chains worldwide and will weigh heavily on the global economy (...) which is not beneficial for anyone," the statement continues. "Too early" to commit For China, Europe must "pursue its strategic autonomy and promote a balanced, effective, and sustainable European security framework." How? "By conducting an equal dialogue between the European Union, Russia, the United States, and NATO," Xi believes. In Beijing, European diplomats are not surprised by China's reservations. "It is too early, China will only engage when it is sure of achieving a positive outcome and when it will be at the center of the photo," says an ambassador from Southern Europe. "At the moment, this conflict only produces losers: Russia, which is becoming a pariah state, Europe, which shows its fragility, and the United States, which are no longer definitively the world's policemen. In comparison, China loses much less. Maybe a little growth. So, it is the winner," judges another diplomat. "The Balkans, Georgia, Crimea, Syria... On all fronts, it sees the retreat of the West," laments a third. Mentioned a few days ago by Josep Borrell, the High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs, the idea of Chinese mediation is not unanimous in Europe. MEP Reinhard Bütikofer (Greens, under Chinese sanctions due to his actions against the repression of the Uyghur minority) considers Beijing to be too close to Moscow. For others, this is not really a disadvantage. "China precisely plays the role of mediator when it is close to one of the parties but judges that this party is going too far," explains researcher Andrew Small of the German Marshall Fund on Twitter. In this case, China "plays the role of the big brother, delivers difficult messages to its partners, conditions the support it provides them, and negotiates behind their backs." But there is no indication that China is ready to do the same towards Moscow, its strategic partner. On the contrary. Washington and Beijing only issued brief statements following the telephone conversation on March 5 between Secretary of State Anthony Blinken and Foreign Minister Wang Yi. "It went badly," decrypts a Western diplomat in Beijing. On Monday, March 7, during his annual press conference, Wang Yi even blamed the United States for almost all international tensions. In this context, Andrew Small even warns those who expect too much from China: "The worst thing would be if China conditions a mediation role on an improvement in relations (...) and obtains concessions without any real return." Is it a coincidence? The Chinese statement begins with a long paragraph on the "green and digital partnerships" that China and the European Union should pursue.
lead: The Chinese government, which does not want to take the spotlight, is concerned about the impact of arms deliveries to Kiev as well as the sanctions imposed on Moscow.

title: Les souffrances et la détermination de Viktoria Godzenko, mère de soldat ukrainien sur le front
text: Viktoria Godzenko stands upright, as if on springs, in the living room of her modest three-room apartment not far from the center of Kiev, on Monday, February 14. At 58 years old, she has maintained the posture of a dancer, long black hair, and an unwavering determination to fight against the invader. The war against Russia has already taken her husband, who fell on the front lines of Donbass in 2016. Today, it is her eldest son who is risking his life on the front lines. "Of course, I suffer like any mother knowing her child is in danger. He doesn't tell me in detail what he does, he probably isn't allowed to, but I know he is on the front lines, facing the enemy. I only have real conversations with him when he comes home on leave." Viktoria Godzenko eagerly awaits October 2022, when her son Alexei, 30 years old, will complete his commitment. "He volunteered for three years and - thank God - has never been injured. So many of his friends have died... You know, I don't wring my hands thinking: how could I let him go to war? First of all, he is an adult man. Secondly, we raised our children this way. A tragedy has befallen our country. We must resist and defend ourselves, it is a duty. I cannot ask my son not to defend his country! It is a matter of honesty and justice." Viktoria Godzenko was born in western Ukraine, in Lviv, into a family of Russian-speaking military personnel. She is quick to point out that "language has never been a problem, contrary to the stereotypes [propagated by Moscow]. Russian speakers do not suffer from any discrimination. My mother tongue is Russian, and I speak Ukrainian perfectly." When the family moved to the capital, Kiev, in 2000, Russian was still spoken at home. "But from the 'Orange Revolution' [uprising against the fraudulent victory of a pro-Russian candidate in the 2004-2005 presidential election], Alexei, who has a revolutionary nature, decided to speak only Ukrainian, even at home. But I still respond to him in Russian," his mother jokes. "Defending democracy" In 2014, the second wave of Ukrainian patriotism, Maidan Square, deeply affected the Godzenko family. "We were shocked by the police violence against the young student protesters. We forbade Ivan [the younger son, then 15 years old] from joining Maidan, but my husband, Alexei, and I participated, like half of Kiev. We were convinced that we had to defend democracy and continue Ukraine's integration into Europe. I couldn't just sit and passively watch television." This was followed by the annexation of Crimea and the occupation of part of Donbass by military forces controlled from Moscow. "I remember that Alexei and his father debated at length about which of the two should enlist. In the end, my husband, Dmitro, decided that he would go. He wanted to spare his son from the war and fulfill his duty as a defender of the homeland," recalls Viktoria Godzenko. A senior engineer at the state-owned company Naftogaz [a Ukrainian equivalent of Engie], Dmitro Godzenko decided to put his flourishing professional career on hold to enlist as a simple soldier. He signed up for a year and a half in 2015 and was deployed north of the city of Gorlovka, in an area where fighting was intense. "At that time, our army was not equipped like it is today. My husband was a big man, over 2 meters tall and wearing size 46 shoes. No uniform fit him. He fought for a long time in sneakers before friends brought him firefighter boots from Germany in his size." Injured by shrapnel from shells, two of which remained lodged near his spine because they were inoperable, he returned to the front two months later despite his wife's pleas. "I did everything I could, but his determination was unshakable. Dmitro always wanted to follow through on his commitments because that was his character." The culprit? "Putin, of course!" Dmitro Godzenko was killed by a mortar shell at 6 a.m. on the very last day of his one-and-a-half-year contract. "I was waiting for him at home the next day. Everything was already prepared to welcome him, his favorite dishes. It was Alexei who first learned the news. A phone call woke him up in the morning. He dismissed the first bearer of bad news. Someone else called him back immediately to confirm. He came out of his room and passed by me saying, 'Dima [short for Dmitro] is dead,' without looking at me. I, not understanding, asked him what he wanted for breakfast. I was so happy about my husband's return, I didn't hear what he was saying, it didn't register. Then he repeated, 'Dima is dead.' I still didn't understand: 'Which Dima?' Because it wasn't possible, he was supposed to arrive the next day. At that moment, my son fell silent. And the news hit me." A stage manager in events, Viktoria Godzenko fell into psychiatric problems and anxiety disorders from which she only managed to fully recover after five years. Now retired, she helps widows or grieving mothers and volunteers at the archives of a military recruitment center. Who is responsible for her suffering? "Putin, of course!" Viktoria Godzenko exclaims without hesitation. "Not alone, of course, I simplify. It's Russia. But I am certain that there are still many normal, reasonable people in Russia who understand the situation. I also know that Russian propaganda works with great efficiency." Like the vast majority of the residents of Kiev, Viktoria Godzenko shows no signs of anxiety in the face of the possibility of a massive attack by the Russian army. "If they attack, it will be from all sides at the same time. But I am convinced that we can resist their offensive. It will not be a walk in the park for them. We are motivated because we are defending our territory, and we have been ready for this for a long time. Putin is ready to shed the blood of the civilian population. He has no qualms about massacring anyone, not just Ukrainians. Perhaps that is why we are finally receiving support from the United States and Europe. Arms deliveries are a different story from the empty words of concern from the West that we were served until now! I think this change has made Putin think." Certainly, but to think is not to yield.
lead: At 58 years old, the war against Russia has already taken her husband, who fell on the front lines of Donbass in 2016. Today, it is her eldest son who is risking his life on the front lines.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : à Mykolaïv, la colère des habitants sous les bombes
text: Fourteen days after the start of the war, Mykolaiv is still blocking the Russian offensive on the road to Odessa. Backed by the Southern Bug River in southern Ukraine, this industrial city of half a million inhabitants has repelled dozens of assaults since February 24 and endured just as many bombardments. Wednesday morning, residents woke up to a second relatively calm night, while rumors of an imminent assault from the north, east, and south are on everyone's lips. "I'm tired of sleeping fully dressed! All night, we are waiting for a Russian guest," complains Tatiana Zakharova, 58, referring to the projectiles fired from the Russian positions a few kilometers away. Modestly dressed, with a plastic bag of provisions in each hand, she chats with her neighbors at the foot of her building, 134 Cosmonauts Street. Monday morning, a "Russian guest" had already shown up in the square formed by the buildings, shattering most of the windows, breaking down walls, and riddling the parked vehicles on the street with holes. "It wasn't just one explosion, but at least a dozen," says a neighbor. "It's a Smerch," Vadim believes, inspecting his damaged van. "A cluster munition rocket, that's why we heard many explosions. Look at this hole in the ground [about a meter in diameter], if it had been a Grad [rockets fired in salvos, widely used by both sides], the hole would have been much bigger." According to residents, no one was killed in this bombing, which occurred at 5 a.m. But the incident traumatized Tatiana Zakharova and triggered a verbal outpouring. "Why? But why are they shooting at us? I don't understand. You know, I've always respected Vladimir Putin," she confides, a little apart from her neighbors. "He straightened out his country and led it with a strong hand. I had a good opinion of him. I don't understand why Putin is sending his army here to massacre us. Did I ask Putin for anything, for him to come save me? No. Did I ask if I wanted to go to Russia? No. Does the Russian people want me to come to Russia? No. And no one wants Russia to come here. This is our country, it's our choice, whether Putin likes it or not," she exclaims, without pausing for breath. A gaping hole. Tears well up in her eyes. "I have lived my whole life in Ukraine, while speaking Russian, I am Ukrainian myself," she continues. "But I speak Russian, like most people here in Mykolaiv. No one is attacking us because we speak the Russian language. My husband is Russian, my father is Belarusian, so what? Why do the Russians want to annihilate us? What have we done to deserve living like rats?" She nods toward her staircase. "The most frightening thing for me is these Russian army officers that I know and who live in Moscow. Their parents live here, in Mykolaiv, in this building, they are 80 years old. I have known them since they were little. These people, they shoot at us even though they have their own parents here, under the bombs. How can one understand such a thing? Why do they treat us like this? The Russians may hate us, fine. But to bomb their own parents! Putin is massacring us, but he is also sending thousands of young Russians to their deaths. I feel sorry for them too." Artillery fire can be heard in the distance. No one seems to pay attention to it. On Cosmonauts Street, like in Mykolaiv in general, you see mostly elderly people moving between grocery stores and their homes. Endless lines of cars and buses filled with children congest the road to Odessa, but those who remain, probably still the majority, take the same stance as Tatiana Zakharova: "I stay here because I have nowhere else to go. At my age, leaving on the roads with my diabetic husband... it's beyond my strength." Her apartment has been damaged by the explosions, two windows have come out of their frames. Plastic films have been hastily fixed to the broken windows awaiting repair. The temperature hovers around 0°C these days, and snowfalls are frequent in the region. In the communal kitchen of her building, a "obchejitié" (workers' hostel), Vitali Komar, 76, watches Cosmonauts Street. Through the gaping hole made by a Russian rocket. "Of course, it's Putin's fault," he explains in a dispassionate voice. "This guy has been in power for too long. At first, he was normal, but now, no one wants him anymore. I liked him at first, but I quickly changed my mind." He grimaces at the piercing sound of an electric saw. Municipal workers are busy on the facade of the building making emergency repairs. A former truck driver, Vitali has been to Russia many times. He gestures wearily with his arm: "They don't like us much there. I've been called all sorts of names because I'm Ukrainian. The Russians look down on us," he says with a shrug. Mischievous air. This thin man, who lives alone in a room in the hostel, is not very optimistic about the military prospects. "If you [the Westerners] don't close the sky, Putin will keep shooting at us until the last one." He refers to the debate on the aerial ban on Russian planes by NATO armies, which Kiev has been demanding for several days. "Putin will destroy the [Ukrainian] nuclear power plants. But you don't act because you're afraid he'll press the red button." The only consolation for Vitali: "It seems that the Russian soldiers are fleeing and crying when caught, claiming they were unaware of their mission to invade Ukraine," he sneers. The former driver rants against the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, "who spent all the money on building new roads instead of rearming the country." The same complaint is heard at Emergency Care Center No. 3 in Mykolaiv. "Close the sky, it's the only way to stop this fascist," exclaims 43-year-old Ioulia Krioukova, referring to the Russian president. "The Russians are bombing us with banned cluster bombs and the world is just watching," laments this employee of the Iounost concert hall, slightly injured the day before. "Our guard was killed during the bombing. The missile fell on a children's playground! They bombed at 5 in the morning, like fascists. They attack at dawn, that's their rule." She suddenly takes on a mischievous look: "In the evening, we go to bed in pajamas, because we trust the Ukrainian army! This phrase has become a saying here." Like all the people interviewed by Le Monde, Ioulia Krioukova speaks in Russian, but she adds: "We are mostly Russian speakers here, but now we all want to speak Ukrainian!" This combative attitude, widely shared, has come at a high cost for Viktor Sokol, a 22-year-old man of sturdy build lying in a bed. His mother, Lioudmila, recounts in a blank voice how her son was seriously injured in the head on March 1st. "He is part of the territorial defense [a branch of the Ukrainian army made up of volunteers]. He threw a Molotov cocktail at a Russian armored vehicle. He ran but couldn't get to safety in time. Something [likely shrapnel from a grenade, according to the doctor] hit him in the temple. Shrapnel entered his skull and is now in his brain," she says through tears. "He is in critical condition. Please make sure he doesn't fall into the hands of the Russians!"
lead: The Russian-speaking city in southern Ukraine is a strategic point for Russian troops on the road to Odessa. Its residents have repelled dozens of offensives since the beginning of the war.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : l’étau russe se resserre autour de Kiev
text: As they discover the extent of the Russian military assault on their country and as columns of tanks inexorably approach Kiev, the inhabitants of the Ukrainian capital have gone from astonishment to fear. Even though they are not giving in to panic, more and more people are trying to leave the city as the hours pass. Even though the war was predicted and announced, it caught the people of Kiev by surprise. "I think that apart from those directly involved in this conflict, no one, during the first hours, believed it... Waking up and learning that Russian missiles were falling on Kiev, I was in disbelief. And I don't know a single friend, neighbor, or colleague who wasn't," says Vladimir, whose family, originally from the Donbass region in the East of the country, is accustomed to the sounds of war. While some residents made a point of moving around the city, others sought shelter in underground bunkers during each air raid alert, and many simply stayed at home waiting for news from the front, thousands of people left for the west. After the columns of cars heading towards the city of Lviv or Poland, the railway and bus stations were overwhelmed. For now, trains and buses are still running. Growing concern among the population In front of the ticket counters at the train station, the people of Kiev sometimes wait for hours before being able to buy a ticket. They are calm. They generally carry few belongings, just a bag or a suitcase, as if they planned to return home quickly once the conflict is over. Some only go to the countryside to a family home, wishing to avoid these days of bombings, while others take trains to Poland without knowing when they will see Kiev again. At the bus station, calm also prevails, except in front of the bus to Lviv. This city in the West, where families connected to the government and foreign embassies have already taken refuge, is the most sought after. It is from there that they will wait to see if Russia will halt its offensive or, on the contrary, if it will invade and occupy the country entirely. It is also from there that it will be easier for them to quickly reach Poland. Musician Ratmir Beloded waits in the parking lot, his guitar in hand. Before his eyes, groups of Ukrainians are grappling with foreign workers and migrants to board the bus. The composer and DJ recounts how his life has changed. "Last night, I finally finished an EP I had been working on for a year. It should be called Death Ambient. I finished my work around 4 a.m. and thought I would finally rest for a few days. An hour later, it was war." Seeking a less crowded bus, Ratmir Beloded says he has "no intention of waiting for the Russians, who may be here as soon as tomorrow." After Lviv, he will continue his journey to Frankfurt, Germany. Despite government statements about the strength of the armed resistance of Ukrainian forces, the population's concern has continued to grow. The speed of the Russian advance, however, leads residents to believe that tanks could appear at any moment. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky did not hide the difficulties in a video message broadcast on the night of Thursday, February 24, to Friday, February 25, following the first day of war. Referring to the "heroism" of Ukraine's defenders and reporting a preliminary toll of victims (137 dead and 316 wounded civilians and military personnel), he warned that "sabotage groups" of Russian special forces may already be infiltrated in Kiev. While designating himself as "their number one target" to "destroy the head of state," he promised not to flee and to "stay in the capital." Arms distribution Mr. Zelensky also indicated that he had signed a decree ordering the general mobilization of conscripts and reservists aged 18 to 60. The government has reportedly already distributed ten thousand automatic weapons in anticipation of urban combat in Kiev, beyond the ranks of the regular army. The Ukrainian president, who is speaking truthfully to the population in this crisis, regretted that Ukraine is "alone" in this struggle against "one of the most powerful armies in the world." "A new Iron Curtain has fallen and separates Russia from the civilized world," added Volodymyr Zelensky. "This is a mistake. It will not be forgiven." As the curfew came into effect with the imposition of martial law, many residents went to sleep in underground shelters. Many are also seeking refuge in metro stations, meant to withstand the worst bombings. After a few hours of respite, the air raids resumed just before dawn on Friday at 4 a.m. While a plane or drone was shot down in the capital's sky and crashed into a building, explosions were heard in the outskirts. Between the armored columns advancing from the north and east and the missiles raining down on the city, the noose is tightening around Kiev.
lead: At the end of the first day of war, the residents went from astonishment to fear, now fearing the arrival of Russian tanks.

title: Les services de renseignement attentifs au départ de volontaires français pour l’Ukraine
text: Former military personnel, idealists, students, adventurers: who are the French people eager to join the Ukrainian army? And most importantly, how many of them are there? Since the call for foreign volunteers launched on February 28 by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, thousands of them have subscribed to hastily created Facebook pages to connect potential candidates for departure. The most frequently cited motivations revolve around the desire to assist the Ukrainian people, whose unexpected level of resistance against an army presented as one of the most powerful in the world has inspired many. However, their exact number remains difficult to accurately assess for intelligence services, notably the General Directorate for Internal Security (DGSI), which primarily focuses on targeted profiles, such as political activists or individuals most determined to join the theater of operations. The latter are estimated to be between 60 and 70, with a majority being sympathizers of the far-right. Among them, a small group of about ten, identified, have already fought in the Donbass region. "They particularly catch our attention," says a source from the intelligence services, "given the involvement in the conflict of the Azov Battalion, which seems to exert a certain fascination on a fringe of the French far-right." Established in 2014 to combat separatist tendencies in the eastern part of the country, this openly neo-Nazi and white supremacist unit has eventually become an official force and is currently attached to the National Guard. This institutionalization fuels accusations made by Russian authorities against the alleged "neo-Nazism" of the Ukrainian regime. Post-war concerns Despite the Ministry of Foreign Affairs reminding that "it is formally advised against traveling to Ukraine until further notice," it is legally impossible to prevent citizens from freely moving, even when the desire to bear arms in a foreign conflict seems evident. Moreover, nothing prohibits these French nationals from participating in a conflict outside the national borders, except for active military personnel, who are subject to an obvious prohibition in principle. While the provisions of the April 14, 2003 law on the repression of mercenarism prohibit any individual who is not a member of a party to the conflict from participating in military operations, they do not apply to "members of the armed forces of that State." However, the Ukrainian authorities incorporate foreign volunteers into their own army, within an "international legion." This incorporation effectively turns the soldiers into members of a regular army and not militiamen, members of paramilitary groups without official status, or mercenaries motivated by financial gain, to whom the prohibition of the 2003 law would fully apply. Specialist in military law, Elodie Maumont, considers that the involvement of French citizens in the Ukrainian army "does not pose any immediate difficulties if it is done within the legal framework of the host country, with a contract similar to that signed by foreigners enlisting in the French Foreign Legion." The lawyer especially notes the "low probability of seeing a prosecutor, who has sole discretion over whether to prosecute, take action against French citizens solely for their departure to Ukraine, even within combat units, based on current laws." Beyond the risks of French citizens dying and the diplomatic implications of their involvement in combat between two third-party countries, intelligence services are particularly concerned about the post-war period: the return to France of veterans now experienced in handling weapons, individuals suffering from post-traumatic syndromes, or those who may have been involved in war crimes. A military source emphasizes that this possibility is always conceivable in times of conflict, especially concerning individuals who are inexperienced and completely unprepared to live under the physical and moral conditions imposed by the life of a combatant during wartime.
lead: According to the DGSI, between 60 and 70 people, mostly from the far right, are determined to join the ranks of the Ukrainian army.

title: La guerre en Ukraine divise le monde des cryptomonnaies
text: It is a major peak, the most significant in a long time: the invasion of Ukraine and the economic sanctions imposed on Russia and Belarus have led to significant volumes of cryptocurrency purchases paid in rubles, as shown by the data compiled by the specialized company Kaiko, based in Paris. On February 24, bitcoin purchases reached a peak of 1.5 billion rubles (approximately 20 million euros, at the exchange rate of that day) on the two main exchange platforms accepting the Russian currency, just before its value collapsed. The data details suggest that these movements are mainly "the work of small investors, with some traders placing larger orders," explains Clara Medalie, an analyst for Kaiko. Russian buyers, "seeking a safe haven," have also invested more heavily in Tether, a "stablecoin," which is a cryptocurrency known to be more stable than "the highly volatile bitcoin," notes Clara Medalie. Russians are not the only ones buying more cryptocurrencies than usual. In Ukraine as well, bitcoin purchases are on the rise, and globally, the cryptocurrency price has exceeded 40,000 euros, well below its historical high of nearly 60,000 euros in November, but showing a clear increase. Cryptocurrencies are also being used by the Ukrainian government since February 26 to directly collect donations. The equivalent of just over 20 million euros was transferred on Tuesday, March 1, to various accounts generated by the Ukrainian government, which thanked the "crypto community as we fight for our freedom." Heated debates On the other hand, a request from the Ukrainian government has sparked heated debates - and divisions - among cryptocurrency advocates: on Sunday, Deputy Prime Minister Mykhailo Fedorov called on major exchange platforms to join the global economic sanctions movement against Russia and freeze the accounts of Russian nationals. These platforms allow the conversion of cryptocurrencies into traditional currencies and are a vital access point for being able to "spend" them. The main exchange platforms rejected the Ukrainian government's request on Monday. Most of them, like the global leader Binance, stated that they could not "unilaterally freeze the accounts of millions of innocent people." For now, there are no sanctions against Russia and Belarus concerning cryptocurrencies or exchange platforms, which emphasize that they comply with current laws. "Furthermore, if we were to freeze the accounts of nationals from countries that attack others and provoke violence in the world, the first step would be to block American accounts," writes Kraken CEO Jesse Powell in the midst of a series of lyrical and provocative messages. In the cryptocurrency community, whose followers predominantly share libertarian ideas emphasizing individual freedom, there are very few voices supporting Vladimir Putin and the invasion of Ukraine. However, many believe that the freedom to control one's own money takes precedence, in principle, over everything else; on social media, sometimes heated debates pit proponents of "freedom" against those who argue, with images of the conflict as evidence, that the "principles" of free flow of money count for little in the face of the suffering of Ukrainian civilians. On Reddit, which hosts one of the largest English-language cryptocurrency discussion spaces (4.5 million subscribers), Binance's decision is overwhelmingly welcomed. The main threads of discussion defend the refusal of exchange platforms to block Russian and Belarusian accounts. One argument recurs frequently: "An entire country cannot be held responsible for the actions of its government." "If you are in favor of a blockade, you have no damn clue what cryptos are. Cryptos = freedom. (...) P.S.: Screw Putin," argues one of Tuesday's most popular posts. The very nature of cryptocurrencies in question These off-the-cuff arguments – which sometimes seem to ignore all the other sanctions already in place against Russia and Belarus – are often contradicted by other enthusiasts who point out that cryptocurrencies and exchange platforms are not the same thing. "Cryptos are decentralized, exchanges are not," summarizes one of them. Faced with very libertarian stances emphasizing the "freedom" of cryptocurrency users, others remind that "cryptocurrencies are just a means to send payments from one person to another without going through financial institutions. That's literally what the first sentence of the Whitepaper [bitcoin's founding document] says. It doesn't talk about 'freedom,' 'sovereignty,' or 'war'." "Satoshi [Nakamoto, pseudonym of the bitcoin creator] never mentioned things like freedom or sovereignty in his text," adds another commentator. "People have simply read too much into what he was writing and make him say things that go far beyond his original text." Vitalik Buterin, on the other hand, made sure his message was very clear: The co-founder of Ethereum, the second most popular cryptocurrency after Bitcoin, was born in Moscow, lives in Canada, and has been very explicit about his stance on the invasion of Ukraine. "It is a crime against the Ukrainian and Russian peoples. I wish you to stay safe, but I know there will be no safety. Glory to Ukraine," he wrote on February 24th. Just a reminder: Ethereum is neutral. Not me.
lead: The main exchange platforms have refused to block access to Russian citizens, as requested by the Ukrainian government.

title: Le pétrole kazakh, manne sous surveillance russe
text: Suddenly, the autumnal torpor of Astana, the Kazakh capital battered by the winds of the steppes, gives way to a disconcerting security frenzy. On this Wednesday, October 12th, thousands of police officers are stationed every 100 meters along the main avenues. Entire streets are closed to traffic. Vehicles are randomly searched around the airport. Schoolchildren are asked to stay at home. The reason: the city is hosting a series of regional summits until the weekend, including a meeting between the Russian President, Vladimir Putin, and his Turkish counterpart, Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Their host, the Kazakh President, Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, displays the diplomatic smile required. Nothing on his face hints at the delicate line he is walking. "He does not support the war in Ukraine and is increasingly distancing himself from Moscow, but he absolutely cannot afford to upset Putin," summarizes Maximilian Hess, a specialist in Eurasia at the Foreign Policy Research Institute, an American think tank. And for good reason: the Kazakh economy is highly dependent on Russia - it accounts for 11.5% of exports and 42.1% of imports - with the two countries sharing a 7,500-kilometer border to the north. Moreover, 80% of the oil that Kazakhstan exports passes through Russian territory to reach the port of Novorossiysk on the Black Sea via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), in which Moscow holds a 31% stake. This pipeline is also vital for the European Union (EU): the oil that flows through it represents 56% of Romania's black gold imports, 25.6% of Italy's, 15.2% of France's, and 8% of the Netherlands'. Russia does not hesitate to leverage this dependence. Since the start of the war in Ukraine on February 24th, it has already suspended the delivery of Kazakh oil via the CPC four times. Officially, for various maintenance reasons. In reality, to remind Astana to fall in line whenever it asserts its independence from Moscow too strongly. "Blackmail" On June 19th, transit was interrupted for several days in response to the St. Petersburg summit: Tokayev had stated, in front of Putin, that he did not recognize the separatist republics of Luhansk and Donetsk. On July 6th, there was another cutoff: two days earlier, Kazakhstan had expressed readiness to send more oil to the EU. "In some ways, this is reminiscent of the blackmail imposed on Europe around the Nord Stream gas pipelines," notes Lucas Anceschi, an expert on Eurasia at the University of Glasgow (Scotland). And this comes at a cost to the former Soviet Republic: black gold accounts for 60% of its exports and 40% of its budget revenues. "A month of pipeline suspension means one less percentage point of GDP for the year," calculate experts at the World Bank. "A senior Kazakh official fears that if Russia were to cut it off permanently, our economy would collapse. The Kazakh oil sector mainly developed after the country's independence in 1991. To understand what is at stake for this Central Asian country, a look at a map of the region is necessary. Its territory is vast – five times the size of France – and its population is small (19 million inhabitants). Above all, 'it has no access to a sea other than the Caspian, and is sandwiched between Russia and China, placing it, despite itself, at the heart of the geopolitical maelstrom between these two powers,' explains Agathe Demarais, director of global forecasting at the Economist Intelligence Unit, the independent research center of The Economist magazine. The Kazakh oil sector mainly developed after independence in 1991. To exploit its main deposits in Tengiz, Karachaganak, and Kashagan, it called upon foreign companies in joint ventures with a state company – Chevron, ExxonMobil, ENI, or Total. 'Frustration among oil workers.' The problem lies in limited access routes to the country due to its geography. In addition to the CPC, a pipeline with limited capacity supplies China. 'For Europe, the other option is shipping by boat across the Caspian to connect to the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline, passing through Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Turkey,' details Michaël Levystone, a research associate at the French Institute of International Relations' Russia/NEI Center. Or through the pipeline connecting Baku to the Georgian port of Supsa. In both cases, a much more expensive route that, for now, is not feasible on a large scale due to insufficient infrastructure. 'It will take a few years of investment before we can seriously consider it,' admits the senior Kazakh official mentioned earlier. This has not prevented Mr. Tokayev from getting closer to Mr. Erdogan in recent months to develop this export route. All while continuing the 'multi-vector' foreign policy deployed by the country for years: maintaining good relations with everyone (Russia, China, Europe, United States) in a delicate and precarious balance... Especially precarious as the domestic situation is also complex. 'The population's frustration is constantly growing, especially among oil workers who resent seeing oil money sucked up by the capital without benefiting from it,' points out Aina Shormanbayeva, founder of a local human rights NGO. 'Popular protests could erupt at any moment.' This was the case in January, in response to rising fuel prices. To suppress the protests, partly infiltrated by violent groups, Mr. Tokayev called for help... from Russia, without it necessarily indicating a closer relationship with Moscow." "In Central Asia, everything is of great complexity," notes Matt Gertken, a geopolitical expert at BCA Research, a consulting firm. Before concluding: "For all these reasons, Europe would be wrong to consider Kazakh oil as a completely reliable resource."
lead: Four-fifths of the black gold that Astana exports passes through Russian territory to reach the Black Sea, via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, of which Moscow is a 31% shareholder. This pipeline is vital for the European Union.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : la théorie de la mise en scène, l’argument très stratégique des prorusses
text: According to those who spread these rumors, nothing would be true. The woman with a swollen face, featured on the front page of the newspaper Libération on October 11th? An extra, made up for the photo. The speeches of Volodymyr Zelensky? Sequences filmed in a studio, against a green screen. As for the videos portraying Russian soldiers as aggressors, it would be Hollywood cinema, in scenes filmed by the American director Sean Penn, believe the most conspiratorial. Since the beginning of the invasion in Ukraine, the Kremlin and its supporters in the French-speaking conspiracy sphere have been contesting the authenticity of images that embarrass them, as if Kiev was behind a massive operation to manipulate reality. Each time, this theory is based on documents taken out of context. A video was shared this fall to prove that the alleged massacres in Ukraine are fictitious. In fact, one of the "corpses" lying under tarps briefly lifts his arm. In reality, it was a performance to raise awareness about the risks of climate change, filmed by Austrian television. The excerpt had already been misused by the conspiracy sphere to denounce the alleged fake deaths of Covid-19 in early 2022. The "comedian-president" In this war that is also fought on the communication front, the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, has the particularity of having been a professional actor, and even played the role of a president in the series "Servant of the People" before taking on the role in real life. Shortly after his election in 2019, Vladimir Putin joked about his professional skills: "It seems that he is a good specialist in his field. But it's one thing to play a role, it's another to be someone." The Russian president continues to nickname Volodymyr Zelensky "the comedian," accusing him of duplicity and cheating. A rhetoric echoed by the French conspiracist Silvano Trotta, a relay of Kremlin propaganda, who claims, regarding the Ukrainians: "In addition to being Nazis, they are also manipulators. Their president being an actor, they know how to do it." On October 11th, a well-known Twitter account of far-right conspiracy theorists published a video of Volodymyr Zelensky in a recording studio, stating that all "videos of #Zelensky outdoors in Kiev are fake. In reality, he films behind a green screen in Poland. He sends hundreds of thousands of people to the slaughterhouse but he hides quietly in Hollywood." Not only does he present a very personal sense of geography, but above all, he forgets to specify that the sequence shows the preparation of a speech given simultaneously and in hologram by the Ukrainian president in four rooms dedicated to new technologies, including VivaTech, in Paris, on June 16 - and absolutely not his video clips in the streets of Kiev. Conversely, in March 2020, Marine Le Pen's former right-hand man, Florian Philippot, was outraged by a video where Volodymyr Zelensky was shooting at Russian deputies - without noticing, or wanting to notice, that the excerpt in question came from a trailer of his series in 2017: for once, it was indeed a fiction for television. Turning massacres into movie scenes It is of course possible that some images are indeed staged. For example, at the beginning of October, the overly perfect video of a Russian armored vehicle and its occupants surrendering without resistance raised more than one eyebrow. But the Kremlin and its supporters not only denounce suspicious images when they exist. They systematically cast doubt on all images embarrassing for Moscow, even when the proof of their veracity has been provided. Denial was total when hundreds of civilian bodies were discovered in Bucha in April. Pro-Russian circles immediately multiplied theories accrediting a staged event: the dead would have been placed by the army or by the Ukrainian police, or they were extras, or even mannequins... This kind of argument had already been used during the war in Syria. In 2017, China claimed that the viral images of little Omran, a Syrian child rescued from the rubble of Aleppo, photographed dazed and covered in dust, were staged by the rescuers. In 2018, based on manipulated images from the filming of a pro-regime feature film, a chemical attack on a neighborhood in Ghouta was described by Russia as a "staged scene in front of the cameras" by the White Helmets, a Syrian rescue organization. A strategy of "desensitization" "This type of disinformation is produced as part of the aggressor's strategy, known in English as Darvo [acronym for "deny, attack and reverse victim and offender"], to attack and discredit victims of violence," explained Stephanie Lamy to Le Monde in April, author of "Agora toxica, civil society in the Internet age" (Editions du détour, 2022). What may seem like grotesque and unworthy gestures is part of a real strategy. "The use of falsehoods and lies has been carefully thought out," says Dimitri Minic, a researcher at the French Institute of International Relations and a specialist in Russian strategic culture. Logical tricks, the inversion of cause and effect, the manipulation of intentions, all of this has been theorized by the Russian army to deceive and even reformat the opponent during psychological-informational confrontation. Russian disinformation does not make war unreal, but seeks to make Ukrainian victims less credible to the international public opinion. "It is a way to cut off any possibility of identification or empathy, a process of dehumanization, even desensitization," analyzes Marie Peltier, author of Obsession: Inside the Conspiracy Theory Narrative (Inculte editions, 2018). The goal is to prevent us from being affected by what is happening."
lead: Ukrainians allegedly disguised as injured for photos, massacres that would be scenes for the cinema, a president filmed on a green screen... The Kremlin's propaganda uses all the tricks to deny images considered embarrassing.

title: Italie : Berlusconi dit avoir « renoué » avec Poutine et attaque Zelensky ; malaise dans la coalition des droites
text: In Italy, former Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi received a lot of criticism on Wednesday, October 19, after expressing a desire to "reconnect" with Russian President Vladimir Putin and blaming Kiev for the invasion of Ukraine. The Italian press widely reported on remarks made by Silvio Berlusconi to members of his party, Forza Italia (right-wing), which were leaked despite initial denials from his circle. The likely future prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, was described as being "hostage to pro-Russians." An audio recording was later released, causing astonishment among party leaders, including Giorgia Meloni of the post-fascist party Fratelli d'Italia, who is working to form a government with coalition allies Forza Italia and Matteo Salvini's League (far-right). Berlusconi, who celebrated his 86th birthday on September 29, stated, "I have reconnected a bit with President Putin, quite a bit, in the sense that for my birthday, Putin sent me twenty bottles of vodka and a very kind letter. I responded by sending him bottles of Lambrusco and a very kind letter. He told me I was one of his five true friends." Forza Italia promptly issued a statement clarifying the party's and its leader's position on Russia, stating it is "in line with that of Europe and the United States." The left-wing opposition strongly criticized the statements, with former Prime Minister Enrico Letta denouncing the shift towards a more ambiguous stance towards Russia by the new majority. The declarations by Berlusconi could jeopardize the position of Antonio Tajani, a close ally and pro-European, who was being considered for the position of Minister of Foreign Affairs. Meloni's circle tried to downplay the situation, with Fratelli d'Italia deputy Francesco Lollobrigida stating, "I would avoid involving Tajani." However, according to sources within Fratelli d'Italia cited by La Stampa, "Berlusconi might have killed Tajani." During the election campaign, Berlusconi had already sparked outrage by claiming that Putin had wanted to overthrow the Kiev government to replace it with "good people." In a further leaked audio recording on Wednesday evening, Berlusconi appears to shift the blame for the war onto Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. "In 2014 in Minsk, Belarus, an agreement was signed between Ukraine and the two newly established republics in Donbass for a peace agreement where no one attacks anyone. A year later, Ukraine disregarded this agreement and began attacking the borders of the two republics," Silvio Berlusconi told his lawmakers. He mentioned "five, six, seven thousand deaths" in these conflicts, and then added, "Zelensky arrives and triples the attacks against the two republics," later reiterating his statements from the end of September that Mr. Putin had been "pushed" by his population and entourage to invade Ukraine. Faced with the backlash from these recent statements, Silvio Berlusconi clarified in a statement that his "personal position and that of Forza Italia do not deviate from that of the Italian government, the European Union, the Atlantic Alliance, neither on the crisis in Ukraine nor on other major international policy issues."
lead: Her remarks have caused astonishment for Giorgia Meloni, the leader of the post-fascist party Fratelli d’Italia, who is working to form a government with Forza Italia and the League, the respective parties of Silvio Berlusconi and Matteo Salvini.

title: « L’idée que la France puisse être un médiateur dans le conflit ukrainien est une pure illusion »
text: In the Russo-Ukrainian war, France has chosen a shaky position by attempting to reconcile two contradictory approaches. On one hand, President Macron calls for dialogue with Moscow and negotiation, in line with the European tradition of balancing powers to counterbalance American influence on the continent. The desire to appease Russia is also fueled by a pro-Russian stance, predominant on the far right and among the Republicans, who argue that humiliating Russia would be an unforgivable mistake. On the other hand, as a member of NATO and the European Union (EU), France modestly contributes to the collective effort and supports Ukraine both diplomatically and militarily. However, far from creating a favorable position for France, this ambiguity has so far only brought disadvantages. Instead of consolidating its position as a leading military power in the EU, the current policy signals its marginalization in the long term. The idea that France could mediate in this conflict is a pure illusion; the UN or Turkey, for very different reasons, are better positioned to play that role. The war is escalating. Indeed, Putin has clearly shown his disdain for European leaders, whom he sees as subordinate to the United States, and will reject them as mediators. Furthermore, the willingness to dialogue with Russia, justifiable before the invasion, is increasingly seen as a position of weakness among Ukrainians and, beyond that, in Central and Eastern European countries. Rightly or wrongly, many of our European partners do not see France as a reliable ally in this crisis. The suspicion, often directly expressed in Ukraine and Poland, is that France is willing to negotiate a makeshift peace at Ukraine's expense. In any case, Ukraine will not accept giving up its sovereignty, especially since military victories in Kyiv, Kharkiv, and Kherson have galvanized a society mobilized to defend its independence, its place in Europe, and a democratic future. As recent developments show, the war is escalating: Ukraine is fighting for its survival as a nation, and Putin for the future of his regime. The strengthening of the Ukrainian army and mobilization in Russia clearly illustrate the intensification of a conflict that now involves entire societies. The idea that Russia should not be humiliated has never made sense, especially as Putin shamelessly annexed nearly a quarter of its Ukrainian neighbor, well beyond the Donbass. Russia's economic and military exhaustion. Any solution other than the liberation of the occupied territories will only entrench a new conflict, which can be reactivated at any time, following the model of pro-Russian enclaves in Transnistria, Abkhazia, and South Ossetia. From this point of view, the European retreat organized by Merkel and Sarkozy during the 2008 Georgian crisis clearly demonstrated the futility of this strategy in the face of a regime seeking to rebuild an empire on its borders. These positioning errors have serious consequences, especially considering that the most likely scenario today, due to Russia's economic and military exhaustion, is a Ukrainian victory - even the reconquest of Crimea is not entirely unthinkable. The defeat of the Russian army and the inevitable ensuing crisis in Moscow will have repercussions on other conflicts fueled or created by Russia (Transnistria, South Ossetia, Abkhazia) and on the stability of the Belarusian regime. We can already see the effects in the conflict between Azerbaijan and Armenia and in the diminishing influence of Russia in Central Asia. With France sidelined and lagging behind, the possibility - for the first time in history - of a political unification of Europe cannot be ruled out. Whether this scenario materializes or not, the redefinition of intra-European balances condemns France's current policy, which will have missed the opportunity to establish itself as a central player in European security matters. French policy is consistently lagging behind the dynamics of the Ukrainian conflict, resulting in France having to give up its ambition to be the leading nation in security matters within the EU, while Germany openly expresses its ambition to form the most powerful army on the continent. This loss of leadership will have consequences in the civilian domain. The reconstruction of Ukraine - costing hundreds of billions of euros - is already underway, and the position of French companies will depend at least in part on France's policy. Consequently, France must act more cohesively on two levels. France's limited engagement Firstly, less ambiguous communication involves abandoning the rhetoric of mediation, which has done more harm than good, and sending clear signals to governments and public opinions in Eastern European countries. Secondly, military assistance, especially in terms of training and supplying military equipment, must align with the role France aims to play in European security architecture. However, while the UK offers significant military support and plans to train nearly twenty thousand troops this year, France will only train two thousand and still provides too little weaponry. This French caution also contrasts with Germany, which is increasing its supply of armored vehicles and, following the US model, is proposing a program to supply tanks to Eastern European countries willing to give their Soviet-made equipment to Ukraine. European security, and therefore our own, is at stake today on the front lines of Kherson and the Donbass; France must support Ukraine more firmly, as it is in its interests and values.
lead: The cautiousness of Emmanuel Macron, as well as his desire to treat Russia and Ukraine equally in the current invasion war, could marginalize France in Europe, note researchers Adam Baczko and Gilles Dorronsoro in a column in "Le Monde".

title: Oleksii Reznikov, le ministre de la défense ukrainien, ne croit pas que Poutine utilisera l’arme nucléaire
text: During a press conference held on Wednesday, October 26, in Kiev, the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, Oleksii Reznikov, stated that he did not believe that the Russian President, Vladimir Putin, was considering using nuclear weapons in Ukraine. According to the former lawyer, the Russian forces "have done many stupid things since the beginning of the war, but they have continued to be pragmatic." The nuclear threat has escalated as Russia, in recent days, has been making accusations against Ukraine, claiming that Ukraine is preparing a "dirty bomb," a conventional weapon (such as a missile) combined with radioactive materials. Ukraine and its Western allies have denied these allegations, while asserting that Russia, facing difficulties on the military front, might precisely attempt to detonate a "dirty bomb" in order to justify, in advance, the use of a form of nuclear weapon. The stakes of relations with China and India On Wednesday, October 26, for the first time, Mr. Putin echoed the accusations of senior Russian officials regarding the use of a "dirty bomb" for provocation. During the day, the Russian army conducted its annual nuclear deterrence exercise with missile tests capable of carrying nuclear warheads. The Minister of Defense believes, however, that Russia would not risk using nuclear weapons, notably due to the crucial importance of its relations with China and India. The Russians "are trying to have agreements with these countries, especially to provide them with gas and oil. However, these countries have sent them very clear signals: do not cross the red line of nuclear weapons," stated Oleksii Reznikov. Ukraine's allies have informed Russia that the use of nuclear weapons would have catastrophic consequences for the country. According to the Minister of Defense, the nuclear threat is primarily aimed at undermining the support of Western countries. Regarding the Ukrainian counteroffensive to reclaim the occupied region of Kherson in the south, he acknowledged that the situation was more complicated than during the counteroffensive in the east of the country in early September. Oleksii Reznikov explained that the rainy season makes the movement of combat vehicles "very difficult" and expressed his desire to spare his troops: "We do not share [with the Russians] the philosophy of cannon fodder, we try to keep our soldiers alive." For Bakhmut, a city in the Donbass region under Russian fire for months, the minister simply reiterated the army's talking points: "the situation is difficult, but under control."
lead: He claims that Moscow, with its accusations of a "dirty bomb," is primarily seeking to undermine the support of Western countries.

title: « Il n’est pas trop tôt pour une concertation derrière des portes closes entre l’Ukraine et ses principaux soutiens »
text: Since the end of September, the war in Ukraine has entered a new phase, with even higher risks of escalation. Despite a series of setbacks, Russian President Vladimir Putin has not changed his interpretation of the conflict: he still believes he can annihilate the resistance of Ukrainians and break the determination of Westerners, especially Europeans. This is the meaning that must be given to the so-called partial mobilization he has carried out, the annexation of four Ukrainian regions, the continuation of the hydrocarbon blackmail and clandestine actions (sabotage of the Nord Stream gas pipelines), the renewal of nuclear threats, and finally, the campaign of more or less indiscriminate strikes deep into Ukrainian territory. This last point may be the most important, at least in the short term. On one hand, it significantly raises the cost of Ukrainian resistance – for example, think of the disruption of electricity distribution. On the other hand, it reveals a willingness to remove all inhibitions in the use of force. This may seem strange, but in the previous phase, both parties imposed certain limits, such as the sanctuarization of Russian territory and restrictions on Russian strikes on Ukrainian critical infrastructure. While the Russian army is losing ground in Kherson, one may wonder if we are not entering a spiral that makes a frontal clash between Russia and NATO almost inevitable. Western countries are not at war with Russia. However, a multifaceted show of force is underway between Moscow and the United States and their allies. One point that may not have received enough attention: in the current situation, a "third force" – albeit not coherent – is playing an increasingly crucial role, these are countries that could be called "uninhibited middle powers." For example, India and Turkey, by expanding their trade with Russia, are easing the burden of Western sanctions, while Saudi Arabia is doing the same by deciding with OPEC+ to reduce oil production quotas. Iran, on the other hand, is supplying Moscow with drones that are striking Ukrainian cities. Uncomfortable position The policies of these powers – some of which are "friends" of the West – contribute to weakening Western strategy and strengthening the Kremlin's resolve. It is feared that Russia may obtain the microprocessors its defense industry vitally needs through one of them. Could escalation lead to nuclear risk? An odd "deterrent" dialogue with historical references has begun between the Russian and American presidents. On September 21, Mr. Putin referred to the "precedent" of Hiroshima. Let's suggest that there may be, not now but at a later stage of the confrontation, a moment when the question could arise for the Russian leaders. Mr. Biden responded to Mr. Putin on October 6 by talking about apocalypse and mentioning the Cuban crisis. It is worth noting that the Cuban crisis ended with a Russian-American compromise. Are we at a point in the Ukrainian crisis where diplomacy can come into play? The answer is no. Americans and Europeans must support Ukraine more, as the French government fortunately does. They should also consider their means of action towards "uninhibited middle powers." However, it is not too early to engage in closed-door consultations between Ukraine and its main supporters on what a negotiating position could be when the time comes. It is important to avoid a public debate on the "war aims," but to leave it to the Ukrainians alone to define them, and the timing of a possible negotiation eventually puts them in an uncomfortable position. During the Second World War, summits occasionally brought together the "great allies." Under obviously different modalities, there is a similar need today for a "war council" between major transatlantic partners. Should there be a "summit" in the literal sense or a discreet discussion among senior officials? With which countries around the table? It is up to the governments to find the most effective formulas. In any case, the points mentioned above should be high on the agenda.
lead: As the risk of a head-on collision between Russia and NATO increases, we cannot leave the responsibility of defining the goals of war and the timeline for potential negotiations solely to the Ukrainians, argues former diplomat Michel Duclos in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: Guerre en Ukraine : face aux missiles et drones russes, quels équipements antiaériens réclame Kiev ?
text: The calls from the Ukrainian government have been heard. French President Emmanuel Macron committed on Wednesday, October 12, to deliver "radars, systems, and [anti-aircraft] missiles to protect [the Ukrainians] from attacks, especially to protect them from drone attacks." The French head of state made this announcement after a series of massive Russian strikes a few days earlier, which targeted several Ukrainian cities, including the capital, Kiev. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky even called for the creation of an anti-aircraft shield over Ukraine, prioritizing the delivery of high-tech anti-aircraft defense systems to protect the population and strategic infrastructure in case of increased Russian strikes. But what anti-aircraft equipment is being referred to? What systems does Ukraine have? Since the beginning of the conflict, several allied countries have pledged to strengthen Ukrainian anti-aircraft defense by providing self-propelled anti-aircraft guns (Gepard) or individual short-range missiles (Stinger). The latter, quickly deployable by ground troops to target helicopters or low-flying combat aircraft, have proven very effective against Russian aviation. However, air defense is "multilayered by nature, it is the multiplicity of capabilities that allows for effectiveness," explains Thibault Fouillet, a researcher at the Foundation for Strategic Research (FRS). A single system, even if highly technological and only adapted for missile interception, will not be able to combat drones. In addition, Ukraine has short and medium-range anti-aircraft systems dating back to the Soviet era. These mainly include the S-300, put into service in the 1980s, and the Buk surface-to-air defense system, also of Soviet manufacture. "Even though these means may seem limited, interception is effective since President Zelensky mentioned a few days ago the destruction of 43 out of 84 missiles and 13 out of 24 drones," says the military expert. What equipment has been promised? Nasams (provided by the United States) However, the Ukrainian government believes its equipment is not sufficient and is eyeing the advanced systems held by some Western countries. Starting with the Nasams systems. "These systems are extremely effective, modern, and designed for intercepting the most technologically advanced threats in high-intensity situations," comments Thibault Fouillet. Therefore, they are perfectly suited for missile defense and anti-drone operations. This surface-to-air missile system, manufactured by the American company Raytheon and the Norwegian company Kongsberg, can intercept any flying object within a radius of about 25 kilometers. It would allow Ukrainians to deter Russian aviation from venturing into their airspace and, thus, protect ground troops from aerial attacks during an offensive. However, no Nasams has yet arrived on Ukrainian soil. The United States confirmed last summer the sending of eight systems of this type, two of which are expected to be delivered in the coming weeks or months. The other six batteries are expected to follow later. On the other hand, the United Kingdom announced the upcoming delivery of Amraam missiles, compatible with Nasams. "These weapons will help Ukraine defend its sky from attacks and strengthen its defense alongside American Nasams," said British Defense Minister Ben Wallace. An IRIS-T (provided by Germany) "A new era of air defense has begun in Ukraine": in a tweet published on October 11, Ukrainian Defense Minister Oleksii Reznikov celebrated the delivery by Germany of the first model of the IRIS-T air defense system, manufactured by the German company Diehl. With a range of 40 kilometers, this modern system, not yet in service within the German army, can destroy cruise missiles and aircraft up to 20 kilometers in altitude. "It will allow Ukraine to protect an entire large city from Russian air strikes," German Chancellor Olaf Scholz had argued in June. Germany has promised to deliver four systems of this type, with a second one expected to be shipped to Ukraine before the end of the year. A Crotale NG (provided by France) Emmanuel Macron confirmed the sending of French anti-aircraft systems, without specifying the type of batteries that will be delivered. However, according to the Ministry of Armed Forces, France is preparing to deliver Crotale NG batteries, a short-range anti-aircraft system (15 kilometers) dating back to the 1990s and capable of shooting down aircraft at low and very low altitudes. In total, the French army has twelve of them. "They are equipped with detection, identification, and sector surveillance radars, and have a capacity of eight missiles per launcher," details the Ministry of Armed Forces. The Ukrainian government was requesting a much more modern French anti-aircraft system: the SAMP/T Mamba. This medium-range surface-to-air system, capable of destroying targets beyond 100 kilometers, is manufactured by the Franco-Italian consortium Eurosam. France currently has eight units, equipped with eight supersonic missiles per launcher. According to the Ministry of Armed Forces, these batteries are capable of providing "a protective bubble for a moving ground force and forces deployed in a theater of operations." MARIE-LAN NGUYEN / WIKICOMMONS Hawk (provided by Spain) Spain has also promised to send four American medium-range air defense systems Hawk. This decision was welcomed on Thursday, October 13, by NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg, who considers the defense of Ukrainian airspace a "priority." The Alliance has encouraged its allies to provide additional air defense capabilities to Kyiv, even if it means pushing for the delivery of older surface-to-air systems to Ukraine while waiting to produce and deliver the more modern systems promised to the country's authorities. The Hawk systems were deployed in the U.S. military in the early 1960s and are no longer used by the United States, which now prefers more sophisticated Patriot systems. Washington is considering providing Ukraine with these long-range batteries, but this complex and extremely costly system requires a lot of maintenance and training. According to Agence France-Presse, Spain has modernized some Hawk systems in recent years to extend their lifespan. Romanian army soldiers deploy a Hawk PIP III R surface-to-air missile launcher during a joint military exercise with the U.S. military in 2016. INQUAM PHOTOS / REUTERS All of these systems are essential "because they enhance the Ukrainian multi-layered capability by offering new effects, particularly in the deficient areas of medium and long-range and anti-missile defense," summarizes Thibault Fouillet.
lead: Fearing an escalation of strikes on civilians and infrastructure in the country, Kiev is urging its allies to strengthen its defense capabilities against aerial attacks.

title: L’avenir de l’Union européenne se trouve-t-il à l’Est ? Pas certain
text: Analysis. For many commentators, the origin of the current rift between France and Germany is to be found "in the East". As a consequence of the enlargements of 2004 and 2007, Brexit, and now the promise of accession made to Ukraine, the geographical center of the European Union (EU) has de facto shifted towards Central Europe. Germany, positioned in the "east of the West", is simply acknowledging this reality. On the other hand, Paris, with its notorious lack of interest and persistent disdain towards the former communist countries, may have missed the direction of history. Can those wrongly referred to as the "Eastern countries" when they are actually at the heart of Europe embody the future of the Old Continent? Their determination to rise to this status is undeniable. Just listen to what Viktor Orban regularly says. "Central Europe is the future of Europe," asserts the Hungarian nationalist prime minister, explaining that it is "not just a geographical concept, but a political, economic, and cultural reality." In his view, Hungary and its neighbors are destined to catch up economically with the West, to impose a more intergovernmental vision of the EU, and to defend a culturally white and Christian Europe. Although his pro-Russian message has blurred Mr. Orban's credibility since the start of the war in Ukraine, the core of his prediction is shared in Poland, by far the largest country in the region with its thirty-eight million inhabitants. Together, Budapest and Warsaw dream of weakening the powers of the Commission and the European Parliament and of warding off any threat of sanctions for their authoritarian drifts. Politically, Germany is far from aligning itself with this vision. In his speech delivered in Prague on August 29, Chancellor Olaf Scholz explained that he is in favor of lifting the unanimity principle and he received Mr. Orban in a cold manner during his recent visit to Berlin. To the extent that Mr. Orban described the SPD as the "most hostile party to Hungary in Europe" upon his return. Many dream of Germany. Nevertheless, economically, the interests of large German companies have sometimes led Berlin to tolerate regional authoritarian drifts. Thanks to their skilled and cheap labor force, the countries of Central Europe have provided a crucial competitiveness boost to the German industry, either by emigrating by the hundreds of thousands to Germany or by hosting subcontracting factories. The GDP per capita of the Czech Republic already exceeds that of Spain, and Poland's is approaching it. The largest battery factory in Europe is soon to be built in Hungary by a Chinese company. For the same reasons, Germany advocates much more than France for an enlargement towards the Balkans. The six countries in the region that are not yet part of the EU have a combined population of nearly 20 million, many of whom already dream of Germany. France, on the other hand, continues to see any enlargement as a threat to its influence and as a risk of social dumping. In its defense, most of the Balkan candidate countries are no longer making much effort to align themselves with European principles. However, with its setbacks in Africa and the failure of the Union for the Mediterranean project, Paris no longer has a comparable zone of influence to leverage within the EU. The miracle of German-style economic development in Central Europe is reaching its limits. The region is experiencing full employment and factories are desperately seeking workers. However, the demographics are deeply depressed despite pro-natal policies. Even if Ukraine joins the EU one day, it is one of the countries experiencing the highest population decline in the world. This reality, which predates the war, is more reminiscent of an ultra-peripheral zone than a thriving core. Poland suggests that a massive European Marshall Plan for Ukraine after the war could help reverse this trend, but this remains highly speculative.
