Trojan Horse Under pressure from employers, governments in the region are indeed starting to open up to non-European immigration, but local populations remain largely hostile for now, as shown by Romanian filmmaker Cristian Mungiu, the best Zeitgeist filmmaker in Mitteleuropa with his latest film, R.M.N.. Demographics are crucial in the political balances of the EU because they form the basis for calculating the qualified majorities that approve directives. Another argument often put forward for the future of Central Europe is its long-standing critical stance towards Russia. The war in Ukraine is a moral victory for capitals that refused to compromise with Moscow, while Berlin and Paris were trying to appease Vladimir Putin. "We in Central Europe knew very well what the threat of Russian imperialism was and its passivity in the face of this imperialism. Today, we can see that our voice was prophetic," said Polish Prime Minister Mateusz Morawiecki in September. However, the idea that Central Europe constitutes a homogeneous bloc on this issue is a Polish lie. While the Baltic countries are on this side, the Czech and Slovak neighbors, now staunch supporters of Ukraine, have long been sending more ambiguous messages. Bulgaria is torn apart. As for Hungary and its ally Serbia – which is not yet part of the EU – they continue to be seen as pro-Russian Trojan horses in the heart of the Old Continent. Surprisingly, Poland is less critical of its ally Viktor Orban's pro-Putin propaganda than of Franco-German hesitations.
lead: With the war in Ukraine, Europe's attention has shifted to the East. But before embodying the future of the EU, Central Europe still has weaknesses to address.

title: « Les sanctions économiques contre la Russie marchent, mais à long terme seulement »
text: In 2014, Agathe Demarais was a commercial attaché at the French Embassy in Moscow, where she had to deal with the sanctions imposed against Russia following the invasion of Crimea and the war in Donbass. Today, as Chief Economist at the Economist Intelligence Unit, she is publishing a book in November on the subject of sanctions, titled "Backfire: How Sanctions Reshape the World Against US Interests" (Columbia University Press, untranslated). The main weapon used by Western countries after the invasion of Ukraine was that of sanctions. Does it work? It all depends on what the initial objective was. The first objective was to send a strong message to Russia, one that was neither a simple diplomatic statement nor an act of war. From that perspective, it has been successful. I believe Putin did not expect such a united reaction from Western countries. The oil embargo, which will soon be in place, is hurting. Certainly, Russia is now selling its oil to China and India, but at a significant discount. Currently, the rise in oil prices caused by the war is offsetting this, but next year, the global economic slowdown is expected to lead to a decrease in prices. The second objective was to complicate the financing of the war. Again, this is working: the Russian economy is estimated to lose about four percentage points of GDP this year. This is a significant impact, even if it is less than the initial forecasts. The third objective is the slow suffocation of the Russian economy. Russia is a rentier economy, heavily reliant on the exploitation of raw materials, which requires access to financing and cutting-edge technologies, especially for drilling in the Arctic. China can replace some of the financing, but it lacks the necessary technologies. Currently, Russia is living off its reserves. However, the sanctions have not brought an end to the war or crippled the Russian economy... Sanctions work, but only in the long term. They are a slow poison that takes time to take effect. In Iran, it took ten years of sanctions before the regime agreed to compromise and sign the nuclear deal. Are sanctions counterproductive because they have a heavy economic cost, especially in Europe? It is Russia that imposes this cost, not the sanctions. There are no sanctions on gas, but Putin decided to gradually use this weapon this summer by cutting off the supply. Similarly, the rise in oil prices after the start of the war is not a result of the sanctions: the oil embargo will only be in place at the beginning of next year. It is essential to be cautious and not listen to what Russia is saying, as it is trying to divide Western countries on the subject and fuel extremism. Poutine had tried to make people believe that the sanctions were blocking the export of grains and cereals and risking causing hunger in the world. That was false. The Russians had imposed a blockade on Ukrainian ports and the Ukrainians had trapped them, but there were no sanctions on food exports. Did Moscow manage to take measures to counter the sanctions? The Russian central bank managed to calm the financial panic at the beginning of the war. However, it had to impose strict capital controls. Regarding oil, Russia managed to find other outlets, but with gas, it's not really possible: the pipelines connected to China do not compensate for exports to Europe. As for semiconductors, it's difficult to know. There are rumors that they go through Serbia and Turkey.
lead: Agathe Demarais, Chief Economist of the Economist Intelligence Unit, had to manage the implementation of sanctions against Russia in 2014.

title: En Italie, premières fractures au sein de la coalition des droites de Giorgia Meloni
text: They are the faces of the new Italian legislature. On Friday, October 14th, Lorenzo Fontana, 42 years old, elected from Verona (North) and close to Matteo Salvini (League, far-right), was elected as the President of the Chamber of Deputies. This was a recognition for this tough member of the party, a traditionalist Catholic, known for his opposition to same-sex marriage and his closeness to Russia. In 2019, he stated his desire to make the League "a link between Trump and Putin." Fontana's election sparked strong protests from the opposition benches. Several members of the Democratic Party (PD, center-left) unfurled a banner with the phrase: "No to a homophobic and pro-Putin president." "The choice of the Chamber of Deputies further distances Italy from the heart of Europe," lamented Enrico Letta, the PD's Secretary-General outside the Chamber. Lorenzo Fontana symbolizes the institutional anchoring of the radical right in Italy, alongside Ignazio La Russa, who was elected as the head of the Senate on Thursday. La Russa, a seasoned politician in Italian politics, was already the Vice President of the upper house in the previous legislature and is the co-founder of Fratelli d'Italia with Giorgia Meloni, who leads the far-right coalition after their victory in the legislative elections on September 25th. La Russa also served as the Minister of Defense under Silvio Berlusconi from 2008 to 2011. He, who was a member of the Italian Social Movement (MSI) in his youth, has never hidden his nostalgia for Mussolini. In a video from 2018 that resurfaced on social media in recent days, he is seen showing a journalist his collection of Mussolini figurines. Ironically, the Thursday session was presided over by the senator for life Liliana Segre, a former Auschwitz survivor, who had asked Giorgia Meloni to remove the flame from the symbol of Fratelli d'Italia. "It is impossible for me not to feel a kind of dizziness when I remember that the same little girl who, in 1938, was forced by the racial laws to leave her primary school desk empty now finds herself, by a strange destiny, in the most prestigious office in the Senate!" explained Liliana Segre emotionally, receiving much applause. Plot twist, however, besides Ignazio La Russa's post-fascist profile, it was the way he was elected that marked this parliamentary session. His appointment was indeed the result of a plot twist. With a majority of 115 seats out of the 200 senators, the votes of the right-wing coalition's elected officials (FDI, League, and Forza Italia) should have made La Russa's election a mere formality. However, to everyone's surprise, the Forza Italia group, led by Silvio Berlusconi, abstained, except for the former prime minister - who was making his return to the Senate after nine years of absence - and the outgoing Senate president, Maria Elisabetta Casellati. Ignazio La Russa was finally elected thanks to the support of 17 opposition votes. This thunderbolt caused consternation for Enrico Letta. "The behavior of these senators, who chose to help an already divided and struggling majority from the outside, is irresponsible beyond all limits," lamented the Secretary General of the PD. As the vote was held by secret ballot, speculations multiplied in the afternoon about the identity of the "17 traitors," as the Italian press called them. "The first Senate of this new legislature is already showing its weaknesses," analyzed Lorenzo Pregliasco, a professor of political science at the University of Bologna and founder of the polling institute YouTrend. "On one hand, we see a divided majority with the abstention of Forza Italia, on the other hand, opposition lawmakers do not all seem to be on the same page when it comes to Fratelli d'Italia." Berlusconi's discontent The government of Giorgia Meloni has not yet been formed, and clouds are already gathering for the leader of Fratelli d'Italia. Silvio Berlusconi, unhappy with the negotiations regarding the distribution of portfolios, expressed his bad mood on Thursday in the Senate. The Cavaliere indeed tried to impose one of his closest advisors, Licia Ronzulli, to join the future government team, but Giorgia Meloni vetoed it. The leader of Forza Italia intends to make his support for the next prime minister costly. He is demanding, among other things, the portfolio of justice.
lead: Positions are fiercely contested in Parliament as in the future government.

title: « Dans la Chine de Xi Jinping, la répression antireligieuse signe l’impuissance du Parti à contrôler les esprits »
text: The death of Mikhail Gorbachev has sparked contrasting reactions in China: Beijing acknowledged the constructive role he played in normalizing relations between the two countries after thirty years of rupture, but the press did not hesitate to heavily criticize the man behind perestroika. Deng Xiaoping had already considered glasnost to be a "foolish" policy that would lead to the collapse of the USSR, as it prioritized political reform by promoting freedom of expression and association, which would lead to massive demonstrations, strikes, and the awakening of national identities. This was the opposite of the policy pursued by President Xi Jinping, whose main goal is the revitalization of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), which he deemed to be in decline when he came to power in 2012. Obsessed with the example of Gorbachev, he draws inspiration from Mao Zedong, advocating a return to socialist, even Maoist, ideals. The 20th CCP Congress, held from October 16 to 22, endorsed his approach and renewed his leadership for a third five-year term, with a possible fourth term in 2027 when he will only be 74 years old. At the previous congress in 2017, he successfully had the two-term limit abolished, which had been imposed after Mao's death, and even had his "thought" enshrined in the Constitution alongside that of the Great Helmsman. However, how can the second largest global power, which is highly capitalistic in many respects – holding the world record for billionaires – embody communist ideals? Nevertheless, according to some observers, China has never truly stopped being communist and is becoming increasingly so under Xi Jinping. In terms of its political regime, it is undeniable that the "new Mao" is pushing the CCP back to its red, even Maoist, roots. Additionally, as a sign of vitality for a party that has been in power for seventy years, membership is increasing rapidly among younger generations, although it is difficult to discern their true motivations, whether ideological convictions or career calculations. However, this perspective focuses on the ideological dimension promoted by the CCP without considering two essential points. Firstly, the specifics of the Chinese economy. While the state plays a significant role, it does not make the country communist. Social inequalities are more pronounced than in the United States, and there are even more billionaires, enthusiastic followers of Deng Xiaoping's belief that "It is glorious to get rich." Many of them are members of the Party and some are even in the Parliament. But more than just the economy, this viewpoint overlooks the profound societal changes that have occurred in China over the past few decades. Are citizens truly convinced today of the benefits of a Marxism-Leninism revitalized by the thoughts of Mao and Xi Jinping? How then to explain, for example, the spectacular resurgence of religions in recent decades? The shift in direction by Deng Xiaoping dealt a fatal blow to the collectivist dream of the Maoist era, replaced by the religion of economic efficiency and the worship of money as king. Rampant consumerism, business dealings, and widespread corruption: the decline of the Marxist utopia and the collapse of traditional ethics have created a moral crisis and a spiritual void that the official ideology cannot fill, unlike religions. The CCP boasts 98 million members but cannot ignore that over 350 million citizens are turning away from Marx and Lenin to find meaning in their lives through religion. With at least four times more believers than CCP members, can one then speak of a "communist" China, rooted in the collectivist and atheistic utopia of the Maoist era? This religious dynamic, a challenge to the militant atheism of the CCP, faces a repression that has worsened alarmingly under Xi Jinping. This is a major difference with Putin's Russia, where there is an objective alliance between power and the Orthodox Church. The Kremlin leader considers the Orthodox religion as an integral part of Russian identity and, as such, stands alongside Moscow Patriarch Kirill for major religious celebrations. In return, the patriarch is a fervent supporter of Putin, even giving a theological dimension to the invasion of Ukraine, a "legitimate" operation as it is necessary to combat the "forces of evil"... "Sinicizing" Christians In China, repression is particularly brutal against the Uighur Islam and Tibetan Buddhism, but it also does not spare Christianity, whose underground churches thrive and refuse to join official "patriotic" associations. The rise of Christianity deeply concerns the government, as the number of believers will soon surpass that of CCP members. Hence the fierce energy of the Party to suffocate Christian communities and "sinicize" this "Western" religion to serve socialist values under the exclusive authority of the Party. The aim is to eradicate faith in Christ by replacing it with faith in the CCP and in its leader, now almost deified, as Mao was. This religious policy reveals the regime's weaknesses and ideological obsessions: repression signifies the CCP's inability to control minds, sinicization is part of the ideological offensive that China is waging, particularly against the West. Will this containment strategy succeed in restoring a truly communist China? Some researchers doubt this, as they see it becoming, by 2030, the first Christian country in the world.
lead: The decline of Marxist utopia and the collapse of traditional ethics have created a moral and spiritual void that official ideology cannot fill, unlike religions, whose revival is spectacular, notes Claude Meyer, an expert on Asia, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: Italie : Giorgia Meloni, dirigeante du parti d’extrême droite Fratelli d’Italia, a présenté son futur gouvernement
text: After her party's historic victory in the legislative elections in September, Giorgia Meloni is set to become the first woman to hold the position of Prime Minister in Italy on Friday, October 21st. This 45-year-old Roman, who managed to "de-demonize" the far-right party Fratelli d'Italia to come to power exactly a century after Mussolini, was tasked by the President of the Republic, Sergio Mattarella, to form a government. She presented the members of the executive on Friday afternoon, who will be sworn in on Saturday morning at the Quirinal Palace. Matteo Salvini, leader of the League, an anti-immigration party, returns to the position of Deputy Prime Minister, which he had already held after the elections in 2018. At that time, he also held the position of Minister of the Interior, which he coveted again this time, but Giorgia Meloni vetoed it. A pro-European appointed as head of diplomacy It is a technocrat who takes the position of Minister of the Interior in this nationalist government focused on security and immigration issues. Matteo Piantedosi, 59, has had a career in the prefectural and senior civil service, largely at the Ministry of the Interior, where he was chief of staff to Mr. Salvini. A Eurosceptic herself, Ms. Meloni has chosen a convinced European to lead diplomacy, the former President of the Strasbourg Parliament Antonio Tajani. He ticks all the boxes to reassure Brussels and European capitals: a former European Commissioner and Vice-President of the European People's Party (EPP, right-wing), this 69-year-old Roman has a network that will prove very useful. Giancarlo Giorgetti, 55, also pro-European, has been appointed to the economy. This discreet but powerful figure of the League was already a minister in the outgoing government of Mario Draghi, in charge of economic development. At odds with Matteo Salvini, he remained a loyal supporter of Mr. Draghi until the end. Forced to juggle with turbulent allies As the third largest economy in the eurozone faces, like its neighbors, a difficult economic situation due to the energy crisis and inflation, Georgia Meloni's task is even more daunting as she will have to manage two turbulent allies: not only Matteo Salvini, but also Silvio Berlusconi, the declining leader of Forza Italia. The two men, who arrived separately with their respective delegations at the Quirinal Palace on Friday, are reluctant to accept the authority of Giorgia Meloni, whose party won 26% of the votes in the elections on September 25, compared to only 8% for Forza Italia and 9% for the League. Giorgia Meloni, after her meeting with the Italian President, Sergio Mattarella, addresses the press at the Quirinal Palace on October 21, 2022. The far-right leader is the first woman to lead a government in Italy. FRANCESCO AMMENDOLA / AFP Herself an Atlanticist and in favor of supporting Ukraine against Russia, Ms. Meloni had to face this week the controversial statements of Mr. Berlusconi, who claimed to have "reconnected" with Vladimir Putin and blamed Kiev for the war. She felt compelled to set the record straight on Wednesday by stating that Italy is "fully part" of Europe and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). A talented speaker, Ms. Meloni, a conservative Christian opposed to LGBT+ rights and with the motto "God, country, family," has nevertheless promised not to touch the law allowing abortion. Record inflation Inflation in the peninsula increased by 8.9% year-on-year in September, and Italy risks entering a technical recession next year, alongside Germany. The room for maneuver is limited by a colossal debt representing 150% of the gross domestic product (GDP), the highest figure in the eurozone after Greece. Giorgia Meloni has given up advocating for an exit from the euro, but she has promised to defend her country's interests more in Brussels. This, while growth depends on nearly 200 billion euros in subsidies and loans granted by the European Union as part of its post-pandemic recovery fund. Caught between the hammer of her "allies" and the anvil of Brussels and the markets, Ms. Meloni appears to be walking a tightrope, almost a tradition in this country known for its chronic government instability. Two far-right presidents in Parliament Regarding two other key positions in the Italian Republic, the Senate has elected Ignazio La Russa as its president, the founder of Fratelli d'Italia with Giorgia Meloni. This 75-year-old Sicilian has militated his whole life on the far right, following in the footsteps of his father, a former local leader of the National Fascist Party under Mussolini who gave his son, as a second name, that of the dictator, Benito. The lower house of Parliament also chose a controversial figure as its president: Lorenzo Fontana, a close ally of Mr. Salvini, with ultraconservative positions on abortion and same-sex marriage. This devout Catholic denounced the Brussels sanctions against Moscow after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and described Vladimir Putin's Russia as a "model" society.
lead: Matteo Salvini has been appointed as deputy prime minister; the pro-European Antonio Tajani, as minister of foreign affairs.

title: Poutine estime que le monde entre dans sa décennie « la plus dangereuse » depuis 1945
text: According to Russian President Vladimir Putin, speaking on Thursday, October 27 in Moscow, the world has entered its "most dangerous" decade since the end of the Second World War, and the conflict in Ukraine is an illustration of the struggle against Western domination. "We are at a historic moment. We are probably facing the most dangerous, most important, most unpredictable decade since 1945," declared the Russian leader at the Valdai discussion forum in Moscow. "The West, without clear unity, is not able to lead the world, but it is desperately trying, and most of the world's peoples cannot accept it," he stated, deeming the planet in a "revolutionary situation." According to him, the assault on Ukraine is part of this "tectonic shift in the entire world order." "Russia will never tolerate the diktat of aggressive, neo-colonial West," he added. Shortly before, he had described his confrontation with Western powers, particularly in the context of the offensive against Ukraine, as a fight for the very survival of Russia. "Russia is not challenging the West, Russia is just defending its right to exist," he further asserted, accusing Americans and other Westerners of wanting to "destroy, erase [Russia] from the map" in a new indictment against his geopolitical rivals, who support and arm Ukraine against Russian soldiers. "Dirty bomb": IAEA investigation Regarding Russian accusations that Kiev is developing a "dirty bomb," the Kremlin leader called on the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) to go to Ukraine "as soon as possible." "The IAEA wants to come (...). We are in favor, as soon as possible and in the broadest possible way, because we know that the authorities in Kiev are doing everything to cover up the traces of these preparations," he stated. "We even know approximately where this is being done [in Ukraine]," added the Russian leader. Ukraine and its Western allies have all strongly condemned Russia's "clearly false" allegations. The IAEA, for its part, announced in a statement on Thursday evening that it would visit "this week" two Ukrainian sites at Kiev's request "to detect any diversion of nuclear material." According to Mr. Putin, Ukraine wants to use such a radioactive weapon "so that later they can say it was Russia that carried out a nuclear strike." The Russian president downplayed his past threats of a possible Russian use of nuclear weapons, stating that he had only responded to threats from his adversaries. "We have never talked about the possibility of using nuclear weapons. We have only made allusions in response to statements from other countries," assured the Russian president. For him, Ukraine and Western countries assert that Moscow is escalating nuclear tensions in order to "influence neutral countries to say to them: 'Look at Russia! Do not cooperate with it!'" He then accused the United States of being "the only country in the world to have used nuclear weapons against a non-nuclear state," referring to Japan in 1945. "If he does not intend to do so, why does he talk about it so much? Why does he talk about [Russia's] capabilities to resort to tactical nuclear weapons?" responded U.S. President Joe Biden in an interview with NewsNation. "His approach to this issue is very dangerous," he said. Alleged "biological weapons" Referring to the economic situation of his country, Mr. Putin claimed that the "peak of economic difficulties" had passed. According to him, "the Russian economy has adapted (...). What is happening is ultimately beneficial for Russia and its future, including in the economic sphere." In the evening, the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs also announced that Russia had requested the United Nations Security Council to open an international investigation into Washington's alleged involvement in the development of biological weapons in Ukraine. Shortly after the start of its offensive in Ukraine, Russia accused Washington of funding a biological weapons program in Ukraine. Both the United States and Kiev denied the existence of laboratories intended to produce such weapons in Ukraine, with Washington calling it a "pure invention." The UN, through Adedeji Ebo, Deputy High Representative for Disarmament Affairs before the Security Council, stated that it was "not aware" of such a program.
lead: During an international conference in Moscow, the Russian president outlined his geopolitical vision, in which the war he is waging in Ukraine is part of a "tectonic shift in the entire global order."

title: Amory Lovins, scientifique : « Vladimir Poutine vient de faire exploser l’ère des énergies fossiles »
text: He is nicknamed the "Albert Einstein of energy efficiency." In 1976, Amory Lovins published a notable article, "Energy Strategy: The Road Not Taken?", in which he described two paths for the United States in this regard: the "hard" path, based on centralized technologies aiming to produce more, and the "soft" path, combining efficiency, renewable development, and phasing out fossil fuels. Since then, this American researcher, awarded the Right Livelihood Award in 1983 - considered an "alternative Nobel Prize" - and founder of the Rocky Mountain Institute, has never stopped advocating for energy savings. In 1976, you described two different energy paths for the United States. Today, what path do you see for Europe? I think that Vladimir Putin, the Russian president, has just triggered the end of the fossil fuel era. He has sparked a global and resolute movement in Europe, supported by the United States, to abandon Russian fuels and accelerate efficiency and renewable energies. Europe was already heading in that direction, but it is now doing so much faster. Could the war in Ukraine therefore play in favor of energy transition? This war has established a global carbon tax much larger than what economists wanted to implement to protect the climate. Of course, it is not exactly a "tax," it is not being applied in the right way, and it is causing a lot of difficulties. But as a "price signal" [the increase in energy costs and its impact on transportation, for example], it will greatly stimulate the shift to efficiency and renewables. And if Europe continues to move away from oil, gas, and coal with the vision and determination it has shown so far, then the great suffering inflicted by Vladimir Putin will not have been entirely in vain. "Some countries have managed to save energy very quickly by 10% or 20% in situations where there was a sense of solidarity and public utility." My four grandparents were from Ukraine. In my bag, I have a yellow and blue hat [in the colors of Ukraine]: wearing a hat is one of the things you can do here in Europe. With it, you won't be cold when you lower your radiator by 2°C or 3°C, and that can make all the difference. I was born in 1947, and what is happening now is the most fundamental change I have ever witnessed. Do you think calls for sobriety will actually have an impact? Some countries have managed to save energy very quickly by 10% or 20% in situations where there was a sense of solidarity and public utility. The goal of reducing consumption by 10% in two years, as set by France, is realistic and could probably be exceeded. Many will find ways to use less energy without any real discomfort. Most people don't realize the extent of the savings that can be achieved through technical solutions. Can you give us an idea? In your life, you could probably save 80% of your energy consumption, maybe even more. I live at 2,200 meters altitude, near Aspen, Colorado, where it can get as cold as -44°C. Yet, in a patio in the middle of our house, we have already grown bananas without any heating system! Built in 1983, it is very well insulated, the building is airtight, with ventilation and heat recovery. The windows look like double glazing but insulate as if there were sixteen, or even twenty-two layers. There are twice as many windows as in a normal house but all are more or less oriented towards the south – in the future, archaeologists will probably conclude that it was a temple! But is such an example generalizable? The approach we took to design our house is not only suitable for cold climates, it has been tested and also works very well in Bangkok, Thailand. Almost everyone lives in a climate that falls between that of Thailand and Colorado! As for mobility, I drive a BMW i3 made of carbon fiber, lighter than a conventional car by about 300 kg. Initially, the automotive industry said that carbon fiber was too expensive. But this car was the most popular electric vehicle in Europe until Tesla came along; 250,000 were sold and all were profitable for BMW. How did the manufacturers do it? First, they used fewer batteries since less weight needed to be moved. And they radically simplified the manufacturing process, which saves energy. This is what you call "integrated design"… Yes, you optimize your car as a system, not each component independently. Similarly, in my house, we didn't just optimize the insulation or windows, but the entire house, which cost less to build than a conventional house. The heating system is the most expensive part, just like batteries are for electric vehicles. Why do you think it's important to also rethink how we manufacture... pipes? Today, more than half of the world's electricity is used for motors, and half of the motors operate pumps and fans that move fluids in pipes and conduits. Installing wide, short, and straight pipes instead of thin, long, and twisted ones could reduce friction by at least 80% or 90%, and thus improve their efficiency. This also means that the pumping system decreases significantly, so the cost of capital decreases. And it's very simple: you design large pipes and small pumps instead of long pipes and big pumps! You should also install the pipes first, and the equipment afterwards: a plumber knows that if a drain has a right angle bend, it will get clogged. If everyone did this, we could save about one-fifth of global electricity, which is half of the electricity produced from coal. However, this is not included in standard engineering manuals or climate bills. Why? Because it's not a technology, and everything is organized around technology! It's more of a design method: how to put the pieces together. It's easy to do physically but difficult to conceive psychologically, because it's not what you're used to seeing. We need to bend minds, not pipes! However, not everything is so simple. In France, comprehensive and efficient renovations are still very rare, for example... Yes, it seems that the goal is to act on as many buildings as possible rather than to save the most energy and carbon... Some of the comprehensive renovation methods can be complex, but new ones are emerging. Take the example of social housing in the Netherlands: there is now an approach called "EnergieSprong," which involves laser scanning a building, then factory-manufacturing a super-insulating "blanket" that is then placed on its exterior walls. Efficient equipment, such as heat pumps, are installed, as well as a solar roof. The building consumes five times less energy. Instead of paying 150 euros per month to their energy supplier, tenants pay 80 to 120 euros to repay the investment. In addition to efficiency, you advocate for the deployment of renewables. What explains why some oppose them? Why do renewable energies cover 80% of Denmark's electricity needs? I think it's related to the fact that there is a real local commitment and that most wind and solar capacities are owned by individuals, cooperatives, or communities. If every time the turbine turns, it puts euros in your pocket, that helps! I'm not saying there are no conflicts, but if you look at the ridiculously long time it takes to get permission to install solar panels on a roof in Paris, you think that surely there is a better way. So, do you believe in rapid and massive development? Every year, the world installs electrical production capacities and removes others. Do you know what proportion of these net additions of capacities are renewables? 95%. In one of these good years, nuclear power represents 0.5% of net capacity additions. It's just a distraction. You have been a long-time opponent of nuclear power, but in the face of the climate emergency, isn't it necessary to not exclude any low-carbon solutions? I simply take economic indicators and market rationality seriously. The more concerned you are about climate change – and I am – the more important it is to invest wisely and discerningly in solutions that will save the most carbon per euro. "Renewables now cover the increase in global demand, and the supply of fossil fuels has shifted into permanent decline." Analysts at Bloomberg New Energy Finance say a new nuclear kilowatt-hour costs five to thirteen times more than a new solar or wind kilowatt-hour; others mention a factor ranging from three to eight. This means that when you buy a nuclear kilowatt-hour instead of a solar or wind kilowatt-hour, you pay three times more. In other words, you are substituting three to thirteen times less fossil fuel per euro, and you are doing it more slowly. So, for reasons of cost and speed, investing in new nuclear power is not an efficient climate solution. We simply cannot afford to invest in all solutions. Renewables are being deployed, but CO2 emissions continue to rise... It is very likely that the peak use and emission of fossil fuels occurred in 2019. With nearly 300 gigawatts of new capacity per year, renewables now cover the increase in global demand, and the supply of fossil fuels has shifted into permanent decline. For many years, renewables simply added to fossil fuels instead of replacing them, but that is no longer the case. Do you still think achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 is possible? We need both strong efficiency and strong growth in renewable energies. These two elements combined put us roughly on track for a 2°C warming; we need to go twice as fast to stay below the 1.5°C threshold. But there are so many untapped energy-saving opportunities that I believe it is possible. And we have made excellent progress in energy savings this year, as expected with this price signal. You mention practicing "applied hope," what does that mean? It is a daily practice of doing things to try to create a world worth hoping for. Focusing on what can be done, not just talking about it. As the British essayist Raymond Williams said, being truly radical means making hope possible. I don't think we can compel people to act by making them despair.
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", the American scientist Amory Lovins, a fervent advocate of energy efficiency and renewables, believes that the war in Ukraine has triggered a global and resolute movement in Europe on the matter, supported by the United States.

title: Thierry Mariani et Yves Pozzo di Borgo visés par une enquête pour corruption en lien avec la Russie
text: One of the most influential political relays of Russia in France is now in the sights of the French justice system. According to information from Le Monde, Dialogue franco-russe is at the center of two preliminary investigations opened on September 29, 2021 by Junalco, the division of the Paris prosecutor's office responsible for combating organized crime. This association, created in 2004 under the patronage of Jacques Chirac and Vladimir Putin, brings together French and Russian political leaders and businessmen with the stated goal of developing cooperation between the two countries. These two investigations target in particular Thierry Mariani and Yves Pozzo di Borgo, two of the most active members of the association known for their public positions favorable to the Kremlin. Mr. Mariani, a Member of the European Parliament and a regional councilor for the National Rally (RN), has been co-chairing Dialogue franco-russe since 2012 and openly defends French economic interests in Russia. Mr. Pozzo di Borgo, a former senator and councilor in Paris, a former member of the centrist party Union of Democrats and Independents (UDI), is also a staunch supporter of Vladimir Putin's foreign policy. Trips funded by Russia The existence of the investigations was confirmed to Le Monde by the Paris prosecutor's office, which, however, refused to comment on the identities of the individuals targeted. "These procedures are still ongoing and their development is covered by secrecy," the prosecutor's office said. According to Le Monde, they have been entrusted to the Central Office for the Fight against Corruption and Financial and Fiscal Offenses (OCLCIFF). The first investigation, which concerns exclusively Mr. Mariani, is based on suspicions of breach of trust and money laundering related to the association – that is, possible embezzlement of funds belonging to the association. It has already led to searches. The second, potentially much more explosive, seeks to establish possible acts of corruption and influence peddling by Thierry Mariani and Yves Pozzo di Borgo, also related to the association. Opened five months before the outbreak of the war in Ukraine, these judicial procedures stem from a report by the anti-money laundering unit Tracfin in May 2021. This report concerns several suspicious financial transactions related to Dialogue franco-russe and mentions the names of several French political leaders, including Mr. Mariani and Pozzo di Borgo. According to the information provided to the justice system, the two men would have benefited from services such as all-expenses-paid trips, funded by Russia through entities that actually serve as intermediaries and fronts for the Kremlin. The justice system is trying to determine to what extent these gifts may have served as direct quid pro quos for positions favorable to Russia. Among the questionable financial operations highlighted by Tracfin are also the flows passing through YPDB Conseil, the consulting company created by Yves Pozzo di Borgo a few weeks after his departure from the Senate in 2017. According to the trade register, this company experienced exponential growth between 2018 and 2020, allowing its president and sole shareholder to grant himself generous dividends (103,838 euros in 2020). However, investigations conducted by the anti-money laundering unit of the French Ministry of Finance have shown that this company generated 85% of its turnover with the Russian bus manufacturer Bakulin Motors Group, suspected of having business ties with a Kremlin-linked oligarch. Difficult criminal offenses to prove The work of the investigators, who will have to establish whether these facts fall under a criminal classification, appears delicate. Certainly, neither Thierry Mariani nor Yves Pozzo di Borgo - who remain presumed innocent - make a secret of their support for Russia, nor do they hide sponsored trips by foreign powers or entities linked to them. Thierry Mariani had notably defended Vladimir Putin after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and condemned the Western sanctions imposed on Russia. His on-site visits have been widely publicized, such as a visit in July 2015 organized in a Russia under sanctions, then in Crimea, with around ten parliamentarians, including Mr. Pozzo di Borgo. But for the justice system, the challenge is to establish the existence of a corruption pact, by tracing a direct and concrete link between the financing of these services and any subsequent benefits. The offense of passive corruption is the act of requesting or accepting an advantage in exchange for the performance - or non-performance - of an act related to one's function. Passive influence peddling, on the other hand, involves accepting or requesting an advantage to use one's influence in exchange. "The question is whether, and at what point, these individuals cross into illegality." "These are individuals known for lobbying and who openly admit it, so the question is whether, and at what point, they cross into illegality," explains a judicial source summarizing the difficulty of the exercise. Faced with these challenges in legal interpretation, this source does not rule out the possibility that it may ultimately be impossible to prove the commission of offenses. The investigation will also need to provide evidence of the irregular or fraudulent nature of certain financial flows. When contacted by Le Monde, Thierry Mariani stated that he was "not aware" of such investigations. "I am still on the same line: I have been interested in Russia since I learned Russian in a military school. Every time I have been to Russia, I have declared my trips to the European Parliament. It's transparent, you can clearly see who is paying." In the past, Mr. Mariani had stated that he had never received any money in connection with his trips as a guest. He now adds that he defends "a position that consists of saying that our current policy [towards Russia] is neither in the interest of France nor in the interest of European security." "If I support [Franco-Russian Dialogue] and remain its president despite all the troubles it may cause me, it is because I remain convinced that it is in our best interest to re-establish healthy and peaceful relations with Russia as soon as possible," he concludes. Example of Russian interference On his part, Yves Pozzo di Borgo refused to respond. "I no longer have contact with the press (...) It is a principle for me to maintain my tranquility," he stated. However, this discretion does not apply to social media, where the former senator openly expresses his admiration for the Putin regime. This is evident in his recent posts on Twitter, such as on September 21, where he wrote: "The NATO narrative relayed by Macron, who wants us to believe that the war in Ukraine is the Ukrainian people reacting to Russia when it is actually a civil war in which many Ukrainians want to join Russia! Let democracy take its course!" Or on September 25: "The NATO narrative tries to make us believe that Russia is isolated! At the General Assembly, we saw Macron, the man of the Americans, speaking to an empty room! On the other hand, Lavrov, the Russian minister, was overwhelmed with requests for meetings and appointments!" Since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the Franco-Russian Dialogue association has faced some financial setbacks with "the desertion of its French sponsors," as reported by La Lettre A in August. The association also had to vacate its premises on the Champs-Elysées, which were provided by the Russian Railways company (RZD). This company has been placed under international sanctions for its role in the war in Ukraine and has been "inactive" in France "since July 29, 2022," according to the commercial register. Regardless of their judicial outcome, the ongoing investigations by the Paris prosecutor's office are an additional and emblematic illustration of Russia's attempts to interfere in Western countries. According to a leaked American diplomatic cable on September 13 by the Associated Press, Russia has secretly spent over 300 million dollars trying to influence political actors in more than twenty countries worldwide since 2014.
lead: The Paris public prosecutor's office is investigating possible acts of corruption and influence peddling related to Franco-Russian Dialogue, an association co-chaired by RN MEP Thierry Mariani.

title: A la foire du livre de Francfort, Volodymyr Zelensky exhorte les auteurs à décrire la terreur imposée par la Russie
text: "Instead of importing culture, Russia imports death," declared Volodymyr Zelensky on Thursday, October 20, in a speech broadcast via video from Kyiv at the Frankfurt Book Fair, one of the main global publishing events held in Germany until Sunday, October 23. According to the Ukrainian president, the two major absentees from this edition, Russia and Iran, are "less present in the cultural field," but "increasingly present where everything is destroyed." Like he did at the last Cannes Film Festival, the Ukrainian president intertwines culture, politics, and soft power, as his country is under fire from Russian bombs. He urged publishers and authors from around the world: "Do everything you can to make known the extent of the terror that Russia has brought to Ukraine and, before, to Chechnya, Georgia, Syria, and African countries with the mercenaries of the Wagner group." He emphasized: "Books, scripts, articles, reports are the responses" to the ignorance of the "murders committed by the Russians," of the "corruption," of this "anti-democratic regime." In his view, this "lack of knowledge" explains the leniency that the Russian president receives from "many public figures in Europe," from various spheres: "politics, business, NGOs, and media." Working under bombs Forty-six Ukrainian publishers are participating in the Frankfurt Book Fair. Over thirty have made the journey, sometimes under difficult conditions. "It took us between two and three days with buses, trains...," details Yulia Naidukh from the Ukrainian Book Institute. They are all there to defend the national cultural vitality. "No Russian publishing house has condemned the war or expressed support for Ukraine," noted Oleksandr Afonin, president of the Association of Ukrainian Publishers and Bookstores. This is why "it is important for us that there is no Russian pavilion" at the Fair, says Zoia Shevchuk, who also works for the Ukrainian Book Institute. Only notable opponents of Vladimir Putin are welcome this year. Such as Leonid Volkov, a close associate of Alexei Navalny, or Irina Sherbakova, a member of the Russian NGO Memorial, co-recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize. Russian science fiction writer Dmitry Glukhovsky, who has denounced the war in Ukraine, is also expected. On the stage of the Ukrainian booth, many guests come and testify. Above the platform, a bright red light flashes every time the air raid sirens go off in Ukraine. It is impossible to forget the war. This is the challenge for Ukrainian publishers: to continue working under bombs. "Nobody really understands, but if we don't work, it's worse, we feel like we're dying, we only think about war, bombings, and family members who stayed in Ukraine," says Alena Litovska, an editor at Kalamar, a publishing house specializing in children's books. Having left her husband in Kyiv and moved to Austria with her two children, she can finally present some of her new releases. Her printer, the only one producing pop-up and pull-tab books, is located in Kharkiv. In the eye of the storm. At the same booth, Taiwanese publisher Rex How, CEO of Locus Publishing Company, was presenting his latest work, fifty speeches by Volodymyr Zelensky delivered since 2019. In its next edition, he will be able to add the one from the Frankfurt Book Fair.
lead: The President of Ukraine communicated via video link on Thursday, October 20, during the event that is hosting forty-six Ukrainian publishers until Sunday, with no Russian stands.

title: En Italie, Berlusconi « renoue » avec Poutine et sème le trouble dans la coalition
text: These confessions have had the effect of a bomb in the Peninsula. Two leaked recordings in the press within a few hours shed harsh light on the fractures within the right-wing majority and on the future of the Forza Italia party (center-right) within the future government coalition being formed by the future Prime Minister and leader of the Fratelli d’Italia party (post-fascist), Giorgia Meloni. In an audio excerpt recorded by a Forza Italia parliamentarian and broadcast on Wednesday, October 19, Silvio Berlusconi can be heard discussing the war in Ukraine with party members gathered in a room at the Montecitorio Palace, the seat of the Chamber of Deputies. "I have reestablished contact with President Putin, a bit... well, quite a bit," confides the former prime minister, who recounts that for his birthday - he turned 86 on September 29 - the Russian leader sent him "twenty bottles of vodka and a very kind letter." "I replied with bottles of Lambrusco and an equally kind letter. He told me I was one of his five 'true friends,'" he continues. The second recording is even more compromising for the Cavaliere, as he explains to his troops that Vladimir Putin would have gone to war because of President Volodymyr Zelensky. "I don't see how Putin and Zelensky can come to the negotiating table. Because it's just not possible. Zelensky, in my opinion... forget it, I can't say it," Berlusconi stops. A final sentence applauded by the Forza Italia parliamentarians. "Meloni is a hostage of the pro-Russians." The sequence could seriously undermine Italy's credibility on the European stage, especially as Giorgia Meloni has always made her support for Ukraine and NATO a strong line in her political program. The applause from the 45 FI parliamentarians also raises questions about the party's real stance on the Ukrainian conflict. Will Antonio Tajani, former President of the European Parliament and coordinator of Forza Italia, still be able to secure the position of Minister of Foreign Affairs as planned? Many observers saw the possibility fading on Thursday evening, shortly after the controversy sparked by the Italian billionaire. "Silvio Berlusconi has always placed himself at the center of attention," notes Giovanni Orsina, a professor at Luiss University in Rome and a specialist in Italian right-wing politics. "But by following a political rationality, a clear line. I must admit that I have never seen a destructive Berlusconi like this before." On Thursday, the Italian press, across all political tendencies, echoed with dismay the toxic atmosphere within the future ruling coalition. "Silvio, stop!" urged the newspaper Libero, despite its right-wing stance, emphasizing that "things could end badly." "Meloni is the hostage of the pro-Russians," was the headline in La Repubblica. "Schizophrenia" Besides her pro-Putin sympathies that weaken the majority coalition, it is Silvio Berlusconi's own ability to continue making politically coherent choices that is being questioned. On Tuesday evening, on the private channel La 7, deputy Alessandro Cattaneo learned live from the presenter that he had been elected leader of the Forza Italia group in the Chamber of Deputies. Silvio Berlusconi had slipped his name to his troops, without informing the interested party. "An episode that gives the feeling of a party deprived of a guide," notes Giovanni Orsina. Silvio Berlusconi's wavering on Vladimir Putin, according to him, is a kind of "schizophrenia," while the parliamentarians of Forza Italia have voted in recent months in favor of all of the Draghi government's amendments on the war, without any ambiguity. "I still believe that the Forza Italia line will remain firm and pro-Ukraine," the political scientist wants to believe, "but the situation has certainly become much more difficult for Giorgia Meloni." The one who is called to govern Italy did not speak directly, but through a statement from Fratelli d'Italia in which she does not hide her firmness. "Italy fully, and with its head held high, is part of Europe and the Atlantic Alliance, whoever does not agree with this cornerstone cannot be part of the government," warns Giorgia Meloni. Putting out the fire Less than two hours later, Silvio Berlusconi tried to put out the fire by explaining that his personal position and that of Forza Italia "do not differ from that of the Italian government, the European Union, the Atlantic Alliance, neither on the Ukrainian crisis nor on other major international policy issues." But the damage is done. Thursday morning, the first consultations began at the Quirinal Palace around the President of the Republic, Sergio Mattarella, who, constitutionally, is the guarantor of Italy's international treaties. Initially group by group, these consultations are set to conclude on Friday with a meeting with the united coalition. Regardless of the names of the ministers who will make up Giorgia Meloni's government, Italy has just achieved the feat of a government not yet born but already in full crisis.
lead: Two audio recordings of the former prime minister are disrupting the formation of Giorgia Meloni's government with her right-wing allies.

title: La Biélorussie fait un pas de plus vers une implication directe dans la guerre en Ukraine
text: Since the beginning of the invasion in Ukraine, Belarus has been serving as a logistical platform for its Russian ally. On February 24, Moscow's troops had departed from Belarusian territory in an unsuccessful attempt to conquer the capital, Kiev. On October 10, a portion of the missile barrage that struck all of Ukraine also came from Belarusian territory. A week later, Ukrainian authorities claimed that the "kamikaze drones" that hit Kiev on Monday, October 17, were launched from Belarus. So far, the Belarusian army has not participated in the fighting on Ukrainian territory, but concerns about its direct involvement in the conflict and the opening of a new front in the north of the country have increased since Belarusian dictator Alexander Lukashenko announced on October 10 the creation of a joint military group with Russia. Officially, this force will be purely defensive and aimed at securing the Belarusian border against "terrorist" attacks allegedly being prepared by Ukraine, Poland, and Lithuania, according to the leader. "We have not killed anyone [in Ukraine] and do not plan to do so. No one has asked us to participate in this operation," he assured on October 14 on the American channel NBC. Formalized complicity When asked about the size of this new force, Lukashenko stated that the Belarusian army would be the "base" of the group, without specifying how many Russian soldiers would be involved. "It is not necessary to ask for 10,000 or 15,000 people from Russia now, because there are other problems there, the ones you know," added the Belarusian leader. The first Russian soldiers of this joint force have already arrived, as announced by the Belarusian Ministry of Defense on Saturday, with supporting images. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky accused Moscow of wanting to "drag Belarus into the war" and requested an international observation mission from the G7 at the border between Ukraine and Belarus. "We know what the Russians are capable of since 2014 [the start of the war in Donbass], but we never imagined that Belarus would submit so cowardly to Moscow," sighed a Ukrainian diplomatic source. In their view, the formation of this joint military group is not a turning point but rather "the formalization of complicity" between Minsk and the Russian government. The head of the Ukrainian Parliament's Foreign Affairs Committee, Oleksandr Merezhko, called on Friday for diplomatic relations to be severed with Belarus. "In my opinion, the question of [this] rupture (...) is overdue. It is increasingly difficult to have diplomatic relations with a country led by a dictator," stated Merezhko, a member of the ruling party. Analysts doubt that Belarusian soldiers could pose a significant threat to Ukraine. With only 48,000 men, the Belarusian army, poorly equipped and unprepared, is one of the smallest in the post-Soviet space, and has few modern weapons. However, Kiev claims to "not exclude anything" and is preparing for all scenarios, including a possible new invasion from Belarusian territory. Belarusian resistance groups On the other hand, the Belarusian population does not seem ready to see its army directly engage in the Ukrainian conflict. According to a study published in August by the British think tank Chatham House, 70% of Belarusians are hostile to it. Eager to avoid new international sanctions, Alexander Lukashenko himself is reluctant to send troops. But his room for maneuver is narrower than ever in the face of Vladimir Putin, to whom he has become indebted since he owes his political survival to him after his contested re-election in August 2020. Has he received instructions from the Kremlin? According to the independent Belarusian newspaper Nacha Niva, Mr. Lukashenko would have decided to carry out a "hidden mobilization" aimed at "increasing the personnel of combat units". This mobilization, of an unknown scale to date, would be carried out "under the pretext of verifying military readiness". The newspaper claims to "know with certainty that, so far, only residents of rural regions of Belarus are receiving summonses" and that large cities "have not yet been affected". "Emphasis is placed on specialties lacking in the Russian army," it specifies, citing drivers, cooks, and technical personnel in particular. Belarusians have been fighting in Ukraine since the beginning of the war, but within the Ukrainian armed forces, against the Russians. Opponents of Lukashenko's regime have formed two regiments, totaling theoretically over a thousand soldiers. Belarusian resistance groups have also organized a "railway war" in their country and carried out dozens of sabotage acts to slow down the advance of Russian troops.
lead: After the announcement of the creation of a joint military alliance between Russia and Belarus, a "hidden mobilization" is reportedly underway, according to the independent Belarusian newspaper "Nacha Niva."

title: « Vladimir Poutine ne comprend pas l’Europe »
text: Jean-Maurice Ripert is a diplomat, former French ambassador to Russia from 2013 to 2017, during the annexation of Crimea and the war in Donbass, and then to China between 2017 and 2019. He analyzes the attitude of the Russian president and his vision of Europe. How did the mutual misunderstanding between Vladimir Putin and Europe arise? Vladimir Putin came to power in 1999, at a time when Europe was strengthening its union, while Russia mainly maintained bilateral relations with each of the states. He was a lieutenant colonel in the KGB, a master of networks, in a predatory system that emerged in the 1990s, particularly in Saint Petersburg, and he quickly showed that he would use force, as in Chechnya. We accepted him as the president of Russia. We failed to anticipate that the country would return to its old demons. Mr. Putin continued his predatory policy as far as possible. He ended up becoming a caricature of himself. He did not realize that the European Union [EU] was becoming a power and that the Russia-United States duopoly was itself evolving. He did not understand that the world had changed. Europe was the largest economic partner, the top investor. Russia should be the richest country on the planet, but it remains a rich country inhabited by poor people. I would like to point out that it is the only place in the world where you can submit a bill to Parliament in the morning and see it enacted the same evening – the Russians have nicknamed this "the crazy printer." Do you think Mr. Putin has changed? I do not believe for a second that he has changed. When asked if Vladimir Putin was crazy, the neuropsychiatrist Boris Cyrulnik replied that he had the rationality of his delusion. The Russian president is perfectly rational within a reasoning that has little to do with reality. Who holds Russia today? The far-right nationalists, the oligarchs dependent on Mr. Putin, and the security forces. I do not know how long this can last. Since the invasion of Ukraine at the end of February, the Russians have voted with their feet; hundreds of thousands of them have left. Vladimir Putin's project is based on a nationalist myth, on a form of Russian empire, in the ethnic sense of the term. The authorities were constantly in confrontation, contestation, and complaints towards European ambassadors. Everything was used to destabilize them and create a power struggle. I myself have often been summoned at odd hours by deputy ministers for all sorts of reasons. It was a form of intimidation. In 2008, during the French presidency of the EU, it was as if the problem was resolved [after the lightning war by Russian forces against Georgia, which resulted in the amputation of 20% of its territory]. Then, in 2014, the annexation of Crimea reverberated like a thunderclap, before war broke out in the Donbass. However, it was the Russians who fomented unrest in Donetsk and Luhansk with gang leaders supported by Russian and Ukrainian oligarchs. And yet, the annexation of Crimea, fundamentally, did not change much. Europe wanted to return to normal relations with Moscow... European ambassadors prepared the Brussels meetings on sanctions in 2014. And suddenly, within the EU, a group of countries (Poland, the Baltic States, and others) emerged with a different opinion and some experience with Russia. This radically changed things in European politics, transatlantic relations, NATO... Suddenly, the concept of Europe from Lisbon to Vladivostok took a serious blow. We shifted to a slightly less naive perspective... This, in my opinion, was an extremely important turning point. The balance of power within the EU began to shift. Russia's aggressiveness even accelerated the debate on Europe as a power. But in France, how did we go from the firm tone used by Emmanuel Macron during Vladimir Putin's visit to Versailles in 2017, to the more conciliatory tone during his reception at Brégançon in 2019? As a diplomat, I will always be in favor of dialogue, but when is it interpreted as a sign of weakness? Once again, Mr. Putin does not understand the EU, which does the exact opposite of him by sharing its sovereignty, while he wants to reunify the Russian empire. He is a victim of his own isolation. He underestimated our capacity and intentions, despite our disagreements. He underestimated Joe Biden and his ability to react, and certainly did not expect Finland and Sweden to request entry into NATO. Europe may be slow, but that does not mean it is weak. The harm it inflicts with sanctions is not masochism, it is evidence of its determination. Vladimir Putin has attacked the democratic world. Democracies have reacted. I emphasize democracies, not the West. He was wrong about everything, just as he is still wrong about his international supporters. Forcing China to choose sides is absurd. Beijing will never choose the Russians over the Americans. That's not how the world works anymore. How do you explain then that Kremlin proxies have had significant influence in France and Europe? Some of Vladimir Putin's propagandists act out of conviction or a historical love for Russia, while others act out of financial interest. When you have devoted your life to Franco-Russian relations, experiencing such a rift is complicated; you become blinded and ready to make excuses for Mr. Putin. There are also those who sell themselves to Russian interests for money, such as tax exiles or former politicians who are paid by Russian companies, like former German Chancellor Gerhard Schröder or former French Prime Minister François Fillon [who resigned from his Russian positions on February 25]. Many French or German companies struggled to accept the sanctions after 2014. While I was ambassador in Russia, I saw in Moscow or St. Petersburg, like other European diplomats, representatives of political parties, almost all from the same side, having high-level meetings with Russian officials. We had no doubt, at the time, that they probably would not leave empty-handed. There are indeed parties that have never hidden receiving funding from Russian banks.
lead: The former French ambassador to Moscow, Jean-Maurice Ripert, considers, in an interview with "Le Monde," that the Russian president is "a victim of his own isolation" facing a European Union that does the opposite by sharing its sovereignty.

title: La leçon d’histoire de Xi Jinping aux hiérarques du Parti communiste chinois
text: It is a tradition: after each congress of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), the General Secretary, Xi Jinping, takes the new party leadership to visit a symbolic location. In 2012, it was Shenzhen, a symbol of China's opening to the world. In 2017, Shanghai, where the party was founded in July 1921. And last week, it was Yan'an, a city in Shaanxi, in the northwest of the country, where Mao Zedong and his supporters stayed from 1935 to 1948 after the Long March, before invading Beijing and seizing power. The objective of this Chinese-style team-building excursion is to "cherish the glorious achievements of the revolutionary old generation" and "demonstrate the firm determination of the new leadership to pass on the revolutionary genes." According to the statement released following this visit on October 29, Xi Jinping particularly emphasized the importance of the 7th CCP Congress held in Yan'an in 1945. A congress that "confirmed Mao Zedong's ideological leadership position within the party," according to propaganda. "Thanks to the Yan'an work style rectification movement, the entire party was united under Mao Zedong's banner and achieved unprecedented unity and solidarity within the party," the statement specifies. The unity was even stronger as Mao did not convene another congress until 1956, a fact conveniently omitted by the propaganda. In Mao's footsteps, the message conveyed by this staging is, of course, that following the 20th congress, where he secured a third term, Xi Jinping fully aligns himself with Mao's legacy and that the other members of the Politburo Standing Committee must "resolutely implement" the "firm and just leadership" defined by Xi. The photos and videos released during this visit show Xi Jinping constantly lecturing the other six members of the CCP leadership, as if these sixty-year-olds with over thirty years of experience were discovering the party's history. At the same time as this visit was taking place, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Wang Yi, who was promoted to the Politburo, called his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov, to report on the "success" of the 20th congress. On this occasion, Wang Yi stated that "China will also firmly support the Russian side, under the leadership of President Vladimir Putin, in uniting the Russian people and guiding them to overcome difficulties and eliminate disturbances, achieve strategic development goals, and enhance Russia's status as a major power on the international stage." This statement, which only briefly mentions the "Ukrainian issue" in the very last line, seems to be intended to confirm that, after the 20th congress, China is maintaining its course of "unlimited friendship" with Russia, as defined by Xi and Putin on February 4th in Beijing. While China is not providing military support to Russia, it also sees itself more than ever in a "struggle" against the West and its values.
lead: The Chinese president took the new leadership of the CCP, which emerged from its 20th congress, to Yan'an, a city in Shaanxi that was the endpoint of the Long March in 1935.

title: Italie : Giorgia Meloni promet que Rome restera « un partenaire fiable de l’OTAN en soutien à l’Ukraine »
text: In a speech to the deputies, the new Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, strongly reaffirmed Italy's membership in the European Union (EU) on Tuesday, October 25th. Italy is "fully part of Europe and the Western world," declared the leader of the post-fascist Eurosceptic party Fratelli d’Italia. The far-right leader attempted to ease concerns raised by her party's historic victory in September. She assured that Italy's approach was not to "slow down and sabotage European integration," but to make the community machinery work better, in her general policy speech before the Chamber of Deputies, which later in the evening voted confidence in her government. The vote, to be followed by a similar procedure in the Senate on Wednesday, was purely procedural, as Ms. Meloni had a comfortable majority in the Chamber thanks to Silvio Berlusconi's Forza Italia party and the League, the far-right party of Matteo Salvini. The successor to Mario Draghi, whom she also paid tribute to in her speech, is assured of winning this vote of confidence, as her coalition has an absolute majority in both chambers. "A reliable NATO partner" "Italy will respect European rules," Ms. Meloni also assured, although Rome also wants to "help change those that do not work." The EU is "a common home to face challenges that member states can hardly face alone," she said, judging that Europe had not done enough in this area in the past. Giorgia Meloni promised that Italy would remain "a reliable partner of NATO in support of Ukraine, which is resisting Russia's aggression." The new Italian leader affirmed: "Giving in to Putin's energy blackmail would not solve the problem, it would worsen it, opening the way to new claims and blackmail and future energy price increases even higher than those we have experienced in recent months." "Stopping illegal departures" Ms. Meloni also sought to reassure about her party's stance, denying any "sympathy" or "proximity to antidemocratic regimes." "For no regime, including fascism," insisted the former admirer of Mussolini in her youth. Regarding the issue of immigration, the new Prime Minister stated on Tuesday that her government intends to "stop illegal departures" from Africa to the Peninsula and "break the human trafficking" in the Mediterranean. As inflation rose by 8.9% year-on-year in September, she committed as a "priority" to "strengthen support measures for households and businesses, both for energy bills and fuel." "An financial commitment that will drain a large portion of the available resources," she acknowledged.
lead: "The leader of the far-right party also stated in front of the deputies that Italy will respect European rules, and she defended herself against any sympathy for fascism."

title: « France : les réseaux Poutine », sur France 2 : entre séduction et désinformation
text: FRANCE 2 - THURSDAY, OCTOBER 27 AT 11 PM - DOCUMENTARY On this February 11, Frédéric Petit, a member of the Democratic group (MoDem and Independents) representing the 7th constituency of French citizens living abroad (Germany, Central Europe, and the Balkans), cannot foresee that he will be trapped when entering the Luxembourg Palace. Believing he is attending a parliamentary meeting on the situation in Ukraine, his speech is broadcast live on a pro-Russian Ukrainian television channel, interspersed with statements from close associates of Vladimir Putin, such as the Ukrainian oligarch Viktor Medvedchuk or the deputy Leonid Slutsky. The latter is "banned from Europe," specifies Laure Pollez, one of the three journalists who investigated the Poutine networks in France, aired on the evening of Thursday, October 27, but the only one to appear on screen. The documentary, as we have seen it, does not mention that Viktor Medvedchuk was arrested on April 12 by Ukrainian forces, detained, and exchanged on September 22. Instead, it focuses on uncovering how such a deception was orchestrated. After this enticing introduction, Vladimir Putin's charm offensive towards French (right-wing) leaders is illustrated by a segment dedicated to the relationship between the Russian president and François Fillon, tracing back to their friendship's origins in Sochi in 2008 when both men were prime ministers. The Kremlin's connections with members of the RN (Marine Le Pen, Thierry Mariani, Philippe Olivier) are showcased through exclusive documents provided by The Dossier Center, an organization led by Vladimir Putin's opponent, Mikhail Khodorkovsky, and through testimonies from diplomats. The final part, focusing on information manipulation, unfolds like fiction. It starts with a report on RT France, the Russian channel banned from broadcasting since March 2 (the documentary explains why), where only the presenter Samantha Ramsamy speaks openly. Standing up to Western powers, Laure Pollez's investigations then take her to Africa (where RT is broadcast) to dissect the false "mass grave" incident in Gossi, Mali - two days after the official departure of the "Barkhane" force from that base, a tweet was posted on April 21: "This is what the French left behind," with a photo of buried bodies. "Wagner's militiamen staged this scene," claims France; Russia denies it. The inquiry provides a unique glimpse into the workings of Viginum, the new French unit established to combat foreign interference. It reveals how MediaPatriot, the media group owned by Yevgeny Prigozhin (head of the Wagner mercenary group), attempted to discredit Emmanuel Macron before the 2022 presidential election. But who is behind these fake articles? How are they published? An article provided ready-made is paid 300 euros to be published in Cotonou (Benin). Russia sends aid and stands up to Westerners, explains an African journalist, "and it is appreciated." Disinformation has a bright future ahead. Despite its ban, RT France is once again broadcasting its programs live and in French via the conspiracy platform Odysee.
lead: An investigation for "Complément d'enquête" explores, from RT France to connections in Africa, passing through the digital tracking unit Viginum, how the Kremlin plays cat and mouse with French politics.

title: TotalEnergies : Patrick Pouyanné, un patron brut
text: That's so typical of him. On Tuesday, October 18, after three weeks of blockades at refineries and fuel depots, Patrick Pouyanné was "tired." Tired of hearing repeated claims that he had "increased his salary by 52% in 2021." So, the CEO of TotalEnergies "threw out" a tweet. Just like that, all by himself. He justified this 2021 increase by explaining that it was meant to bring his compensation back to its normal level of around 6 million euros annually, as he had "voluntarily" reduced it in 2020, the year of Covid-19. The boss thought he was setting the record straight mathematically. Instead, he was publicly criticized. Mocked by members of the New Popular Ecological and Social Union (Nupes) in unison, labeled "out of touch" by Laurent Berger, the head of the CFDT union. Even the Minister of Economy and Finance, Bruno Le Maire, who had been equally scolding both the strikers and the TotalEnergies management during the conflict, called on business leaders to show "basic decency." "France is a strange society where I should be ashamed and apologize for what I earn" – Patrick Pouyanné, CEO of TotalEnergies Faced with this general outrage, many would dodge the issue, attributing it to a moment of confusion. Not Patrick Pouyanné. "I'm not out of touch, it's just that I can't stand dishonesty. That's precisely why I chose the business world over politics," the industrialist who was an advisor to Edouard Balladur at Matignon (1993-1995) and chief of staff to François Fillon at the Ministry of Information Technologies (1995-1997) before joining Elf Aquitaine twenty-five years ago, told Le Monde. And he added provocatively: "At least we're not talking about the 52% anymore, the debate has shifted to the absolute value of my compensation. I stand by it. France is a strange society where I should be ashamed and apologize for what I earn." "Dialogue of the deaf" At 59, the man who has been leading the world's fourth-largest oil company since 2014 has become a divisive public figure. Even before the pump incident, he had been involved in all the societal upheavals this year, from the invasion of Ukraine to the energy crisis, from taxing superprofits to climate urgency. In the days of "Les Guignols de l'info," this Polytechnique graduate built like a rugby forward would have had his puppet, like the famous "J6M" for "Jean-Marie Messier, master of the world," the former head of Vivendi. Undoubtedly, the CEO of TotalEnergies would have been parodied by the cult Canal+ show, as a giant straight out of "Tintin in the Land of Black Gold," with a keffiyeh and jerrican, renamed "6P" ("Patrick Pouyanné, oil polluter profits Putin"). Instead, he got a hashtag, comparing the "contribution to society over compensation" ratio of a business leader and a footballer. If their show of strength against the CGT has caused the government's popularity to drop, Patrick Pouyanné sympathizes, but no more. "I manage a company with a long-term perspective. It's not the one who shouts the loudest who is right," he insists, more saddened by the troubles faced by motorists. "We witnessed a real dialogue of the deaf, with, on one side, the management, which refused to negotiate under pressure, claiming that there was a scheduled meeting, and, on the other side, the CGT, which did not help foster social dialogue," laments Geoffrey Caillon, CFDT TotalEnergies coordinator. "We distributed the equivalent of 15% of our payroll." The CEO acknowledges a misjudgment. "The decision to pay one month's salary to all of our 100,000 employees worldwide was made in July," he reveals. On July 28, the multinational corporation reported a record net profit of $10.6 billion (€10.7 billion) for the first half of the year. "I had informed the unions in September that we would be generous. My HR director had suggested specifying the amount, but I preferred to wait for the negotiations that started in November," he admits. A human resources department that later decided, during the conflict, to make public the average salaries of the refinery workers. When gas stations found themselves in dire straits, Patrick Pouyanné claims to have been caught between two fires. On one side, Prime Minister Elisabeth Borne urged him to resolve the conflict, and on the other, reformist unions as well as the rest of the employers secretly encouraged him not to give in to the CGT. In fact, on Tuesday, October 18, during the small celebration for the 40th anniversary of the French Association of Private Enterprises, the lobby of major business leaders, some of his peers rebuffed him in the aisles of the Centre Pompidou in Paris, concerned that the 7% salary increase granted by TotalEnergies would become a benchmark. "We distributed the equivalent of 15% of our payroll. I don't think many French companies do that," says the energy company executive. Traveling in mid-October to San Diego, California, and then to Qatar, the CEO managed everything remotely, appearing sometimes with disheveled hair in the middle of the night. Should he have stayed in France? He dismisses it: "Teams works great. I run a global company, business must go on." "There is enough to put the executives in custody." A matter of habit: leading TotalEnergies means managing crises. Even tragedies.
