The sinking of the oil tanker Erika off the coast of Brittany in 1999, followed two years later by the deadly explosion of the AZF factory near Toulouse, tarnished the company's reputation in France. To the point that Christophe de Margerie, the previous CEO, had toyed with the idea of offering "Total doormats" on which the French could vent their frustrations at will. Perhaps it should be considered again? Not a day goes by without the multinational being targeted, dragged to court over its oil or gas activities in Myanmar (the group eventually left the country), in Uganda, Mozambique, or South Africa. "TotalEnergies embodies the face of evil in the 21st century. Its obsession with profit endangers humanity" - Claire Nouvian, founder of the marine conservation association Bloom. Claire Nouvian, the founder of the marine conservation association Bloom, whose father was also an executive at Total, minces no words: "TotalEnergies embodies the face of evil in the 21st century. Its obsession with profit endangers humanity." Raphaël Glucksmann, Member of the European Parliament (Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament), adds: "Total is emblematic of this profit-driven race at the expense of human rights." On his Twitter account, MP François Ruffin (Nupes) chimes in: "There is enough to put the [group's] leaders in custody." The disgrace spreads like an oil spill, sticking to Patrick Pouyanné like oil on a seagull's wings. Even in his own stronghold, the Ecole Polytechnique. In June, during the graduation ceremony on the Saclay campus (Essonne), where X-Mines was acting as the patron of the promotion, some students turned their backs on him. Unprecedented. The insult was limited: having had a scheduling conflict, he had recorded a video. "The young generation remains in the 'just have to' mentality." And to think that the students themselves had chosen him. At X, tradition dictates that the outgoing class designates the patron for the new admits. A mentor with a generous streak, who, as a reward for this honor, donates over 100,000 euros to the Kès (the student union), to fund beer nights and ski weekends. In early 2017, the young engineers had therefore chosen TotalEnergies to pamper the X 2017 class. "That was before the ecological awakening of X," notes Benoit Halgand, one of the rebellious graduates. Following this, Jacques Biot, then president of the school, had proposed to Patrick Pouyanné, during a visit to the campus, to set up a research center on renewable energies there. "I thought I was doing good by putting our 200 to 300 researchers at Saclay rather than in the United States, as some members of my executive committee recommended. The authorities were delighted," recounts the boss, before concluding: "I was naive." In January 2022, he threw in the towel in the face of strong opposition from students against the invasion of the private sector, supported by Greenpeace and Anticor. "He did not expect this resistance at all. He did not anticipate this shift in the new generation," continues Benoit Halgand. The father of four children defends himself: "I listen to society. I have two sons with strong environmental convictions. That makes me think. The younger generation accuses us of not taking our responsibilities. But they stay in the 'just have to' mindset. It's up to us to convince them that our path is the right one." In 2022, nearly 17 billion euros distributed to shareholders. Patrick Pouyanné is certainly not an environmentalist. Yet, even his critics acknowledge that he accelerated, ahead of his competitors, the shift to move Total away from oil. And he is evolving. In 2016, he declared that "electricity is the future of energy." A shock in a company dominated by exploration-production lords. It's as if, at Haribo, they declared "the future belongs to kale." "At one point, I thought he was going to lose Total," said a board member who requested anonymity. His strategy is to use oil revenues to invest in renewable energies: 4 billion euros in 2022. "This puts us in the top five globally," the CEO assures. "We will increase it to 6 billion. I wrote to my 60 country heads to present me with multi-energy projects." "I warned my competitors: soon, we will be accused of orchestrating oil scarcity" - Patrick Pouyanné However, in 2022, he distributed nearly 17 billion euros to shareholders in the form of dividends and share buybacks. Some in the financial community see this as evidence of "cynicism": "Climate is a gimmick, dividends remain Patrick Pouyanné's priority," says an investor. "We face conflicting demands. I also get scolded by shareholders who want me to invest more in oil, with Brent near $100," retorts the CEO. And he brings the oil rig back to the village square: "Crude prices didn't rise because of the war in Ukraine; it started before. Supply is decreasing as oil companies reduce their investments, and demand is still there. I warned my competitors: soon, we will be accused of orchestrating oil scarcity." "Disruptive for politicians" "The speech is well rehearsed," sighs a renowned climatologist. "Oil companies present themselves as benefactors of humanity, of the middle class, by supporting lower fuel prices and helping poor countries in need of energy. Even if Total is not the worst major, its core business, whether we like it or not, remains exploration." Cécile Duflot, the former leader of the Greens, now head of Oxfam France, concludes: "TotalEnergies' model is not suited to a scenario that limits global warming to 2°C. It is up to the State to create the conditions that drive companies to change." Now, the revolt of the "yellow vests" or the midterm elections in the United States (on November 8) are here to remind us of an axiom that Patrick Pouyanné always keeps in mind: nothing is more political than energy. Joe Biden, the Democratic president, who had slowed down the granting of drilling permits in the United States after his election in 2020, saw his popularity plummet as gasoline prices rose. Since then, he has urged Chevron and ExxonMobil to pump, pump like Shadoks on amphetamines. It is probably because of these ambiguities that the politicians approached by Le Monde for this portrait did not respond. "Patrick Pouyanné is unsettling for politicians. He is not just any CEO. He is the boss who faces energy crises, inflation, superprofits, and climate issues all at once. It is difficult for them to speak positively about him. And they cannot speak negatively either, because TotalEnergies is also a source of French pride," says Julien Vaulpré, co-founder of the consulting firm Taddeo. "Moreover, he likes to stand his ground, which personalizes conflicts. He emphasizes his rationality, but today, that is not enough to convince." "Do you think we are not human beings?" Some elected officials even treat him like a pariah. At the request of the mayor of Lyon, Grégory Doucet (Europe Ecologie-Les Verts), the Opera and the Museum of Fine Arts in the city deprived themselves of subsidies from the TotalEnergies Foundation, one of the richest in France, in 2022. In 2019, during the municipal election campaign, Anne Hidalgo (Socialist Party), mayor of Paris, took a stand against TotalEnergies becoming a partner of the 2024 Olympic Games. "We were being courted by the authorities. We were hesitating. As soon as the controversy started, we decided to stop," explains Mr. Pouyanné, before adding in a tough-guy tone: "We're not going to spend 100 million euros just to get slapped." However, behind this tough facade, a thinly veiled bitterness emerges. When asked what it feels like to be the most hated CEO in France, for once, he avoids the question: "There is a Parisian aspect to all this. With the discounts, a month and a half ago, we were the most loved." In reality, this animosity affects him. So much so that sometimes his emotions overwhelm him. In March, he rushed to RTL, a few hours after an attack by Yannick Jadot, the Green Party candidate for president, against the oil company, a shareholder in several Russian companies. "I am an angry CEO. When Mr. Jadot accuses the 100,000 employees of TotalEnergies of being complicit in war crimes, it is very serious. Do you think we are not human beings?" he defended himself, his voice breaking, holding up a photo of two Ukrainian employees fighting on the front lines. "He had good relations with Putin," Libération saw it as a performance "worthy of the Cours Florent." Yet, Patrick Pouyanné is not an actor. Russia is a sensitive subject for him and for the group, far beyond the economic aspect. It was at an airport in Moscow that Christophe de Margerie met his death on October 20, 2014, when his Falcon-50 collided with a snowplow. For a long time, "Papou" - as some call him internally - could not mention his mentor without tears in his eyes. "I cannot break the contract in Russia, unless I am expropriated by the Russians or if Europe imposes sanctions," - Patrick Pouyanné Under the leadership of "Big Moustache," the nickname of the deceased CEO, the French group embarked on an incredibly complex project: to build a natural gas liquefaction plant in the Yamal Peninsula, in Siberia, 600 kilometers north of the Arctic Circle, where temperatures can reach -50 °C. A technological feat that made this engineering company proud. In 2017, the first LNG cargo was shipped to Europe in the holds of an ice-breaking LNG carrier adorned with beautiful white bacchantes, named Christophe de Margerie by Vladimir Putin himself. Just a month after his appointment, Patrick Pouyanné was received by the Russian president at his residence in Sochi, on the Black Sea coast. "He had good relations with Putin. He was a key player in the economic relationship with Russia. Once a year, he led the CEOs of the CAC 40 to meet him," said Sylvie Bermann, former French ambassador to Moscow. Such was the relationship that President François Hollande, acting as a relay for Pierre Ferracci, owner of Paris FC, had asked the TotalEnergies CEO to intercede with the Kremlin for a Russian investor to support the Parisian Ligue 2 club. Patrick Pouyanné complied in Saint Petersburg, at a dinner for the Mariinsky Theatre patrons organized by Vladimir Putin, quickly put in his place by the Russian president. A "parallel diplomacy" "Regarding Russia, it's not a matter of emotion. There was a violent aggression that we condemn," the CEO cuts in, "on the contrary, I seek to bring rationality to protect the company." He adds: "When my competitors say they have left Russia, it's false. The issue for me is that TotalEnergies has committed to buying liquefied natural gas for twenty-five years. I cannot break this contract, unless I am expropriated by the Russians or if Europe imposes sanctions." Like all CEOs of major oil companies, Patrick Pouyanné deals with the powers that be, placing him in a unique category within the French establishment. And beyond. In September, as the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, was heading to Qatar to secure Germany's LNG supply, the French CEO was signing a major contract with the emirate and posing with a smile next to the Minister of Energy, an old acquaintance from his years in Doha between 1999 and 2002. In a country like Angola, where the French oil company refines 50% of the country's hydrocarbon production, the head of the TotalEnergies subsidiary easily secures a meeting for a minister on a trip than the French ambassador. This has led to accusations of conducting a "parallel diplomacy" against the multinational: the CEO "can be received in certain countries with the respect worthy of a head of state, and the group can use this to pressure French diplomacy," denounces the NGO Friends of the Earth. "He is respected because he is a man of his word." "Another fantasy," retorts the CEO, "when I meet the Emir of Qatar, I talk to him about my business. Not about France's. However, I can be useful to French diplomacy." He recounts that, following a visit to India a few weeks after the Russian invasion of Ukraine, he alerted the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs about the North-South divergence regarding the perception of the conflict. He emphasizes that on October 11, TotalEnergies signed an agreement to exploit a gas field in Lebanese waters under a treaty negotiated under the auspices of the White House, in consultation with the Elysée, concerning the maritime borders between Lebanon and Israel. This did not prevent Emmanuel Macron from distancing himself from the group regarding the controversial gas pipeline that is supposed to connect Uganda and Tanzania. "The State does not finance this project and does not support it either," declared the president on France 2 on Wednesday, October 12. While Christophe de Margerie charmed with his friendliness, his successor cultivates a more direct style. "He is respected because he is a man of his word, which counts a lot internationally," assures Momar Nguer, former member of the TotalEnergies executive committee. Whether enjoying very spicy curry at Indian billionaire Gautam Adani's or Wiener schnitzels in Davos, his dining manners impress as much as his geopolitical analyses. "I've seen major competitors seek his advice," testifies Bertrand Deroubaix, former head of public affairs at the group. "When he gives you a dressing down, it's staggering." Patrick Pouyanné travels the world in two Bombardier business jets acquired by the group. In July, he was called out by the Twitter account @l’aviondebernard, which tracks billionaires' jets, for flying between Paris and Marseille just after calling for "moderation" among the French. When asked if he plans to switch to trains for domestic travel, his "no" is firm and final: "These two planes are tools for me and my colleagues. During the Covid crisis, we were very happy to be able to continue traveling to sign contracts, in Iraq, for example." And to specify, like a grand lord, that these jets run on nearly 40% biofuel, produced in one of TotalEnergies' refineries, of course. "The most precious thing is my time and that of my colleagues," he explains. Everything at his place is timed. Woe to anyone who hands him a double-sided printed document, because he only tolerates single-sided, which he finds more efficient to read. He consumes reports, PowerPoints, and other business reviews. In the shadow of the photocopier, near his office on the 44th floor of the tower in La Défense, near Paris, suitcases lie around. Not to store the boss's shirts, but to stuff them with files on Friday evening, to go through during the weekend. He even reads emails in his inbox from disgruntled motorists about the cleanliness of a gas station's toilets. And then, he bombards his teams with messages, no matter the hour. He gets involved in everything, decides everything, down to the seating plan for a lunch. Working next to this steamroller-like volcano requires a lot of physical and moral resistance. When Patrick Pouyanné was head of refining, his team would put candies on the meeting table to relax the poor souls facing his questioning. He has crushed many collaborators, not competent enough, not fast enough for his taste. "Where there's a will, there's a way," he simply replies, having adopted his father's maxim, who was a regional customs director. Met in a Parisian café, a former executive of the group, who left several years ago, is on the verge of tears when recalling that period of her life: "When he scolds you, it's shocking. It turns your brain into mush. Everyone looks at their feet. I wish I could have stood up and said, 'Enough.'" "He has gained confidence." "He speaks his mind, whether it's a quality or a flaw," sums up a close associate. "Patrick has improved," assures a board member, "he was thrust into the leadership of Total during a difficult period when the price of oil was collapsing. Since then, he has gained confidence. The more self-assured you are, the more you trust others." If most of his collaborators endure his outbursts of anger – the others leave – it's because they believe that Patrick Pouyanné manages the company well. He breathes TotalEnergies, he lives TotalEnergies, which he wants to leave his mark on: he changed the name in 2021, launched the construction of the tallest tower in La Défense to house the headquarters. By the end of the year, he plans for the group to be debt-free. Ready for a new cycle? In 2024, the former French Petroleum Company will celebrate its 100th anniversary, and Patrick Pouyanné will mark ten years of reign... and conflicts.
lead: At 59 years old, the person who has been leading TotalEnergies since 2014 has become a divisive public figure. From the invasion of Ukraine to the energy crisis, from taxing windfall profits to the climate emergency, he has been involved in all the upheavals of the year.

title: Colère en Iran, drones kamikazes en Ukraine, 49.3 en France… Le mois d’octobre en dessins
text: In October, the news was notably marked by the continuation of the Iranians' mobilization against the mullahs' regime, a fuel shortage in France caused by strikes in refineries, the resignation of Liz Truss after only forty-four days at Downing Street. And the continuation of the war in Ukraine, where Russia now uses kamikaze drones. "La Matinale" offers you an overview in eleven drawings, classified by theme. Article 49.3 and fuel shortage in France Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 12, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The fake suspense ended on October 19 in the National Assembly when the Prime Minister, Elisabeth Borne, engaged her government's responsibility by triggering Article 49.3 to pass the first part of the budget bill without a vote. The next day, the same process was repeated for the revenue side of the Social Security budget. The motions of censure subsequently filed by the New Popular Ecological and Social Union (Nupes) and the National Rally were unsurprisingly rejected on October 24 by the deputies. Two days later, Elisabeth Borne again used Article 49.3 to pass the entire Social Security financing bill. Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 20, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The strike by employees of TotalEnergies refineries, demanding salary increases, led to fuel shortages and long queues at gas stations. By Wednesday, October 26, the movement was only affecting two sites of the group, in Normandy and in the Rhône, but more than 20% of gas stations were still facing supply difficulties in twenty-five departments, with the Paris region being particularly affected. War in Ukraine Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 11, 2022, signed by the Ukrainian cartoonist Vladimir Kazanevsky. KAZANEVSKY Peace negotiations with Moscow are currently impossible because "no one believes" the Russian authorities, said Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on October 10. His defense minister, Oleksii Reznikov, ruled out any territorial compromise with Russia, especially regarding Crimea, annexed by Moscow in 2014, and the Donbass, partly controlled by separatists supported by Moscow, where Moscow organized "referendums" for annexation. Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 21, 2022, signed by the Mexican cartoonist Darío. DARÍO Russia used kamikaze drones to attack Ukrainian cities, including the capital, Kiev, on October 17. These Shahed-136 drones, of Iranian origin and repainted in Russian colors, are used to target civilian areas and terrorize populations. According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, Moscow has ordered around 2,000 of these military vehicles from Tehran. The European Union (EU) reacted on October 20 by adopting a series of sanctions against Iran. Political instability in the United Kingdom Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 26, 2022, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIM The swift resignation of Liz Truss on October 20, after only forty-four days at the helm of the United Kingdom, marks the failure of the promises made by Brexit supporters during the 2016 referendum. The project to transform the island into a tax haven "liberated" from the social, financial, and environmental rules of the EU had been set aside by Boris Johnson, due to Covid-19. Liz Truss, on the other hand, wanted to implement it, causing financial panic and leading the country to the brink of collapse. Rishi Sunak succeeded her on October 25, becoming the third Conservative British Prime Minister of 2022. Presidential election in Brazil Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 5, 2022, signed by Swiss cartoonist Herrmann. HERRMANN In Brazil, Jair Bolsonaro will face Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva in the second round of the presidential election on Sunday, October 30. The incumbent president had achieved a higher score than predicted by polling institutes in the first round on October 2, gathering 43.2% of the votes. Since the beginning of his term on January 1, 2019, the far-right leader has systematically dismantled nature protection agencies. Since that date, deforestation in all ecosystems has reached 42,000 square kilometers, equivalent to the surface area of Switzerland, and that of the Amazon, in particular, has increased by 75% compared to the previous decade. Protests in Iran Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 6, 2022, signed by Algerian cartoonist Dilem. DILEM The anger of the population in Iran shows no signs of abating, six weeks after the death on September 16 of Mahsa Amini, a 22-year-old Iranian Kurdish woman, following her arrest by the morality police for violating the strict dress code of the Islamic Republic. Her death sparked a widespread protest movement against the regime of the mullahs, with Iranian women, many without headscarves, at the forefront. FIFA World Cup in Qatar Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 13, 2022, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIM One month before the opening of the FIFA World Cup on November 20 in Qatar, a sense of unease is spreading in French society. In the media and on social networks, the debate is growing on the relevance of watching the competition. The main reasons cited include: The working conditions often deplorable inflicted on the workers who built the tournament's infrastructure, the numerous deaths on the construction sites, and the high environmental cost of the upcoming competition. CCP Congress in China Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 18, 2022, signed by the Taiwanese cartoonist Stellina. STELLINA The 20th Congress of the Chinese Communist Party, held from October 16 to 22, saw Xi Jinping re-elected as the party's leader, ensuring him a third presidential term in March 2023. Xi had broken a taboo in 2018 by amending the Constitution to serve more than two presidential terms. The closing ceremony was marked by the eviction of former President Hu Jintao (2003-2013), escorted out apparently against his will. Winter Games in... Saudi Arabia Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 7, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The Olympic Council of Asia (OCA) announced on Tuesday, October 4, that the desert kingdom of Saudi Arabia will host the 2029 Asian Winter Games. The events will take place in Neom, a futuristic megacity under construction in the northwest of the kingdom, a project worth several hundred billion dollars led by Crown Prince Mohammed Ben Salman.
lead: Once a month, "La Matinale" offers you a review of the news in illustrations, signed by members of the international collective of cartoonists Cartooning for Peace and published on the front page of "Le Monde".

title: Russie : la fusillade qui a fait au moins onze tués sur une base militaire soulève des questions
text: Two days after the incident, the deadly shooting that occurred on Saturday, October 15 at the Russian military base in Soloti, in the Belgorod region, continues to raise many questions. These questions stem from the minimal communication from the Russian Ministry of Defense, which only issued a brief statement ten hours after the incident. According to the ministry, eleven soldiers were killed and fifteen others were injured in what is described as a "terrorist attack" carried out by "two citizens from a CIS country" - the Commonwealth of Independent States, comprising nine former Soviet republics. "During a shooting training exercise (...), the terrorists opened fire with automatic weapons" before being shot down "in a return fire," the military further writes, without providing more details on the identity of the victims and the perpetrators of the shooting. The exact number of casualties is also subject to debate. Several independent media outlets have cited witnesses who mentioned a higher number of fatalities. Sota, one of the very few independent media outlets still maintaining teams in Russia, reports a death toll of twenty-two and sixteen injured, based on an anonymous testimony. The same source also mentions a third assailant who managed to escape. Independent media and bloggers accustomed to relaying information from the army or the FSB (Russian security service) also agree that these assailants were Tajik citizens who volunteered for the "special operation" in Ukraine. Since September, Moscow has been offering Russian nationality to foreigners who volunteer. "Cannon fodder" Astra, a media outlet formed in exile by former members of several dissolved publications, quotes another witness who provides a fairly detailed, albeit independently unverifiable, account of the events. According to this source, who is reportedly among the injured, a conflict arose when three mobilized soldiers from different Muslim regions of Russia stated they did not want to fight. The senior officer at the base then gathered the soldiers to rally them, mentioning a "holy war" and asserting that "Allah must be a coward if he does not allow you to fight for your country." These words reportedly sparked outrage among Muslim soldiers and even led to a confrontation. It was an hour and a half later, as a training session was about to begin, that the two or three Tajiks allegedly opened fire on their comrades, armed with automatic weapons and a machine gun. This source mentions twenty-nine killed, including the senior officer. It also claims that the Tajiks in question had repeatedly complained about not being able to perform their prayers. There is also doubt regarding the identity of the victims. Since the end of September, several testimonies have revealed that the Soloti base houses around a hundred conscripts from the Bryansk region who refuse to go into combat. These soldiers claim that their officers want to send them into battle in the Lyman region after minimal training. On October 15th, a group of women from the Bryansk region even published a video addressing Vladimir Putin to explain that their husbands and sons were being treated as "cannon fodder," untrained, subjected to pressure, and even theft. Accidents and suicides The precise identity of the victims - conscripts or volunteers - is an important point, as the mobilization declared on September 21st has caused tensions in Russia. The first deaths are now being sent back from the front. In total, seventeen conscripts have also died on Russian soil (not counting those from Soloti), victims of accidents, sudden health problems, or suicides. Among the many violations of the rules meant to regulate the mobilization, the systematic absence of medical examinations or even brief interviews is highlighted. Even observers close to the government fear that the army is incorporating thousands of men with unsuitable profiles. On October 14th, Vladimir Putin stated that the mobilization process could last another two weeks. However, recent days have seen an intensification of these efforts. Videos from across Russia show men being arrested on the streets or in public transport, and recruiters going as far as positioning themselves in building lobbies to distribute summonses. Independent reports also mention summonses being handed out in recent days to Central Asian nationals with only residence permits.
lead: Soldiers were killed on Saturday near Belgorod in what the Russian Ministry of Defense is presenting as "a terrorist attack." Other sources mention Muslim soldiers who refused to fight.

title: Russie : Alexeï Navalny dit être visé par de nouvelles accusations passibles de trente ans de prison
text: In Russia, imprisoned opposition leader Alexei Navalny claimed on Thursday, October 20, to be facing new criminal charges for "promoting terrorism," "inciting extremism," "financing extremist activities," and "rehabilitating Nazism." "The lawyers have calculated that it adds up to about thirty years [in prison], taking into account the penalties provided for each of these articles in the criminal code," he said in a message released by his team on social media. The 46-year-old anti-corruption activist, considered the main critic of Russian President Vladimir Putin, specified that he had received a notification informing him of the opening of this new criminal case while he is already imprisoned. Already sentenced to nine years in prison, he sarcastically remarked, "I am a criminal genius of the world. (...) You all thought I had been in isolation, in prison for two years, but in fact I was actively committing crimes," praising Russian investigators for their "vigilance." According to him, these new accusations are partly related to videos published by his allies in exile, who continue to advocate against the Russian government from abroad. Alexei Navalny was arrested in January 2021 upon his return to Russia after surviving a serious poisoning attempt, which he attributes to the Kremlin. In March 2022, he was sentenced to nine years in a "strict" regime for what he considers to be fictitious charges of "fraud." He continues to convey messages denouncing Vladimir Putin and his intervention in Ukraine to his lawyers, which are then published online by his team. This summer, he repeatedly claimed to have been placed in a disciplinary cell in his penal colony near Vladimir, 200 kilometers east of Moscow.
lead: The 46-year-old opposition figure, considered to be the main critic of Vladimir Putin, specified that he had received a notification informing him of the opening of this new criminal case while he is already imprisoned.

title: Rishi Sunak affiche son soutien à l’Ukraine lors de ses premiers échanges diplomatiques
text: It was Volodymyr Zelensky who received the honor of the first call from Rishi Sunak to a foreign leader. The new British Prime Minister assured the Ukrainian President of his "unwavering support" and hoped that they would "meet in person soon," according to a statement from Downing Street. Earlier, Mr. Zelensky had congratulated the new British leader. "I wish you success in overcoming all the challenges facing British society and the world today. I am ready to continue strengthening the Ukrainian-British strategic partnership together," said the Ukrainian President on Twitter. Ukraine was at the heart of the first words addressed by U.S. President Joe Biden to the new British Prime Minister. "I am pleased that we are strengthening our cooperation on essential global security and prosperity issues, including continuing to firmly support Ukraine," he wrote on Twitter. During their first phone call later in the day, the two leaders also agreed "to hold Russia accountable for its aggression, confront the challenges posed by China, and ensure sustainable and affordable energy resources," emphasized the White House in a statement. Congratulations from Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz Congratulating Mr. Sunak, Emmanuel Macron wrote on Twitter: "Together, we will continue to work to address the current challenges, including the war in Ukraine and its multiple consequences for Europe and the world." As for German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, he said he was "looking forward to continuing our cooperation and partnership within NATO and the G7 as close friends." In Moscow, however, where British support for Ukraine in the form of diplomatic support, arms deliveries, and combat training for Ukrainian soldiers is clearly viewed negatively, the tone was decidedly cold. "At this moment, we see no prerequisites, no basis, and we have no hope for any positive changes in the near future," reacted Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov to the press. He assured that Russia remained "open and ready to discuss difficult issues at the negotiating table," but not "at the expense of its interests." The European Commission attentive to Brexit compliance The European Commission urged Mr. Sunak to "fully" respect the agreements reached with the European Union after Brexit. "In these difficult times for our continent, we rely on a strong relationship with the UK to defend our common values, in full respect of our agreements," wrote the President of the Brussels executive, Ursula von der Leyen, on Twitter. "I remain determined to work intensively and constructively to promote such a partnership, in full respect of our agreements. This includes our work on common solutions within the framework of the protocol for Ireland and Northern Ireland," stated the European Commissioner responsible for post-Brexit relations with the UK, Maros Sefcovic. This topic was discussed during a meeting between Joe Biden and Rishi Sunak. The two leaders "discussed their shared commitment to protecting the achievements of the Good Friday Agreement in Northern Ireland," as reported by the White House, and the "need to reach a negotiated agreement with the European Union on the Northern Ireland Protocol," which outlines the post-Brexit arrangements in this British province. Support from Boris Johnson Former Conservative Prime Minister Boris Johnson had revisited the key text of the Brexit agreement, the Northern Ireland Protocol, signed and ratified by both parties, and unilaterally questioned the post-Brexit customs status of Northern Ireland. Mr. Johnson was in fact one of the first personalities to publicly congratulate his former Chancellor of the Exchequer, despite their notoriously strained relationship: "Congratulations to Rishi Sunak on this historic day," tweeted the former leader. "It is time for every Conservative to give our new Prime Minister their full and unwavering support."
lead: The new Prime Minister of the United Kingdom has spoken with Volodymyr Zelensky and Joe Biden, after receiving congratulations from numerous heads of state.

title: En Russie, « la mobilisation remet en question les fondements du pouvoir poutinien »
text: Lev Gudkov is a sociologist and scientific director of the Levada Center, the last independent polling institute in Russia, classified as a "foreign agent" by the authorities. To what extent can we trust the polls conducted in Russia? The data we collect is reliable. Those who claim that polls cannot be trusted in an authoritarian or totalitarian regime, and that people are afraid to respond, have a poor understanding of these issues. This is often the case with liberals who struggle to accept that they are in the minority. Refusals to respond are around 40%. However, most of the time, these are technical refusals from people who are busy and do not have the time. We estimate that refusals motivated by political considerations are around 10% to 15% of those surveyed. They are mainly found in two categories of the population: elderly, less educated women living in small towns, who are loyal to the government but feel they have no political opinion to express; and young people who show no interest. In contrast, educated men in large cities want to voice their opinions. They are against the regime and have no other means of expression. Is the support for the "special military operation" real and widespread in the Russian population? Formally, it stands at around 75%. However, in my opinion, it would be more accurate to speak of an absence of resistance or opposition rather than support or endorsement. We study the level of declared support, as well as the emotions triggered by specific events. We observe that the "special operation" evokes mixed feelings. There is indeed pride, satisfaction, but also, at the same level and often in the same individuals, negative emotions: shock, confusion, depression, despair, shame... This indicates significant dissatisfaction, even guilt, and also a clear awareness that Vladimir Putin's blitzkrieg has failed. "It's a mentality of subjects rather than citizens, based on fear and dependence on the state." Furthermore, the hypocritical term "special military operation" was chosen precisely to reassure. The justifications for its initiation have constantly evolved without coherence. Today, what the Russian public is being told is about a total war triggered by the West... All of this is part of a context of propaganda and censorship. Trust in television has dropped from 70% to 55% in a few years, which is still a high level. At the same time, all other sources of information have been eliminated, and opposition and civil society structures destroyed. Do people believe in the reasons used to justify the conflict? To a large extent, yes. Once again, there is only one opinion, which is based on the firmly rooted idea that Russia is a besieged fortress. Anti-Ukrainian propaganda is also old. It began in 2004, with the "Orange Revolution" [after Viktor Yanukovych's victory in the Ukrainian presidential election, whose results were deemed falsified by the opposition], and then grew in intensity and aggressiveness after 2014, with the annexation of Crimea and the start of the war in Donbass. The result is that people expected a major war. In recent years, steadily, over 70% of respondents said they feared a war. This is a very high level, placing this concern in second position, after concerns about the health of loved ones. "Skull - Russia," 2019, Mr. Strange. MR STRANGE Opinions seem very changeable. The same surveys show that 60% of Russians would support Vladimir Putin if he launched a new attack on Kiev, and 75% would support him if he immediately stopped hostilities... That's the key point. These opinions are primarily guided by conformity and identification with the State. In a significant part of society, the prevailing idea is that the State is a master who is always right and is accountable to no one. It is a mentality of subjects rather than citizens, based on fear and dependence on the State. The State also confers an identity: "If the State is strong, then so am I, despite poverty, corruption, humiliation..." "The 'Homo Putinus' is indeed an extension of the 'Homo Sovieticus.' It is the same but more cynical, and with even less faith in the future." This is why, once again, I speak of an absence of opposition rather than support. It is a passive position, without cost or responsibility. Only 10% of respondents say they feel responsible for what is happening in Ukraine. For a large part of Russians, politics, like war today, is a field that has nothing to do with them. [The German political scientist and philosopher] Hannah Arendt said it well: this passivity is the foundation of a totalitarian or authoritarian State. Is this apathy a consequence of the Soviet experience? Of course, similar logics are found. The Soviet system consecrated the reign of doublethink, a mixture of external approval and distancing. Today as then, people do not really believe in elections; they consider it does not concern them. It is simply a proof of loyalty that, for an individual, is inexpensive. The Homo Putinus is indeed an extension of the Homo Sovieticus. It is the same but more cynical, and with even less faith in the future. The cult of strength and violence is supposed to compensate for this lack of perspective and offer the ingredients of self-respect. At present, the government demands more than just superficial support. The discourse is much more demanding, more radical than during the years when only stability was discussed. Does it work? Very polarized positions, in any direction, only exist among very marginal groups. For example, Vladimir Putin inspires deep and enthusiastic love in a minority segment of the population, mainly middle-aged women. For others, especially those whose income is somehow linked to the State, approval is mainly due to lack of choice. Similarly, regarding the war, there are hawks and aggressive patriots. They can be seen on television, their comments can be read on the Internet – where they rejoice in the bombing of Ukrainian cities... Propaganda tries to make it seem like they represent the majority, but it is a core group that does not make up more than 6% or 8% of the population, mainly retirees, which can expand on occasion. The majority, on the other hand, may adhere to some propaganda speeches, but aspires to a peaceful life, without shocks. The "mobilization" announced on September 21 is a shock. What effect does it have on public opinion? It is obviously a shock. There is no more pretending: you have to go, or flee. It is a radical psychological change: war is no longer a distant thing, fought with "high-precision weapons," on foreign soil. Moreover, no one believes that this mobilization will be "partial," as the government claims. The myths in which many Russians have placed their pride – a strong State, a powerful army – are being tarnished. This shock goes beyond the question of who leaves or stays, or the effects on the economy. It is a questioning of many certainties. The war was supposed to be quick and obviously victorious, since we have the second largest army in the world and extraordinary weapons. Until the end, the Ministry of Defense concealed the situation by issuing soothing statements. When the mobilization was announced, reality became apparent. Failure became evident, and we can already see a new rise in negative sentiments: fear, confusion... The percentage of people saying they feel proud of the "special operation" dropped from 51% to 20%. And this is just the beginning. The myths in which many Russians have placed their pride – a strong State, a powerful army – are being tarnished. If we continue the parallel with the Soviet period, there were other reasons for pride at that time – from cultural production to space conquest. Today, there are none. This will likely increase resentment and violence in society. It also strikes at the foundations of Putin's power. The president's popularity has been built not only on the promise of stability, but also on the idea of strength, on victorious wars, starting with Chechnya, and on protecting the people from external threats. His legitimacy and credibility are being questioned. Right after the announcement of mobilization, his approval rating has already dropped from 83% to 72%. This is still very high, of course. This process of delegitimization will be slow. Could it lead to a protest movement? No, not in the near future. It would first require a clear military defeat, a serious and prolonged crisis, for people to radically reconsider their relationship with the authorities. This is not the case yet. It would also require organizations that are capable, not only of offering an alternative discourse, but above all of convincing people that they can change something. As early as August, 48% of those surveyed were in favor of halting hostilities, but those willing to openly say so make up a very small minority. Once again, the most widespread belief in Russia is that the individual cannot change anything.
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde," Russian sociologist Lev Gudkov notes that the Russian society's attitude towards the "special operation" in Ukraine is more about the absence of resistance or opposition rather than genuine support.

title: En Ukraine, le crowdfunding en première ligne
text: The time when Serhiy Prytula used to wake up Ukraine with slightly crude jokes and his perfect son-in-law smile is long gone. It was on the Novyi Kanal channel, from 2008 to 2011, when he was one of the three star presenters of "Pidiom," a TV show sponsored by a soluble coffee brand. Almost all Ukrainians remember it. For nearly twenty years, this man from the western part of the country was a celebrity on radio and television in the early programs that mixed entertainment and information in the Ukrainian language - at that time, Russian still dominated the media landscape. Nowadays, his days are more serious, more important as well. Serhiy Prytula, 41 years old, with a wife and three children, heads one of Ukraine's most well-known charitable organizations: the Prytula Foundation. Since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, his teams have raised nearly 3 billion hryvnias (around 83 million euros) for the benefit of the Ukrainian armed forces. On the foundation's website, with one click, you can choose to contribute to the purchase of drones, vehicles, communication equipment, funding for mysteriously named "cotton" secret operations, or the repair of captured Russian military equipment on the battlefield. More than 200 "volunteers" provide humanitarian aid and a project for kit houses for refugees are also offered. "We are what is called 'volunteers,' people who help our army have the equipment that the state does not have the ability to buy or does so very slowly," explains Serhiy Prytula between meetings with military personnel placing orders. "I have faster logistics, no bureaucracy, I can buy and deliver to the front." In February, there were ten in the organization, today there are over two hundred. With bases scattered throughout Ukraine to store and quickly transport equipment to the front. The man retains an easy and friendly demeanor from his TV years. He sprinkles jokes and witty remarks here and there in the conversation. But he is now also filled with unwavering concentration and determination. Since the start of the Russian offensive in February, he has lost friends, feared for his life and those of his loved ones. He has changed, of course. But who in Ukraine has not changed? An action man. The clean-shaven face of the 2000s presenter has given way to a salt-and-pepper three-day beard. No more shirts and suits, he dresses in t-shirts and sweatshirts, sometimes covered with a bulletproof vest when the situation demands it. Indeed, in recent months, Serhiy Prytula has personally delivered the equipment purchased through his foundation. "But the soldiers told me to stop, because a good volunteer is not a dead volunteer," he says with a hearty laugh. Nevertheless: these past few months have turned him into a man of action. "With the other private foundation 'Reviens vivant,' Prytula's foundation is the most well-known in Ukraine," notes sociologist Ioulia Shukan, a lecturer in Slavic studies at the University of Paris-Nanterre. "But it should be known that behind these charitable organizations, there is a myriad of small structures that also help the Ukrainian army. Since 2014, very flexible forms of action and support have developed, from preparing rations for soldiers to purchasing drones. Practically everyone in the country is involved today." A professionalized version of Ukrainian mutual aid, the Prytula Foundation is a highly professionalized version of this mutual aid that emerged in the wake of the Maidan revolution. Its popularity skyrocketed in June when a special collection was launched to purchase three Bayraktar, these Turkish combat drones. In three days, nearly 17 million euros were raised, enough to acquire four. But, in a twist, the Turkish company decided to donate the drones. In close discussion with the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense, Prytula's teams reinvested the money in an already-orbiting satellite acquired from a Finnish company. "Ukrainians, you are in space. We have bought a satellite," he announced on his YouTube channel, with a huge backlit map of Ukraine behind him. "But it's more than a satellite, as we now have access to a whole database of satellite images (...). You can be proud of yourselves." The video has 1,200,000 views. It shows Prytula halfway between a news anchor and a politician thanking his supporters. A trajectory similar to that of Volodymyr Zelensky. Recently, after the Russian army shelled the entire Ukrainian territory in retaliation for the sabotage on October 8 of a bridge connecting Russia to Crimea, he teamed up with a Ukrainian blogger to launch a collection titled "You have enraged Ukrainians" to purchase kamikaze drones. Result: nearly 9.8 million euros in twenty-four hours. His trajectory obviously brings to mind that of Volodymyr Zelensky, an actor and comedian who became president of Ukraine in 2019 and has enjoyed immense popularity among his compatriots for the past seven months. Moreover, having been involved in stand-up and show business circles, the two men, while not friends, have known each other for a long time. Before the war, Prytula had criticized the president's positions – not clear enough for his taste – towards Russia and certain oligarchs. The commitments of this native of Ternopil (West) are longstanding. As a child, he fervently supported Ukrainian athletes – then assimilated with Soviet teams – he spoke, sang, and danced in Ukrainian. He remembers, as a student, participating in the "Orange Revolution" in 2004 and especially in the one in 2014. He recalls dedicating part of the budget of his show at the time to support pro-democracy protesters at Maidan Square in Kiev, and later volunteers in Donbass. A political party on hold. He founded his foundation in 2020 to help Ukrainian hospitals cope with the Covid pandemic. He entered politics in 2019 during the legislative elections. Serhiy Prytula found himself in thirtieth position on the list of Holos ("Voice") - a liberal, pro-European, and social-democratic party. He was not elected as a deputy. In 2020, under the same banner, he ran for mayor of Kiev and finished third. Just before the Russian invasion, in early February, he announced the creation of a party called "24 August," in tribute to Ukrainian independence day in 1991. Centrist, patriotic, pro-European, anti-oligarchs, the movement was supposed to be made up of activists, army volunteers, and figures from civil society. However, the specifics of this nascent party remained uncertain. When asked about it today, he says it's a different life. "I am out of the political game. Because there is only one player who can win today: our army. Ukrainians don't care about politics right now, we are in survival mode, the survival of our state. After that, we'll see." For now, there is a sense of unity around President Zelensky. But after the war, it is likely that the legitimacy of Ukrainian politicians will be measured by their role in this conflict. And Serhiy Prytula's likability will work in his favor. In the meantime, he has resumed public management studies in Lviv. And his Twitter account still describes him as a "Ukrainian politician."
lead: Comedian Serhiy Prytula's foundation is behind the collection of nearly 83 million euros from individuals, which has enabled the Ukrainian army to acquire drones and even a satellite. The comedian, who had just entered politics, set aside his ambitions in the early days of the invasion.

title: Elon Musk, Bill Gates... « Il faut imaginer des moyens de contrôler la propension de milliardaires à se substituer aux Etats »
text: They believe themselves to be the masters of the world, but no one has elected them. They are not dictators and they ask us to call them "benefactors." From Carnegie to Rockefeller, the influence of the great American philanthropists is not new. But their successors in the 21st century have global, even interplanetary ambitions, and do not bother with morals. Most importantly, they wield their power in a world dominated by the influence of social networks, where democratic states and international organizations are weakened. The most problematic is also the richest person on the planet: Elon Musk is not satisfied with being the CEO of Tesla, whose valuation surpasses that of all other car manufacturers combined, and leading SpaceX, an essential partner of the Pentagon and NASA. The man, with a fortune estimated at $220 billion (equivalent in euros) according to Forbes magazine, dabbles in geopolitics, and he has the means to do so. Connected to more than 2,200 satellites of his Starlink network, the 25,000 terminals he delivered for free to Ukraine ensure the country's civilian and military communications in the midst of devastated infrastructure. They enable the guidance of missiles towards Russian targets as well as the operation of hospitals and banks. However, the versatility of Ukraine's benefactor, his potential double-dealing, is chilling. In mid-October, the 51-year-old billionaire publicly raised the idea that he might stop funding Starlink in Ukraine, before reconsidering and boasting about it. "Happy to help Ukraine," he tweeted, a network he ultimately acquired on Thursday, October 27, for $44 billion. Previously, he had sparked the anger of Volodymyr Zelensky - and likely pleased Vladimir Putin - by advocating for his own "peace plan" that included Ukraine relinquishing Crimea to Russia. Elon Musk's geopolitical whims and conflicts of interest - such as proposing to attach Taiwan to China, a country where Tesla has a factory - worry even US authorities. Also concerning is the presence of foreign investors in his bid to acquire Twitter. The billionaire's plans for the blue bird social network, where he has 110 million followers, and his desire to weaken moderation procedures to better "free" expression, are troubling. The clash between Elon Musk's libertarian beliefs - maximum individual freedoms, minimal state intervention - and the international responsibilities that his success de facto gives him, allowing him to bypass American diplomacy, is inevitable. The limits of "philanthrocapitalism" have already been felt by Bill Gates. The Covid-19 pandemic, which he claims to have foreseen and fought on the front lines with his foundation, the wealthiest in the world (worth 70 billion dollars), has shed harsh light on the contradictions and limits of the "philanthrocapitalism" he embodies. His defense of property rights over patents, aggressively practiced at Microsoft and contributing to his wealth, has proven disastrous in enabling rapid vaccination of populations in poor countries, of which he claims to be a champion. Under pressure, he had to concede an exception for Covid-19 vaccines. However, Bill Gates' complicity with pharmaceutical companies has become very visible. The image of the man who dedicated his fortune to "saving lives" has abruptly given way to "fake news" claiming he seeks to exterminate or control populations through vaccine passports or microchips. The financial power of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, which spends more on health than many states, has allowed him to lead the alliance of private organizations that has taken on the international management of the fight against Covid-19. This coalition, "strengthened by close relationships with pharmaceutical companies, has replaced governments, but without being held accountable like governments," notes an investigation by Politico and Welt. Dramatic health inaction Such criticisms add to those highlighting the contradictions between the health and climate goals proclaimed by Bill Gates and his investments in polluting or harmful industries, or his choices favoring intensive fertilizer use in Africa. Intended to combat food insecurity and poverty, the "green revolution" promoted and funded by his foundation is fiercely criticized for increasing the financial dependence of small African farmers on multinational fertilizer and pesticide companies. The Gates Foundation has wielded this exorbitant power in African health for years. Its leader has positioned himself as the de facto health minister of the continent, supplementing but also reinforcing the dramatic health inaction of many states and contributing to their weakening. This claim to lead public policies is also concerning in the field of climate change where Bill Gates positions himself as a leader. American journalist Tim Schwab points out "the scant references, untested solutions, and staggering financial conflicts of interest" of the billionaire. He especially emphasizes that "the assertion of his power without democratic basis comes precisely at a time when democratic institutions have become essential to address the issue of climate change." The whole question is to imagine new ways - fiscal, parliamentary, judicial - to subject the apprentice masters of the world to a minimum of control. Otherwise, billions of dollars will continue to give a few self-proclaimed benefactors the right to take the place of states and international organizations to "save the world."
lead: Thanks to their financial power, Elon Musk and Bill Gates are multiplying initiatives in geopolitical, health, and climate areas, thereby weakening democracies and international organizations, as explained in his column by Philippe Bernard, an editorialist at "Le Monde".

title: Guerre en Ukraine : Moscou admet une situation « tendue » sur le terrain pour son armée
text: Such an admission is extremely rare in times of war. Through the voice of General Sergei Surovikin on Tuesday, October 18, Moscow acknowledged that its army was facing serious difficulties in the face of the Ukrainian counteroffensive in the south and east of the country. "The situation in the zone of the 'special military operation' can be described as tense. The enemy does not give up its attempts to attack the positions of Russian troops," declared the general in charge of operations in Ukraine for the past ten days. "The Ukrainian regime is seeking to break through our defense" by gathering "all its reserves" for the counteroffensive, and the situation is particularly "very difficult" in Kherson (South), specified Sergei Surovikin. The capital of the eponymous region occupied by Russia since spring and annexed in September, the city is currently the target of Ukrainian strikes targeting its "social, economic, and industrial infrastructures," according to the Russian general. These bombings are causing disruptions in the supply of electricity, water, and food, which pose a "direct threat to the lives of the inhabitants." This justifies, in the eyes of Sergei Surovikin, the fact that "the Russian army will ensure above all the safe evacuation of the population." "Subsequent actions concerning the city of Kherson itself will depend on the military situation," he added, saying without further details "not ruling out a very difficult decision." For his part, the leader installed in the Kherson region by Moscow, Vladimir Saldo, announced an evacuation of residents from several localities to the left bank of the Dnieper River to allow the Russian army to set up "large-scale defensive structures" in the face of a "vast counteroffensive" prepared by Ukrainian forces. Ukrainian bombings in Russia The Russian army is indeed on the defensive on most of the front in Ukraine. It has been retreating since September, both in the north and east and south of the country. The only area where it is still advancing is the city of Bakhmut (East), which it has been trying to take from the Ukrainians since the summer. In Russia itself, the Ukrainian army bombed two villages in the border region of Kursk, according to Moscow. In Belgorod, also bordering Ukraine, Ukrainian fire hit a railway station, injuring one person, according to the local governor. Russia has not lost its bite, as it bombed "the military command and energy systems of Ukraine" again on Tuesday, announced the Russian Ministry of Defense, ensuring that "all targets were hit" thanks to unidentified precision and long-range weapons. "Since October 10th, 30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed, causing massive power outages throughout the country," acknowledged Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, who reiterated his refusal to negotiate with his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin. "The situation is now critical," added a presidential adviser, asking all of Ukraine to "prepare" for possible "power, water, and heating outages." As winter approaches, "1,162 localities remain without electricity," according to the Ukrainian emergency services. On Monday, "kamikaze" drones, which caused the deaths of at least nine people, had already caused power outages in three regions. Iran in the crosshairs Moscow's use of Iranian drones, according to Kiev, was further supported on Tuesday by the Ukrainian armed forces headquarters, stating that the Russian army had sent "43 Iranian-made Shahed-136 drones" in the "last twenty-four hours," of which "38 were shot down by Ukrainian soldiers." This "call for help" to Iran is "the Kremlin's acknowledgment of its military and political failure," reacted President Zelensky in the evening. "We do not have such information," responded Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov to a journalist's question about the use of Iranian drones Shahed-136 in Ukraine. "Russian technology is being used, with Russian names," he added, while Iran, for its part, reiterated that it has never "exported weapons to any of the warring parties." At the same time, as a sign of increasing tensions on the international stage since the start of the war on February 24th, Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov suggested a reduction in Russian diplomatic presence in Western countries. "The most important thing is that there is no work there since Europe decided to close itself off to us, to suspend all economic cooperation with Moscow," he explained, saying he wants to prioritize Asia and Africa.
lead: The Russian army once again heavily targeted Ukraine's energy infrastructure on Tuesday, while acknowledging that its own troops were facing difficulties, especially in Kherson, where Moscow intends to evacuate the population.

title: Le prix Sakharov du Parlement européen attribué au « courageux » peuple ukrainien
text: The European Parliament announced on Wednesday, October 19, that it had awarded the Sakharov Prize to the Ukrainian people as a sign of support in the face of the invasion of Ukraine by Russia, initiated by Vladimir Putin on February 24. Launched in 1988, the Sakharov Prize (named after the physicist and Soviet dissident Andrei Sakharov, Nobel Peace Prize laureate in 1975) "for freedom of thought" rewards individuals or organizations defending human rights and fundamental freedoms each year. It comes with a prize of 50,000 euros. "This prize is dedicated to the Ukrainians fighting on the ground. To those who have been forced to flee. To those who have lost loved ones and friends. To all those who resist and fight for their convictions. I know that the courageous Ukrainian people will not give in, and neither will we," said the President of the European Parliament, Roberta Metsola, in the Strasbourg hemicycle. The 50,000 euros in prize money will be distributed to civil society figures. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky welcomed the awarding of this prize. "Ukrainians prove their commitment to the values of freedom and democracy every day on the battlefield against the Russian terrorist state," he reacted on Twitter, emphasizing that Europe's support was "very important" for Kiev.
lead: President Volodymyr Zelensky praised on Wednesday the awarding of the prestigious prize to Ukrainians, commending his people who are fighting for "freedom and democracy."

title: La crise énergétique peut-elle mener à un black-out en France cet hiver ?
text: The energy crisis is hitting France hard. Between the gas restrictions imposed by Vladimir Putin, the nuclear power plants shut down, and the dams lacking water after summer droughts, the French electricity production park is under pressure... to the point that the risk of shortage, even blackouts, is dominating public debate. To prevent the worst, the government is proposing an austerity plan to get through the winter. But is the risk real? Will power cuts be necessary? Sources: Outlook for the electricity system for autumn and winter 2022-2023 (RTE report)Unavailability of the nuclear power plant (RTE service)Hydraulic stock (RTE service)Annual national wind and solar production park from 2001 to 2021 (energy network open data)Real-time national consumption data (Eco2mix)Annual national production park by sector (energy network open data)RTE electricity balance sheet for 2021 and 2020.
lead: Nuclear power plants undergoing maintenance, threatened gas imports, dams facing water shortages... Is our electrical system ready for the winter of 2022?

title: A Rome, Macron en militant d’un dialogue entre Kiev et Moscou
text: While the fighting continues to rage in Ukraine, the proposal may seem incongruous in some European capitals, including Kiev, which do not want to hear about a peace agreement with Russia: Emmanuel Macron was supposed to take advantage of his trip to Rome on Sunday, October 23 and Monday, October 24, to nurture the perspective, which he knows is still distant, of negotiations between the two enemy countries, eight months to the day after the start of the Russian invasion. Upon his arrival on Sunday afternoon, the head of state is invited to open the annual conference of the Community of Sant'Egidio, entitled "The Cry of Peace." He was then scheduled to have dinner with intellectuals and religious leaders hosted by the founder of this movement, Andrea Riccardi, known for his efforts in interreligious dialogue and mediation in Africa, Latin America, and the Balkans. Andrea Riccardi explained in an interview with Le Monde that he wants to "recreate and promote a culture of peace on the international stage" during the Rome gathering. On Monday morning, Macron will meet with Pope Francis, particularly to discuss Ukraine, less than a year after his last visit to the Vatican in November 2021. In his speech at Sant'Egidio, the head of state is expected to place the conflict between Ukraine and Russia in the current international context of an increasingly fragmented world. The Elysée Palace stated in advance of the visit that "the idea will be to take into account and face the various crises that are affecting our planet today, whether they are related to climate, inequalities, or the war that Russia is conducting in Ukraine, and to reflect on the different ways to build peace." Officially, there is no talk of launching any mediation, even though Macron has been trying in recent weeks to advance this perspective so that France and Europe can be at the discussion table when the time comes. At the launch summit of the European Political Community in Prague on October 6, he discussed with Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan the goals and methods to be put in place to organize potential talks, as Erdogan is currently the only leader in a position to mediate between Moscow and Kiev. For the head of state, these talks cannot take place until the Ukrainians, who are in the midst of a counter-offensive in the Donbass and Kherson region, decide when they believe they have achieved their war objectives. Without openly stating it, Paris, however, fears an attempt to retake Crimea by force, fearing that it could be a pretext for a new escalation by Russia. This viewpoint sharply contrasts, on the continent, with the positions of Ukraine's staunchest allies, who support its efforts to reclaim all occupied territories, including the peninsula unilaterally annexed by Moscow in 2014, and who only hope for one thing: for Vladimir Putin to be judged for war crimes or aggression. In the meantime, the trip to Rome should allow Emmanuel Macron to find receptive ears to his approach and eager to rely on him to make their message of peace heard. Pope Francis himself has called in vain for an end to the fighting. He has also sought to act as a mediator both before and after the start of the Russian invasion, while avoiding a break with the Patriarch of the Russian Orthodox Church, Kirill, who is a religious endorsement for Vladimir Putin. His efforts had been poorly received by Ukrainians, who do not appreciate seeing the head of the Catholic Church equate the warring parties. "Dialogue remains a necessity" "Talking about peace is not a betrayal of Ukrainians. My first visit to Ukraine dates back to 1989, and I understood the Ukrainians' desire for independence," justifies Andrea Riccardi. Those who continue to talk to Putin are right. We are not naive; we know the nature of the Russian interlocutors, but dialogue remains a necessity. Macron is the only European leader who has maintained a tough political line towards Russia while keeping the door open." According to the founder of Sant'Egidio, "peace in Ukraine cannot be achieved without Washington or Beijing, but it must be done with the help of Europe on a position that would be close to that of Emmanuel Macron." "Now, and it's absurd, peace is considered utopian or as a pro-Putin concept. But in the current context, with the nuclear risk, it is necessary to start talking about peace again," says Andrea Riccardi, referring to the recent nuclear confrontation threats made by the Kremlin. However, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has definitively ruled out talking to Vladimir Putin since Moscow's illegal annexation of the occupied territories in late September. On the Ukrainian side, it is believed that the conditions for restarting mediation, desired by Kiev before the February invasion, are no longer in place. "Currently, we cannot restart such a process, with nearly a fifth of our territory under Russian occupation. A ceasefire under these conditions would only lead to a legitimization of this situation," says Andrii Yurash. Ukraine's ambassador to the Holy See hopes that "President Macron will be able to invite the Pope to take into account not only the horror of war but also the political causes of this horror, namely Moscow's desire to destroy [their] country."
lead: The head of state is scheduled to speak on Sunday at a meeting of the Community of Sant'Egidio and meet with Pope Francis on Monday.

title: En Ukraine, les bombardements russes touchent sévèrement les infrastructures énergétiques
text: Winter is approaching and Ukraine is seeing its energy capabilities greatly reduced. Approximately one third of the country's power plants, which are top targets of Russian bombings, have been destroyed in a week, according to Kyiv. "Russian armed forces continued to strike with high-precision, long-range aerial and naval weapons the military command and energy systems of Ukraine," the Russian defense ministry acknowledged in its daily report on Tuesday, October 18, ensuring that "all targets have been hit." Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky confirmed that "30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed" and lamented "massive power outages across the country." On Tuesday, over 1,100 localities were still without electricity. Up to 4,000 communities have been affected by power cuts in the past ten days. Power outages, water shortages, and heating issues According to the Ukrainian Minister of Territorial Development, Oleksi Tchernychov, 45 energy facilities are among the 400 damaged infrastructures since October 10. Additionally, 180 civilian buildings have reportedly been hit. "Such terrorist actions by the aggressor mobilize us and make us even stronger," emphasized the senior official. "The situation is critical now throughout the country, as our regions are interdependent," stated the deputy head of the presidential cabinet, Kirill Timoshenko, who called for "the entire country to prepare for the possibility of power outages, water shortages, and heating issues." The new attacks on Tuesday resulted in at least one death in Mykolaiv (South) and two in Kyiv, according to authorities. Power outages were again reported in the capital and other regions. The previous day, Russian strikes, carried out with the help of kamikaze drones among others, resulted in at least nine deaths, including five in Kyiv, and caused power cuts in three regions. Concerns surrounding the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant In the energy sector, the Ukrainian operator Energoatom accused the Russian army on Tuesday of having "abducted" two executives from the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant (South), the largest in Europe, which has been occupied by Moscow's forces since March. The site has been regularly targeted by bombings and power cuts since the start of the Russian invasion on February 24, raising fears of a nuclear disaster. "Over the past twenty-four hours, the enemy launched ten missile attacks and fifty-eight aerial attacks, and conducted up to sixty multiple rocket launcher strikes," summarized the Ukrainian military headquarters on Tuesday morning. The Ukrainian military also reported the Russian army's deployment of forty-three "Iranian-made Shahed-136 drones," of which "thirty-eight were shot down by Ukrainian soldiers," and claimed "twenty-two strikes" carried out by the Ukrainian air force on Monday. "The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, replied to a journalist's question about Moscow using Iranian drones in Ukraine, saying, 'We do not have such information. Russian technology is being used, with Russian names.' While Tehran continues to deny exporting weapons to 'warring parties,' the United States has threatened to sanction companies or states collaborating in Iran's drone program following Monday's attacks."
lead: According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, 30% of the country's power plants have been destroyed by Russia. More than 1,100 communities were without electricity on Tuesday.

title: « Erdogan agit en fonction d’une seule échéance : l’élection présidentielle de 2023 en Turquie »
text: Recep Tayyip Erdogan: a sultan pursuing a neo-Ottoman expansionist and anti-Western policy? Perhaps. But let's listen carefully to the speeches he delivers at an increasing pace. Let's not overestimate the importance he places on international politics. Between the national holiday on October 29, 2022, and the celebration of the centenary of the founding of the Turkish Republic on October 29, 2023, Erdogan thinks and acts with only one deadline in mind: the presidential election scheduled for the end of spring 2023. His actions abroad stem from personal ambition, which follows a policy of history. Unlike Vladimir Putin, he must face the ballot box. More than the reconstitution of an empire, the invasion of a neighbor, or the shifting of borders, he wants to win elections. But he has a problem: he has built his popularity around the values promoted by his political party, the Justice and Development Party (AKP). However, the judiciary is under the control of the government, and corruption scandals have multiplied. As for development, it is now just a memory: average purchasing power had tripled since 2002, but between 2013 and 2022, GDP per capita dropped from $12,600 to $7,500 (according to World Bank figures). "Are you ready?" Erdogan often asks his base. Certainly not to become a poor but proud nation. Putin made that bet, he did not. To make people forget record inflation rates (the official rate of 83% per year is underestimated), he presents himself as the president of future investments. Preparing the nation for war In his speeches, international politics come after the litany of figures on social housing created, infrastructure built, and hospital beds opened. It is conveyed through catchy phrases, bravado where he invokes history through calculated provocations. On September 3, in Samsun, on the Black Sea, where Mustafa Kemal had landed on May 19, 1919, before launching the war of independence that led to victory against the Greek occupying forces, he warns his Mediterranean neighbor: "And you, Greece, look at history, return to history (...) Do not forget Izmir." Not forgetting Izmir, the Greeks know, is to recall what happened a century earlier, almost to the day: the great fire that occurred following the entry of Kemalist troops on September 9, 1922, resulted in tens of thousands of deaths. It is preparing the Turkish nation for war, without necessarily taking the risk that it will occur. It is subjecting Turkish history to a memory of resentment. As historian Edhem Eldem pointed out in his inaugural lecture at the Collège de France in 2017, "Turkey is cliopathic [sick of history], through its myths and inventions, but above all through its fears, complexes, silences, taboos, denials, and denialism." Rhetoric of revenge: "We have been colonized in our own country," Erdogan had said almost ten years ago, before interpreting the clauses of the Treaty of Lausanne (July 24, 1923) in a revisionist manner. History will mark a turning point if he believes that this will help him confront the wall of economic realities and extend his policy of denial (the disastrous effect of low interest rates), he will multiply declarations against the West ("them") to his electorate ("us"). Between the coming weeks and the elections in June, possibly moved up to May 2023, he will provoke the former powers, the "West" that he challenges by speaking to it in the second person singular and accusing it of always wanting to weaken Turkey. He will use the international stage for electoral purposes. He will follow a timeline that will lead him from the abolition of the sultanate (November 1, 1922) to the celebration of the centenary of the Republic (October 29, 1923). It is then that history will definitively mark a turning point for him. Kemalist historiography has made Atatürk the "unique man" (tek adam) of 20th-century republican Turkey. Erdogan wants to be the one for 21st-century Turkish political Islam. He is preparing to write a new national narrative. It is up to us to read it. It is up to us to understand it. Neither laugh nor cry, but understand, Spinoza taught.
lead: Recep Tayyip Erdogan aims to embody Turkish political Islam of the 21st century. But more than for the reconstruction of an empire, it is primarily for electoral purposes that he uses the international stage, analyzes Olivier Bouquet, a professor of Ottoman history, in an article for "Le Monde".

title: Le barrage de Kakhovka, nouvel enjeu de la contre-offensive ukrainienne
text: He was captured at the beginning of the invasion. The Kakhovka hydroelectric dam, built on the Dnieper River, was a priority target for the Russians at the start of the conflict, as it helps supply water to the Crimea Peninsula, annexed by Moscow. With the start of the Ukrainian counteroffensive at the end of the summer, it has become the focus of the warring parties, who accuse each other of wanting to destroy it. Kakhovka is located about 60 kilometers east of Kherson, the first major city to fall into Russian hands in March. The front line is situated about forty kilometers to the north. 16 meters high, 3,850 meters long Built on the Dnieper, the structure, partly made of concrete and earth, is 16 meters high and 3,850 meters long. It is one of the largest infrastructures of its kind in Ukraine. According to the website of the Ukrainian operating company, Ukrhydroenergo, the power of the hydroelectric plant is 334.8 megawatts (MW).
