Built in 1956, during the Soviet period, the Kakhovka hydroelectric dam sends water into the North Crimean Canal, which runs from southern Ukraine and crosses the entire Crimean Peninsula. Upstream from the structure is the Kakhovka reservoir, an artificial water reservoir formed on the Dnieper River, 240 kilometers long and up to 23 kilometers wide. Ukraine accuses Russia of wanting to cover its retreat While the Russian army said it wanted to "evacuate" the population of Kherson in the face of the advance of Ukrainian troops, Kiev fears that Moscow may be seeking to destroy the dam to cover a possible retreat. Russia's goal would be to stop the advance of Ukrainian troops in the region and protect Russian forces, said one of the presidential advisers, Mykhailo Podolyak, on Twitter. On Thursday, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky openly accused the Russians of mining the infrastructure, the destruction of which could have dramatic consequences. "If the dam is destroyed (...), the North Crimean Canal will simply disappear," and it would be "a large-scale catastrophe," he warned on Thursday evening. "More than 80 localities, including Kherson, would be in the fast flooding zone," said Mr. Zelensky before the European Council. "This could destroy the water supply to a large part of southern Ukraine," and affect the cooling of the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant, which draws its water from this artificial lake of 18.18 billion cubic meters, he added. On Friday, Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Chmyhal called for international intervention. "We call on the UN, the EU, and other organizations to conduct an international observation mission of Kakhovka," he said. According to him, a potential explosion of the dam of this hydroelectric plant "could cause several thousand casualties and [result in] the flooding of dozens of localities." Pro-Russian forces say they fear "a massive missile strike." The Russian occupation authorities in Kherson deny any mining of the Kakhovka dam and denounce President Zelensky's "lies." While it is impossible to verify the claims of both Ukrainians and Russians, an analysis note published on Wednesday by the Institute for the Study of War (ISW), a non-governmental American organization, notes that Russian sources accuse Ukrainian forces of bombing the hydroelectric plant and widely disseminate animations describing the flood zones in case of dam failure. Vladimir Saldo, the governor of Kherson Oblast installed by the Russian army, explained on Telegram that he has information about Kiev's "preparation for a massive missile strike on the Kakhovka hydroelectric plant dam. In this context, there is an imminent danger of flooding in the territories of the Kherson region and massive destruction of civilian infrastructure." On Tuesday, General Sergei Surovikin, head of Russian troops in Ukraine, had advanced similar ideas. The Russian counter-fire strategy According to the ISW, this could be a way to prepare the ground for an attack on the Kakhovka hydroelectric plant dam and blame the Ukrainians for it. The Russian authorities "probably intend" to use these warnings about a supposed Ukrainian attack on the infrastructure to create a narrative "allowing them to damage the dam and blame Ukraine" for the resulting damages, the organization analyzes. "Russian military personnel may believe that the dam breach could cover their retreat from the right bank of the Dnipro and prevent or delay Ukrainian advances on the river," the Institute further states. Such a maneuver would allow the Kremlin, still according to the ISW, to "overshadow the news of a third humiliating retreat of Russian forces" and contribute to Russian propaganda that Ukraine is a "terrorist" state deliberately targeting civilians. On the ground, an advisor to the Ukrainian presidency, Kirill Timoshenko, reported on Friday "88 localities retaken" from Russian forces in the Kherson region.
lead: According to Ukrainian authorities, an explosion of the dam of this hydroelectric power plant near the front line could result in several thousand casualties and lead to the flooding of dozens of communities.

title: La guerre en Ukraine complique la quête d’une « souveraineté européenne »
text: The question is at the heart of tensions between France and Germany. Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz were supposed to try, on Wednesday, October 26, in Paris, to bring their positions closer on sovereignty issues, in areas such as defense and energy, where the French and Germans are far from being in agreement. While no breakthrough is expected, the two leaders should at least try to save face, a few days after the indefinite postponement of a Franco-German ministerial council. For Europeans, it is one of the cruel paradoxes of the Russian invasion of Ukraine: the return of war to the continent validates, on paper, the quest for "European sovereignty" that Emmanuel Macron has been advocating since 2017 and that the German Chancellor shares, according to the speech he delivered in Prague on August 29. "European sovereignty essentially means that we become more autonomous in all areas, that we take on greater responsibility for our own security, that we are even more united in defending our values and interests in the world," the German Chancellor had said at the time. In reality, the conflict makes this ambition difficult to achieve, as the current urgencies test the independence and cohesion of the Twenty-Seven, starting with that of the Franco-German duo. "The geopolitical context supports Emmanuel Macron's agenda, but the pursuit of European sovereignty may not necessarily align with the French views," says Thierry Chopin, special advisor to the Jacques Delors Institute. "Germany has the capacity to react without following the French line." For him, the "change of era" advocated by Olaf Scholz from the beginning of the conflict, with the creation of a special fund of 100 billion euros to modernize the German army, demonstrates that Berlin has means that France lacks. The decision of the German coalition, at the end of September, to commit 200 billion euros to help households and businesses affected by the soaring energy prices confirmed this feeling. Hence the fear, in Paris, of a new gap, especially in military and economic terms. "Admission of weakness" The concern is all the more acute as Germany sees its position as a pivotal country in the center of Europe being reinforced, especially in the event of further enlargements to the Balkans, or even one day to Ukraine, while France fears being marginalized on the western flank of the continent. "This surge of tensions can also be an admission of weakness on the part of Paris," says Thierry Chopin. Another difficulty: the longer the war lasts, the more the balance of power evolves within the European Union (EU). Thus, the authority of the Franco-German duo is being contested like never before by the states of Central, Baltic, and Scandinavian Europe. For them, Paris and Berlin have made multiple mistakes in dealing with Moscow and are too timid in their support for Kiev. "We were right and you were mistaken about Russia," repeats the Polish Prime Minister, Mateusz Morawiecki. "Do you believe that Europe would be better off if we had followed Germany's recommendations all these years?" The countries that emerged from the Soviet bloc are gaining influence and are no longer hesitant to make their voices heard, although they are not leading forces. "The shift of Europe's center of gravity to the East was theorized by Scholz in his speech in Prague," notes Tara Varma, director in Paris of the European Council on Foreign Relations. "However, I do not believe that the center of gravity has shifted yet: the debate on Ukraine and Russia is led by the Baltic states and the countries of Central Europe, but they are not currently formulating alternative proposals to those of the Franco-German duo." "The indispensable value of the transatlantic partnership" To defend their sovereignty, this part of Europe is turning to Washington rather than Paris or Berlin. In the urgency created by Vladimir Putin's decision to go to war, the United States is making a major comeback and NATO is experiencing a revival on the European continent, at the expense of calls for a "European defense" repeated by Emmanuel Macron. The French President had hoped to take advantage of Donald Trump's presidency, who considered NATO "obsolete," to encourage Europeans to strengthen their military cooperation and defense industry. However, the decisive commitment of the Joe Biden administration in support of Ukraine has changed the situation: American aid to Kiev is crucial both militarily and financially and far surpasses the efforts made by Europeans. "For a number of countries, the American presence is so reassuring that there is no need for an alternative and no need to deepen European integration," observes Tara Varma. According to her, the "American narrative on NATO is very strong, especially in Central, Baltic, and Northern Europe." "In frontline countries, the Russian invasion is an extremely tangible threat and the response must be American," adds Tara Varma. As a sign of the increased attractiveness of the American umbrella in the face of the threat, Finland and Sweden are on the verge of joining the Atlantic Alliance. Germany is not immune to this transatlantic enthusiasm, to the point of neglecting Franco-German defense projects in favor of equipping itself with American equipment - much to the chagrin of French authorities, who hope instead that some of the military budgets freed up by Berlin will strengthen continental programs, such as the Future Combat Air System (FCAS). "The fact that a staunch supporter of the transatlantic relationship, in the person of President Biden, was elected to the White House is a blessing for all of us, as Olaf Scholz reminded in Prague. We have witnessed the indispensable value of the transatlantic partnership over the past few months." "The EU has demonstrated its adaptability." In passing, Europe has become aware of its energy dependence on Russia and is trying to break free from it in favor of other suppliers who are not hesitant to sell their resources at a high price. The energy decoupling initiated with Russia amid the "gas war" waged by Vladimir Putin could lead to new dependencies, especially on the United States. "It will be necessary to accept that European sovereignty requires a new sharing of national sovereignty," warns Thierry Chopin. "This is something very difficult on defense and energy issues, but similar to what happened at the time of the creation of the euro on the monetary level," in a context of major geopolitical upheaval following the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989. A new leap in integration, however, appears complicated in a context of rising sovereignist forces, which erode the maneuvering room of minority governments, as seen in France and Spain. "Over the past fifteen years, the EU has demonstrated its adaptability. It has accelerated its integration process following successive crises: the global subprime crisis, waves of refugees, Brexit, and the Covid-19 pandemic. But these turbulences have also awakened strong nationalist currents within the member states," recently assessed political analyst Ivan Krastev in an interview with diplomat Michel Duclos on behalf of the Montaigne Institute. This nationalist awakening affects many EU member states, starting with countries on the front line facing the Russian threat, such as Poland, where the eurosceptic majority in power is accused of violating the very foundations of the rule of law. Following the establishment of a conservative government supported by the far-right in Sweden, the rise to power in Italy of the post-fascist Giorgia Meloni is just the latest example.
lead: The Central, Baltic, and Nordic European countries accuse Germany and France of underestimating the Russian threat and are turning to the United States to ensure their security.

title: « Poutine veut nous faire crever de froid dans l’obscurité » : à Kharkiv, les frappes russes terrorisent les civils
text: It is 8:15 AM on Tuesday, October 18th, in Kharkiv, in eastern Ukraine. Nine powerful explosions ring out within three minutes, shaking the walls. Customers come out of the store at a gas station to scan the horizon. "It's the anti-aircraft defense," someone hesitantly suggests, between assertion and question. "No, those are strikes," confidently replies a gas station employee. "Look, you can see the smoke," shouts a third person, pointing to two plumes of dust rising rapidly diagonally in the sky. The question is settled. Two customers quickly snap photos with their smartphones, while others have already turned away to go about their business. The morning traffic of cars in the streets of Kharkiv continues as if a terrifying event had not just occurred. The second-largest city in Ukraine, located in the northeast of the country, is being bombed by Russian missiles fired from the neighboring region of Belgorod. It has become a grim routine for this major industrial center, which, until the Russian invasion in February, was a stronghold of Russian-speaking culture with a population of one million. The industry has come to a halt, a third of the city has fled, and many of those who remain – especially those under 30 – prefer to speak Ukrainian out of disgust for their neighbor. "The Russians missed their target today, no electrical or energy infrastructure was hit," explains Serhii Magdysiouk, director of the municipal services department for the Kharkiv region, to Le Monde in a weary tone. The man has his work cut out for him. "The Russians have been trying to demolish our infrastructure since the beginning of August. They are primarily targeting electrical substations. Four have been destroyed, including a very important one. They are also targeting high-voltage lines and power plants." Kharkiv regularly experiences power outages, and areas in the east of the region, recently liberated but still close to the front line, have neither electricity nor gas. "There are still 1.3 million inhabitants in the region, and all are at risk of being deprived of gas and electricity this winter," sadly admits the official. According to him, Ukraine's Western allies could help by providing transformers. "They can easily be transported here by rail. Thanks to their mobility, transformers can be quickly moved in case of danger." Russian missile salvos have been targeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure for the past two weeks, sparing no region of the country. In an alarmist tone, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky stated in his daily video address on Tuesday that "since October 10th, 30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed, causing massive outages throughout the country." For her advisor Kirill Timoshenko, "the situation is now critical throughout the country, as our regions are dependent on each other." The Ukrainian emergency services have counted 1,162 localities without electricity. "No one is safe anymore." "Putin wants to freeze us to death in the dark," raged Nadejda, a stylish and talkative 65-year-old retiree living in Saltivka, a residential area in the east of Kharkiv, on Monday evening. Wrapped in a bright red down jacket, she firmly states that she will not leave her home, no matter what happens. "There is no heating in my building, no gas, no water," she complains, sitting on a frail bench at the foot of her staircase. "I have nowhere to go, and I don't want to be relocated to a 'obchejitié' [kind of workers' dormitory] with only one toilet for 50 people." The retiree lives on Lessia-Serdiouka Street, in a building severely affected by Russian bombings. Many windows are broken, and shell impacts can be seen on the facade. During the height of the fighting in March, Nadejda left with her daughter and granddaughter to seek refuge in Malmö, Sweden. But she made the "bad decision" to return home alone after a month. "Now, the journey is no longer free across Europe for Ukrainian refugees." Given the damage to her building, the municipality has temporarily abandoned repairing the water and gas pipes. "At my age, I am forced to carry jerry cans of water to the 8th floor, without an elevator, because those haven't been restored either," she laments. The building, which has 242 apartments, currently houses only 23 residents. At the bottom of the neighboring staircase, Ihor, 42, is busy clearing the apartment he shares with his mother, Marina. "The building suffered because it is on the front line. Across the street, there were Russians trying to take the city," recounts the unemployed mechanic. "In my apartment, there were Ukrainian soldiers, which is why we were heavily shelled. All the walls, all the furniture are riddled with shrapnel!" For several months, he couldn't return home due to the fighting. Today, he insists on staying in his home out of fear of looting. "We're lucky to have electricity, otherwise it would be unbearable," his mother, Marina, remarks. The rest of the family lives with friends in a central neighborhood. "But now that the Russians have been pushed far from the city [the border is 30 kilometers away], they are firing missiles that mostly hit the city center. No one is safe anymore," she grumbles. Further up Natalii-Oujvi Street, the scene is one of devastation. A 16-story building is torn open and threatens to collapse at any moment. No housing is intact, and the neighborhood is so quiet that a closed curtain or a spoken word immediately attracts attention. The fate of each building will be decided on a case-by-case basis. Serhii, 51 years old, is trying to repair the pipes in the basement himself. "Since everyone left last winter, during the fighting, they burst due to freezing. Everything needs to be changed quickly, otherwise the building will be permanently uninhabitable," he explains. No one is currently living there because the power has not been restored. "The situation is getting worse day by day." The situation is more favorable in the building across the street. On the 4th floor, Ihor, 57 years old, proudly shows off the new windows he has just installed. They had all been blown out by explosions. "Now we are waiting for all the residents to do the same, because if there are still holes in the facades, the communal services will not reconnect the central heating or water." It all depends on the occupants of three apartments out of the 42 in the building, of whom there is no news. "They are probably refugees abroad," grumbles Vladimir Mikhailovich, the building's syndic manager. The prospect of a winter without heating or electricity is swelling the ranks of Ukrainians displaced by the conflict. In Kupiansk, a medium-sized city located 120 kilometers east of Kharkiv, evacuation buses were boarding a few dozen destitute people towards the west of the country on Tuesday. "Here, the situation is worsening day by day. At home, we have no electricity, no water, no gas," says Alexandre, a 43-year-old farm worker. "On February 24, the Russians took the city without a fight. Then the Ukrainians took the city back without a fight a month ago. Kupiansk was intact then. But now, every day, the Russians are destroying the city with their artillery. What is the point of this revenge?" Russian forces are only 15 kilometers from Kupiansk and the artillery frequently thunders. "The conditions are too dangerous for us to work on repairing the lines. The residents scold me every day, but I can't do anything," laments Mykola Koulba, a municipal services employee. Day after day, the Russian army's onslaught is slowly but surely pushing Ukraine towards darkness.
lead: The second largest city in Ukraine, like elsewhere in the country, is being bombed by Russian missiles targeting energy infrastructure in order to deprive residents of electricity and gas as winter approaches.

title: En se retirant de l’accord céréalier, Moscou mise sur une aggravation de la crise alimentaire mondiale
text: The agreement on grain exports from Ukrainian ports on the Black Sea, the only significant compromise negotiated between Kiev and Moscow since the beginning of the conflict in February, has collapsed. Even though cargo movements to and from these ports had not immediately stopped on Monday, October 31, the decision announced by the Russian side on Saturday raises concerns of a new increase in wheat prices and a worsening of the global food crisis. The agreement, named the "Black Sea grain initiative," was negotiated in July under the auspices of the UN and Turkey. It allowed Ukraine to export millions of tons of grains that were previously subject to a strict Russian blockade in the Black Sea, where the grain ports are located. On Sunday evening, Russian representatives at the Joint Coordination Center, responsible for inspecting ships passing through the Bosphorus Strait, announced their departure from this body "for an indefinite period." The fate of the remaining ships in transit is still uncertain. "Insignificant" damages Moscow justifies its decision by blaming a "terrorist attack" attributed to the Ukrainian army, whose drones reportedly struck the Russian fleet based in annexed Crimea's Sevastopol on the night of Friday to Saturday. Described as "massive" by Russia, the strike allegedly caused only "insignificant" damage to a Russian navy minesweeper, without any casualties, according to Moscow. Ukrainian sources present a different picture of this attack, which they claim involved the joint use of aerial and maritime drones. According to them, four Russian military ships were damaged, including the fleet's flagship, the frigate Admiral Makarov, used to launch Kalibr missiles towards Ukraine and which replaced the cruiser Moskva, sunk in April. The Kiev government and its Western allies are held responsible by Moscow, which accuses Ukraine of carrying out the attack against the Black Sea fleet with the support of the UK. Russian diplomacy mentions the involvement of "British specialists" in the operation without providing evidence. Russia also alleges that civilian ships supposedly transporting grain may have been used in this context. On Monday, Russia carried out new attacks on infrastructure throughout Ukrainian territory. On Sunday, President Zelensky accused Moscow of blocking Ukrainian grain-carrying ships in the Black Sea since September. Was the Sevastopol incident, humiliating as it was for Moscow, used as a pretext? On Sunday, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called the Russian decision "predictable" and accused Moscow of blocking Ukrainian grain-carrying ships in the Black Sea since September. Currently, he revealed, more than 170 ships are stranded at sea, carrying about 2 million tons of goods. "This is an obvious attempt by Russia to resort once again to the threat of widespread famine in Africa and Asia," he emphasized in a video address from Kiev, broadcasted on Saturday night. On Sunday, the European Union, NATO, and the United Nations also urged Russia to reconsider its decision. The Russian withdrawal should not come as a surprise, as the grain agreement was hanging by a thread. Signed in Istanbul on July 22 under the auspices of the United Nations and Turkish diplomacy, it was set to last for 120 days. On November 19, it was due to be renewed or expire. However, in recent months, Russia has been trying to reject it, even claiming that it had "aggravated" the global food crisis. The Russian position was clear well before the attack on Sevastopol: Vladimir Putin had complained to his Turkish counterpart, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, about the disadvantages of the agreement, stating that his country was unable to export its cereals and fertilizers not due to direct sanctions, but because of logistical and financial disorganization. Russian exports had dropped by 22% in July and August compared to the previous year, also affected by a strong ruble. This stance was reiterated by Maria Zakharova, the spokesperson for the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, on Friday, October 28, during a press briefing in Moscow. "This situation does not suit us," she declared. "The accounts of some banks are frozen, so payments cannot be made, all because of Western sanctions." On the same day, the United Nations Secretary-General, Antonio Guterres, urged Moscow and Kiev to renew the agreement. Market stabilization Since the first ship left Odessa on July 28, more than 9 million tons of foodstuffs have been exported, with over 5 million tons going to African and Asian countries. The establishment of this corridor helped stabilize markets and lower prices, which had reached record levels during the Russian invasion of Ukraine. For Kiev, these exports brought in foreign currency and prevented the stored grain from spoiling during the spring. However, Moscow claims that the agreement was undermined by the fact that some of the cereals were exported to Europe, a point not mentioned in the document. By exiting the agreement, the Russian government hopes to regain flexibility, especially as its military situation is challenging and Europe is proving less vulnerable than expected to its energy blackmail. The Russian Minister of Agriculture, Dmitri Patrushev, who is also the son of the Secretary General of the Security Council, Nikolai Patrushev, stated that his country was ready to "provide up to 500,000 tons of cereals for free to the poorest countries over the next four months," with the support of Turkey. Ankara, which played a leading role in the "cereal initiative," has not commented on the Russian withdrawal. Eleven ships were still able to be inspected on Sunday before Moscow's representatives withdrew definitively. Meanwhile, Russian exports continue freely. An investigation by the Financial Times, published on October 30, also shows how Russia manages to export cereals harvested in the occupied areas of southern Ukraine by passing them off as Russian grain.
lead: Depriving Kiev once again of the ability to sell its agricultural production through the Black Sea could worsen the global food crisis.

title: Lutte d’influence entre les Européens et les Russes dans le Caucase
text: The mediation efforts undertaken by Emmanuel Macron between Armenia and Azerbaijan did not take long to stir up tensions with Vladimir Putin's Russia. While Russia is preoccupied with its setbacks in the war in Ukraine, risking weakening its positions in the Caucasus, it nonetheless defends its traditional role as an arbiter in the region, especially in the face of Europeans. The recent clash between the Russian and French heads of state gives an idea of the intensity of the struggle for influence. On October 6, in Prague, during the first summit of the European Political Community, Emmanuel Macron had praised the gathering, along with the President of the European Council, Charles Michel, of the leaders of the two warring countries, Nikol Pashinyan for Armenia, and Ilham Aliyev for Azerbaijan. After several hours of discussions, it was agreed to send a civilian mission from the European Union to the border between the two states to verify, following the resurgence of violence in September, that it was being respected by Azerbaijani forces. A few days later, on France 2, Emmanuel Macron stated that Moscow had "played the game of Azerbaijan with Turkish complicity" during the 2020 conflict between Baku and Yerevan for control of the enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh. "It is a destabilization maneuver by Russia, which, in the Caucasus, seeks to create disorder to weaken and divide us all," he said, adding that France would "never" abandon the Armenians. "Lack of understanding" These statements provoked a strong response from those concerned. "I think there is a lack of understanding of the course of the conflict in these statements and, evidently, of information from France on the positions of the parties," reacted Vladimir Putin on Friday, October 14, during a summit of former USSR countries in Astana, Kazakhstan. He judged Macron's remarks as "incorrect" and "unacceptable." Ilham Aliyev, also present in Astana, criticized the statements as "insulting, unacceptable, and false." "We no longer see any possibility (...) for France to play any role in the normalization of Azerbaijani-Armenian relations," he declared. The incident comes at a bad time, as Europeans and the United States seek to restart negotiations for a peace treaty between the two enemies. Essentially, Moscow does not appreciate the civilian mission announced in Prague, which should be deployed in the coming days. "We see this as a new attempt by the EU to interfere in every way possible in the normalization of relations between Armenia and Azerbaijan, to undermine the mediation efforts of our country," reacted Maria Zakharova, the spokesperson for the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, on October 11. The Russians "not credible" For the EU, this mission is a first step, but above all a symbolic victory against Russia, now unable to play the role of arbiter it claims in the region. "For the first time since the first war in Upper Karabakh, we will have an international presence on the ground that is not Russian," rejoices a French diplomatic source. "It is the beginning of a more substantial involvement of Europe in the peace process." So far, only the Russians had the ability to act, "but today, they are nowhere to be found," adds the same source, "and they are not credible on the issue of territorial integrity" - even less so since the annexations, on September 30, of four Ukrainian provinces. Beyond this mission, the Elysée identifies a second "point of effort" within the framework of the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE, of which Russia and Azerbaijan are members): "We are trying to obtain an agreement from all members to be able to deploy a mission with a much broader scope, which will more generally address peace and security issues for people," indicates a French diplomat. Another way to challenge Russian tutelage in the region. Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev and his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin, on October 13, in Astana (Kazakhstan). VYACHESLAV PROKOFYEV / AP Relations between Azerbaijan and Armenia escalated after the attack by Baku forces on September 13 and 14 on several dozen Armenian cities and villages, including Goris, Jermuk, Kapan, Vardenis, and Sotk. These clashes, the deadliest since the end of the war in Upper Karabakh in 2020, have resulted in at least 286 deaths on both sides. According to Yerevan, Azerbaijani soldiers took advantage of the situation to advance 10 square kilometers into Armenian territory, of which they now occupy over 50 square kilometers. The first meeting of the border demarcation commissions was held on May 24. The next one is scheduled by the end of October in Brussels.
lead: France's mediation efforts between Armenia and Azerbaijan are irritating Moscow, the traditional arbiter of tensions in this region.

title: Cible de rumeurs prorusses, l’ambassadeur de France à Kiev nie avoir démissionné
text: "Resignation of the French Ambassador in Ukraine", "the French Ambassador in Ukraine has left Kiev and said that Zelensky has also left!"... While new Russian strikes hit the Ukrainian capital on Tuesday, October 18, many pro-Russian accounts were circulating a rumor about the resignation of the French Ambassador in Ukraine, Etienne de Poncins. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs assures Le Monde that "there has never been any question of a resignation." "The ambassador is at work, in Kiev," adds the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, denouncing "an attempt at manipulation." Shared several thousand times on social media, the false information had been denied by the ambassador himself. "This is 'fake news,' please remove it immediately," he posted on Twitter Wednesday morning. He was responding to a tweet, since deleted, from the departmental delegate of the National Rally in Mayenne, Jean-Michel Cadenas. Unsuccessful candidate in the last legislative elections in the 2nd district of Mayenne (16% in the first round), he had relayed the rumor that Etienne de Poncins had resigned. Without any source, he quoted what was presented as a statement from the ambassador ("We no longer know what we are defending here, even Volodymyr Zelensky is no longer here in Kiev, why do you continue [sic] to sacrifice the Ukrainian people"), while adding his comment: "We wonder too!" At the origin of this fake quote, a completely fabricated piece of information, sometimes accompanied by a reference to an interview with the French Ambassador in Kiev in which he recounted, on October 11 on BFM-TV, having "spent the afternoon in the basement" during the Russian bombings on Kiev the day before. The implication: shocked and demoralized, Mr. de Poncins would have since given up his position. In passing, the alleged verbatim repeats a popular refrain among pro-Kremlin circles: the idea that Volodymyr Zelensky had fled Kiev and was filming himself against a green screen, supposedly from Poland. The rumor was spread, among others, on Monday night by Agora Actualités, a Facebook page located in Algeria that spreads many pro-Kremlin rumors, before being picked up by many other similar pro-Russian African news pages, such as International Panafricaniste. However, its dissemination by a Facebook page presenting itself as RT Afrique's has popularized it. This last message, still without a shadow of a source, was picked up in France by the conspiracy theorist influencer Silvano Trotta, for example. "Nothing in the media... except the Russian media. Would they lie when it will be easily verifiable in the following days?" wondered the pro-Kremlin antivax ufologist on Tuesday night, on the verge of realizing that, once again, he was spreading false information. Update, Wednesday, October 19 at 4:37 p.m.: addition of the reaction from the Quai d'Orsay
lead: Messages widely shared on social media, including by a member of the National Rally, claimed that Etienne de Poncins had left his position in Ukraine.

title: « URSS, l’empire rouge », sur Arte : chronique fouillée de l’histoire du pays des soviets
text: ARTE - TUESDAY, OCTOBER 25 AT 8:55 PM - DOCUMENTARY The late USSR (1922-1991), born just a century ago from the union between Russia, Belarus, Ukraine, and Transcaucasia (a region consisting of Georgia, Armenia, and Azerbaijan), has inspired numerous documentaries. Recently, some have even made an impact with their quality, the interest of the archives, and the testimonies collected - such as "The Red Army" (2021) by Michaël Prazan, or "Gulag, a Soviet Story" (2019) by Patrick Rotman, Nicolas Werth, and François Aymé. This time, with this extensive German documentary in three parts, the authors did not want to focus on a specific aspect but chose to chronologically unfold the entire history of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, which Vladimir Putin considers the disappearance of as "the greatest geopolitical catastrophe of the last century." This history lesson, somewhat unsurprising in its form but valuable in its reminders, and sometimes surprising with anecdotes about daily life, naturally benefits from numerous filmed archives. The most interesting and rare ones come from the distant Asian republics, Georgia, or the Baltic countries. Not to mention excerpts from quite bewildering fiction films. From "Cosmic Voyage" (1936), through "Letters from a Dead Man" (1986) and "The White Sun of the Desert" (1970), there is something for everyone. Diversity and quality of witnesses Another strength of the documentary is the diversity and quality of the witnesses gathered. Russians, Kyrgyz, Lithuanians, Belarusians, Ukrainians, or Georgians, the women and men interviewed on camera were, in the USSR, political leaders, police officers, or military personnel. But also intellectuals, artists, or ordinary citizens recounting their adolescence. Their words are worth more than any history lesson. Among these testimonies, that of Svetlana Alexievich stands out. Born in Ukraine to a Belarusian father and a Ukrainian mother in 1948, she won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2015. A journalist and writer of exceptional works that help us better understand life in the USSR (from "The Unwomanly Face of War" in 1985, to "Voices from Chernobyl" in 1997, and "Second-Hand Time" in 2013, to name a few), she recounts, in a gentle voice, the role of women in war, housing problems, the scourge of alcoholism, secret conversations in communal kitchens... In three times fifty-two minutes, the entire history of the largest country in the world, populated at its peak by 240 million inhabitants, unfolds. A complex and tumultuous political, cultural, military, and social history. A red history.
lead: A substantial German documentary recalls the communist empire founded by Lenin and disappeared in 1991. Academic, but informative.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : le marathon de l’opposition biélorusse pour ouvrir des relations officielles avec Kiev
text: The decoration of the office set up in an imposing building in the Ukrainian capital is limited to two flags, discreet but enlightening clues: the blue and yellow of Ukraine; the red and white of democratic Belarus. The main objective of the representation of Belarusian democratic forces, opened in June, is to establish official relations with the authorities in Kiev and to secure a meeting, which has not yet taken place, between the main figure of the opposition, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, and the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky. "We wish for a relationship that would allow us to adopt a joint approach on Belarus and on how we could win the war in Ukraine," explains Valery Kavaleuski, in charge of foreign affairs for the opposition leader, visiting Kiev on Monday, October 17. The Belarusian delegate, who is no stranger to Ukraine, has been sent on a mission to establish contacts with the Kiev authorities. "We have appointments and unofficial meetings with officials from the Ukrainian government and members of the Verkhovna Rada," the Ukrainian Parliament, he explains, without going into details. Resistance from within Valery Kavaleuski is a former diplomat who worked for a time in the United States before joining the ranks of the democratic opposition that emerged in August 2020. At the time, the dictator Alexander Lukashenko's reelection with 80% of the vote sparked an unprecedented wave of protests in the country, harshly suppressed by the Minsk regime. The main opposition candidate in the presidential election, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, has since been living in exile in Vilnius, the Lithuanian capital, like the majority of opposition members and civil society. Many of these organizations have also sought refuge in Ukraine. At the end of February, as Moscow's forces launched their offensive on the Ukrainian capital from Belarusian territory, the democratic opposition began a communication campaign to unite forces between the two countries. "For us, Ukraine is a priority, as its fate is closely linked to the internal situation in Belarus," explains the charge d'affaires. Ukrainians know that they will necessarily have to deal with Lukashenko's regime to win this war. It is crucial that Belarus becomes democratic and no longer poses a threat. The opposition relies on the support of Western partners, its extensive network of organizations established abroad, and its ability to act directly on Belarusian territory to convince Ukraine. In Vilnius, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya's team has a wealth of information on the situation in the country. At the beginning of the war, Belarusian resistance fighters notably distinguished themselves by disrupting the railway traffic on which the Russian forces depended to transport weapons and military vehicles. This network, called the "railway partisans," is dependent on a structure called Bypol, which brings together former Belarusian police officers who resigned after the repression of the summer of 2020. From their exile, these individuals organize resistance from within. "These people work in Belarus, on the ground," emphasizes Valery Kavaleuski. "It is important for Ukrainians to understand that we can be useful to Ukraine at the international level, but also, and most importantly, in Belarus." Some indifference The numerous calls from the Belarusian opposition to establish official relations are currently met with some indifference from the side of Kiev. And the silence was already noticeable before the war. After the 2020 protests, the Ukrainian authorities had discreetly supported the opposition movement and refrained from imposing economic sanctions, unlike other countries in the region, such as Lithuania or Poland. "In a way, Ukrainians contributed to keeping Lukashenko in power during difficult times, while Western countries, the European Union, and the United States imposed sanctions against his regime," laments the opponent. However, the diplomat assures understanding the current caution of Ukrainians. "If we compare with the position of France or Germany, for example, the stakes are not as high as for Ukraine." The establishment of official relations between the Belarusian opposition in exile and Kiev could be considered a "provocation" in the eyes of the Belarusian leader, as he "can still inflict damage and harm the Ukrainian population. The Belarusian territory is used by Russia to launch attacks since the beginning of the war," Valery Kavaleuski recalls. On an international scale, discussions and exchanges with Ukrainian diplomats stationed in foreign capitals are simpler and clearer. "They are always willing to meet, exchange, and listen to us. The mutual interest is there."
lead: Former diplomat Valery Kavaleuski, advisor to exiled opposition leader Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, has been sent on a mission to Ukraine to establish contacts with the authorities.

title: Dans le port de Hongkong, la présence embarrassante d’un mégayacht russe
text: It has been over a week since the sleek silhouette of the Nord, the megayacht of one of the wealthiest Russian oligarchs, Alexei Mordashov, appeared in the bay of Hong Kong. The 142-meter-long vessel anchored in full view of the gleaming towers of the International Finance Center (IFC) and the ferry routes connecting Central to the neighboring islands. A large Russian flag flies on its rear deck. Despite protests from the United States and Europe, the presence of this symbol of the Putin regime was explicitly embraced by the new head of the special administrative region. Former police officer John Lee stated that Hong Kong did not impose sanctions "without legal basis." Nevertheless, as Hong Kong seeks to reassure the Western world of its financial reliability, and with an upcoming international summit where Hong Kong hopes to attract top figures from Wall Street and the City, "the presence of the Nord certainly gives a bad impression," according to an unnamed Western banker. In the maritime industry, professionals were surprised by the Nord's choice of anchorage. "Why place it right in the middle of Hong Kong Bay if not to ensure everyone sees it?" they wondered. It would have seemed more normal and discreet for the Nord to anchor on the Gold Coast, the western coast of the New Territories of Hong Kong, specially equipped to accommodate superyachts. A heavyweight in the Russian economy, the price of the Nord, launched from the German Lurssen shipyards in 2021, was estimated at over 500 million euros (including a pool, cinema, and two helipads) with a maintenance cost of around 100,000 euros per day. However, the issue with the Nord in Hong Kong has nothing to do with its features; simply put, its owner, Alexei Mordashov, 57 years old, is one of the oligarchs targeted by the sanctions imposed by the United States and Europe following the outbreak of the war in Ukraine. "I have absolutely nothing to do with the current geopolitical tension and I do not understand why the European Union has imposed sanctions on me (...) nor how these sanctions against me will contribute to resolving the dreadful conflict in Ukraine," he reacted at the time. While the man was already a billionaire before Vladimir Putin became president, he remains a symbol and a heavyweight in the Russian economy. His fortune, amassed in mining and steel, was $29 billion before the sanctions. He is also one of the main shareholders of Rossiya Bank, closely linked to financing the Putin system. Mr. Mordashov's other vessel, the Lady-M, was seized in March in Italy. According to Marine Tracker, a website specialized in real-time tracking of ships across the oceans, the Nord left the Seychelles at the beginning of March, at the start of the operations to seize the assets of key figures of the Russian regime. It arrived in Vladivostok, Russia, in April. Its appearance in Hong Kong on October 5 immediately caused a stir, not only among Western diplomats. The war in Ukraine is a sensitive topic in Hong Kong. The riots of 2019 were partly inspired by the Euromaidan movement of 2013. Some heroes of this revolution, like the military man Volodymyr Parasyuk, became celebrities among the youth of Hong Kong at that time. Three years later, unable to express hostility towards the Nord, a portion of the Hong Kong population is discussing its presence with a mix of sarcasm and fatalism on encrypted social media. "It's reassuring to know that we can always count on Russian money when American banks have left Hong Kong," posted an internet user on the LIHKG forum (pronounced Lindeng). Unwelcome guest "Hong Kong's reputation as a financial center depends on respect for international laws and standards," quickly responded the U.S. consulate in Hong Kong. "The possible use of Hong Kong as a refuge (...) questions the transparency of the business environment." "The European Union is aware of the efforts of certain Russian individuals and companies to circumvent European sanctions," said the European representation in Hong Kong, adding that "since October 6, individuals or companies that assist targeted individuals in evading sanctions could themselves be sanctioned." In other words, by not imposing Western sanctions, Hong Kong could be exposed to secondary sanctions, as well as individuals and companies that facilitated the arrival and stay of the yacht in Hong Kong by handling its customs formalities, organizing the boat's refueling, ensuring its security, etc. The local government emphasized that it always scrupulously implements sanctions decided by the United Nations but not those "unilaterally adopted by independent jurisdictions." On Tuesday, the Chief Executive, John Lee, once again dismissed Western protests, stating, "As an international financial center, our regulatory system is in line with the rest of the world. But if a certain place takes measures and those measures have no legal basis, we have no reason to do the same." John Lee took the opportunity to describe the imposition of sanctions on individuals as "barbaric," reminding that he too was "sanctioned" by the United States, along with about a dozen Hong Kong leaders. Behind these boastful statements, however, there is no doubt that Hong Kong would have been better off without this cumbersome guest. "International finance is still dominated by the United States and the dollar. Hong Kong cannot go too far in its confrontation with Washington, London, or Europe," observes a Western banker. The territory is trying to restore its reputation. The current priority for Hong Kong is therefore to make people forget the last three anni horribiles: the 2019 riots that drove away Chinese tourists, followed by the harsh and erratic handling of the Covid-19 pandemic, alongside Beijing's brutal reassertion of political and social control... All of this has destroyed Hong Kong's reputation as a free city and driven away hundreds of thousands of well-educated Hong Kongers, as well as many expatriates who gave Hong Kong its valuable label as an international city. In addition to the International Finance Summit, which some of the biggest names in the industry have already promised to attend in November, the Hong Kong Sevens rugby tournament, Hong Kong's equivalent of the Venice Carnival, canceled three times, is also set to take place in November. As the territory tries to restore its reputation, Hong Kong has no interest in becoming a haven for blacklisted oligarchs. However, the risk exists, says Bart Jan Kimman, a maritime broker and superyacht agent in Hong Kong: "Other superyachts owned by other sanctioned oligarchs are currently waiting in Vladivostok... As the ice approaches, they are wondering where to go, and Hong Kong is obviously a destination of choice if they feel protected there..."
lead: The "North", owned by a Russian oligarch under sanctions, has dropped anchor in the bay of the special administrative region, likely at the invitation of Beijing.

title: Giuliano da Empoli lauréat du grand prix de l’Académie française
text: This is the most coveted and prestigious literary prize among the many awarded by the Immortals. Italo-Swiss Giuliano da Empoli received, on Thursday, October 27, the grand prize for the novel from the French Academy for "The Mage of the Kremlin" (Gallimard, 280 pages, 20 euros, digital 15 euros), a novel critical of Russian President Vladimir Putin. Giuliano da Empoli, 49 years old, born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, was a political advisor to the Italian Prime Minister Matteo Renzi. In his novel, published in April by Gallimard, he provides an overview of the power mechanisms in Russia over the past thirty years. The long monologue he imagines in the mouth of the fictional Kremlin advisor Vadim Baranov is inspired by the career of Vladislav Surkov, co-founder of Vladimir Putin's United Russia party for the 2001 presidential election. Vadim Baranov reveals the cynicism of a president he calls "the Tsar," offering the opportunity to revisit the events that have shaken Russia's history since the breakup of the Soviet Union in 1991. "My book is truly imbued with a certain French literature, which has had its place in the Academy for a long time, and which dissects power, observes it," said the winner to the press. Mr. da Empoli received nine votes, compared to five for Jean Michelin, a career military officer, and three for Le Monde journalist Pascale Robert-Diard. This choice was not very surprising as the prize is presided over by a secretary of the French Academy with expertise in Russian affairs, Hélène Carrère d'Encausse. Mr. da Empoli could also win the Goncourt Prize on November 3, as he is one of the four finalists. Winning both of these literary prizes in the same year is an achievement accomplished by few authors, as both Academies generally value the independence of their choices.
lead: The Italo-Swiss author is being rewarded for his critical novel about Russian President Vladimir Putin, "The Wizard of the Kremlin."

title: En Norvège, plusieurs Russes accusés d’avoir piloté des drones illégalement près de sites stratégiques
text: This is the seventh Russian national arrested in Norway in less than two weeks. On Wednesday, October 19, the police in Finnmark, in the far north of the country, revealed that they had arrested a 47-year-old man two days earlier in the port of Hammerfest. He has been placed in provisional detention for two weeks on suspicion of flying a drone in the Norwegian archipelago of Svalbard, in violation of the ban on Russians flying over the country's territory since the beginning of the war in Ukraine. According to Norwegian media, the man is believed to be Andrei Yakunin, the son of Vladimir Yakunin, former head of Russian Railways and close to President Vladimir Putin. He was traveling on his yacht, the Firebird, under British flag, currently docked in the port of Hammerfest. During his arrest, the Norwegian police seized a drone and electronic devices, which are currently being analyzed. On Wednesday at noon, the Norwegian Intelligence Service (PST) announced that they would coordinate the investigations related to the multiple arrests that have occurred in recent days. The first arrest took place at the Storskog border post on October 11. A 50-year-old man, holding two Russian passports and an Israeli passport, was arrested as he was about to enter Russia. In his luggage, border guards found two drones and a series of partially encrypted photos and videos. "Ordinary tourists" On October 13, in the town of Mosjoen in the west of the country, the police arrested three Russian men and a woman in their twenties suspected of violating a ban on photographing a strategic site. The following day, a 51-year-old Russian national was also arrested after being seen photographing Tromso Airport. According to investigators, he was in possession of a drone and various equipment with which he photographed Kirkenes Airport near the border with Russia and Norwegian military helicopters. At the moment, the four cases seem to have no connection, except that all the suspects are Russian nationals. Nevertheless, they are causing some anxiety in Norway, less than a month after leaks were discovered about the Nord Stream gas pipelines in the Baltic Sea, while unauthorized drone flights have been observed repeatedly near Norwegian gas and oil platforms. On Wednesday, Hedvig Moe, the number two at PST, stated that these activities could be "used for espionage purposes or to sow fear and uncertainty about, among other things, Norway's ability to supply gas to Europe." A few hours earlier, air traffic had to be briefly interrupted at Bergen Airport, the country's second-largest city, after drones were observed. On its Facebook page, the Russian Embassy in Oslo denounces the "hysteria" of Norwegians and laments that "ordinary tourists" could be targeted.
lead: On four occasions in less than ten days, Russian nationals have been arrested for flying drones over Norwegian territory.

title: « Le règne de glace. L’homme qui a osé s’opposer à Poutine » : quand Bill Browder raconte sa croisade pour faire adopter le « Magnitsky Act »
text:
Book. Initially, nothing predisposed William Browder, the head of a speculative investment fund, to become a staunch advocate for human rights. But ten years spent in Russia changed the trajectory of this 58-year-old American businessman, who became a naturalized British citizen. Through sheer determination, he succeeded in getting the "Magnitsky Act" passed in the United States in 2012. Originally targeting thirteen Russian officials for their role in the disappearance and torture death of Sergei Magnitsky, the lawyer for Hermitage Capital, the sanctions expanded in 2016 to include other state officials, such as those involved in the murder of Saudi journalist Jamal Khashoggi or the repression of Uighurs in China.

To achieve this result, Bill Browder led an incredible crusade, which he describes in detail in his narrative - the second after "Red Notice" (Kero, 2015). With him, we jump from one plane to another, rubbing shoulders with not always well-intentioned lawyers, motivated whistleblowers, corrupt police officers, and skeptical politicians. We enter the offices of the powerful, become familiar with the circles of a global elite where fortunes are made. And one also becomes frankly paranoid reading about all the maneuvers Moscow attempted to thwart Browder's plans: legal harassment, witness poisoning, surveillance, dirty tricks, buying complicity... Endemic corruption.

When he ventured into capitalism - "to annoy" his family, as he confessed to Vanity Fair in 2017 - Bill Browder, the grandson of the former General Secretary of the Communist Party in the United States, Earl Browder, had no idea what awaited him in Russia. At first, business was going rather well, even very well: "At the peak of my career, I was responsible for managing $4.5 billion invested in Russian securities," he writes. However, colossal sums disappeared into the depths of endemic corruption that he set out to expose. Barred from entering Russia in 2005, targeted by Interpol red notices, the businessman was accused of embezzlement until Sergei Magnitsky, who remained in Russia, uncovered a fraudulent tax refund of $230 million from the fund to Russian shell companies. Things went so wrong that Browder fought even harder, drawing the wrath of Russia up to the highest levels. In July 2018, during his bilateral meeting with former U.S. President Donald Trump in Helsinki, Finland, while Russian interference in American elections was still scandalous, Vladimir Putin mentioned Bill Browder as one of the most acute points of contention between the two countries. The attack failed. Since then, Canada and the European Union have in turn adopted texts inspired by the Magnitsky Act.
lead: The American businessman who became a British citizen is behind the law, adopted by the Obama administration, which allows for the sanctioning of government officials who violate human rights.

title: La Russie suspend l’accord sur les exportations de céréales ukrainiennes après une attaque de drones sur ses navires
text: The agreement on grain exports from Ukrainian ports, vital for global food supply, was suspended by Russia on Saturday, October 29th. Moscow justified this suspension by a drone attack targeting the Russian Black Sea fleet stationed in the bay of Sevastopol, in annexed Crimea, on Saturday morning. Ukraine, on the other hand, denounced it as "a pretext" and called for pressure on Russia to "once again commit to respecting its obligations" under the agreement concluded in July under the auspices of the United Nations (UN) and Turkey, the only agreement between Moscow and Kiev since the conflict began on February 24th. The Joint Coordination Center responsible for overseeing this agreement confirmed that no cargo movements had been approved for Sunday. Nine cargoes were able to pass through the Black Sea maritime corridor on Saturday, and "more than ten others" are ready to do the same in both directions. According to the Ukrainian Minister of Infrastructure, Oleksandr Kubrakov, a ship loaded with 40 tons of grain was supposed to depart from Ukraine to Ethiopia on Sunday, "but due to Russia's blockade of the grain corridor, exports are impossible," he wrote on Twitter. In his daily video posted on the internet, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky stated that the Russian decision "did not actually date from today." "Russia began exacerbating the global food shortage in September when it started blocking the movement of ships carrying our agricultural products," he said. A "scandalous" decision, according to Biden "This is a transparent intention by Russia to once again threaten large-scale famine in Africa and Asia," he added. According to him, at least 176 ships carrying over two million tons of grain were already blocked by Moscow. "A strong international response is needed. At the UN level, and especially at the G20 level," from which Russia should no longer participate, he said. The President of the United States, Joe Biden, for his part, deemed Russia's decision "scandalous." "There was no reason for them to do this," he told the press. Secretary of State Antony Blinken, on the other hand, stated that Moscow is "once again using food as a weapon," exacerbating already severe humanitarian crises and food insecurity worldwide. The UN Secretary-General, Antonio Guterres, expressed deep concern about the situation, as reported by his spokesperson in a statement on Sunday. Mr. Guterres is engaged in "intense consultations" to urge Russia to reverse its decision and has decided to postpone his departure for one day to attend the Arab League summit in Algiers scheduled for Tuesday. He notably spoke with the European Union's foreign policy chief, Josep Borrell, who called on Moscow to "return to the agreement" in a tweet. "The EU will do its part to tackle the global food crisis," added Mr. Borrell. The cereal agreement allowed for the export of millions of tons of grains that had been stuck in Ukrainian ports since the beginning of Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The blockade had caused a spike in food prices, raising fears of famines. London accused of complicity by Moscow Russian President Vladimir Putin has been critical of this agreement in recent weeks, emphasizing that his country's exports, as another major cereal producer, were suffering due to international sanctions. Moscow justified this suspension by citing a drone attack on Saturday morning in annexed Crimea. "The preparation of this terrorist act and the training of military personnel from the 73rd Ukrainian center for special maritime operations were carried out by British specialists based in Otchakov [Otchakiv in Ukrainian], in the Mykolaiv oblast, Ukraine," the Russian defense ministry said on Telegram. The British defense ministry responded by denouncing "false information" intended to "distract attention." "The maritime drones were moving in the security zone of the 'cereal corridor,'" the Russian defense ministry said on Sunday, claiming to have recovered drone debris and that some of these drones had "navigation modules made in Canada." One of them may have been launched "from one of the civilian ships chartered by Kiev or its Western masters for the export of agricultural products from Ukrainian seaports," it judged. Moscow also accused London of being involved in the explosions that damaged the Russian Nord Stream 1 and 2 gas pipelines in the Baltic Sea in September and promised to bring the issue to the UN Security Council. The British defense ministry denounced a "fabricated story" by Russia to "distract attention from its disastrous handling of the illegal invasion of Ukraine."
lead: Very critical of this agreement signed in July under the auspices of Turkey and the UN, Moscow had been looking for a pretext to denounce it for several weeks.

title: Midterms 2022 : les Européens inquiets d’une éventuelle remise en cause de l’aide américaine à l’Ukraine
text: Lingering British crisis, new Italian government dominated by the far right, Hungarian obstructions, and Franco-German tensions: Europe is shaking on all fronts. An additional storm threatens, coming from the United States and linked to the war in Ukraine. Could the bipartisan unity that has prevailed in Washington since February regarding the massive aid provided to Kiev be at risk of crumbling in the event of a Republican majority following the midterm elections scheduled for November 8? The latest polls, showing a rise in support for the Grand Old Party in voting intentions, give hope for at least regaining control of the House of Representatives, or even the Senate. However, within the House, a faction within the Republican Party is pushing for more oversight or even a reduction in aid to Ukraine. During a visit to Washington on Friday, October 21, to prepare for French President Emmanuel Macron's state visit in early December, French Minister of Foreign Affairs Catherine Colonna emphasized the need to maintain a united front among allies. "We must not give the Kremlin the opportunity to divide us," warned the diplomat during a speech at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a think tank. "If Russia's actions go unanswered, wars of conquest could become the norm," she cautioned, reiterating these remarks later during a joint press conference with Secretary of State Antony Blinken. In May, the Senate approved a large $40 billion aid package for Ukraine. In the House, 57 Grand Old Party representatives voted against it (28% of the group). Their numbers appear to be growing, fueled by rising concerns about inflation among voters. Some weariness and even exasperation are starting to show within a segment of the Republican base, tempted by isolationism and sometimes fascinated by Vladimir Putin's brutal authoritarianism. Their leading advocate is Fox News host Tucker Carlson, who consistently shifts blame, accusing his own government of instigating and encouraging the current war. According to him, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is a "client" of the Biden administration. No "blank check" for Kiev. The general public, however, observes Ukraine from a distance. Unlike in Europe, the war does not resonate as a familiar and close crisis. In a Pew Research Center poll from September, 37% of respondents believed that U.S. support for Ukraine was at an adequate level, but 20% thought it was too high, compared to only 7% in March. One in three Republicans now considers this aid to be excessive. Kevin McCarthy, the Republican leader in the House of Representatives, explained this week that there would be no "blank check" signed by his party for Kiev in case of a majority in Congress. He linked his comment to the recession that the United States could enter. In reality, the lawmaker is thinking a lot about his personal trajectory. Aspiring to become the House Speaker in January 2023, he needs to win the favor of the lawmakers from the MAGA movement ("Make America Great Again," Donald Trump's slogan). However, the former president holds a strong resentment against McCarthy. He blames him for allowing a House inquiry commission to be formed on the January 6, 2021 assault, which he considers 100% hostile despite the presence of two Republicans. On Friday, this commission formally issued a subpoena to Donald Trump to testify. A stronger consensus in the Senate On the other hand, in the Senate, the Republican leader, Mitch McConnell (Kentucky), issued a clear statement calling on allies to act "faster and more proactively" in support of Ukraine. He mentioned specifically anti-aircraft defense systems, long-range firing capabilities, as well as economic and humanitarian aid. McConnell assured that a future Republican majority in the Senate would ensure the "timely delivery of necessary weapons and greater assistance to Ukraine's allies." In the Senate, the consensus between Democrats and Republicans seems to hold better, even though some candidates – like J.D. Vance in Ohio – have also expressed skepticism about American investment. An argument often raised, beyond the significant sums allocated, is that Joe Biden would be more concerned about Ukraine's borders, a non-strategic state in their eyes, than those of his own country, especially the one with Mexico, where a record number of migrants have been apprehended this year. "These guys don't understand. It goes far beyond Ukraine," explained Joe Biden during a visit to Philadelphia on Thursday. "It's Eastern Europe. It's NATO. It's really serious, with significant consequences... They have no sense of American foreign policy." Democrats are considering voting on a new aid package for Ukraine in Congress between the November elections and the next term. It could amount to several tens of billions of dollars and garner support from some Republicans with traditional foreign policy convictions. Such aid would ensure continuity of American policy towards Russia in the course of 2023.
lead: As the mid-term elections approach, some Republicans want to reconsider the support provided to Kiev since the beginning of the war.

title: Aide humanitaire à Gaza, manifestation contre l’A69, l’affiche de la finale du Mondial de rugby… Les cinq infos à retenir du week-end
text: Have you fallen a bit behind on the news these past two days? We summarize the main information from Saturday, October 21, and Sunday, October 22. Israel-Hamas War: First humanitarian convoys entered Gaza After fifteen days of war between Israel and Hamas, humanitarian convoys entered Gaza on Saturday and Sunday, under Israeli blockade. These trucks crossed the Rafah terminal, a border town with Egypt, which was opened exceptionally for this humanitarian operation. A first convoy of around twenty trucks arrived in Gaza on Saturday, but according to the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA), at least a hundred trucks per day are needed. On Sunday, a second convoy of seventeen trucks passed through Rafah. Additionally, six tanker trucks delivered fuel on Sunday, according to a journalist from Agence France-Presse. Rocket fire from Palestinian groups targeting Israel continues in response to Israeli strikes on the Palestinian enclave, where, according to the United Nations, one million people have fled the bombings in the North to gather in the South, near the border with Egypt. The situation is "catastrophic," five UN agencies stated on Saturday. Meanwhile, Cairo was the scene of a "peace summit" that failed to forge an "international consensus" to call for a ceasefire in the Gaza war and respect for international law. The summit, attended by heads of state, ministers, and diplomats, mainly from Europe and the Arab world, ended without a joint final statement. The Israeli military also announced on Saturday that it would intensify its strikes on Gaza as part of preparations for the next phase of the "Operation Iron Swords," which aims to invade the Palestinian territory, controlled by Hamas, in order to "annihilate" the Islamist movement. Tensions also remain high at the border between Israel and Lebanon. Residents are fleeing border areas as exchanges of fire increase between the Israeli army and the pro-Iranian Hezbollah, an ally of Hamas. A69 Toulouse-Castres: a demonstration brings together several thousand people, an opponents' ZAD evacuated Demonstrators in Saix on October 21, 2023. CHARLY TRIBALLEAU / AFP Thousands of opponents of the A69 highway project between Toulouse and Castres demonstrated on Saturday near Castres. The mobilization ended on Sunday after law enforcement officers evicted opponents from the construction site at La Crémade, east of Castres, where they had set up a ZAD (zone to defend). Nine people were arrested, six law enforcement officers were slightly injured, and the organizers reported around thirty minor injuries, without the need for the fire department or emergency medical services. The day before, the demonstration had gathered more than 10,000 people, according to the organizers. The prefecture reported 2,400 protesters in the main procession, as well as "2,500 violent individuals" who targeted two companies, the Carayon cement plant and a construction company, Bardou et fils, both involved in the A69 construction site, according to the concessionaire Atosca. During a press conference at the end of the day on Sunday, the prefect of Tarn mentioned around 150 people still present near the evacuated site, in a neighboring field. However, "the majority of them are packing up their tents and dismantling the marquees," according to the prefect. "There is no longer any form of aggression towards the gendarmerie forces," he assured. In Ukraine, a Russian strike on a postal depot kills six in the Kharkiv region. At least six people were killed and seventeen were injured on Saturday by Russian missile strikes on a postal depot in the outskirts of Kharkiv, in northeastern Ukraine, as reported by local officials. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky shared a video on social media showing what appears to be a severely damaged warehouse next to debris and a container bearing the logo of the Ukrainian private postal operator Nova Pochta. The six people killed and the injured were "employees of the company who were inside the Nova Pochta terminal," said the governor of the Kharkiv region, Oleh Synehoubov. "The victims, aged 19 to 42, were injured by shrapnel and the blast [from the explosions]," he added. Bobby Charlton, World Cup winner with England and Manchester United star, has passed away. English football legend Bobby Charlton, a World Cup winner in 1966 and former Manchester United star, has died at the age of 86, as announced by the English club and his family on Saturday. "Manchester United is in mourning following the death of Sir Bobby Charlton, one of the greatest and most beloved players in the club's history," Manchester United stated in a release. Born in Ashington, a working-class town in the northeast of England on October 11, 1937, Bobby Charlton joined United at the age of 15. Known for his powerful strikes, he also became a symbol of elegance and sportsmanship throughout his career. Rugby World Cup: South Africa advances to final to face the All Blacks. Defending champions South Africa will defend their world title against New Zealand in a week, after breaking through a tightly contested semi-final against England (16-15) on Saturday. "We are in the final, that's all that matters," declared the colossal second-row Eben Etzebeth at the end of a match where the Springboks were trailing for seventy-eight minutes. In the final, it will be a rematch of the 1995 final between the top two teams in the World Rugby rankings: the Springboks against the All Blacks. Back then, South Africa clinched victory in a hard-fought match (15-12, ap) thanks to the kicking game of their fly-half Joel Stransky. Twenty-eight years after their first title at home, the South Africans have the opportunity to become the first team to sew a fourth star on their jersey (after 1995, 2007, and 2019), a quest also pursued by the New Zealanders (1987, 2011, and 2015). Also: Israel-Hamas. Hamas releases two American hostages. Hurricane Tammy. After hours of violet alert, "diminished danger" in Guadeloupe and end of the lockdown. Niger. Overthrown president Mohamed Bazoum is with his family and "doing well." International. France confirms the suspension of adoptions of children from Madagascar. Switzerland. Populist right-wing party leads by a wide margin in legislative elections with 29.2% of the votes.
lead: If you haven't been following the news, here's what you need to know from the last forty-eight hours.

title: Russie : la détention de la journaliste russo-américaine Alsu Kurmasheva prolongée au moins jusqu’au 23 octobre
text: She has become the second American reporter detained in Russia. Arrested this week, the Russian-American journalist Alsu Kurmasheva had her temporary detention extended at least until October 23, during a hearing at the Sovetskiy Court in Kazan, in the Russian region of Tatarstan, on Friday, October 20. "By decision of the court on October 20, 2023, the detention period of A. Kurmasheva is extended by seventy-two hours," the court announced. The journalist working for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty (RFE/RL) was arrested in the Russian city of Kazan on Wednesday. She is accused of not declaring herself as a "foreign agent." Ms. Kurmasheva was present at her hearing on Friday, in the defendant's cage, wearing an FFP2 mask. She was escorted there by hooded agents, as observed by journalists from Agence France-Presse. Up to five years in prison could be faced. Russia is accused of conducting a campaign of repression against independent media, NGOs, journalists, lawyers, and opponents since the beginning of its invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. While many activists and Russian journalists have fled the country, others - famous or unknown - have been imprisoned. Several Americans are also detained, including journalist Evan Gershkovich who is still in detention, arrested for espionage by the Russian security services (FSB) while reporting in Yekaterinburg on March 29. Russian authorities deny carrying out any form of "persecution" against US citizens. "There are American citizens who violate laws and appropriate measures are taken against them," said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov on Friday when asked about the issue, adding that he was not following Ms. Kurmasheva's case. The Sovetskiy Court in Kazan stated that the American media journalist was being prosecuted for failing to register as a "foreign agent," while engaging in "intentional collection of information regarding military activities" that could be damaging to "the security of the Russian Federation." She faces up to five years in prison. This status of "foreign agent," reminiscent of the Soviet term "enemy of the people," imposes heavy administrative constraints and financial control on the targeted individuals or entities. It also requires that any publication, including on social media, be accompanied by this label. Some of the most respected critics of Russian President Vladimir Putin, such as Nobel Peace Prize laureate and editor-in-chief of the independent newspaper Novaya Gazeta, Dmitry Muratov, are among these "agents." Mrs. Kurmasheva's passports have been confiscated since May. She usually resides in Prague with her husband and children, but she traveled to Russia for a "family emergency" on May 20 and was unable to leave because her American and Russian passports were confiscated. According to the website Tatar Inform, she was fined on October 11 for not declaring her American citizenship to Russian authorities. The website, citing anonymous police sources, reported that she had been involved in mobilizing teachers for the Russian army's offensive in Ukraine. Alsu Kurmasheva, who joined RFE/RL in 1998, works for the service in Tatar and Bashkir languages, covering these ethnic minorities in Russia, particularly in Tatarstan and Bashkortostan, regions in the Volga and Ural areas. Based in Prague, RFE/RL is funded by the U.S. Congress and was founded during the Cold War to counter Soviet propaganda in Eastern Bloc countries. The media outlet still publishes content in multiple languages, often sensitive in countries ruled by authoritarian regimes. "I came to work for [RFE/RL] because this media outlet is important to me, its mission of providing objective information to my people, the people who speak my language, Tatar, in particular," Mrs. Kurmasheva explained in 2014. In recent years, several American citizens arrested in Russia have been released after prisoner exchanges with Washington.
lead: The journalist working for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty was arrested in the Russian city of Kazan on Wednesday. She is accused of not declaring herself as a "foreign agent," a charge for which she could face up to five years in prison.

title: Election présidentielle à Madagascar : l’ex-judoka Siteny entre en campagne et fragilise le boycott de l’opposition
text: He denies having broken the unity of the opposition. The former judoka Siteny Randrianasoloniaiko has entered the campaign to challenge the outgoing Malagasy President, Andry Rajoelina, leaving the other ten candidates of the "collective of 11" to continue their boycott of an election they consider biased and predetermined. On Sunday, October 22, candidate number 13 held his first rally in Vohémar, in the north of the island, where the collapse of the vanilla sector has ruined thousands of planters. "It was not a secret. I had warned that I would not let Andry Rajoelina go to the election alone," explained the former athlete, known to be close to the Russians, to Le Monde. "We do not trust the institutions organizing the election [the Independent National Electoral Commission (CENI) and the High Constitutional Court (HCC)], but history shows that those who do not participate always end up losing. I have no choice." "Siteny," as the Malagasy call him, nevertheless maintains his support for the demands of the "11," including the postponement of the election. The opposition argues, based on the Constitution, that the election cannot be held as long as one of the candidates is unable to participate. One of them is still recovering in Mauritius.
