Seriously injured in the right eye by tear gas fired by law enforcement during a march on October 2, Andry Rajoelina was evacuated and underwent surgery. Doctors have prohibited him from flying for thirty days. On Monday, his lawyers filed a new request with the HCC to recognize a situation of "force majeure" related to their client's health and to once again postpone the election. With significant financial resources, the first round scheduled for November 16 has been delayed by only one week, which "Siteny" considers a joke. But the former judoka has not always been so critical of the authorities. Although he says he feels "betrayed, like all Malagasy, by unfulfilled promises," it was under the banner of the presidential majority IRD that the deputy of Toliara, the city of his origin, was re-elected in 2019 after turning his back on the party of former President Hery Rajaonarimampianina, to which he was affiliated. In Madagascar, the imposing former judoka is primarily known for the roles he holds in national and international sports organizations. President of the Malagasy Olympic Committee, he also heads the African Judo Union and is vice-president of the International Judo Federation (FIJ). In this position, he has forged a friendship with the Austrian-born Romanian Marius Vizer, president of the FIJ and close to Vladimir Putin – himself an accomplished judoka and ambassador of the FIJ until his suspension at the beginning of the war in Ukraine. These connections have led to suspicions that the Malagasy candidate is being secretly financed by Russia. Accusations that he defends himself against, without clarifying the origin of the funds injected into his campaign. Because with Andry Rajoelina, "Siteny" is certainly the candidate with the most significant financial means, especially for traveling by helicopter through a country lacking roads. As early as January, and well before announcing his candidacy, he embarked on a "mihava tour" ("listening to the people" in Malagasy) to gather the grievances of the population and build a program that emphasizes decentralization. "I have no political ties to Russia," he asserts. "My money comes from my business, my family, and those who trust me in Madagascar and abroad." In addition to establishing the British School of Madagascar, "Siteny" has also developed activities in the fields of communication and real estate. Strike notice In the meantime, his solo departure does not seem to dampen the determination of the rest of the opposition, which includes two former heads of state, Marc Ravalomanana and Hery Rajaonarimampianina. After gathering some 50,000 supporters on Saturday at the Antsonjombe coliseum in Antananarivo, the collective resumed its peaceful marches in the capital on Monday, as it does almost every day since the official launch of the campaign on October 10. Gatherings are also organized in the provinces. "The electoral environment has not changed. The conditions are not met for a fair election," notes Hajo Andrianainarivelo, president and candidate of the MMM party. The collective demands the establishment of impartial electoral institutions, the cancellation of the HCC decision entrusting interim power to Prime Minister Christian Ntsay, and the disqualification of Andry Rajoelina's candidacy due to his French nationality acquired in 2014 through naturalization. Fearing that the situation may escalate, the Solidarity Syndicate of Madagascar platform, which brings together 86 organizations from the public, private, and informal sectors, threatens to block the country's administration if the outgoing president and the twelve candidates do not sit down together to find a political solution to the crisis. A strike notice has been filed for October 26.
lead: The candidate held his first rally on Sunday, while a new request was filed with the High Constitutional Court to ask for a postponement of the election.

title: Myroslav Marynovytch, figure ukrainienne de la dissidence soviétique : « J’attends des Russes une résistance organisée contre la guerre en Ukraine »
text: Rector of the Ukrainian Catholic University in Lviv, Myroslav Marynovytch, 74 years old, is one of the last living Ukrainian Soviet dissidents. His commitment to defending human rights led to a seven-year sentence in the gulag in 1977. He served this sentence in the infamous Perm-36 camp, 1,500 kilometers from Moscow in the Ural Mountains, followed by five years of exile in Kazakhstan. Today, he is one of the most respected intellectual figures in Ukraine. Like any country facing an existential threat, Ukraine, since the Russian invasion, needs unity. However, this war is prolonged. Do you think it is desirable and possible to criticize the political power in such a situation? I am a democrat, and therefore, I believe that citizens of any country should have the right to criticize. But, at the same time, while observing what has happened in Ukraine, I can only be pleased that the intensity of political controversies is decreasing. This is not due to political persecution or censorship. Our nation has shown a reflex of unity, which reduces political debate. Nevertheless, freedom of speech remains, as does press freedom. There are television channels in the hands of the opposition where relatively critical views of our president [Volodymyr Zelensky] can be heard. I am not worried about freedom of expression in Ukraine. Do you think it is necessary to hold the presidential election as planned in 2024? This topic surprises me a lot. How can one consider an election in times of war? When so many of our fellow citizens are abroad and so many others are under Russian occupation! An American friend argued that it is crucial for Westerners to hold such an election. But I want a fair and honest election. I do not want my country to appear as an autocracy. Is there a public debate on this issue in Ukraine today? Absolutely, the debate exists, on social media as well as in the media. The Ukrainian Parliament is currently considering a law to ban the Ukrainian Orthodox clergy affiliated with Moscow. Is this a wise decision? Are all priests of this Orthodox Church agents of enemy influence? Undoubtedly, some of the clergy are sincere in their faith. There was a statement from the clergy of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate [UOC-MP] at the end of 2022 to address the issue of their independence. This includes the right not to refer to the Patriarch of Russia, Kirill, during prayers. This move did not sit well with the higher clergy, who began to dismiss the one hundred and fifty signatories of the statement. At the same time, Ukrainian society itself has repeatedly demanded an end to this affiliation. The Church should have cleaned up its act by excluding collaborators, stopping the flow of parishioners' money to separatists, ceasing to hide weapons in crypts, and refraining from passing information about our army to the enemy. The reasons why Ukraine is holding the UOC-MP accountable are numerous and relevant in the context of the Russian invasion. National security imperatives take precedence above all else. The UOC-MP enjoyed complete freedom for thirty years after Ukraine's independence. It abused this freedom following the Russian invasion. The Ukrainian political system has long been under Russian influence. How do you explain that Viktor Medvedchuk, a close associate of Russian President Vladimir Putin who participated in the repression of dissidents during the Soviet era, was able to become a billionaire and hold official positions until 2020? This is a question that continues to puzzle me. Over the past thirty years, Ukraine has embraced freedom imperfectly, failing to complete its transition from a state of national subjugation [to Russia] to a democracy. At the time of the declaration of independence in 1991, there was an interesting configuration because the idea was supported by democrats, the ruling elite, and miners from the Donbass region. The former Soviet elite, still in power, began to suffer from a sense of inferiority and thus took Moscow as a political model. Medvedchuk is, to me, the epitome of this elite corrupted by Russian money. He is among those who early on helped Moscow and promised that when the Russian army would occupy Ukrainian territory, they would hand over the country. This is one of the reasons why Putin was convinced he could subjugate Ukraine in less than eight days. If I were in Putin's shoes today, I would either condemn Medvedchuk to prison or confiscate his money. Ukraine's past continues to spark heated debates both inside and outside the country. Streets are being renamed in honor of controversial figures like the ultranationalist Stepan Bandera (1909-1959), accused of collaborating with the Nazis, or Simon Petliura (1879-1926), suspected of allowing anti-Jewish pogroms to take place when he was the leader of the Ukrainian People's Army from 1918 to 1920. Where do you stand in this debate? I hope that Ukrainian civil society will form its own opinion on these two figures, preferably in a peaceful time and with complete freedom. CAIMI & PICCINNI Which historical figures embody your ideals? My heroes are my fellow dissidents. Ievhen Sverstiouk [essayist, philosopher, and poet], Vassyl Stous [poet and journalist, who died in the Gulag under Gorbachev in 1985], Viatchelsav Tchornovil, and Mykhaïlo Horyn [human rights defenders]. I could mention many others. None of them ever claimed any glory. They died with the certainty of having done their duty. Are there common reference figures for the whole country today? The Ukrainian heroes of tomorrow are those who fall on the battlefield defending the homeland. They will be part of the Ukrainian pantheon. The efforts made by the authorities to remove symbols of Russian culture, such as renaming Alexander Pushkin streets, are not understood in the West... If you are referring to "cancel culture," it is a real phenomenon in the United States, but not in Ukraine. Those who attack George Washington because he owned a black slave do so in a free country, while we are at war. The difference is huge. The United States is not facing an existential threat. Ask an elderly French person how they felt hearing a Wagner opera during the German occupation. I remember my mother's stories of how she shuddered when she heard German being spoken. It's the same with "Pushkin Street." Personally, I understand why Ukrainians do not want to have Russian cultural names in front of them every day. At the end of the war, in ten or twenty years, cultural exchanges will naturally resume with Russia. It should also be noted that during the Soviet era, Ukraine was subjected to a policy of forced Russification. Many in Ukraine also reject any dialogue with the Russian opposition, which is actually against this war. Why? I continue to have remote conversations with a number of old Russian acquaintances. I know very well that not only are they opposed to this war, but they also recognize the imperialistic nature of Russia. But I must also say that in March 2022, I declined a friend's proposal to organize an evening dedicated to reading Vassyl Stous' poetry in Russian and Ukrainian. It is not the time, in the midst of war, to recite poetry together. Instead, I expect organized resistance and concrete actions against the war from my Russian contacts. During the Soviet era, there were very organized opposition movements in the socialist republics. They proved that a part of the population wanted the regime to fall. I am grateful to the Russian opposition for the sympathy they show towards Ukraine in these tragic times. But their first duty is to find a way to change the situation in Russia. You argue that the war will stop when there is a change within Russian society. What feeds this hope? My point of view is not widely shared in Ukraine. Many are inclined to think that there will never be a change in Russia. This reasoning is also based on a long history. The Russian elite considers it their duty to subdue Ukraine. Moscow believes itself to be the heir of Kievan Rus [or Kievan Russia, a political entity founded in 980 and encompassing territories across Belarus, Ukraine, and Russia]. Since the Christian baptism of Prince Vladimir of Kiev is part of Russian history, the Moscow elite believes that Kiev must, at all costs, be part of the Russian state. Russian society also thinks so. This war, today, can be a source of salvation for Russia, as the surrender at the end of the Second World War was for Germany in 1945. The defeat of Russia is important in a moral sense, as it will give the Russian people the opportunity to rethink their history.
lead: In ten or twenty years, cultural relations could resume with Russia, but not before, asserts the intellectual who was sentenced to seven years in a gulag in 1977, in an interview with "Le Monde". According to him, the primary duty of Russian opponents to the war is to act to change the situation in their country.

title: Exposition « Destins de Verdun » au Mémorial de Verdun, la guerre racontée à hauteur d’hommes
text: Two million. This is the staggering number of soldiers who participated in the Battle of Verdun, the devastating fights that the French and Germans engaged in during the First World War. The exhibition "Destins de Verdun," open since May 24 at the Verdun Memorial, aims to explore the lives of some of them, often unknown, through a series of portraits. Twenty of these destinies have been selected by the two curators of the exhibition, Florence Guionneau-Joie and Nicolas Czubak. French or German, women or men, elevator operator or doctor: they form a kaleidoscope of society at that time. The building hosting the exhibition is located on the front line of the Battle of Verdun, transformed a few years after the 1914-1918 war into a memorial forest. Under the trees, the ground is still littered with bones, inactive shells, and gas masks. Here, more than 300,000 lives were lost, 400,000 men were wounded, and thousands of souls remained haunted by the memory of the "hell of Verdun." While military advances were minimal. This museum was erected to honor the memory of these men, whom the soldiers sought to keep alive after the end of the Second World War, as the memory of the "war to end all wars" began to fade. Mobilized on the front lines in 1914, the writer Maurice Genevoix became the voice of these veterans eager to pass on their story. Known for his accounts of the Great War, compiled in "Ceux de 14" (Flammarion, 1949, last reissued in 2013), he chaired the National Committee for the Memory of Verdun, which works for the creation of the Verdun Memorial. Inaugurated in 1967, the building embodies a unifying vision of the memory of Verdun, devoid of its patriotic component. The handshake between President François Mitterrand and German Chancellor Helmut Kohl on September 22, 1984, in front of the Douaumont ossuary, became the symbol of this Franco-German reconciliation. Pedagogical and immersive setup Thirty-two years later, François Hollande and Angela Merkel continued this gesture by commemorating together the centenary of the battle on May 29, 2016. On this occasion, the memorial had been renovated: the building had been renovated and expanded after three years of work. The establishment also established a scientific committee, led by historian Antoine Prost, who modernized the museum and allowed the telling of the Battle of Verdun on site through a pedagogical and sometimes immersive setup. The two permanent spaces, inaugurated in 2016, now allow us to delve into the lives of those who experienced it. In a maze of objects and archives, we discover the evocative power of the "hell of Verdun." Period photos and reconstruction films, where former soldiers often play their own roles, are shown on a large screen placed at the center of the tour. A fake muddy ground, cannons, and vintage trucks plunge into the somber landscape of the trenches. Every week, French fighters cross paths with those who come to replace them on the "sacred way," a road that connects the rear to the front. And discover the horror of war on the haggard faces of the survivors. The emotions experienced by the fighters are then conveyed through multiple testimonies. A text by Romain Darchy, a soldier stuck for several hours in the mud, describes a fear shared by many fighters: the fear of dying anonymously. Gas masks embody the horror of the battlefield, where it is so difficult to breathe. A four-leaf clover and a soldier's amulet express the hope of surviving. The portraits of pilot Eugène Bullard and boxer Eugène Criqui, exhibited at the Verdun Memorial in 2021. VERDUN MEMORIAL/BATTLEFIELD The exhibition "Destinies of Verdun" is located one floor above. Striking faces are revealed in a life-size photographic frieze. Their lives are unfolded in the form of audio narratives, available on mediation terminals. French actors have lent their voices to present these unique journeys, where heroism often encounters misfortune. For example, the story of Eugène Criqui, a Parisian boxer and war-wounded soldier, is narrated by Mathieu Amalric. On the front lines, a German bullet severs his tongue and shatters his jaw. Despite these severe injuries, he returns to boxing. An iron device welds his jaw back together, forcing him to transform his playing style. He finds his way back to victory and becomes the world featherweight champion in 1923. Increase in attendance The journey continues, outside the walls, on the battlefield. Other destinies are presented around the forts of Douaumont and Vaux, connected to the memorial. Omar Sy, the exhibition's sponsor, tells the story of Moussa Dansako, a Senegalese tirailleur who took part in the offensive at Fort Douaumont. On the night of October 25-26, 1916, he saves four wounded brothers-in-arms. His forgotten destiny reminds us of the significant mobilization of colonial troops from Africa. Nicolas Barret, who has been at the helm of the memorial since 2021, is pleased that the site's attendance is experiencing a new surge since the Covid-19 crisis. "The establishment recorded 300,000 entries in 2023, a 30% increase compared to 2022," he explains. According to him, international events, which have brought back the specter of Verdun, may have played a role in this increase in attendance. During a visit to Paris on March 23, 2022, Volodymyr Zelensky referenced it in his speech to the French Parliament: "The damage in Mariupol and other Ukrainian cities recalls the ruins of Verdun." Memorial tourism also attracts many curious visitors interested in the history of their fighting ancestors. The last part of the exhibition is dedicated to this family aspect of memory. Three testimonies, sometimes moving, are heard from descendants of soldiers who followed in their ancestors' footsteps. On Tuesday, October 31, one of these voices is unexpectedly encountered. Peter Sauter, 71 years old, is the son of a German soldier wounded at Verdun. For thirty years, he has been trying to reconstruct his father's story. On this rainy day, he has just combed through the forest of Verdun in the company of his grandchildren, nephews, and nieces, who are involved in his quest. To encourage this process of transmission, of which Peter Sauter is the embodiment, the exhibition closes with a call for testimonies: "You too may have a Verdun destiny in your family." The trenches of Verdun's memory have not finished being dug.
lead: The exhibition traces the journey of twenty men and women whose lives were transformed by World War I. Many guided tours are organized at the memorial sites this Saturday, November 11th.

title: Les Européens tiraillés entre les fronts en Ukraine et au Proche-Orient
text: Since the terrorist attacks by Hamas on October 7, Europeans have been visiting Israel one after the other. We have seen the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, the French President, Emmanuel Macron, the Dutch Prime Minister, Mark Rutte, and also his Italian counterpart, Giorgia Meloni. "It's the new Kiev," notes a European diplomat, recalling the parade of the same leaders in Ukraine a few months ago. As the Israeli-Palestinian conflict dominates the headlines, the Twenty-Seven fear sending the message that the war in Ukraine is no longer their priority. On Thursday, October 26, European heads of state and government, meeting in Brussels, wanted to reaffirm their support for Kiev. "Europe stands with Ukraine," declared Olaf Scholz. However, two other leaders expressed a different stance. The new Slovak Prime Minister, Robert Fico, announced that, unlike his predecessor, he would no longer provide military aid to Ukraine and would not vote for new European sanctions against Russia until he had assessed their impact on his country. As for Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, he said he was "proud" to have shaken hands with Kremlin chief Vladimir Putin on October 17 in China. "In the face of a country suffering daily under Russian attacks, it's a real slap in the face," reacted Luxembourg's Prime Minister, Xavier Bettel. Budapest also does not support the European Commission's proposal to unlock 50 billion euros in aid to the Ukrainian government between 2024 and 2027. "There are warning signs that could worry Zelensky." This is not the first time Viktor Orban has distanced himself from his partners on the Ukrainian issue, but so far, he has never followed through on his threats. Accustomed to blackmail on the community stage, Hungary hopes to bend the Commission, which, in the name of the rule of law, has frozen over 13 billion euros in funds destined for it. "Orban has criticized Ukraine and supported Ukraine," nuanced Estonian Prime Minister Kaja Kallas, "we hope he will continue to do so." As for what Robert Fico will do, who during his election campaign claimed closeness to Moscow, Europeans are waiting to see: "He needs European funds too much to go all the way," judges a diplomat. "As long as he says what he shouldn't say and does what he should do, I think everything is fine," concluded Kaja Kallas. "Now is not the time to give Putin the opportunity to say that fatigue has set in and that we are looking the other way," explained Roberta Metsola, President of the European Parliament. The Twenty-Seven know: the Kremlin is just waiting for that. Fundamentally, the war in Ukraine "still threatens our direct interests," repeats a European diplomat. "We have more concrete means to act in Ukraine than in the Middle East." "We can tell ourselves whatever we want, it is not the European Union that will manage the crisis in the Middle East, it is the regional powers," one of his colleagues continues. Except for Bratislava and Budapest, no one within the EU is considering withdrawing their support for Kiev. But "we have less political energy to focus on issues concerning Ukraine," admits a diplomat. The hesitations of the United States, where Congress has refused new funding to Kiev, do not help. "There are warning signs that could worry [Ukrainian President, Volodymyr] Zelensky," acknowledges another source. "Talking about it strengthens our unity." The Twenty-Seven knew that the issues concerning Kiev - financial and military aid, twelfth package of sanctions against Moscow, seizure of Russian central bank assets in Europe, enlargement - were not ripe for a decision on Thursday, but they took the time to discuss them, so as not to remove Ukraine from their agenda. "There are ongoing debates regarding Ukraine that require a new impetus. Talking about it also strengthens our unity," confides a European diplomat. The 50 billion euros in financial aid that Europeans are considering safeguarding are not unanimously supported. Budapest opposes it, and a handful of Southern countries, including Italy and Greece, condition their support on the release of fresh funds for migration flows or natural disasters. Berlin or Amsterdam respond that they do not want an increase in the common budget, aside from the envelope for Kiev. The military aid, which the Twenty-Seven also wanted to make more predictable by committing to pay 20 billion euros over four years, is also not guaranteed. At the beginning of the war, Europeans had funded a program to accelerate arms transfers to Ukraine. Now, Berlin prefers to focus on bilateral aid to Kiev, while Paris advocates for joint purchases of European equipment. "Any long-term support program for Ukraine clearly indicates to Russia that its aggression is futile," emphasized Volodymyr Zelensky, addressing his European counterparts via video. A "second front" for the "free world." While on Thursday, the Twenty-Seven discussed Ukraine, they devoted most of their energy to drafting a joint statement on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. Between, on one side, Germany, Austria, and Hungary, who support the Israeli side, and on the other, Ireland, Portugal, or Spain, who are more sensitive to the Palestinian cause, it was difficult for them to find common ground. After debating whether to call for a "ceasefire" - ruled out by Berlin, which asserts Israel's right to defend itself - or for "a humanitarian window," the Twenty-Seven finally called for the establishment of "corridors" and "humanitarian pauses." They reaffirm "Israel's right to defend itself in accordance with international law" and reiterate their call to Hamas to release the hostages. Finally, they wish to "support the principle of an international conference for peace, which should be held soon," added Charles Michel, the President of the Council. Aware that the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is changing the situation, Volodymyr Zelensky did not confront the Europeans. "The enemies of freedom have every interest in bringing the free world to [this] second front," he declared. "The sooner security prevails in the Middle East, the sooner we will restore security here in Europe."
lead: With the exception of Slovakia and Hungary, the Twenty-Seven, gathered on Thursday in Council, reiterated their support for Kiev while dedicating most of their time to drafting a joint statement on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

title: La course contre la montre pour rapatrier Bogdan Ermokhin, orphelin de Marioupol déporté en Russie
text:
A race against time is underway to repatriate Bogdan Ermokhin to Ukraine. The teenager, an orphan deported from Mariupol (Donbass) occupied by Russia, will turn 18 in a few days, on November 19. He has already received a summons from the Russian military recruitment office, which has scheduled a meeting for him on December 19. His cousin, Valeria Poparcha, a resident of Odessa and now his legal guardian, and his Ukrainian lawyer, Kateryna Bobrovskaya, fear losing contact with him during his military service and that he may disappear within the Russian army, or even be forcibly mobilized to fight on the Ukrainian front. Bogdan Ermokhin is not a stranger. He is part of a group of children known as "the 31 of Mariupol," who were transferred from an orphanage in the city captured by the Russian army in May 2022 to the Polyani center in the Moscow region. These 31 Ukrainian orphans were showcased on television for propaganda purposes by Moscow's children's rights commissioner, Maria Lvova-Belova, who herself adopted one of them, Filip Golovnya. Bogdan Ermokhin was then placed in a Russian foster family. Moscow claims to have "welcomed" 744,000 Ukrainian children, accompanied or not, on Russian soil. Kiev, on the other hand, claims to have identified 19,546 children as cases of "deportation" and "forced population transfer," in violation of the Geneva Conventions. This Russian policy of deporting and Russifying Ukrainian children, under the guise of humanitarian evacuations, led Russian President Vladimir Putin and Maria Lvova-Belova to be targeted on March 17 by an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court in The Hague, tasked with judging individuals suspected of "genocide," "crimes against humanity," and "war crimes." To date, only 386 deported children in Russia have been repatriated to Ukraine, by their families or through humanitarian organizations. The case of Bogdan Ermokhin is sensitive. While Filip Golovnya's case became famous because he was adopted by the architect of this policy of transferring Ukrainian children to Russia, Bogdan Ermokhin's case is emblematic from another perspective. He has not only expressed his desire to return to Ukraine, where he has a legal guardian and proper documentation, but the principle of his return was agreed upon on October 24 during a video call organized by his lawyer, by both the Russian Human Rights Ombudsman, Tatiana Moskalkova, and his Russian foster mother, Irina Rudnitskaya. However, since that meeting, nothing has happened, communication with him has become nearly impossible, and his legal age is approaching. "Tatiana Moskalkova promised that he would return to Ukraine, and I am ready to go get him in Russia if necessary," says Valeria Poparcha during a phone interview from Turkey, where she is currently staying. "But since that meeting, I haven't been able to reach Bogdan. His host family just sends me 'smile' emojis, as if everything is fine, even though he has received his military summons. He had previously told me that he was under intense psychological pressure. I believe that Russia wants to keep him there." His lawyer, Kateryna Bobrovskaya, laments that, apart from the notable exception of the ombudsmen of both countries in Bogdan's case, the only communication channel remains between the families, without any help from international organizations such as the UN or the International Red Cross.

The case of Bogdan Ermokhin is sensitive. Since being taken to Russia, the teenager has attempted to escape the country at least once before being arrested at the Belarusian border. He has posted messages and songs on social media praising Ukraine and expressing his pride in being Ukrainian. Shortly after, he recorded a video in which he claimed to be happy in Russia, but he later explained to his cousin and his lawyer that he had to make the video under pressure from Russian security services.

The case is also complicated by his friendship with Filip Golovnya. The two teenagers, friends since the Mariupol orphanage, have stayed in touch. Their conversations make Bogdan Ermokhin a direct witness not only of the system of deporting Ukrainian children in which he was engulfed but also of how Maria Lvova-Belova personally got involved. There is little doubt that neither the children's rights commissioner nor her protectors in the Kremlin and security services want repatriated children in Ukraine to testify in the media and perhaps one day before international justice about their involvement.

"At their majority, Ukrainian children are sent to military institutions, and their repatriation becomes impossible," denounces Kateryna Rashevska, a lawyer from the Regional Center for Human Rights NGO specializing in children's rights. Of the 386 deported children who have been able to return to Ukraine, none were of legal age, she points out. Rashevska believes that the deportation of Ukrainian children was "planned" by Russia. She despairs at the silence of the international community. "We need a resolution from the UN General Assembly, we need mediating countries, we need international organizations to get involved, otherwise it will be too late! These children will be Russian, their brains will have been washed, and some will have become soldiers," she declares. For Bogdan Ermokhin, there are only a few days left to regain his freedom, before a birthday that could once again change the course of his life.
lead: The young Ukrainian will celebrate his 18th birthday on November 19th. His cousin and his lawyer are fighting to secure his return, fearing that he may be forcibly conscripted by the Russian army once he reaches the age of majority.

title: Dmitri Mouratov, journaliste russe, Prix Nobel de la paix : « Je suis agent de l’étranger numéro 665 »
text: Editor-in-chief of the now-closed Russian independent newspaper Novaïa Gazeta (closed in 2022) and co-recipient of the 2021 Nobel Peace Prize, Dmitri Mouratov, 61 years old, has chosen to continue living in Russia while part of his team has gone into exile in Latvia, in Riga, where the newspaper is published. He granted this interview to Le Monde and Le Temps in Geneva, where he was a guest of the Freedom Cartoonists Foundation, the Kofi Annan Foundation, and the Geneva Graduate Institute. He has lost a lot of weight: "I stopped drinking vodka," he explains, "and I will only start again at the end of the 'special military operation.' When it is over, you will see me as I was before, I promise." He also publishes a new magazine, Gorby, limited to 999 copies to avoid exceeding a thousand, which would require media registration. Will you return to Russia? Don't you think you would do your job better as a journalist in Europe? You may be right... or you may be wrong. It is very strange to live in one's homeland without freedom, but it is just as strange to conceive of freedom without one's homeland. The journalists who left are doing remarkable work without censorship, fulfilling their mission. Those who stayed are staying with their readers. For readers, it is important to have among them the people they have been reading for a long time. We must respect those who stayed and understand those who left. For us, it is very complicated, half of the editorial staff works in Europe, the other half in Russia. Half of our colleagues are on one planet, the other half on another. It is a difficult separation to explain. This division is a personal tragedy. We do not know if we will ever be able to reunite. For the journalists who left, the drama is not knowing when – or if – they will return to their country. For those who stayed, the drama is not knowing if they could leave and come back. Several of our departed colleagues have been labeled "undesirable elements," "foreign agents," and even "extremists." Many have left their parents, families, and friends, but returning is impossible, as they risk being arrested. You yourself were designated a "foreign agent" on September 1st. Why do you think this happened now? What does this change for you? My "foreign agent" number is 665: I am enemy number 665 of the people. It's good that I'm not number 666, the cursed number! Imagine how many are before me. Only fools think that this status brings any merit. It is a real stigma, heavy to bear. Vladimir Putin claims that foreign agents are funded by foreign sources. However, in the motivation behind the decision to label me as a "foreign agent" – "gives negative assessments of Russian foreign and domestic policy" – there is not a word about foreign funding. I am expressing my opinion. Therefore, I am considered an enemy of the people simply because I am doing my job. Out of nearly 700 foreign agents, one-third are journalists. There are nearly 600 political prisoners in Russia – convicted criminally, but in reality, for political reasons. Alexei Navalny's lawyers have been arrested, and Vladimir Kara-Murza's lawyer had to leave Russia. How far can this go? I know these lawyers very well. I witnessed Kara-Murza's trial, I was a witness for Navalny's defense at his prison trial, and at his request, I testified at Ivan Safronov's trial, a journalist sentenced to over twenty years in prison. I would like to mention another trial that I often attend, that of Yevgenia Berkovich and Svetlana Petritschuk; these two directors were imprisoned because of a play that received the most prestigious theater award. The power in Russia has practically invalidated Chapter 2 of the Constitution on rights and freedoms. Article 29 of the Constitution states: "Freedom of thought and speech is guaranteed to everyone. Censorship is prohibited." However, all these people are imprisoned for their beliefs. Censorship is total. Anyone who criticizes the "special military operation" [the invasion of Ukraine] can be designated as an enemy of their country. Alexander Dugin, one of the regime's ideologues, is a genuine fascist. A fascist who openly declares himself as such, stating that Russia needs fascism, and he looks forward to the shadow of fascism reigning over Russia. He presented a report on the future of Russia by 2040. How does he envision this future? According to him, Russia needs censorship and repression. There is repression, but we are on the brink of a wave of major repression. It is no longer about rehabilitating the victims of Stalinism, but about rehabilitating Stalin himself. The answer to your question can be summed up in one word: repression. Is Russia fascist today? I will refrain from using this terminology because I have a return ticket to Moscow in my pocket, and I want to continue my work at Novaya Gazeta. You have the right to ask your question in this way, and I have the right to give you a complex answer. There is a common mistake made, that of seeing Russia as a regime led by one person, Vladimir Putin. This is not accurate. No regime can survive without the support of a significant portion of the people. So, are the 40 million retirees who support Putin fascists? Your question excludes propaganda and another factor, realpolitik. The situation in Russia is not only the result of Putin or the support of a part of the people, it also stems from the activity of the "collective West." Because the "collective West" has always traded human rights for oil. For this West, human rights were crap, something they were willing to give up for oil. That's realpolitik. Remember [George W.] Bush, who claimed to have looked into Putin's eyes and seen that he was a good person. This Western hypocrisy made Putin laugh a lot. And several Western political leaders proved him right. Some became collaborators of Putin: a German chancellor, an Austrian chancellor, a French prime minister... Putin is smart, he perfectly understood that human rights could be discussed, but it was the price of a barrel of oil that mattered. Everyone contributed to the creation of the current regime. How do you think Russian society is evolving? There is no reliable measuring instrument. The only sure criterion is fear. Sociologists tell me that when they interview people, two-thirds of them refuse to answer. This means that 70% of people are afraid. They fear denunciations, social media control, very harsh criminal trials. Are there divergent opinions in society today? Yes, a lot. According to our surveys, in the age group 18-40, over 80% of people wish for the "special military operation" to stop. But over 80% of those over 65 ask for it to continue until victory. Thus, people with not much future are sending those with a future to their deaths. On February 24, 2022, you said, "There is no future anymore." Is that still your opinion? A friend of mine told me, "I will not allow myself to be humiliated by hope." Putin and his team have already succeeded in gaining total control over Russia's past. Our history is now smooth, it has become ideal. We have managed to improve the past. The State is always right. What is the strength of the current State? In the fact that it prevails over human rights and the individual. It is the same now for the future. The Church has become the foundation of propaganda, saying, "Love for the State is more important than life for the homeland." Surely, Jesus Christ will be named an "agent of a foreign power"! For the New Testament, a person can participate in demonstrations and say, "Thou shalt not kill." But for the Russian Orthodox Church and propaganda, the New Testament is not quite correct anymore. Life is the individual, death is the State. We are being prepared for a future where death will occupy a more important place than life. The Metropolitan of Murmansk said that immortality is only acquired on the battlefield. We have a real cult of death, fueled by propaganda. Vladimir Putin knows his people perfectly. He offered them a stroke of genius: today, when a soldier dies in combat in Ukraine, the family immediately receives 12 million rubles [120,000 euros]. The average salary in Russia is around 300 euros per month. To reach the sum received for a soldier's death, one would have to work without spending for twenty-five years. Death has become an investment. A fallen soldier does more for his family than if he had stayed alive. And here, it must be acknowledged that Putin and the state pay their debt promptly. What role can war play in the presidential election scheduled for March 2024 in Russia? Putin enjoys the support of two essential categories of people. First, the defense and security forces, about 3 million people, plus their families. They are well paid, their children have access to the best universities. Putin's officers are the new Russian middle class, they will fight for themselves and their families. And I laugh until it hurts when I hear that the military will stage a coup... For what purpose? The second pillar is the elderly. In their eyes, Putin is their employer, he pays their pensions. He has given up on being the president of all Russians. The urban population and the youth do not need this "special military operation". They want to live, study, have an open world. This generation remembers the animated movie Shrek, when Lord Farquaad says, "Many of you will die for my ideas, but I am willing to make that sacrifice." Putin wants to end the story of democracy and create a new world order. He senses the weariness of Western voters. He sees the rise of right-wing parties. He explains to the Russians that if they live poorly, it is because the West has deprived them of their economies. Putin believes that the West no longer plays a dominant role: Russia and the Global South will determine the future. This idea resonates worldwide. How do you see this war coming to an end? Putin knows how to wait. Your governments change all the time, but he is always there. He sees the strengths of democracy as weaknesses. He relies on the weariness of Western populations with taxes and high gas prices. Several Western governments have started to see their aid to Ukraine as an investment and are waiting for a return on that investment. This weariness will lead citizens to demand that their leaders give up aid to Ukraine. The experts were wrong to say that Putin would not have enough missiles, that he would run out of soldiers, that Ukraine would succeed in its counteroffensive. Where are these experts now? Putin's main asset is time. And, let's be honest, he is right. There is no global anti-war movement. We, journalists, are not astrologers, we verify the facts. It is the main asset that must be protected in any war. That's why I'm not going to play the doomsayer, except to say one thing: from the very first day, I was convinced that one can destroy Ukraine, but one cannot invade it.
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", the editor-in-chief of the independent newspaper "Novaïa Gazeta" explains how Vladimir Putin wants to put an end to the history of democracy and create a new world order.

title: L’Europe face à deux guerres
text: Managing a war on its borders is already a challenge for the European Union (EU), born out of the desire to consolidate peace. Facing two wars at the same time, with the second one in the Middle East carrying a powerful potential for division among its members, proves to be a challenge that the Twenty-Seven, gathered at a summit on Thursday, October 26 in Brussels, have fully realized. For the first time in almost two years, a war other than the one Russia is waging in Ukraine was at the top of the agenda. While Israeli tanks made an initial incursion into the Gaza Strip, possibly beginning the ground invasion announced after the terrorist attack carried out by Hamas in Israel on October 7, heads of state and government debated for five hours on formulating a common position on the conflict between Israel and Hamas. The outcome of these discussions is certainly not likely to disrupt the ongoing tragic dynamics: the Twenty-Seven could only agree on calling for the establishment of "humanitarian corridors" in Gaza and "pauses for humanitarian needs." The idea of calling for "a" pause for humanitarian reasons was deemed too close to a call for a ceasefire, immediately rejected by some member states, notably Germany, which emphasizes Israel's right to self-defense. This minimal formulation, accompanied by the wish for the rapid organization of an international peace conference, at least allowed the debates to be concluded with a common position after the deep divergences revealed during the hasty visit of the President of the Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, to Israel. Hamas in Moscow In addition to the risk of division over the Middle East, European leaders must also guard against the possibility of Ukraine being relegated to a secondary concern, as Russian forces have regained the initiative on the Eastern front, facing a visibly struggling Ukrainian army. The two conflicts are not entirely independent of each other: while the Twenty-Seven listened to President Volodymyr Zelensky addressing them from Kiev on Thursday in Brussels, a Hamas delegation was being received in Moscow for the third time since the start of the war in Ukraine. An Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister, Ali Bagheri Kani, was also in the Russian capital on the same day. A reorientation of Russian policy in the Middle East is evidently underway due to this new escalation of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. President Vladimir Putin hopes to capitalize on the widespread rejection of Western positions in the Arab world and several countries in the South to overshadow his own invasion, one of an independent state that had not engaged in any provocation. On Thursday, the EU reaffirmed its support for Ukraine, without dramatizing the unglamorous game played by two Central European prime ministers, Hungarian Viktor Orban and Slovak Robert Fico. These two populist leaders have no qualms about wanting to benefit from all the advantages of EU membership, which they constantly criticize, while refusing to support Ukraine without, of course, risking a rupture with Brussels. Treating this cynicism with contempt is a good tactic. However, maintaining the course and the means for massive support to Ukraine must remain a key priority for the EU.
lead: Faced with the challenge of their divisions over the conflict in the Middle East, European leaders cannot afford to weaken their support for Ukraine.

title: Le Caucase russe en proie à de multiples incidents antisémites
text: The airport of Makhachkala, the capital of the Russian republic of Dagestan, was the scene of riots on Sunday, October 29th - a word that in Russian can be translated as "pogrom". For several hours, a crowd stormed the airport in search of either "Israelis" or "Jews". The events were filmed by dozens of phones. After breaking through the doors of the main terminal shouting "Allah Akbar", hundreds of people, some carrying Palestinian flags, stormed the airport. The arrival of a flight from Tel Aviv had been announced beforehand, inflaming already heated tensions in this republic of nearly 3 million inhabitants, mostly Muslim, where Salafist influence has greatly increased in recent years. In the videos, groups of enraged men searched every corner in search of their prey. Others checked vehicles and pedestrians around the airport, demanding to see passports and berating people with "suspicious" appearances - like the Uzbek doctor who was targeted by the angry crowd. In other images, a man points to a bus whispering, "They are there, the Jews, they are all there." Police passivity After tearing down security barriers, the protesters invaded the runways. Several planes were besieged, with passengers and crews barricaded inside. Faced with police passivity, airport employees tried to negotiate by offering the crowd to designate representatives who would be allowed to board to ensure, camera in hand, the absence of Israeli nationals. The intervention of riot police only took place later in the evening. Monday morning, authorities announced sixty arrests and the opening of a criminal investigation for public disorder. "My Jewish friends are afraid, they wonder if people will show up at their homes to 'check their passports'", testifies an activist from Makhachkala who has long been committed to peaceful coexistence between communities. These events were all the more shocking as they occurred as the culmination of a weekend marked by multiple anti-Semitic incidents throughout the Russian Caucasus. In Khassaviourt, also in Dagestan, dozens of men besieged a hotel after rumors identified it as hosting "Israeli refugees". In Nalchik, the capital of the Republic of Kabardino-Balkaria, a community center was burned, and graffiti saying "death to Jews" was written on the building. Anti-Semitic rhetoric In the neighboring region of Karachay-Cherkessia, also predominantly Muslim, protesters demanded the "expulsion of Jews" from the territory. At the same time, addresses of synagogues and rabbis across southern Russia were circulated on Telegram channels calling for riots. An interview on the Podem website with the rabbi of Derbent, a city near the border with Azerbaijan with a millennia-old Jewish history, has left a lasting impression. Ovadia Iissakov stated that he is considering the "evacuation" of the 300 to 400 families that make up his community. When reached by phone, the rabbi claimed it was a "fake" before abruptly hanging up. These incidents in Dagestan and neighboring republics are a rebuke and a threat to the federal authorities. Russian President Vladimir Putin continues to praise the "multinational" character of the Russian Federation and the good relations between communities. Opposing the West Meanwhile, the Kremlin's shift in the Middle East dossier in favor of Palestinian interests and even Hamas, whose delegation was received in Moscow on October 26, has only served to inflame tensions. This choice, primarily driven by the desire to oppose the West, has been accompanied by strong anti-Israeli propaganda on television and a rise in anti-Semitic rhetoric. This is primarily the work of ultranationalist supporters of the war in Ukraine and the most extreme propagandists of the regime, but Mr. Putin himself has been distinguishing between "good Jews" and "bad Jews" for months, with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky being singled out. In September, he referred to Anatoly Chubais, a key figure in the regime who defected, as "Moche Izrailevich," a caricatured and contemptuous way of referring to a Jew. Regarding Gaza, the Russian president compared the siege of the Palestinian territory by the Israeli army to the blockade of Leningrad, which resulted in 800,000 deaths during World War II. He also waited a week before calling Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, without condemning Hamas attacks. These recent developments are unfolding in the fertile ground of the Caucasus, where religious radicals are tolerated as long as they show loyalty to Moscow and local authorities, who turn a blind eye to honor crimes or the oppression of women. A sign of the turmoil is that no high-level official has commented on the incidents in Makhachkala on Sunday evening, and official news agencies waited until Monday morning to report on them. Television stations also ignored the events. Local officials were left on the front lines, with the mufti of Dagestan calling for calm. Authorities in Chechnya, a territory where radical Islamism is promoted by the Kadyrov clan, also played a pacifying role. The prevailing line on Monday was to attribute these various incidents over the weekend to actions "orchestrated by enemies of Russia," in the words of Dagestan's president, Sergei Melikov. These suspicions, regarding the incidents at Makhachkala airport, target a Telegram channel linked to former deputy Ilia Ponomarev, who is now fighting the Kremlin from Kiev. This channel is said to have contributed, among other channels, to inflaming tensions and organizing the protest. On Monday, Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov also echoed the rhetoric attributing these disturbances to "attempts by the West to destabilize Russian society" and announced a "major government meeting" on the subject in the evening, with the participation of Vladimir Putin.
lead: In Dagestan, the airport in Makhachkala, the capital of this Russian republic with a Muslim majority, was closed after being stormed following the announcement of the arrival of a plane from Israel.

title: Israël-Hamas : la colère du Sud global
text: Anger is rising, it comes from the South and is directed against the North. This is one of the global effects of the new war between Israel and Hamas. The violence of the Israeli army's response in Gaza to the barbarity of the terrorist operation carried out on October 7 by Hamas against Israeli civilians has triggered a powerful wave of protest, tinged with a spirit of revenge, against Western countries, accused of hypocrisy in choosing which victims to support. The accusation is not new. It mainly revolves around the principle of "double standards" reproached to Western democracies depending on whether the target of an attack is part of their camp or not. But the speed and intensity with which this protest has spread over the past ten days are indicative of a political shift, under the dual pressure of the Russian war in Ukraine and the Israel-Hamas conflict. American and European leaders were surprised in 2022 by the reluctance of major Southern countries to join them in their unconditional condemnation of the Russian army's invasion of Ukraine. The renewed outbreak of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and its exorbitant cost for civilian populations, while the Russian war in Ukraine continues to rage, have reignited the debate. Helplessness The chronology of events on the ground fueled controversy: after the massacres of October 7, several Western leaders visited Tel Aviv to show their support for the Israeli ally at a time when it was carrying out massive bombings on Gaza and its population. The deadly explosion that struck Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza - for which there is currently no evidence of an Israeli bombing - while the American president was flying to Israel, accentuated the perception of imbalance. The Arab part of his trip had to be canceled due to a lack of interlocutors, as they withdrew in the face of the outrage in Arab public opinion. While Joe Biden was on this difficult mission in Israel, Chinese President was gathering representatives of the global South in Beijing to celebrate his grand project, the "new silk roads." The star guest was Russian President Vladimir Putin, a pariah in the West but who, in Beijing, received special honors and a three-hour bilateral meeting. Neither China nor Russia condemned the Hamas attack on October 7; they reserved their criticism for Israel and the United States. Western governments are now paying the price for their inability to find, or even seek, a solution to the Palestinian issue. They are also paying for their inability to adapt international governance, which they dominated, to the new realities of global balances. In the current climate of tension, their support for Israel, perceived as exclusive in the rest of the world, risks jeopardizing their efforts to convince Southern countries that international security is at stake in Ukraine. None of the countries that challenge "American hegemony," including China, have proposed an alternative plan to resolve the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. If Western leaders want to maintain some room for maneuver, they will have to, on one hand, more vigorously and visibly accompany their support for Israel with the demand for respect for humanitarian law, and on the other hand, ensure the involvement of leaders from Southern countries in mediation efforts and the search for solutions in the Middle East.
lead: The speed and intensity with which the protest against Western democracies has spread since the new escalation of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict are indicative of a political shift, under the dual pressure of the Russian war in Ukraine and the Israel-Hamas conflict.

title: Un juge d’instruction parisien reprend l’enquête sur l’oligarque russe Souleïman Kerimov
text: The resounding Kerimov case was left in the midst of negotiations in the summer of 2022 between the courts of appeal in Aix-en-Provence and Paris, awaiting to know who would inherit this emblematic case of the French justice system's inability to apprehend certain Russian assets. According to information from Le Monde, a decision was made this summer to transfer the case to a specialized magistrate at the Paris judicial court. "A judge in Nice relinquished the case to a judge from the national jurisdiction for combating organized crime," the Paris prosecutor's office stated. The procedure concerns the offense of organized money laundering from the direct or indirect proceeds of a crime, including corruption, embezzlement of public funds, and aggravated tax fraud. This latest development, far from being an epilogue, is part of a legal saga that began in 2014 when the Nice judiciary started investigating strange real estate transactions in Cap d'Antibes. During wiretaps in an unrelated case, investigators discovered that the purchase of a villa named "Hier" allegedly involved the payment of tens of millions of euros in under-the-table money by the presumed buyer, Souleïman Kerimov, a Russian oligarch close to President Vladimir Putin. While the official price of the villa was 35 million euros, the sellers actually pocketed an additional 92 million euros undeclared. On the sellers' side, the investigation led to the indictment of thirteen individuals and two companies. Their referral to the criminal court is currently under appeal. On the buyers' side, the investigation quickly expanded to three other villas suspected of also belonging to Souleïman Kerimov. The judicial police are particularly interested in the role played by the houses' steward, Cédric F., and their official owner, Alexander Studhalter, suspected of acting as a front man. However, due to the investigating judge's misinterpretation of the facts, the money laundering charges against the three men were overturned by the Aix-en-Provence Court of Appeal in June 2018. The investigation was revived with the war in Ukraine. After re-indicting Mr. Kerimov's entourage in September 2019 for money laundering of tax fraud allegedly committed by the oligarch, the investigating judge in Nice decided to split the sprawling case in December 2019 to extract the "Kerimov" component into a separate procedure. The goal is to investigate the origin of the money used to purchase and maintain the villas, and to understand who the true owner is. The investigation uncovered a dual financing network based on offshore companies and the use of large amounts of cash to operate the villas, suspected to come from tax fraud in Russia. More quickly, this new procedure also quickly falls through, unable to demonstrate the existence of the original offense. The investigation is finally closed in October 2020, with no investigative actions taken, apart from the investigating judge's decision in July 2020 to reverse the indictment of Suleiman Kerimov's entourage. A general dismissal seems inevitable, but the invasion of Ukraine by Russia changes the situation. It becomes difficult for judicial authorities to close the investigation against the oligarch as he appears alongside Vladimir Putin in the hours following the start of the Russian offensive and is listed on the European sanctions list. Especially since Mr. Kerimov finds himself in the media spotlight. The investigative unit of Radio France reveals in March 2022 that the declared beneficiary of the villas targeted in the investigation is now Gulnara Kerimova, the oligarch's daughter, while he has always denied being the owner of the properties. When questioned by journalists about this strange coincidence, Alexander Studhalter simply explains that the villas were sold to the "highest bidder" at the end of 2019 after "receiving several offers." He mentions being accompanied by the audit firm EY, which ensured the regularity of the sale price. This discovery piques the interest of Tracfin, the French financial intelligence unit, which initiates verifications to understand how Ms. Kerimova came to head the companies owning the villas. The financial transactions are deemed suspicious enough to warrant a report to the public prosecutor's office in Nice in June 2022. Tracfin details several transactions that allowed Gulnara Kerimova to replace Alexander Studhalter at the helm of the Swiss holding company Swiru at the end of 2019, and then to repurchase the villas for herself to house them in a brand new Swiss company, Sunset Properties AG, in 2021. According to a source close to the investigation, these operations followed negotiations between the Kerimov family and the tax authorities to clarify the ownership chain of the properties and designate a true beneficiary. A dossier seen as a "legal burden" Tracfin nevertheless estimates that the 268 million euros used to repurchase the villas reached Gulnara Kerimova "under particularly opaque conditions." "The financial operation was made possible by significant money transfers made, under the guise of donations in Russia, by Mr. Said Kerimov, brother of Ms. Gulnara Kerimova," notes the intelligence service, concluding that "the financing circuit (...) is particularly opaque and devoid of apparent economic rationality." These elements could constitute a money laundering offense through concealing its origin. This report triggers a dual reaction. Initially addressed to the public prosecutor's office in Nice in June 2022, it caught the attention of the financial section of the national jurisdiction for combating organized crime (Junalco) at the public prosecutor's office in Paris, which invoked its right of referral for cases of "very high complexity" to open a preliminary investigation into these new facts in the fall of 2022. The work of Tracfin also enables the public prosecutor's office in Nice to revive the investigations in the initial aspect of the case, which was on the verge of being dropped. The magistrates in Nice take this opportunity to request the transfer of the procedure, perceived as a "legal burden," to a financial investigating judge in Paris from Junalco. After lengthy debates between jurisdictions, the judicial hierarchy ultimately arbitrates in favor of this decision last summer. In addition to the legal proceedings in France, Souleïman Kerimov is also the subject of an investigation in the United States, where the U.S. Department of Justice announced on October 23 that it had initiated proceedings to confiscate the yacht Amadea, estimated at 300 million dollars (284 million euros). According to U.S. authorities, Mr. Kerimov is the true owner and has violated sanctions measures against him by using the U.S. financial system to maintain and improve the boat. The Russian oligarch denies being the owner.
lead: The legal procedure opened in Nice on suspicions of money laundering related to the acquisition of villas in Cap d'Antibes (Alpes-Maritimes) was heading towards dismissal. A report from Tracfin in June 2022 has revived the investigation.

title: « Face aux conflits russo-ukrainien ou Israël-Hamas, le constat est le même : il n’y a pas de “communauté internationale” susceptible d’imposer un règlement »
text: The wars of the time are a reflection of a fragmented, divided, antagonistic international scene - and therefore largely powerless. Whether it is the Russo-Ukrainian conflict or the one between Israel and the Palestinian Hamas movement, the observation is the same: there is no "international community" capable of promoting or imposing a settlement. The massacres committed on October 7 in southern Israel should have been unanimously condemned, from south to north, from east to west. One could have expected a united front in condemnation from major powers, all of which maintain close relations with the State of Israel. At least if one accepts that the event is a trauma comparable to that caused by the attacks of September 11, 2001 in the United States. This latter interpretation was that of Western countries that condemned the Hamas operation. Elsewhere - in Asia, Africa, Latin America - the interpretation has often been different. In an atmosphere of absolute relativism, October 7th was relegated to the realm of terrorism that the Middle East is accustomed to: as everyone knows, the terrorists of some are the resistance fighters of others... Then came the Israeli response, against a backdrop of water, electricity, fuel embargo, and no way out for the Gazans. The ferocity of the bombings and the thousands of civilian casualties quickly overshadowed or downplayed the specificity of the events of October 7. But even before the Israeli intervention, China under Xi Jinping and Russia under Vladimir Putin did not explicitly denounce the Hamas attack. These two countries have very good relations with the State of Israel, but also with the entire Arab world and with Iran. "Unparalleled level of hatred" In many ways, the Chinese and Russians were well positioned to exercise a form of mediation. Their choice was different: they showed solidarity with the Palestinians and criticized the Israelis, finally denouncing US policy in the region. It was impossible to agree on a common starting point, in this case, a condemnation. The mere threat of a veto from one or the other paralyzed the UN Security Council. Why? Signatories of a declaration of "unlimited friendship," concluded on February 4, 2022 in Beijing, a few weeks before the Russian assault on Kiev, the Chinese and Russians maintain a common politico-ideological purpose: to denounce American (or Western) "hegemony" over the international system inherited from 1945. The ambition is to weaken the United States and to steer the system in a direction more favorable to the interests of Moscow and Beijing. Washington is on the defensive. By unconditionally supporting the war waged by the government of Benjamin Netanyahu in Gaza, the United States is increasingly isolated. European leaders are calling for a humanitarian pause in the fighting - a way to distinguish themselves from Washington. In Africa, Asia, and Latin America, as well as in a good part of Western opinions, the solidarity expressed after a month of bombings in Gaza goes to the Palestinians. In the Arab world, "even among the most independent minds, there is an unprecedented level of hatred" towards Israel, according to an expert from the Gulf region. China and Russia are taking advantage of this situation, positioning themselves as advocates for an anti-American global order. The goal is to rally a significant portion of the informal and vague collective known as the Global South - Africa, Asia, Latin America - against the United States. Russians and Chinese are actively working towards this. The New York Times of November 3 notes that Moscow, Tehran, and to a lesser extent, Beijing, are flooding official media and online platforms with "a deluge of propaganda in favor of Hamas and against Israel's main ally, the United States." "This is followed by hundreds of millions of people around the world," says an expert quoted by the newspaper. The Islamic Republic of Iran is a significant ally that Putin and Xi cultivate closely. This regime, which readily oppresses women and arms Hamas, is not considered a pariah, thanks to the support of Moscow and Beijing. Iran has been welcomed into the BRICS, the group of emerging economies from the South, and was chosen to preside over the annual forum of the UN Human Rights Council on November 2 and 3 (discrediting the United Nations). Tehran sells weapons to Russia for Putin's war in Ukraine and offers discounted oil to China. The limits of bloc logic In private, Joe Biden tried to moderate the Israeli offensive in Gaza. However, his apparent lack of success confirms another aspect of the international scene: the diminishing power of superpowers, as demonstrated by Israel's autonomy in its relationship with its American protector. Moscow and Beijing are gaining ground at the expense of the United States or Western countries in the so-called "Global South." Parts of Africa and the Arab world, as well as India and Brazil, have been hesitant to condemn Russia in the Ukrainian affair. Nevertheless, it would be incorrect to speak of a China-Russia-emerging countries bloc, whether in the war in Ukraine or the Middle East. India, a founding member of BRICS, supports Israel and opposes Chinese expansionism in Asia. Turkey, under Recep Tayyip Erdogan, is reluctant to leave NATO but supports Hamas in Gaza. Qatar, a financier of Palestinian Islamists, hosts one of the largest American military bases in the Middle East. The logic of blocs has its limits. A silver lining: while seeking to weaken the United States everywhere, the Chinese and Russians do not want the tragedy in Gaza to escalate into a regional conflict.
lead: The United States being on the defensive, China and Russia positioning themselves as advocates of an anti-American world order, the UN Security Council finds itself paralyzed, notes Alain Frachon, editorialist at "Le Monde".

title: Jake Sullivan, conseiller à la sécurité nationale de Joe Biden : « Protéger les civils à Gaza est une nécessité morale et stratégique »
text: Jake Sullivan was unable to enjoy the festive atmosphere at the White House on Monday, October 30, for Halloween. The national security advisor to President Biden has been absorbed by the Israeli-Palestinian conflict since the Hamas attack on October 7. In an interview with Le Monde, just before meeting with Prince Khaled Ben Salman Al Saud, the Minister of Defense of Saudi Arabia, 46-year-old Jake Sullivan outlined the American strategy and vision in this crisis. The White House has shown impressive empathy and support for Israel after the Hamas attack. Since then, you have had to repeatedly readjust your position, first by focusing on humanitarian aid for Gaza, then by discussing the laws of war. How do you explain American isolation in the Middle East? In his first major speech just days after the crisis began, President Biden mentioned the laws of war and the fact that Hamas does not represent the Palestinian people. So I refute the idea that we only introduced this later. The president then unequivocally stated that Israel has the right and even the duty to defend itself against terrorist attacks, that Hamas presents exceptional challenges by hiding among the population, using civilians as human shields. This represents an additional burden for the Israeli army. But it does not diminish its responsibility in distinguishing between terrorists and innocent civilians. You speak of isolation, but the United States gathered enough voices to pass a resolution at the Security Council condemning Hamas, which addresses these humanitarian issues. The only reason it did not pass is the veto of Russia and China. But in reality, we have obtained the support of a significant number of countries around the world, in Latin America, Asia, and Europe. So we will continue to work in New York. We will continue to work with Arab states. On Sunday, the president spoke with the Egyptian president, Al-Sissi. He will speak with other Arab leaders this week. We will continue to uphold our principles and offer our best advice to Israel. Did you give Israel full approval for its military operations in Gaza? I think that is a mistaken way of presenting it. Israel is a sovereign nation. They do not come to us to ask if we have given the green light or not, if the fire is green or red. We ask them difficult questions. We advise them as friends. We tell them that we have been in a similar situation, although I do not want to make a full comparison, after September 11, 2001. We were determined to pursue the terrorists, and nothing was going to stop us. But we also made mistakes and learned from them. What are they? First, there was an exaggerated military engagement in the broader Middle East following 9/11, and the way we conducted ourselves in these conflicts. If we could turn back time, we would do things differently. President Biden has spoken eloquently about this regarding Afghanistan for over ten years. But I will add this. When the president speaks with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, when I talk to my counterparts, when Secretary of State Antony Blinken, Secretary of Defense Lloyd Austin, and General Charles Brown [Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff] speak with their counterparts, we convey our deep conviction: protecting civilians and facilitating the delivery of vital aid to them is both a moral and strategic necessity. This is a central point in the public and private statements we make, and it will remain so. Whenever U.S. officials are asked about the disproportionate use of force by the Israeli military in Gaza, the response is: "We do not discuss specific strikes." But isn't this a matter of principle, in light of the past three weeks and past wars in Gaza? In reality, I have repeatedly mentioned, as has the president, the principle of protecting civilians and the sacredness of every innocent civilian life: Palestinian, Israeli, or otherwise. And we will continue to invoke this principle. What we do not want is to react to specific reports, to specific qualifications. We will not be drawn into a role of judge and jury in the midst of all this. Is it possible to destroy Hamas? It is estimated to consist of 20,000 to 25,000 armed men, and each new cycle of confrontation brings forth a new generation... From my perspective, it is possible to ensure a future where Gaza cannot be used as a terrorist base to threaten Israel or anyone else. When the president rules out returning to October 6 [the situation before the attack], he means two things. First, Hamas must not be able to preserve the capabilities to continue to pose a strategic threat to Israel. Second, there must be a political horizon for the Palestinian people, anchored in the two-state solution. This is so that the future in Gaza and the West Bank is one of peace, not violence. I believe this outcome is possible. Of course, it is difficult to achieve. It has eluded everyone for decades, but I think it is attainable. And I believe that the events of the past month only reinforce the president's conviction that we must work towards this. This requires working with Israelis and Palestinians, but also in a broader vision of regional integration with key Arab countries. But have you told the Israelis that with an organization like Hamas, when you cut off one head, another one grows back? Our own experience indicates that dealing with terrorist groups requires a multifaceted approach. There is a military aspect, but not only that. There is an effort in terms of financing, political, social, and economic conditions. An effort to seek diplomatic and political responses to these conditions, in order to forge a more sustainable solution over time. We have been clear with the Israelis. As we have stated publicly, we believe that ultimately the solution does not lie solely in military action, even though military action against terrorists is a part of the solution. It also lies in the political horizon that we have described. Obviously, not everyone in the Israeli government shares this perspective. Many do not support the two-state solution. President Biden firmly and unequivocally supports it. He has made this clear in his response to this crisis. Several Israeli officials have suggested that Gaza should be reduced in size after the war, with the creation of a demilitarized zone. Would this restore Israel's security? I do not want to comment on specific opinions. From a general point of view, I do not believe that the territory of Gaza should be reduced. I prefer to leave it to others to consider these questions, the operational aspects, how to ensure that attacks cannot be launched from Gaza towards Israel. I do not think it is sensible or acceptable to forcibly relocate the inhabitants of Gaza. And as the president has said, a reoccupation of Gaza by Israel would make no sense. On the issue of buffer zones or others, I would defer to a consultation with a wide circle of involved parties, including the Palestinian people. When President Biden met with the President of the Palestinian Authority, Mahmoud Abbas, in July 2022, he reaffirmed his commitment to the two-state solution. But he also said, "The ground is not currently fertile for restarting negotiations." Did you make a mistake by following in Donald Trump's footsteps, by prioritizing the normalization process between Israel and Arab countries before any attempt to resolve the conflict? Do you think the ground was fertile? If we assume that the ground was not fertile for direct negotiations, the question is: how do we achieve a two-state solution, or at least preserve this possibility in the future, without it completely disappearing from our field of vision? We had an answer. It lay in seeking a normalization process between Israel and Saudi Arabia that included a substantial Palestinian component. He offered political and economic benefits to the Palestinian people. I haven't heard a better solution or approach than that. I think the effort to bring both parties together in a room to agree was not going to get us anywhere. So we believed that this alternative would actually help progress and create the possibility of significant outcomes for the Palestinians, which could ultimately preserve the two-state solution. That's how we saw things, and how we continue to see them. The Biden administration has deployed significant military forces in the Eastern Mediterranean. How do you assess the threat posed by Hezbollah? What are the differences compared to Hamas? There are obvious differences between them, both in terms of capabilities, religious affiliation (Sunni for Hamas, Shiite for Hezbollah), as well as in terms of the relationship between Hezbollah and the Lebanese government. But there are also similarities. Both receive a huge amount of funding, training, and sponsorship from Iran, and both represent a significant threat to Israel. I would describe the risk of a further escalation on Israel's northern border as very real. The president was attentive to this from the beginning when he said that no nation or group should try to take advantage of this crisis. We have moved forces, including two naval groups, to send a deterrent message and warn: don't even think about it. The United States has been clear about its intentions. Regarding Iran, we have been clear as well: if you attack American forces, we will respond. We have done so before. And if these attacks continue (in Syria and Iraq), we will respond again. The Biden administration is asking for over $105 billion from Congress as part of a security package, mainly for Ukraine and Israel. Could your foreign policy in these two major crises be hindered by a minority of Trump-supporting lawmakers?
