I still believe that ultimately, even if the path is not completely straight and short, there will be strong bipartisan support, both in the House of Representatives and the Senate, for aid to Israel and Ukraine. I cannot predict exactly how things will turn out. But we have put forward a set of proposals because we believe that all these pieces fit together and must be adopted together. When asked about the purpose of American support for Ukraine, you say, "A Ukrainian victory based on territorial integrity and sovereignty." And when asked to define this victory, you say it is up to President Zelensky to do so. Shouldn't the American administration have its own strategy and war objectives? We have a strategy. Our strategy is to maintain a coalition of 50 nations to provide Ukraine with the necessary support so that they can make as much progress as possible on the battlefield, which can then be translated into maximum leverage at the negotiating table. However, it is up to the Ukrainians to determine their destiny. They are a sovereign nation. It is not for America to do it for them. We are not here to tell the Ukrainians how their war should end, in which they are fighting and dying every day. They define that. We define ways to assist them in achieving their goals. In these two crises, it is easier to say what the United States does not want – a regional conflict in the Middle East, a war against Russia beyond Ukrainian borders – than what they do want. Is this new form of containment the essence of your foreign policy? First and foremost, I would describe it as common sense, as a way to safeguard the fundamental interests of the American people. A third world war would not be good for Americans, nor would a massive regional war in the Middle East. Do not lose sight of the fact that, in many respects, national security, defending the interests and values of a nation and its people, allies, and partners, involves defending against threats and preventing bad things from happening. That is a big part of what we are trying to do. But we are also strongly in favor of something in these two cases. In the midst of the crisis, President Biden spoke from the White House to develop a vision for a future between Israelis and Palestinians, and the region at large, rooted in something we strongly believe in, not something we oppose. As for Ukraine, the President visited Kiev in February, and I had the privilege of accompanying him. He then spoke about how Ukraine had already achieved a significant part of the vision they are fighting for. Kiev stands. A sovereign and democratic Ukraine stands, increasingly oriented towards a Euro-Atlantic direction. This is a positive vision, a vision that few would have believed possible when Russian tanks crossed the border in February 2022. Why is Joe Biden opposed to Ukraine's accession to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)? Is it a definitive no, or is it as long as the war continues? First, the United States and every other NATO ally signed the declaration in Vilnius [July 2023] stating that Ukraine's legitimate place is in the Alliance. And every year, since the Bucharest summit [2008], we have renewed the commitment that Ukraine will become a NATO member. President Biden raised two questions. First, how do we ensure that Ukraine is implementing all the necessary reforms to become a full member of NATO? And then, how do we create the necessary conditions for its entry? Because currently, with the ongoing war, if Ukraine were to join NATO tomorrow, NATO would be at war with Russia. The United States would be at war with Russia. And that is not something the president is willing to support. So, these are the two things driving his thinking: the question of Ukraine's membership and the conditions he envisions for moving that forward.
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", Jake Sullivan, the National Security Advisor of the White House, describes the American vision of the war between Israel and Hamas and refutes Washington's isolation on this conflict.

title: Quatre enfants ukrainiens rapatriés grâce à la médiation du Qatar
text: Four Ukrainian children are going to be reunited with their families in Ukraine. On Monday, October 16, in the morning, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Qatar announced that they had reached an agreement with Russia to repatriate these children who had been living in the territory of the Russian Federation since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, 2022. Lolwah Al-Khater, Minister of State for Foreign Affairs, expressed gratitude to Russia and Ukraine for their commitment to the successful reunification of the Ukrainian children with their families. She stated that Qatar will continue its mediation efforts in this and other contexts, in line with its principles of conflict resolution and peace restoration. The return of these "three boys aged 6, 3, and 9, and a 17-year-old girl," according to Daria Herasymchuk, advisor on children's rights to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, marks the first success of Doha since it engaged as a mediator between the two enemies in this particularly sensitive issue. The first public signs of Qatar's involvement in the deported children issue date back to the summer of 2023. In June, Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed Ben Abderrahmane Al Thani visited Moscow and met with Russian President Vladimir Putin. A month later, he traveled to Kyiv to meet with President Volodymyr Zelensky and promised the delivery of a humanitarian aid package worth $100 million. Following this visit, Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Chmyhal thanked Qatar "for its willingness to undertake mediation efforts for the return of Ukrainian children illegally abducted by Russia and still held on its territory." The return of these four children who were in Russia or in occupied territories at the beginning of the invasion represents a diplomatic success for Qatar. Due to the complexity of these returns, only 386 children have been repatriated to Ukraine, either through Ukrainian non-governmental organizations or because families took the risk of going directly to Russia, according to the Children of War portal. The authorities in Kyiv have documented 19,546 children who were illegally deported to the Russian Federation or occupied territories. While this practice has existed since the start of the Donbass war in 2014, it has become more widespread since the beginning of the February 2022 invasion. Various methods have been documented by human rights organizations, including Russian soldiers deporting children from areas under Russian control. Ukrainian families living in these same territories were forced to entrust their children to schools or summer camps for several weeks, only to never see them again. These children ended up in orphanages, Russian foster families, or other institutions scattered across regions of the Russian Federation. Moscow has made the assimilation of Ukrainian children a true policy. As early as May 2022, Vladimir Putin signed a decree to facilitate the adoption of children arriving from the war by Russian families. In November 2022, in a report on these transfers, Amnesty International condemned this law which allows for "facilitating the absorption of children (...) into Russian society in a way that fundamentally denies their rights to choose and preserve their nationality." Adoption by Russian families further complicates the efforts of Ukrainian families and authorities to locate the children. According to Ukrainian human rights organizations, in addition to receiving a Russian passport, the children's name, date, and place of birth are deliberately changed to erase their traces. Furthermore, Kyiv accuses Russia of pursuing a policy of "Russification" of the children, aiming to erase their identity, even going so far as to "re-educate" them to make them hate their country of origin. In March, the Russian president and Moscow's children's rights commissioner, Maria Lvova-Belova, who proudly adopted a child from Mariupol, became the first actors in the war to be targeted by an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court in The Hague.
lead: The emirate has managed to reach an agreement with Russia, where nearly 20,000 children are said to have been deported illegally since the beginning of the invasion.

title: L’oligarque russe Alexeï Kouzmitchev mis en examen en France pour blanchiment de fraude fiscale
text: Troubles continue for Alexei Kouzmitchev. After two days in police custody, the Russian oligarch was brought before an investigating judge at the financial division of the Paris judicial court on Wednesday, November 1st, and indicted on charges of "aggravated money laundering, presumption of money laundering, and organized concealed labor" according to the National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF). Despite the prosecution's request for detention, he left the court freely under judicial supervision, with the obligation to pay a bail of 8 million euros. He is also prohibited from leaving France. Police conducted searches on Monday morning at his villa in Saint-Tropez (Var) and his mansion in Paris. These operations involved forty elite police officers from the Raid and BRI units, tasked with securing the locations. According to our sources, the PNF investigation into money laundering related to tax fraud began in February 2023. "The suspected fraud is linked to the ownership of real estate in France and undeclared income," said a judicial source to Le Monde. This lead was prompted by a report from Tracfin, the financial intelligence unit of the French Ministry of Finance, during the summer of 2022. The Kremlin's support The justice system is also investigating whether Mr. Kouzmitchev violated the European sanctions imposed on him on March 15, 2022, in response to the Russian invasion of Ukraine. While the mere presence of the businessman in France could constitute a serious offense under customs law, "the potential characterization of these offenses requires additional investigative actions," said the judicial source. The businessman's entourage denies any wrongdoing and explains that he was already in French territory when the sanctions were imposed. The case, entrusted to officers from the National Brigade for the Repression of Tax Offenses (BNRDF), is now proceeding as a judicial investigation under the supervision of an investigating magistrate. Alexei Kouzmitchev, a co-shareholder of the Alfa Group consortium, which owns Alfa Bank, is considered by European authorities as "one of the most influential people in Russia," with "well-established ties to the President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin." The Kremlin stated on November 1st that it was ready to "protect the rights" of the Russian businessman following his arrest. "We must be informed of the detention of a citizen of the Russian Federation through our diplomatic mission," said the spokesman for the Russian presidency, Dmitry Peskov, as reported by AFP. "Upon receiving the information, and if the detainee wishes, we will of course assist in protecting his rights as a Russian citizen." Do you have any information to share with us? Feel free to securely contact our investigations team, who is at your disposal to exchange with you if you have information or documents of public interest.
lead: The justice system is investigating the assets of this close associate of Vladimir Putin, who has remained in French territory despite being placed under European sanctions in March 2022.

title: « En Chine, les “nouvelles routes de la soie” adoptent un rythme de croisière moins spectaculaire mais peut-être plus durable »
text: What do the Tchaikovsky Moscow Conservatory of Music, the National Bank of Serbia, the road network around Dakar, the port of Chancay in Peru, a high-tech food preservation research center in South Africa, the Mongolian forest, and a future global women's chess tournament have in common? They will all soon receive support from China as part of its "Belt and Road Initiative" investment program. These are just seven examples out of a total of 369 practical cooperation agreements that China has signed with around 150 countries and 30 international organizations during the third "Belt and Road" forum, officially known as the "Belt and Road Initiative" (BRI), presided over by Chinese leader Xi Jinping in Beijing on October 17 and 18. While much attention was given to the red carpet rolled out for Russian President Vladimir Putin and numerous speeches that did not mention Ukraine or the Middle East, the essential information from these two days is that China is continuing its global investment program while reorganizing it. Xi Jinping did not express any reservations about the past ten years, but a close examination of the signed agreements shows that China has still taken some criticisms into account. While large projects are not entirely out of fashion, priority will be given to "small but beautiful" investments, as described by the Chinese president. Additionally, there are more "green projects," from solar power plants in Poland and Romania to the establishment of a research center on combating drought and desertification in Saudi Arabia. Furthermore, the fight against corruption is becoming more systematic. Promoting "an honest BRI with an evaluation of the integrity of the companies involved" is one of the eight priorities announced by Xi Jinping. Budgetary realism seems to be prevailing. It appears that after an impressive start, the "Belt and Road Initiative" is settling into a more sustainable pace. In 2013, China was both overly optimistic and proud. The goal was to establish the stature of the new emperor at the center of the world, which has been achieved. Now, realism, especially in terms of budget, is key. For the coming years, Xi Jinping has announced lines of credit totaling around 100 billion euros, a significant amount but ten times less than the commitments of the past decade (around 1 trillion euros). Should we speak of failure? Yes and no. Who can deny that China plays a more significant role on the international stage today than before? The "Belt and Road Initiative" has played a major part in this. While China is strong, it is often more feared than loved, although this is not true everywhere. "Africans have eyes only for her," notes a European diplomat who has traveled extensively in over twenty African countries. But the closer one gets geographically to China, the more intimidating it becomes. In Beijing, the most pro-Chinese speeches were given by the Argentine president, Alberto Fernandez, and by the Brazilian Dilma Rousseff, president of the New Development Bank of the BRICS. In contrast, Asian speakers showed more restraint. A sign of the times: unlike the two previous forums, China did not offer foreign leaders - who were also fewer in number than before - to sign a joint declaration. As for the Europeans, apart from the Hungarian Prime Minister, Viktor Orban, and the Serbian President, Aleksandar Vucic, they were reportedly not unhappy that the presence of the Russian president in Beijing provided them with an excellent excuse not to make the trip. If Putin did not get what he hoped for - the signing of an agreement for a new gas pipeline connecting Russia and China via Mongolia - he wholeheartedly supported the "new Silk Roads," connecting them directly to the Eurasian Economic Union that he is trying to build. Even if he has the will, Putin no longer has the means to compete with Beijing.
lead: Beijing will inject ten times less money than in its previous cooperation program, but these investments will be made in less expensive and more "green" projects, notes Frédéric Lemaître in his column.

title: Bombardements à Gaza, tempêtes Ciaran et Domingos, marathon de New York… Les cinq infos à retenir du week-end
text: Have you fallen a bit behind on the news these past two days? We summarize the main information from Saturday, November 4, and Sunday, November 5. • Israel-Hamas: refugee camp bombed, calls for a "humanitarian truce," and demonstrations Forty-five people, mostly children and women, were killed and around a hundred others were injured in an Israeli bombing on Saturday evening against the Al-Maghazi refugee camp in the center of the Gaza Strip, according to the Ministry of Health of the Gaza Strip, administered by Hamas. Earlier on Saturday, a bombing hit a UN school where displaced people are taking shelter in a refugee camp in the northern Gaza Strip, causing several casualties, according to the UN agency for Palestinian refugees, UNRWA. The Ministry of Health of the Gaza Strip stated that fifteen people were killed in this bombing, which it also attributes to the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF). The French Minister of Foreign Affairs, Catherine Colonna, called on Sunday for an "immediate, sustainable, and supported humanitarian truce[, which] is absolutely necessary and [which] must lead to a ceasefire," following a meeting in Doha with her Qatari counterpart, Mohammed Ben Abderrahmane Al Thani. She emphasized that France is working to have a resolution to this effect adopted by the UN Security Council, which has been deeply divided on the issue since the start of the conflict. US President Joe Biden reported progress on Saturday in obtaining a humanitarian pause, an option currently rejected by Israel, which dismisses this possibility without the release of hostages held by Hamas. Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas linked the return of the Palestinian Authority to Gaza after the war between Israel and Hamas, as discussed by Washington, to a "political settlement" that also includes the occupied West Bank and East Jerusalem. Several peace demonstrations took place in France and around the world over the weekend. On Sunday in Marseille, 2,700 people marched in support of the Palestinian people, calling for a ceasefire in Gaza, according to the police prefecture. On Saturday in Paris, a procession of 19,000 people, according to the police, marched to demand an end to the bombings in Gaza. About forty other gatherings were announced throughout France, with 5,000 people in Lyon and 1,500 in Strasbourg. Demonstrations also took place in Washington, London, Berlin, and Tehran. • Jews in Europe "are living in fear again," warns the European Commission. Stars of David were spray-painted on buildings in several districts of Paris. MICHEL EULER/AP Tags, assaults, protests, desecrations... The European Commission condemned on Sunday the "resurgence" of anti-Semitic acts since the outbreak of the conflict between Hamas and Israel, and expressed concern that "Jews in Europe are once again living in fear." "The increase in anti-Semitic incidents across Europe in recent days has reached exceptionally high levels, reminiscent of some of the darkest periods in history," denounced the European executive. "In these difficult times, the EU stands by its Jewish communities. We condemn these despicable acts in the strongest terms. They go against everything that Europe represents, our fundamental values, our way of life." These acts are contrary to the "European model of society based on equality, inclusion, and full respect for human rights," emphasized the European executive. The prefect of police in Paris, Laurent Nuñez, announced that there had been 257 anti-Semitic acts in the Paris metropolitan area and 90 arrests, out of the 857 recorded nationwide, since October 7, the start of the conflict between Hamas and Israel, which is "more than in a whole year." • After storm Ciaran, depression Domingos A Gujan-Mestras, in Gironde, Sunday, November 5, 2023, after the passage of depression Domingos. THIBAUD MORITZ / AFP Succession of storms in France, with a growing toll: an Enedis employee mobilized after storm Ciaran was electrocuted and died on Saturday evening in Brittany, while another depression, named Domingos, caused new damage on the Atlantic coast. This storm caused significant damage to the railway network in Nouvelle-Aquitaine and led to delays for two trains on the Paris-Orléans-Limoges-Toulouse (POLT) line, whose passengers had to spend the night at Brive station. "The signaling installations between Bordeaux and Hendaye were also severely damaged," according to SNCF. The electrocution death of a 46-year-old agent brings the death toll to at least three victims in France after the passage of Ciaran between Wednesday and Thursday, which also left forty-seven injured. Domingos, described as "less severe" but accompanied by very strong winds reaching up to 152 km/h in Gironde or 144 km/h in Charente, caused five injuries. • For Zelensky, the war with Russia is not at a "standstill" Volodymyr Zelensky, in Kiev, Ukraine, on November 4, 2023. ANATOLII STEPANOV / AFP Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky challenged on Saturday that the war between his country and Russia is at a "standstill," also denying any pressure from Western countries to start negotiations with Russia. His comments come after the Chief of Staff of the Ukrainian army, Valeri Zaloujny, stated this week that the Russian and Ukrainian armies were trapped in a war of attrition and positions. "Just like in the First World War, we have reached a technological level where we are at an impasse," he told the British weekly. "There probably won't be a magnificent and deep breakthrough," he added, dampening hopes raised by the Ukrainian counteroffensive launched in early June. "No, the conflict is not at an impasse," Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov had already responded to journalists, stating that "Russia [was] relentlessly pursuing its special military operation" and that "all set objectives [had] to be achieved." • Ethiopian Tamirat Tola wins the New York Marathon in less than 2 hours and 5 minutes; Kenyan Hellen Obiri in 2 hours and 27 minutes From left to right, Kenyan Albert Korir, second place, winner, Ethiopian Tamirat Tola, and third place, his compatriot Shura Kitata. CRAIG RUTTLE / AP Ethiopian Tamirat Tola won the New York Marathon on Sunday in 2 hours, 4 minutes, and 58 seconds, setting a new record for the American race. Starting with the best time among the participants after the withdrawals of Kenyan favorites Evans Chebet and Geoffrey Kamworor, he took the lead to run the second half of the race alone at the front. He finished ahead of Kenyan Albert Korir (by 1 minute and 59 seconds), the winner in New York in 2021, and Ethiopian Shura Kitata (by 2 minutes and 13 seconds). Tola also breaks the record of the most famous marathon in the world, which had stood since 2011. On the women's side, Kenyan Hellen Obiri won the last major marathon of the year in 2 hours, 27 minutes, and 23 seconds. The women started at a very slow pace before accelerating abruptly at the 35th kilometer, and it was Obiri who sprinted to victory ahead of Ethiopian Letesenbet Gidey (by 6 seconds) and Kenyan Sharon Lokedi (by 10 seconds), who had won in New York last year. At 33 years old, Obiri secures her second marathon victory this year, one year after her debut at the distance and seven months after her win at the Boston Marathon. Also: New earthquake in Nepal Novak Djokovic crowned for the seventh time in Paris, the fortieth time in Masters 1000, by defeating Grigor Dimitrov Russia claims to have successfully tested an intercontinental ballistic missile Police thwart the abduction of a girl by her father who had entered the tarmac of Hamburg airport Socialist Party leader Olivier Faure calls on "all political forces" to rally against anti-Semitism Death of Charles Piaget, emblematic figure of Lip Budget of Social Security: rejection of the no-confidence motion by LFI, the text adopted in the first reading
lead: If you haven't been following the news, here's what you need to know from the last forty-eight hours.

title: Electricité : entre la France et l’Allemagne, un compromis sous haute tension
text: For months, unprecedented tensions had been brewing between France and Germany over the reform of the European electricity market. For the past ten years, the two countries had been touting the virtues of their respective energy models: a strong nuclear base for Paris, and a mix of fossil and renewable energies for Berlin. This issue had become the main stumbling block in the Franco-German relationship. This was all the more unjustified considering that both nations were supposed to jointly pursue three objectives: prevent price spikes, strengthen industrial competitiveness, and decarbonize their economies. At the summit in Hamburg on October 9 and 10, Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz reopened the issue without reaching a conclusion, although the French president and the German chancellor remained optimistic about the future. The final resolution came on Tuesday, October 17, in Luxembourg, where the energy ministers of the twenty-seven member states finally reached a compromise on the electricity market reform, now subject to approval by the Members of the European Parliament. The Elysée, which had been advocating for nuclear energy to be considered in the new pricing mechanism, declared victory. Germany also welcomed the outcome, as Brussels will ensure that support for EDF and the modernization of its 56 reactors do not give France a competitive advantage contrary to European competition laws. A historic conflict that Germany had feared, given its recession and the challenges faced by its industry due to the loss of cheap Russian gas, competition from American companies subsidized by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) to green their activities, and the threat from Chinese electric vehicle manufacturers. The immediate issue was the market configuration rather than the price of electricity for consumers, whether fossil, nuclear, or renewable, according to the negotiators. Does the Luxembourg agreement put an end to the longstanding Franco-German conflict over energy models? Berlin's transition initiated in 2001 did not fully deliver on its promises of decarbonizing electricity, despite remarkable developments in wind, solar, and hydrogen energy. While France is not exemplary in overall greenhouse gas emissions, its electricity production is seven times less "polluting" than that of its neighbor, which has had to rely on coal to compensate for the definitive shutdown of nuclear power. This is a crucial advantage in light of a projected 50% increase in electricity consumption by 2050. In February, Paris achieved a partial victory when Berlin finally agreed to include nuclear energy in the European taxonomy of decarbonized energies, making it easier to access public and private funding. The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, could not help but qualify the success by stating that this energy is "important" for decarbonization but "not strategic." Germany will remain the spearhead of the anti-nuclear camp in Europe, led by its Vice-Chancellor and Minister of the Economy, the ecologist Robert Habeck. The crisis has its roots in history. Since 1945, France has chosen military and civilian nuclear power, driven by its armed forces, the Atomic Energy Commission, and EDF. They support an entire industrial sector and benefit from tacit public support. Nuclear power is in the national DNA, a unique situation in the world, a source of pride, despite the setbacks in the industry over the past decade. After years of uncertainty about its importance, Emmanuel Macron has revived it. He decided to extend the lifespan of nuclear power plants as long as they are deemed safe by the Nuclear Safety Authority, announced six EPR 2 reactors by 2035-2040, and passed a law to accelerate their construction. "The German obsession: destroying EDF" Nothing of the sort in Germany. Germany embraced civilian nuclear power in the 1970s, without enthusiasm, and it never exceeded 22% of electricity production (compared to 75% in France). For thirty years, some dreamed of an "Airbus of energy," symbolized by the EPR reactor from Framatome-Siemens. The Greens, on the other hand, fought against it under the banner "Atomkraft? Nein Danke!" ("nuclear power? no thanks!"), combining ecological and pacifist concerns. Coming to power in 1998, the SPD-Greens coalition (an alliance between the Social Democratic Party of Germany and the Greens) began to unravel Franco-German cooperation. Before Chancellor Gerhard Schröder chose Russian gas from his "friend" Vladimir Putin. A strategy continued by Angela Merkel that led her country into a perilous dependence. The first rupture was confirmed with Siemens' withdrawal from the EPR in 2009. It became definitive with the decision to phase out nuclear power after the Fukushima accident in 2011. France, on the other hand, has a powerful pro-nuclear lobby that cries foul of a German conspiracy, convinced that Germany refuses to have a neighboring country with a "competitive advantage like EDF," as stated by its former CEO (2009-2014), Henri Proglio: "For twenty-five or thirty years, the German obsession has been to destroy EDF. They have succeeded." There is some truth in this accusation, illustrated by the obligation to sell a portion of its nuclear electricity at a loss to alternative suppliers. But this absolves the group of its responsibility in the mismanagement of nuclear power and supports the idea that Paris has no voice in Brussels, where Berlin dictates terms. EDF must adapt to the new situation in the difficult negotiation with the state shareholder on the selling price of its nuclear production. With Brussels acting as a peacekeeper to ensure the "sincerity" of a price that should not greatly exceed the company's production costs, the Elysée admits. And Berlin as a vigilant censor of any violation of the Luxembourg compromise.
lead: While Paris and Berlin have finally agreed on the method of setting the price of a megawatt-hour at the Hamburg summit on October 17, the two countries continue to defend two completely contradictory energy models.

title: Ursula von der Leyen, les faux pas de l’hyperprésidente
text: When she sits at the table of European heads of state and government, who are set to meet in Brussels on October 26 and 27, Ursula von der Leyen will undoubtedly be greeted very courteously. True to herself, the President of the Commission will also show herself to be perfectly friendly. In this exclusive club of men and women who shape the European Union (EU), one must maintain a good image, even when facing criticism. Lately, Ursula von der Leyen has been severely criticized by the Twenty-Seven on multiple occasions. She is reproached for overstepping her duties by taking initiatives, sometimes unfortunate ones as in the Israeli-Palestinian issue, in foreign policy matters. It is objected that she makes certain decisions without consulting the Member States, such as signing the EU-Tunisia agreement on migration. Concern is raised about her efforts to please Washington, even when European and American interests are not always aligned. As for her perpetual "war" with Charles Michel, the President of the Council, it is extremely exasperating. Since taking office at the end of 2019, Ursula von der Leyen has repeatedly encroached into the realm of European foreign policy, which falls under the Member States, Charles Michel, and the High Representative, Josep Borrell. "The difficulties of the Franco-German couple leave her an opening that she exploits," analyzes a European diplomat. On Ukraine, the Twenty-Seven, who were relatively united on the issue, did not make it a casus belli. Paris and Berlin may have found that she went too far in the promises she made to Volodymyr Zelensky to open the doors of the EU to him or to seize Russian assets in Europe. But, as the Social Democratic MEP Raphaël Glucksmann says, "she has managed to embody Europe in a time of war, and that is no small feat." On the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, things are quite different as the Europeans are deeply divided. Every statement at the level of the EU institutions must be carefully measured, which Ursula von der Leyen evidently forgot during her visit to Israel on October 13, which she did not inform anyone about. When she asserts Israel's right to defend itself after Hamas' terrorist attacks, without reminding that Israel must respect international law and protect civilian populations in Gaza, the Twenty-Seven are outraged. In doing so, she does not convey the position that the European foreign ministers agreed upon on October 10. Worse, she gives the impression to those who do not always grasp the subtleties of Brussels that Europe's voice is pro-Israeli. And she sends a disastrous message to the Southern countries that refuse to condemn the Russian invasion in Ukraine: Israel could exempt itself from this international law to which Western countries want to subject Moscow. Spain, Portugal, Ireland, Belgium, and Luxembourg, sensitive to the Palestinian cause, strongly criticize it. His remarks in Israel were "thoughtless and even unconscious," ruled Irish President Michael D. Higgins on October 16. Other member states, including Germany, which claims its duty to stand by Israel, are exasperated by this "mistake" which forces them to display their divisions and muddles their message. "We are all trying, and it's difficult, to remain as united as possible. And we are witnessing a pathetic parade in the Middle East of political leaders from institutions that no one knows," exclaims a European diplomat. An ego story "I don't understand what the President of the Commission has to do with Europe's foreign policy, which is not her responsibility," the Macronist MEP Nathalie Loiseau ranted on X (formerly Twitter) on October 14. "It's a blunder. She let herself be carried away by emotion. She is German, she was Angela Merkel's defense minister, she has often been to Israel in that capacity," argues a source. For a less benevolent senior European official, who highlights her management style as solitary and vertical, "she also lacks instinct, from being locked in her windowless apartment on the 13th floor of the Commission, where she had a studio set up for herself." It's also an ego story. Learning that Roberta Metsola, the President of the European Parliament, is invited by the Knesset, Ursula von der Leyen invites herself to accompany the Maltese, who had already been the first to visit Kyiv after the Russian aggression. The former minister of Angela Merkel does not like to let others take the spotlight. Charles Michel, equally concerned about his image, and with whom she maintains terrible relations, knows this well. On October 11, in the photo that Ursula von der Leyen posted on social media after the minute of silence organized in front of the European Parliament, attended by the presidents of the three EU institutions side by side, she cropped out the Belgian President of the Council. "In this institutional war, Michel and von der Leyen have gone crazy," judges a close associate of Emmanuel Macron. As unfortunate as it may be, this sequence should not prevent Ursula von der Leyen from seeking a second term after the European elections in June 2024. Since then, she has corrected her course, and her record - European post-Covid-19 recovery plan, joint vaccine purchases, financial and military support to Ukraine, Green Deal, digital regulation, etc. - remains her best asset. But "she must be careful. The member states [who must appoint her] have sent her signals," warns a European diplomat. At this stage, "we don't have a better candidate, but we are considering alternatives," adds one of his colleagues. The message is clear. Transatlantic seduction company More than the Israeli-Palestinian issue, it is Ursula von der Leyen's strong transatlanticism that worries Europeans. France, of course, which has cultivated its difference since Charles de Gaulle. But not only. Italy and even Germany, despite their strong ties with Washington, fear that she may not defend their economic interests well against the United States. "With Biden, one might have the impression that the Americans are benevolent. In reality, it's still 'America first' and the American agenda on industry, technology, or trade remains very harsh," notes a European diplomat. Joe Biden has an obsession: to involve Europe in his fight against Beijing. With Ursula von der Leyen, he has found an ally who has seemed ready, on several occasions, to sacrifice the interests of the continental industry on the altar of this fight. Just recently, the Twenty-Seven had to restrain her so that an unbalanced agreement in their favor was not signed during the United States - Europe summit held in Washington on October 20. "Von der Leyen was ready to do anything to get an agreement," says a source angrily. Especially since Washington is not rushing to meet the demands of the Europeans to amend the Inflation Reduction Act, which provides massive subsidies for the American green industry and makes them fear a wave of offshoring across the Atlantic. On March 10, the President of the Commission had already visited the White House to plead the case of the Old Continent. Not only did she not get anything, but she also said she was in favor - once again without consulting the capitals - of setting up "export controls" and "investments" for European companies, which everyone understood would concern movements towards China. Despite the recurring reproaches of the Twenty-Seven, Ursula von der Leyen persisted in her transatlantic seduction company, fueling doubts among those who, despite her denials, imagine her wanting to take the lead of NATO at the end of the year. On March 30, she delivered a particularly critical speech towards Beijing, which was appreciated in Washington, but much less in Berlin, Paris, and Rome. "Without any mandate, she defined the EU's foreign policy towards Beijing," still chokes a European diplomat today. In June, she presented a strategy to strengthen European economic security in which she tried, with no more success, to revive the projects outlined in Washington three months earlier. In July, the Commission hired American economist Fiona Scott-Morton for a strategic position before backing down under pressure from the Elysée, among others. In September, she announced the opening of an investigation into subsidies for Chinese electric vehicles, which certainly addressed a longstanding French request but was also seen as a gesture to Washington and greatly displeased Germany. "There are twenty-seven European leaders, she is invited to the Council table, but she is not at their level," a European diplomat quips.
lead: The President of the European Commission has exasperated some member states by displaying a more pro-Israeli stance than that of the Twenty-Seven in Israel following the Hamas attack. On other occasions, she has already overstepped her role, particularly in the relationship with the United States.

title: Christopher Steele, auteur d’un rapport sur les liens supposés de Donald Trump avec la Russie, jugé à Londres
text: His controversial report on supposed links between Donald Trump and Russia, mentioning without evidence a video with prostitutes, triggered a political storm in 2017: a former British spy is facing justice in London on Monday, October 16. The former American president, now a candidate for the Republican nomination for the 2024 presidential election, is suing Christopher Steele, 59, and the private intelligence firm Orbis Business Intelligence, founded by this former British intelligence agent, under data protection laws. Commissioned by the Democratic camp during the 2016 US election campaign, Christopher Steele had compiled raw, unverified intelligence linking Donald Trump to Russia. Evidence of Russian interference Some of his findings fueled the investigation by special counsel Robert Mueller, who after two years on the case concluded that there was evidence of Russian interference in the election campaign, but not of collusion with Donald Trump's team. "President Trump is pursuing this case because he seeks to assert his legal rights (...) regarding the fact that the statements contained in these memoranda are false," his lawyer Hugh Tomlinson stated at the hearing at the High Court in London. He specifically targets two notes in the report describing the alleged orgies in which Donald Trump supposedly participated in St. Petersburg, as well as others with prostitutes in Moscow. While the former American president, who was not present at the hearing, acknowledges that the consulting firm Orbis is not responsible for the report's publication, he believes that they "handled" the data contained in the report. On their part, Orbis' lawyers have requested the dismissal of the lawsuits filed solely to "harass Orbis and Mr. Steele and perpetuate old grievances." According to them, their clients are not "responsible" for any damage to Donald Trump's reputation caused by the report's publication without their knowledge. The hearings are set to continue on Tuesday. Compromising allegations Published by the BuzzFeed website ten days before Donald Trump's inauguration in January 2017, this report contained numerous compromising allegations about the former American president, including the existence - never confirmed - of a sexually explicit video involving prostitutes filmed during a trip by Donald Trump to Moscow. It also mentioned information exchanges with the Kremlin over nearly a decade and suggested that Russian President Vladimir Putin had "supported and directed" an operation to "boost" Donald Trump's candidacy for the US presidential election for "at least five years." Christopher Steele had previously passed it on to the FBI, which relied on certain passages to put associates of the Republican billionaire under surveillance. Donald Trump had denounced this report as false, while an investigation by the New York Times had shown that no evidence supported the claims it contained. In October 2020, a defamation lawsuit by Russian businessman Alexeï Goubarev, mentioned in the same report, was dismissed by the British court, which ruled that while the references to Mr. Goubarev were indeed defamatory, he could not prove that the former spy was responsible for their publication. In his testimony submitted to the proceedings, Christopher Steele then stated that he had never intended to disclose the information in question and that if he had been aware, he would have done "everything in [his] power to prevent it." In November 2021, Igor Danchenko, a Russian analyst and former consultant for the renowned think tank Brookings Institution, was indicted for perjury for lying to FBI investigators about the sources provided to Christopher Steele for his report.
lead: Commissioned by the Democratic camp during the 2016 American election campaign, the former British spy had compiled raw, unverified intelligence linking Donald Trump to Russia.

title: Alsu Kurmasheva, journaliste russo-américaine arrêtée le 18 octobre en Russie, restera en prison au moins jusqu’au 5 décembre
text: Arrested on Wednesday, October 18, before having her pretrial detention extended for the first time on Friday, Russian-American journalist Alsu Kurmasheva had her pretrial detention extended again on Monday, October 23, this time at least "until December 5," according to the Russian news agencies. The Sovetskiy District Court of Kazan, in the Tatarstan region, stated that it had approved the investigators' request who arrested the journalist on Wednesday, accusing her of violations related to her registration in the "foreign agents" registry. On Friday, the same court had already extended the journalist's detention for seventy-two hours, who works for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty (RFE/RL). The latter once again called for her "immediate release" on Monday after the court's decision was announced. Up to five years in prison Alsu Kurmasheva, who joined RFE/RL in 1998, works for its Tatar and Bashkir language service, covering these ethnic minorities in Russia, particularly in Tatarstan and Bashkortostan, regions of the Volga and the Urals. The journalist, who usually resides in Prague with her husband and children, traveled to Russia for a "family emergency" on May 20 but could not leave because her American and Russian passports were confiscated. According to the Tatar Inform website, she was fined on October 11 for not declaring her American citizenship to the Russian authorities. According to this media outlet, citing anonymous police sources, she had worked on mobilizing teachers for the Russian army for the offensive in Ukraine. She is being prosecuted for violations related to her registration in the "foreign agents" registry while being engaged in "intentional collection of information concerning military activities" that could be harmful to "the security of the Russian Federation," according to the Russian justice system. She faces up to five years in prison. Crackdown on journalists and opponents Alsu Kurmasheva is the second American journalist to be detained in Russia, along with journalist Evan Gershkovich, who is still imprisoned, arrested for espionage by the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) during a report in Yekaterinburg on March 29. In recent years, several American citizens arrested in Russia have been released after prisoner exchanges with Washington. Russia is accused of conducting an increased crackdown campaign against independent media, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), journalists, lawyers, and opponents since the beginning of its invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. The status of "foreign agent," reminiscent of the Soviet term "enemy of the people," imposes heavy administrative constraints and financial control on the targeted individuals or entities. It also requires that any publication, including on social networks, be accompanied by this label. Some of the most respected critics of Russian President Vladimir Putin are classified by the Russian justice system as "foreign agents," such as Nobel Peace Prize laureate and editor-in-chief of the independent newspaper Novaya Gazeta, Dmitry Muratov, since September 1st. In an interview with Le Monde published on Sunday, Mr. Muratov stated: "I express my opinion. Therefore, I am an enemy of the people simply because I am doing my job. Out of nearly 700 foreign agents, one third are journalists. There are nearly 600 political prisoners in Russia - criminally convicted, but in reality, for political reasons."
lead: The journalist working for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty was arrested in the city of Kazan. She is accused of not declaring herself as a "foreign agent," a charge for which she could face up to five years in prison.

title: Le Burkina Faso resserre son alliance avec la Russie
text: What did an Ilyushin Il-76 come to do in Ouagadougou on Friday, November 10th? While, since taking office in Burkina Faso in September 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré has been ambiguous about his alliances, the landing of this Russian plane, with about twenty soldiers on board, at the Thomas Sankara airport in the capital, has shed some light on the situation. Nine months after the end of French operations in Burkinabe territory, the junta has entered into a new alliance with Russia under Vladimir Putin. The Burkinabe government has been showing signs of closer ties with Moscow in various areas in recent weeks: "Humanitarian aid, energy, nuclear, culture, etc.," as stated in a government press release in October. Mid-month, the construction of a civilian nuclear power plant by the Russian agency Rosatom, "to meet the energy needs of the Burkinabe population," was announced; on Tuesday, cooperation in healthcare to combat the dengue epidemic in the country was highlighted. However, it is primarily in the military sector that Ouagadougou needs Moscow. Three days before the arrival of the Ilyushin Il-76, on November 7th, the Burkinabe Minister of Defense, Colonel Kassoum Coulibaly, was in Moscow to meet his counterpart, Sergei Shoigu. Mr. Coulibaly praised the entry into the "practical phase" of cooperation between the two countries in defense matters. The theoretical phase had taken place a few weeks earlier when a Russian delegation led by Deputy Defense Minister Yunus-bek Yevkurov was received in September by President Ibrahim Traoré. "Areas of cooperation primarily concern the military sector, including the training of Burkinabe soldiers and officers at all levels, especially pilots in Russia," stated the Burkinabe presidency. In a delicate position, the implementation of the alliance between the two countries has accelerated since the meeting between Ibrahim Traoré and Vladimir Putin at the Russia-Africa Summit in St. Petersburg at the end of July. During his only trip abroad since taking power, the junta leader stood out with his diatribe against "imperialist countries." With Russia, "we share the same history as forgotten peoples of the world," declared the 35-year-old captain, who had not removed his uniform or beret. The young head of state plays up his closeness to Thomas Sankara, the Burkinabe president who, in 1986, dared to confront François Mitterrand at a gala dinner. Feeling "like family," Ibrahim Traoré assured Vladimir Putin of the "support of the Burkinabe people and [his] government regarding the situation Russia is facing with the 'special operation' in Ukraine." This new ally could be particularly useful to him, as the Burkinabe leader is in a delicate position. Upon taking office, he based his policy and propaganda on the effectiveness of his response to jihadist groups. He recruited thousands of civilians to bolster the ranks of fighters and declared a "general mobilization" in April, signing a decree allowing him to requisition young people over 18 years old. The Volunteers for the Defense of the Fatherland (VDP), around 90,000 according to official figures, require a considerable supply of weaponry. However, on the ground, no improvement has been observed. Despite the government's promises to the population, entire regions of the country remain destabilized by jihadist attacks, around 2 million people are internally displaced, thousands are refugees in neighboring countries, and the violence - which has claimed at least 16,000 lives since 2015 according to the NGO Acled - shows no signs of decreasing. On November 5, another massacre was reported in the village of Zaongo, in the Central-North region, which left at least 70 victims, "mostly children and elderly people," according to the Burkina Faso prosecutor. The "perpetrators of the atrocities remain unknown," he admitted on Monday. Burkinabe soldiers, as well as VDP members, have also been involved in killings in recent months, without ever being brought to justice. In this context, several historical allies of Burkina Faso have refused to cooperate militarily with Ibrahim Traoré. In January, Ivory Coast had agreed to deliver a thousand Kalashnikovs, pickup trucks, and ammunition to its neighbor. "Initially, we played the card of cooperation in the face of the terrorist threat that threatens us all," acknowledged an Ivorian minister speaking anonymously, "but we will not do it again. Ibrahim Traoré is not a reliable partner." Previously, Ibrahim Traoré had turned to France, which had refused to provide him with weapons. Following this refusal, Burkina Faso had requested the departure of the French ambassador, Luc Hallade, and the French special forces from the "Sabre" operation deployed in the country. Propaganda campaigns Now, the young captain openly chooses to follow in the footsteps of Assimi Goïta, at the helm of neighboring Mali, with whom he has formed an "Alliance of Sahel States" alongside the Nigerien coup leaders, threatened by a military intervention from regional countries following their seizure of power in late July. In Mali, the 1,600 mercenaries from the Russian Wagner group deployed since December 2021 are engaged in combat alongside the Malian army, especially in recent days to retake Kidal from the rebels of the Permanent Strategic Framework for Peace, Security, and Development. This new alliance allows Russia to strengthen its influence in Africa, where it continues to be involved despite the death of Wagner's boss, Yevgeny Prigozhin, in August. Russia is conducting propaganda campaigns and attempting to build closer relationships with certain regimes or political actors in the region. Based on an exchange between security offerings and the exploitation of mineral resources, this partnership is also expected to provide Ibrahim Traoré with a valuable benefit: his personal security, similar to the protection provided by Wagner to President Faustin-Archange Touadéra in the Central African Republic. Ibrahim Traoré, who staged a coup, the second in less than a year, overthrowing Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba, knows that power is fragile. Facing growing trade union protests, the government claimed to have thwarted a coup attempt on September 27 and announced a restructuring of the army. "In some respects, the fight against terrorism is a cover to protect the regime. The coup plotters intend to remain in power for a very long time," said a former Burkinabe diplomat in exile. Elections to end the transition are supposed to be held in July 2024, according to the junta.
lead: Nine months after the end of French operations in the country, military cooperation between Ouagadougou and Moscow is accelerating and entering a "practical phase." Around twenty Russian military personnel have arrived in the capital.

title: Jean Lopez, historien : « Il n’y a rien eu de comparable à l’Ukraine depuis 1945, sauf la guerre entre l’Iran et l’Irak en 1980 »
text: Historian and journalist, Jean Lopez is the editor-in-chief of the magazine Guerres & Histoire, which he founded in 2011. He has notably published, with Benoist Bihan, Conduire la guerre. Entretiens sur l’art opératif (Perrin, 2023), and L’Ours et le Renard, with Michel Goya (Perrin, 2023). You have published a book titled L’Ours et le Renard, on the war in Ukraine, with Michel Goya. Why this title? The bear, as one might guess, represents the Russians, who were thought to have the numbers and strength in a war; and the fox symbolizes the Ukrainians, who were assumed to rely on flexibility and agility. However, things unfolded differently. The large battalions were not on the Russian side but on the side of Kiev. Russia attacked Ukraine on February 24, 2022, with the equivalent of one hundred twenty to one hundred thirty battalions, around 130,000 soldiers from the army, while the Ukrainians had 500,000 to 600,000 soldiers engaged. On the other hand, the quantity of war material was on the Russian side, organized in small units with equipment that was not necessarily top-notch. In contrast, Ukraine fielded equipment that was often newer, more modern, and from a much more diverse range of sources, such as Turkish drones. Can we describe the Russian offensive on Kiev from Belarus as a blitzkrieg? I wouldn't use the term "blitzkrieg." I have the impression that the Russians wanted to replicate what they achieved in Afghanistan by assassinating the Afghan president on Christmas 1979, that is, a swift operation with the landing of a brigade at the Hostomel airfield near Kiev, supported by the infiltration of commandos to try to politically decapitate Ukraine. Once this task was completed, it would pave the way for mechanized forces to take control of a headless country. However, things did not unfold as planned. The Russian columns were ill-prepared, they only knew their objectives at the last moment, and many did not have maps. The Russians had successfully executed their offensive in Crimea in 2014, where, without firing a shot, they managed to seize the Autonomous Republic of Crimea with two or three brigades facing fifteen thousand astonished Ukrainian soldiers. The Ukrainian army failed in 2014. It is now known that it was heavily infiltrated by Russian intelligence services, the FSB, through Ukrainian intelligence. In 2022, the Russians envisioned a country without a backbone, infiltrated by their propaganda, assuming sympathies towards Russia in the east. None of this materialized. Even where the Russian-speaking population was the majority, the Russians received no support. In Kharkiv, it is striking: this predominantly Russian-speaking city resisted the attacker. It was undoubtedly a very unpleasant surprise. After the failure of the attack on Kiev, why did the Russians retreat in good order to the natural border of the Dnieper River, and the Donbass region which they already partially occupied? When the Russians realized they couldn't take Kiev, they couldn't even capture Chernihiv, which controls the Northeastern route, or Kharkiv. They then decided to retreat to the east in good order, and the Ukrainians did not pursue them, either due to a lack of reactivity or exhaustion. In the south, the opposite scenario unfolded: everything went as Russia had planned. Crimea is a natural fortress with few access points, an 8-kilometer-wide bottleneck, and the Russians managed to pass through. Two armies spread out in the south of Ukraine towards the east, along the coast of the Sea of Azov, advancing up to Mariupol, connecting with the Donbass region occupied since 2014, establishing territorial continuity. This is the essential victory for the Russians in this war. It is becoming clear what Vladimir Putin has mentioned several times: the return to the lands conquered by Empress Catherine - Catherine the Great - in the 18th century from the Ottomans, essentially the strip of territory along the Black Sea. The intention was clear: to cut off Ukraine from the sea, turning it into a landlocked state dependent on Russia economically. After managing to cross the Dnieper by seizing the Kherson bridge that should never have been taken intact, they crossed. They occupy Kherson and advance towards Odessa. However, there is an obstacle: Mykolaiv, the major shipyard on the Black Sea coast. They fail and retreat, securing a large bridgehead west of the Dnieper. This war is a mix of classical warfare and modern conflict with drones and sophisticated missiles... completely different from asymmetric wars where a state's armed force faces materially weak fighters... It is evident that the Russians are pursuing an imperial objective reminiscent of the 19th century. In the way they conduct the battle, it feels more like the 20th century, with the massive use of armored units and artillery. Additionally, there are elements of the 21st century with drones dominating the sky, satellites, electronic warfare to disrupt enemy communications, and the intensive use of missiles. Currently, the fighting resembles the 20th century. The Russians have entrenched themselves, mining 100,000 square kilometers. There are three lines of defense, buried bunkers. Simultaneously, the front is covered by electronic warfare, drones, and missiles capable of dispersing thousands of mines. This is nothing like asymmetric wars, such as the one in Iraq. In Ukraine, the fighting is fierce, and the level of casualties is terrible. Since 1945, there has been nothing comparable, except for the war between Iran and Iraq in 1980, which lasted eight years and resulted in a million deaths. Observers described a demoralized Russian army, lacking ammunition, on the verge of collapse. It seems that this is not the case... During the attempt to take Kiev, images showed Russian armored vehicles stopped without fuel or destroyed by Ukrainian infantry. What was the command of this army doing? The only major city the Russians have taken is Kherson. In Mariupol, it was like Stalingrad, with almost total devastation. Ukrainian propaganda depicted a Russian giant battered by blows. And in September 2022, the Ukrainians achieved a magnificent feat southeast of Kharkiv with their surprise counteroffensive, where they regained 20,000 to 30,000 square kilometers, and there we saw the retreat of an enemy brigade. Indeed, the Ukrainians advanced 20 to 30 kilometers, then everything stopped. The Russians took advantage, they rebuilt a very strong front, adapting the type of warfare they conduct to the type of soldiers they have. So far, it holds, on a front that is 1,000 kilometers long, which is huge. It has been four months since the Ukrainians have been trying to break through this defensive line. In some places, they have breached the first line, the second is already compromised, but we know that the Russians are building a fourth. This is not an indication of an army disintegrating at the moment. The Russians lack ammunition, that's true, but so do the Ukrainians. Is the privatization of war, on both the Ukrainian and Russian sides, an important factor in this conflict? In Afghanistan or Iraq, the Americans privatized part of the logistics to secure a road axis, escort trucks or personalities. It was about support, not combat. In Ukraine, it's different. Alongside the national army, there are units, among the most combative, like the Azov Battalion, for example, funded by local initiatives or oligarchs. This has been happening since 2014, even though, under Western influence, the Ukrainian army is becoming more national. It's quite similar with the Russians. Vladimir Putin allowed the development of armed forces that are like fiefdoms. Alongside the traditional army, the Wagner Group was assigned tasks in which the Kremlin did not want to be seen in Africa or the Middle East, and then in Ukraine. There are other sectors of the armed forces in Russia that depend on oligarchs: Gazprom has its brigade, the Chechens have their brigade. The Russian National Guard is under the command of the President of the Russian Federation and not the armed forces. In Russia, the sharing of the monopoly of state violence was organized by the Kremlin, but after the attempted "coup" by Yevgeny Prigozhin, the leader of Wagner, on June 24, 2023, and his elimination two months later, there is clearly a reassertion of control by the federal army. In this war, which is both conventional and technological, did foreign arms deliveries, especially American ones, have a decisive influence? Initially, no. The battle of Kiev was won through the mobilization of the Ukrainian population and soldiers, with equipment that was 90% Soviet and 10% Western. In Washington, London, or Paris, little was expected from the Ukrainians. But when Kiev's allies realized that the Russians had significant weaknesses and that the Ukrainians were eager to fight, they began to provide them with what they lacked. So they received a variety of weapons, especially those that made a difference, such as German tracked artillery, American Himars howitzers and rocket launchers, and French Caesar cannons. These weapons outperform Russian equipment thanks to their guided shells. This allowed the Ukrainians to target Russian logistics, forcing them to move their equipment landing points 500 kilometers back. This qualitative advantage helped the Ukrainians somewhat rebalance the situation starting from June 2022. Kiev also received an effective medical infrastructure, which is crucial for the morale of Ukrainian soldiers. Western allies must deliver more tanks, aircraft, and long-range missiles. Can these weapons change the course of the war? I don't think that a handful of F-16 aircraft – around sixty to seventy planes – will enable the Ukrainians to regain control of the air. The arrival of German Leopard tanks and American Abrams tanks will boost certain brigades, but how many? Four, five, six out of a hundred... All discussions revolve around a more regular supply of ammunition; Volodymyr Zelensky demands shells, more and more shells, and breaching equipment. These Cold War-era materials are capable of penetrating Russian defenses. They include bridge-laying tanks, equipment for digging trenches, and mine-clearing vehicles. Kiev's allies have very few of these sophisticated and expensive materials, and it is very difficult to deliver a large quantity of them. Ukraine's allies have made tremendous efforts, but it is not enough. (C) XEMARTIN LABORDE, VICTORIA DENYS, AND DELPHINE PAPIN
lead: After a year and a half of conflict in Ukraine and far from the usual explanations about these battles, the military historian dissects, in an interview with "Le Monde", how we have reached the current situation of fierce trench warfare on a front stretching 1,000 kilometers.

title: En Russie, le retour du front ukrainien des meurtriers graciés par Poutine
text: The case is still fresh, the murder is less than four years old. A particularly barbaric femicide: a rejected boyfriend who proclaims in advance his intention to kill; a victim, Vera Pekhteleva, a 23-year-old student, fell into a trap as she came to retrieve her belongings; a rape; one hundred eleven wounds on the young woman's body, inflicted with punches and a knife; and finally death by strangulation, with the cord of a clothes iron. The police's reaction, or rather their lack of reaction, to this sordid incident that occurred in 2020 in Kemerovo, Siberia, was shocking. During the three and a half hours of torture and then loud agony of Vera Pekhteleva, neighbors called the security forces at least seven times, with no response: not enough patrols, officers on break, and a profound disinterest in domestic violence. Since then, two police officers have been sentenced to short suspended prison terms for "negligence," without suspension. The murderer, Vladislav Kanious, on the other hand, received a seventeen-year prison sentence. This was in July 2022. Today, he is back in Kemerovo, free. Recruiting in prison The victim's parents learned this in early November, after turning to the justice system. Vladislav Kanious was granted a presidential pardon in April, probably at the time he was joining the Russian army to fight in Ukraine. At that time, the Ministry of Defense had already regained the ability to recruit in prison, previously reserved for the Wagner mercenary group. According to the common saying, after a few months on the front lines, he "redeemed his guilt through spilled blood." The parents are devastated, also worried, but powerless. The judicial case was still to be examined by the Supreme Court, at the request of the civil parties, when the murderer was released. Kanious even stopped paying the monthly compensations he owes them. "We are hundreds in the same situation," says Oksana Pekhteleva, Vera's mother. Their number is unknown, but the independent and exiled media outlet Agentstvo made a sensation on November 9 by compiling seventeen cases of murderers returning from Ukraine after being pardoned by Vladimir Putin. With no public list existing, the number is a low estimate: these are only stories shocking enough, or known enough, for local sites to report on them. Agentstvo's compilation has the feel of a chamber of horrors. It includes stories of young girls raped and then killed or elderly ladies murdered for a meager loot (the vast majority of victims are women), of hardened psychopaths or alcoholics in decline, of dismembered, burned, decapitated bodies thrown into the river... Many of the murderers are repeat offenders and, in most cases, the crime that led them to prison is relatively recent. 2018, 2019, 2020... Buried as "heroes," all are now free, often back in their hometown or city, burdened by the trauma - or the thrill - of their war experience, protected from any criticism by the law that prohibits "discrediting the participants of the special military operation," the official name for the war in Ukraine. Other cases are reported by local websites: the return of coffins of former murderers who died in combat, the number of which is equally difficult to estimate. These individuals are entitled to hero burials, which sometimes causes tensions at the local level. Another difficult fact to accept is that those who return alive only fought for six months (most of the time in the ranks of the Wagner Group, in units with significant losses), while fathers mobilized in September 2022 are still at the front, with no prospect of returning soon. State media are instructed to remain silent on the subject, but Kremlin spokesperson Dmitri Peskov was still questioned on November 10. He reiterated that there are two procedures to obtain a presidential pardon: one, complex and lengthy, exceptional; the other, simplified, "under bullets and shells, through bloodshed." Increase in domestic violence The issue is even more sensitive as it recalls the difficulties posed by the return of fighters. If they are pampered with significant salaries or reserved university placements, the question of violence inevitably arises. By the end of 2022, official statistics already showed a general increase in crime in Russia, particularly an unprecedented rise in murders, attempted murders, and domestic violence in the past twenty years. Since then, no figures have been published. Of the seventeen pardoned murderers identified by Agentstvo, three have already resumed killing since their return from Ukraine. In general, around thirty murders have been committed in the past year by former combatants - and about half by Wagner veterans, who heavily recruited inmates convicted of various crimes or offenses, not limited to homicide, from prisons. These cases only represent those reported in local media where the alleged perpetrator is named. They also share extreme brutality. In just the first few days of October, three murders were committed by suspected Wagner members: in the Krasnoyarsk region (Siberia), one set fire to the neighbors' apartment, killing two people; in Nizhny Novgorod region (northeast of Moscow), another burned his sister alive; and in Lipetsk (about 510 kilometers south of the capital), a third killed his partner's 4-year-old daughter. Furthermore, on Tuesday, November 14, the Telegram channel Baza reported that the organizer (and not the sponsor, never identified) of the murder of journalist Anna Politkovskaya in 2006 had been released from prison due to the war in Ukraine. Sergei Khadjikurbanov, a former police officer who was supposed to be released in 2034, has reportedly been in the "special military operation" zone since the end of 2022.
lead: At least seventeen convicted murderers have been granted a presidential pardon after fighting in Ukraine, according to available data. And at least three of them have already killed again.

title: La contre-attaque d’Anatoliy Tymoshchuk, le renégat du football ukrainien
text: On February 24, 2022, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk went from being an idol of all Ukraine to a traitor reviled by his country. That day, the former player and captain of the national football team refused to condemn the Russian invasion. Living in Saint Petersburg, he, who had become the assistant coach of Zenit, the club sponsored by Gazprom and favored by Vladimir Putin, remained silent, a silence that his compatriots could only judge as complicit. Only on March 9 did he post a bland message on Instagram, while Russian tanks were still at the gates of Kyiv: "We need peace. For the sake of our children and our parents, for the sake of life on earth." There was no reference to the aggressor country and its president. A meager and rather weak reaction from someone who had worn the colors of Ukraine for fifteen years in all European stadiums. While soldiers filmed themselves on the front lines trampling on his number 4 jersey, official sanctions were not long in coming. In March 2022, the Ukrainian Football Federation removed the former star from its hall of fame. They erased his one hundred and forty-four national team appearances, the national record. They stripped him of all his titles won on Ukrainian fields and revoked his coaching diplomas. The man is banned from football on national soil. Far from repenting, the player is now counterattacking before the sports court. In late September, he filed a request for annulment with the Court of Arbitration for Sport (CAS) in Lausanne, Switzerland. When contacted by Le Monde magazine, his lawyer, the Bulgarian Georgi Gradev, refused to comment: "My client does not want to participate in media propaganda." The hearing for the case "CAS 2023/A/9443 Anatoliy Tymoshchuk v. Ukrainian Association of Football (UAF)" has not been scheduled yet. Tymoshchuk vs. Ukraine... How did this unthinkable divorce come about? In the early 2000s, the midfielder, along with striker Andriy Shevchenko, was one of the stars of the young national team, nicknamed the Sbirna. The team had reached the quarterfinals of the 2006 World Cup. A source of pride in a country where football reigns, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk was courted by top clubs. His long blonde hair was a symbol of Ukrainian sport. He won the Champions League in 2013 with Bayern Munich. After each victory, the player always appeared draped in the national colors. He spoke Ukrainian to journalists. In an interview with Le Monde in November 2013, when he had inherited the captain's armband left by Andriy Shevchenko who had retired, the player spoke of his pride in representing Ukraine. He praised the Sbirna, "[its] team spirit, [its] fighting spirit, [its] dedication to the national team, and [its] commitment." Anatoliy Tymoshchuk in the jersey of Zenit Saint Petersburg (Russia), in Dortmund (Germany), on October 28, 2020. ALEX GOTTSCHALK / DEFODI IMAGES VIA GETTY IMAGES However, in 2014, when Russia annexed Crimea, the player who was with Zenit Saint Petersburg did not react to the Kremlin's power grab. In April 2015, he did visit wounded Ukrainian soldiers on the Donbass front alongside other members of the national team. In 2017, Maksym Demtchychyn, his wife's half-brother, was killed by separatists. After retiring as a player in 2017, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk returned to Saint Petersburg as an assistant coach for Zenit. His friends and teammates from the national team noticed a change in attitude, with him displaying pro-Russian positions. At that time, other players from the national team like Andriy Voronin at Dynamo Moscow, or Yaroslav Rakitskiy at Zenit as well, maintained a similar ambiguity. Rakitskiy often angered the Ukrainian public by refusing to sing the national anthem during team presentations. After the invasion in 2022, both players left Russia in solidarity with their people who were suffering from the bombings. The former stars of the national team formed a united front behind their army. Defender Vladyslav Vashchuk (sixty-three caps), who had played for Spartak Moscow, even enlisted. Ballon d'Or winner Andriy Shevchenko (one hundred eleven caps), despite his past ties with Russian oligarch Roman Abramovich, for whom he played when the billionaire owned Chelsea, also clearly supported his country. He was appointed as an external advisor to President Volodymyr Zelensky at the end of September, while also receiving the title of national hero. A more mercantile than political motivation Anatoliy Tymoshchuk's patriotic betrayal was particularly disconcerting given that he hails from Lutsk, a nationalist stronghold in the west of the country. Even though he played in Donetsk for a long time, a region in the Donbass invaded by the Russians in 2014, he is not from the Russian-speaking East, known to be more susceptible to Kremlin propaganda. His motivation appears to be more financial than political. Close to the oligarchic circles of Saint Petersburg, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk has opened several restaurants in the city. He refuses to give up his lucrative sporting and non-sporting activities. His former national team colleagues are not sympathetic to the renegade. "Anatoliy is probably the last subject I would want to discuss right now," replied Oleksandr Shovkovskiy, the former goalkeeper with ninety-two national team caps who lives in Ukraine. "What is happening to him is the result of his choices. And he will have to live with that." His family is no more forgiving. "I'm not going to talk to you about Anatoliy," Oleksandr Tymoshchuk said before hanging up. His father, who joined a territorial regiment. His now ex-wife, who moved to Germany with their children, poetically referred to him as a "Russian ass-kisser" in the Ukrainian press. Her former husband stopped paying alimony in 2014, citing difficulties in transferring money due to economic sanctions against Russia following the annexation of Crimea... Stripped of the title of honorary citizen of Lutsk, sanctions have continued to rain down on the former star. By a decree on January 7, 2023, Volodymyr Zelensky banned him from any financial, administrative, or real estate operations in Ukraine for a period of ten years. Symbolic humiliation: Lutsk, his hometown, which had made him an honorary citizen in 2008, stripped him of this title this year. Ukrainian media also echoed accusations from the player's former personal secretary. Displaying documents with unverifiable sources and accuracy, Vitaliy Yurchenko claimed in March 2022 that the player had been an agent of the FSB, the Russian secret services, since 2012, while he was still a pillar of the national team. Perhaps too good to be true evidence, but it further tarnished the former player's reputation. Faced with all these accusations, the man remains silent, preferring to rely on a court to regain, if not his honor, at least his caps and diplomas.
lead: In 2022, the most capped player in the history of Ukraine was stripped of his titles for not condemning the Russian invasion. As an assistant coach of the Saint Petersburg team, close to Russian oligarchs, he filed a request for annulment before the sports court at the end of September.

title: Un voyage de Volodymyr Zelensky en Israël remis en question après une fuite dans les médias
text: The rumor of an imminent visit by the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, had spread like wildfire in the Israeli media. On Saturday, November 4, citing an anonymous Ukrainian diplomat, the Times of Israel finally announced that the trip was being called into question due to a leak revealing the planned visit to a national news channel. According to the diplomat, the Ukrainian president "wanted the trip to be made public when he set foot on Israeli soil." "He is very disappointed," the diplomat added. Ukrainian authorities have not commented on this decision reversal, just as they had not made any announcements beforehand. It is difficult to know whether the visit will be maintained, postponed, or canceled. This leak in the press nonetheless remains a weak sign of change since Ukraine positioned itself in support of the Israeli state and Moscow took a hostile turn towards Israel at the beginning of the conflict with Hamas, a month earlier. At the end of October, the Israeli ambassador to Ukraine, Michael Brodsky, explained to local media that he supported the idea of a visit by the president of one of the most pro-Israeli countries in Europe, in his words. Shortly after the Hamas massacres on October 7, the Ukrainian president had been one of the first leaders to offer to visit the region in solidarity with the Israeli state. This initial request had been rejected through the press. According to Iliya Kusa, an analyst based in Kiev, this public support can be explained in part by Ukraine's ambition to "capitalize" on the deteriorating relations between Russia and Israel.
