"Since Russia has seemingly taken a pro-Palestine stance [since the beginning of the war], many hoped that this would lead to a rupture in the usual relations between the two countries and that it would help Ukraine to sway Israel to our side," he explains. Israel's dependence on Russia Kiev has been trying to rally Israel to its cause since the beginning of the invasion, hoping to receive military support, but without much success. Unlike Western leaders, the Israeli Prime Minister has not visited Ukraine since February 24, 2022, due in particular to Israel's strategic dependence on Russia. Moscow, which has had a presence in Syria since 2015, allows the Israeli air force to carry out precise strikes against Iranian interests in the country. However, this new pro-Israeli positioning, inevitable due to Kiev's relationship with the United States, is not without risks. "Ukraine is torn between the need to keep Israel as a valuable partner and the interest in improving relations with non-Western countries, especially in the Middle East, as we want the Middle East to remain neutral, at least in the war between Ukraine and Russia," Mr. Kusa explains. Since the beginning of the conflict between Israel and Hamas, some countries playing strategic mediation roles between Ukraine and Russia have accused the West of using double standards, between the general condemnation of Russian war crimes and the silence surrounding civilian victims in Gaza.
lead: The Ukrainian president, who has a pro-Israel stance, has been trying to rally the Israeli state to his cause since the beginning of the invasion of his country by Russia.

title: A Kiev, des épouses de soldats réclament leur démobilisation après dix-huit mois de guerre
text: A small crowd has gathered in Maidan Square in Kiev, mainly women, demanding the demobilization of their husbands after eighteen months of service in the army. On Sunday, November 12, in the central square of the Ukrainian capital, a few dozen wives, some accompanied by their children, brave the wind and rain, holding signs. "Left to defend a free country and became a serf of war"; "Now it's someone else's turn." A little girl holds a sign with pink letters: "It's my turn to hug my father." "We want a time limit, because there is now this feeling that you can enter the army and never leave," explains Anastasiia Troshyna, 23, whose husband, Oleg, is fighting in the Donbass region in the east of the country, in Donetsk. "They are tired, demotivated, they want rest," insists the young woman, referring to her husband and the soldiers in his unit. "They want to come here, see their children and loved ones." Under martial law, demobilization can only occur in cases of severe injuries, urgent family reasons, or reaching the maximum mobilization age of 60. Since Oleg enlisted in March 2022, Anastasiia has only been able to see him once, when he was sent to a hospital in the capital for a leg injury. While Ukrainian law states that soldiers are entitled to thirty days of leave per year, "this law doesn't work," his wife asserts, "because there is no one to replace them." Refusal to enlist by some is another goal of these small protests that have been taking place in several cities across the country for the past two weeks: to convince those who have not yet fought to join the armed forces. "I know people around me who want to go but hesitate because they don't know how long it will last," says Anastasiia Troshyna. "Will it be for five years? For ten years? Today, the feeling is that you have to be there until the end." For Olha, who refused to give her last name, demobilization after eighteen months would provide some perspective. "No one wants to go fight, and those at home realize there is no end," she says. "We go until we die or become disabled. Maybe this will motivate others, maybe it will alleviate their fears a bit, because people are afraid, afraid to go there and die." The massive enlistment in the early months of the invasion, which had made Ukrainians proud, has given way, in recent months, to increasingly numerous criticisms against men determined to do anything to avoid the army. Some limit their movements or hardly leave their homes anymore. This is also evidenced by the popularity of Telegram channels that track the movements of recruiting officers in the cities. The refusal of a portion of the male population to enlist annoys Iryna Topynko: "We are told that there is no one to replace our men on the front lines, but we see what is happening here!" she exclaims. "We know through hearsay who paid, how they paid, who did what, who went abroad. This injustice... well, it can drive us crazy, and our loved ones too." However, this woman in her fifties wants to clarify that her husband "will not come back without his brothers in arms, as in other units where they say they will stay until the end of the war." "A little more motivated" Many videos of civilians being forcefully arrested by recruiting officers are circulating on social media. The various corruption scandals within the Ministry of Defense and regional recruitment offices that have occurred during the first two years of the war have had a certain impact. Recently, a new scandal has sparked outrage and anger. On November 3, 19 soldiers from the 128th Mountain Assault Brigade were killed, and dozens were injured, in a Russian missile attack while they were gathered for a medal ceremony, about thirty kilometers from the front line in the Zaporijia region. President Volodymyr Zelensky has addressed the event several times, offering his condolences and stating that an investigation would shed light on a "tragedy that could have been avoided." For Olya, whose husband Mykhaïlo also fights in Donbass, a demobilization after eighteen months would give hope to those on the front lines. "It will bring them a bit of strength, they will be a little more motivated, especially among those who have been there since day one," she says. "They do not want to stay there without a return date."
lead: On Maidan Square, as in the rest of Ukraine, women have been demonstrating for two weeks for the return and replacement of their loved ones who left for the front lines in the early hours of the Russian invasion.

title: A Lyon, « Nous ne sommes plus… » fait le deuil d’une Russie perdue
text: THEATER Six Russian actors dressed in white and armed with flashlights enter the stage of the Théâtre des Célestins in Lyon. Cautious spelunkers, they take the measure of the space before presenting themselves to the audience in still hesitant French. Programmed as part of the Sens interdits festival, they offer a mourning spectacle titled "We are no longer...", which is conjugated in the present to better express the imperfect, irremediable, and heartbreaking nature of the story being told. Tatiana Frolova and the actors of the KnAM Theater had been working (this verb is now conjugated in the past) for thirty-seven years in Komsomolsk-on-Amur, a city created by Joseph Stalin in the 1930s, in the heart of the taiga. Far from Moscow, but not from the persecutions carried out by the Russian president, Vladimir Putin, against any form of dissent. On February 24, 2022, Tatiana Frolova and her troupe, creators of a theater too critical for the political authorities of their country, decided to flee. Russia had just begun its invasion of Ukraine. It was "the last straw," comments the Frenchwoman Bleuenn Isambard. A companion of the KnAM since 2011, she translates the actors' words live during a show whose subject is this lost Russia, in more ways than one. Because the actors will not return there. Because it is "irradiated," they say, by the specter of the defunct USSR and the grip on the people of the current Putin regime. "Today, it is impossible to live in Russia and not want to die there," is heard throughout a plural narrative, enriched by the experiences of the protagonists and oscillating between documentary and metaphorical writings. No longer afraid Shortly after February 24, 2022, preferring uprooting to "imprisonment that would have turned her into an animal," Tatiana Frolova packed her bags. Twenty-three kilos. Not a gram more was allowed on the plane. The luggage transited through Kyrgyzstan before reaching Lyon. "Five members of the KnAM arrived in March 2022, the three others four months later," testifies Pierre-Yves Lenoir, director of the Célestins. They applied for political asylum, obtained it quickly, and are now awaiting their residency permits. The actors were financially supported in their efforts by the Célestins, the Sens interdits festival, the National Dramatic Center of Besançon, and the Théâtre populaire romand de La Chaux-de-Fonds (Switzerland): "We are not alone around the cradle. The Ministry of Culture, through the regional direction of cultural affairs, also supports the KnAM company with a project grant," confides Pierre-Yves Lenoir, who has appointed Tatania Frolova as an associated artist of his theater for "a minimum of three years." A natural support from a municipal house more than ever concerned with international creation, for whom France is a paradise. At 62, the author and director from Russia, adopted by the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region, is learning not to be afraid when she walks in the street. "I knew I was being watched in Komsomolsk-on-Amur," says Tatiana Frolova. "When I arrived here, I stayed indoors for six months. I was paralyzed, unable to do anything. Gradually, I thawed out." Like her comrades, she left behind family and friends. What belongings to take when one goes into exile for good? A book of songs for one, an embroidered tablecloth for another. A third chose the teddy bear with which he played puppetry as a child. These remnants of a bygone life are held up by the actors, who unravel, both literally and figuratively, the strings of their bundles. Fleeting music The stage is makeshift. A window, a mirror, a few tables, paper costumes, scissors, carnival masks, a camera zooming in on details of faces or hands, small or large piles of earth, videos and their news images: one of a Russian soldier, a prisoner in Ukraine, whose mother claims he must die for the homeland. Another of Vladimir Putin asserting that Russia has no borders. In the background, on the wall, adhesive tape outlines its territory, 17,095,000 square kilometers, with figures delivered as is by the actors: "Fertile land represents 13%, but only a fifth is developed, which is 2.5% of the total territory. The remaining four fifths are beyond the capabilities of the Russian man. But for a reason I do not know, the Russian needs to appropriate the lands of others," exclaims one of the performers. It is this gaping heart where blood no longer flows that is the matrix of the show. A void where the human is absent, but which Vladimir Putin fantasizes about expanding infinitely, in a desire for unrestrained contamination that Tatiana Frolova and her troupe stage by pointing out, through their words, the slow and insidious poisoning of Russian mentalities: "The serf is still inside people," laments the director. What to do then, except to leave, to escape the irradiation? When the actors evoke "The Cherry Orchard" (1904), a play by Anton Chekhov (1860-1904), the abandonment of the beloved house by its ruined inhabitants and the closing doors on what was and will be no more, emotion surges through the audience. This discreet and impressionistic theatrical work unfolds in small touches. In a continuum of short narratives, fleeting music, ephemeral gestures, it gives shape to intimate fractures, moral disintegration, and the ontological void of a Russia whose cartography, drawn on the wall by a precarious adhesive tape, ends up in tatters.
lead: At the Théâtre des Célestins, the Russian actors from the KnAM company, exiled in France since the beginning of the war in Ukraine, are staging their homeland under the control of Vladimir Putin.

title: En visite à Pékin, Vladimir Poutine dément son statut de paria
text: For two days, the journalists embedded with the Russian delegation invited to the "New Silk Road" Forum in Beijing, kept a close eye on the signs of attention given to their leader, Vladimir Putin. From the protocol welcome, seating arrangements during meetings, speaking opportunities, to entering alongside Chinese President Xi Jinping into the grand banquet hall on the evening of Tuesday, October 17th, the Russian president was the most pampered among the twenty-four heads of state and government received at the forum. "No matter which channel you turn on, Putin is everywhere on Chinese television," exclaimed the star host of the Rossia 1 channel, sent to Beijing. "Even with a hundred and fifty delegations, he is the main guest and the guest of honor at this summit." While the second part of the statement leaves little doubt, the hosts of the Russian-language Telegram channel "Chinese Threat" verified the first part: on the day of his arrival in China, Vladimir Putin was only shown three times on the seventeen channels of Chinese public television, for brief interventions praising Xi Jinping's role as a "leader." For the Russian president, the main focus is elsewhere. It is about refuting the pariah status in which Western states try to confine him - a status reinforced by the issuance, in March, of an international arrest warrant by the International Criminal Court against him. Since that date, Mr. Putin has only made one foreign trip, on October 12th, to Bishkek (Kyrgyzstan), as part of a CIS summit. Beijing was supposed to mark an important milestone in the international agenda of the Russian president, who has been particularly active on the Israeli-Palestinian issue in recent days, speaking on the phone with most regional leaders. The previous sequence had been mainly marked by the long visit of the North Korean leader, Kim Jong-un, to Russian territory, one of the very few states in the world to provide unconditional support to Moscow in its war in Ukraine. Speeches on Western duplicity Russian grievances against the West also take on an additional dimension in this multilateral framework. Putin's speeches on Western duplicity, even his devotion to "satanism," have become a perfectly routine exercise in Russia. In Beijing on Wednesday, his plea for "a more just multipolar world" and "the right of each state to its own development model" resonate with Chinese statements. According to him, Beijing's projects align with Russia's desire to create a "great Eurasian space." While the French delegation, led by former Prime Minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin, left the room during this speech, Putin received support in his efforts to rebuild international respectability from Viktor Orban. The Hungarian Prime Minister met with the Russian President on Tuesday, something no leader of the European Union has done since April 2022. He showed great respect, emphasizing that Budapest had "never sought confrontation with Russia, on the contrary." During his meeting with Vladimir Putin, Xi Jinping also expressed friendship towards his host, mentioning "the steadily growing mutual political trust between our two countries." However, the meeting was brief and does not erase the divergences that have accumulated in the bilateral relationship, mainly to the detriment of Moscow – whether it be the halt of drone deliveries to Russia due to fear of sanctions or Chinese hesitations regarding the Siberia 2 gas pipeline project, intended to partially compensate Moscow for the loss of the European market.
lead: The Russian president was welcomed with honors in China for his second trip outside of Russian territory since the International Criminal Court issued a warrant for his arrest.

title: Adhésion de l’Ukraine et de la Moldavie à l’Union européenne : la Commission recommande l’ouverture des négociations
text: Ukraine has taken a further step towards joining the European Union (EU). Through its president, Ursula von der Leyen, the European Commission officially recommended the opening of negotiations in this regard on Wednesday, November 8, with Ukraine and also with Moldova. "Today is a historic day," said Mrs. von der Leyen. In June 2022, the EU granted Ukraine candidate status, in a highly symbolic gesture a few months after the start of the Russian invasion, as well as to neighboring Moldova. The opening of formal negotiations is the next step before a potential membership. The Commission's favorable opinion must now be approved or rejected by the twenty-seven member countries at their next summit on December 14 and 15 in Brussels. "Ukraine continues to face enormous difficulties and the tragedy caused by Russia's aggression," said Ursula von der Leyen, emphasizing that despite these difficulties, Ukraine has continued to reform "deeply" in preparation for its EU membership. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky welcomed the European Commission's "good" decision on Wednesday. "Our state must be in the EU. Ukrainians deserve it," said Mr. Zelensky in a video posted on Telegram. However, this recommendation is still far from marking the end of the road for Ukraine and other candidate countries covered by this Commission report on EU enlargement, released on Wednesday. "Geopolitical response to Russia's aggression" If the Twenty-Seven endorse the Commission's opinion in December, Ukraine will join other European countries that have entered into negotiations with the EU, some of which, like Turkey, have been waiting for many years. Negotiations between the Union and Ankara were opened in 2005 but have been frozen since June 2018. Nevertheless, enlargement to the East is considered by several countries as a fundamental element of security in Europe in the face of Russian ambitions. "A stronger, larger, and more united European Union is the geopolitical response to Russia's aggression," said German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock. In contrast, Hungary, which maintains strong ties with Moscow, has been critical. "We believe that it is not appropriate to take further steps in the accession negotiations with Ukraine," said Hungarian Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto, adding that Kiev cannot "join the European Union until there is peace." Furthermore, a possible entry of Ukraine, a country of over 40 million inhabitants and an agricultural power, into the Union will not be without difficulty. The FNSEA, the largest French agricultural union, even stated that it would be a "catastrophe for European agriculture." Gestures for Georgia and Bosnia The situation is different for Moldova, a small country among the poorest in Europe. Chisinau regularly denounces destabilization attempts by Moscow. The country has undertaken "significant reforms" in view of the accession process, praised the President of the European Commission. Ms. von der Leyen also announced that she had recommended granting Georgia, a country partly occupied by Russia since 2008, an official candidate status for EU membership. "Today is a day of celebration in Georgia," said Ms. von der Leyen, who nevertheless emphasized that this country still needs to undertake "significant reforms" corresponding to the "aspirations of the overwhelming majority of its citizens to join the EU." An announcement that was immediately "welcomed" by the pro-Western Georgian President, Salome Zourabichvili. Finally, the European Commission also made a gesture towards Bosnia, a candidate country for accession, by showing support for the opening of accession negotiations with this deeply divided Balkan country once certain progress has been made. "We have opened the door wide," but results are now expected, emphasized Ms. von der Leyen.
lead: The European Commission's favorable opinion must now be approved or not by the Twenty-Seven at their next summit on December 14 and 15 in Brussels. Ursula von der Leyen recommends granting candidate status to Georgia.

title: La Commission européenne recommande l’ouverture de négociations d’adhésion avec l’Ukraine et la Moldavie
text: In front of the European Parliament, which was preparing to vote on his nomination to head the Commission in July 2014, Jean-Claude Juncker had stated that there would be no further enlargement of the European Union (EU) during his term. "The EU needs to take a break in its enlargement process in order to consolidate what has been done with the Twenty-Eight," he explained, noting that thirteen states had joined since 2004. He did not imagine at the time that the United Kingdom would leave the European community. Nor did he imagine that, nearly ten years later, the Twenty-Seven would be outlining the contours of a EU with more than thirty member states. "Enlargement is a call from history, it is the natural horizon of our EU," said Ursula von der Leyen, who succeeded Jean-Claude Juncker at the end of 2019. In line with this logic, the Commission proposed opening accession negotiations with Ukraine and Moldova, as well as granting candidate status, subject to conditions, to Georgia. The EU executive also announced measures aimed at providing reassurance to the six Western Balkan countries that have been waiting in the EU's waiting room for years (Albania, Montenegro, Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Kosovo) and may fear being overtaken by Kiev and Chisinau. After being put on hold, "enlargement is back," according to a senior European official. The invasion of Ukraine by Russia on February 24, 2022, has radically changed the situation, and even the member states most resistant to any further enlargement, notably France, have been forced to change their stance. In this new geopolitical and security context, "our neighbors must choose" between "democracy" and "authoritarian rule," between the EU and Russia, Ursula von der Leyen assured. Ukraine and Moldova have made their choice. Since being granted candidate status in June 2022, despite the war for one and the strong tensions at the Russian border for the other, they have multiplied reforms so that negotiations can begin. According to the European Commission, Kiev and Chisinau have completed "more than 90%" of what was asked of them in terms of fighting corruption, respecting minority rights, judicial independence, and measures to reduce the influence of oligarchs on power. "Not historic," according to Volodymyr Zelensky. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky praised the "historic step" taken by the Commission. "Our state must be in the EU. Ukrainians deserve it," he emphasized, as the Ukrainian counteroffensive stalls, the Israeli-Palestinian conflict diverts the attention of its allies, and the question of the sustainability of American support arises. For her part, Maia Sandu, the Moldovan president, stated that her country "is firmly committed to the path of EU accession and [will] continue to work tirelessly to achieve this goal." The announcements on Wednesday mark an important step towards the accession of Kiev and Chisinau to the EU, but they do not guarantee the outcome of a process fraught with obstacles. It will already be necessary for the Twenty-Seven, whose heads of state and government are set to meet on December 14 and 15 in Brussels, to unanimously adopt the Commission's proposal. While the vast majority of them are expected to support it, Hungary, which is using all means of pressure at its disposal to obtain the EU funds that the Commission has blocked in the name of the rule of law, could voice dissent. The government of Viktor Orban has already indicated that it may oppose the opening of negotiations if Ukraine does not provide enough guarantees to the Hungarian minority living in the western part of its territory. "Once the Council gives its green light, we can start negotiations immediately," asserts Ursula von der Leyen. "As of the evening of December 15, the Commission will send teams to Kiev and Chisinau," adds a senior official, to begin assessing the tasks ahead and prepare for what comes next. However, to move forward and formally open accession negotiations, Ukraine and Moldova will need to complete the reforms requested of them in June 2022. The Commission will review progress in March 2024, but it is "fully confident" in the ability of both countries to make progress. Legislation to be thoroughly reviewed Assuming this step is also successfully completed, Volodymyr Zelensky and Maia Sandu will need to undertake a profound overhaul of their legislation to align it with EU practices, not only in terms of the rule of law, but also in areas such as free movement, energy market, social regulation, competition, combating climate change, economic environment, financial services, and agriculture. In total, thirty-five "chapters" will need to be opened and closed for Ukraine and Moldova to acquire the necessary "acquis communautaire" for their EU accession. The experience – Serbia has been negotiating since 2013, Montenegro since 2012... – shows that the matter is not simple. One thing is certain: even though the political context is more favorable today, the war will not make Ukraine's task easier. Especially since Kiev cannot join the Twenty-Seven as long as the conflict persists: the treaties would require the EU to go to war alongside Ukraine under the mutual assistance clause. Compared to the Western Balkans, "Moldova and Ukraine are moving at full speed," according to a senior European official, who assures that the Commission, for its part, is determined to move as quickly as possible. "The preliminary phase, which allows for an assessment of the projects to be carried out, usually takes one to two years. We intend to complete it in six months," this source illustrates. On Wednesday, Ursula von der Leyen delivered a passionate plea for enlargement. "Past enlargements have shown the enormous benefits that both candidate countries and the EU gain. We all win," she stated. Not all member states see things the same way, as the entry of Ukraine and its 40 million inhabitants into the EU would deeply alter the financial, economic, and political balances of the European integration. Some countries, including France and Germany, believe that prior reforms are needed in both the functioning of the EU and its policies, should it absorb a country as large and as poor as Ukraine. This is also expected to be a challenging task.
lead: The Commission's requests, including the granting of candidate status to Georgia under certain conditions, will be submitted to the European Council in December, which must make a unanimous decision.

title: Guerre Israël-Hamas : des divergences persistent dans l’Union européenne
text: In a tense atmosphere, twenty-six European heads of state and government met virtually on Tuesday, October 17th. The only one absent was Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who was visiting China and meeting with Vladimir Putin. This extraordinary European Council meeting took place ten days after the brutal attack by Hamas on Israel, which resulted in around 1,400 deaths, and Israel's subsequent response, leading to the deaths of over 3,000 Palestinians under the bombs. "It was a moment of unity," explained Charles Michel, the President of the European Council. Unity was necessary after a week of confusion regarding the future of European aid to Palestine and divergent positions among the Union's leaders, making the European position inaudible in the world, especially in the Middle East. "It is of the utmost importance that the European Council, in accordance with the treaties and our values, defines our common position and establishes a clear and unified course of action that reflects the complexity of the current situation," Charles Michel emphasized before the discussions. "We strongly emphasize Israel's right to defend itself," the European leaders stated in a joint declaration, in accordance with international humanitarian law and international law, in the face of such violent and indiscriminate attacks. "We emphasize the importance of ensuring the protection of all civilians at all times, in accordance with international humanitarian law." Criticism of von der Leyen As early as October 10th, foreign ministers had agreed on this balanced position. However, Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the Commission, struggled to articulate it. During a surprise visit to Israel on October 13th, alongside Roberta Metsola, the President of the European Parliament, she did not speak on behalf of the Twenty-Seven, according to several diplomats, but rather expressed her own views. She condemned Hamas attacks and supported the Netanyahu government's policies, without any public reference to the situation of Palestinians in the Gaza Strip. Nonetheless, her team insists that the issue of civilians and respect for international law was directly addressed with Benjamin Netanyahu. At the European Council, French President Emmanuel Macron and Irish Prime Minister Leo Varadkar reiterated their thinly veiled criticisms of the Commission President's stance. "For two years, we have been trying to convince Southern countries that international law must prevail, especially in Ukraine, and now we are losing them because of these types of statements," laments a European official. "We must avoid any double standards," adds another source in Brussels. The Twenty-Seven and the leaders have pledged to better coordinate in the future and speak with one voice. At the end of the meeting, Mrs. von der Leyen assured that "there is no contradiction between solidarity with Israel and action in favor of the humanitarian needs of the Palestinians," recalling the 200% increase in humanitarian aid planned for the population of Gaza (75 million euros) and the launch of an airlift to Egypt to deliver aid as quickly as possible to the bombarded territory. "The Americans, on the other hand, are on the ground." However, the President of the Commission still appeared to be at odds with Mr. Michel. Responding to a question about the explosion at Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza, which killed several hundred people on Tuesday, the latter stated that "an attack on a civilian infrastructure is not in line with international law." Mrs. von der Leyen, "who did not have time to get informed," refused to answer the question, while Israel and the Palestinians are blaming each other for the strike. A few minutes later, Josep Borrell stated on the social network X that "the news from Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza adds horror to the tragedy unfolding before our eyes for several days. Once again, it is innocent civilians who are paying the price. The responsibility for this crime must be clearly established and the perpetrators must be held accountable." The leaders emphasized the importance of continuing their consular cooperation to evacuate the thousand European citizens still living in Gaza. They also stated that they are working on the issue of Hamas hostages, "several dozen" of whom, according to a European diplomat, have EU nationality. Finally, according to the Elysée, "the leaders agreed to work together with regional actors to end the violence and work towards creating the conditions for lasting peace." However, there is still a lingering unease on this matter in Brussels. Ten days after the start of this new war, "we are still trying to get on the same page internally," laments a diplomat in Brussels. "The Americans, on the other hand, are on the ground trying to make progress, especially for civilians."
lead: The heads of state and government have tried to put an end to a week of European cacophony regarding the situation in the Middle East. They express their support for Israel and call for respect for international law and humanitarian law in Gaza.

title: Après le déclenchement de la guerre Israël-Hamas, « Vladimir Poutine se frotte les mains »
text: One conflict can hide another. The war that has just broken out between Israel and Hamas, and the risks of regional escalation that it is feared to bring, has pushed another war off the media's radar, the one that Russia has been waging in Ukraine for twenty months. Yet, the two crises intersect, through the actors who can benefit from them. Foremost among them: Vladimir Putin. The most obvious reason why this new explosion in the Middle East comes at an opportune time for the Russian president is that, while Ukraine's allies turn with horror to the south, Russia is re-launching its offensive in the Donbass. These days, Russian forces relentlessly shell the Avdiivka area, a city of 30,000 inhabitants that now has less than 2,000 due to the suffering, in an attempt to dislodge the Ukrainian defenders. Since 2022, everyone in Europe knows the names of Mariupol and Bakhmut. But who, today, has heard of Avdiivka? The battle is raging there. "The enemy is suffering significant losses," reported Ukrainian General Oleksandr Syrsky on Monday, October 23, "but it constantly replenishes its forces with reserve troops, including those brought in from Russia." Not a word about Ukrainian losses; one can easily imagine them. On Saturday, October 21, a Russian missile killed six civilians in a postal sorting center in Kharkiv. Radio silence. From the start, Vladimir Putin has been betting that Western public opinion will eventually tire of the war in Ukraine. He could not have dreamed of a better scenario than a new level of horror reached in the Middle East. Fracture There are other, less immediate, reasons why this crisis benefits the Russian president. He invaded Ukraine in the name of an imperial design: the conquest of a nation whose existence as a state he denies. Operations on the ground did not go as he had planned; the "non-existent nation" resisted, supported by Western democracies. On the diplomatic front, however, Russia encountered less resistance: the war highlighted a fracture between these democracies, convinced of defending, along with Ukraine, international law, and some states from the rest of the world who see it as merely a European conflict and a new proof of Western hypocrisy, based on "double standards." The spontaneous solidarity of Western countries with Israel after the massacres of October 7 exacerbated this fracture, especially when the Israeli army imposed a blockade on Gaza, ordered the evacuation of its population from the north to the south, and began bombing the territory. On October 19, President Joe Biden linked, in a national address, the crises in Ukraine and Israel to request Congress to release $100 billion (approximately 94 billion euros) in emergency funds to help these two countries. Establishing this link was aimed at obtaining the overall agreement of the Republicans, who were reluctant to help Ukraine but very focused on Israel. However, this link is a double-edged sword. The next day, the American president received the leaders of the European Union (EU) at the White House. At the end of the meeting, Josep Borrell, who heads EU diplomacy, expressed his concern about the rise of anti-Western anger, observed by EU embassies in Southern countries since October 7 and specifically fueled by the accusation of "double standards": how, ask these critics, can one claim to rally global opinion against Russia's violations of international law in Ukraine, and then support Israel bombing Gaza? Vladimir Putin's dream is for this anger to spread to the very heart of multiethnic European societies. Overturning the "colonial narrative" It can always be argued that the invasion of Ukraine was not triggered by the massacre of 1,400 civilians or accompanied by hostage-taking. Joe Biden can always, to warn Israeli leaders, acknowledge the mistakes that the spirit of vengeance led his country to commit after 9/11. All in vain, resentment overwhelms the facts. And when the United States, alone, veto a Brazilian resolution in favor of a ceasefire in Gaza at the UN Security Council, the confusion is total: until now, the veto technique was a Russian specialty. "All of this is going to complicate our task considerably," laments a diplomat in Brussels, where commendable efforts have been made for months to "not lose the global South." In the Kremlin, Vladimir Putin is rubbing his hands together. He does so even more because the outbreak of violence between Israel and Hamas allows the "colonial narrative" that Ukraine is trying to associate with Russia, a fragmented empire seizing one of its former possessions to recolonize it, to be overturned. Because when Israeli settlers forcibly evict Palestinian Bedouins from the hills of the West Bank, when more than 90 Palestinians have been killed since October 7 in these territories, in the battle of narratives, the colonial argument shifts to the other side. Not everything is going as well for Vladimir Putin. By refusing to condemn Hamas for the massacres of October 7 – Hamas leaders he has met twice in Moscow since the beginning of the war in Ukraine – he has abandoned his balanced stance with Israel. Returning to the region since its intervention in Syria in 2015, Russia has taken advantage of American withdrawal. But if the new escalation of tensions in the Middle East leads the United States to reinvest there, Moscow will struggle to maintain its position on this front while waging war in Ukraine. Xi Jinping, the Chinese president, is more cautious. He is keeping a military distance from this explosive region, while congratulating himself that it is keeping the Americans occupied.
lead: The Russian president plans to take advantage of the crisis in the Middle East, which diverts attention from the war in Ukraine and fuels resentment against the "collective West." This is a danger that Europeans are concerned about, as noted by Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at "Le Monde," in her column.

title: Comment Benyamin Nétanyahou est devenu le leader autoproclamé du « monde civilisé »
text: In the beginning was Jonathan. Benyamin Netanyahu's older brother was indeed killed in 1976 while leading an Israeli hostage rescue raid at the Ugandan airport of Entebbe. An institute named after Jonathan was founded in his memory three years later in Jerusalem by Benyamin and his father, Benzion Netanyahu, an ideologue of the Irgun (literally "The Organization," responsible for numerous attacks in Palestine from 1937 to 1948, including massacres of Arab civilians). This institute identifies terrorism, naturally Arab, as the ultimate evil and associates it with the Soviet threat, against which Netanyahu proposes "an anti-terror alliance of all Western democracies." Such ideas were adopted by Ronald Reagan and his administration from 1981 to 1989 in the name of a "new Cold War" against the "evil empire" of the USSR, to which, in this binary vision, the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) of Yasser Arafat would be affiliated. From Beirut to Baghdad, François Mitterrand, during the first state visit of a French president to Israel in 1982, mentioned both the "fighters" of the PLO and the prospect of a Palestinian state. Shortly after, during the Israeli invasion of Lebanon, he did everything to prevent the long siege of West Beirut from turning into street battles, which Israel would have undoubtedly won, but at an exorbitant cost. France, on the contrary, supervised the evacuation of Arafat and his fedayeen, convinced that it would preserve an interlocutor for Israel when the time came. However, it was only after the PLO recognized Israel that Arafat was received by Mitterrand at the Elysée in 1989. Four years later, Arafat signed the Oslo peace accords with Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, which Netanyahu, now the opposition leader, condemned as an intolerable concession to "terror." Regardless of the disappearance of the USSR, he stigmatized the PLO as the head of a terrorist hydra to be fought relentlessly. Netanyahu appeared at rallies where slogans like "Rabin, Arafat's dog" and even "Death to Rabin" were chanted. When Rabin was assassinated in 1995, albeit by a terrorist, but a Jewish and Israeli one, Netanyahu believed his political career was over. However, he quickly resumed hammering the mantra of "terror," inevitably Arab and anti-Western, managing to narrowly win the election in 1996 to head the government. During his first three years in power, he systematically undermined the Oslo accords. As a mere member of parliament again, he saw the rhetoric of the Jonathan Institute triumph with the attacks of September 11, 2001, and George W. Bush's "global war on terror." In 2002, Netanyahu mobilized in Washington to equate the PLO with Al-Qaeda while contributing to the misinformation campaign about weapons of mass destruction in Iraq. Jacques Chirac then denounces the blindness of an offensive to overthrow Saddam Hussein that would not prepare for "the day after." France thus avoids the disastrous invasion of Iraq leading to a globalized confrontation between Islam and the West. As for Netanyahu, in 2006, he prefers to celebrate the sixtieth anniversary of the Irgun bombing at the King David Hotel in Jerusalem, which killed ninety-one people, including forty-one Arabs, twenty-eight British, and seventeen Jews. The plaque placed on this occasion is probably the only one in the world to honor the perpetrators of a bombing rather than their victims. The Gaza abyss Nicolas Sarkozy organizes an international conference in Paris in 2007 to finance the Palestinian state, before stating that "Gaza cannot continue to be the largest open-air prison in the world." In 2011, during a private conversation with the American president, Barack Obama, he even calls Netanyahu a "liar," who had returned to power two years earlier. It is true that the Israeli prime minister continues to invoke the terrorist threat, not only to reinforce the blockade of Gaza but also to discredit Mahmoud Abbas, Arafat's successor, and thus continue the colonization of the West Bank. France, hit in January 2015 by a series of jihadist attacks, still refuses to make the same connections as Netanyahu. While the Israeli prime minister attends the solidarity march on January 11, 2015, François Hollande reacts by also inviting the Palestinian president. Netanyahu declares on this occasion "to all the Jews of France" that "Israel is your home," forcing Prime Minister Manuel Valls to solemnly remind that "France without the Jews of France is no longer France." Ousted from power in 2021 after four elections in two years, Netanyahu returns as prime minister in December 2022, with the support of the supremacist far-right, to whom he entrusts key ministerial positions. Still facing triple charges of corruption, fraud, and breach of trust, he launches a judicial reform, described as a "coup" by his opponents, who take to the streets in protest by the hundreds of thousands. Washington and Berlin have harsh words to denounce this attempt to undermine the judiciary's checks and balances. However, the horror of the terrorist campaign on October 7 transforms Netanyahu into a self-proclaimed leader of the "civilized world" against a Hamas equated with the Islamic State organization. As he evades his heavy responsibility in the initial collapse of Israel's security and as the families of hostages accuse him of not giving top priority to the release of their loved ones, Benjamin Netanyahu recycles his slogans from the Jonathan Institute to impose the sole goal of eradicating Hamas, without having any more vision of the "day after" in Gaza than the Bush administration had in Iraq in 2003. Western leaders who succeed each other in Israel to show active solidarity with Netanyahu should nevertheless remember that the true friends of the Israeli prime minister are, and have long been, Donald Trump, Viktor Orban, and, of course, Vladimir Putin, the clear winner at this stage of the crisis in Gaza. It is, in fact, to appease the master of the Kremlin that Netanyahu refused the solidarity visit of Volodymyr Zelensky to Israel. This obviously does not bother the leaders of the "civilized world," who are ready to fall into the trap set by Hamas in Gaza with Benjamin Netanyahu.
lead: The "war on terrorism," which Netanyahu has tirelessly preached for four decades, is imposing its ideological mechanisms on Western democracies.

title: Comment Emmanuel Macron et Angela Merkel se sont leurrés sur Vladimir Poutine : lisez les extraits des « Aveuglés », de Sylvie Kauffmann
text: By deciding to invade Ukraine on February 24, 2022, Vladimir Putin shatters the European order. He also forces the French and Germans to question themselves: why did their leaders make such a serious mistake regarding the Russian head of state? To answer this question, Sylvie Kauffmann, in her investigation "The Blinded. How Berlin and Paris cleared the way for Russia" (Stock), goes back to the very beginning of Emmanuel Macron's first term in 2017. At that time, this young president with assertive diplomatic ambitions was convinced that he could "anchor Russia to Europe." Excerpt from the book.] Putin and us Vladimir Putin knows: since General de Gaulle, who equipped it with nuclear weapons, France sees itself as a "power endowed," one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council, which can therefore negotiate on an equal footing with other powers. This is the big difference with Germany, whose power is primarily economic. With the end of the Cold War and the disappearance of the Soviet Union, the structures on which the organization of security in Europe was based are disappearing one after the other. A new era has begun. Every French president aspires to establish "a new security architecture in Europe." Emmanuel Macron cannot miss the call. To this ambition, he adds another openly declared one, that of making Europe a geopolitical actor. For him, the time when the United States and the USSR, or now Russia, managed Europe's security over the heads of Europeans is over. Europe must be at the negotiation table. "Engaging Russia" is necessary. Just three weeks after his election, the young French president receives [his Russian counterpart] Vladimir Putin at Versailles on May 29, 2017. (...) The context is delicate: there is Ukraine, of course, but also Syria, where Russian aviation bombs hospitals and other civilian targets under the guise of the fight against terrorism. The French, engaged in the fight against Daesh [the Arabic acronym for the Islamic State organization], note that the Russian forces do not at all keep their word in targeting Daesh structures. Moscow's priority, and it will be successful, is the return of Russia to the region and the consolidation of the Bashar al-Assad regime. (...) There is also the thorny issue of Russian intelligence services interfering in the French electoral campaign. Burned by the unprecedented Russian interference that disrupted the American presidential campaign in 2016, the French took preventive measures; this did not prevent the hacking of Macron's campaign headquarters' email accounts and disinformation operations about his private life. But Macron wants to reconnect and uses the exhibition on Peter the Great at the Grand Trianon in Versailles as a pretext to invite Putin. All the splendors of the Republic and the monarchy are on display. At the press conference following the meeting, Macron's intended approach with Moscow is evident: dialogue with firmness. (...) The tone is appreciated by critics of Putin. The dialogue continues in the following months, over the phone or at G20 summits. According to a witness of these exchanges, Macron "feels a connection with Putin." In 2018, he attends the St. Petersburg Economic Forum, Putin's grand economic showcase, where contracts are signed with the CAC 40 and business is booming. The French President praises what he believes is the "historical and strategic vision" that the Russian President has of the relationship with Europe. Macron is convinced that "Russia must be anchored to Europe." In reality, the desire for dialogue takes precedence over the demand for firmness. Macron cannot resist the temptation to maintain his trip to Russia for the semi-final and final of the 2018 FIFA World Cup – won by France – in July 2018, despite tensions over the Skripal case, a former double agent who was a victim of a poisoning attempt, along with his daughter, in the UK. Relations with Putin are cordial, and the two men address each other informally. Macron devises a more ambitious plan: transitioning from bilateral to "strategic" on a European scale. Is this ambition too much? Or is it a methodological error, with this solitary exercise of power that often characterizes the President's initiatives? Macron decides to launch his own reset, somewhat grandiosely named the "Confidence and Security Initiative." The kickoff is an invitation extended to Vladimir Putin for talks at Fort de Brégançon on the French Riviera on August 19, 2019, a few days before a G7 summit hosted by France in Biarritz. (...) The August sun beats down fiercely on the two delegations seated on the terrace. Everyone is sweating both literally and figuratively, and when they finally sit down for dinner at 11 p.m., they can breathe a little easier. Nothing fruitful comes out of these hours of discussions, except the idea of a new summit in the "Normandy format" on the issue of Donbass, bringing together Macron, Angela Merkel, Putin, and the newcomer in Kiev, President Volodymyr Zelensky, who has just been elected and whom the Russian President dismisses as a clown because he is an actor. Undeterred, Macron asserts his determination to "build a new architecture of security and trust between the European Union and Russia" in this "historic moment" of the "recomposition of the international order." There are two problems with this Brégançon initiative. The first is that it goes completely against the internal dynamics consistently observed in Moscow, which are repressive, expansionist, and revisionist. The Russian paramilitary group Wagner has already set up camp in the Central African Republic to push out the French; on several occasions in recent months, when Macron questions Putin about this issue, Putin responds evasively, claiming not to be aware and saying he will inquire further. The second issue is that the Elysée Palace carried out this operation alone, without involving any of its European partners, while presenting it as a European initiative. Putin suspects this, as he asks Macron, "Will you be able to convince the other Europeans?" Macron has made his personal capacity for charm and persuasion an essential tool of his diplomacy, although this tactic failed with Trump a few months earlier in Washington. Why bother with twenty-six partners when you are the best? Even Berlin was kept out of the loop. Naturally, the Germans are furious. "We were a bit surprised, even shocked," admits a senior German diplomat in a tone that hints at understatement. The phone lines between Berlin and Paris are busy in the days that follow, with "very frank and direct" conversations between people who are used to working together. On the phone, French diplomats loyally defend the Elysée's initiative, but their counterparts in Berlin sense "a certain unease." Some of these diplomats from the French Foreign Ministry had strongly suggested to the Elysée to at least involve Berlin, but they were rebuffed: "That is not what the president wants." The Germans are angry because they fear that this way of decoupling European security from American security will give Putin the opportunity to divide the Alliance. And they are angry because they suspect that the Eastern Europeans will take it very badly. In reality, Berlin shares the essence of Macron's approach to Russia, which, "whether we like it or not, will not disappear from the map," and with which a modus vivendi must be found. But the Germans find the method "so clumsy" that it risks, they say, compromising everything. They are even more offended because their partners in Central and Eastern Europe do not for a second believe that Berlin was not aware! Wary, the Poles and the Balts suspect a new Franco-German maneuver behind their backs, staged through a role-playing scenario in which Paris would pretend to take the lead while Berlin would be pulling the strings with Moscow. Macron assigns Pierre Vimont, a seasoned diplomat and specialist in delicate missions to the point that he is nicknamed the "Swiss Army knife of the French Foreign Ministry," to go heal wounds and sell his initiative in the chancelleries. The Germans welcome him kindly, but their verdict on the French reset is already in: it will not work. The Baltic and Central European countries are not showing a better disposition. "There were too many doubts about our intentions in the Franco-Russian dialogue," acknowledges a diplomat involved in this operation today. "We took our partners' trust for granted too much, and assumed that their security interests aligned with ours." In passing, Emmanuel Macron also irks another category of partners: his own diplomatic corps. The skepticism of a part of the Quai d'Orsay towards his initiative reached him. At the end of August, during the annual meeting of ambassadors, he scolds them at the Elysée like a schoolmaster reprimanding his students. He suspects a "deep state" of seeking to "undermine the presidential word" and, essentially, sabotage his project. "I know that many of you have been trained to be distrustful of Russia," he asserts. "In general, on this subject, we listen to the president and proceed as usual. I strongly advise you not to follow this path." Mistake. "I found it deeply unfair," testifies one of the diplomats present, summarizing the general feeling. "At the Quai, one can have their own ideas, but we are loyal." The accusation deepens the gap between the Elysée and the Quai d'Orsay, a structural problem of the Fifth Republic. Especially since the works on the "architecture of trust and security" are struggling to get started on the Russian side. Jean-Yves Le Drian, Minister of Foreign Affairs, and Florence Parly, Minister of the Armed Forces, will act as scouts. They fly to Moscow to restart the "2 + 2" consultations with their Russian counterparts, which had been interrupted since [the annexation, in 2014, of] Crimea. A way, they think, to test Moscow's goodwill. Their political directors accompany the ministers, and the delegation is substantial. "Look, the deep state descending from the plane," a witness quips. Things seem to be going rather well at first. After the two bilateral meetings, everyone gathers for lunch at an official guesthouse. "A Russian-style lunch," notes a French participant: vodka flows freely, toasts are made against the backdrop of imperial hunting scenes hanging on the walls. They are all there, Sergey Lavrov, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Sergey Shoigu, the Defense Minister, Igor Kostyukov, the head of the GRU, the military intelligence... top-notch. The French present several concrete cooperation proposals – there is no shortage of "deconfliction" topics. The Russians had hinted that the two French ministers would be received by Putin in the afternoon, but the meeting does not take place: this is a first sign that alerts the French. They have dinner in the evening at the embassy with representatives of Russian civil society, journalists, historians from the Memorial association, whose courage impresses them; they return to Paris the next day. In the following weeks, they regularly follow up with their official contacts in Moscow. "But the Russians don't engage. We proposed dates, but they didn't follow up." Months go by... and nothing happens. "There was the decorum, but nothing behind it." A wasted effort. (...) Zelensky flips the table, but Macron still manages to secure Putin's agreement to come to Paris on December 10, 2019, for a "Normandy summit," as outlined in the Minsk agreements on the Donbass. It is the first time the Russian president meets the new Ukrainian president, who was elected eight months earlier – and it will be the only time. The Germans believe that this framework, with Merkel and Macron, is more suitable for this first meeting: "Knowing Putin a little bit, we thought he would eat Zelensky for breakfast if we left them alone!" The preparation for this summit costs the diplomats involved endless hours of laborious negotiations, but when the time comes, something unexpected happens at the Elysée Palace: Zelensky flips the table – figuratively. He questions the agreements that, when signed in 2015, were much more favorable to Russia than to Ukraine since the Ukrainian army was in a weak position. However, since then, it has strengthened, notably thanks to the training its soldiers received from the British and Americans. The former comedian no longer wants to be walked over. "I see the text very well," he declares, "but if we continue like this, it will take decades. And I don't have the luxury of time." The meeting is disrupted. Putin is furious, but he doesn't show it. The session leads to interesting exchanges between the Russian president and the German chancellor, who have known each other for twelve years now. "Listen, I'll tell you the truth," Putin begins. "But Vladimir, I hope you always tell the truth!" Angela Merkel interrupts, feigning innocence. "Angela," he retorts. "Everyone lies, I lie, you lie, Emmanuel lies, even Mr. Zelensky will lie, it's normal!" Sergey Lavrov, who had probably assured his president that everything was settled, becomes very nervous as he sees the negotiation falling apart. He gets up, starts running back and forth. "He wanted to negotiate amendments to the text that had been previously agreed upon with us," says a participant, amused. "In short, it was a bit chaotic." At the end of a long day, the differences between Zelensky and Putin become quite clear when the four leaders meet the press. They have at least reached an agreement on a prisoner exchange, which will take place, and promise to meet again in the same format in Berlin four months later. In the meantime, their ministers and advisors are supposed to iron out the difficulties. There will be no new summit, neither in Berlin nor elsewhere. In March 2020, Covid-19 appears in Europe and Putin isolates himself in the Kremlin, in Sochi, or in another one of his residences. The dream of the Russian president to host Trump, Macron, Merkel, and other world leaders on May 9 for the 75th anniversary of the Soviet victory in the "Great Patriotic War" collapses. The parade on Red Square is canceled. (...) In reality, this summit in December 2019 in Paris probably produced an unexpected result: Putin became aware of the political dimension of the character Zelensky. He understood that he could not "eat him for breakfast" as the Germans feared. Macron had played this point quite well. On the advice of the French ambassador to Ukraine, Isabelle Dumont, who had watched all the episodes of the TV series "Servant of the People" in which the actor Volodymyr Zelensky played the role of a teacher who became president despite himself, and then followed, fascinated, his election campaign, the French president invited the iconoclastic candidate to the Elysée in 2019, even before he was elected. A personal relationship was thus able to be established at that time between the two presidents. It will allow to counterbalance, at least a little, the Putin card that Macron will play to the full, until the disaster of February 2022. Merkel bites the dust. Some believe that it is from this Paris summit that Putin begins to conceive the large-scale invasion of Ukraine. His resentment towards the European turn of the Ukrainians and their elites is exacerbated by the isolation imposed by the pandemic, which he applies in a particularly draconian manner. What information from the field reaches him? It is impossible to know for sure: the Kremlin is becoming increasingly opaque. On her part, Angela Merkel is worried. The arrest and imprisonment of the Russian opposition figure Alexei Navalny upon his plane's descent in Moscow in January 2021, after his stay in Germany where he was treated for his Novichok poisoning, is a very bad sign that reinforces her concern. In Russia, the grip tightens further around NGOs, which are increasingly repressed. In May, three German NGOs were banned from Russia, leading to a freeze of the "St. Petersburg Dialogue," a structure formed in 2001 to promote exchanges between civil societies of the two countries. The chancellor expresses her concern in informal conversations with other leaders on the sidelines of the G7 in Carbis Bay, Cornwall, from June 11 to 13, 2021. She has a premonition. She argues that we must maintain contact with Moscow, as during the Cold War. A few days after the G7 summit, on June 16, 2021, President Joe Biden leaves his European friends behind and flies to Geneva to meet Vladimir Putin. He did not involve any of them in this meeting on European soil. From their respective capitals, Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel observe this summit with a hint of bitterness: when they talk to Putin, their Central European partners cry betrayal, but when the President of the United States does it, no one says anything. On June 18, Macron visits Merkel in Berlin. It is the first time in a year that the Chancellor receives him at her home, as the pandemic has prevented physical exchanges; everyone is still wearing masks. Before their meetings with the press, Merkel explains that they will prepare for the European summit on June 24 and 25, a week later; she explicitly states that Russia will be discussed and that she is in favor of contacts with Moscow, beyond the very limited ones that exist within the framework of the Minsk agreements. No one pays attention to this statement. However, the Chancellor has an idea in mind, which she submits to President Macron during a long discussion that evening, in order to turn it into a Franco-German idea. This idea is to invite Putin, for the first time since the annexation of Crimea, to a Russia-European Union summit, with the other twenty-five leaders of the member states. Paris finds the option of a 27 + 1 summit very generous for a Putin who has not moved since 2014, and believes that a summit with only the presidents of the European institutions would suffice. But Macron can only approve of the idea of reopening dialogue with Moscow: he has been trying in vain since 2019. And he will not contradict Angela so close to her departure... In the aftermath, Merkel calls Vladimir Putin and also Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. On June 22, Putin signs a gentle article in Die Zeit, calling for the "restoration of a full partnership with Europe." Merkel just forgets to mention what has become a "Franco-German project" to the other Europeans, who discover it, furious, on the eve of the European Council. Because in all the preparatory meetings for the European Council that Charles Michel has organized, as he does before every summit, this idea has never been raised. The President of the European Council attaches great importance to this preparatory work, knowing how much the Chancellor dislikes improvisation. The European Council is the body of the Union that brings together the twenty-seven heads of state and government. The discussion is very open, everyone gives their opinion before moving on to the vote; the preparatory work carried out by the president and his team with the Member States is therefore crucial to defuse possible conflicts and ensure the smooth running of the debates. This June Council meeting was no exception; a final video conference meeting with Ursula von der Leyen [President of the European Commission], Angela Merkel, and Emmanuel Macron had helped to iron out the details. Everything seemed in order. Charles Michel was calm. However, two days before the summit, during a final video conference, Merkel surprised everyone by announcing that Macron and she would propose a summit with Russia. This was the result of the meeting on June 18 in Berlin. The Council presidency tried to defuse the situation, fearing the reaction of the Member States. "Suddenly telling the Baltic States, 'Angela and Emmanuel are proposing to invite Putin for a meeting at the European Council,' is not going to work, they are not at all prepared," European officials explained to Merkel's advisors. In vain. The Germans persisted. This was the Chancellor's last Council meeting before the federal elections in September, which would see her step down after sixteen years of not only ruling Germany but also the European Council. She wanted to push through. The day before the summit, an article in the Financial Times revealed Merkel and Macron's initiative regarding Russia. The leaders of the Twenty-Seven arrived in Brussels, some feeling tense. The meeting began, behind closed doors as usual, in a tense atmosphere. Merkel spoke first. She recounted the history of relations between Russia and the EU. She said that for sixteen years, she had been observing Putin and seeing his regime harden. He thought he could consolidate his power by relying on the economy, but he realized he knew nothing about the economy and fell back on his fundamentals: security and intelligence services. "He sees us as enemies," she said. "I have no illusions, but we must talk to him. The Americans do it. We are the target of destabilization maneuvers and we are depriving ourselves of this tool." Macron then took over with the familiar argument: we must talk to Putin, "we cannot just be passive spectators of American-Russian dialogue when we are the most affected." (...) Italian Prime Minister Mario Draghi then spoke, but much more modestly. He simply said that he supported the initiative. Dutch Prime Minister Mark Rutte said he was not opposed to such a summit but that he himself would not participate. Since the tragedy of flight MH17 and its 298 deaths, more than half of whom were Dutch, the Netherlands have been deeply affected by the relationship with Russia. Silence. Embarrassed, the officials of the Council discover that "M&M", Merkel and Macron, want to sneak in a line about this EU-Russia summit into the final statement, which was not mentioned during the prior consultations. That's when Kaja Kallas steps in. The Estonian Prime Minister, who just celebrated her 44th birthday, has only been in power for five months; this is her first in-person Council meeting, as everything was done virtually until now. She may not yet be the rising star of the new Europe, but she has the audacity; her experience as a lawyer has accustomed her to supporting her arguments, which she formulates in perfect English. Blonde, slender, she does not have the modesty in dress of the previous generation of female leaders; Kaja Kallas confidently embraces a very feminine elegance. All eyes turn to the Estonian, who confronts Merkel head-on. "A summit about what," she asks. "For what purpose?" When, in May 2023, two years later, I ask Kaja Kallas at her home in Tallinn what memories she has of the June 2021 Council, her eyes immediately light up: "Oh, I have many memories! It's a Council I will never forget!" Because she delivered a devastating critique of the Franco-German proposal. "She tore it to shreds," says a witness, "the tone was very harsh. That's a bit of her temperament." "I just said it wasn't right," she recounts, falsely modest, "and I argued." The knockout argument: "We always said we wouldn't have a summit with Putin until he returned Crimea. But the conditions are not met, so what will we look like? It will mean our word is worth nothing!" Merkel takes this challenge very poorly. Contrary to her usual demeanor, according to several witnesses, she gets angry, loses her temper, "turns all red." The discussion continues but Kaja Kallas does not back down. A participant describes "a groundswell, both in form and substance"; "it was quite painful." Viktor Orban, the Hungarian, known for his pro-Putin stance, revisits Merkel's argument that Putin knows nothing about the economy: "It's true, he's not an economist," he says. "But he perfectly understands what controlling the production of raw materials means from a strategic point of view." This dig at Germany, alluding to Berlin's dependence on Russian gas, probably does nothing to improve the chancellor's mood. Finally, Kaja Kallas recounts, Mario Draghi says, "She's right," turning to her, and the proposal is abandoned. "You must have been quite a lawyer!" he whispers to her after the meeting... However, as she leaves the room, the young Prime Minister is horrified by what she has just done: "I thought I had just shattered my country's relations with Germany!" The usual press conference of the Presidents of the Council and the Commission has been postponed, without explanation. Merkel still makes it known that there is no agreement for a direct meeting between European and Russian leaders. "Personally, I would have liked a bolder step forward," she acknowledges. But no one suspects the violence of the confrontation that has just taken place in the Council. The next day, to her great surprise, Kaja Kallas' colleagues pat her on the back to congratulate her. Her surprise grows when she later receives a phone call from Angela Merkel, who, being a great lady, wants to apologize. Kaja Kallas also apologizes for losing her temper. The two women will have lunch together to clear up any misunderstandings. Kaja Kallas is unaware, but the Chancellor also calls the other two Baltic leaders and tells them she regrets launching her initiative without consulting them. That's Merkel for you! In the heat of the moment, at the end of the second day of the Council, the Chancellor still admits to being "saddened" by the experience, which, she says, shows that the Member States "do not have enough trust in each other." A lucid and meaningful comment. In reality, the episode almost alone symbolizes the shift within the EU and the revenge of the "lands of blood." The countries of the former communist Europe had become accustomed to not raising their voices. That day, June 24, 2021, "Merkel bit the dust and Kallas defeated her," one of the participants summarizes. For her last European summit, the Chancellor of the most powerful country in the Union was thwarted by a rebellion led by the young head of government of a country of one and a half million inhabitants, on the eastern fringes of the Union. And the discord was, of course, about Russia. Doubts about the Franco-German tandem's Russian policy were growing among the countries of Central and Eastern Europe. This hastily prepared move behind their backs was the last straw. How could Merkel shoot herself in the foot like this, she who is usually so cautious and organized? Was it the prospect of her departure from the chancellery that prompted her to rush things? She had already stumbled six months earlier by pushing through a highly contested agreement on investments with China at the end of Germany's EU presidency, sealed during a video conference summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping on December 30, 2020. Bad move: relations with Beijing deteriorated so much afterwards that the agreement was suspended before even coming into effect. Perhaps the Chancellor was dreaming of leaving power on a triple geopolitical success, the Euro-Chinese summit with Xi in December 2020, a summit with Joe Biden scheduled for July 2021, and ending on a high note with a Euro-Russian summit with Putin in September. Instead of a triple success, she ultimately exits the European stage with a double failure. Sign of the times, it concerns China and Russia. (…) On August 22, the chancellor is in Kiev to bid farewell to Volodymyr Zelensky. Her heart is not in it. Ukrainians celebrate their 30th anniversary of independence two days later; she preferred to come before. They would have wanted her to stay to participate in an international conference on Crimea; she chooses to slip away. The exchanges are polite, but Zelensky cannot help but twist the knife in the wound of Nord Stream 2. Until the last day, the gas pipeline will have poisoned Angela Merkel's policy in the East. The horizon darkens more and more. The chancellor leaves Kiev without having succeeded in reassuring Ukraine.
lead: In a book to be released on Wednesday, October 18th by Stock, the editorialist at "Le Monde" decrypts, through testimonies and key episodes, what prevented Europeans from perceiving the reality of Vladimir Putin's Russia. We are publishing extensive excerpts focused on the period leading up to the invasion of Ukraine.

title: Vladimir Poutine acte la sortie de la Russie du traité interdisant les essais nucléaires
text: Russian President Vladimir Putin signed on Thursday, November 2, the law on the withdrawal of the ratification of the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT), against the backdrop of the conflict in Ukraine and tensions with Western countries. The CTBT was opened for signature in 1996 but never entered into force because it was not ratified – a necessary step for its entry into force – by a sufficient number of states among the forty-four countries that possessed nuclear facilities at the time of its creation. In early October, Mr. Putin had announced that Russia could withdraw its ratification of the CTBT in response to the United States' failure to ratify it. "I am not ready to say whether we should resume testing or not," added the Russian president, while touting the development of new powerful missiles capable of carrying nuclear warheads. "Odious attitude of the United States" Since the start of the conflict in Ukraine in February 2022, the Russian president has sent mixed signals regarding the use of nuclear weapons, deploying tactical nuclear weapons in Belarus, its closest ally, during the summer of 2023. The law on the withdrawal of the CTBT ratification was first adopted by the State Duma, the lower house of the Russian Parliament, before being unanimously approved by the upper house at the end of October. The President of the State Duma, Vyacheslav Volodin, described this vote as a "response to the odious attitude of the United States towards their obligations on maintaining global security." In late October, Russia also conducted test launches of ballistic missiles to prepare its forces for a "massive nuclear strike" in retaliation. Russian nuclear doctrine envisions a "strictly defensive" use of nuclear weapons in response to an attack on Russia with weapons of mass destruction or in the event of aggression with conventional weapons "threatening the very existence of the state." In February, Russia also suspended its participation in the New START nuclear disarmament treaty signed with the United States in 2010, the last bilateral agreement between Russians and Americans.
lead: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty was signed in 1996 but was never ratified due to a lack of sufficient states coming together. The United States has never ratified it.

title: « Entre la Chine et la Russie, les relations ont toujours été marquées par une profonde défiance »
text: Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping like to describe the relationship between their two countries as a "comprehensive strategic partnership for a new era, not excluding any area of cooperation." The closeness between the two leaders established through more than forty meetings, their strong ideological and political connivance, anti-American and anti-Western solidarity, as well as the good performance of economic relations could perfectly justify this designation. However, these relations have never been completely peaceful throughout their long history. Since the very first contacts established in the 17th century, periods of open or silent hostility have always been longer than periods of friendship and brotherhood. And in all cases, a deep distrust has always marked their relations. The first period of open hostility lasted more than fifty years, between 1860 and 1911, the year of the fall of the Qing dynasty. The Russian Empire had actively participated in the "break up of China," the rush towards China, along with the European colonial powers and Japan. St. Petersburg had indeed been the biggest beneficiary by seizing, under the "unequal treaties," nearly 2 million square kilometers. The memory of these spoliations, even if put aside by the current Chinese authority, is still present in the memory of the Chinese. The second period of frank hostility took place between the 1960s and 1980s, for more than twenty years, when relations between the two communist powers deteriorated to the point of bringing them to the brink of nuclear war. The main reason was Mao's claim to wrest the leadership of the international communist movement from the Soviet leaders who succeeded Stalin. On the other hand, it is possible to describe as "bittersweet" the period beginning with the Bolshevik revolution of 1917 - "the volcanic event that illuminated the consciousness of the Chinese elite," according to François Fejtö, the eminent historian of the international communist movement, and ending with the founding of the People's Republic of China (PRC) by Mao in 1949. Certainly, the Soviet Union of Lenin and Stalin had strongly supported the Chinese "anti-imperialist" movement throughout these thirty-two years. Not wanting to "put all its eggs in one basket," Moscow had then supported, either alternately or simultaneously, Mao Zedong's Chinese Communist Party and Chiang Kai-shek's Guomindang. However, its support, never disinterested, had always been in the service of Soviet interests, notably through the Comintern. Above all, the USSR, which had officially renounced the privileges acquired in China by the Romanov Empire, was careful not to return the spoliated territories. This ambivalent policy came to an end with the victory of the Communists in 1949. As for the periods of friendship and brotherhood, the first one began with the founding of the People's Republic of China in 1949 and the signing of the Treaty of Friendship, Alliance, and Mutual Assistance in February 1950, and ended with Nikita Khrushchev in the early 1960s. The new China, exhausted after several decades of Japanese invasion and civil war, then had a vital need for economic and military assistance from the "big Soviet brother" to recover from its ruins and fully adopted its development model as well as its ideological and political authority. However, the onerous conditions demanded by Stalin reinforced the old distrust existing between the two countries and leaders. Stalin distrusted Mao, suspected him of wanting to be "another Tito," and called him a "margarine communist." Mao had to swallow the dictates of the Soviet dictator and did not forget it upon his death. The troublemaker from Moscow. The second prosperous period began in the late 1980s with the rise to power in Moscow and Beijing of two pragmatic leaders, Mikhail Gorbachev and Deng Xiaoping. Their policy of normalizing bilateral relations was further amplified by their successors, especially in the 2000s by Vladimir Putin, who called for "the sails of the Russian economy to benefit from the Chinese wind," and by Xi Jinping. However, very quickly, Sino-Russian relations became very unbalanced in favor of Beijing. Currently, China's Gross National Product (GNP) is six times higher than that of Russia, which holds only a minimal share of the Chinese market. China is Russia's top trading partner, but even though significant in absolute numbers, trade between the two countries is six times less than that between China and the United States.
