Weakened Russia is gradually losing ground in its former Soviet "backyard" in Central Asia and the Caucasus, where Beijing already holds a dominant economic and commercial position and is preparing to do the same politically and in terms of security. In global forums like the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) or the BRICS, Russia is no longer able to maintain its position against a dominant China. The Russian aggression against Ukraine has only exacerbated these asymmetries. During his visit to Beijing at the beginning of February 2022 for the Olympics, Vladimir Putin apparently failed to inform his Chinese counterpart of the imminent launch of the "special military operation." This went against the principles of international law that Beijing upholds, such as territorial integrity and non-interference in internal affairs. China has nevertheless adopted a "pro-Russian neutral" attitude, with Moscow remaining essential to Beijing by guaranteeing diplomatic support in its conflict with the United States, ensuring tranquility on their common border while Beijing is preoccupied with the Taiwan and South China Sea "fronts," as well as ensuring a steady supply of raw materials and energy at low costs. This does not mean that the Chinese, who fear secondary sanctions from Washington and want to maintain American and European markets, desire a total victory for the Russian army. Beijing refrains from providing lethal weapons to Russia and learns from its setbacks on the Ukrainian front, which could be useful in a potential conquest of Taiwan. Furthermore, China's ideological support for its Russian partner is far from flawless. While it echoes most of Russia's arguments justifying the aggression, Beijing allows Chinese researchers critical of the themes promoted in Moscow regarding the defense of "conservative values" and the alleged "messianic role" of Russia to express their views. For some of these thinkers, the Ukrainian adventure has significantly tarnished Russia's international image and diminished the importance of the Sino-Russian partnership. Concerned about its relations with the West and international stability, Beijing aims to maintain a certain distance from the disruptive behavior of Moscow. Russo-Chinese relations remain complicated, ambivalent, and marked by distrust. Historical scars related to territorial disputes that China does not rule out raising one day, as well as significant imbalances, have turned Russia into a "junior partner," or even a vassal, of China. XEMARTIN LABORDE, VICTORIA DENYS, AND DELPHINE PAPIN
lead: During the Cold War, the USSR was ahead of China in the fight against the capitalist bloc, recalls diplomat Pierre Andrieu. Today, Beijing is rivaling with Washington, leaving Moscow far behind in the competition of powers, and maintaining a certain distance from Russia since its invasion of Ukraine.

title: « Sans la poursuite durable de notre aide, l’armée ukrainienne ne pourra pas l’emporter »
text: Since October 7th, our eyes have been fixed on Israel and its response, as well as on the responses of the countries in the region, Europe, and the United States to the atrocities committed by Hamas. Legitimately, the Middle East concerns us. Gradually, we are talking less about Ukraine. However, the war being waged by Russia should still hold our attention. So far, Europeans and Americans have acted together to support Ukraine, without going to war. The Ukrainian army is holding its ground, and its resistance protects us from a Russian threat that concerns us all. But without the sustained continuation of our aid, Ukraine will not be able to prevail. Yet, this Western determination is now in jeopardy. The regime of Russian President Vladimir Putin, who met with Hamas and Iran in Moscow on October 26th, could be the geopolitical winner in a scenario where we look away. The Kremlin is counting on our weariness to prevail: let's not fall into this trap. In the United States, Trump supporters are blocking the functioning of Congress, whose approval is necessary to continue funding military aid to Kiev, a preview of what a new Donald Trump presidency could look like. The former Republican American president is now in a better position in the polls to win than in 2016. Among some Democrats, voices are also rising to consider that Ukraine is not a priority. It would be wrong not to take these signals seriously. Polite silence Europe may soon have to take over. France has long been calling for strengthening the strategic autonomy of the Old Continent. Europe must heed this call if it is to continue supporting Ukraine alone or almost alone for its own security in the future. After fully realizing the return of war to their soil and providing unprecedented military aid to Kiev, Europeans are getting tired. For months, a new tranche of funding for sending weapons to Ukraine has been blocked by only one country, Hungary. The rise to power of Robert Fico in Slovakia does not bode well on this issue. At the end of August, the EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs, Josep Borrell, proposed to the 27 member states to provide more visibility and a form of security guarantee to Ukraine by committing to a ceiling of 20 billion euros in military aid over four years. To date, his proposal has received little more than a polite silence. Certainly, Europe's military support is not only through the European Peace Facility mechanism overseen by Borrell. European countries that can do so provide additional bilateral aid of nearly equivalent amounts. In 2022, France had set up a support fund for Ukraine of 200 million euros, an innovative initiative that allows Kiev to directly purchase from our industries, but its funding has not yet been renewed. During a successful trip to Ukraine in September, the Minister of Armed Forces, Sébastien Lecornu, proposed institutionalizing this mechanism to replace direct sales, which are costly for our stocks. The examination of the budget for 2024 is an opportunity to renew it and it must absolutely be seized. Filling the gaps At the same time, our arms industries struggle to produce enough to meet the needs of an unprecedented conventional war. The tools put in place by the European Union, such as joint procurement or a munitions plan, are a good start but they are still very new and the amounts allocated to them are insufficient. Strengthening European defense requires a long-promised European defense investment plan in the medium term that is still awaited. This involves financial incentives that require a rethink of the mid-term European budget, as this budget was adopted before the return of war to Europe. At the moment, Member States show little sign that they are ready to embark on this revision, yet it is essential. They also show reluctance to borrow jointly, as suggested by the Estonian Prime Minister, Kaja Kallas, during the European Council on February 9, 2023. The Twenty-Seven are divided but propose little. Furthermore, there are still gaps to be filled in the sanctions, both European and American, against Russia. Despite the arms embargo targeting Russia, Moscow continues to import essential components for its military equipment, purchased from Western companies through intermediaries and ending up in Russia. This is particularly the case with microprocessors. Europe has established a mechanism to combat sanctions evasion. However, companies are not attentive enough to the final destination of their production and should be more encouraged to be vigilant. It is urgent for us to wake up. From Jerusalem to Kiev, passing through Yerevan, the chaos of the world is at our doorstep. Let's not wait for this avalanche of crises to reach us before giving ourselves the means to protect the attacked democracies.
lead: As the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is on everyone's minds, Members of Parliament from Renaissance and Horizons, Benjamin Haddad and Nathalie Loiseau, call for increased military support for Ukraine in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: « Une tempête parfaite se profile sur la guerre en Ukraine : où que l’on regarde, les tendances négatives convergent »
text: If the West cannot win the war in Ukraine, then what war can it win? Good question! This is posed by the Ukrainian Foreign Minister, Dmytro Kuleba, in an interview with the German newspaper Die Welt, published on Friday, November 10. The interest of the question, in fact, lies not so much in the answer it should evoke, but in the fact that it is being asked today, after twenty months of war. It implies that the hypothesis of Ukraine's defeat, despite Western support, is not only considered but plausible. Destructive and deadly, this war, launched by Russia against Ukraine on February 24, 2022, is geographically confined, but it has challenged the continent's security order; the defense of the invaded country is therefore presented as existential by European leaders. However, the conflict is currently at an impasse and clouds are gathering, to the point that what Americans call a "perfect storm" is looming: a particularly serious crisis caused by the convergence of several unfavorable phenomena. Wherever one looks, indeed, the negative trends for Ukraine and its allies converge. On the military front, the situation is stagnant. In an interview with the British magazine The Economist that made a big impact, the chief of staff of the Ukrainian army, General Valeri Zaloujny, admitted that the counter-offensive led by his forces for the past five months "will not result in a beautiful and deep breakthrough." "As in the First World War," he says, "we have reached a technological level that leads us into a deadlock." He has lost hope of pushing Russia back by inflicting massive losses on them: "That was my mistake. Russia has lost 150,000 men. In any other country, this level of losses would have ended the war." Extensive Russian recruitment However, while Russia has apparently abandoned mobilizing civilians due to its unpopularity in September 2022, it manages to regenerate its forces by recruiting massively for its professional army and focuses the bulk of human effort on the war in Ukraine. "At this moment, Norway could take Murmansk with police forces because the north of Russia is so thinly populated," quips a Scandinavian expert. The size of the Ukrainian population, which is also suffering heavy losses, does not allow for recruitment on such a scale; there are even protests in Ukraine these days from soldiers' wives who have not had leave for eighteen months on the front lines. Compensating for the lack of numbers with the power of weaponry is another challenge, both in quantity and quality. All of Russia's industrial capabilities are being called upon to contribute to the defense industry to produce ammunition twenty-four hours a day. Russia has shifted to a war economy mode. In Europe, some have talked about it, but the actual implementation is still pending. The promising commitment to deliver one million units of ammunition to Ukraine by March 2024 has only materialized so far with the production of 300,000 units. The European Union's foreign policy chief, Josep Borrell, emphasizes that the European arms industry is indeed producing, but not enough and primarily for its export clients. Unlike the Kremlin's control over the Russian defense industry, the large private arms groups in Europe are not requisitioned. As for the "technological leap" that General Zaloujny is advocating for, it requires a strategic decision: since the start of the war, Western countries, preoccupied with avoiding an escalation that would draw them too far in, carefully calibrate each level of sophistication in the aid they belatedly provide to the Ukrainian army. Embarrassment for Western leaders On the diplomatic front, the outbreak of war between Israel and Hamas has diverted the attention of the United States, which was forced to reinvest in the Middle East after being compelled to do so in Europe due to Russian aggression. Ukraine fears being in competition with Israel for American arms and ammunition supply. The global public opinion mobilization against the treatment of the civilian population in Gaza by the scale of the Israeli response to Hamas's terrorist attacks on October 7 embarrasses Western leaders. Inevitably, Russia takes advantage of this embarrassment to discredit their support for Ukraine. Finally, the inefficiency of the decision made nearly a year ago by the G7 and the EU to cap the price of Russian oil transported by sea at $60 (55 euros) to reduce Russia's revenues is now evident. The circumvention of this sanction is so extensive that in October, Russia's oil revenues increased by a quarter compared to October 2022, thanks in particular to the organization of "ghost fleets" of tankers. Russian propagandists promote a triumphant narrative. Far from collapsing, they claim their economy is perfectly resilient. They exploit the differences between President Zelensky and his chief of staff. The Prigojine affair and the challenge posed to Putin by the former head of Wagner are just a bad memory, buried in the smoking ruins of his plane: Putin will be triumphantly reelected in March 2024. Who would doubt that? All that is missing is the return of Donald Trump to the White House to unleash the "perfect storm." The time has come for Europeans, instead of wringing their hands at this thought, to plug the leaks and seriously fortify their house, as they prepare to open it to Ukraine and Moldova: a few solid additional beams will not be too much.
lead: Militarily, diplomatically, and economically, Ukraine and its allies are facing a difficult phase that Russia is taking advantage of, analyzes Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at "Le Monde," in her column.

title: Protéger les infrastructures sous-marines, un casse-tête pour les pays de la mer Baltique
text: The information went almost unnoticed. However, the press conference held in Gothenburg, Sweden, on Tuesday, October 17, brought together two ministers, the Minister of Defense, Pal Jonson, and the Minister of Civil Defense, Carl-Oskar Bohlin, as well as the head of the Swedish Coast Guard, Lena Lindgren, and the Chief of the Navy, Rear Admiral Ewa Skoog Haslum, who came together to announce that damage had been detected on an undersea telecommunications cable connecting Sweden to Estonia. This discovery comes a week after Finnish and Estonian authorities revealed that the Balticconnector gas pipeline, as well as an undersea cable connecting the two countries, had been damaged on the night of October 7-8. Because the damage to the Swedish-Estonian cable was limited – "It is not a total break of the cable, but partial damage," Mr. Bohlin stated – it was not immediately detected. However, given the geographical proximity, Stockholm does not rule out a connection to the previous incidents. In Helsinki, the criminal police have opened an investigation for "aggravated sabotage." Some are already pointing fingers at Russia. "It is not surprising, given the current situation and the number of Russian ships in the area when the infrastructures were damaged," notes Ilmari Käihkö, an associate professor in war studies at the Swedish Defense University. "A show of force." Speaking on the sidelines of a summit in Bishkek, Kyrgyzstan, on October 13, Russian President Vladimir Putin denied his country's involvement, stating that he did not even know "that this gas pipeline [Balticconnector] existed" and that the damage could have been caused by an accident. "It could have been hit by some kind of hook," or "there may have been an earthquake," he suggested, arguing that the accusations against Russia "have only one purpose: to conceal the terrorist act committed by the West against the Nord Stream pipelines." The investigation into the sabotage of the two pipelines connecting Russia to Germany, pierced by violent explosions on September 26, 2022, is still ongoing. Recently, Swedish prosecutor Mats Ljungqvist indicated that he hopes to conclude it by the end of the year. This time, the impact of the damage is more limited. If the malicious act is confirmed, "the goal would be more to show strength and create insecurity, especially in the debate on Europe's energy supply," notes Hans Liwang, a researcher at the Swedish Defense University. On the Finnish side, it is not ruled out that this could be a reaction to the country's accession to NATO. Gathered on the island of Gotland on October 13, the heads of state and government of the British Joint Expeditionary Force (including the Netherlands, Nordic countries, and Baltic States) tasked the defense ministers of the ten countries with improving the protection of underwater infrastructure: "There are spaghetti-like cables, wires, and infrastructures on the seabed that are absolutely essential for data traffic and everything that is digitally controlled, so vulnerabilities are much greater today," noted Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson. In the fall of 2022, the shutdown of the Nord Stream gas pipelines caused a shockwave around the Baltic Sea, as well as in Norway, which had become Europe's top gas supplier following Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Professor at the Norwegian Naval College in Bergen, Stale Ulriksen was among those who regretted the country's lack of response, despite its intelligence services having warned as early as November 2020 about the risks of sabotage against offshore installations. One year later, he sees a change: "In two months, after Nord Stream, the 8,800 kilometers of Norwegian pipelines were inspected, thanks to the mobilization of the navy, coast guard, and industry, which has many underwater drones," he explains. The images showed that there were no explosives on the pipes. "They can also be used, the next time a portion of a pipeline is filmed, to make comparisons and detect any potential changes," says Stale Ulriksen. Operations "difficult to detect" Nevertheless, the protection of cables and pipelines is complicated, notes Patrik Hulterström, a specialist in naval and amphibious tactics at the Swedish Defense University: "There are a huge number of infrastructures on the seabed, connecting different countries over very long distances. For a malicious actor who would want to damage them, they are there permanently. However, the very nature of these kinds of operations makes them difficult to detect and attribute. It would therefore be necessary to have a very strong presence everywhere and at all times, which is impossible." Another difficulty is when damages are caused outside the territorial waters of a country - as is the case for all recent incidents. "It is much more difficult to conduct an investigation and the legal consequences are not as clear," notes Hans Liwang. As evidenced by Stale Ulriksen: "In 2021, two underwater cables were destroyed off the archipelago of Svalbard and the Lofoten Islands. The investigation determined that Russian trawlers were responsible, but there were no legal consequences."
lead: One year after the explosions on the Nord Stream gas pipelines and ten days after the rupture of an underwater cable and a leak in a pipeline between Estonia and Finland, Sweden has detected damage to a second cable.

title: L’Ukraine se prépare à affronter un nouvel hiver de guerre
text: Winter is gradually setting in, and Ukrainian authorities expect Russia to once again target its energy infrastructure to paralyze the country. Several nighttime attacks have already caused power outages since mid-September. However, so far, relatively few Russian missiles have targeted power plants and heating systems compared to the winter of 2022-2023, and Kiev fears that Moscow may be waiting for subzero temperatures, while preparing supplies, to strike these sites massively. "As winter approaches, Russian terrorists will try to cause more damage," warned Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on the X platform (formerly Twitter) after a night of Russian bombings in ten regions of the country on November 3. The previous year, hundreds of Russian missiles and Shahed kamikaze drones had hit critical infrastructure. "Nearly 60%" of Ukraine's electricity production capacity had been damaged, according to Mariia Tsaturian, communications director of the public operator Ukrenergo, and "all major thermal and hydroelectric power plants were attacked." This campaign of strikes, which took place between October 2022 and March, also caused damage to over 43% of the entire high-voltage network, according to the company's spokesperson. In a report on the consequences of the winter of 2022-2023 published in April, the United Nations Development Programme estimated the cost of the destruction at "10 billion dollars" (9.3 billion euros), leaving "12 million people" without access to energy, or with limited access. These massive strikes traumatized the population, forced to adapt overnight to blackouts and power cuts lasting several hours organized by the authorities to relieve pressure on the grid. "Last year was very tough for us because everything happened suddenly, we were not prepared," recalls Nina Antochtchenko, co-manager of an Indian restaurant in downtown Kiev. Living with four hours of power a day, Anna Lebedeva, a mother of two living in a neighborhood on the left bank of the Ukrainian capital, particularly affected, remembers how she and her husband had to organize their lives with four hours of power a day, during which they had to cook, do laundry, charge their phones, endure the cold the rest of the time, and use candles. The family got through the winter with the help of a couple of friends: "When they had electricity, we went to their place, and vice versa." At the generator section in the Epicenter supermarket in Kiev on October 12, 2023. GUILLAUME HERBAUT FOR "LE MONDE" This year, Ukrainian authorities started preparing the population for the possibility of another winter under Russian strikes as early as the end of summer. Energy operators have been working on damaged infrastructure to repair 90% of the network, according to Ms. Tsaturian. However, this does not mean that the installations have been restored to the level they were before the massive bombing campaign. "This network," the official continues, "was built over decades, and it is impossible to completely restore it in eight months." In order to better protect its strategic infrastructure, Kiev has installed concrete walls around them. Compared to the first winter of the war, Ukraine now has more anti-aircraft defense systems. President Zelensky and members of his government have been requesting such systems from allies for months to protect the cities. While the equipment is now more numerous, it does not cover the entire territory larger than France. Indeed, while residents of Kiev remain relatively confident after witnessing the effectiveness of the Patriot surface-to-air missile batteries provided by the West, other cities have proven to be much more vulnerable. Beyond Ukrainian energy operators, the entire population is mobilizing to save its economy and prepare for the cold. Department stores in the outskirts of Kiev have stocked up on various types of generators. Informed by experience, residents have identified shopping malls and places where they can go to access electricity and internet in case of power outages. "Encouraging people to revolt" Ms. Lebedeva explains that she has purchased gas canisters for cooking and fluorescent stickers that she and her children will wear at night to be visible to drivers if the street lights go out. "We have warm clothes, strong spirits, we are ready," she asserts with a triumphant laugh. "I spoke with the girls at the bar, and asked them how they were preparing for winter," recounts Nina Antochtchenko. "We agreed that the first thing to do is to pray for our anti-aircraft defense..." A generator in downtown Kiev, November 5, 2023. ADRIEN VAUTIER / LE PICTORIUM FOR "LE MONDE" According to Ms. Tsaturian, the objective of the Russian strike campaigns, in addition to "paralyzing the economy," is also to "create discontent and push people to revolt against the authorities." However, while the country's unity held during the last winter, the situation in which Ukraine finds itself today is very different. The Ukrainians, who were then celebrating the military successes achieved by their army, with the reconquest of the Kharkiv region and part of the Kherson region in the winter of 2022-2023, have little reason to rejoice now. The front has hardly moved since then, and Ukrainian losses have accumulated. In May, Russian forces seized what remains of the city of Bakhmut after months of intense fighting. In June, the Ukrainian counteroffensive launched in the south and east of the country, followed optimistically on Ukrainian television for months, gave way to a sense of powerlessness. The commander-in-chief of the armed forces, Valeri Zaluzhny, recently admitted in The Economist that it probably would not lead to a "deep and magnificent breakthrough." "Avoid getting caught up in quarrels" The impact of such a statement, which President Zelensky tried to soften during an interview with the American channel CBS News, is difficult to measure. But the atmosphere in recent months, between news from the front, doubts about continued support from the American ally in front of a divided Congress, and the conflict between Israel and Hamas that diverted international attention, is dampening the morale of the population. A survey conducted from September 30 to October 13 by the International Institute of Sociology in Kyiv reveals that while "the majority of Ukrainians are optimistic about the country's future," the number of those who believe that "things are moving in the right direction" has decreased from 68% to 60% compared to the same study conducted in May. The proportion of those who believe that things are "definitely going in the right direction" has dropped from 32% to 17%, and the percentage of those who think the situation is "moving in the wrong direction" has increased from 16% to 29%. On the evening of Monday, November 6, in his daily address to the nation, the president announced that there will be no presidential election in March 2024 due to the mobilization of thousands of soldiers on the front and the situation of millions of displaced Ukrainians within the country or beyond its borders. Volodymyr Zelensky mainly called for unity: "Now, everyone must think about defending our country. We must pull ourselves together, avoid relaxing and getting caught up in quarrels or other priorities. The situation is the same today as it was before: without victory, there will be no country."
lead: Traumatized by the massive bombing campaign on the country's energy infrastructure during the winter of 2022-2023, the population is anticipating and equipping themselves with gas cylinders and generators. Faced with a general decline in morale, President Zelensky is calling for unity.

title: Un mois après la tempête qui a ravagé l’est de la Libye, le jeu dangereux des tenants du statu quo
text: There was no "Derna effect" on the Libyan political scene. Neither a citizen revolution, nor authorities' mea culpa, nor an electroshock unlocking the institutional deadlock. One month after the dramatic floods caused by the storm Daniel in the city of Cyrenaica (eastern Libya) - with 4,000 deaths according to a provisional official toll - a heavy silence descends on the traumatized population, especially in the east of the country living under the relentless rule of Marshal Khalifa Haftar. The disaster in Derna leaves Libya doubly wounded: both in mourning and locked down. The protest movement that seemed to emerge on September 11 in the coastal city, amidst the ruins, initiated by residents demanding accountability from failing local authorities, was quickly quashed by Marshal Haftar's forces affiliated with the Libyan National Army controlling all of Cyrenaica. The swift crackdown immediately neutralized any risk of a citizen movement similar to the one that had emerged in late August 2020. For nearly a week, both Tripolitania and Cyrenaica had been the scene of gatherings where the population expressed their frustration with the deteriorating socio-economic situation. Unprecedented since the 2011 revolution, the wave seemed to herald the awakening of a civil society rebelling against the country's control by predatory militias. Nothing of the sort happened in post-cyclone Libya, due to repression. The public stage was rather monopolized by militias from the East (such as Saddam Haftar's Tariq Ibn Ziyad brigade, the son of Marshal Haftar) and the West (the Tripoli 444 brigade) who, although militarily opposed until 2020, "staged their fraternization" around the aid provided to Derna, notes Wolfram Lacher, a researcher at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs based in Berlin. If these sudden reunions between East and West, whose fracture has shaped the politico-military landscape in Libya since the summer of 2014, had been without ulterior motives, there would be cause for rejoicing. However, they were "exploited by armed groups to prevent accountability for the disaster in Derna," explains Mr. Lacher. Stalemate prevails in Libya. Since the ceasefire in October 2020, which marked Marshal Haftar's failure to seize the capital, Tripolitania and Cyrenaica have reached a form of mutual non-aggression pact. Each side has its own executive authority: the "Government of National Unity" of Abdel Hamid Dbeibah in Tripoli (recognized by the international community) and the "Government of National Stability" of Osama Hammad linked to Marshal Haftar in the East. Their formal rivalry is actually accompanied by fruitful backstage arrangements, particularly concerning the redistribution of oil wealth. And each one is perfectly fine with it. It is as if the two political-security apparatuses, both in the West and the East, are primarily seeking to survive. And to maintain their rents carved out of predatory networks while pretending, to the international community, to be discussing an illusory reunification. Not only did the tragedy of Derna not disrupt this amicable partition - a "sharing of the cake," as many Libyans denounce - but paradoxically it has reinforced it. As the reconstruction plans for Derna begin to take shape, each side is trying to preempt future funding. An intense struggle for influence is thus unfolding around the control of international funds to rebuild Derna. "Everyone is looking for international approval to win the race for funding," summarizes Mr. Lacher. The risk of paralysis, or at least of stagnation, is evident. The reinforcement of the status quo takes on another form: an unprecedented authoritarian tightening. The repression in Derna illustrates an obvious anxiety on Haftar's part in the face of popular demands for accountability. "There is currently anxiety and nervousness in him," emphasizes Oussama Assed, director of the Libya Center for Strategic and Future Studies, based in Tripoli. The director of the Benghazi branch of this think tank, Siraj Daghman, was actually arrested on October 1 following a meeting on the responsibilities for the disaster in Derna. Tribal Clashes Another manifestation of Haftar's "nervousness" was the order given on October 6 to his forces to capture Mahdi Al-Barghathi, former defense minister of the former government of Fayez Al-Sarraj (in power from 2016 to 2021) in Tripoli and a known opponent of the marshal, whom he had fought militarily. After a long exile in Tripolitania, Al-Barghati had returned to his city of Benghazi after apparently obtaining guarantees regarding his personal safety. These guarantees apparently were not enough as his return led to a mobilization of Haftar's forces aiming to capture him. This resulted in tribal clashes, as Al-Barghati's clan is affiliated with the large Awagir tribe, one of the most important in Benghazi. Tensions in Benghazi between the Awagir, historically rooted in the city, and the Ferjani, Haftar's tribe originating from Tripolitania, are not new in the Eastern metropolis. But their reappearance in the post-Derna context is a worrying challenge for Haftar, especially since his rapprochement with Vladimir Putin, which he sees as an insurance policy, is not universally supported in his regional stronghold. Behind the apparent deadlock, the lines are shifting silently.
lead: Instead of prompting introspection on the governance failures between the authorities in the west and east of the country, the cyclone Daniel that hit Derna on September 10 fueled security reflexes amidst rivalries over the reconstruction.

title: Comprendre les enjeux géopolitiques de la Russie.
lead: From November 20th to December 18th, Sylvie Kauffmann, editorial director at Le Monde, will lead a series of geopolitical conferences dedicated to contemporary Russia since the fall of the Soviet empire.

title: Les ressources minières kazakhes suscitent les convoitises
text: Scandium, Lanthanum, cerium... Essential for the energy transition, rare earth elements are the oil of tomorrow. Since the West has opened its eyes to its excessive dependence on China in this matter, it has been multiplying diversification strategies. "To secure its resources, the European Union (EU) is increasingly turning to countries like Canada, Australia, and Kazakhstan," summarizes Thomas Gomart, director of the French Institute of International Relations. On Saturday, October 28, the death of at least forty-five miners at an ArcelorMittal site tragically reminded that this distant country in Central Asia is rich in underground resources. On Wednesday, November 1, and Thursday, November 2, it will receive, like its Uzbek neighbor, a visit from the French President, Emmanuel Macron. The reason: "To support these two countries in their reform efforts," emphasizes the Elysee Palace. But also, to strengthen ties "in the economic field, particularly in energy and supply." Accompanied by about fifteen business leaders, including those from EDF, Suez, and Orano (formerly Areva), as well as the Bureau of Geological and Mining Research, Emmanuel Macron promised "contract announcements." Paris is notably in the running to participate in the construction project of the first Kazakh nuclear power plant. However, access to critical metals will also be on the agenda. Difficult reserves to evaluate Since its independence in 1991, the former Soviet country boasts of having "all the minerals and elements of Mendeleev's table" in its subsoil. According to the United States Geological Survey, it is the largest producer of uranium (40% of global production), the second largest producer of chromite (13%), the fifth of cadmium (5.9%), and its mines are rich in rhenium, zinc, and manganese. However, the total state of its reserves is difficult to evaluate. "The economy is partly opaque," summarizes Agathe Demarais, from the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR), an independent think tank. This is because a large part of the infrastructure and mining inventories date back to the Soviet era. And because powerful clans close to the government share a portion of the resources. Western executives working in the sector readily admit, under the guise of anonymity, that corruption is not uncommon there. Nevertheless, the country is one of the most attractive in the region for foreigners, thanks in part to its reform efforts. "At its independence, it was able to exploit its hydrocarbon fields by calling on international companies with the technical expertise necessary to do so," explains Dominique Menu, former head of Central Asia for BNP Paribas, who regularly writes about the region. Its main deposits of Tengiz, Karachaganak, and Kashagan have been developed through joint ventures between a state company and Chevron, ExxonMobil, or TotalEnergies. "Oil is still the lifeblood: it accounts for nearly 80% of the country's mining production," adds Mr. Menu. And the majority of its exports. Uranium extraction is dominated by Kazatomprom, the state-owned company that operates around fifteen mines - again, often in joint ventures with foreign companies. Including Orano, present since 1996, with 1,200 employees. More than 50% of the country's uranium production is exported to China, including a significant portion from Orano. "Multivectorial" foreign policy The exploitation of critical metals is largely carried out by local actors. And upon closer inspection, Europe's charm offensive in this area began years ago. In 2012, Germany and former President Nursultan Nazarbayev concluded a partnership aimed at guaranteeing German companies the right to exploit Kazakh rare earths in exchange for investments. Ten years later, in November 2022, the EU signed a memorandum of understanding with the country, which then promised to provide it with at least sixteen of the thirty critical metals it needs. However, these agreements are not easy to implement. Notably because this country, the size of five Frances, is landlocked between Russia and China - placing it at the heart of rivalries between these two powers. "It only has access to the Caspian Sea, getting materials out is costly and sometimes complex," adds a European engineer in the sector. The case of oil illustrates this: 80% of the black gold that Kazakhstan exports passes through Russia to reach the port of Novorossiysk on the Black Sea, via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, in which Moscow is a 31% shareholder. A dependence that Vladimir Putin's country regularly exploits. "In 2022, he suspended oil deliveries from Kazakhstan through this pipeline four times," recalls Marie Dumoulin, director of the ECFR's Expanded Europe program, in a note on the subject. A decision that was widely interpreted as a reaction to Kazakhstan distancing itself from Moscow following the invasion of Ukraine. Since then, President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, who came to power in 2019, has continued the "multivectorial" foreign policy deployed by his predecessor. Namely, maintaining good relations with everyone: Europe, the United States, Turkey, Russia despite everything, but also China, which closely eyes its subsoil... "In this context, Kazakhstan will undoubtedly raise the stakes around access to its rare earths, foreshadowing a trend to come in many emerging countries," predicts Agathe Demarais. The latter will demand strong commitments from the candidates, such as, at a minimum, access to technologies.
lead: On Wednesday, November 1st and Thursday, November 2nd, President Emmanuel Macron will be visiting Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan. France hopes to strengthen its supply of critical metals in the region.

title: De Russie, le Hamas exclut toute libération des otages sans cessez-le-feu
text: This is a very rare move at a time when Hamas leaders' contacts with the outside world are mostly limited to discreet consultations in Arab countries that host them. On Thursday, October 26, Russia officially hosted a delegation from the Palestinian Islamist movement led by one of its members of the political bureau, Moussa Abou Marzouk. First announced by the official news agency RIA Novosti, this visit was confirmed by the spokesperson of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Maria Zakharova. It was later revealed that this delegation was received by Mikhail Bogdanov, who is both the Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs and Vladimir Putin's representative for the Middle East. On the same day, Mr. Bogdanov also met with the Iranian Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs, Ali Bagheri Kani, as reported by Russian diplomacy. After the meeting with Hamas, Russian diplomatic sources anonymously assured news agencies that the focus was on the issue of hostages held in Gaza since the terrorist attack on October 7 in Israel. Moscow reportedly insisted on the "immediate release of foreign hostages," according to these sources, and discussed "issues related to the evacuation of Russian citizens and other foreign citizens from the Palestinian enclave." New positioning According to the latest figures provided by the authorities, Russia has twenty nationals among the victims of the October 7 attack, and three others are reportedly being held hostage in Gaza. Mr. Abou Marzouk did not respond to questions from Le Monde, but another member of the delegation, identified as "Abou Hamid," told the Russian newspaper Kommersant that the release of "civilian prisoners" was impossible without a ceasefire, essential to "locate" the people who have been captured by "various Palestinian groups" in the Gaza Strip. According to this representative, fifty hostages have been killed so far "due to Israeli bombings." In addition to Moscow's unusual concern for its nationals held hostage, the visit of such a delegation, which is not the first for a group that Russia does not designate as a terrorist organization, also corresponds to the new positioning taken by Moscow in this matter, for very political purposes. Russia maintains significant leverage in the region by engaging with almost all actors, from the Middle East to the Arab-Persian Gulf. Playing a role in any negotiation would be a way for the Kremlin to refute international isolation and the pariah role that the West would like to see it confined to. Comparison with Leningrad Since October 7, Moscow has notably taken a decidedly anti-Israeli turn, as evidenced by its initiatives at the UN as well as official reactions after the Hamas attack. Russian diplomacy did not condemn the group or even use the word "terrorism." Vladimir Putin himself waited more than a week to call Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, theoretically a close partner, and compared the siege of Gaza to the blockade of Leningrad, which resulted in approximately 800,000 deaths during World War II. This Russian shift is mainly explained by the desire to weaken the Western front, to press on its internal fractures and divergences with the "Global South," which Moscow hopes to align with its cause. In its very first reaction after October 7, the Kremlin leader had only words for the "American failure" in the region. The main target is the support for Ukraine that Russian diplomacy aims to weaken. On Thursday, the Hamas delegation made it known that they appreciated this stance. "The Russian Federation is a friendly country to the Palestinian people and supports all representatives of this people," "Abu Hamid" praised to Kommersant. The Israeli side called the visit of Hamas members to Moscow a "shameful move," but the situation forces them to maintain a cautious stance towards initiatives and comments coming from Russia.
lead: An official delegation from the Palestinian Islamist movement was received on Thursday by the Russian Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs. Since October 7th, Moscow has clearly taken a hostile stance towards Israel in hopes of weakening the Western powers.

title: La Russie utilise le conflit à Gaza pour affaiblir le soutien à l’Ukraine
text: A "gift from heaven" for the Kremlin: the expression is repeated over and over in the comments of Western diplomats and external observers. Israeli retaliations in the Gaza Strip, in response to the terrorist attack carried out by Hamas on October 7, are a windfall for Russia in its war against Ukraine and its confrontation with the "collective West," as the prevailing term in Moscow goes. In the aftermath of these attacks, Russian media barely concealed their satisfaction, despite the risk of a widespread escalation in the Middle East, including Syria. Beyond a part of fantasy - the idea that the United States would already be unable to supply arms to both Kiev and Tel Aviv - there is indeed a reality: the resurgence of conflict in the Middle East offers a welcome diversion for Moscow and weakens the Western position on Ukraine. "The situation could play in favor of Russia," summarized the popular newspaper Moskovski Komsomolets as early as October 9. The stakes go beyond simply relegating the Ukrainian issue in the hierarchy of television news. It concerns the efforts made by Western countries since February 2022 to isolate Russia and rally countries designated as part of the global South to their cause. These efforts, met with only relative success, are on the verge of being swept away, both in public opinion and among a number of governments in the Middle East, Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Isolation threatens... the Western bloc. "A godsend for Putin" The brutality of the Israeli response and the support it receives in Western capitals weaken two pillars of the Western discourse on Ukraine: Russia's disregard for international law and the crimes committed by its army. A comparison was quickly drawn between the Russian and Israeli occupations, which Western countries refuse to equate. Similarly, the differential treatment of potential war crimes revives the discourse - old and recurrent in the Israeli-Palestinian issue - of "double standards" and Western "hypocrisy." In other words, in the current feverish atmosphere, it is difficult to avoid comparisons between Russian bombings on Ukrainian energy infrastructure and Israel's decision to cut off electricity to Gaza. "It's a godsend for Putin," acknowledges a European diplomat. "The entire discourse and efforts of the Western countries are weakened," also notes Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean, from the French Institute of International Relations. It is a windfall for Moscow, which hopes to break out of its international isolation with the opening of this new front in the Middle East." This new development was immediately understood and exploited in Moscow. In the name of the absolute priority given to the Ukrainian issue, the Russian authorities did not hesitate to sacrifice their traditional policy of balance and to go against a rather pro-Israeli public opinion. This reversal was evident from the early days through breaches of established norms, unusual for an experienced Russian diplomacy: it took Vladimir Putin more than a week to call his Israeli counterpart, Benjamin Netanyahu, on October 16, and offer his condolences "to the families of the victims." Neither during this call nor in any of the official Russian communications did Moscow condemn Hamas or refer to "terrorism," despite the death of nineteen Russian nationals in the attacks, as of the latest count. Putin even went as far as comparing the siege of Gaza to the blockade of Leningrad by the Nazis between 1941 and 1944. Mediator role The strategy, supported by a media treatment openly hostile to Israel, is clear: it aims to strengthen as much as possible a diverse anti-Western front and emphasize the internal fractures within this Western camp. This obsession was noticeable from Putin's first public comments on October 10, where he mentioned the subject only to make it "a striking example of American policy in the Middle East." A few days later, from Beijing, he warned - or rejoiced - that conflicts in the world "strengthen" the bond between Russia and China. The Kremlin takes even more liberties with Israel, a usually cherished partner, as the risk of Tel Aviv deciding to militarily assist Ukraine has become almost nil. Milan Czerny, a specialist in Russo-Israeli relations, notes that Israel was still "surprised by the Russian position given Israel's restraint on Ukraine. Russo-Iranian relations are known, but Russia was previously trying to appear more neutral." However, Israeli officials, aware of Moscow's influence on Damascus and Tehran, refrained from any protest. Moscow even continues to claim a mediator role that, if granted, would be another divine surprise. In addition to phone consultations with several Arab leaders, Russian diplomacy presented a resolution project to the United Nations Security Council on October 16 - which was rejected - condemning violence against civilians (once again, without mentioning Hamas). This move could one day put Western powers in the uncomfortable position of having to veto a text calling for de-escalation. The move is a win for Putin. The Russian stance is seen as "totally cynical" at the Elysée: "The Russians calculate that by supporting Hamas, they will align themselves with the opinion of many Southern countries and gain an advantage on the Ukrainian front." The German Chancellor Olaf Scholz also denounced the "cynicism" of Vladimir Putin calling for the protection of civilian populations. The contrast between the crimes committed by Russia and the attitude of Kiev is all the more striking. Ukraine has shown unwavering support for Israel since October 7th. Volodymyr Zelensky defended Israel's "unquestionable right" to defend itself and called Benjamin Netanyahu the next day to express his solidarity. In the eyes of many Ukrainians, the attacks and atrocities committed by Hamas resonate with those inflicted by Russia on Ukrainians since the beginning of the invasion on February 24, 2022. On social media, widely shared video montages drew parallels between the crimes committed during the attack on the rave party in the Israeli desert and those in Bucha, a suburb of Kiev. This discourse is also a way to reaffirm Ukraine's alignment with the Western camp, demonstrating that this alignment goes beyond the immediate question of the war against Russia. However, Kiev's total support for Israel could also backfire. "The Global South may wonder why support Ukraine if it takes only one side, does not condemn Israeli strikes, and does not express compassion for Palestinian civilians," notes Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean. Like other heads of state, Volodymyr Zelensky, who comes from a Jewish family, offered to visit Israel to show his support, but he was met with a refusal, as revealed by the Israeli press on October 16th. "It's not the right time," he was reportedly told, another sign of Israel's extreme caution towards Moscow. In Ukraine, this refusal has caused deep confusion.
lead: Moscow has departed from its traditional position of balance between Israel and the Arab countries by not condemning the Hamas attack. The Kremlin sees the conflict as an opportunity to turn the accusations against it regarding the violation of international law and crimes against civilians back on the "collective West."

title: La Russie se retire de deux traités d’armement et procède à des tirs de missiles balistiques
text: Since the huge success of his song "I Am Russian" in 2022, the ultranationalist pop singer Shaman has become the favorite artist of the Russian regime, which subsidizes his shows with millions of rubles. On November 4th, for the Unity Day, no less than three television channels were broadcasting the concert in which he was participating. The singer had saved his hit song for the finale, but viewers still got a surprise: as Shaman lip-synced enthusiastically to his chorus ("I am Russian, I go all the way..."), a stagehand in a suit and sunglasses came on stage to present him with an open briefcase; in the middle, a big red button that the singer eagerly pressed to trigger... just some fireworks. The episode is not only indicative of a climate that trivializes the use of nuclear weapons and promotes a deadly ultranationalist escalation - in line with Vladimir Putin's statement as early as 2018 regarding a potential nuclear confrontation: "As martyrs, we will go to heaven, and they [the Westerners] will simply die." It comes at a time when Moscow is working to revive its nuclear threats. Potential "large-scale tests" The most significant move concerns the denunciation of the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT) on November 2nd. On that day, Vladimir Putin signed a law revoking Russia's accession to this treaty in 2000. Although opened for signature in 1996, it never entered into force due to insufficient ratifications by countries, including the United States. This decision also coincides with another Russian withdrawal, that of the Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe (CFE), announced in May and effective on Tuesday, November 7th. In response, NATO countries announced on the same day that they would suspend their participation in this agreement limiting conventional arsenals on the continent. Russia formally withdrew from this treaty, signed in 1990 to turn the page on the Cold War, blaming the United States for undermining the security architecture in Europe by favoring the expansion of the Atlantic Alliance. One by one, the regulatory instruments of the 1990s are being renounced. Following the CTBT withdrawal, the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs assured that Moscow intends to "continue to respect the moratorium on nuclear tests," but also emphasized that the potential conduct of "large-scale tests" by the United States would "force" Russia to "do the same." When announcing his intention to withdraw from the treaty in early October, Mr. Putin was less formal, indicating only: "I am not ready to say whether we should resume testing or not." At the same time, the American television channel CNN reported that Russia, China, and the United States were conducting construction work, including underground, at known nuclear testing sites – the Arctic archipelago of New Siberia, in the case of Russia. Negotiating posture Following the withdrawal from the Ticen, on November 5, the Ministry of Defense announced the successful testing of an intercontinental ballistic missile of the Bulava type, a first in a year, from a fourth-generation nuclear submarine towards its target located on a test site in the Kamchatka Peninsula, in the Russian Far East. With a range of 8,000 kilometers, the Bulava (SS-NX-30 in NATO classification) can be equipped with ten nuclear warheads. Earlier, the Kremlin leader personally supervised training exercises involving all components of Russian nuclear deterrence – land, sea, and air. On this occasion, the Ministry of Defense showed images of the launch of an intercontinental missile of the Yars type from the Plesetsk Cosmodrome in northern Russia. A Soviet thermonuclear aerial bomb AN-602 (Tsar bomb), the most powerful nuclear weapon ever created and tested, at the Russia Expo event in Moscow on November 4, 2023. According to nuclear experts like Yuri Fedorov, quoted by the independent Russian media Agentstvo, this renewed activism does not indicate a profound change in Russian capabilities or posture, but it represents a new "signal" to the West. While nuclear blackmail by Russia was prominent in the early months of the war, it gradually faded. In September 2022, when annexing several Ukrainian regions, the Kremlin promised to defend them with the same means as any other part of Russian territory – meaning, if necessary, using nuclear weapons. Then, nothing more until the alleged transfer of nuclear warheads to Belarusian soil in the spring. The various initiatives observed in recent weeks may also aim to strengthen Russia's negotiating posture, in the very theoretical perspective of a comprehensive discussion between Moscow and Washington, going beyond the fate of Ukraine towards a strategic dialogue on nuclear issues. Moscow's "suspension" of the New START treaty on nuclear arms reduction in February, the last bilateral agreement of its kind between Russians and Americans, had already been interpreted in this way. In a column in the Izvestia newspaper on October 18, Deputy Leonid Slutsky, head of the Duma Committee on Foreign Affairs, also seemed to suggest this: "Russia's withdrawal from the Ticen is by no means an escalation." This is a clear signal and an invitation to dialogue. (…) We will observe the reaction and actions of the United States.
lead: Moscow has revoked its membership in the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty after also endorsing its withdrawal from the Treaty on Conventional Armed Forces in Europe, intended to turn the page on the Cold War.

title: « La Foire du livre de Francfort déclare rendre les voix israéliennes “audibles” tandis qu’elle réduit l’espace accordé aux voix palestiniennes »
text: The tragic and upsetting events that began on October 7th and continue today have repercussions worldwide, even in the publishing industry. Palestinian writer Adania Shibli, a recipient of several awards and a finalist for the 2020 National Book Award for her book "Minor Detail" (translated by Stéphanie Dujols and published by Actes Sud/Sindbad), was supposed to receive the LiBeraturpreis 2023 for the same work, published in German as "Eine Nebensache" (translated by Günther Orth and published by Berenberg Verlag) during the 2023 Frankfurt Book Fair, held from October 18th to 22nd. On October 13th, the literary society LitProm, organizer of the award partially funded by the German government and the Frankfurt Book Fair, announced that the award would not be presented to the writer at the Fair. A public event with Adania Shibli and her translator, Günther Orth, scheduled to take place at the Fair was also canceled. LitProm stated in their announcement that the decision was made in agreement with the writer. This claim was later repeated without verification in an article by The New York Times. However, this is false: Adania Shibli stated that the decision was not made in consultation with her but was imposed on her. A missed opportunity, she said, as she would have used the ceremony to offer reflections on the role literature can play in such difficult and painful times. (LitProm and The Times have since corrected their texts.) Adania Shibli's American publisher, Barbara Epler of New Directions, sent a letter to The New York Times, in which she wrote: "At a time of unimaginable pain on all sides, spreading lies serves no purpose, especially when they concern the author of a novel about the Nakba, whose historical accuracy is exemplary. Cancelling the ceremony and thus attempting to silence Adania Shibli's voice because of the war in Israel is a cowardly act. But to claim that Adania Shibli had agreed (even as Gaza is going through such a trial) is far worse." The Book Fair states its intention to make Israeli voices "particularly audible" while diminishing the space given to Palestinian voices. Although "Minor Detail" has been accused of antisemitism by two journalists and literary critics, more serious reviews have strongly refuted this accusation in the German press and elsewhere. The responsibility of the Frankfurt Book Fair The book refers to well-documented events related to the rape of a young Bedouin woman in 1949 by an Israeli army unit. Adania Shibli's British publisher, Jacques Testard of Fitzcarraldo Editions, wrote: "One of the main objectives of literature is to promote understanding and dialogue between cultures. In these times of violence and terrible suffering, the world's largest book fair must promote the literary voices of Palestine and Israel. We wish to express our solidarity with Adania Shibli and Berenberg Verlag, her German publishing house." It is in this same spirit that we, who belong to the world of writing, translation, and publishing, affirm loudly and clearly that the cancellation of cultural events is not a solution. We remember that the Frankfurt Book Fair supported Turkish publishers and that in 2022, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky addressed the Fair through a prerecorded video. The Frankfurt Book Fair has a responsibility, as a major international book fair, to create spaces that allow Palestinian writers to share their reflections on literature in these grave and terrible times, rather than silencing them. We need to find new ways to write, speak, think, and approach this dark period. For this, more than ever, we need writers, including Palestinian writers.
lead: More than six hundred writers - including three Nobel Prize in Literature laureates, Annie Ernaux, Abdulrazak Gurnah, and Olga Tokarczuk - publishers, translators protest, in a column in "Le Monde", against the cancellation of the public award ceremony of the LiBeraturpreis 2023 to the Palestinian writer Adania Shibli, for her book "A Minor Detail", scheduled for October 20, during the Frankfurt Book Fair.

title: En visite à Kiev, Ursula von der Leyen apporte un bol d’air à l’Ukraine
text: It was a visit that the Ukrainian authorities needed. The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, was in Kiev on Saturday, November 4th, for a day of meetings and speeches aimed at reaffirming the "unwavering" support of the European Union (EU) for Ukraine. Beyond the effects of the conflict between Israel and Hamas that have caused "attention to shift away from Ukraine," as acknowledged by President Volodymyr Zelensky during a press conference, the country is going through a critical period, between the supposed "fatigue" of its allies and the difficulties faced by the Ukrainian army on the front line. Ursula von der Leyen's sixth visit since the beginning of the Russian invasion took place a few days before the expected publication on Wednesday, November 8th, of a crucial report by the Commission on the progress made by Kiev in the seven reforms that were submitted to it at the same time as its candidate status for the EU in June 2022. The implementation of these seven recommendations, which touch on justice, corruption, national minorities, and media, is considered necessary before any potential accession negotiations for Ukraine to join the EU can begin. "You have made excellent progress, it is impressive to see, we will testify to this next week when the Commission presents its enlargement report," assured Ursula von der Leyen during a press conference. "Ukraine is ready and will continue the reforms," responded the Ukrainian President. "Ukraine has completed all the tasks [expected of it] within the framework of the seven recommendations from the EU Commission," stated Olga Stefanishyna, Deputy Prime Minister responsible for European and Euro-Atlantic integration on X (formerly Twitter). "Our progress is recognized. That being said, we must not forget that these are only the first steps towards full integration into the EU. Reforms are ongoing, and more are to come." Aligning with EU legislation The European Commission's report, to be published on November 8th, is expected to have a decisive impact on the Twenty-Seven, who will be tasked, at a summit in Brussels in mid-December, with voting for or against the opening of accession negotiations with Ukraine. In the case of a positive response, Kiev will then begin a lengthy process to complete its reforms and align with EU legislation in over thirty areas, including the rule of law and the economy. In June, during an initial assessment, the European Commission had stated that two of the seven recommendations had been fulfilled by Kiev. Ursula von der Leyen also mentioned the EU's efforts to deliver a €50 billion aid program to finance Ukraine for the next four years, for which she hopes to reach an agreement "by the end of the year." She also stated that a twelfth package of sanctions against the Russian Federation will be announced "next week" to the Member States. This package will include a list of sanctioned individuals, "new import and export bans," as well as "measures to strengthen the cap on oil prices" and "to continue the fight against sanctions evasion," she specified on X. The President of the Commission also committed to publishing a proposal before the end of the year to use the proceeds from Russian assets frozen in the EU since the beginning of the invasion. This initiative, coordinated with other countries and strongly desired by Kiev, would unlock more funds to help the country. "It would be a blow to the authoritarian Russian regime if, with the frozen assets, we started financing [European] integration of Ukraine," tweeted Olga Stefanishyna during a conference on October 21. Fatigue of the population after twenty months of war Ursula von der Leyen's visit brought some light a few days after the publication by The Economist of an article by the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian armed forces, Valeri Zaloujny, in which he meticulously describes the technological leap necessary for his army to break out of a very unfavorable positional war for Ukraine. His remarks on the counteroffensive launched in early June - in a "deadlock" - as well as the mention of a "defeat" in case of delays in arms deliveries had an alarming echo in the country. Asked about this article on Saturday, President Volodymyr Zelensky began by mentioning the fatigue of the Ukrainian population after twenty months of war: "It is understandable. There is nothing wrong with that. But it is not a deadlock," he asserted, openly contradicting the commander-in-chief of the armed forces. Earlier in the day, Ihor Jovkva, deputy head of the president's office, was even more incisive in a comment on Ukrainian television, stating that a military leader should not make such statements, "because then we make the aggressor's job easier." On Sunday evening, a segment from an interview of the head of state by the American channel NBC News particularly circulated on social media. "War takes the best of us," says Volodymyr Zelensky, visibly moved, as a journalist asks him if he ever feels defeated. "But we are not ready to give our freedom to that damn terrorist Putin. That's it. That's why we fight."
lead: While the war between Israel and Hamas monopolizes the world's attention, the President of the European Commission stated on Saturday that Kiev had made "excellent progress" in the reforms deemed necessary before opening potential negotiations for Ukraine's accession to the EU.

title: « Il faut procurer d’urgence à l’Ukraine les moyens de fermer le ciel ukrainien aux missiles russes »
text:
Since the beginning of the year, more than 4,000 long-range drones and nearly as many ballistic or cruise missiles have struck the population, civilian buildings (hospitals, schools, universities, residential buildings), and economic and energy infrastructure in Ukraine. These destructive bombings do not target Ukrainian military positions. Russia's primary goal is to terrorize the population, disintegrate social fabric, and provoke the economic collapse of Ukraine by threatening and disrupting every aspect of daily life, interrupting the distribution of water and energy, destroying hospital and educational facilities, and devastating the country's industrial installations. For example, in the case of the electrical grid, over 60% of electricity production capacity has been destroyed since the invasion began. In a month, this represents a loss of 6 gigawatts, equivalent to the consumption of 12% of the population. Vladimir Putin's objective is clearly the total paralysis of the Ukrainian energy system when winter 2024-2025 arrives. The Russian bombing tactic also demonstrates a refinement in the terror imposed on civilians, as a second strike occurs minutes after the first to target rescue teams. These terrorist practices aim to force Ukraine into capitulation, trying to convince its allies of its inevitable defeat, with the help of Kremlin allies in Europe and the United States. In the face of Putin's obsession, we must respond with firmness and determination to meet the existential challenges of this war. We have been too slow to provide the weaponry that Ukrainian fighters need, especially that which would enable Ukraine to protect civilians from deadly aerial attacks. We all remember the recent failure of Israel's aerial attack by Iran, which used four times more vectors in one night than Russia does in a day. Despite the difference in the size of the territories to cover, if protecting the sky is technically possible in the Middle East, it could be possible in Ukraine. It requires above all the political determination of our governments. At a time when the Ukrainian front is running out of ammunition, the rear, meaning the cities and industrial installations, critically lack anti-aircraft defense means. By coordinating and pooling their resources, European states can make these defensive weapons available. This is particularly the case with long-range surface-to-air missile batteries (from 80 to 160 kilometers) like the American Patriots or the Franco-Italian SAMP/T-Mamba, capable of destroying the Russian ballistic missiles that Ukraine is currently unable to stop. In addition to batteries, there are also the essential vectors and long-range air surveillance radars (250 kilometers) for detecting and tracking planes, missiles, and drones. It is this combination that urgently needs to be provided to Ukraine in order to close the Ukrainian sky to Russian missiles. As the world's second-largest arms exporter, France is particularly well positioned to contribute to this effort: we could supply at least one SAMP/T-Mamba battery and the Aster 30 missiles they use, as well as two Thales GM 200 radars. Let's start by delivering this equipment, which can be supplemented as it is produced, and prioritize Ukraine in our exports. 

Furthermore, we must draw another lesson from the failure of the Iranian assault on Israel: in conjunction with anti-aircraft weapons, the role of allied aviation was decisive. The weakness of the Ukrainian air force is therefore a critical point. While Ukraine will receive F-16 fighter jets this year in limited quantities, France, once again, has a role to play by accelerating the training of Ukrainian pilots on our Mirage 2000 and eventually on our Rafale jets - their performance is superior to that of the F-16. 

Most importantly, the situation demands a near-term evolution in our military doctrine to be able to take measures to protect the Ukrainian sky. France and Ukraine's allies in Romania, Bulgaria, and Poland have significant ground-to-air and air assets. At the very least, these could be mobilized to intercept missiles that come close to or cross the borders of the European Union. But we could be more ambitious: a coalition formed by the allies of the Ukrainian army could use our detection and airborne command systems to detect Russian missiles in flight and destroy them using air-to-air missiles fired from French and allied fighters or our anti-aircraft batteries. 

Establishing an air protection zone does not require intercepting Russian planes themselves. Similar to the recent American intervention against Iranian bombings, such a defensive operation would not constitute a declaration of war against Russia. As the United States finally releases the funds needed for military aid that Ukraine has been waiting for six months [61 billion dollars were sent to Kiev in April], the European Union must not slow down but, on the contrary, intensify its efforts. We must understand that we, as European citizens, are at a turning point in our history. Our effective mobilization is essential for the survival of a world governed by common rules for all, rather than the brutal force of a few dictators.
lead: A group of intellectuals and military officials, including Jonathan Littell and General Michel Yakovleff, argue in an opinion piece in "Le Monde" that France and the European Union should assist Kyiv in defending itself against Russian aerial attacks and also update their military doctrine.

title: « Dans la bataille Chine - Etats-Unis autour de TikTok, la mauvaise foi est la chose la mieux partagée »
text: Parliamentarians vote overwhelmingly to blacklist a foreign company. Demonized because of the nationality of its owner, it is accused of threatening national security. A president rushes to authorize the sale of the company to carefully selected investors. A former finance minister smells a good deal and joins the ranks to buy it. We are not in Vladimir Putin's Russia, but in the United States, the standard-bearer of free enterprise. The Chinese social network TikTok, since that is the company in question, might it not be causing America to lose its mind, not only because of the addictive and time-consuming short videos it allows to be shared? The bill "Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversaries' Controlled Apps Act" passed in the Senate on April 23 requires ByteDance, the Chinese owner of TikTok, to sell the application within a year or face a ban. Steven Mnuchin, former Treasury Secretary under Trump, says he is ready to put together a consortium. The Sino-American battle over TikTok will now be settled in court in a case where bad faith is the most widely shared thing. Proponents of the bill point out that the app was already banned in India in 2020, and that American platforms like Facebook, YouTube, or X have not been allowed in China for fifteen years. In short, tit for tat. A problematic addiction When Beijing accuses Washington of "abusing state power to suppress foreign companies," it's the pot calling the kettle black. But the fact that the United States is adopting behaviors similar to those of the Chinese government risks seriously tarnishing America's leadership on values of freedom and the guarantee of an open Internet. Criticisms of censorship in autocracies may now ring hollow in the global South. Behind the debate on the real or supposed danger of the application lies a remarkably effective artificial intelligence. From billions of short videos, TikTok accumulates an unprecedented amount of information about users. Thanks to heightened virality, TikTok's algorithms have allowed the app to reach one billion subscribers in half the time it took Facebook. On average, Americans spend 50% more time on the Chinese network than on Instagram. The addiction fueled by TikTok is undoubtedly a problem. But TikTok is an easy target. Even if the Chinese-origin app has taken a step up in the sophistication of addiction, the same criticisms can objectively be directed at its American competitors. While Europeans are concerned and regulate to protect the youngest users, the United States has remained passive in the face of the phenomenon. The attempts by American platforms to imitate the success of Chinese technology are stumbling. Reels (Facebook and Instagram), Shorts (YouTube), Watch (Pinterest), Spotlight (Snapchat), or Fast Laughs (Netflix) are not even close to TikTok. China, long accused of only copying and pasting, has scored a point against American technology. Behind a campaign with McCarthyist undertones lies the frustration of Americans being challenged in their hegemony. Another characteristic of the TikTok model is its lesser efficiency in monetizing content. American platforms, unable to ignore the TikTok wave, are forced to develop applications that turn out to be less profitable, a transformation that could weigh on their stock prices. This fuels the anti-TikTok campaign. The issue of data protection However, one must not be naive. The link between TikTok and the Chinese government through ByteDance is not a fantasy. The fact that Beijing has never agreed to give up its prerogative to demand access to the data of American users – a possibility China claims to have never used until now – remains very problematic. The TikTok threat at least pushes the United States to pay more attention to data protection, after initially opposing and then vehemently opposing European regulatory attempts on the subject. The truth is that today, by neglecting this dimension, under the pretext that the United States controlled the main platforms, American citizens are very poorly protected in terms of privacy. It is illusory to believe that the disappearance of TikTok will prevent China from collecting data as it pleases. Advocates of the anti-TikTok law also cite the danger that the Chinese Communist Party could influence content according to its current interests, or even manipulate public opinion in the context of an election. Given that a quarter of Americans consider TikTok a source of information, the issue should not be taken lightly. But so far, how have Facebook and Twitter (renamed X in July 2023) been barriers against foreign interference? Europe, which has failed to produce its digital champions, finds itself caught between two fires. The difference in nature between the Chinese autocracy and the American democracy should not overshadow the intrinsic danger of social networks, regardless of their nationality. The regulatory approach adopted by the EU is probably the poor man's weapon in this technological race, but it is the only possible way to remain faithful to principles that are becoming variable in the United States. Unable to participate in the match, Europe cannot become a supporter of either team. She must settle for a role as a referee at a time when everyone is tempted to no longer respect any rules.
lead: Behind legitimate concerns about the issues posed by the Chinese social network, there is the frustration of Americans facing a success that could challenge their technological hegemony, notes Stéphane Lauer, editorialist at "Le Monde".

title: Rencontre entre Macron et Xi : parler avec la Chine, sans illusions
text: Expected in Paris on Monday, May 6, Xi Jinping will not only celebrate the sixtieth anniversary of diplomatic relations between France and the People's Republic of China with Emmanuel Macron. The Chinese president will take advantage of his first trip to the Old Continent in five years to visit two other European countries: Serbia and Hungary. These destinations are not random. In Paris, Xi Jinping will once again flatter his counterparts by emphasizing the "special relationship" between France and China since the recognition of the People's Republic by Charles de Gaulle. He will undoubtedly praise France's "strategic autonomy" towards the United States. In Belgrade, he will denounce the bombing of the Chinese embassy by NATO on May 7, 1999, with Washington claiming it was a mistake. The tragedy had outraged Beijing, and propaganda continues to exploit it to denounce "American imperialism." In Hungary, he will highlight Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who constantly seeks to weaken Europe from within and show conciliation with Vladimir Putin. China readily asserts that it wants a "strong Europe." This is far from the truth. It aims to weaken Western democracies and thus undermine the transatlantic relationship and even the European Union itself as much as possible. In 2012, it launched the "17 + 1" summits to try to attract Central and Eastern European countries away from Brussels. Fortunately, this initiative has not been successful. Emmanuel Macron is not fooled. Since 2019, he has consistently involved European officials in his meetings with the Chinese leader, whether in Paris or in China. Rightly so. Faced with the second world power, which Europeans now refer to as a "systemic rival," France has no interest in going it alone and being swayed by the flattery of its Chinese counterpart. Only a united Europe has a chance of being heard by Beijing. And even then, not in all areas. Continuing to believe that China is neutral in the war Russia is waging against Ukraine is counterproductive. As will be shown again by the upcoming visit of the Russian president to Beijing - Vladimir Putin is expected to make his first foreign trip to China after his re-election on May 7 - the two regimes have formed a true informal alliance. While China refrains from selling weapons to Moscow, it provides equipment to help modernize its defense industry. When recognizing communist China, Charles de Gaulle had stated: "There is nothing in this decision that implies any approval of the political system currently dominating China. (...)
