France simply recognizes the world as it is." Sixty years later, this statement remains perfectly valid. The space occupied, in the meantime, by China in world affairs makes it even more relevant. Not speaking to China is not a solution. But treating Xi Jinping as a friend is not one either. Emmanuel Macron, who invited his Chinese counterpart to the Hautes-Pyrénées on May 7, should not harbor any illusions. This "intimate" part of the trip will not bring any more to France and Europe than Vladimir Putin's visit to Fort de Brégançon in 2019.
lead: Faced with the world's second power, France has no interest in going it alone and being swayed by the flattery of its Chinese counterpart. Only a united Europe has any chance of being heard by Beijing. And even then, not in all areas.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : les Européens démunis pour renforcer la défense antiaérienne de Kiev
text: "Air defense is now our priority." Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky reiterated this on network X on April 18: while Ukraine had been successful in intercepting a large portion of the drones and missiles launched daily against its territory by the Russians, it is now close to breaking point due to a lack of sufficient anti-aircraft systems to protect itself, and ammunition to supply them. According to Kiev, 80% of the country's energy system is currently damaged, and the number of civilian casualties in bombings is on the rise. On April 17, a missile strike killed 17 people and injured 78 others in Chernihiv. Aware of the danger facing Ukrainians, European states are trying to organize assistance, following the resumption of American support, which may take a few weeks to materialize after the House of Representatives approved a new package of $60.8 billion (57 billion euros). At the request of Josep Borrell, the EU's High Representative for Foreign Affairs, European ministers will try to strengthen their support on Monday, April 22 in Luxembourg. However, progress is slow. During the European Council meeting on April 17 and 18, the heads of state and government of EU countries refrained from making any promises. Only the outgoing Prime Minister of the Netherlands, Mark Rutte, expressed readiness to purchase equipment held by some countries in order to transfer it to Ukraine: "We know that several countries have stocks of [medium-range surface-to-air defense] Patriot systems, but may not want to deliver them directly," he said. On the sidelines of the summit, NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg also urged member states to take action, stating that the priority was to support Ukraine's air defense, rather than strictly adhere to internal equipment standards of the Atlantic Alliance. This was a way to encourage European capitals to draw from their arsenals to transfer equipment to Kiev. France is also under pressure. A good student in the class, Germany has already delivered two of the twelve Patriot systems it possesses, and has assured that it will soon provide a third. Chancellor Olaf Scholz is encouraging European allies to do the same. However, "other countries are reluctant to part with them," noted a German diplomat, referring to member states also equipped with Patriot systems, such as Spain, Greece, the Netherlands, Poland, and Romania, the latter being keen on maintaining their air defense capabilities due to their proximity to Ukraine and Belarus. France is also under pressure. During a G7 meeting last weekend in Capri, Italy, the Ukrainian Foreign Minister, Dmytro Kuleba, asked his French counterpart, Stéphane Séjourné, to deliver a new copy of the SAMP/T Mamba system, the French-Italian equivalent of the Patriot system. Paris and Rome had already struggled to provide the first one. "We are working on it both within the French administration and with our Italian partner, but no response is expected so quickly," confirms a French source. "I am knocking on the doors of the Member States to tell them that if they do not have anti-aircraft batteries, they will have to buy them," said Mr. Borrell in a recent interview with Le Monde. In his mind, it would be less costly to quickly acquire American Patriot systems, even with European funds, than to participate in the reconstruction of destroyed installations at the end of this third winter of war. Since the beginning of the conflict twenty-six months ago, Kiev has nevertheless received enormous quantities of air defense equipment. The United States, Germany, and the Netherlands have delivered three Patriot batteries. Berlin has sent twelve IRIS-T missiles (twenty-two more have been promised), another medium-range system. Washington has also provided twelve Nasams, a very effective medium to long-range battery. Insufficient to cover all of Ukraine. For close defense, especially against drones, Ukraine also has 52 Gepard armored vehicles supplied by Germany, six British Stormer armored vehicles, two Crotale batteries, and six Mistral firing positions from France. Not to mention several thousand portable missiles, useful for repelling attacks carried out by helicopters or low-flying combat aircraft. The United States alone has provided over 2,000 Stinger missiles to the Ukrainians. But this equipment is very insufficient to cover all of Ukraine, which must protect its cities, where the largest concentrations of civilians are located, as well as its energy infrastructure, and also the front line, where its troops are targeted by Russian planes, which bomb them with unguided bombs or guided glide bombs. "The Ukrainians have all the means, but in quantities too low to really be multi-layered everywhere," says Yohann Michel, head of the air power department at the Institute of Strategic and Defense Studies at the University of Lyon-III. Managing a diverse fleet is also a headache for the Ukrainians, who must provide as many training and logistical chains as they have equipment. "If we had a single anti-drone system, the military would more easily thwart these [Russian] attacks," acknowledged Alexandre Zavitnevych, president of the national security, defense, and intelligence committee of the Rada, the Ukrainian Parliament, during a hearing at the National Assembly on February 14. However, according to experts, it is nevertheless unrealistic to believe that Ukraine will one day have the same air defense as Israel, which intercepted almost all of the approximately 330 drones and missiles launched by Iran on the night of April 13-14. The territory of the Israeli state is twenty-seven times smaller than that of Ukraine, and Tel Aviv has an arsenal incomparable to that of Kiev: twenty-four batteries of the Arrow 2 and 3 ballistic missile defense systems, and over forty others (Patriot, Iron Dome, David's Sling, etc.), according to the International Institute for Strategic Studies. "It is currently impossible to cover the entire Ukrainian territory in a way that makes it invulnerable," confirms Yohann Michel. "However, a better air defense system would allow them to consider a freeze in the conflict, with the maintenance of a normal or near-normal economic activity."
lead: Several countries, including France, are reluctant to part with their most advanced defense systems. Foreign ministers must try to intensify their support on Monday in Luxembourg.

title: Le premier ministre slovaque, Robert Fico, blessé par balles
text: Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico, in a "critical" condition after being shot several times on Wednesday, May 15, underwent a successful surgical procedure, a member of the government of this Central European country stated. "I think in the end he will survive," Deputy Prime Minister Tomas Taraba told the BBC, who also mentioned that the surgery went well. "He is not in a life-threatening situation at the moment," he added. The incident took place in Handlova, a town located about 150 kilometers northeast of the capital, according to the Slovak television channel TA3. Interior Minister Matus Sutaj Estok stated that the assailant fired five shots at the pro-Russian leader. A suspect has been arrested. Robert Fico, 59 years old, was evacuated by helicopter to Banska Bystrica, a city in central Slovakia, as announced by the government in a statement. The incident occurred in front of the local House of Culture. According to Jean-Baptiste Chastand, a correspondent for Le Monde in Central Europe, the Prime Minister had approached a small group of people who came to see him on the street after a cabinet meeting on Wednesday morning. A meeting for the European elections was scheduled for Wednesday afternoon. Defense Minister Robert Kaliniak, who visited the hospital where the Prime Minister was operated on, described this attack as "a political assault" that will require a "response accordingly." Slovak television broadcasted images of a man in jeans handcuffed on the ground. The detained and suspected person is a 71-year-old man from the central region of the country, identified by Slovak media as a local writer. The suspect's son told the news site aktuality.sk that his father had a legally registered firearm. When asked about his father's feelings towards Robert Fico, he replied, "I'll tell you one thing: he did not vote for him. That's all I can say." Several statements from the suspect are available on social media. In a video posted eight years ago, he stated, "The world is full of violence and weapons. People seem to be going crazy." He also claimed to have founded a "Movement against Violence" in Levice, which is defined on its Facebook page as "an emerging political party whose goal is to prevent the spread of violence in society." "Heinous attack" Slovak President Zuzana Caputova condemned the "brutal" attack and wished Robert Fico a speedy recovery. Fico returned as Prime Minister last year after previously holding the position between 2012 and 2018 and between 2006 and 2010. "A physical attack on the Prime Minister is an attack on an individual, but also an attack on democracy," she stated in a release. Members of Mr. Fico's party have also expressed their support and condemned the attack. Fico immediately accused the opposition of being responsible for the attack, even though the shooter's motivations were not precisely known. President Emmanuel Macron said he was "shocked by the shots that hit the Slovak Prime Minister," adding: "I strongly condemn this attack. My thoughts and solidarity are with him, his family, and the Slovak people," in a message posted on X. The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, condemned the "heinous attack" against the Slovak Prime Minister. "Such acts of violence have no place in our society and undermine democracy, our most precious common good. My thoughts are with Prime Minister Fico and his family," wrote the German official on X. Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban said he was "deeply shocked by the heinous attack on my friend, Prime Minister Robert Fico," on X, saying he was "praying for his health and speedy recovery." "The attack on Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico is appalling," also reacted Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on X. "We strongly condemn this act of violence against the head of government of a neighboring partner state." Russian leader Vladimir Putin denounced a "heinous crime." US President Joe Biden also condemned the attack, calling it "heinous" in a statement. Josep Borrell, the EU's foreign policy chief, said on X that his thoughts were with the Slovak Prime Minister and his family, expressing being "shocked" and adding: "Once again, we are witnessing unacceptable attacks against political representatives." German Chancellor Olaf Scholz said on X he was "shocked by the cowardly attack" because "violence has no place in European politics." Italian Prime Minister Georgia Meloni expressed in a statement on X "the strongest condemnation of any form of violence and attack on the basic principles of democracy and freedom," saying she was "shocked" by this "vile attack." British Prime Minister Rishi Sunak wrote on X that he was "shocked to learn this terrible news." NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg said he was "shocked and dismayed." "I wish him the strength for a speedy recovery," he wrote on X. UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres also condemned the "shocking attack." After returning to power as Prime Minister in October, Robert Fico questioned Ukraine's sovereignty and halted all military aid to the country. This former member of the Communist Party, who founded his own party and rejects the labels of "populist" and "demagogue," opposed sanctions against Russia. In April, however, he changed his tone regarding the war in Ukraine, advocating for a peaceful solution that respects the "territorial integrity" of the country.
lead: The populist leader, a supporter of Russia, was shot, evacuated urgently, and hospitalized. The motive for the attack is believed to be political. A suspect has been arrested.

title: En Russie, Vladimir Poutine, récemment investi, reconduit son premier ministre
text: Russian President Vladimir Putin, inaugurated for a fifth term on Tuesday, maintained on Friday, May 10, Mikhail Mishustin as the head of his government, after weeks of intense speculation about a possible reshuffle in Moscow. Vladimir Putin is expected to appoint or confirm his ministers, following his electoral victory in March in a presidential election devoid of any opposition. "A lot has been done in difficult conditions, and it seems to me that it would be good for us to continue working together," the president told his prime minister on Friday, according to a Kremlin report. "I think we are on the right track," he assured him, saying he had submitted his nomination to the State Duma, the lower house of the Russian Parliament. Unsurprisingly, the Duma then approved the nomination. Possible reshuffle Mikhail Mishustin, former head of the Federal Tax Service, was appointed prime minister in 2020. He is seen as a technocrat who faithfully implements measures dictated by the Kremlin. "We will do everything in our power to develop our economy, to justify the trust of our people," he promised Vladimir Putin on Friday. The names of the other ministers are expected to be announced in the coming weeks. Observers strongly suggest that the Russian president may reshuffle his government, more than two years after the start of an offensive in Ukraine that has disrupted the Russian economy and society. Vladimir Putin generally surrounds himself with allies whose loyalty is unquestionable, while ensuring that no senior official gains enough power or popularity to be seen as a rival or successor. After nearly a quarter of a century in power, the Russian leader will remain in office until 2030, with the possibility of serving another term until 2036.
lead: Mikhail Mishustin, former head of the federal tax service, was appointed prime minister in 2020. He is seen as a technocrat who faithfully carries out measures dictated by the Kremlin.

title: « Portrait d’un monde cassé » : défendre l’utopie européenne dans un monde fracturé
text: The review of reviews. 2024 is a unique election year. Citizens from seventy-six countries are called to vote. While some elections are particularly scrutinized, such as the presidential election in the United States, this phenomenon has become commonplace. Because "there is no longer a clear distinction between the electoral campaign phase and the governing phase," notes the writer Giuliano da Empoli, who directs the new print edition of the online magazine Le Grand Continent. In the past, governmental realism followed the lyricism of electoral conquest. "We campaign in poetry but we govern in prose," summarized American Democratic Senator Mario Cuomo (1932-2015) in a stroke. Today, we have entered a logic of permanent campaigning, observes Giuliano da Empoli. However, poetry - even political poetry - has not become the rule of democratic life since digital media maintain the public space in a lingering agonistic climate. It must be said that the world is fractured. Divided between supporters of ecological transition and climate skeptics, Europe sees two "lifestyles" opposed, between "multiculturalists and progressives on one side, and conservatives and identitarians on the other," analyzes political scientist Jean-Yves Dormagen. The European Union, an essential objective The conflict in Ukraine has strengthened the European belonging of this nation led by Zelensky, this "Churchill with an iPhone," but Europe must help more, according to British historian Timothy Garton Ash: "Ukraine has made its European choice. Europe must now make its Ukrainian choice coherently." The conflict in Gaza, on the other hand, increases divisions within the European Union (EU). "The simultaneity of these two conflicts poses problems of coherence (…) vis-à-vis the rest of the world," acknowledges Josep Borrell, EU foreign policy chief. In summary, writes this advocate for a ceasefire in Gaza and a two-state solution, "our lack of unity" on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict "has weakened our credibility in defending international law." An impression of "double standards" is particularly prevalent in the Global South, a notion criticized by political scientist Bruno Tertrais. He sees it as an "intellectual and political trap" that "values the idea of a political confrontation with the West," while researcher Aude Darnal, on the contrary, defends the "most polysemic and neutral expression available" to designate the Southern States, despite their heterogeneity. Contemporary of a "broken world," the young people in charge of Le Grand Continent are not part of a "disenchanted generation." That is why, in his afterword, Spanish novelist Javier Cercas displays an avowed Europeanism and federalism. Certainly, Europe must "stop being an elitist project and become a project of the people," but, he writes with lyricism, the EU embodies "the most ambitious, urgent, revolutionary, and necessary project of the 21st century."
lead: As citizens from 76 countries are called to vote in 2024, the magazine "Le Grand Continent" delves into the geopolitics of a world in permanent campaign.

title: La Russie accroît sa présence en Libye, au grand désarroi des Occidentaux
text: Piece by piece, the strategic edifice outlined by Moscow in North Africa is solidifying. Russian presence in Libya, already tangible since 2019 in the form of paramilitary units (former Wagner Group), has experienced a sudden acceleration since the beginning of the year that has not gone unnoticed by Western powers feeling powerless. "Russia has been transferring military personnel and Russian fighters to Libya for the past three months," concludes a note published on Friday, May 10 by All Eyes on Wagner. This international investigative collective on Russian networks in Africa adds that "the delivery of equipment and military vehicles from Syria to Libya constitutes the most visible aspect of [this] increased involvement." Citing the figure of 1,800 Russians now deployed across the country, All Eyes on Wagner notes that two Moscow naval vessels - landing ships - departed from the Syrian naval base of Tartous and arrived on April 8 at the port of Tobruk in Cyrenaica. Supported by photos, the investigative collective asserts that vehicles and weapons, such as 2S12 Sani mortars or BTR and BM armored transport vehicles, were unloaded. This would be "the fifth delivery" of this kind to Tobruk in forty-five days. Western diplomatic sources consulted by Le Monde confirm this increased Russian presence in this pivotal state in North Africa, a junction between the Mashreq and the Maghreb. "The increase concerns more equipment than personnel," however, nuances a diplomat from a European state. These recent movements in Libya, he adds, are part of "a broader Russian breakthrough" aimed at "installing pro-Moscow governments everywhere in East and West Africa. The only missing piece is Chad to divide Africa in two." Chad itself is also the subject of eager Russian interests. Projection platform If Libya is crucial in this African offensive, it is because it serves as a platform for projecting equipment and personnel to neighboring states: war-torn Sudan, Niger, and Mali - and further south Burkina Faso - all led by Kremlin-friendly juntas, and potentially Chad. The strategic hub of this new setup is located in Jufra, a Libyan district 350 kilometers south of the Gulf of Sirte, where equipment and personnel arrive from Tobruk before being redirected to the regional theaters coveted by Moscow. The major development is that "the Russian state is no longer afraid to show its direct involvement in Libya," notes Jalel Harchaoui, an associate researcher at the Royal United Services Institute for Defence and Security Studies. When Wagner entered Libya in 2019 by supporting the self-proclaimed Libyan National Army (LNA) of the dissident Marshal Khalifa Haftar - the "strongman" of Cyrenaica (East) - in his failed assault on Tripoli, Moscow was in official denial. This era is over as the Russian Deputy Minister of Defense, Yunus-bek Evkurov, has already made four visits to Benghazi, the political and military base of Khalifa Haftar, since August 2023. This Libyan marshal, who has established a parallel authority in Cyrenaica to the government in Tripoli, himself traveled to Moscow at the end of September, where he met with Vladimir Putin. Since then, the Russian presence has become more noticeable as the African branch of the former Wagner mercenary group, decapitated by the disappearance in August 2023 of its leader Yevgeny Prigozhin, was taken over by the Ministry of Defense in Moscow under the new label of Africa Corps. Political challenge Faced with this Russian push in Libya, alarm bells are ringing in Western chancelleries, which are vainly seeking a response. In addition to the risk of Moscow's influence spreading in the regional periphery, the United States and Europe are confronted with two challenges. The first is the potential establishment of a Russian military presence on the coast in the form of a naval base, in Tobruk or Sirte, which would pose a direct threat to NATO forces in the Mediterranean. Sirte would represent a nightmare scenario for Western powers as the city, located at the juncture of Cyrenaica (East) and Tripolitania (West), is only 600 kilometers from the coast of Sicily. The idea of a Russian base in Sirte is actually an old plan that Moscow had failed to impose on Muammar Gaddafi in the years 2009-2010. The city is now under the control of Marshal Haftar, under whose shadow Russian paramilitaries are locally active during military maneuvers alongside the LNA, as noted by researchers from All Eyes on Wagner. During an exercise on March 16, Russian Pantsir anti-aircraft systems were displayed at the Ghardabiya air base, on the outskirts of Sirte. The other challenge is more political. Beyond its military presence in Cyrenaica (Tobruk, Sirte) and in southern Fezzan (Tamanhint, Brak Al-Shati), Moscow is engaging in diplomatic activities on all fronts, including in Tripoli - a city that is supposed to be under Turkish influence - where its embassy reopened at the end of February. The new ambassador, Aydar Aghanin, fluent in Arabic, is holding numerous meetings with Libyan political figures of all affiliations. By combining military assets and diplomatic capital, Russia is accumulating resources for the future. "Moscow is likely to establish itself as a mediator, the preferred interlocutor of Libyan factions," anticipates Jalel Harchaoui. Even worse, as migration flows from Sudan and Niger pass through regions controlled by Marshal Haftar, and therefore de facto by Russian paramilitaries, Moscow is strengthening its position vis-à-vis the European Union in the long run.
lead: Moscow is increasing the deployment of troops and equipment in the region, thus strengthening an influence that could impact migration flows to Europe.

title: Au Parlement européen, le RN, soutien constant des régimes autoritaires
text: Jordan Bardella likes to describe the European Parliament as a mere "rubber-stamp chamber." An assembly whose sole role would be to endorse decisions made by the "technocrats of Brussels." However, within the community Hemicycle, the Members of the European Parliament from the National Rally (RN) outline their vision of the world through their votes in session. Support for authoritarian regimes, refusal to denounce the application of the death penalty or other abuses against political opponents, ignorance of cases of violations of fundamental freedoms: the hundreds of decisions made by RN MEPs in Brussels and Strasbourg, analyzed by Le Monde, offer a panorama of international relations as envisioned by the far-right movement. Their positioning is all the more free as the place does not benefit from the media exposure - and possibly controversy - of the National Assembly. During the last term (2019-2024), RN members only had to justify their unwavering support for the Russian regime. The invasion of Ukraine decided by the President of Russia, Vladimir Putin, has indeed highlighted a constant support for the Kremlin. Until the outbreak of the war in February 2022, Jordan Bardella and his colleagues systematically voted against, whether it concerned the denunciation of the Russian law on "foreign agents" (December 19, 2019), the resolution following the poisoning of the main opponent Alexei Navalny (September 17, 2020), or the support for the repressed civil society and particularly for the human rights organization Memorial - Nobel Prize 2022 - (December 16, 2021). Principle of non-interference But the attention paid to the conflict in Ukraine since its outbreak has not convinced the RN troops to fall in line with the criticism of Vladimir Putin. Contrary to a large majority of the community assembly, the French delegation of the Identity and Democracy group now prefers to abstain on most texts in support of Kiev, whether it is to fear the "escalation of war" (October 6, 2022), to demand the creation of a "tribunal for the crime of aggression against Ukraine" (January 19, 2023), or to provide "unwavering support from the EU [European Union] to Ukraine" (February 29). The Russian regime no longer faces the reproach of the RN as before, even when it came to acknowledging the "inhumane detention conditions" endured by Alexei Navalny (ten abstentions and one opposition on February 16, 2023). "It is not up to the EU to decide whether a particular state has done things right. Diplomacy, as well as the military and defense, fall within the competence of the States. (...) We tried to adhere to this principle throughout the term," defended Jordan Bardella after the death in detention of the Russian opponent, announced on February 16. The study of Front National votes in the European Parliament quickly dismisses the doctrine that the president of the National Rally claims to have defended for five years. When a country accused of repression is among the opponents of the National Rally, the latter disregards all "sovereignty." Thus, its elected officials have voted on resolutions calling for the defense of oppressed opponents in Venezuela (March 14), Cuba (September 16 and December 16, 2021), Nicaragua (September 15, 2022), or Bolivia (April 29, 2021). The National Rally's variable application of a principle of non-interference in diplomatic affairs, depending on its proximity or affinity for the leaders in power, is particularly evident in the Maghreb. In 2023, its MEPs voted for a resolution condemning the detention of a journalist in Algeria (May 11), after unanimously opposing texts based on the same grounds regarding Tunisia (March 16) and Morocco (January 19). Blindness Marine Le Pen's party has often been criticized for its unwavering support for illiberal Polish and Hungarian leaders. For Poland, they opposed resolutions on the criminalization of sex education (November 14, 2019), on the "de facto ban on abortion rights" (November 26, 2020), or on the deterioration of the rule of law (September 16, 2021). For Hungary, they voted against resolutions on "violations of EU law and the rights of LGBTIQ citizens" (July 8, 2021) or on the disregard for the rule of law (June 1, 2023). Each time, the far-right MEPs invoke the democratic legitimacy of their allies and praise their alleged resistance to the European Commission. The coordinator of the National Rally delegation, MEP Thierry Mariani, denounces the European Parliament's desire to act as an "NGO" to deny certain members of the Twenty-Seven their "national competences." But this blindness to the repeated and documented violations of fundamental rights is assumed well beyond the EU's borders. Since 2019, the National Rally has consistently refused to criticize numerous authoritarian regimes, such as Egypt, whose President Abdel Fattah Al-Sissi's "fight against Islamism" is invoked to oppose several resolutions noting a deterioration in human rights (December 18, 2020, and November 24, 2022); an argument repeated to vote against a text denouncing the detention of human rights activist Ahmed Mansour in the United Arab Emirates (September 16, 2021). Thierry Mariani repeats that European Parliament resolutions, without binding value, "serve absolutely no purpose," and that their content is often much more complex than their title alone. But the National Rally delegation assumes the "consistency" of the line drawn by its votes on texts that are nevertheless devoid of legislative significance. Mariani also mocks the "acrobatics" of the MEPs from the presidential party who protest in the Community Hemicycle against leaders with whom the head of state is forming partnerships. "No team discipline." Behind the voting explanations of the RN MEPs, there is also a more vehement anti-Atlanticism than that carried by Marine Le Pen on the national stage. Because they refuse to be blindly pro-United States, they allow themselves to defend regimes that the three-time presidential candidate could not publicly support as easily. Near Russia, Belarus benefits consistently from the abstention of the former National Front MEPs when it comes, in recent years, to denouncing "inhumane treatment and hospitalization" of an opposition leader (May 11, 2023) or a "new wave of mass arrests" against opposition activists (February 8). China receives the same considerations from the RN. In five years and on dozens of votes, Jordan Bardella and his colleagues have only voted in favor of one resolution, on January 17. It concerned Beijing's growing influence on the EU's "critical infrastructure." They have never supported texts that express concern, for example, about forced labor and the situation of the Uighurs (abstention on December 17, 2020), violations of fundamental freedoms in Hong Kong (opposition on January 20, 2022), or the abduction of Tibetan children and "practices of forced assimilation" (abstention on December 14, 2023). As the European elections approach, the RN leadership knows that particular attention is paid to the recent votes of its MEPs. Sometimes instructions are given from Paris to prioritize abstention over opposition, in order to avoid hindering their "de-demonization" efforts. The party's choices during the last session of the European Parliament, from April 22 to 25, will not change the geopolitical orientations defended over the past five years, sometimes even in opposition to their allies in the Identity and Democracy group. "Radical right parties are still not accustomed to the negotiation and compromise that are necessary for majority groups," confirms Awenig Marié, a political science doctoral student at the Free University of Brussels. Within their own group, the RN MEPs vote without being subject to any team discipline. Therefore, they express themselves freely, according to their ideas or the message they wish to convey to their voters.
lead: The study of the votes in the European Parliament sessions of the National Rally MEPs during the 2019-2024 term paints a picture of international relations as the far-right party envisions them. Their inconsistent decisions show little regard for the defense of human rights.

title: L’Afrique du Sud demande à la CIJ d’ordonner à Israël de stopper ses opérations à Rafah
text: Will the International Court of Justice (ICJ) order Israel to "cease its military offensive" in Rafah, as urgently requested by South Africa? Seized by Pretoria, the judges established on January 26 that there is a plausible risk that the rights of Palestinians to be protected from the crime of genocide may be violated. Twice since then, they have issued orders against the Israeli state. "The very manner in which Israel is conducting its military operations in Rafah and elsewhere in Gaza is itself genocidal. It must be stopped," South Africa requests in its latest application filed on Friday, May 10, in the evening. The seizure of the two border crossings of Rafah and Kerem Shalom by the Israeli army on May 6 does not simply signify an escalation of the conflict, argues Pretoria in its plea to the judges. "With the destruction of Rafah, the destruction of Gaza itself will be complete," making the "survival" of Palestinians impossible. Israel now has "total control" over what enters and exits Gaza, notes South Africa. Rafah is the "last refuge" for some 1.5 million Gazans who are there, but the operation has "hermetically sealed off Gaza from the outside world, further paralyzing the delivery of aid to and within" the enclave. Shortly before the start of the operation in Rafah, Palestinians received evacuation messages for the Al-Mawasi area in the Khan Younis sector, already largely reduced to "rubble" by Israel, "uninhabitable," according to South Africa, with "people living crowded in tents" and also targeted by Israeli fire. On Sunday, around 300,000 people had been displaced. Urgency to preserve evidence South Africa highlights the "extreme risks" for those who cannot or do not want to move again. Lawyers recall recent testimonies of Israeli soldiers published in the newspaper Haaretz, recounting that evacuation zones were treated as "extermination zones" in which all remaining Palestinians are considered legitimate targets. It is also urgent to preserve this evidence, asserts Pretoria, which asks the judges to order Israel to allow access for foreign journalists to Gaza and the entry of international investigators. On Friday, the United Nations Security Council vaguely "emphasized" the importance of unfettered access for investigators to mass graves discovered after military operations against the Nasser and Al-Shifa hospitals in the city of Gaza. According to Pretoria, "more than 390 bodies have been discovered (...) and show signs of torture and summary executions." South Africa once again denounces the "genocidal rhetoric" of Israeli officials, including the finance minister, Bezalel Smotrich. The Court had ordered Israel to punish those inciting violence. This rhetoric generally constitutes a central proof of the intention to eradicate a population in genocide trials. On Sunday, the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, Volker Türk, said he did not know how "the latest evacuation orders, let alone a total assault in an area where the civilian presence is extremely dense, can comply with the binding requirements of international humanitarian law" and the ICJ orders. Furthermore, Egypt announced on Sunday evening its intention to join the legal battle initiated by South Africa, denouncing in a statement "the severity and extent of Israeli attacks against civilians," and Israel's systematic targeting "of civilians, and the destruction of infrastructure." Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has repeatedly stated that nothing will stop him, not even the ICJ. Various forms of pressure Despite criticism against South Africa, accused by Israel of playing into Hamas's hands, the judges confirmed that Pretoria, like all states that have ratified the genocide convention, has the obligation to prevent but also to punish. Punishing is normally the work of the International Criminal Court (ICC), which, unlike the ICJ, prosecutes individuals and not states. Informed in mid-April of arrest warrants being prepared against Israeli officials, Benjamin Netanyahu accused the ICC of "antisemitism." On Friday, South Africa's Minister of Foreign Affairs, Naledi Pandor, asked the ICC to speed up, without fearing pressure. Pressure that she herself has faced, she told journalists. "You saw that the prosecutor [of the ICC] received a letter from members of Congress [American]. I received the same letter." Signed by twelve senators and addressed to prosecutor Karim Khan at the end of April, this open letter is written in a style reminiscent of organized crime. It orders the prosecutor to stop his investigations, threatens his family and colleagues, and concludes: "Consider yourself warned." On May 3, the magistrate, who is facing various forms of pressure, warned the authors against attempts to "obstruct justice." As he did when issuing an arrest warrant against Russian President Vladimir Putin due to the war in Ukraine, the best chance to escape pressure is to act quickly.
lead: While 300,000 people have fled Rafah, Pretoria denounces the "extreme risks" faced by the population.

title: Les liens troubles entre le président du CIO et Vladimir Poutine mis en lumière dans un livre-enquête
text: The president of the International Olympic Committee, the German Thomas Bach, at the opening of an executive committee meeting of the IOC at the headquarters of the organization in Lausanne, Switzerland, on December 8, 2015. LAURENT GILLIERON / AP The charge is precise, documented, and relentless. "Like the Russian oligarchs who have managed to survive until now, ours has followed the rule to the letter: money and power in exchange for unconditional loyalty and political fidelity to Vladimir Putin," explain the German journalists Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner. Their target? Thomas Bach, 70 years old, at the helm of the International Olympic Committee (IOC) since 2013, portrayed as "the oligarch" of the Russian head of state, in an investigative book (Putins Olygarch, dtv, 320 pages, 17.99 euros, not translated), published just under three months before the opening ceremony of the Paris Games (July 26-August 11). The work of the two reporters from the Süddeutsche Zeitung, the major left-leaning daily newspaper in Munich, is the result of several years of research and interviews. It shows that Thomas Bach has long been in Moscow's corner, despite the repeated denials of the main party involved and the recent crisis between the Kremlin and the Olympic movement, which suggests that the two sides are now irreconcilable; to the point that Russia is increasingly organizing parallel events, such as the BRICS Games in June and the Friendship Games in September. Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner presented their book on Monday, April 29, in the Bavarian capital, emphasizing that it could be read as a "handbook of corruption at all levels of global sports governance," which it traces with as much meticulousness as relish in previous episodes, most of which are known but with fewer details. This includes the saga of the German sportswear manufacturer Adidas, presented as the original matrix of corruption. This "true CIA of sneakers," which used all intelligence techniques to establish its power and networks, especially in the communist bloc USSR-GDR during the last two decades of the Cold War. A young lawyer - an Olympic gold medalist in team fencing in Montreal in 1976 - is part of the setup, in the circle of Horst Dassler, the son of Adolf Dassler, the brand's founder: his name is Thomas Bach. Alfons Hörmann, a key witness The centerpiece of the book is the sworn testimony of Alfons Hörmann, former president of the German Olympic Committee, who succeeded Mr. Bach in 2013. He shared with the authors a story that had remained secret until now, establishing the long-standing closeness between the IOC president and the Russian autocrat, as well as the foundations of the pact sealed between them. We are in February 2011. At that time, Mr. Hörmann was the head of the German Ski Federation. Important international events are taking place in the Bavarian resort town of Garmisch-Partenkirchen. It is essential that the cream of German sport be present and show unity, as Munich and Garmisch are candidates to host future Summer and Winter Games. Shortly after arriving in the Alps, Mr. Bach "apologized for the next day and, citing absolute confidentiality, told me that he would fly to Moscow at the invitation of Vladimir Putin," recalls Mr. Hörmann. He had explicitly stated that he would be transported by plane to and from Moscow on a private aircraft. Less than twenty-four hours later, Thomas Bach is back in Garmisch. To Alfons Hörmann, he reports that the conversation with the then Russian government leader turned out to be "pleasant and fruitful." "Putin told me that he agreed with my candidacy for the presidency of the IOC and that he would actively support it," confides the former fencing champion. "(...) Now, I am sure that I can become it." This will be done, two years later. On September 10, 2013, as the applause of the International Olympic Committee's bigwigs has not yet subsided at a congress in Buenos Aires, Thomas Bach's cell phone vibrates in his pocket. On the line, a man is determined to be the first to congratulate him: the master of the Kremlin. "A targeted disinformation campaign" Does this connection explain the extraordinary passivity of the IOC around the revelations, starting at the end of 2014, of an institutionalized doping system in Russia? This is what Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner suggest in their investigation. "The greatest sports scandal in history was initially taken lightly by the institution," they claim, "then there were multiple pressures on the World Anti-Doping Agency. Each time, it was about allowing Moscow to save face, and until very recently." The hesitations lasted six years, from half-measures to half-sanctions. At one point, playing the national anthem was banned, then Russians were not allowed to display their flag or nationality during events, competing under the baroque designation of "Russian Olympic athlete," and then under the banner of the Russian Olympic Committee at the Tokyo Summer Games. On February 24, 2022, Kremlin troops invaded Ukraine. Its athletes were temporarily banned from the global sports scene, before a question stirred the IOC headquarters in Lausanne, Switzerland, a few months later: how will Russian athletes be able to participate in the Paris Games? To placate Putin, at all costs. This decade of compromise is nevertheless coming to an end in the fall of 2023, when it becomes clear to Moscow that the presence of Thomas Bach at the head of the organization is no longer sufficient to defend its interests, and that the President of the IOC will eventually give in to international pressure by toughening sanctions. On October 12, 2023, the Russian Olympic Committee is suspended for having placed four organizations from occupied Ukrainian regions under its authority. It is now open war with the IOC leadership, exchanging accusations. The International Olympic Committee did not respond to the questions from the authors of "Putin's Oligarch." Instead, it stated: "Your new questions suggest that they are based on misleading information, which could be part of a targeted disinformation campaign that has been ongoing for several months against the IOC and its president." "Putin's Oligarch," dtv, 320 pages, 17.99 euros, untranslated. DTV VERLAG Serge Enderlin (Munich, special correspondent). The space for contributions is reserved for subscribers. Subscribe to access this exchange space and contribute to the discussion. Subscribe Contribute Reuse this content.
lead: If the relations between Moscow and the International Olympic Committee have deteriorated due to the tightening of sanctions against Russia, Thomas Bach has long been considered an ally by the Kremlin, reveal two German journalists in the book "Putin's Oligarch".

title: Lors de son investiture, Vladimir Poutine inscrit dans la durée la confrontation avec l’Occident
text: "Together, we will prevail." By dedicating the first and last words of his inauguration speech to war on Tuesday, May 7, Vladimir Putin firmly placed his new mandate, the fifth in the Kremlin, under the sign of confrontation, not with Ukraine, too insignificant to even be mentioned, but with the West, whose "policy of aggression" will not prevent Russia from becoming "even stronger." Re-elected on March 17 with officially 87.28% of the vote, the Russian leader greeted, on Tuesday, from the first seconds, the "heroes participating in the special military operation," some of whom were among the approximately 3,500 guests - members of the elite gathered under the gilding of the presidential palace, as well as ambassadors from foreign countries, including that of France. Just as quickly, he mentioned "our historical lands that have defended the right to join their homeland," in reference to the Ukrainian regions annexed by force in March 2014 and September 2022. Even though this meticulously organized and lavish ceremony leaves little room for improvisation, some developments are notable. While Mr. Putin made a point, during his early terms, of mentioning the country's democratic development and the rights of citizens, hand on the Constitution, these terms disappeared from 2012, a year marked by massive protests over alleged electoral fraud. "A sacred duty" On Tuesday, Vladimir Putin preferred to prioritize "defending the interests and security of the Russian people," seeing this mission as "a sacred duty" - he then attended a mass during which the Orthodox Patriarch Kirill called him, before correcting himself, "Your Majesty" (or "Your Highness," in a more modest translation). True to his line of ensuring a form of normalcy in the daily lives of the Russian people, the president did not forget to mention the projects for "development" and the "quality of life for families." For the first time, the ceremony was broadcast by almost all local television channels - including sports and entertainment channels, with laudatory comments from the presenters. "We look to the future with confidence," emphasized Mr. Putin, as the internal and external situation seems to confirm these optimistic assurances: on the Ukrainian front, his army is advancing, albeit slowly and at the cost of significant losses; his defense industry is running at full capacity, supported by China, to the point of overheating the Russian economy; the elite and society as a whole show, if not cohesion, at least loyalty; the opposition is stifled, its leaders dead or in prison. A reshuffle to come The only uncertainty in this post-election sequence concerns not a hypothetical change in direction, but the extent of the expected government reshuffle. According to the Constitution, Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin has submitted the resignation of his government. However, analysts consider his reappointment likely, as they believe Vladimir Putin is not inclined to reignite speculation about his succession. The fate of several ministers is drawing attention, particularly that of Sergei Lavrov, who has been the Minister of Foreign Affairs since 2004 and has been rumored to be leaving on several occasions, as well as Sergei Shoigu, who has been weakened by the disastrous start of the invasion of Ukraine and implicated in a large-scale corruption scandal in his ministry. While addressing the Russian people primarily, Vladimir Putin also took care, in a relatively short speech, to send a message to Western countries: "We do not reject dialogue with Western states," he asserted. "The choice is theirs: do they intend to continue trying to contain Russia's development, to pursue a policy of aggression and continuous pressure against our country, or to seek the path of cooperation and peace? (...) I repeat, discussion is possible, including on issues of security and strategic stability, but only on an equal footing." Nuclear exercises In support of this speech, the Kremlin leader had announced the upcoming conduct of nuclear exercises by the "non-strategic" forces of the Southern Military District, bordering Ukraine, an initiative that the Ministry of Defense explicitly linked to the "threats" made by "various Western leaders" against Russia. The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, specifically named Emmanuel Macron and his "very dangerous rhetoric," as the French president had mentioned the possibility of deploying French troops on Ukrainian soil. The attitude of Western countries towards Vladimir Putin's inauguration was also closely watched. While most European countries chose not to attend the ceremony, ambassadors from six countries were present: Malta, Hungary, Slovakia, Greece, Cyprus, and France. The presence of the French ambassador, Pierre Lévy, at the Kremlin echoed Emmanuel Macron's words, spoken on Monday during Xi Jinping's visit, stating that France "is not at war with Russia or the Russian people" and "is not seeking a change of regime in Moscow." French diplomacy, it is also argued, recognizes a state and its institutions without commenting on their officeholders. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs, which criticized the conditions of Vladimir Putin's reelection, intends to maintain channels of discussion. However, as if to emphasize on the Russian side that no reconciliation is possible with France, which has positioned itself in recent months as a leading supporter of Ukraine, Mr. Lévy was summoned to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Monday for the third time this year. The British ambassador has also been summoned, but chose not to be present on Tuesday, as did the United States ambassador. "We do not consider this election [on March 17] to be free and fair, but he [Vladimir Putin] is the president of Russia and will continue to hold this role," nuanced the spokesperson of the U.S. State Department, Matthew Miller, in front of the press. On May 9, for the ceremonies marking the anniversary of the Soviet victory in World War II, eight foreign heads of state, mostly from former Soviet countries, are expected in Moscow.
lead: "We look towards the future with confidence," declared the Russian president as he began his fifth term in the Kremlin, during a ceremony attended by the French ambassador.

title: Les Etats-Unis incluent des missiles ATACMS de longue portée dans leurs livraisons d’armes à l’Ukraine
text: A sigh of relief passes through the ranks of Ukraine supporters in Washington. On Wednesday, April 24, President Joe Biden signed the bill adopted the day before by the Senate, a few days after the House of Representatives, which grants $61 billion (57 billion euros) in aid to Kiev. The package is part of a larger $95.3 billion package, also benefiting Israel and Taiwan. In a speech, Joe Biden acknowledged that the parliamentary process "was a difficult path," an understatement given six months of negotiations and reformatting of the project, blocked by a minority of radical Republican lawmakers. A first installment of one billion dollars was immediately announced by Washington, including long-range missiles. The president emphasized that this support for Kiev represents an "investment" in the security of Ukraine, Europe, and the United States. He noted that Russia also benefits from valuable support: Iranian drones, ballistic missiles, North Korean artillery shells, and Chinese electronic components. Joe Biden stressed that military deliveries stimulate American production: Patriot missiles are made in Arizona, Javelins in Alabama, artillery shells in Ohio or Pennsylvania... Finally, he paid tribute to the Republican and Democratic leaders in Congress, who managed to agree on a text that ultimately gathered a very large majority of lawmakers. "There is bipartisan consensus for this kind of American leadership," Joe Biden said. "We do not abandon our allies, we support them. We do not let tyrants win, we oppose them. We do not just watch the world evolve as spectators, we shape it." Kiev troops on the brink of collapse in several places In the afternoon, Jake Sullivan, national security adviser, appeared before the press at the White House. He did not rule out that "Russia may make additional tactical gains in the coming weeks" in eastern Ukraine, due to the human and material disadvantage currently suffered by Kiev. But the adviser indicated that "the structural dynamics of this conflict favor the party defending its own territory." According to Jake Sullivan, the Ukrainian army has managed to destroy 700 armored vehicles and about 250 tanks since the beginning of the year. However, on the retreat since the failure of their summer 2023 counteroffensive, Kiev's troops are on the brink of collapse in several places on the front line. On April 20 and 21, the Russians broke through the Ukrainian line for nearly 10 kilometers at Otcheretyne, a town in Donbass located northwest of Avdiivka, raising fears for a moment of a breakthrough in Kiev's defensive system. The city of Kharkiv, located in the northeast of the country and with a population of 1.4 million inhabitants before the war, is daily under Russian fire. A sign of the growing concern in Kiev, "the key [to the conflict] is now speed," acknowledged Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in a message broadcast on the X network on Wednesday, adding that "any leader who does not waste time saves lives." The first delivery to Ukraine, estimated by the Pentagon at one billion dollars, is one of the largest since the beginning of the war. The last delivery on March 12 amounted to 300 million dollars. The most notable fact: the presence of more advanced ATACMS (Army Tactical Missile System) surface-to-surface missiles. Since October 2023, the versions provided to Kiev of this GPS-guided and inertial system projectile had a limited range of 165 kilometers. The new delivery will include a version capable of reaching targets located at 300 kilometers, with a precision of just a few dozen meters. This capability would bring within reach of Ukrainian Himars rocket launchers the strategic Kerch Bridge, located southeast of Crimea, about 250 kilometers from the front. Similarly, the Russian fleet in the Black Sea, already significantly reduced, will become even more vulnerable. The ability to target a symbolically important target like the Kerch Bridge explains the German Chancellor Olaf Scholz's stubborn refusal to approve the delivery of Taurus missiles by his country. Jake Sullivan clarified on Wednesday that the ATACMS missiles would be used only "within the sovereign territory of Ukraine." New redefinition of American red lines According to information from The New York Times confirmed by an official source, the United States has already begun delivering these longer-range ATACMS in February. The repeated Russian attacks on Ukraine's essential civilian infrastructure motivated this new redefinition of American red lines regarding deliveries. Jake Sullivan also mentioned the transfer of ballistic missiles from North Korea to Russia. Upon receiving these ATACMS, the Ukrainians began using them, The New York Times reported, citing two recent targets: Russian troops in the port city of Berdiansk and a military airfield in Crimea. The new massive delivery from the United States also includes 155mm shells, which the Ukrainians desperately need to counter the Russian artillery, which fires five to ten times more projectiles than its adversary in some areas of the front. Additionally, Bradley infantry fighting vehicles have been announced. Even though it is an old armored vehicle - it was introduced into the US army in 1981 - Ukrainians praise its versatility and especially its armor, which saves the lives of many soldiers, an essential resource to allow Kiev to continue its war. The question remains how long it will take for all this American equipment to reach Ukraine. "In the coming hours," promised Joe Biden. According to various sources, some of it has been prepositioned in Germany and Poland to be delivered as quickly as possible.
lead: In the first military aid package of one billion dollars, the White House agreed on Wednesday to include projectiles capable of reaching targets located 300 kilometers away, with an accuracy of a few tens of meters.

title: « Trêve olympique » et parfum de bataille commerciale au premier jour de la visite de Xi Jinping en France
text:
At dinner, cognac was given all the honors, symbolizing an evening of highs and lows in the relationship between France and China. From Bernard Arnault (LVMH) to Carlos Tavares (Stellantis), most of the top executives of French companies and some celebrities, such as Mireille Mathieu and Sophie Marceau, gathered on Monday, May 6, at the Elysée Palace for the state dinner hosted by Emmanuel Macron in honor of his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping. Earlier in the day, the guest assured the French president that cognacs would not be subject to additional tariffs by Beijing at this stage, while Chinese authorities complete an antidumping investigation against them. Amidst toasts, crab meat and Bresse chicken supreme prepared by top chefs, the evening was meant to conclude a busy first day of the visit, aiming to bridge differences on current pressing issues. Following a simple welcome ceremony at Les Invalides and various meetings at the Elysée Palace, Xi Jinping and Emmanuel Macron jointly called for an "Olympic truce" during the Paris Games this summer. Their joint statement came shortly after the Russian Ministry of Defense announced upcoming exercises to "train in the preparation and use of non-strategic nuclear weapons," a move criticized by the United States but barely mentioned in public by Macron and Xi. Deep divergences While this potential "Olympic truce," already supported by a UN resolution, could temporarily halt fighting during the sports events of the Olympics (July 26 to August 11) and possibly the Paralympics (August 28 to September 8), in hopes of initiating uncertain discussions for a more lasting ceasefire, Moscow has already rejected such a prospect. It could take advantage of the situation in the spring to further its position on the ground, while Kiev, demanding the withdrawal of Russian forces as a precondition for negotiations, is wary of a pause that could solidify its enemy's territorial gains. The joint statement between Beijing and Paris masks deep divergences on the Russian aggression, which is shaking Europe and granting China an unprecedented say in the continent's security. "Conflicts can only be resolved through negotiations," emphasized the Chinese president, urging both parties to "resume contact and dialogue." This was a way to encourage Kiev's allies to exercise restraint in their military support, more than two months after the French president did not rule out sending military reinforcements to Ukraine. While the United States warns Beijing about the support China is providing to the Russian war effort and asks Europeans to relay the message, Emmanuel Macron has welcomed the "commitments" made by Xi Jinping, for the first time at this level according to the Elysée, not to supply weapons to Moscow, while also exerting more control over the export of dual-use (civil and military) materials to Russia. "We oppose using the Ukrainian crisis to shift blame onto others, tarnish a third party, and trigger a new Cold War," Xi Jinping warned in response to Western criticisms. He believes that an "international conference" should finally bring together Russians and Ukrainians to try to find a negotiated solution to the war. However, China seems to be boycotting the meeting organized by Switzerland on June 15 and 16, in close consultation with Kiev, to discuss the terms of the "peace plan" proposed by Volodymyr Zelensky. Russia has also refused to participate in this meeting. 

Apart from cognac, which has been granted a reprieve from potential sanctions, progress is barely more tangible regarding the numerous trade disputes affecting the rebalancing of relations between France, Europe, and China. The discussions held on Monday mainly allowed French and Chinese leaders to speak frankly. Macron stated to the press, without taking questions, that the talks had helped "clarify" disagreements "to improve the economic relationship and give it a new impetus." Earlier in the day, Ursula von der Leyen, President of the European Commission, emphasized the current stakes following a trilateral discussion with the two presidents. "As we have shown before, we will defend our companies, we will defend our economies. We will never hesitate to do so when necessary," she assured, calling on China to reduce its industrial overcapacity: "As Chinese domestic demand does not increase, the world cannot absorb China's excess production," she warned. Xi Jinping, however, refuses to consider a truce on the matter: "The so-called issue of Chinese overcapacity does not exist," he stated, believing that the "Chinese new energy industry" actually helps "increase global supply and alleviate global inflation pressure." While electric vehicles - targeted in Europe by an anti-subsidy investigation denounced by Beijing - and low-cost Chinese solar panels will be essential for the energy transition, as Chinese leaders argue, Europeans are concerned about their industry being decimated by Chinese products. For a "fully loyal" trade aiming to be conciliatory, Emmanuel Macron intends to continue exchanges after sixty years of Franco-Chinese diplomatic relations, but insists on the notions of "respect" and "loyalty." "There is no logic of decoupling vis-à-vis China, it is a will to preserve our national security as you do, yourself. A will of respect and mutual understanding, reciprocal, and a will to continue to open up trade, but to ensure each time that it is fully loyal, whether it be tariffs, aids, market access," Emmanuel Macron developed in the afternoon in front of a group of French and Chinese businessmen gathered near the Elysée for economic meetings. By the way, the Head of State invites Chinese companies to invest more in France. French investments in China are three times more important than those of Chinese companies on our territory, he noted. Earlier in the day, the Minister of Economy, Bruno Le Maire, made a call in this direction: "France welcomes all industrial projects. BYD and the Chinese automotive industry are welcome in France," he said during a meeting with Chinese and French automotive sector bosses. The French minister had visited a BYD factory in Shenzhen in July 2023, but the world leader in electric vehicles had finally chosen to set up its first European site in Hungary, where Xi Jinping is to visit from Wednesday to Friday, after a stop in Serbia. As for Mr. Macron and Mr. Xi, they were to fly to the Hautes-Pyrénées on Tuesday to continue their discussions at the Col du Tourmalet, a high point of the mountain stages of the Tour de France. The weather was expected to be just as chilly as in Paris.
lead: Emmanuel Macron and the Chinese leader issued a joint call on Monday for a ceasefire in Ukraine on the occasion of the Paris Games this summer. As for trade disputes, progress seems to be very limited.

title: Xi Jinping à Paris : le président chinois appelle à l’instauration d’une trêve mondiale pendant les Jeux olympiques
text: Emmanuel Macron praised, on Monday, May 6, the willingness expressed by his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping, to "call on all stakeholders to observe an Olympic truce during our upcoming Games." "We believe together" that this initiative "could be an opportunity to work towards a lasting settlement in full respect of international law," assured the head of state. The French president also welcomed China's commitments to "refrain from selling any weapons" to Moscow, during a press statement alongside Xi Jinping, who was visiting Paris on May 6 and 7. On his part, the Chinese president called for not "tarnishing" his country's reputation in the Ukrainian issue, stating that Beijing is playing a "positive role" in finding a peaceful solution to the war. "We oppose the use of the Ukrainian crisis to shift blame onto others, tarnish a third country, and trigger a new Cold War," said Mr. Xi in reference to the recurring criticisms from Western countries regarding the China-Russia relationship. Earlier in the day, President Macron called, in front of his counterpart, for "fair rules for all" in trade exchanges between the European Union and China – as disputes accumulate between the Twenty-Seven and the Middle Kingdom – and for "decisive" coordination on the war in Ukraine. "I thank President [Xi Jinping] (...) for his openness regarding the temporary measures on French cognac and his wish not to see them implemented," also stated the French head of state to the press, at the end of the day. Emmanuel Macron, who still hopes to position France as a "balancing power" in the Sino-American standoff, is celebrating the 60th anniversary of the establishment of bilateral diplomatic relations between France and the People's Republic of China. "The international situation, very clearly, more than ever requires this Euro-Chinese dialogue," he said at the opening of discussions with his Chinese counterpart, while some opposition members, such as the socialist candidate and Place publique for the June European elections, Raphaël Glucksmann, accuse him of "rolling out the red carpet" in an "obsequious" manner to a "dictator." "The future of our continent will clearly also depend on our ability to continue to develop balanced relations with China," said Emmanuel Macron at the Elysée at the start of a trilateral meeting in the presence of the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen. "China and the EU must remain partners," according to Xi. "As two important global powers, China and the EU must remain partners, continue dialogue and cooperation, strengthen mutual trust, consolidate their consensus, and engage in strategic coordination," Xi Jinping told his counterparts at the beginning of this trilateral meeting. "In order to promote the regular and healthy development of China-EU relations and to continuously make new contributions to peace and development in the world," continued the leader of the Asian superpower. The Twenty-Seven officially consider China as a partner, but also as a competitor and a systemic rival. Emmanuel Macron, for his part, explained that he wants to raise concerns "in all friendship and trust" in order to try to overcome them, warning against "a decoupling logic that would be harmful" economically, and advocating for "fair rules for all." The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, also called for "equal access to markets." Before the meeting, she had emphasized that Europe could not "accept" the "unfair trade" caused by the influx of Chinese electric vehicles or steel manufactured with "massive subsidies." The EU "will not hesitate to take firm decisions" if necessary to "protect its economy and security," she also stated. There are numerous trade disputes that could lead to increases in tariffs. Faced with the threat of being caught between the American and Chinese economies, both heavily supported by their respective governments, the EU has in recent months increased investigations into Chinese state subsidies in several industrial sectors, particularly in electric vehicles. The EU and France are urging Beijing to "use all its influence" on Russia to stop the war. After a grand ceremonial welcome at Les Invalides on Monday afternoon, the Franco-Chinese duo had a more political tête-à-tête on their agenda, followed by press statements and a banquet at the Elysée Palace. Emmanuel Macron plans to ask his Chinese counterpart to support the "Olympic truce" during the Paris Games this summer. "Coordination" with Beijing on "major crises" in Ukraine and the Middle East is "absolutely crucial," he emphasized on Monday. Paris wants to ensure that China, the main ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin, does not clearly support Russia's war effort against Kiev. Paris even wants to "encourage China to use the leverage" it has on Moscow to "contribute to a resolution of this conflict," according to the Elysée. "China and the EU have a shared interest in peace and security," highlighted Ursula von der Leyen, affirming her "determination to stop the Russian aggression war against Ukraine and to establish a just and lasting peace." "We have an interest in demonstrating that our collaboration is effective," insisted the President of the Commission, mentioning the "challenge" of the "substantial economic relationship" between Brussels and Beijing. The EU and France are thus counting on China to "use all its influence on Russia" to end the war in Ukraine, she continued after the meeting. Ursula von der Leyen and Emmanuel Macron also called on the Chinese president to make "more efforts to limit the delivery to Russia of dual-use equipment [that can be used for both civilian and military purposes] that ends up on the battlefield in Ukraine," she added. The President of the European Commission also expressed confidence that China would continue to temper Russian nuclear threats. "President Xi has played an important role in de-escalating Russia's irresponsible nuclear threats, and I am confident that he will continue to do so," Ursula von der Leyen said in the French capital, as Moscow has just ordered upcoming nuclear exercises. Emmanuel Macron and Xi Jinping will travel to the Pyrenees on Tuesday for a more personal getaway, accompanied by their wives.
lead: Paris wants to ensure that China does not clearly support the war effort against Kyiv, and even "encourage it to use the leverage" it has on Moscow to "contribute to a resolution of this conflict," according to the Elysée.

title: Ukraine, Gaza : la politique étrangère de Joe Biden empêtrée dans ses contradictions
text: Elected President of the United States in 2020, Joe Biden had promised to put an end to "endless wars." Four years later, as his term is soon to end, a strange paradox is emerging. The leader kept his word by withdrawing American troops from Afghanistan as early as the summer of 2021, even though the debacle of this withdrawal still haunts his administration. Since then, he has had to get involved in two conflicts with global shockwaves: in Ukraine and Gaza. The United States has exerted all its military and diplomatic weight in these conflicts, risking its reputation. Their credibility is now tarnished, considering the means employed, the results obtained, and, above all, the untenable contradictions in the way these two crises are being addressed. Seven months before the presidential election, the Biden administration appears bogged down, even though foreign policy was supposed to be its strong suit. When coldly considering the strategic interests of the United States, the balance sheet is far from negative. No American soldier has died in Ukraine, while the Russian army suffered a historic setback during the initial phase of the war. Its ranks have been decimated, its capabilities diminished at sea, on land, and in the air. In addition to this unexpected result, a direct confrontation between NATO and Russia in a conflict that would spill beyond Ukrainian borders has been avoided. The goal set by the White House has been achieved. There was no escalation. It is in the name of this priority that Washington refused to offer Kiev a timetable for NATO membership at the Vilnius summit in July 2023. As for relations with allies, which were strained during Donald Trump's presidency, they have been restored and intensified, although there is growing apprehension in Europe at the prospect of the businessman's possible return to power. In the Middle East, on the other front, the United States is facing a precipice. After the unprecedented attack launched by Hamas in Israel on October 7, 2023, which shocked American officials with its atrocities, the Biden administration reacted promptly and decisively. "It's not just about politics or strategy, it's personal for us," explained National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan ten days later. Washington set a horizon from the beginning of the Israeli ground offensive in Gaza: to prevent a regional conflict involving the Lebanese Hezbollah and Tehran. The goal was then achieved through the massive deployment of naval forces in the eastern Mediterranean. But the effectiveness of this deterrence policy is being questioned since the death on April 1 in Damascus of General Mohammad Reza Zahedi, commander in Syria and Lebanon of the Quds Force of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, in strikes attributed to Israel. This senior figure in the Tehran regime and six of his lieutenants were killed while they were in the consular section of the Iranian embassy. On April 13, Iran's spectacular retaliation - nearly three hundred drones and missiles launched towards Israeli territory - required intense defensive coordination between Israel, several neighboring Arab countries, the United States, the United Kingdom, and France. The damage was very limited, but a threshold was crossed, plunging the region into the unknown. The White House tried to convince Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu not to escalate. However, in the night of April 18-19, Israel launched an attack against Iran, which was meant to be carefully considered. Explosions were heard east of the city of Isfahan, near a military airbase and an important nuclear research center that was reportedly spared. Since the start of the war in Gaza, Washington has promised to stand by its ally under all circumstances. Joe Biden finds himself trapped by his own loyalty to Israel, whose leader seems tempted by escalation. In Gaza as in Ukraine, the United States have found it easier to determine what they do not want than what they do want. Negotiations with Russia? Up to the Ukrainians to decide. Ground operation in Gaza? Up to the Israelis to define. In both cases, Washington offers military support, advice from its experts, but claims not to impose the parameters of a settlement. However, by weaving strategic ambiguity, the United States have ended up entangled in their own web. Semantic shift There is another similarity: these two wars have lasted longer than the United States anticipated. During the last visit of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the White House on December 12, 2023, Joe Biden promised that his country would support Ukraine "as long as possible." Until then, the established expression was "as long as necessary." The semantic shift is clear, it marks a weariness. The "indispensable nation," as former Secretary of State Madeleine Albright famously put it, seems to be caught up in the reality of a fractured world, without a policeman or a code of conduct, where everyone defends their own interests. In September 2023, in his speech before the United Nations General Assembly, Joe Biden had already abandoned the theme of the clash between authoritarian regimes and liberal democracies - which had been at the core of his worldview. The boldest commitment of the Biden administration in support of Ukraine preceded the Russian invasion on February 24, 2022. Its role was decisive, starting in November 2021, with public warnings and the declassification of information on Russian army mobilization at the borders. The United States managed to unmask Kremlin propaganda. When Russian tanks headed towards Kiev, Washington and European countries reacted quickly and in unison, offering military assistance to Ukraine and imposing unprecedented sanctions against Moscow. These American successes now seem distant. The sanctions have shown their limitations. Workarounds have emerged through China, Turkey, or Central Asia. The Russian economy has reorganized around the mobilization of the military-industrial sector. The national currency, the ruble, has held strong. Furthermore, the American administration - especially Joe Biden, an octogenarian leader marked by the Cold War - imposed questionable limitations, out of fear of a direct confrontation with nuclear-armed Russia. These red lines regarding certain types of weaponry led to systematic delays in deliveries to Kiev - particularly during Ukraine's counteroffensive in late summer 2022. These restrictions were lifted after a few months, as seen with the Abrams tanks and F-16 fighter jets. Since February 2022, the overall package of American military aid - $44 billion (€41.31 billion), in addition to the European effort - is significant, but it does not cover Ukraine's needs for artillery or anti-aircraft defense. Kiev no longer knows how to implore the Western powers to provide additional Patriot batteries. The United States could have facilitated the redeployment of this equipment to Ukraine, currently located in the Middle East or with Asian allies. However, such a move could have been interpreted as a regional weakening of the American arsenal by China, the sole systemic rival of the United States. Republican blockade In addition to these hesitations, concerns about a global rise in energy prices, especially in this election year, are added. Washington is urging Kiev to stop long-range strikes on oil infrastructure inside Russia, which remains one of the world's largest energy exporters despite the sanctions. According to The Washington Post, these calls for restraint were conveyed to Volodymyr Zelensky by Vice President Kamala Harris in February, on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference (Germany). Kiev ignored them, much like Benjamin Netanyahu ignored many American recommendations. The White House rejects this interpretation and emphasizes the historic magnitude of the aid provided to Ukraine. It argues that no weapon is decisive in this type of multifaceted conflict. Rightly so, it points to the Republican majority in the House of Representatives, which has been preventing the adoption of a new $60 billion package allocated to Ukraine since November 2023. The drift of the Republican Party and the influence of the MAGA elected officials (Make America Great Again; Trump supporters) in the House cannot be attributed to the Democratic administration, as they have adopted language elements from the Kremlin. Faced with this block of hostility, the White House gave the impression of navigating blindly, seemingly convinced that the country's interest and Joe Biden's negotiating skills with Congress would eventually prevail. However, Donald Trump included in his electoral promises a miraculous end, within twenty-four hours, to the war in Ukraine - which would require Ukraine to abandon the territories annexed by Russia (Crimea and Donbass), in favor of the latter. From this perspective, military aid to Kiev would only prolong the conflict. The Republican candidate therefore maneuvered behind the scenes for his supporters to reject any compromise in the House. Until the dramatic turn of events on April 12: on that day, the former president received the Republican speaker, Mike Johnson, at his residence in Mar-a-Lago, Florida. Donald Trump seemed to agree to the idea of military aid to Kiev in the form of credits.
