Three days later, Mr. Johnson presented a legislative action plan, separating the allocations for Ukraine, Israel, and Taiwan. This series of texts was to be voted on Saturday, April 20. Catch-all package Some of the extremist Republican members of the Freedom Caucus in the House remain opposed to these provisions. Since the summer of 2023, a shift has occurred. Initially, the argument put forward was that priority should be given to the border with Mexico and the immigration issue. Then this comparative approach - devoid of meaning, as if the United States did not have the means to handle these issues simultaneously - gave way to the blocking of any new military aid. The mistake of the White House was, in the fall, to agree to include aid to Ukraine in a broader negotiation - one of those popular catch-all packages in Congress - encompassing military assistance to Israel and Taiwan, as well as measures to strengthen American borders. Even if aid to Ukraine was voted on in April, the accumulated delay weighs heavily on the ground and in people's minds. It has generated worrying pessimism around the Ukrainian cause and exposed the vulnerabilities of the United States. Allies are questioning: can they still rely on their historical protector for long? Washington has often given the impression of helping Ukraine to defend itself, but never to win. To prevent a victory for Moscow, without ever defining the contours of the desired Russian defeat. During his visit to Kiev on March 20, Jake Sullivan outlined the American vision of a victorious Ukraine: a country "sovereign, independent, and free, capable of preventing future aggression with a strong and vibrant democracy, with democratic institutions, and economic growth." Many grand concepts, appealing slogans, but no roadmap. What would be the long-term security guarantees for Ukraine? What would be its integration into the Western bloc? On the Republican side, a subtle idea has spread that Americans and British prevented the conclusion of peace negotiations between Kiev and Moscow in February and March 2022. In reality, these discussions, which took place in Belarus and Turkey, were not taken seriously by Russia, whose delegation was led by lower-ranking officials demanding Ukraine's surrender. But this theory of an artificially prolonged conflict is wreaking havoc. Republican Senator J. D. Vance (Ohio) is now one of the most consistent critical voices regarding American involvement. In an op-ed published on April 12 by The New York Times, he asserts that the White House has no "viable plan" for a Kiev victory. "The calculations on Ukraine don't add up," argues the senator, who lists the fatal disadvantage in terms of manpower and ammunition that Kiev suffers from. The senator recommends a drastic revision of objectives: "By maintaining a defensive strategy, Ukraine can preserve its valuable military personnel, stop the bleeding, and buy time to start negotiations. But for this to happen, American and Ukrainian leaders would need to acknowledge that Mr. Zelensky's stated goal - a return to the 1991 borders - is fanciful." In short, Crimea and the Donbass would need to be abandoned. Late shift On April 7, President Zelensky stated for the first time that his country "will lose the war" if the aid promised by the United States remains blocked in Congress. A pessimism coupled with a warning, coming at a time when some experts and diplomats are questioning the possibility of a Ukrainian military collapse in the coming months around Kharkiv and Odessa. The American assistance would be a huge relief, but how long will the United States have to continue such extensive aid? And what if Donald Trump becomes president again in November? The Biden administration never publicly discusses this. Even as the wear and tear of American support for Ukraine was confirmed, Washington showed its inability to influence Israeli decisions and to contemplate post-war scenarios. This created the impression of an administration being more reactive to events rather than shaping them. It took the deaths of seven Western and Palestinian humanitarians from the World Central Kitchen organization in Gaza on April 2, killed by Israeli strikes, to see a shift in American policy and unprecedented pressure from the United States on the Israeli state. The greatest vulnerability of the United States does not concern the means deployed or the objectives chosen. It lies in the image projected, its coherence in the eyes of the rest of the world. From Kiev to Gaza, the stark contrast in the United States is all the more flagrant, in terms of the principles and values invoked, as it is denied. The White House, which had sought to gather the widest possible coalition against Russia in the name of international law, found itself alone in blocking several resolutions at the UN Security Council calling for an immediate ceasefire in Gaza. The same White House, which rightly condemned Russia's massive war crimes in Ukraine, turned a blind eye to the situation in the Palestinian enclave. As of April 4, it was still asserting that the State Department had not "identified any incidents in which Israelis violated international humanitarian law" in Gaza. Countries in the Global South are realizing that Washington does not place the same value on Palestinian and Ukrainian lives. This observation holds true, even when considering the obvious differences between the two crises, especially the atrocities committed by Hamas that triggered the crisis in the Middle East. Perhaps never before, since the lies of the Bush administration about the existence of weapons of mass destruction in Iraq that preceded the 2003 invasion, has American credibility been so weakened. Week after week, officials shifted from one euphemism to another, refusing to set red lines. By the end of October 2023, anonymous sources in Washington were expressing discomfort in the press about the human toll of the ground operation in Gaza. On December 12, Joe Biden even criticized the "blind bombings" by the Israeli army in the Palestinian enclave. However, this discomfort did not translate into action in the following months, as the Israeli state systematically destroyed universities, schools, medical centers, warehouses, obliterating any future for the people of Gaza. Journalists and humanitarian workers were killed, among tens of thousands of other civilians. American officials tried to convince their Israeli counterparts to recalibrate the operation while supporting its ultimate goal: the destruction of Hamas. Despite a shift in rhetoric in Washington, taking more account of Palestinian suffering, the strategy remained the same: massive support for Israel. In a strange turn of events, the great power in a way submitted to its ally driven by burning grief, unbridled anger, and existential fear. The United States tolerated the extremist impulses of the coalition led by Benjamin Netanyahu, responding only marginally, for example with symbolic sanctions against settlers guilty of violence in the West Bank. The Biden administration had publicly ruled out the establishment of a buffer zone along the Gaza Strip, which would have further reduced livable space. Israel did it. And? Nothing. The bombing of the Iranian consulate in Damascus can be seen as a bold, precise, and successful Israeli operation. But, once again, it is an act with ill-timed and indefensible timing in terms of international law. Fearing that American troops in the Middle East might be targeted again, the White House denied any involvement in the attack, but did not condemn the fact that it targeted a diplomatic compound. The issue of humanitarian aid crystallized American contradictions. In early August 2023, before the UN Security Council, Secretary of State Antony Blinken denounced the Russian "blackmail" of blocking the movement of Ukrainian grain in the Black Sea: "Enough of treating the most vulnerable people as a tool of pressure! Enough of this unjustified and unacceptable war!" Yet, what has Israel done if not block humanitarian aid to Gaza under false pretenses? While the far-right within the Netanyahu coalition saw it as a tool of revenge against the Palestinians, some supposedly more strategic minds saw this organized famine as a means of pressure on Hamas to release the hostages. Between the cruelty of the former and the naivety of the latter, the result is the same. While aid flow has certainly increased recently, it does not correct what came before. The American administration claims that it has been committed, since Joe Biden's visit to Israel on October 18, 2023, to facilitating the delivery of humanitarian aid - such as its floating pontoon project off the coast of Gaza. But Washington has never pressured the Israeli government, never set an ultimatum, nor conditioned its military support. According to official briefings, obstacles to aid distribution seemed to stem only from a chaos without a specific origin, or a natural catastrophe. Washington's isolation is not the result of encirclement by its rivals or a conspiracy. It results from its choices, and sometimes their absence.
lead: The ambiguities of American diplomacy in the face of the two major conflicts have weakened the Biden administration, just months before the presidential election. By seeking to avoid escalation without ever imposing the conditions for a settlement, the United States has lost credibility.

title: La Russie annonce le retrait partiel de ses troupes de maintien de la paix en Arménie
text: On Thursday, May 9, Russia announced the withdrawal of some of its soldiers and border guards from Armenia, following a meeting between President Vladimir Putin and Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan. Yerevan has been distancing itself from Moscow for several months. The two leaders met on Wednesday evening in Moscow on the sidelines of a meeting of the Eurasian Economic Union, which includes both countries. The number of soldiers affected has not been specified by the two countries. Hayk Konjorian, head of the Civil Contract party (the ruling party in the Armenian Parliament, which also includes Mr. Pashinyan), stated on Thursday that the agreement concerned Russian military and border posts established in five Armenian regions since the end of 2020. However, the withdrawal does not seem to affect the main Russian military base in the country, located in Gyumri (with around 3,000 soldiers). "Mr. Pashinyan stated that at the present time, due to the evolving situation, this need no longer exists," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov stated on Thursday. "President Putin has therefore agreed, and the withdrawal of our soldiers and border guards has been decided," he added, as reported by Russian news agencies. Armenia's closer ties with the West Armenia, a traditional ally of Russia, criticizes the Russian peacekeeping force for not intervening during Azerbaijan's offensive that led to the recapture of the disputed enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh in September 2023. This attack forced around 100,000 of the 120,000 Armenians living in the mountainous region to flee to Armenian territory. Since the fall of 2020, following a war lost by the Armenian separatists of Nagorno-Karabakh, 2,000 Russian peacekeeping soldiers had been deployed there to enforce the ceasefire terms. Faced with their inaction during the Azerbaijani offensive, Yerevan began a diplomatic rapprochement with the West. Armenia notably joined the International Criminal Court at the end of January, obliging it to arrest Vladimir Putin if he were to set foot on Armenian territory. Armenian authorities had already announced in early March the end of the presence of Russian border guards at Yerevan's international airport. On April 17, the Kremlin stated that Russian soldiers deployed in Nagorno-Karabakh had begun their withdrawal.
lead: Yerevan reproaches Russia, its traditional ally, for not intervening during the Azerbaijani offensive that allowed the disputed enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh to be retaken in September 2023.

title: Agression contre Leonid Volkov, un proche d’Alexeï Navalny : arrestation de trois suspects en Pologne, dont un Biélorusse soupçonné d’en être le commanditaire
text: The Polish Prime Minister announced on Friday, April 19, the arrest of a Belarusian suspected of being the mastermind behind the attack on Leonid Volkov, a close ally of the late Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny, in March in Vilnius. "A Belarusian working for the Russians, who instructed two Poles to carry out the attack against [Alexei] Navalny's associate, has been detained. The assailants are already in custody as well," wrote Donald Tusk on X, specifying that the attackers were "linked to radical hooligan circles." Earlier on Friday, Lithuanian President Gitanas Nauseda had announced that two other individuals suspected of assaulting Leonid Volkov had been arrested in Poland. "The suspects were arrested on April 3 in the Republic of Poland, in Warsaw," said Justas Laucius, chief prosecutor in Vilnius, to the press. "They are two Polish citizens," he added, mentioning that the opposition figure had been attacked "due to his political activities and opinions." "During the arrest operation, five searches were also conducted, leading to the seizure of objects and evidence necessary for the investigation," stated Saulius Briginas, deputy chief of the Lithuanian criminal police. "The detained individuals are known to the Polish police," he continued. During a joint press conference, the Lithuanian police and prosecutor's office, however, declined to confirm if they had evidence linking Volkov's assault to Russian special services. "If everything goes as we hope, we should see these two detainees in Lithuania in May," Mr. Laucius further stated. The Polish police, for their part, simply stated during the day on X that they had arrested two individuals under a European arrest warrant, suspected of "attacking a Russian opponent." Leonid Volkov praises the police work Mr. Volkov immediately expressed his happiness at this announcement. "I have seen with what energy and perseverance the Lithuanian police have worked on this case over the past month, and I am very pleased that this work has borne fruit," wrote the Russian opposition figure on the Telegram network. "We will soon know the details. I am looking forward to learning them!" he added. Attacked on March 12 with a hammer in front of his home in Vilnius, 43-year-old Mr. Volkov had to be briefly hospitalized. Lithuanian intelligence services, who quickly mentioned a likely attack "organized by Russia," later suspected that it had been carried out by someone recruited locally. His assault took place nearly a month after the death of Alexei Navalny on February 16 in a Russian Arctic penal colony where he was serving a nineteen-year sentence for "extremism," and a few days before the election that confirmed Vladimir Putin's continued hold on power. Navalny's death is attributed by his loved ones to the Russian president, against whom he was a leading opponent.
lead: The announcement of the arrest of the Belarusian citizen was made by the Polish Prime Minister, Donald Tusk, on Friday. Earlier, the Lithuanian President had announced that two other individuals suspected of assaulting Leonid Volkov on March 12 in front of his home in Vilnius had been arrested.

title: « Accueillir Xi Jinping est une politique de complicité du génocide ouïgour »
text: At a time when the Russian state is rightfully banned from the Paris Olympics and severely sanctioned by Europeans for the aggressive war it is waging in Ukraine, at a time when its recently re-elected leader, Vladimir Putin, is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court (ICC), Emmanuel Macron is hosting Xi Jinping in France, the president of a country, China, which continues with impunity its genocidal policy against the Uighurs. China continues to use forced labor and subjects hundreds of thousands of Uighurs to slavery, millions of Uighurs have been imprisoned in camps and prisons because of their ethno-religious identity, families of French Uighur origin are still detained in China, the vast majority of Uighurs in France have lost all contact with their relatives and live under constant threat and intimidation. But France welcomes, in the name of a proclaimed reopening of the country after the Covid years and a policy of rapprochement with China, the architect and main perpetrator of the genocide targeting my people and inexorably destroying them. Our nights filled with nightmares, our disrupted genealogies, our sense of powerlessness, and our fear of being erased from the history of humanity therefore make no sense to Emmanuel Macron. Inviting the genocidal ogre to the Elysée, sharing a "more personal" time with him, all this to improve our commercial relations and to celebrate sixty years of "diplomatic friendships," what a strange banquet of ashes! Concentration camps and prisons But what, exactly, will he still have the right to discuss with Xi Jinping? The planned annexation of Taiwan? Tibetan children forcibly separated from their families and placed in state orphanages? The repression of dissident voices and political prisoners? Uighur victims, in Europe and in France, of transnational repression? Chinese ghost police stations violating France's territorial sovereignty? On January 20, 2022, in a solemn resolution, the National Assembly condemned the genocide of the Uighurs, recognized that the Chinese regime was imprisoning and torturing Uighurs, Kazakhs, and other Turkic peoples in concentration camps and prisons, that Uighur women were forcibly sterilized, that the Uighur population had halved in record time – in some prefectures, this drop is 84%; it recognized that hundreds of thousands of Uighur children were separated from their families to be assimilated into Chinese culture in state orphanages or Han families. The National Assembly recognized that Uighur identity was criminalized, Uighur language banned; it recognized that Uighur elites (artists, intellectuals, businesspeople, athletes, religious leaders...) were arrested and sometimes tortured, killed, disappeared. Even the parliamentary group of the President of the Republic - undoubtedly with his consent - overwhelmingly supported this resolution. Mr. Macron has even intervened in the past in favor of an Uyghur woman who survived the hell of the camps. He knows very well what is going on. But he remains silent and does nothing to stop this genocidal policy and to hold those responsible accountable. The Uyghur issue does not deserve such contempt. Yet it concerns France, which is connected in multiple ways with China. It concerns the Uyghurs residing in France, many of whom are also French. As French or Uyghurs in France, we are not only devastated, psychologically and sometimes physically, by the genocide: we live under the threat, intimidation, and insecurity. China benefits in France from numerous and powerful relays. Sense of injustice Welcoming our oppressor with all the honors of a state visit only reinforces the sense of injustice, powerlessness, and insecurity of the Uyghur diaspora in France. The message being sent is that the lives of the Uyghur people weigh little against the billions of Chinese yuan. This policy of contempt is also a policy of complicity, as economic cooperation with China strengthens Chinese racial capitalism that relies on forced labor. Mr. President, I have repeatedly called on you before your official trips to China or before Xi Jinping's visit to France, to pay attention to the crimes of this regime and Chinese colonialism, which is intrinsically linked to the policy of eradicating the Uyghurs. Neither the open letter co-signed by MEP Raphaël Glucksmann in 2019 [published in the newspaper Libération], nor the 250,000 signatories in 2021 of our national petition, have been enough for you to listen to us. As a Frenchwoman of Uyghur origin, as a professor specializing in Uyghur studies and colonialism, I am outraged, on behalf of the Uyghur diaspora in France, by your "friendship" with the oppressor of the Uyghur people. Words have meaning: it is indeed, in the Uyghur region, an ongoing genocide. You, who have acknowledged the tragic mistakes of French policy in Rwanda without being able to cleanse the honor of France, permanently tarnished for having supported the Hutu government that would perpetrate the genocide of the Tutsis, do not remain in history as the one who remained silent on the Uyghur genocide, knowing and doing nothing when you could have.
lead: The sociologist Dilnur Reyhan, a French woman of Uyghur origin, calls on President Macron not to remain silent on Beijing's genocidal policy when meeting with Xi Jinping.

title: Géorgie : « Le gouvernement a peur des jeunes »
text:
The ruling party in Georgia, a former Soviet republic in the South Caucasus, has called on its supporters to demonstrate on Monday, April 29 to support its controversial draft law on "foreign agents," which was passed in the first reading on April 17 despite public outrage and condemnation from Western countries. Opponents of the bill, which was abandoned a year earlier under pressure and modeled after a Russian law aimed at silencing independent media and civil society, have also called for demonstrations on the same day. On Thursday, April 25, the European Parliament also condemned the draft law and called for sanctions against the founder of the ruling party, the oligarch and former Prime Minister Bidzina Ivanichvili. Georgian political analyst and former Minister of Education in 2008, Ghia Nodia, who is a professor at Ilia State University in Tbilisi, decrypts the government's strategy and describes himself as "pessimistic."

Why did the ruling party, Georgian Dream, choose to reintroduce the draft law on foreign agents, which was abandoned under popular and international pressure in March 2023? There are several hypotheses. Parliamentary elections are scheduled for October. Elections can be a challenging time for those in power, as there can be accusations of fraud and protests. One of the objectives is therefore to silence the media and civil society before the election. According to another theory, Georgian Dream, whose popularity is declining, wants to engage in a decisive battle with the weakened opposition even before the election. These two theories are not mutually exclusive.

Is this strategy not risky for the ruling party? Yes, it is a very risky gamble for Bidzina Ivanichvili, who runs the country from behind the scenes. The more optimistic view is that he made a mistake, galvanizing the population against him, including those among his supporters who are still pro-European. Has he been too confident? He is a very secretive man, but like Vladimir Putin, he is paranoid and convinced that the opposition and civil society, with Western support, are plotting a "Maidan" [referring to the pro-European revolution in Ukraine in the winter of 2013-2014 that ousted pro-Russian President Viktor Yanukovych]. He is convinced that there is an attempt to get rid of him and will do everything to prevent it. However, Ivanichvili cannot openly be pro-Russian because it would be too unpopular [Russia is seen as an enemy by the population, traumatized by the 2008 war with Moscow, whose troops currently occupy 20% of Georgian territory]. Therefore, officially, Georgian Dream, of which he is the founder, continues to claim to be pro-European while treating Western countries as enemies. When this bill was first introduced in 2023, the government backed down under pressure. How is the situation different today? Last year, the government seemed very determined and harsh. Protesters had been arrested, beaten, and sprayed with tear gas. The abandonment of the bill had been a surprise. Nothing has changed in substance today. But the new development is that in the meantime, Georgia has obtained the official candidate status for the European Union, even though Georgian Dream ignored its recommendations to achieve it - only three out of twelve were fulfilled. The population was happy to obtain this status, but this European decision was perceived as a sign of weakness by Mr. Ivanichvili. He concluded that, no matter what he does, Europe still needs Georgia, that it will not let it fall into Russia's hands, and will make compromises. Was the European Union wrong to grant Georgia this candidate status in December 2023? The mistake was not granting it this status in 2022 [Georgia faced an initial refusal from Brussels in June 2022, unlike Ukraine and Moldova, two other former Soviet republics that had requested this status at the same time]. If this decision was based on geopolitical considerations and the population's investment, then it should have been done in 2022. As it stands, the EU's attitude generates uncertainty. What is the mood of the Georgians since the return of this bill to Parliament? They feel that the country is at a turning point. Some of them are very angry, worried, and determined not to let this law pass, as it would steer the country away from the path towards European integration. Another part is more resigned: they think the country is heading in a very bad direction but that they can do nothing about it and will have to give up the dream of Europe. For everyone, the return of this bill was a total surprise. If the protests are truly massive, the ruling party may back down - they have done so before. But this time, I am quite pessimistic. The protests were significant on the day the bill was adopted in the first reading, but unfortunately, we would need more people to make a "Maidan." We are probably not as good as the Ukrainians [during their 2013-2014 revolution], who were organized and demonstrated massively every day. Here, it is mainly the youth who are protesting, and the opposition is marginalized. The strategy is therefore to keep resources for decisive moments. In 2023, the surprise and spontaneous mobilization of Generation Z had been decisive. What about today? The youth are once again very mobilized. They all speak English, are very active on social media, and feel they belong to the West, even though many have never been there. They understand that the government wants to steer them away from Europe towards Russia. The threat is real. We could lose candidate status for the EU and our visa regime [since 2017, Georgians traveling to the EU do not need visas for short stays]. The government is afraid of the youth, as it is much easier to pressure older people who have jobs and families than 16-year-old teenagers. If the law is passed, it will be very difficult to calm the anger of society.
lead: The political scientist Ghia Nodia believes, in an interview with "Le Monde", that the return of the controversial bill on foreign agents bears the mark of Bidzina Ivanichvili, the shadow leader of the country, convinced, "like Vladimir Putin", that the West supports civil society to overthrow the government.

title: Elections européennes 2024 : Saidali Boina Hamissi, candidat sur la liste du RN, critiqué pour ses propos complotistes et racistes
text: Two ministers strongly criticized, on Tuesday, April 23, the presence on the list of the National Rally (RN) of Saidali Boina Hamissi, a Mahoran whose Libération revealed had made conspiracy and racist remarks on his Facebook page. The ministers also called him out for his openly expressed admiration for Vladimir Putin and his support for polygamy. During a visit to Mayotte, the leader of the National Rally's deputies, Marine Le Pen, announced that the party's departmental representative, aged 63, was joining the list led by Jordan Bardella for the European elections, "probably in a non-eligible position, unless the voters decide otherwise." "It's a signal that is being sent to Mayotte, to say that, historically for the first time, there is a Mahoran candidate who will be included on a list for the European elections," she declared on Outremer La 1ère last Saturday. "As usual with Marine Le Pen and the National Rally, (...) they have allies whose statements they don't really check: this gentleman clearly admires [Russian President Vladimir] Putin, he supports polygamy," denounced the Minister Delegate for Overseas, Marie Guévenoux, on Sud Radio. Saidali Boina Hamissi "participated in conspiracy theories, made extremely serious and dangerous remarks about women's supposed submission to their spouses," criticized the government spokesperson, Prisca Thevenot, on LCI. On Monday, Libération had indeed denounced "violently racist and conspiratorial content" posted by the RN representative in Mayotte on his Facebook page. Among the posts highlighted by the newspaper were messages calling the Comorian residents of a neighborhood in Mamoudzou "vermin" and "cockroaches," as well as messages about women's submission to their husbands. There were also conspiracy posts in which Saidali Boina Hamissi expressed his rejection of anti-Covid vaccines, and "several recent posts [in which] he praises the Russian leader [Vladimir Putin] and denounces France's support for Ukraine," as reported by Libération.
lead: After the revelations by "Libération", two ministers denounced the online content published by the representative from Mayotte of the party, who will be on Jordan Bardella's European list, as announced by Marine Le Pen during a visit to Mayotte on Saturday.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : « La confiscation des avoirs publics russes constituerait une avance sur les réparations »
text: We welcome the initiative of the European Council, which has agreed to tax the interest generated by Russian public funds immobilized in various European states. This tax, allocated 90% to the purchase of armaments for Ukraine, constitutes a first step. However, this decision seems quite timid when comparing the one billion euros promised to Ukrainians with the 200 billion euros in assets that the Central Bank of Russia has deposited in European financial institutions (out of a total of nearly 300 billion euros placed outside of Russia). Ukraine could immediately use these funds to begin repairing the damages suffered from the destruction of its civilian infrastructure, systematically targeted by Russian missiles. The reconstruction of hospitals, schools, universities, and power plants cannot wait for the indefinite end of hostilities. How can Ukraine's finances, whose revenues are reduced by the decline in economic activities, ensure the payment of pensions to war invalids, widows, and orphans? How to finance the long and costly care of the countless wounded? How to invest in the reconstruction of the hundreds of thousands of destroyed homes? How to continue the education of the generation that will have to rebuild Ukraine after the war? The confiscation of Russian public assets would be an advance on the reparations that the aggressor will ultimately have to pay. The damages caused by the Russian Federation on Ukrainian territory are estimated by the World Bank at nearly 450 billion euros to date. This is already significantly more than the frozen Russian assets. While Emmanuel Macron has argued that no limits should be set a priori on the action of Ukraine's allies, why not implement, as part of reparations, legitimate confiscation measures in accordance with customary international law? A document entitled "State Responsibility for Internationally Wrongful Acts" has been developed by the International Law Commission established within the United Nations (UN): a state that has suffered damages caused by another state can take countermeasures, such as confiscating the assets of the aggressor, to compel them to repair the harm they have caused. This same customary law is a source of law, enshrined in the statute of the International Court of Justice (ICJ), which it can apply in disputes between states. It is up to the aggressor to pay. The Russian aggression has been unanimously recognized as unlawful and condemned by both the UN General Assembly (resolution of March 2, 2022) and the ICJ (March 16, 2022), the Council of Europe, the European Union (EU), and the European Court of Human Rights. The freezing of assets of the Central Bank of Russia is among the thirteen economic sanctions imposed on Russia, a freeze that Russia has not challenged in court. From a legal standpoint, it is up to Ukraine, recognized as the injured state, to claim compensation for its damages from Russia. Ukraine's formal request to confiscate the immobilized Russian public assets is a necessary prerequisite for its execution, with the assistance of the states holding the capital. Switzerland and the United Kingdom have already begun exploring the modalities of implementing such confiscation. Why are the EU and its member states delaying the implementation of this procedure? Is it the fear of Kremlin's wrath? It is worth noting that Russia did not hesitate, in direct violation of international law, to acquire European companies at low cost, forcing them to transfer their assets to the Russian state or Putin's loyalists. What more can Putin's regime do, having declared war on the "West"? Is it because confiscation measures might scare other states that have entrusted us with their assets? This argument does not hold, as only an aggressor state can, under the law, be targeted by a countermeasure taken for the benefit of an aggressed state. And most importantly, how do we explain to European citizens that no measure is excluded and that we will have to increase our commitment if we do not engage in such a simple and just action? It is up to the aggressor to pay for repairing their crimes, not to the taxpayers of the allied countries of Ukraine. Cooperation is necessary not only among EU states but also among G7 countries, which hold Russian assets. However, given the urgency, France, which holds around 20 billion euros of Russian assets, can and must set an example. This is the purpose of the bipartisan resolution project submitted to the National Assembly by deputies Benjamin Haddad (Renaissance) and Julien Bayou (non-affiliated, former EELV). It is imperative that our country, at Ukraine's request, implements all necessary measures for the confiscation of Russian public assets deposited in France. Assisting the Ukrainian state in confiscating these assets would be an act of upholding international law. Indirectly, it would also help alleviate the Ukrainian state budget allocated to reconstruction, as it must ensure essential military expenses in the fight for its freedom... and ours.
lead: A group of academics and legal experts, including Antoine Garapon, Thomas Piketty, Sylvie Rollet, and Dominique Schnapper, are calling on European heads of state to use Russian money deposited in banks on their territory by invoking customary international law, as stated in an article in "Le Monde".

title: Xi Jinping flatte et cherche à renforcer les dirigeants eurosceptiques
text: There has been much talk of "friendship" in Serbia and Hungary during Xi Jinping's visit. A friendship "as solid as a rock" in Belgrade, the "best [friendship] in history" in Budapest... The Chinese president used superlatives to praise his hosts, Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic and Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who rolled out the red carpet for him and covered their capitals with Chinese flags. While the French leg of the trip, from Sunday, May 5 to Tuesday, May 7, highlighted, despite the cordial tone, the accumulation of disputes between China and the European Union, from subsidies to the electric vehicle industry to the increasing support given by Xi to Vladimir Putin, the rest of the trip was meant to show that Beijing has allies in Europe. Vucic and Orban share a pronounced anti-Western rhetoric, both are implementing democratic setbacks in their countries, and have stormy relationships with the EU, even though Serbia is not yet a member. "Our voice is lonely in Europe," Orban acknowledged in a press statement alongside Xi Jinping, arguing that "there is now a new world order, no longer unipolar, but multipolar, with one of its pillars being the People's Republic of China." Xi did not hear any criticism during his visits to Serbia and Hungary, with the relationships being radiant, far from the complaints expressed in Paris about his trade policies. The relationship with Hungary was elevated to the status of a "comprehensive global strategic partnership," placing it among the most highly regarded by Beijing. Numerous sectoral cooperation agreements were signed - "26" in Belgrade, "18" in Budapest. Nuclear cooperation Their content remains somewhat vague, but in Serbia, there was talk of developing cooperation between Serbian and Chinese media, while Brussels has been calling on this Balkan country for years to improve its press freedom if it wants to join the EU. In Hungary, new railway projects were discussed, in addition to the train line between Belgrade and Budapest currently under construction by China, and for the first time, cooperation in the nuclear sector. However, contrary to expectations, no concrete investments were announced, even though Chinese companies have heavily invested in both countries in recent years. With this double visit, Xi mainly succeeded in showing Brussels that he was counting on these two capitals to prevent potential retaliation against his economic policies. China "supports Hungary in playing a more significant role within the EU," he stated, as Hungary prepares to take over the rotating presidency of the European Council in July. On the other hand, Serbia signed a free trade agreement with China in 2023 that could be used by European importers to bypass trade barriers. Prior to his arrival in Serbia, Mr. Xi had mentioned in a local press article the NATO bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade in 1999: "We will never allow such a tragic history to repeat itself." However, the Chinese president does not appear to have visited the site, which has been transformed into a Chinese cultural center. For an Olympic truce, in front of the cameras, both Mr. Vucic and Mr. Orban also pledged to support Beijing on the Taiwan issue, much to the delight of the Chinese leader. "Taiwan is China," Mr. Vucic clearly defended in front of a crowd of supporters he had brought in to cheer the Chinese president. He added, "China supports the territorial integrity of Serbia in the same way, without any reservations." Beijing, which interprets international affairs in light of its own claim on Taiwan, does not recognize the independence of Kosovo. "We have always believed in the principle of one China," echoed Mr. Orban in Budapest. The contrast was striking with Mr. Xi's visit to France, where the exchanges were "friendly and very frank," according to the French side, but did not mask deep divergences. Brussels is expected to announce the conclusions of its investigation into Chinese subsidies for electric car factories in a few weeks. The EU "will not hesitate to take firm decisions," warned European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, who was present at the Elysée on Monday, considering "unfair trade" and "massive subsidies" as unacceptable. A dialogue of the deaf. "The so-called issue of Chinese overcapacity does not exist," retorted the Chinese president, claiming that the Chinese new energy industry helps increase global supply and thereby mitigate inflation. The gift of French cognac bottles underscored fears of Beijing targeting French alcohol sales, as China believes Paris pushed for the investigation into the automotive sector. The main concrete progress was a joint call for an Olympic truce in conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East. However, on the economic support that allows the Russian arms industry to operate at full capacity, China has not budged. "We oppose using this crisis to shift blame onto a third country, tarnish its image, and incite a new Cold War," warned Mr. Xi. In return, Mr. Xi expressed hope that Hungary will help "maintain the correct direction" in the China-EU relationship. In an article in the pro-government newspaper Magyar Nemzet, before his arrival, he praised the "golden journey" of their bilateral relationship, comparing it to a Tokay wine, "sweet, deep, and age-worthy." He quoted a Chinese proverb, as if to show that in his view, Budapest is now politically closer to Beijing than to Brussels: "Neither mountains nor oceans separate those who share the same aspirations."
lead: Welcomed with great fanfare in Serbia and then in Hungary, the Chinese leader praised the economic partnerships with these two countries, with which he shares a similar distrust towards Europe.

title: En France, l’inquiétude montante des défenseurs des libertés individuelles
text: More than thirty political groups or associations have been dissolved since 2017; multiple bans on demonstrations against pension reform or in support of Palestine; bans on political meetings such as the one by Jean-Luc Mélenchon in mid-April; police summons of opposition figures - for example, Mathilde Panot, president of the La France insoumise group in the National Assembly - for "apology of terrorism"; the conviction of a CGT union leader to a one-year suspended prison sentence for the same offense... Is France experiencing an authoritarian shift, as denounced by part of the left and the far left? Since October 7, 2023, the day of the Hamas terrorist attack in Israel followed by the Israeli military response, mobilization around the defense of Gaza residents has been intensifying: calls for demonstrations are multiplying, as are protests in universities. Daily political positions, often controversial, are being taken. Most of the time, they come from the left and the far left, who denounce a risk of "genocide" by Israel. The Minister of Justice, Eric Dupond-Moretti, issued a circular as early as October 10, 2023, calling for "a firm and rapid penal response" to antisemitism and "apology of terrorism," which, according to the text, is constituted by "statements praising the attacks [by Hamas], presenting them as a legitimate resistance to Israel." "First shift" after 2015 But the repressive turn did not begin on October 7. The first cuts in the exercise of certain freedoms are a bit older, note those who regret them. "A first shift occurred after 2015 and the state of emergency following Islamist attacks, then with the integration of certain measures into common law," says Arié Alimi, lawyer and member of the League of Human Rights (LDH). Furthermore, since that date, many social movements have taken place, sometimes harsh, resulting in a very forceful police response. For example, opposition to the labor law (2016), the "yellow vests" movement (2018-2019); the movement against the "global security" law (2021), and then against the pension reform (2023)... "During this period, we have seen an increase in police violence, a brutalization of law enforcement," continues the lawyer. "On the right to demonstrate, there has been a paradigm shift: before, freedom was the principle and prohibitions were extremely rare." According to Stéphanie Hennette-Vauchez, professor of public law at Paris-X and author of Democracy in a State of Emergency. When the exception becomes permanent (Seuil, 2022), the current phenomenon is complex and multifaceted. "The first aspect involves clear political choices: Emmanuel Macron's presidency is marked by a peak in administrative dissolutions, by bans on demonstrations." "The very broad use of the offense of apologizing for terrorism should also make us question," says the academic. She adds a second element to her reflection: "The challenge by political leaders of the established standards of the rule of law, that is, the principle that public action is constrained by the law." Stéphanie Hennette-Vauchez cites Gérald Darmanin, who said he "assumes" not to respect a decision of the European Court of Human Rights regarding the expulsion of an Uzbek suspected of being an Islamist. Another example: the way the government behaved with the Constitutional Council, at the end of December 2023, during the adoption of the "immigration" law. Gérald Darmanin, Elisabeth Borne, and Emmanuel Macron all acknowledged that the text contained provisions contrary to the Constitution. "All of this put together erodes the edifice of the rule of law," worries Mrs. Hennette-Vauchez. "We are not in Russia." Yves Sintomer, a political scientist and professor at Paris-VIII, tempers: "We must not overestimate the seriousness of the situation. We are neither in Putin's Russia nor in Pinochet's Chile. We must not deny certain worrying facts, such as the repressive turn, but also the bans on demonstrations and pressures on academic freedoms." On this last point, he describes a "climate of self-censorship and a sense of threat never experienced before." The other question that arises is whether these restrictions affect all political families. Marion Ogier, a lawyer and member of the LDH and the Syndicat des avocats de France (SAF, left), is convinced: certain struggles are particularly targeted. "As soon as it concerns certain themes like Palestine, the defense of Muslims, the fight against Islamophobia or police violence, the risk of prohibition is very high. Each time, it is assumed that this will lead to disturbances or anti-Semitic messages," she believes. However, in terms of dissolutions, organizations from the far right, far left, or advocating for political Islam have been targeted in recent years, even though these three currents are of different natures and do not represent the same danger, especially for the safety of individuals. The same goes for bans on meetings and demonstrations. Precisely, the Police Prefecture "is considering" banning the annual parade of the radical far right, in tribute to the nationalist militant Sébastien Deyzieu, who died in 1994, scheduled for May 11 in Paris. "This demonstration is likely to pose several public order difficulties," notes the Prefecture.
lead: Dissolutions, bans on demonstrations, police summonses have multiplied since the establishment of the state of emergency in 2015, with accelerations in 2017 and following the terrorist attack by Hamas on October 7, 2023. Some see these as violations of the rule of law.

title: Géorgie : l’Eglise orthodoxe, fidèle alliée du pouvoir et du Kremlin
text: To pass its controversial law on "foreign influence," the Georgian government can count on the support of the Orthodox Church, whose ties with Moscow are alarming pro-Europeans in the country. In a statement published on April 27, the patriarchate broke its neutrality, siding with the government's position. It expressed support for this new legislation, inspired by a Russian law aimed at silencing opposition to Vladimir Putin's regime, which requires organizations receiving more than 20% of their funding from abroad to register as an "entity defending the interests of a foreign power." Warning against "dangerous" ideologies that exacerbate societal polarization, criticizing "NGOs and television channels funded by foreign capital that conduct campaigns to discredit the Church," the senior clergy in Georgia praised the government's choice to protect "traditional values" in its statement. Beyond the law, the current confrontation between the Georgian Dream, the ruling party, and the main actors of civil society will be decisive in determining whether the small Caucasus republic maintains its fledgling democracy or succumbs to the authoritarian Russian model. "It is a choice between two ways of life, between European values, respectful of human dignity, and the Russian vision of the world, based on expansionism, war, death, and destruction. Personally, I am for the European Union because its aspirations seem closer to Christian values," explains Father Zaza, 65, who serves at St. Nicholas Church in the historic Narikala district of Tbilisi. Resolutely opposed to the law, the priest supports the protest movement that has been ongoing for three weeks in Tbilisi and other cities in Georgia and is expected to resume on Monday, May 13, when the Parliament will consider the bill in its third reading. "This law is toxic; it aims to create 'enemies of the people' as during the purges of 1937. If adopted, independent media will disappear, any criticism will be punished, and the vibrant and enlightened forces of society will have to leave the country to build a future elsewhere," predicts the clergyman, with a somber look. He is aware that his position is extremely minority. His hierarchy does not share it. As a simple priest, he has never been very compliant, which has earned him reprimands and a petition calling for his removal, "but I am still here, alongside my parishioners," he says with a mischievous smile. In favor of "traditional values," the senior clergy is just the opposite of his ideas. Of the forty-seven bishops who make up the Holy Synod, the council that assists the patriarch, only three have declared themselves against the law. Resolutely anti-LGBT, homophobic, anti-Western, the Georgian Church has expressed support for the "traditional values" defended by Vladimir Putin and by Patriarch Kirill, the head of the Russian Orthodox Church, whose influence remains strong on the Georgian clergy. "Our Church has never left the sphere of influence of the Kremlin," confirms Father Zaza, a slender figure, wearing a navy blue cassock and sneakers, with a small black beret on his head. In solidarity with the Moscow Patriarchate, the Georgian Church abstained from recognizing the autocephalous status of the Ukrainian Church, granted by the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople in 2019, and also avoided condemning the Russian invasion of Ukraine. "Normally, the role of the Church is to denounce wars and dictatorships. What is happening in Ukraine is horrible, brothers are killing each other, how can the Moscow Patriarchate endorse this?" laments the priest who fears for the future of his country. "With the war in Ukraine, it is clear that Russia no longer recognizes the sovereignty of the states around it. This is worrying. Especially since Georgia endured Russian occupation for two and a half centuries, a time of misfortune... I believe that the Georgian people deserve happiness. They will certainly find it more with Europe than with Russia." In 1978, at the age of 19, Father Zaza participated, along with tens of thousands of people, in the protests that took place in Parliament Square in Tbilisi to protest against the decision of the Soviet Politburo - Georgia was then part of the USSR - to restrict the use of the Georgian language in favor of Russian. "Under pressure from the streets, the Soviet leadership eventually abandoned its law. They had deployed special forces, we were expecting the worst but nothing happened, a real miracle. God was with us!" he recalls, hoping for the same outcome with the law on "foreign agents." Most stable partner of Russia In recent years, in the absence of support from the political elite and society, the Georgian Church has found itself increasingly courted by the Kremlin. A large majority of Georgians, fed up with the Russian army occupying 20% of the country's territory and always seeking to encroach a few more kilometers along the border between Georgia and South Ossetia, largely view Moscow as a threat. Pro-Russian groups are extremely unpopular in the country. But the fact that the Georgian population is very religious makes the Georgian Church the most stable partner of Putin's Russia. A report published by the Carnegie Foundation in 2021 describes the Church as "one of the most reliable institutions in Georgia." Such is the Georgian paradox: 89% of the population declares itself to be Orthodox and pro-European in equal measure, while the clergy is vehemently anti-Western. "In reality, Georgians are united by citizenship, by civic duty, more than by religious affiliation," argues theologian Beka Mindiashvili. As evidence, he points to the current mobilization against the law. "It is because their citizenship is threatened that people are coming out to protest. The Church says it is against European globalization, that Europe wants to take away our identity, morally corrupt us, that our savior is Russia, but this propaganda is not effective. The proof is that the youth do not want this model, which is why they stand in front of the riot police waving the European flag," notes the researcher, convinced that the European Union "has never been as popular as it is in Georgia." If the European enthusiasm of the youth is broken, "there will be many candidates for emigration because Georgians will never accept living under a regime like the one in Russia or Belarus," fears the theologian. According to him, "we must never forget that these Churches, both Russian and Georgian, were formed in 1943 by Stalin, who infiltrated them with agents of the secret police [KGB]. Despite the collapse of the USSR, they have remained bastions of anti-Western propaganda."
lead: If the vast majority of the population is both Orthodox and pro-European, the clergy of this Church, which owes so much to Stalin, remains anti-Western.

title: Visite de Xi Jinping en France : le soutien crucial de la Chine à la Russie dans la guerre en Ukraine au cœur des discussions
text: Xi Jinping's visit to France will once again bring Western diplomats back to a question that has been troubling them for two years: is it possible to dissuade China from further assisting Russia? The support that the Chinese economy provides to the Russian arms industry is expected to be on the agenda of discussions between the French president and his Chinese counterpart on Monday, May 6 and Tuesday, May 7. "The President of the Republic will address concerns about the activities of certain Chinese companies that could directly participate or significantly contribute to the Russian war effort," confirmed sources at the Elysée. More than direct arms supplies - a red line that China seems to have been careful not to cross so far - it is the deliveries of machine tools and components for the production of these weapons that are at the center of attention. Through commercial transactions of its companies, Beijing has enabled Moscow to revive its arms industry and gain an advantage in the conflict. On this point, Washington is urging European capitals to be more direct with Beijing. "These are not isolated incidents, but a comprehensive strategic plan implemented by China and Russia. It has had a huge impact. We believe it has helped Russia rebuild its military capabilities. These activities continue," observed US Deputy Secretary of State Kurt Campbell, speaking to a few journalists in Paris on Thursday, May 2, just before Xi Jinping's visit. "We must hold China accountable. Given France's determination to play an active and important role on the Ukrainian issue, it is essential that President Macron be clear and resolute in expressing French concerns in this area," he added. Drone engines and missile propellants While Europe has largely closed its doors to trade with Russia since the invasion of Ukraine, China has largely taken over, including in sectors that have helped revive Russian factories producing weapons and vehicles for the front lines. Chinese exports to Russia surged by 64.2% between 2021 and 2023. In recent weeks, US official sources have pointed to the role of Chinese groups like Dalian Machine Tool in supplying machine tools used in the production of ballistic missiles. In the last quarter of 2023, 70% of Russian imports of machine tools - amounting to around $900 million (835 million euros) - came from China. The same US sources accuse companies such as Wuhan Global Sensor Technology, Wuhan Tongsheng Technology, and the surveillance giant Hikvision of supplying optical components used by Moscow, especially for its tanks and armored vehicles. China has also reportedly supplied its neighbor with drone engines and propulsion systems for its cruise missiles. According to Washington, Russia was able to rapidly expand its artillery ammunition production in 2023 thanks to China's supply of nitrocellulose, which is used in the composition of shell powders. The United States also believes that China is assisting Russia in enhancing its satellite capabilities and has directly provided Moscow with imagery for the war in Ukraine. In addition to these factors, the key role played in this war by commercially available civilian drones gives China a central role as a major producer. The global leader DJI, based in Shenzhen, closed its stores in Russia and Ukraine, but Moscow acquired $14.5 million worth of drones directly from Chinese sellers in the first half of 2023, while Ukraine received drones directly from China for only $200,000 - with the majority of its other deliveries coming from European intermediaries. This imbalance is also evident in immersive piloted drones capable of carrying ammunition at speeds of up to 150 km/h, which have become prominent on the front lines: Russia assembles them itself, but primarily with Chinese components. Chinese industrial support is closely linked to the increasing alignment between Beijing and Moscow to reshape the global order - in other words, to counter the West. Chinese deliveries of CNC machines increased tenfold in a year between July 2022 and July 2023, rising from $6.5 million to $68 million. A report from the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) in Washington highlights a significant increase in deliveries of these machines, crucial in precision industries including defense, starting in March 2023. In that same month, Russian President Vladimir Putin hosted Xi in Moscow, marking Xi's first foreign visit after starting his third term as China's leader. "Changes are happening now that we haven't seen in a hundred years - and we are leading these changes together," Xi Jinping said before departing, to which Putin replied, "I agree." "Clever calculation" by Beijing "There is a real convergence of interests in seeing the West weaken. And China's calculation is very clever. The West consumes a lot of resources trying to contain Russia, and Russia also consumes a lot in the war, while China believes it benefits without having to get too involved," explains Maria Snegovaya, a researcher at CSIS. Beijing presents itself as a neutral actor. "China is neither the cause nor a party to the crisis in Ukraine, and we have done nothing and will do nothing to benefit from it," defended Chinese Foreign Ministry spokesperson Mao Ning on April 8. The People's Republic is expecting a visit from Vladimir Putin in the second half of May, his first since his controversial reelection. After placing six Chinese groups on their sanctions list for supporting Russia in February, the United States added twenty more from mainland China and Hong Kong on May 1. The European Union has also finally sanctioned some Chinese companies suspected of circumventing Western sanctions. "Our own policy towards China" Chinese actors, highly dependent on international markets, are not completely immune to pressure. Under the threat of sanctions from the U.S. Treasury, Chinese banks have been much more cautious in their dealings with Russia in recent months. During visits to Beijing in April, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen and then Secretary of State Antony Blinken issued repeated warnings. Washington is urging Western European leaders to do the same in their interactions with Chinese officials, as they believe that Beijing, as the world's top exporter, has a strong need for the European market. American officials have therefore emphasized this message before Chancellor Olaf Scholz's visit to China from April 14 to 16, and before Xi Jinping's arrival in France on Monday, May 6. However, German and French diplomacy do not seem to automatically align with the United States on this issue. "We have our own independent policy towards China that is separate from the public stance taken by American authorities," a French source explained. Without harboring any illusions about the ambiguities of China's position in the war in Ukraine, Paris hopes to maintain a dialogue with Beijing while Washington takes a more confrontational approach.
lead: Chinese companies have allowed Moscow to revive its arms industry and gain an advantage in the conflict.

title: Pour l’Ukraine, l’aide militaire des Etats-Unis doit permettre de « stabiliser la situation », puis de « reprendre l’initiative »
text:
The Ukrainians have learned that in war, things rarely go as planned. Along with feelings of gratitude and relief after the US Congress vote on military aid to Ukraine on Saturday, April 20, there are many unanswered questions about the future of the conflict. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky praised the American support, stating that it will "save thousands and thousands of lives" and keep history on the right path. He added that this support will be felt by soldiers on the front line, as well as by cities and villages suffering from Russian terror, and that it will bring a just end to a war that Putin must lose. The $60.8 billion (57 billion euros) aid from Washington will help the Ukrainian armed forces strengthen both the thousand kilometers of the front line and the anti-aircraft defense of cities.

In recent weeks, faced with the deadlock in the US Congress, Kiev had been increasingly pessimistic. The idea of a military collapse was no longer taboo. Zelensky had warned on April 7, "If Congress does not help Ukraine, Ukraine will lose the war." Ukrainians find it difficult to grasp the reality of the military situation and the concrete consequences of this new American aid. While they have been reading testimonies from fighters for months about the lack of ammunition against the Russian firepower, they also see that Moscow has not significantly advanced despite its military superiority.

The Russian army, which initially aimed to conquer Ukraine in a few days to overthrow the government and neutralize its armed forces, has only managed to take two cities in Donbass after eighteen months of stabilizing the front lines: Bakhmut and Avdiivka. The Russian offensive continues on these two fronts, currently threatening Tchassiv Yar, west of Bakhmut. The Ukrainian military commander, General Oleksandr Syrsky, recently mentioned "a significantly deteriorated situation" on the eastern front, while President Zelensky admitted in an interview with NBC after the vote in the House of Representatives that Ukraine has "lost the initiative" and that the new American aid should help "stabilize the situation" and then "regain the initiative."

For Ukrainians, these difficulties on the front line mainly mean suffering for the fighters. However, it is hard to imagine that the same Russian army, inching forward for months near Tchassiv Yar, could once again threaten the country with a general invasion. The prospect of a collapse of the front is not ruled out in military circles in Kiev, even if it is considered that catastrophic speeches may also have a tactical dimension aimed at obtaining more Western support. "Russia has regained the strategic advantage. The question is which major city could we lose this year before having the ability to go back on the offensive," testifies, anonymously, an officer from the general staff. The Russian offensive of 2024 is expected "between May and July," predicted President Zelensky, "in June," declared General Kyrylo Boudanov, head of Ukrainian military intelligence. The question is whether American military aid will help counter these bleak prospects. An officer from a combat unit, listing the military achievements accomplished, against all odds, by the Ukrainians over the past two years, puts into perspective, even if it were to happen, a potential significant territorial loss. Instead of talking about a collapse, he calmly mentions what could be "a bad year in a very long war."
lead: The money released by Washington should be used to strengthen the front line as a new Russian offensive is expected in the coming weeks.

title: Aide militaire des Etats-Unis : une bonne nouvelle pour l’Ukraine
text: The Senate of the United States Congress overwhelmingly voted (79 votes to 18) on April 23rd for crucial military aid to Ukraine, which is facing increasingly threatening pressure from the Russian army. Following the approval of this aid by the House of Representatives on April 20th (311 votes to 112), this vote highlighted what the vociferations of the most radical wing of the Republican Party, loyal to former President Donald Trump, had managed to mask. More than two years after the brutal aggression for which Vladimir Putin's regime is unforgivably responsible, a clear majority of lawmakers still refuse to allow, through complicit passivity, the crushing of a nation. It took Mike Johnson, the Republican Speaker of the House, six long months of indecision to rediscover the merits of action for this truth to finally emerge. While the Republican voices in this Assembly were fewer (101) in approving this military aid than those opposing it (112), the margin is slim, unlike the Democrats who were united in their support. In the Senate, Republicans were much more numerous (31 to 15) in choosing solidarity with a nation being bombed day and night rather than cowardly indifference. These votes have thus revealed the Trumpist wing for what it is in the United States: a minority entrenched in a worldview that stops at their borders. Political vicissitudes Entangled in his first trial in New York where the baroque competes with the sordid (the hush money paid to a pornographic actress on the eve of the 2016 presidential election), Donald Trump had nothing to say about it. Yet it was he who imposed this long hesitation, while claiming to be able to make peace between the two warring parties in a day, a feat he failed to achieve in four years. Because the ongoing war actually began in 2014 with the unilateral annexation of Crimea and Russian support for separatist forces in the Donbass. The epilogue that occurred on April 23rd will not erase the doubts that the hesitations of the country, which defined itself a generation ago as the "indispensable nation," have nurtured. The Europeans, along with other crucial allies of Kiev, would be wise to conclude that this finally unblocked aid will primarily give them the necessary time to reinvigorate an arms industry that has been atrophied by decades of underinvestment. This is not only about Ukraine, but also about the ability to defend independently from the political vicissitudes that could now paralyze Washington. This threat has not disappeared. The presidential election in November will determine whether the crisis resolved by the vote of Congress was temporary, or if it signals, in the wake of a potential return of Donald Trump to the White House, a fundamental questioning of the alliance architecture established by the United States in the name of their well-understood interests. In the immediate term, the approval of this aid is good news for Ukraine, which has been lacking it significantly for months. May it silence the defeatist preachers who have not learned anything from the exemplary resistance that a nation in love with its freedom is opposing to the master of the Kremlin.
lead: After much back and forth in Congress regarding aid to Kiev, the votes of the Republicans reveal the Trumpist wing for what it is in the United States: a minority entrenched in a worldview that stops at their borders.

title: Jordan Bardella, l’invisible du Parlement européen
text: Before returning, possibly with the largest delegation from the upcoming European elections, Jordan Bardella left Strasbourg quietly on Wednesday, April 24. The young man in a hurry did not attend the last lunch of the National Rally (RN) MEPs at a restaurant in the European quarter where everyone paid their own share. Nor did he show up the day before at the cocktail party organized by the Identity and Democracy (ID) group, where his party is a member. There was also no sign of him during the last plenary session of the term on Thursday, April 25: Jordan Bardella had a morning TV appearance and a press conference in response to Emmanuel Macron's speech on Europe at the Sorbonne that day. The week turned out to be a good summary of the far-right leader's time in the European democracy temple. Focused on his media rise and role as president of the National Rally – but very little on his other mandate as a regional councilor in Ile-de-France – Jordan Bardella did not pretend to be interested in the European Parliament or his own delegation. According to observations by Le Monde, he only participated in the petitions committee, the only one he is registered in, during remote meetings between 2020 and 2022 due to the Covid-19 pandemic. When criticized for his non-existent parliamentary work, Jordan Bardella points to his party's polling intentions and says he prioritizes his presence in the media and on the ground. "The fight we lead at the National Rally may be more useful than the fight led [in the European Parliament] by [The Republicans' lead candidate for the European elections François-Xavier] Bellamy or [The Unsubmissive France's lead candidate Manon] Aubry, who haven't changed anything in the lives of the French," he said on Franceinfo on February 12. He sticks to a strictly tribunician role, vilifying the European Union in one-minute speeches recorded in the empty Strasbourg hemicycle. The party shares them on social media, giving Bardella's diatribes considerable exposure and a sense of grandeur. Jordan Bardella's displayed disdain for parliamentary work is reminiscent of what Jean-Luc Mélenchon claimed at the same place between 2009 and 2017. Serving Fidesz In early April, Jordan Bardella showed rare involvement in parliamentary work for his delegation. As Le Monde learned, the RN president tried to pressure his MEP Gilles Lebreton to manipulate a report on lifting immunity. A member of the European Parliament's Committee on Legal Affairs, Mr. Lebreton had been entrusted with a report on the lifting of immunity of a Hungarian deputy, a member of the Renew group (center) and a political opponent of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban. Anna Julia Donath is being prosecuted in Hungary for allegedly obstructing a search of another opponent of the prime minister. Gilles Lebreton, a law professor whose heart clearly beats for justice as much as for the far right, saw this as a case of fumus persecutionis, a prosecution aimed at silencing opponents. Jordan Bardella, who may have seen an opportunity to assist Fidesz, Viktor Orban's party, with whom the RN would like to ally in the next term, did not care about this. Perhaps not including Gilles Lebreton on his list did not help the RN president convince the jurist. According to several sources within the European delegation of the National Rally, Jordan Bardella has shown more interest in the Brussels faction since being elected head of the party in November 2022. He initiated a change in stance on the Russian issue, choosing to attend the visit of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to Brussels in February 2023, against the majority opinion, and shifting from voting against to abstaining on most texts concerning the invasion of Ukraine. By entrusting the "foundation" of the ID group to his close associate, MEP Mathilde Androuët, he also expressed a desire to use this funding source provided by the European Union to finance more substantive work. Within the group, it is surprising that Jordan Bardella did not take advantage of these five years and available funds to train and enlist a few technicians or jurists - especially since his Parisian entourage is considered insufficient for his ambitions. His European office, described as idle, has had three different compositions in less than five years. The least demanding committee of the European Parliament in Brussels, the heart of European parliamentary work, remains a terra incognita for the RN president. It was during the Covid-19 pandemic, via video conference, that he discovered the committee in which he had chosen to sit. His last presence dates back to May 2022, which was also the last session accessible remotely. The petitions committee, known as the least demanding in the European Parliament, allows citizens to raise issues with deputies, often seen as disconnected from their constituents. Almost all other members of the petitions committee participate in at least one other committee. "If he asks for my opinion, I would recommend he continue the same," said Philippe Olivier, special advisor to Marine Le Pen, also a Member of the European Parliament. When asked if he had ever attended a meeting of his committee in Brussels, Jordan Bardella did not respond. To defend his record, the president of the RN generally highlights his attendance rate at plenary sessions, which are held once a month. It stands at 93%, a high figure at first glance. However, very few French deputies miss these sessions, which are just a two-hour train ride (free) from Paris and pay 350 euros. Out of the 79 French MEPs, nine are doing worse than Jordan Bardella: one has health issues, four have almost deserted after being sidelined by the RN, another lives in French Polynesia, and the last three have joined the institution less than a year ago.
lead: During his previous term, the head of the National Rally's list in the European elections was not very involved in parliamentary work and with his delegation. He acknowledges this low commitment, preferring to focus on his presence in the media and on the ground.

title: Elections européennes 2024 : à Strasbourg, Raphaël Glucksmann attaque le bilan d’Emmanuel Macron
text: "Taking back the European flag" from Emmanuel Macron's hands. Raphaël Glucksmann, the head of the list for the Socialist Party (PS) and Place publique in the European elections on June 9, has made this his battle cry. So, when the French president announced that he would deliver his major speech on Europe at the Sorbonne on Thursday, April 25, the MEP wanted to make the meeting he had planned to hold in Strasbourg the evening before a highlight of his campaign. "I had written fifty pages," he said after three-quarters of an hour to a room that, initially full, had started to empty. Finally, after reading nineteen pages in a very professorial tone, he shortened his "long speech," which aimed to present his program, to switch back to political meeting mode. "I will spare you," he continued, "the rest will be published." The full text will be available on the website of the geopolitical review Le Grand Continent. "I come to talk to you about this European power that can, that must, that will emerge from the current chaos," Raphaël Glucksmann declared, stealing the spotlight from Emmanuel Macron, who also plans to put "European power" at the heart of his second Sorbonne speech on Thursday. Throughout his speech, which lasted just over an hour, he praised the merits of a "sovereign" Europe, once again taking up a theme dear to the President, which had permeated his first Sorbonne speech in 2017. "There were very good ideas in the [first] Sorbonne speech," acknowledged the PS candidate, particularly the need for Europe's strategic autonomy. But, since he had advocated, in August 2019, for a rapprochement between the European Union and Russia, according to Raphaël Glucksmann, Emmanuel Macron "killed the Sorbonne speech." "Rearming our continent" Undoubtedly, the 44-year-old essayist, who on Wednesday evening quoted the German philosophers Hegel and Hölderlin, expects to be once again captivated by the President's new intervention in the grand amphitheater of the Parisian university. But one must remember "all [his] retreats and betrayals" over the past seven years, he told his audience. While listening to Thursday's speech, "we must all keep in mind this line from Hamlet: 'Words, words, words,'" he emphasized. Before concluding, somewhat playfully: "We are not goldfish." Although he did not have time to list all his proposals, Raphaël Glucksmann did reveal several of them. Military aid to Ukraine must be a priority, he emphasized, as Russian President Vladimir Putin is leading a "military, ideological, and political crusade against our democracies" and Emmanuel Macron "missed" his "appointment with history on February 24, 2022" by "refusing to lead the resistance against Putin." To achieve this, he argues, the 206 billion euros of assets from the Russian central bank immobilized in European banks should be used, and efforts should be made to combat Moscow's circumvention of sanctions. If Republican Donald Trump were to return to the White House, "Europe would find itself alone. Alone in the face of war, alone facing Putin," he warned. It is also necessary, added Raphaël Glucksmann, to "rearm our continent" through a "defense fund of 100 billion euros, financed by a new European loan." In this context, defense industry companies, which would benefit from community aid, "must accept European control over exports" and prioritize delivering to Ukraine. "Finally bring about a European ecological power" This "security revolution" must extend to energy, industry, health, agriculture... so that "our Europe," he said, once again echoing a very Macronian expression, becomes "a continent of producers" again by 2030. A European sovereign fund would invest 200 billion euros per year, while a European preemption right would prevent continental companies – such as the French generic drug producer Biogaran, which the Servier laboratory could sell – from becoming the property of foreign groups. By buying American, Chinese, and until recently Russian, "our continent has sacrificed its independence and power," Raphaël Glucksmann asserts. "Our agenda has only one goal: to finally bring about a European ecological power without which we will disappear from history," he reiterated, ensuring that "the era of generalized free trade has come to an end" and that "our entire project is designed to speak to the victims of globalization, bring back jobs, and talk about reindustrialization." On the agenda: phasing out fossil fuels, a strategy of "made in Europe," social and environmental standards that would apply to imports within the EU, taxing the wealthiest and superprofits, massive subsidies for green technologies following the American model, and a "Buy European Act" that would give European productions privileged access to public procurement within the Twenty-Seven. "We are reviving" the left-right divide In the last quarter of his speech in Strasbourg, Raphaël Glucksmann became much more critical of Emmanuel Macron, denouncing his "strategic failure." Before accusing him of defending "the interests of the powerful" and of serving "as a stepping stone to the far right constantly," "The major difference between Emmanuel Macron and us is what this European power should do, it is the conception of the general interest and our relationship with justice," he summarized. With this election, Raphaël Glucksmann wants to believe that "we are witnessing the rebirth" in France of the left-right divide, which "has never disappeared on a European scale," where the right-wing European People's Party is the leading political force, ahead of the socialist group and the liberals of Renew.
