On Wednesday night, the Socialist Party candidate made Emmanuel Macron his main rival, thus trying to break free from the match that Renaissance wants to set up with the National Rally between "progressives" and "populists." In recent weeks, Raphaël Glucksmann has gained ground in the polls, notably because he manages to attract left-leaning Emmanuel Macron voters. To the point of getting closer to the list led by Valérie Hayer - given in second place, far behind that of Jordan Bardella. The presidential majority is starting to worry about his momentum in the polls. For a week now, a Macron supporter claims, "WhatsApp loops for activists provide talking points to criticize Glucksmann."
lead: On the eve of the Sorbonne speech on Europe that the head of state is set to deliver, the socialist party's lead candidate unveiled the main points of their program on Wednesday evening.

title: Pourquoi Vladimir Poutine est le grand vainqueur de la guerre de Gaza
text: For a quarter of a century, Vladimir Putin has been taking advantage of the mistakes made by the United States in the Middle East to systematically rebuild the power of his country in that region, as well as in Europe. As early as 2001, the Russian president justified his fierce repression of Chechen nationalism by associating it with George W. Bush's "global war on terror." Ten years later, he launched his own "war on terrorism" against the Syrian revolution with the support of the Islamic Republic of Iran. When Barack Obama refused to strike Bashar al-Assad in 2013, despite the chemical bombings in Damascus, the Kremlin leader understood that he could, six months later, go on the offensive against Ukraine, first by annexing Crimea, then by fueling the war in the Donbass region. When Putin decided to intervene directly in Syria in 2015, he deepened collaboration with Iran, particularly in the field of drones, a collaboration that proved valuable to him in 2022 during the widespread invasion of Ukraine. The war techniques developed and normalized in Syria are also devastating in Ukraine. Neutralizing Netanyahu without discouraging Iran Western leaders, however, continued to treat Europe and the Middle East as two separate theaters, even as Putin skillfully played on both fronts. Washington did not even react to the trap that eventually closed at Israel's northern border: Benjamin Netanyahu became convinced that only Moscow could contain Iran's intervention in Syria, before realizing, too late, that Israel had to act directly and now depended on Russia's approval to carry out such strikes. This unprecedented vulnerability of Israel explains its lack of military support for Ukraine since 2022, despite Kiev's urgent requests in the face of Russian waves of drone bombings of civilian infrastructure of Iranian manufacture. Nevertheless, this did not prevent Volodymyr Zelensky from displaying unwavering solidarity with Israel on October 7, 2023, after attacks by Hamas. But Netanyahu then refused to welcome him, once again not to upset Putin. The Israeli prime minister also did not dissuade his "Christian Zionist" allies in the U.S. Congress from continuing to block aid to Ukraine, while voting for exceptional assistance to Israel. The Russian army greatly benefited from this blockade for six months, intensifying its pressure on Ukraine on all fronts. At the same time, it allowed Israel to increase its strikes against Iranian targets in Syria, culminating with the bombing of the Iranian consulate in Damascus on April 1. The Iranian response on April 13 was unprecedented in scale, replicating the waves of multifaceted attacks launched against Ukraine against Israel. These strikes were fortunately thwarted by 99%, thanks to the mobilization of the United States and its allies, while Zelensky regretted that such mobilization had never been as effectively demonstrated in support of Ukraine. A diplomacy of combat Besides these remarkable tactical and operational achievements, Russia was able to position itself in the Middle East as a paradoxical champion of international law, after having long violated it in Syria and Ukraine. Moscow's propaganda had little difficulty in highlighting the "double standards" of Western discourse, as the emotions stirred by the carnage in Gaza intensified over the months. The painstaking work of Ukrainian diplomacy to popularize its cause beyond Europe, and above all in the Arab world, was thus compromised. As for American diplomacy, after blocking calls for a "truce" in Gaza at the UN Security Council, it finally rallied to the principle of a ceasefire on March 22, only to be met this time with a Russian veto against a text deemed "hypocritical." A month later, the United States found themselves alone in opposing the admission of Palestine to the UN, a veto that confirmed their diplomatic isolation. Once again, the Kremlin is quick to point out the inconsistencies of the White House in the face of the "two-state solution." Ukraine will struggle to withstand the Russian invader's onslaught if it continues to make advances in the Middle East. The Kremlin's latest maneuver consists, in the name of "inter-Palestinian reconciliation," in favoring the integration of Hamas into the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO). While the Islamist movement had previously committed to supporting any diplomatic progress of the PLO, but leaving it to negotiate alone, such integration would jeopardize the revival of the Israeli-Palestinian peace process. Moreover, Moscow is counting on an Israeli offensive against Rafah, which, inevitably devastating, would further discredit Joe Biden, both domestically and internationally. Because Putin, like Netanyahu, is hopeful of seeing Donald Trump return to the White House. The only way to avoid such a catastrophic scenario for Europe is to resume talks between Israel and the PLO as soon as possible, based on the historic agreement of mutual recognition in 1993 between the nationalisms of the two peoples. As soon as possible and with determination, before it is too late.
lead: The Russian president benefits the most from Western inconsistencies and the paralysis of the United Nations, a dynamic that only a proactive revival of the peace process could reverse.

title: « C’est l’un des effets géopolitiques les plus spectaculaires de la guerre en Ukraine : le renforcement de l’axe Chine-Russie, face aux Etats-Unis »
text: Before the visit of the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, to China, which ends on Wednesday, April 17, and before the trip of Chinese President Xi Jinping to Europe, where Emmanuel Macron will host him in Paris on May 6, Washington has hammered a message to the Europeans: since you maintain high-level relations with Beijing, use them to pressure China to stop assisting Russia in its war in Ukraine. This message, actively relayed in recent weeks by Stéphane Séjourné, the French Minister of Foreign Affairs, in Beijing, by American leaders themselves, and by Olaf Scholz, who complied on Tuesday, reflects the United States' concern about one of the most spectacular geopolitical effects of the war in Ukraine: the deepening China-Russia relationship. To convince those who doubted that this deepening has a concrete impact on the course of the war and partly explains the resilience of the Russian economy, Washington made public, on April 12, previously classified data: for example, in the last quarter of 2023, China provided over 70% of Russia's imports of machine tools used in the production of ballistic missiles. And for the entire year of 2023, 90% of Russian imports of microelectronics, crucial for the manufacturing of missiles and tanks, came from China. A significant trend. Why this sudden emphasis from the Americans on the Chinese factor? Washington was surprised by how quickly the Russian industry managed, despite Western sanctions, to recover and produce an arsenal in quantity that makes a difference on the ground against struggling Ukrainian forces, slowed down by the slowness of Western supplies. The considerable revenue contribution that Chinese and Indian imports of Russian oil have provided to the Kremlin was known; these have now compensated for Moscow's loss of the European market. But the material assistance provided by China, the extent of which was previously underestimated, seems to play an important role, even if it does not cross the red line of delivering lethal weapons. Another explanation is the observation of this significant trend in the evolution of power balances between major powers, which is the strengthening of the Russo-Chinese axis. For the United States, which measures everything in terms of the rise of Chinese power, this is not a trend to be neglected: after all, the element that cements the Sino-Russian relationship is the common opposition to American dominance. This rapprochement is not entirely new: Moscow turned to China as early as 2014 when relations between Russia and Western countries cooled following the annexation of Crimea, which led to a first wave of sanctions. Commercial exchanges between China and Russia have significantly intensified since then. However, the European Union remained an important economic partner for Moscow - until 2022. Once the large-scale war against Ukraine was launched, Russia had to pivot completely towards China. The meeting of the two presidents, Putin and Xi, on February 4, 2022 in Beijing and their joint statement proclaiming an "unlimited friendship" had been seen as an important step in this rapprochement. This was three weeks before the invasion of Ukraine; speculations have never ceased about whether Putin had warned Xi of the offensive he was planning. But their relationship has solidified since then. "The only way for Russia to support its confrontation with the West is to integrate Russia's economy, intellectual resources, and military technology into a 'Pax Sinica,' a Chinese-dominated order with Eurasia at its geographical core," writes Alexander Gabuev, an expert at the Carnegie think tank, in Foreign Affairs. Russia is in the process of locking itself into a vassal relationship with China." A somewhat forced marriage. Thus, not only was the objective sought by those in the West who advocated for dialogue with Vladimir Putin - to prevent Russia from falling into the Chinese orbit and to anchor Russia to Europe - not achieved, but the war in Ukraine has, in practice, thrown Russia into the arms of China. However, Beijing does not seem to approach this somewhat forced marriage with love in its eyes. Despite frequent contacts between the leaders - Putin is expected in Beijing again in May - the phrase "unlimited friendship" has not been repeated, and the two countries have not formed an alliance. Some Chinese scholars and experts who speak out externally emphasize reservations about the authenticity of Russia's pivot towards Asia: according to Guan Guihai from Peking University, Putin's stubbornness on Ukraine is explained by his fear of losing Russia's European identity. If Russia were to lose Ukraine, he writes on the Council on Foreign Relations website, "its link with Europe would be substantially weakened, its strategic space would move away from the heart of Europe, and Russia would become more of an Asian country." Another Chinese reservation: the two countries do not share the same vision of the evolution of the world order. "Russia seeks to undermine the existing regional and international order through war, while China aims to resolve conflicts peacefully," writes academic Feng Yujun in The Economist, who is also unimpressed by Putin's military performance in Ukraine, where the "myth of Russia's invincibility has collapsed." The suzerain is not gentle with the vassal. Faced with this China-Russia axis, the United States are strengthening their Indo-Pacific bloc, while asking Europeans to have a "tough conversation" with Beijing about Ukraine. However, it remains to be seen if said Europeans are willing to do so.
lead: In the global game of balancing great powers, Russia's pivot towards China and its potential integration into a "Pax Sinica" are prompting Washington to ask Europeans to put pressure on Beijing, analyzes Sylvie Kauffmann, columnist at "Le Monde".

title: Ukraine, Cisjordanie, dopage… Les cinq infos du week-end
text:
An American aid package of 61 billion dollars to Ukraine was approved by the US House of Representatives. After long and difficult negotiations, the US House of Representatives adopted a significant aid package for Ukraine on Saturday. The text provides for 60.8 billion dollars (57 billion euros) to assist Kiev in its struggle against Russia. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky immediately praised the assistance as "saving thousands and thousands of lives." American lawmakers also approved a 26 billion dollar (24 billion euros) aid package to strengthen the Israeli missile defense system. Additionally, they passed a resolution to support Taiwan and counter China, and adopted a measure issuing an ultimatum to TikTok, which includes banning the social network in the US unless it cuts ties with its parent company ByteDance, and more broadly with China. 

Ten "terrorists" were killed in the West Bank, according to the Israeli army, after a raid. Israeli soldiers during a raid in the Nour Shams camp in the West Bank on April 20, 2024. The Israeli army announced on Saturday that they had killed ten people and arrested eight others during an "anti-terrorist" raid in the Nour Shams camp near Tulkarem in the northern West Bank. The Palestinian Red Crescent announced that fourteen people had been killed. The Palestinian Authority's Ministry of Health reported that eleven people were also injured, seven by bullets. Among them, a paramedic was hit by a gunshot, the same source added in a statement. On Sunday, two other Palestinians were killed by Israeli soldiers in the West Bank. According to the Israeli army, they had attempted to stab and open fire on soldiers. 

Doping: Chinese swimmers, crowned at the Tokyo Olympics, tested positive at the beginning of 2021 and were not sanctioned. A Chinese flag at the Tokyo Olympics on July 29, 2021. An investigation by the New York Times and the German channel ARD reveals that twenty-three of the top Chinese male and female swimmers tested positive for doping in January 2021, just a few months before the Tokyo Olympics, where some of them later excelled. Although an investigation was conducted by the Chinese Ministry of Public Security, followed by a report from Chinada, the national anti-doping agency, no provisional suspension was imposed, even though the case was concluded in March 2021. According to ARD - which had previously exposed the Russian doping scandal at the end of 2014 - and the New York Times, the World Anti-Doping Agency and the International Swimming Federation accepted the explanations provided by the Chinese authorities, citing unintentional ingestion due to "contaminated food supply." "Robert Badinter would have supported this text" on aid in dying, assures Elisabeth Badinter. The Republican Guards carry the coffin of Robert Badinter during a national tribute ceremony in his honor in front of the Ministry of Justice on February 14, 2024. The philosopher Elisabeth Badinter stated in a letter published on Sunday in La Tribune that her late husband, former Minister of Justice Robert Badinter, would have voted for the text. "I have noticed that my husband's words are being used, not to say instrumentalized, by opponents to any legislative evolution on this subject," wrote the philosopher in a letter addressed to the rapporteur of the text, Olivier Falorni. The work of the special commission, tasked with preparing the first reading of the bill on the support for the sick and end of life at the National Assembly, is set to begin on Monday. Central African Republic: Fifty-eight people died in a shipwreck. The sinking of an overloaded barge caused the death of fifty-eight people on Friday on the M'Poko River in Bangui. Many others are still missing. The wooden boat, called a "baleinière," was carrying over 300 people, far beyond its capacity, and was heading towards Makolo, a village located 45 kilometers from Bangui, to attend the funeral of a village chief. The boat capsized shortly after leaving the dock. And also... Gaza. Benjamin Netanyahu promises to increase "military pressure" on Hamas "in the coming days." Iran. Ali Khamenei praises Iran's "successes" after the attack in Israel. Six Nations Tournament. The French XV team overcomes the Welsh obstacle (40-0) and sets up a final against England. Champions League. Lyon's women's team overturns Paris in a spectacular first leg of the semi-final. Liège-Bastogne-Liège. Tadej Pogacar dominates, Romain Bardet finishes second in the oldest of the classics.
lead: Did you not follow the news on Saturday, April 20th and Sunday, April 21st? Here is what happened during the last forty-eight hours.

title: En Géorgie, le Parlement adopte le projet de loi décrié sur l’« influence étrangère »
text: The Georgian Parliament adopted, on Tuesday, May 14, the controversial bill on "foreign influence", despite significant protests against the text. According to its critics, the law diverts the Caucasus country from Europe to bring it into Moscow's orbit. Thousands of Georgians marched in the streets following the adoption of the bill on Tuesday. Thirteen protesters were arrested "for disobeying police orders," according to the Ministry of Interior. The wife of activist David Katsarava claimed that her husband was beaten by the police after his arrest. During the third and final reading, lawmakers voted 84 to 30 in favor of the bill, as shown on public television. In debates leading to the bill's adoption, majority lawmakers physically clashed with opposition members, as seen on television. Around 2,000 protesters gathered in front of the Parliament in the late afternoon, surrounded by a large police presence. The Prime Minister of Georgia, Irakli Kobakhidze, had promised that the bill would be passed despite ongoing protests for over three weeks in several cities. The new law requires independent media and civil society organizations to register as entities "pursuing the interests of a foreign power" if they receive more than 20% of their funding from abroad. NGOs vow to never comply with the law. "I will never register as a foreign agent," warned Baia Pataraia, a leader of a major women's rights organization in Georgia. Non-compliance with the law could result in hefty fines or closure. Eka Gigauri, head of Transparency International's Georgian branch, also stated she would never submit to the foreign influence law. Georgian NGOs are extremely dependent on foreign funding. For the EU, it is seen as a "barrier to the European perspective." The text is similar to the one passed by the Russian Duma in 2012 to silence opponents of President Vladimir Putin, which Mr. Kobakhidze denies. For him, without this law, Georgia will "share the fate of Ukraine." This is the strong argument of the Georgian Dream party, the ruling party, convinced that the European Union (EU) and the United States are seeking to instrumentalize Georgia in order to "open a second front" against Russia in the Caucasus. "This law will be a serious obstacle for Georgia in its European perspective," said the EU spokesperson, Peter Stano, before the vote. Georgia has been an official candidate for EU membership since December 2023. Public opinion was not consulted, even though consultation between the political power and civil society is among the recommendations made by Brussels in the framework of Georgia's candidacy for accession, recognized in December 2023. The U.S. diplomacy, on the other hand, urged Georgia to "change course" after the adoption of this law. It is not yet "clear whether the Georgian Parliament will try to override a possible veto," emphasized the spokesperson for the U.S. executive, Karine Jean-Pierre. "We will see what the Parliament will do," but this law "will force us to fundamentally reassess our relationship with Georgia," she added. On his part, U.S. Deputy Secretary of State James O'Brien, who met with Georgian Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze in Tbilisi on Tuesday, warned that Washington would impose "financial sanctions and travel restrictions on individuals involved." In addition, the $390 million (360 million euros) aid allocated this year by the United States to Georgia would be "reviewed if we were to be considered adversaries rather than partners," he stressed. The foreign ministers of the Baltic countries and Iceland are traveling to Georgia to protest against the bill. "We call on the Georgian government to withdraw this bill and reaffirm its commitment to European integration," said Lithuanian Foreign Minister Gabrielius Landsbergis to Agence France-Presse, stating that the bill "was incompatible with the country's pro-European orientation." The Icelandic, Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian ministers plan to meet with Georgian President Salome Zourabichvili, who opposes the law, as well as the Speaker of Parliament and the Minister of Foreign Affairs, added Mr. Landsbergis. They are also expected to join the thousands of people who have taken to the streets to protest against the law, he added.
lead: The text is similar to the one passed in Russia in 2012 to silence opponents of Vladimir Putin. Demonstrations have been taking place every day for over three weeks in several cities in Georgia in an attempt to prevent its adoption.

title: Le secrétaire d’Etat américain, Antony Blinken, est à Pékin pour entretenir une relation fragile entre les Etats-Unis et la Chine
text: The United States and China are talking, and that's a start: in a tense, even conflictual relationship on many issues, the visit of U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken to Shanghai and then Beijing helps to maintain dialogue. In a positive sign, Mr. Blinken was finally received on Friday, April 26, by Chinese leader Xi Jinping, who struck a relatively conciliatory tone: "China is happy to see the United States confident, open, prosperous, and thriving. We hope that the United States can also consider China's development from a positive perspective," said the Chinese president, according to local state media. This was a reference to what China sees as U.S. efforts to "contain" China, hindering its economic and technological development. In the morning, Mr. Blinken had a five-hour discussion with Foreign Minister Wang Yi, who had already addressed this issue in a harsher tone: "The United States has adopted a series of measures aimed at preventing the development of China's economy, trade, science, and technology. This is not fair competition, it is containment. This will not eliminate risks, but create more," warned the Chinese foreign minister, mentioning the risk of entering a "negative spiral." Mr. Blinken, for his part, indicated that he had expressed his concerns to Mr. Xi regarding China's support for Russia, which includes providing materials, components, and machine tools essential to the Russian war effort against Ukraine. "Russia would struggle to continue its assault on Ukraine without China's support," Blinken told reporters after his meeting with Xi. He also addressed the issue of Taiwan, which Beijing dreams of annexing, and tensions in the South China Sea, reaffirming the "unwavering" support of the United States for the Philippines, an American ally in the region. He emphasized, however, the importance of dialogue, especially military dialogue, which resumed following Xi Jinping's meeting with Joe Biden in San Francisco in November 2023. "We are committed to maintaining and strengthening communication channels to advance this agenda, and once again, to manage our differences responsibly to avoid any miscommunication, misperception, or miscalculation," Blinken said. This is indeed progress: China, which had largely isolated itself from the world during the "Covid years," had suspended military-to-military dialogue in response to the visit of Nancy Pelosi, Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives, to Taiwan in August 2022. In early 2023, attempts to restore exchanges had faltered after the incident involving a Chinese spy balloon drifting in U.S. airspace, eventually shot down by the U.S. Air Force. Washington advocates for a strong relationship with Beijing while keeping communication channels open to prevent any unintended escalation. Both sides are also seeking to maintain appearances as the Chinese economy has experienced a significant slowdown since 2022. Relations with the United States have a significant impact on the confidence of economic actors, both Chinese and foreign, who are considering investing in China. Beijing is actively courting major foreign companies. Chinese leaders are also cautious ahead of the November American elections. Hostility towards China is one of the few bipartisan consensus topics in the United States, leading to a tense atmosphere during the campaign as both sides seek to demonstrate their firmness against the "Chinese threat." On Wednesday, April 24, Joe Biden signed a law that would force the sale of TikTok to an American company or, failing that, ban the app in the United States. The bill had been passed with a large bipartisan majority the previous week. Mr. Blinken had initially started his visit with a more relaxed agenda, highlighting the peaceful economic and cultural ties that the United States seeks to maintain with China. Upon arriving in Shanghai on April 24, he visited the Yu Garden, dating back to the 16th century, like any good tourist. In the evening, he attended a basketball game and spent the following day with American and Chinese students from the Shanghai campus of New York University, as well as with American entrepreneurs. In early April, the visit of the U.S. Treasury Secretary, Janet Yellen, did not lead to any concrete agreements. Shortly after her departure, the Russian Foreign Minister, Sergey Lavrov, was warmly received by Xi Jinping in a much more cordial atmosphere than with the Americans. The Chinese leader praised the joint efforts of both countries to "reform global governance." Lavrov, on his part, stated that "consolidating and strengthening" relations with China was "the priority of Russian foreign policy," according to Chinese state media. Russia announced on Thursday, April 25, that Vladimir Putin would visit Beijing in May, marking his first foreign visit since his reelection. This visit is likely to take place after Xi's visit to Europe in early May, during which he is scheduled to visit Serbia, France, and Hungary.
lead: Areas of agreement are rare between the two global powers, but both Beijing and Washington have an interest in maintaining a minimum level of dialogue to prevent the escalation of tensions.

title: En Géorgie, la jeunesse mène la protestation contre la loi sur l’« influence étrangère » : « On va continuer jusqu’au retrait de la loi, on n’a pas peur ! »
text: As every evening for three weeks, thousands of people marched in the center of Tbilisi on the evening of Friday, May 3 to protest against the "foreign influence" bill adopted on Wednesday in its second reading by Parliament. "No to the Russian law!", "Europe is the only path to civilization!", chanted the protesters, gathered in the early evening at Freedom Square, facing the Paragraph Hotel, one of the properties of billionaire Bidzina Ivanichvili, the founder and current leader of the Georgian Dream, the party in power in Georgia for nearly twelve years. Described as a "Russian law" by its critics, the text is similar to the one adopted by the Duma in 2012 to silence opposition to Vladimir Putin's regime. In Georgia, as in Russia, the law requires independent media and civil society organizations to register as entities "pursuing the interests of a foreign power" if they receive more than 20% of their funding from abroad. "Being labeled as a 'foreign agent' in one's own country is humiliating, it amounts to making us traitors," laments Kety Gujaraidze, an environmental specialist with the local NGO Green Alternative. For the past three weeks, this 52-year-old activist has been present at all gatherings, believing that the future of her country is being decided in the streets of Tbilisi. "In 2008, Russia invaded us, it was the enemy, it was simple. Today, we are being attacked by our own government which seeks to silence civil society and cut us off from the European Union. We will not stand idly by," she promises. "We feel European" Youth is the driving force behind the protest. With Georgian flags draped over their shoulders, Nia, Salomé, Tengo, Nodariko, students or high school students, do not feel drawn to the Russian model. "Our government is subservient to Moscow, not us. We feel European," says 16-year-old high school student Salomé Aladachvili. "We will continue until the law is repealed, we are not afraid!" exclaims her classmate, 15-year-old Nia Khourstsilava, her youthful face framed by two brown braids. The law, she believes, "shatters the country's European dream" and "compromises the future" of the youth. In what way? "All areas, the media, NGOs, education will be affected. Especially organizations that facilitate student exchanges, for example under the Erasmus program. Our relations with the European Union will deteriorate, that's what the government wants." The youth is mobilized, yet not very politicized. "Most young people do not vote," notes Zourab Japaridze, 48, who leads the Girchi party ("more freedom" in Georgian, an opposition, liberal party), founded in 2020. He is certain that "thanks to this protest movement, they will have to organize themselves." The moment of truth will come on May 13 when the deputies, currently on vacation for Orthodox Easter, will examine the law in its third and final reading. "If the law is passed, NGOs will not be able to register. The government wants us to believe that this text is not intrusive, but we cannot believe them. In Russia, when the law was passed in 2012, leaders talked about it as a mere formality, but currently, Russian activists are being thrown in prison as foreign agents." Every evening on Rustaveli Avenue, vendors selling Georgian and European flags rub their hands together, hoping that the protest movement will continue. It does not seem to be weakening, even though there were fewer protesters on Friday than the day before due to the Easter holidays. On the same day, the country's main start-ups spoke out against the law, fearing they would lose the aid they receive from various international financial organizations, including the World Bank. Footballers and rugby players, members of the national teams, also expressed their opposition to the restrictions imposed by the law. "This law is absurd." Georgian authorities, on the other hand, claim that the new legislation will bring "transparency" to the funding of NGOs and media. "This law is absurd considering that the government itself is the largest recipient of Western aid," notes Zourab Japaridze, convinced, like many Georgians, that Bidzina Ivanichvili, who made his fortune in Russia in the 1990s, was pressured by the Kremlin to impose the law in order to compromise the country's European future. The fact that Georgia was granted candidate status for the European Union in December 2023 did not please Vladimir Putin, who, after losing influence in Armenia, wants to bring his former Georgian vassal back into his geopolitical orbit. It is no coincidence that senior Russian politicians, including Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov and Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov, praised the reintroduction of the controversial bill in Georgia. In recent days, Georgian leaders have begun to raise their voices against their Western partners, who are strong critics of the law. The EU's foreign policy chief, Josep Borrell, called it a "very worrying development," fearing "a negative impact" on Georgia's progress towards accession. The UN and the United States have also spoken out against the law. Stung, Georgian Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze stated on his X account that Washington had tried several times to foment revolutions in Georgia. The break with the West seems to be accepted. Fearing potential sanctions, the Georgian authorities have recently had a law passed in Parliament encouraging businessmen to repatriate their assets held in offshore accounts. Some observers in Tbilisi believe that Bidzina Ivanichvili would be one of the first targets if sanctions were imposed.
lead: The protesters marched again on Friday to oppose the Russian-inspired law requiring independent media and NGOs to register if they receive more than 20% of their funding from abroad.

title: Vidéo. Ukraine : une triple frappe russe sur une grande ville du Nord a fait au moins 17 morts
text: At least seventeen people were killed, and over sixty injured, in a triple Russian strike on Wednesday in Chernihiv, a large city in northern Ukraine. The death toll may rise, as "people are likely still trapped under the rubble of the partially destroyed building," authorities warned at midday. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky argued that Ukraine lacked sufficient air defenses to prevent this attack, which is likely the deadliest in this historic city located about fifty kilometers from the border with Belarus, a Russian ally, and about a hundred kilometers north of Kiev. As Western aid, especially from the United States, dwindles, Ukraine desperately urges its partners to deliver more weapons and air defense systems.
lead: Following the bombing of Chernihiv, Volodymyr Zelensky stated that Ukraine did not have enough air defenses to protect itself.

title: La curieuse défection d’un juge polonais réfugié en Biélorussie après des accusations d’espionnage
text: The case of Judge Tomasz Szmydt, who sat on the regional administrative court in Warsaw and fled to Belarus, continues to cause a stir in Poland. Appearing on May 6 on social media and at a press conference in Minsk, the Polish judge announced that he was seeking "protection" from Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko. On social media platform X, he stated that he had been "persecuted and intimidated for his independent political stance" in his home country. "I protest against the Polish authorities who, under the influence of the United States and Great Britain, are leading the country to war. The Polish people are in favor of peace and good neighborly relations with Belarus and Russia," wrote the fugitive, also praising Alexander Lukashenko as a "very good president." An ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin in the large-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the Belarusian leader, who has been in power since 1994, fiercely suppresses any democratic aspirations among his fellow citizens. Lukashenko stated on May 9 that he would "consider" Tomasz Szmydt's request, calling him "a brave man." In Poland, the national prosecutor's office accuses Tomasz Szmydt of engaging in espionage for a foreign power. Dismissed from his position, the judge, who lost his immunity on May 9, faces a potential life sentence. Prime Minister Donald Tusk has labeled Tomasz Szmydt's actions as "high treason." "There is every reason to believe that he could have been a very valuable source for foreign services," he stated on May 8 after an emergency meeting of intelligence officials, adding that "Mr. Szmydt's connections with the Belarusians are longstanding." Tomasz Siemoniak, coordinator of the intelligence services, echoed this sentiment during a second intelligence services meeting on May 10, indicating that the investigation into Russian influences would be relaunched promptly. Established in 2023 by the national-conservative government of the Law and Justice party (PiS) and seen as a tool to sideline Donald Tusk from the upcoming legislative elections, the commission had been sidelined under diplomatic pressure. Siemoniak aims to "amend" and "eliminate its unconstitutional provisions." Access to confidential data Due to the cases he presided over, Judge Szmydt had access to confidential information concerning NATO and the European Union. He was set to rule soon on cases related to the denial of security clearances to officials. "He worked in the classified affairs department. As such, he was very useful to both Russia and Belarus," noted Igor Tuleya, a judge at the Warsaw District Court, in an interview with Le Monde. "Several indications suggest that Tomasz Szmydt had already been collaborating with Belarus and Russia much earlier." "He was welcomed with honors in Belarus, which is not insignificant," adds Krzysztof Zegota, a professor at the University of Olsztyn, specializing in security issues related to Russia and Belarus. Tomasz Szmydt had already visited Belarus in June 2023. "It is doubtful that he went there as a tourist," notes Mr. Zegota, for whom "a joint operation between Russia and Belarus" is beyond doubt. The real danger, argues the academic, lies in the fact that the judge "had worked on cases involving officials who had been denied access to secret documents [due to alcohol or drug dependence, debts, or a desire to conceal their sexual orientation...]." The deserter thus knows perfectly well which individuals could be subject to blackmail "and be recruited to cooperate with Russia and Belarus." Fear of being charged It turns out that the judge was also involved with his ex-wife in a dark story of harassing judges critical of the judiciary reforms under PiS, accused of undermining judicial independence. Revealed in the media in 2019, the case is under investigation and Tomasz Szmydt subsequently spoke to the press and confirmed his involvement. It is not excluded that the fugitive fled because he feared being charged soon. In Poland, speculations are rife about the fate that will be reserved for the magistrate. In late 2021, Emil Czeczko, a Polish soldier, deserted to Belarus after serving on the Polish-Belarusian border during a migrant crisis. He then spoke out in Belarusian media denouncing "the inhumane treatment of refugees" by Poland. The Polish national, seeking asylum in Belarus, was found hanged in his home in Minsk three months later.
lead: Stripped of his immunity, the magistrate Tomasz Szmydt has requested protection from the Belarusian president Alexander Lukashenko, known to be a close ally of Vladimir Putin.

title: La visite en France de Xi Jinping n’a pas permis de dissiper les nuages qui planent sur la relation bilatérale
text: On the second day of Xi Jinping's state visit to France, the Chinese and French presidents ditched their ties, a sign of relaxation between the leaders. The Elysée wanted to stage a more personal moment, after a very official agenda in Paris on Monday, May 6. It was an opportunity to discuss important topics without an army of advisers, in hopes of influencing the position of the president of the second world power, as France hoped. For China, the moment allowed them to show a president warmly received everywhere in the world. However, when they arrived at the Col du Tourmalet, a thick blizzard welcomed the two heads of state: the beautiful postcard moment hoped for did not happen. This was emblematic of the entire state visit, which did not dispel the clouds threatening the bilateral relationship. The main progress was the commitment of the two presidents to an Olympic truce in the ongoing conflicts during the Paris Games this summer. The Elysée also welcomed a joint declaration on the situation in the Middle East, mentioning the two-state solution. On the contrary, commercial issues led to firm exchanges on Monday between Xi Jinping and Emmanuel Macron, supported by Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the European Commission, invited by France. While Mr. Xi dismissed the "so-called overcapacity problem," his French counterpart affirmed his willingness to "continue to open trade, but to ensure each time that it is fully fair." The Elysée was at least pleased to have obtained a reprieve for cognac, which was threatened by a Chinese anti-dumping investigation launched in retaliation for the EU investigation into subsidies for Chinese electric vehicles. A "friendly but also very frank" dialogue Should we see in this gesture – not confirmed by the Chinese side – a desire for appeasement? "Xi is trying to limit the damage, but he knows very well that we are going to take measures: that's what bothers him the most, in the context of China's economic slowdown," says sinologist Jean-Pierre Cabestan, a researcher at the Asia Centre. Since 2023, Chinese officials have been sending messages of economic openness to try to attract investors back after three years of closure due to the zero-Covid policy implemented in the country. While household consumption struggles to recover, exports are booming. In this context, it is difficult to imagine the European Union making concessions: "We lost on solar panels, on wind turbines, we are not going to continue losing on electric vehicles," adds Mr. Cabestan. On Tuesday, Mr. Macron offered a bottle of armagnac to his counterpart before sitting down at the altitude restaurant L'Etape du Berger, owned by a long-time friend of the French president, along with a list of very "terroir" gifts from the Southwest: A beret, a black pork ham, a wool blanket, a signed jersey from the latest winner of the Tour de France, and vintage 2008 wine, the year of the Beijing Olympics – nothing is left to chance. All of this is meant to lighten the atmosphere for this somewhat disrupted day due to heavy rains: there was no agenda for the lunch between the two presidential couples, which finally started at 2 p.m. "Today's sequence embodies the intimacy that we seek between the two leaders in order to have a dialogue that is both friendly and very frank. Few leaders in the world have this type of moment with the Chinese president," the Elysée Palace rejoices. The folk group Les Pastourelles de Campan performing in front of the Chinese and French presidents and their wives, in front of the restaurant L'Etape du Berger, at the Tourmalet (Hautes-Pyrénées), on May 7, 2024. MORGAN FACHE FOR "LE MONDE" Human rights addressed "in private" The visit also shows the distance between the Chinese regime and Europe. In Paris as well as in the small villages of the Pyrenees crossed by the presidential caravan, Xi Jinping was cheered by groups of students and members of the Chinese diaspora all wearing the same white cap and waving huge Chinese flags – and a few French flags for good measure. An impressive mobilization capacity of overseas Chinese. "This is new compared to what we have known in the past," notes Jean-Pierre Cabestan. "It will please Chinese television. No protesters in favor of human rights but supporters of China: this shows that China has become powerful and that France remains silent on these issues." While gatherings in support of the Uighurs and Tibetans did take place in Paris on Sunday, the security measures put in place on Tuesday prevented any demonstration, with hundreds of police officers and gendarmes along the route. The topic of human rights was indeed discussed on Monday, the Elysée Palace indicates, but "in private," for reasons of efficiency, with Paris deeming "megaphone diplomacy" counterproductive. It is better to negotiate on individual cases, especially those of relatives of French Uighur citizens detained in China. At 7 p.m., after a farewell ceremony, Emmanuel Macron gave one last hug to Xi Jinping, and the imposing Chinese delegation flew off to Serbia, then Hungary, two states close to Russia. Upon returning to China, Mr. Xi is expected to host Vladimir Putin, for their fourth meeting since the start of the war in Ukraine. Paris hopes that the two days of exchanges will have allowed Mr. Xi to understand how crucial the Ukrainian issue is for Europe, and that he will be able to convey a message of peace to his Russian counterpart. But there is no guarantee that Emmanuel Macron's efforts will be enough to challenge the "limitless friendship" between Mr. Xi and Mr. Putin.
lead: On Tuesday, the two heads of state visited the Hautes-Pyrénées. A "personal" moment spoiled by wintry weather.

title: Elections européennes : Raphaël Glucksmann dénonce la « brutalisation du débat public » et accuse les « insoumis », après son éviction d’une manifestation
text: Following his expulsion from the May 1st demonstration in Saint-Etienne, the candidate of the Socialist Party (PS) and his Place publique movement in the European elections, Raphaël Glucksmann, denounced on Thursday, May 2nd, on the set of France 5 a "toxic atmosphere" in public debate. "At some point, it has to stop, because the violence of words always leads to violence in actions. We need to return to reason, to republican debate," he declared. The MEP had to be escorted out by his security service after being heckled with shouts of "Get out", "Glucksmann, leave" and "Palestine will live" by protesters who threw paint at him. The socialist candidate once again pointed the finger at Jean-Luc Mélenchon and the "insubordinate circles" who, according to him, "orchestrated a violent, deceitful campaign against [his] person, against [his] votes in the European Parliament, against our positions." "And that's what produced this atmosphere in Saint-Etienne," he criticized. Like the day before, Raphaël Glucksmann once again accused communist and "insubordinate" militants of preventing him from participating in this May 1st gathering. A few hours earlier on Thursday, the president of the La France insoumise (LFI) group in the National Assembly, Mathilde Panot, had denied any involvement of sympathizers in this action, accusing the socialist candidate of having "lied" on this matter. According to Ms. Panot, the MEP "exaggerated [this incident] himself to accuse the 'insubordinates'." "Raphaël Glucksmann, apologize!", also thundered Jean-Luc Mélenchon in a message posted on X Wednesday afternoon. According to LFI, this action was carried out by the Young Communists of Loire, who admitted their participation. But TF1 and LCI obtained the testimony of a militant, former substitute of an unelected LFI candidate in the 2022 legislative elections, claiming, with an "insubordinate" flag on his shoulder, "to have been one of those who expelled him." "I am attacked by radical and violent militants from the Young Communists and the 'insubordinates' and I should apologize? Well, I will not apologize," Mr. Glucksmann replied on Thursday evening. "They do not intimidate us." "I want to tell these people who brutalize public debate that they do not intimidate us," he added, stating that his campaign "is designed as an antidote to this constant brutalization of public debate." The socialist candidate expresses his intention to conduct "a campaign on the subject of the election, Europe." "What Europe of defense against Putin? What Europe of ecological transition facing the prospect of climate collapse?" he listed. Asked about the reasons for this action, Mr. Glucksmann believes that "it is probably because our camp has momentum [in the polls]." The socialist candidate is placed in third position – leading the left and approaching the candidate from the presidential camp, Valérie Hayer – according to the fourth wave of the electoral survey conducted by Ipsos, in partnership with the Center for Political Research at Sciences Po (Cevipof), the Montaigne Institute, the Jean Jaurès Foundation, and Le Monde. "They are getting upset because we are ahead, that he [Jean-Luc Mélenchon] thought he owned the left," Mr. Glucksmann continued. "I want to show that there is still a humanist, pro-European left that stands firm, that is not intimidated by violence." The socialist candidate has received several endorsements from the left, including that of the national secretary of the Communist Party, Fabien Roussel, who stated that "nothing justifies Raphaël Glucksmann being excluded from a mobilization on May 1st in Saint-Etienne." "The social movement gains nothing from practicing diktat and exclusion," the deputy from the North had stated in a message on X. "The excessive conflict and anger fueled by some are bearing fruit," also criticized the (socialist) president of the Occitanie region, Carole Delga. "This is the society they propose to us, it is not ours." "I will always stand against any form of violence in politics. Politics can sometimes be a fight in the noble sense of the term, but it must always be done with respect for the integrity of individuals," reacted the Prime Minister, Gabriel Attal, during a visit to Beaugency (Loiret).
lead: The socialist Member of the European Parliament had to be evacuated from a demonstration in Saint-Etienne on May 1st after being booed with shouts of "Get out", "Glucksmann get out" and "Palestine will live" by protesters.

title: Après l’attaque de l’Iran sur Israël, la Russie et la Chine font bloc derrière Téhéran contre Washington
text: Behind the calls for restraint, issued in unison by most capitals, the Russian and Chinese diplomacies have firmly sided with Iran in the crisis that pits this country against Israel. Neither Moscow nor Beijing condemned the massive aerial attack carried out by Tehran on the night of Saturday, April 13, to Sunday, April 14. On the contrary, during a telephone conversation on Monday between the Chinese Foreign Minister, Wang Yi, and his Iranian counterpart, Hossein Amir Abdollahian, the former reiterated that "China strongly condemns and firmly opposes the attack on the consular section of the Iranian embassy in Damascus [on April 1]," according to the account of this conversation published by the official Chinese news agency Xinhua. In contrast, Beijing "takes note of Iran's statement that the action taken was limited and constituted an act of self-defense." During a call on Tuesday with his Iranian counterpart, Ebrahim Raisi, Vladimir Putin also noted Iran's willingness "to avoid further escalation." Russia's Permanent Representative to the United Nations, Vassily Nebenzia, had already mentioned the day before a "legitimate response" from Iran and preferred to emphasize the "hypocrisy" of the West on the matter. "Simona, remind me when Israel condemned the strikes by the Kiev regime on Russian territory," said Russian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Maria Zakharova to the Israeli ambassador in Moscow, Simona Galperin. The Iranian raids were even greeted with a form of euphoria in Russian propaganda channels, which first described Tehran's "victory" and Israel's "panic" before adopting the rhetoric of "limited strikes" with a simple "warning" value. "Iran has become the center of gravity of the Muslim world," said the NTV channel on Monday. A vital ally of Moscow There is more than just a gesture of goodwill to an important partner in these positions. Iran is a vital ally of Moscow in its war against Ukraine and provides drones and missiles to Russia. It is also one of Beijing's main suppliers of hydrocarbons, and China has always opposed sanctions imposed on Tehran. In mid-March, the three countries were still conducting joint military maneuvers in the Sea of Oman. The Russian and Chinese stances are primarily in line with the shift that occurred after the Hamas attacks on Israel on October 7, 2023, which both countries condemned belatedly and often in ambiguous terms. Since that date, Beijing has been keen on taking the exact opposite stance of the United States and the G7 in Middle Eastern affairs, positioning itself as the self-proclaimed spokesperson of the "Global South." It is also in the name of the confrontation with the West that Moscow has sacrificed its good relations with Israel and a regional policy marked until then by a concern for balance. The war in Ukraine is an absolute priority for Russia, and tensions in the Middle East are analyzed through this lens. They are seen as a way to drive a wedge between the West and the rest of the world, with the hope of diverting Western attention and resources away from Kiev. Russian analysts quoted in the press also suggest that an escalation could lead to an increase in energy prices, benefiting the Russian economy. However, neither Moscow nor Beijing have an interest in an uncontrolled escalation. Not only is the region vital for China's energy supplies, but there is also uncertainty about whether the Iranian regime could withstand severe shocks – a question that also applies to Damascus, Moscow's main stronghold in the region. Furthermore, a military escalation could reduce Tehran's ability to supply weapons to Moscow. The formation of a new "axis of evil" – a term coined under George W. Bush that is now being used to describe the converging interests of Moscow, Beijing, Tehran, and Pyongyang – could also work against its members, as economist Vladislav Inozemtsev writes on Telegram: "For now, discussions about such an 'axis' are purely rhetorical, and each of the issues it presents is being addressed separately. (...) This could change if Western leaders decide that these various actors constitute a single adversary."
lead: Moscow and Beijing, who have not condemned the Iranian attack on Israeli territory, see this crisis as an opportunity to drive a wedge between the West and the rest of the world.

title: Elections européennes : face à Valérie Hayer, Jordan Bardella brocarde « l’Europe de Macron »
text: It was their first face-to-face debate, highly anticipated by both camps, nearly a month before the European elections. The candidate from the National Rally (RN), Jordan Bardella, and the Macronist lead candidate, Valérie Hayer, both Members of the European Parliament since 2019, debated for over two hours on BFM-TV on Thursday, May 2nd, presenting two diametrically opposed visions on agriculture, Ukraine, and immigration. Throughout the debate, the far-right elected official, who has been leading in the polls so far, continuously sought to nationalize the stakes of the election, primarily targeting the President of the Republic. "On June 9th, we must both sanction Emmanuel Macron's policies, as this is the only national election of the five-year term before the next presidential election, and of course, sanction Macron's Europe, which just a few weeks ago had thousands of farmers protesting in the streets," he declared, stating that Ms. Hayer "represents the political power in France tonight." Mocking "the duplicity" of Mr. Bardella – "he talks about what he doesn't vote for, he doesn't vote for what he talks about" – and regularly accusing him of interrupting her, Ms. Hayer positioned herself as a "deeply European" candidate. "I deeply believe that by strengthening Europe, we will strengthen France and the French people," she argued, as her list is threatened in the polls by the one led by Raphaël Glucksmann (Socialist Party-Public Square). At the start of the program, when asked about the recent succession of news events – including the death of Matisse, 15 years old, killed in Chateauroux on April 27th by a minor of Afghan nationality – the Macronist candidate denounced the "indecency" of the RN, which "systematically exploits tragedies for political gain": "You choose your victims, you choose your perpetrators when there are tragedies." "You are the actors in the rise of tension in France, you contribute to dividing our country," she added, drawing a parallel with the series La Fièvre, which depicts a fragmented society. "Emmanuel Macron's France is experiencing record insecurity, there is no longer a single place in our country where the French are safe from violence, from insecurity," retorted Mr. Bardella, advocating for "firmness, more firmness, always firmness." Mr. Bardella considers Mr. Macron's statements on Ukraine "dangerous." During the program, the two candidates debated the war in Ukraine, one of the major topics of this European campaign. While Mr. Macron reiterated his position on a possible deployment of troops on the ground in Ukraine in an interview published Thursday by The Economist, stating that "we must not rule anything out," Mr. Bardella deemed these statements "dangerous." "[They] pose a major risk, that of escalation." Today, Ukraine needs equipment to ensure its defense, particularly anti-missile and anti-tank equipment. Mr. Bardella assured, "President Zelensky is not asking for troops." He then reiterated the position of the RN: "To provide Ukrainians concretely with what they need to hold the front and allow France to prevent any escalation by consolidating our security architecture in Eastern Europe." Ms. Hayer acknowledged "the strategic ambiguity of the President of the Republic, which is useful." She stated, "[Vladimir] Putin does not set any red lines, and he even crosses them. He surpasses them one after the other. He only understands the balance of power." She then questioned Mr. Bardella about his votes in the European Parliament during the last term. "Not once did you support Ukraine, not once did you condemn the conditions of detention of the Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny [who died in prison in February]," she accused, adding that he "finally did so the day after his death, when his widow came to the European Parliament." She asked, "How could you look her in the eyes?" In response, Mr. Bardella accused Ms. Hayer and her camp "of playing around, of playing games in Parliament by voting on resolutions that are only symbolic." The far-right candidate then reaffirmed his opposition "to any European enlargement," particularly to Ukraine's entry, which he believes would "spell the end of French agriculture." The two candidates then discussed President Macron's statement that he is ready to "open the debate" on a European defense that would also include nuclear weapons. "I am in favor of opening this debate, which must therefore include missile defense, long-range weapon firing, and nuclear weapons for those who have them or have US nuclear weapons on their soil," the President said in an interview with regional newspapers on Sunday. For Ms. Hayer, this proposal aims to "provide our European partners with the nuclear umbrella that we sometimes benefit from." "But we will not decide as Twenty-Seven to activate the nuclear button. It will remain the decision of the President of the Republic," she assured. In contrast, Mr. Bardella believes that through this proposal, "Emmanuel Macron is not defending French interests," arguing that France "would then lose its veto power" at the United Nations Security Council. "Without Europe, our French agriculture would not be as strong," according to Ms. Hayer. These two visions of Europe also clashed on agriculture, several weeks after farmers mobilized across much of the continent. Bardella notably criticized trade agreements, including the one with Canada, CETA, which he believes weakens farmers by "putting them in competition with the whole world." "You are therefore organizing unfair competition against which our farmers cannot compete. You are increasing environmental standards with more and more constraints and sometimes with bans on plant protection products without any alternatives," he continued. The candidate from the presidential camp - Renaissance, Democratic Movement, Horizons, and Union of Democrats and Independents - defended the Common Agricultural Policy, which "has secured 10 billion euros in aid that comes every year for French farmers," while calling for "accelerating the fight against unfair competition." "My personal conviction is that, without Europe, our French agriculture would not be so strong," she stated. Finally, the two Members of the European Parliament were questioned about immigration. They discussed the migration pact adopted by the European Parliament in April. The text provides for tightening control over the arrival of migrants within the European Union (EU) and the establishment of a system of solidarity among Member States in the distribution of refugees. "With this pact, we finally have a concrete solution on the table to regulate irregular immigration," praised Mrs. Hayer, stating that this will allow "for the first time to register people arriving at the EU borders. We will take their identity, fingerprints, passport, photo...". In contrast, Mr. Bardella, who voted against this pact in the European Parliament, believes that it imposes "a very simple equation: mandatory distribution of migrants among Member States or financial punishment." The far-right MEP then defended "the principle of the double border." "I think we should allow freedom of movement in the Schengen area only for EU nationals. Arriving in Italy and obtaining any residence permit should not give you the right to move freely in all European countries," he suggested, using the example of airports, "where, if you are a non-EU national, you must undergo a check." Furthermore, Mrs. Hayer attempted to attack Mr. Bardella on the allies of the RN in the European Parliament and on the history of the far-right party. The Macronist candidate asked the MEP to declare that "Jean-Marie Le Pen was the disgrace" of his political party "for fifty years." Mr. Bardella then admitted that the founder of the RN had made "eminently anti-Semitic remarks" in his life. "But if, once again, ten years later, you are resorting to calling on Jean-Marie Le Pen for help, it is probably because you don't have much to say about what interests our fellow citizens," he then retorted. The two candidates will meet again on Sunday, May 5th in a televised debate that will bring together the main party leaders this time.
lead: One month before the June 9th election, the candidate from the presidential camp and the one from the National Rally strongly opposed each other during a debate on BFM-TV, presenting diametrically opposed views on agriculture, Ukraine, and immigration.

title: Elections européennes 2024 : « Les sondeurs ont beau affirmer que l’écart entre les listes Bardella et Hayer se rattrape difficilement, Macron y croit dur comme fer »
text: Once again, all eyes are turning towards him, everything depends on him, and everything is organized around him. The speech on Europe that the head of state is to deliver on Thursday, April 25, at the Sorbonne, in memory and as a continuation of the one he had delivered on September 26, 2017, will mark the majority's entry into the campaign for the European elections. The real one, the one that matters. This is how the Macronian narrative unfolds. Everything that happened before is destined to fade away: the poor polls, the rise of the National Rally (RN), the difficulty that the lead candidate Valérie Hayer (Renew) has been facing for weeks in embodying and defending the executive's European policy in a campaign dominated by national concerns: purchasing power, health, immigration, security. Also swept aside are the government's initial surprise and subsequent divisions in the face of the sudden increase in public deficits, as well as the saturation of the television space by violent incidents involving minors. When the President speaks, everything will truly begin. If, on the contrary, he remains silent, everything will continue to drift aimlessly. From start to finish, the adventure that began in 2017 remains centered around a man who believes in his destiny, fears nothing, and displays unwavering optimism to his close circle, while many others in the majority doubt and seek shelter. Despite pollsters claiming that a 10-point or more gap between the Bardella and Hayer lists is difficult to overcome, he firmly believes it can be done. This campaign is not lost, the President swears by it! Certainly, the European cause deserves to be championed loudly and proudly, as it is inseparably linked to the actions and achievements of the Presidents of the Republic. Emmanuel Macron is all the more inclined to engage as he has never been ashamed of Europe nor allowed himself to be dominated by it. Unlike François Mitterrand, he did not have to undertake the unpopular austerity measures of 1983 to demonstrate the deep conviction that salvation lies within the European Union. He did not, like Jacques Chirac, transform into a resigned European after being a critic of "subordination." The European routine disrupted In 2016, European construction was his act of faith as a candidate faced with the exhaustion of the right and left, undermined by their internal contradictions on the subject. In 2017, Europe was the battleground for the President he had become, emphasizing the concept of European sovereignty, supported by that of European identity. The goal was not to erase nations, but rather to prevent their erasure by increasing their ability to act in a globalized world. This new approach, at the time, disrupted the European routine, awakening suspicion towards the French unhealable pretension to leadership. In practice, both concepts have proven relevant in light of the continuous rise of threats: the pandemic, Russia's aggressiveness, the war in Ukraine, the risk of American disengagement, the rise of protectionism, the need for democracies to defend their model against aggressive autocrats, and so on. Developed across a wide spectrum (security and defense, border control, ecological and energy transition, digital, industry), the strengthening of European sovereignty has experienced varying fortunes over the past six years, but the results are sufficiently convincing to be claimed. They will serve as a springboard for a new wave of proposals, in defense as well as in industry and finance, as Europe, increasingly marginalized by the China-US competition, does not have the optimal organization to resist. The Sorbonne speech is first and foremost intended to make an impact on the European stage, like the one in 2017. In a context of budget slippage, however, the ripple effect may be less strong than the original. A possible downgrade of France's credit rating is feared the next day, which would bring the head of state back to this reality: if France wants to be influential, it must keep its finances in order! The camp of progressives has fragmented. On the domestic front, the ineffective move at the beginning of the year shows that the presidential firepower has diminished. When, in February, Emmanuel Macron sounded the alarm about the war in Ukraine and the Russian threat, the far right should logically have been put on the defensive due to its complacency towards Vladimir Putin. The handicap was quickly overcome, and the Ukrainian issue was swiftly brushed aside. Unwilling to detail his European project filled with shadows and contradictions, Jordan Bardella, the president of the National Rally, centered his entire campaign around insecurity and the "incompetence of the Mozart of finance," in order to turn the European election into an anti-Macron referendum, presented as a decisive step towards the alternation in 2027. The fact that the head of state enters the arena is not displeasing to him. It is around the rejection of the presidential figure that the National Rally seeks to maintain the momentum of the useful vote. In 2019, two camps clashed: the progressives and the nationalists, namely Renaissance and the National Rally. This time, the camp of progressives has fragmented. There is Emmanuel Macron, but also Raphaël Glucksmann (Place publique), who advocates for a Europe that is powerful but more social and more ecological than the one now embodied by the President of the Republic. If the challenge for Emmanuel Macron is to rally his 2019 electorate to stand against the National Rally, it is this competition that he must manage to contain. Having contributed to advancing Europe, wanting to continue doing so is certainly a strong argument with this electorate, but it is no longer enough. The rightward shift in his second term, the almost structural unpopularity brought on by the pension reform, and recent setbacks on environmental issues are working against the president. By heavily relying on himself and his rhetoric, Emmanuel Macron risks realizing how much power can isolate.
lead: The speech on Europe scheduled for Thursday, April 25 confirms it: from start to finish, the adventure that began in 2017 remains centered around the figure of a man who is not afraid of anything. However, in a context of budgetary slippage, the impact may be less strong today, as noted in her column by Françoise Fressoz, editorialist at "Le Monde".

title: La surprenante présence de l’ambassadeur de France à l’investiture de Vladimir Poutine
text: The presence of the French ambassador, Pierre Lévy, among the few Western diplomats who agreed to attend the inauguration ceremony of President Vladimir Putin in Moscow on Tuesday, May 7, was surprising for several reasons. The first reason is that the election through which Mr. Putin obtained a fifth presidential term with 88.5% of the votes from March 15 to 17, took place under such undemocratic conditions that France, like most Western countries, merely acknowledged it, emphasizing that Russian voters were deprived of a genuine choice and strongly condemning the repressive context in which it was held. For reference, opposition leader Alexei Navalny had died a month earlier in mid-February in unclear circumstances - Mr. Lévy had actually attended Navalny's funeral in Moscow with his Western colleagues. It is therefore all the more paradoxical that France sent its ambassador to endorse, by his presence, an election whose conditions it had disapproved of. The second reason is that the vast majority of Western countries boycotted this ceremony. Apart from France, no G7 country was represented. As for the European Union (EU) countries, the French ambassador found himself in the company of only his colleagues from Hungary, Slovakia - known for their complacency towards Moscow - Malta, Cyprus, and Greece. The EU ambassador conspicuously stayed at home. Germany, on its part, chose to recall its ambassador to Berlin for consultations to signal its condemnation of cyberattacks attributed to Russia. At a time when France continues to advocate and promote European unity, breaking ranks in this way sends an incomprehensible signal of division. Russian state media did not hesitate to highlight this. Ambiguity The third reason is that, on the eve of the inauguration ceremony, the same French ambassador had been summoned to the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs for the third time since the beginning of 2024, to be reproached for the "provocative" policy of the French authorities. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs had timely denounced this "misuse of diplomatic channels for the purpose of information manipulation and intimidation." To honor the Kremlin with his presence twenty-four hours after being scolded by his own government is a strange way to show France's outrage. The fourth reason, last but not least, is that this president, enthroned on Tuesday like a tsar by Patriarch Kirill, had invaded Ukraine, an independent state, without reason on February 24, 2022, and has since been waging a large-scale aggressive war that is shaking the entire European continent. Around fifty countries, including France, are providing military aid to Ukraine against Russia. Poutine is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court for war crimes. In justifying the presence of its ambassador on Tuesday, Paris argued that France did not want to "cut all ties" with Russia. Preserving communication channels in times of war can indeed be useful. However, doing so in this manner, without coordination with our European partners, at the very moment when Russia is raising the stakes by once again waving the nuclear threat, contradicts the strong stance that France has displayed in recent months and appears more as ambiguity than strategy.
lead: France displayed a message of confusion rather than firmness by sending its ambassador, Pierre Lévy, to the Kremlin without coordination with its main European partners, at a time of extreme tension with Russia.

title: Xi Jinping en France : l’Ukraine et les tensions commerciales au cœur des enjeux de la visite
text: Xi Jinping has never visited Kiev and does not seem to have any intention of doing so. However, the war in Ukraine is expected to dominate the upcoming tour that the Chinese president is preparing to make in Europe, as the conflict continues to drag on, twenty-six months after the Russian invasion. The Communist leader arrived on Sunday, May 5th, in the afternoon in France, where he is expected to stay for two days before heading to Serbia and Hungary. Welcomed at Orly airport by Prime Minister Gabriel Attal, Xi Jinping will be officially received on Monday morning at the Elysée Palace by the President of the Republic, Emmanuel Macron, for a series of meetings and an official ceremony at Les Invalides. A state dinner will then be held to commemorate the 60 years of relations between France and the People's Republic of China. The following day, the Macron and Xi couples will travel to the Hautes-Pyrénées, the department where one of the President's ancestors lived, for a high-altitude stop at the Col du Tourmalet, presented by the Elysée as "a personal sequence, with their wives, to create a framework for a frank and friendly exchange." In April 2023, China had organized a similar sequence for Emmanuel Macron in Canton (Guangzhou), with a tea ceremony at the former residence of Xi Jinping's father, who was governor of Guangdong province. The tour program, the first in Europe for Xi Jinping since 2019, was long uncertain and fiercely negotiated: while the Elysée acknowledges that it is not in control of its guest's agenda, Paris is pleased to be the first stop on his trip, asserting its preeminence over Belgrade and Budapest, both close to Russia and open to Chinese influence on the continent. The arrival of the top Chinese leader in Serbia on Tuesday evening will coincide with the 25th anniversary of the NATO bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade in 1999. A symbol for a leader, like Vladimir Putin, who is concerned with challenging the Atlantic Alliance and the presumed hegemony of the United States. "Evolving the Russian position" "It is not in China's interest today to have a Russia that destabilizes the international order (...) We must therefore work with [Russia] to build peace," believes Emmanuel Macron, as stated in an interview with The Economist magazine published on Thursday, May 2nd. It is about "asking China to use the levers at its disposal to evolve the Russian position," according to sources at the Elysée, without being too optimistic about China's stance at a time when the war is dragging on, possibly turning in favor of the Kremlin. While Western countries accuse China of supporting the Russian war effort, Mr. Xi is preparing to host Vladimir Putin in Beijing once again. Xi Jinping, on the other hand, has only spoken to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky once over the phone, a year ago. Since then, the latter has been asking his allies to convey his messages to his Chinese counterpart, as explained at the Elysée. To this day, China has no intention of participating in the Peace Conference organized by Switzerland on June 15 and 16, in close consultation with Ukraine. Russia has already warned that they will not be attending. "We will not be there if Russia does not come," said the Chinese ambassador to France, Lu Shaye, ahead of Xi Jinping's visit. This diplomat is quick to adopt the Russian narrative, shifting the responsibility for the conflict onto NATO. According to him, Western countries should reconsider China's "position on the political settlement of the Ukrainian crisis" published in February 2023. China, which has never condemned the invasion, does not mention war and its proposal, from Kiev's perspective, favors Russia, as it does not demand the withdrawal of its troops, risking to solidify its territorial gains. Resolving the trade deficit Ukraine is not the only sensitive issue, as Europeans seek to "rebalance" their relations with a China that now has the ability, through Russian aggression, to influence the security of the Old Continent. France aims to one day reduce its trade deficit, which exceeded 40 billion euros in 2023, particularly by gaining access to the Chinese market for the export of meat and dairy products. While the signing of major contracts is not expected during Xi Jinping's visit, Paris hopes to attract investments in France in the electric vehicle sector, where Chinese companies have a clear technological advantage. However, there are also numerous contentious issues, especially at the European level. As during her visit to China in April 2023, the French president can count on the presence of Ursula Von der Leyen on Monday to address these issues. In Beijing, the President of the European Commission had taken a firmer tone compared to that of Mr. Macron, who was then keen on restoring ties weakened by the Covid-19 pandemic. This time, it seems difficult to avoid a direct confrontation regarding the European investigation into Chinese subsidies for electric vehicles, actively supported by France. While the Elysée states that this issue falls under the European Union, China's initiation of an anti-dumping investigation on brandies and cognacs in January, 96% of which come from France, shows that China holds a grudge against Paris. At a time when Brussels is increasing trade defense measures against China, a relaxation on this issue seems unlikely. "With elections looming, both in Europe and the United States, it would be unwise for Beijing to start a trade war that could fuel harder lines on both sides of the Atlantic. However, once these deadlines have passed, it is likely that Beijing will come to concretely test the determination and unity of Europeans," says François Chimits, a researcher at the Mercator Institute for China Studies (Merics). French "strategic autonomy" While relations between Brussels and Beijing are becoming increasingly tense, particularly since the Twenty-Seven referred to China as a "partner, competitor, and systemic rival" in 2019, Beijing favors capitals more open to it. Thus, China is now the largest foreign investor in Serbia, a candidate country for the EU, ensuring the construction of infrastructure there. The two partners signed a free trade agreement on the sidelines of a "Silk Road" forum in October 2023 in Beijing. At the other end of the Belgrade-Budapest railway line, being renovated by Chinese companies, Xi Jinping will then spend three days in Viktor Orban's Hungary. The illiberal leader, quick to clash with European institutions, is the only leader of the Twenty-Seven to have attended the October forum. He systematically opposes European resolutions condemning China's behavior. New infrastructure projects are expected to be concluded during Xi Jinping's visit to this country, which has become one of the main destinations for Chinese investments in the EU, thanks to the construction of battery and electric vehicle factories. Discussions are also expected to focus on the implementation of a recent bilateral agreement providing for the presence of Chinese police officers in Hungary's tourist areas. "The message sent by Xi is clear: opposition to NATO, influence struggle with Americans in Europe, and support for illiberal regimes," analyzes Marc Julienne, director of the Asia Center at the French Institute of International Relations (IFRI), in a note.
