In the context of this trip, it is French "strategic autonomy" that interests Beijing. "China is obviously eager for any possible criticisms from France against the United States on this or that aspect of American policy," notes François Godement, a specialist in China at the Montaigne Institute. Sharp criticisms While the Elysée claims to be able to "talk to everyone," the staging of a personal relationship with dictator Xi Jinping, reminiscent of Emmanuel Macron's invitation to Vladimir Putin to Fort de Brégançon (Var) in 2019, has sparked sharp criticisms. "Giving the impression that we are on a middle ground between China and the United States is terrible. It weakens us because our partners do not know exactly where we stand." "We may be in competition with the Americans, but we are in the same system. Over there, we are facing people who are destroying us from within," laments the (centrist) senator for French citizens living abroad, Olivier Cadic, who was a victim of a Chinese cyberattack in 2021 along with 116 other parliamentarians from different countries critical of Beijing. Beyond strategic issues, Chinese and French leaders must finally discuss human and cultural exchanges, which have suffered greatly from China's closure during the pandemic and the increasingly strict control of the Chinese society by the Communist Party. However, there are no plans regarding Chinese interference, whether in France or the rest of Europe.
lead: The Chinese president, on a state visit to France on May 6 and 7 before visiting Serbia and Hungary, wants to encourage Europeans to distance themselves from the United States.

title: En Ukraine, la désinformation en ligne inquiète les autorités
text: Fast, efficient, and requiring little internet connection, the Telegram application has established itself, in just over two years, as the primary source of information for Ukrainians. The platform perfectly meets the demand for immediacy in a country at war. It is a patchwork of thousands of information channels allowing people to stay informed about the military situation in the country while tracking the trajectories of Russian drones and missiles during air attacks. But as Kiev has been trying for several years to rid itself of Russian influence in the country, Telegram is also one of the last digital bridges between Ukraine and Russia. The two countries are engaged in a fierce information war on the platform. As the situation worsens for the Ukrainian army, the total freedom enjoyed by users of the network has prompted some officials to sound the alarm. At the end of March, a bill aimed at better regulating Telegram was even presented to Parliament. "What is happening on Telegram is a major source of danger to national security," said Mykyta Potouraev, a deputy from the ruling party Servant of the People and a signatory of the bill. The lawmaker, also the head of the parliamentary committee for humanitarian and information policy, advocates for new rules that would allow Kiev to hold Telegram accountable in order to control channels accused of serving Moscow's interests. This is something the platform with 900 million subscribers has always refused to do. Total anonymity "The main danger is that it is a network that is practically unmoderated," argues Mykyta Potouraev, who is also convinced that the application founded and led by a Russian citizen (naturalized French in 2021), Pavel Durov, is controlled by the Russian domestic intelligence service (FSB). Pavel Durov, exiled in Dubai since 2017, denies any connection to Russia, claiming the application's total neutrality. Nevertheless, the platform has become the favorite application of Ukrainians. The percentage of those using it as a source of information has increased from 21% in 2021 to 72% in November 2023, according to a survey by the media support organization Usaid-Internews. As a result, all officials in the country have their own channels. However, while Telegram remains very popular among authorities, it is also criticized for its operating mode that allows complete anonymity for content creators. This creates an ecosystem where reliable sources and propaganda coexist with illicit content. Moreover, while some official propaganda sources identify themselves as such, the application is full of channels that leverage their anonymity to disseminate destabilizing content. "Obviously, these channels do not say they are from the FSB," says the annoyed Deputy Potouraev. "This is a disinformation operation where certain channels managed by Russians may present themselves as pro-Ukrainian." "Playing on our own difficulties" At the head of the organization CAT-UA (for Communication Analysis Team-Ukraine), tasked since the beginning of the invasion with monitoring trends on social networks in order to "fight against manipulations," Artem Zakhartchenko, 41, observes an evolution in the techniques used by Russia to target the population of his country. If, at first, "they tried to convince us by highlighting their own strategic narrative," today, "they have understood that they must play on our own difficulties," affirms this university teacher, with the aim "to weaken Ukrainian society and exacerbate our divisions." Hundreds of information channels on Telegram thus focus on the most sensitive topics for Ukrainians: the struggling military mobilization, the precarious situation on the front line, the lack of support from allies while the country suffers from an ammunition shortage, corruption scandals... All crucial issues for a population tired of official Ukrainian speeches, often considered too far from reality. These destabilization attempts take various forms. Some channels, which present themselves as pro-Kiev, even go as far as writing in Ukrainian to reinforce this impression. Others maintain a facade of neutrality. All have in common the massive dissemination of content taken from Russian propaganda. The social network TikTok is also widely used. Anait Khoperiya, from the Center for Countering Disinformation, a state organization, claims not to see a specific narrative in the campaigns of Telegram channels. "Their methods consist of using a true story and intensifying the dissemination of a single case," she explains, citing the example of the city of Odessa, where many instances of violence by army recruiters against civilians have been filmed. "The Russians will amplify the effect of these videos by spreading them as much as possible and by accompanying some of them with fake debates," asserts this Ukrainian. These maneuvers also involve creating photo montages disseminated in Russian media and picked up by Telegram channels. The teams of the Center for Countering Disinformation regularly publish denials on their website: "False information about Volodymyr Zelensky buying a manor from the King of England"; "False information about the mobilization of 1,000 students from the Taras Shevchenko National University [in Kiev]"; "False information about border guards being authorized to open fire on 'fugitives' from the country." Artem Zakhartchenko, from CAT-UA, acknowledges that Russia may not necessarily be behind the handling of certain sensitive issues in the country, but that it could be due to political opposition, activists, bloggers, and ordinary Ukrainian citizens. The difference is that "Russian agents will do it by playing on emotions, outrage, and amplifying them," he says. "It is important to have internal discussions. But the Russians use our democratic procedures to fuel our divisions."
lead: The Telegram application, which has become indispensable for staying informed about the war, is also used by Russia to fuel the concerns and divisions within Ukrainian society.

title: « La Chine soutient la production d’armements de Moscou »
text: David O'Sullivan is a very busy senior official. As the European Union's (EU) special envoy for the implementation of sanctions against Russia, this seasoned Irishman based in Brussels is tasked with ensuring that Russia does not circumvent them. Russia has been successful in doing so since the first package came into effect at the beginning of the war triggered against Ukraine in February 2022. Despite sanctions on oil and coal, strategic components, companies, executives, and politicians, Brussels has not completely cut off all exchanges with Russia. New sanctions are being added as the months go by. In early March, a thirteenth package targeted two hundred entities and individuals without focusing on a specific sector, following the ban on diamond trade on January 1st. The EU was protesting against the death of Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny on February 16th in an Arctic penal colony. However, gas, oil, and uranium are still being exchanged, albeit indirectly. Not to mention certain metals, a lucrative activity for Moscow, although the sanctions have reduced the value of trade from 23 billion euros in 2022 to 14 billion in 2023. Copper disguised as scrap metal. The United States and the United Kingdom announced on Friday, April 12th, the ban on importing aluminum, copper, and nickel produced in Russia, as well as restrictions on over-the-counter derivatives trading. Crucial for their trade, the London and Chicago metal exchanges will no longer accept them. "This will prevent the Kremlin from injecting more money into its war machine," said Jeremy Hunt, the UK's Chancellor of the Exchequer. According to Russian customs data, sanctions have had an impact: in 2023, Russia's exports to the European continent dropped by 68% and by 28% globally, showing that trade with countries refusing sanctions did not compensate for the loss of the European market. However, every day brings new ways to circumvent the sanctions. While the Treasury announced the embargo on metals, American officials revealed that China supports Moscow's arms production by selling machine tools, electronic components, and technologies for drones or missiles. The two countries exchange copper disguised as scrap metal, as reported by Reuters. And large quantities of goods imported from the West by Turkey, the Arabian-Persian Gulf, and Central Asia are re-exported to Russia to support its war effort. It would take ten Mr. O'Sullivans to monitor all this traffic...
lead: While the United States and the United Kingdom announced on Friday a ban on importing aluminum, copper, and nickel produced in Russia, Moscow is bypassing European sanctions by turning to Beijing to support its war effort in Ukraine. This demonstrates the close ties between the two countries, as noted by Jean-Michel Bezat, an economic journalist at "Le Monde."

title: Guerre en Ukraine : Kiev annonce l’arrestation de deux « agents » qui comptaient assassiner Volodymyr Zelensky sur ordre de Moscou
text: Ukrainian authorities claimed on Tuesday, May 7, to have thwarted a Russian plot to assassinate President Volodymyr Zelensky. The Ukrainian Security Service (SBU) announced in a statement that they had "disrupted a network of agents" from the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) who were planning "the assassination of the Ukrainian president," and had arrested two Ukrainian officers who were part of it. Other "high-ranking officials" from military and political spheres were also targeted, such as the head of the Ukrainian military intelligence service (HUR), Kyrylo Boudanov, and the head of the SBU, Vassyl Maliouk, the same source specified. Since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, 2022, Kiev has reported several assassination attempts targeting President Zelensky, attributed to Moscow. Russia has long been known to have agents in the Ukrainian military and security apparatus, although Ukraine's resistance to the Russian attack and the failure of the offensive on Kiev in the spring of 2022 have shown the limits of Moscow's intelligence services. This time, the SBU claimed to have arrested "two colonels" from the Ukrainian State Security Service responsible for the security of public officials. "They were arrested a few days ago," a source within the Ukrainian law enforcement told Agence France-Presse. These two officials allegedly "passed confidential information" to Russia and had been recruited by the FSB before the 2022 invasion, according to the SBU. The suspects are charged with "high treason" and "preparations for a terrorist attack" and face life imprisonment, according to the source. They reportedly sought to recruit military personnel "close" to President Zelensky's security service in order to "take him hostage and kill him," the SBU further reported. Multiple attempts according to Kiev In a video released by the SBU, a man with a blurred face recounts that he had recommended to the FSB a person willing to "block" President Zelensky, likely when he was about to record his traditional evening address. One of the members of this alleged network had obtained drones and explosives, the SBU claimed. This network had planned the "liquidation" this week of Kyrylo Boudanov, whose death would have been a "gift" to Vladimir Putin for his inauguration on Tuesday, according to the SBU. One of the arrested colonels was tasked with observing a building where Mr. Boudanov was supposed to arrive by car and passing this information to the FSB so that Moscow could strike the site with a missile. This colonel was then supposed to launch a drone attack against any potential survivors, after which a second Russian missile was to be sent to "erase the traces" of the drone's use, still according to the SBU. Kiev has repeatedly denounced assassination attempts against its president and other high-ranking officials or their relatives. For example, in April 2024, a man suspected of assisting Russian intelligence in preparing an attack against Volodymyr Zelensky was arrested in Poland, according to Polish and Ukrainian prosecutors. Russia has been repeatedly accused of killing opponents of the Kremlin, both in the country and abroad, but has always denied these allegations. In recent months, the situation has been more favorable on the front for the Russian army, which had suffered humiliating defeats in the spring and fall of 2022. While the Ukrainian army lacks ammunition and recruits, Russian troops are on the offensive in the east of the country and are carrying out strikes on populated areas throughout the country.
lead: Other "high-ranking officials" from military and political spheres were also targeted, such as the head of the military intelligence service (HUR), Kyrylo Boudanov, and the head of the Security Service of Ukraine, Vassyl Maliouk.

title: En Europe, Xi Jinping a choisi son camp
text: Mission accomplished: in light of Chinese ambitions, that was likely the prevailing sentiment on the Chinese presidential plane as it took off from Budapest on Friday, May 10, at the conclusion of Xi Jinping's first tour in Europe in five years. This trip, which took the Chinese president to France, then to Serbia before Hungary, primarily allowed him to showcase China's commercial and geopolitical objectives, without offering concessions to European Union (EU) leaders. The itinerary chosen by Mr. Xi was already a message in itself. Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic and Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban are two difficult interlocutors for European leaders and do not hide their good relations with the Kremlin. Choosing Belgrade and Budapest after Paris was a way to highlight where Europe's divisions hurt the most. In Paris, President Emmanuel Macron had conveniently included European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen in the initial talks with President Xi, as he always does. Unfortunately, he did not succeed in attracting German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, whose China policy is still dominated by the interests of his own industrialists. Minimal engagement on Ukraine This absence – Angela Merkel had come to Paris during President Xi's previous visit in 2019 – weakened the firm stance clearly articulated by Ms. von der Leyen and Mr. Macron on the serious trade disputes between China and the EU, which were one of the two major issues, from Paris's perspective, in the discussions with the Chinese leader. While Brussels seeks to counter Beijing's public policies that allow Chinese electric vehicle producers, in particular, to flood European markets, Mr. Xi steadfastly denied "an alleged overcapacity problem." The same went for the second top priority for Paris and Brussels: the war in Ukraine. Faced with counterparts asking him not to support the Russian war effort, Xi Jinping did the bare minimum in private, then publicly expressed outrage that anyone would seek to "blacken China's image," or even "incite a new Cold War." The Chinese president will host Vladimir Putin in Beijing later this month. The Serbian and Hungarian stops were much warmer – and more economically fruitful. In Belgrade, choosing the anniversary of the bombing of the Chinese embassy by the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) during the Kosovo War on May 7, 1999 – Beijing has never accepted Washington's claim that it was a mistake – was a way to perpetuate NATO's aggressor image. As for Hungary, where Mr. Xi praised the "independent" foreign policy and was rewarded with 18 economic cooperation agreements and a promotion to one of the highest ranks in the qualification of its strategic partnership with Beijing. Clearly, Xi Jinping has chosen his side in Europe, that of leaders with autocratic tendencies, eager for Chinese investments without scrutiny. Experts who believed that the Chinese president was ready to flatter European strategic autonomy to drive a wedge with the United States and strengthen his idea of a "multipolar world" are now proven wrong. Xi Jinping's multipolar world is primarily intended to reflect Chinese characteristics.
lead: The Chinese president's first tour of the Old Continent in five years was mainly an opportunity to reaffirm Beijing's economic and geopolitical ambitions, by exploiting divisions within Europe.

title: Discours d’Emmanuel Macron à la Sorbonne : « Sur la défense de l’Europe, les progrès ont été minimes depuis 2017 »
text: President Macron's assessment of Europe is certainly not negligible, as he made clear in his speech at the Sorbonne on April 25, 2024. However, on one issue, progress has been minimal since his first "Sorbonne speech" in 2017, especially given the destabilizing risk posed to the Old Continent by Russia's aggression in Ukraine. This issue is the defense of Europe. Why do Europeans find it difficult to do more in this area? We might be tempted to summarize this complicated subject in a simple formula: underestimation of the Russian threat for a long time, and overestimation of the American security guarantee (at least, its sustainability). The French certainly professed not to fall into the second of these two pitfalls, but they went very far in the first. And from this perspective, Mr. Macron has been a particularly zealous continuator. This is the dark side of his European policy. The meeting at Brégançon in August 2019 illustrated a surprising confidence in dialogue with the Kremlin, even after the invasion of Ukraine, not to mention a series of unfortunate statements ("not to humiliate Russia," for example). Under these conditions, it is difficult to play a leadership role in Europe, especially to convince our partners of the need to move towards greater strategic autonomy. Some argue that the French authorities' illusion about Russia was shared by Germany and many others, including American foreign policy gurus such as Henry Kissinger or Zbigniew Brzezinski. Fine, but France did not have as pressing interests in the matter as Germany, while being less removed from the Russian reality than post-Cold War America. The UK did not fall into the trap. From 2011-2012, and even more so from the annexation of Crimea in 2014, it should have been obvious that Vladimir Putin had chosen a ruthless confrontation with the West. Collective blindness. In fact, it was not only Mr. Macron who went astray, but a good part of the French political class, as was evident in 2017 when he was the least "pro-Russian" of the presidential candidates. His predecessor, Mr. Hollande, was more insightful than many others, but he too wanted to believe in "dialogue" with Russia by inventing the so-called "Normandy negotiations" (Germany, France, Ukraine, Russia) to resolve the Ukrainian crisis. How to explain this collective blindness? One possible key to understanding lies in the fact that French politicians live, in terms of foreign policy, on a distorted version of General de Gaulle's legacy. They equate this heritage with the challenge to the "policy of blocs," leading to dialogue with Moscow to counterbalance American dominance. The same inspiration - the same strategic compass - has survived the end of the Cold War, with formulas such as "multipolarity" or "France, a balancing power." However, this approach attributed to General de Gaulle corresponded to a specific period, that of détente in the mid-1960s. During the Berlin Crisis (1960-1961) or the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), or even after the invasion of Czechoslovakia (1968), de Gaulle did not hesitate to support allies; it would never have occurred to him to seek common ground with Nikita Khrushchev or Leonid Brezhnev. Unfortunately, the current situation regarding Russia today more closely resembles that of the early 1960s than détente, and is even worse as war now rages in the heart of the Old Continent. "Europe as a power." This is why it is time for France to change its strategic compass. Are we heading in that direction? In any case, President Macron confirmed in his second speech at the Sorbonne a more relevant assessment of European security issues: "The essential condition for our security is that Russia does not win the war of aggression it is waging in Ukraine," he reiterated once again. The president finds new arguments in this observation for the "Europe as a power" that he has long advocated. To be fully understood by its partners, it will undoubtedly be necessary for France to increase the level of military support our country provides to Ukraine, which is currently lower than that of Germany. For example, at a time when the Russians are increasing their strikes on the rear, could the French not do more for Ukrainian air defense, as the Germans did by sending an additional Patriot battery? French diplomacy will also have to deploy - given the differences that separate us from Berlin on these issues - a clever strategy so that the next European Council meeting in June makes strong decisions on new sources of European-level financing, both to further arm Ukraine and to support a European defense industrial base. For example, the "community preference" for armaments advocated by the president in his speech is not self-evident beyond our borders. A change in strategic compass implies other reorientations. However, it should not abolish France's vocation to be a bridgehead to the global South. Our country could set the goal of contributing to the emergence of two coalitions among nations: A progressive North-South coalition, serving global issues such as development and climate; and a "stabilizing" coalition, bringing together Western countries and their allies as well as Southern states (which generally seek stability), in order to contain revisionist powers - aggressive or potentially so - with Russia under Putin's leadership serving as a model.
lead: If the head of state's intervention at the prestigious university shows his awareness of the extent of the Russian threat, there is still much to be done to implement a real change in strategic direction, according to former ambassador Michel Duclos, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: Guerre en Ukraine : trois ans après l’invasion russe, la montée de courants antisystème et d’extrême droite fragilise la famille européenne
text: The Hungarian Prime Minister can rub his hands together. Encouraged by Donald Trump's eagerness to negotiate peace in Ukraine with Vladimir Putin, behind the backs of Kiev and the European Union (EU), of which his country is a part, Viktor Orban has welcomed the return of Russia to the great game. "If the American president makes peace, I think Russia will be reintegrated into the global economy, the European security system, and even the European energy and economic system," he declared on Magyar Radio, the state radio, on Friday, February 14. The stalemate in the conflict, public opinion fatigue, and fear of escalation play in favor of Moscow and to the detriment of Brussels. A fracture is emerging in Europe between states determined to protect themselves from Russian predator appetites, such as Poland and the Baltic countries, and advocates of appeasement towards the Vladimir Putin regime. Already consisting of Hungary and Slovakia, with a potentially hesitant Bulgaria and Serbia, a non-EU member historically close to Russia, this camp could expand during crucial elections scheduled this year in Germany, the Czech Republic, and Romania. Still dependent on Russian hydrocarbons, opposed to sanctions against Moscow, Hungary has "a lot to gain from peace," which would, as Mr. Orban reiterated during his radio address, give a "huge boost to the national economy," especially if cheap Russian gas, perceived by his government as the key to economic recovery, starts flowing freely again. Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico, whose country is equally dependent on Gazprom, is on the same page. The two Eurosceptic leaders share a lack of empathy towards the fate of Ukrainians. It matters little to them that this peace could come at the expense of two fundamental EU principles - respect for the will of peoples and borders. They don't care, as Mr. Orban explained on the air, "the days of the European Union are numbered." "The future is not being written in Europe," he predicted, forgetting the benefits his country has reaped since joining the EU in 2004. With politicians within it betting on its downfall, the European family has reason to worry. At the beginning of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the Hungarian nationalist seemed isolated, but his worldview, in line with that of Russian President Vladimir Putin, is now finding followers in the former Soviet bloc. "Orban is recruiting more and more member states to join his axis of paralysis," notes Gabrielius Landsbergis, Lithuanian Minister of Foreign Affairs from 2020 to 2024. Paralysis, as Hungary "blocks more than half of the EU's foreign policy decisions and continues to obstruct the European Peace Facility, a fund intended to support Ukraine's defense," he writes in an article published on the website of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace on February 11. "No one feels safe" "This part of Europe is now the scene of a geopolitical showdown where no one feels safe," notes Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean, who heads the Russia/Eurasia Center at the French Institute of International Relations. Tensions sometimes arise unexpectedly, as shown by the example of Romania, a country deeply rooted in the EU and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization [NATO], which had to cancel [on December 6, 2024] the presidential election due to Russian interference." Romania has not recovered from the democratic cataclysm that this election represented. To everyone's surprise, a conspiracy theorist and pro-Russian candidate, Calin Georgescu, came in first in the first round on November 24, 2024. Unknown in Romanian politics, this former senior official managed to secure 23% of the votes in a country where all polls show strong attachment to the EU and NATO. After a campaign exclusively conducted on TikTok, Mr. Georgescu took advantage of a large-scale manipulation of this social network, orchestrated, according to Romanian intelligence services, by a "state actor." The spotlight then turned to Russia. Obviously, the arrival of a Kremlin-friendly president in Bucharest would weaken the defense of the Alliance's eastern flank, for which losing a member with strategic access to the Black Sea would be a blow. A prospect that delights Mr. Georgescu, who opposes NATO's projects in his country: the installation of the missile defense system in Deveselu, in southern Romania, and, on the shores of the Black Sea, in Constanta, the massive expansion project of the air base destined to become the largest military installation in the region. As he campaigns for the upcoming presidential election scheduled for May, the TikTok candidate continues to spread messages aligned with those of the Kremlin. In a podcast released on January 29, he expressed his belief that "Ukraine is a fictional state" and that its "borders will certainly change." Believing that "the war is lost" for Kiev, he suggested that Romania could reclaim certain territories where a Romanian-speaking minority lives, such as "Northern Bukovina," a region annexed by Joseph Stalin as part of the German-Soviet pact of 1939, to be attached to what was then the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic. A topic dear to Russia, which was suggested a few days later by Sergei Naryshkin, the head of the foreign intelligence service, for the upcoming organization in Moscow of a conference of Hungarian, Slovak, Polish, and Romanian historians invited to discuss the division of the western Ukrainian regions. Faced with Mr. Georgescu's radical statements, the Romanian Constitutional Court could decide to prevent his candidacy, as it had blocked that of Diana Iovanovici Sosoaca, a representative of an extreme right-wing, anti-Semitic, and pro-Russian party in the fall of 2024. However, these judicial decisions deeply divide the country, where supporters of the far right regularly take to the streets to denounce a "coup d'état." Even the pro-European camp is not certain that canceling the election was relevant in the face of a disgruntled electorate, fueled by anger against the two major parties currently in power. Time bomb in Romania "One should not draw false conclusions from the 2024 election," reminds Remus Stefureac, director of the Inscop polling institute, which published a survey in January in Romania. "Even Georgescu's voters are overwhelmingly pro-EU and pro-NATO," he notes, certain that this is primarily a "anti-system message" that has appealed to voters from "rural areas and small towns, with low wages and few job opportunities." Indeed, Mr. Stefureac points out, "Russia's very sophisticated disinformation campaigns never mention direct support from Moscow; they use internal issues and economic frustrations." According to the survey, 48.5% of Romanians feel like a "colony of the EU and the United States," and 68.1% are "opposed to any legal recognition of same-sex couples," the most prominent themes of Russian propaganda. However, Romanians do not want to get closer to Moscow. "In the eyes of most of them, Russia and Putin remain a great danger," says Dacian Ciolos, Romania's prime minister from 2015 to 2017, a centrist and pro-European. "Russia is not the cause of our weaknesses, but it capitalizes on them." He also laments that the reasons for canceling the election remain "poorly explained." Although a judicial inquiry has been opened, it has not yet yielded any public information. The upcoming election in Romania is a real time bomb for the EU. Mr. Georgescu could achieve an even better score than in November 2024, according to current polls. However, this trend should be taken with caution, as the other candidates are not yet known. "The Romanian president has less power than his French counterpart," Mr. Stefureac also points out. But there is no doubt that in case of victory, Mr. Georgescu would align his diplomacy with that of Viktor Orban and Robert Fico. And he would still have some decision-making levers. "The president is the head of the security council and can seriously affect our cooperation with the West, not to mention that he also has control over the intelligence services," worries Iulian Fota, State Secretary from 2022 to 2024 at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Romania. The red-brown, anti-system, and far-right surge also looms over Moldova, a candidate country for EU membership, where pro-Russian parties, gathered within the Alternativa coalition, are gaining momentum. Their goal is to bring down the government led by the pro-European president, Maia Sandu, in the upcoming elections, which must be held before October 26. Skilled at exploiting public discontent, fueled by energy that has become too expensive, these Kremlin allies accuse the current government of being "incapable" of negotiating preferential gas tariffs with Moscow. It is true that, since the beginning of the invasion of Ukraine, Gazprom has cut gas deliveries to Moldova by three, from which 80% of electricity is produced, causing high inflation and a drop in purchasing power. As a model, the far-right cites the Transnistria region, separatist and pro-Russian, which has been receiving free Russian gas for thirty years, while exporting its goods to Moldova and the EU. "Moldova could be the next move on the chessboard in the power struggle between Moscow and Brussels," fears Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean. This country has several vulnerability factors: poverty, the number of Russian speakers who follow Kremlin media, and a long-standing separatist region, Transnistria. "Another region resistant to central authorities is Gagauzia, an autonomous pro-Russian province whose governor, Evghenia Gutul, was photographed with Vladimir Putin in 2024," she adds. "Maia Sandu (...) risks losing her parliamentary majority." Georgia, once a model student Another key player on the chessboard, Georgia is increasingly looking towards Moscow. The invasion of Ukraine has pushed the ruling party since 2012, Georgian Dream, to choose its side. Until then oriented towards Europe - Euro-Atlantic integration is even enshrined in the Constitution - the country has, after February 2022, made a spectacular turnaround by getting closer to Moscow. By choosing to appease its northern neighbor at the expense of Brussels, Bidzina Ivanichvili, the oligarch who secretly runs Georgia, made an "opportunistic calculation," analyzes Marie Dumoulin, a researcher at the think tank European Council on Foreign Relations. "Like Presidents Ilham Aliyev in Azerbaijan and Alexander Lukashenko in Belarus, he is counting on a victory for Russia in Ukraine and wants to be on the side of the winners," she says. Paradoxically, this shift accelerated at the very moment when the EU was offering Georgia a historic opportunity to begin accession negotiations. Despite lagging reforms, Brussels granted Georgia candidate status in December 2023. Primarily strategic, this decision aimed to prevent the country from falling into Russian hands. "With the invasion of Ukraine, Europeans suddenly became aware of this geopolitical confrontation with Russia," notes Ms. Dumoulin. Previously, they either ignored it or refused to see it." Indeed, by giving its approval, Brussels inadvertently hastened Georgia's return to the Russian sphere. Not only because European integration and its corollary – reforms to improve transparency and democracy – would have been incompatible with Mr. Ivanishvili's plan to maintain his grip. But also because the Kremlin did not want to lose this former Soviet republic on the eastern shore of the Black Sea, a maritime area always considered by Russian leaders as their enduring sphere of influence. After Georgia obtained its candidate status for the EU – much to the relief of the population, 80% of whom are pro-European – Georgian Dream swiftly moved to abort the process. Georgia first adopted legislation on "foreign influence" in May 2024, modeled after a 2012 Russian law aimed at silencing civil society and independent media, then enacted a law restricting the rights of the LGBT+ community five months later. The victory – marred by fraud – of Georgian Dream in the October 2024 legislative elections, followed by the announcement the following month of the suspension of the EU accession process and a violent crackdown on pro-European demonstrations, definitively buried the prospect of a European Georgia. This country, which once stood out as a model student in the region for democratization, with financial support from Europeans and Americans, is now sinking into authoritarianism and increasingly courting Russia, whose troops have occupied 20% of the territory since the 2008 war. At that time, Russian tanks stopped 80 kilometers from Tbilisi. Today, Moscow applauds: in late November 2024, Vladimir Putin said he was "impressed by the courage" of the Georgian government, which has become his most reliable support in the Caucasus, even though Tbilisi continues to maintain a pro-European facade in order not to completely alienate the population. "Poutine doesn't even need to militarily invade Georgia anymore," sighed Gotcha Javakhishvili, former ambassador of Georgia to France and the first diplomat of the country to resign in May 2024 in protest against the law on "foreign influence." Nevertheless, thousands of Georgians demonstrate every day against the government, hoping to save their European future. Even though a portion of the population is susceptible to government propaganda, which compares Europe to a realm of depravity, most Georgians associate the EU with security, prosperity, freedom, democracy, justice, and respect for human rights. Their desire for Europe is as strong as their rejection of Russia, perceived by an overwhelming majority as an enemy power and an existential threat, even among the supporters of the Georgian Dream party. Traumatized by the Russo-Georgian war of 2008, pro-European protesters strive to remain peaceful to avoid a new conflict. Their priority is not to give credence to the government's propaganda, which accuses them of wanting to organize a "new Maidan" - referring to the Ukrainian revolution of the winter of 2013-2014, after which the pro-Russian president, Viktor Yanukovych, fled, exfiltrated by a commando from Moscow. However, in Georgia, the political opposition is too weak to embody a credible alternative. Despair is spreading among the ranks. Many are now considering leaving the country. Weakened by internal divisions, preoccupied with other battles, the EU merely condemns the "democratic backsliding" in Tbilisi, unable to agree on sanctions. "Russia knows very well how far Brussels can go, limiting itself to expressing its 'deep concern'," laments Mr. Javakhishvili. If Georgia falls into the hands of Russia, Armenia could in turn have to give up its ambitions of closer ties with the EU. "This is a huge concern in Armenia, as Georgia is its gateway to Europe, with the border with Turkey being closed," points out Areg Kochinyan, president of the Armenian think tank Research Center on Security Policy in Yerevan. The noose would tighten around this country, subject to a blockade by its Turkish and Azerbaijani neighbors, just as it seeks to reduce its dependence on Russia by diversifying its alliances. Russian toolbox From Hungary to Georgia, the EU has a lot to do to compete with Russia and its toolbox, ranging from the allure of gas to electoral manipulations, through the capture of social networks and the instrumentalization of minorities. To push its advantage, the Kremlin also exploits the "fragilities of democracies, which it knows how to exploit very well," notes Ms. Kastouéva-Jean, surprised by the slow reactions of Western countries. "Paris took time to shut down pro-Kremlin media outlets like Russia Today [RT] and Sputnik," she explains. "Now, Xenia Fedorova, the former director of RT France, is publishing a book denouncing 'attacks on freedom of expression' in Europe. Conspiracy theory media have a strong presence in European countries." Agencies aimed at countering foreign digital interference have been established in European territory, such as the French Viginum, but, according to the researcher, "the investment is insufficient, and this fight faces internal opposition from forces that consider migrants, not Russia, as the real risks." "These forces," she concludes, "are the best allies of the Russian regime." To win hearts and minds, Moscow emphasizes its defense of 'traditional values,' in opposition to gender and diversity issues. Its arguments appeal to conservative populations in parts of Eastern Europe and the former USSR, where 'these issues really don't resonate,' says historian Françoise Thom, a specialist in these regions. The Russian Orthodox Church is a significant ally in spreading this message. Several Eastern European Orthodox Churches, including those in Moldova and Georgia, are directly affiliated with or have close relationships with it, allowing Moscow to bypass local governments and media to disseminate a pro-Russian discourse in hundreds of parishes. For the EU, the assessment is bitter. Not only is Vladimir Putin gaining allies in the former Soviet states and Central Europe, where 'anti-system' currents are gaining ground in public opinion, but his methods are now endorsed by the new American administration. Speaking at the Munich Security Conference on February 14, US Vice President J.D. Vance took care to downplay the issue of Russian interference. He compared the cancellation of the presidential election in Romania to practices from the communist era, stating that it occurred 'based on weak suspicions from an intelligence agency' and thanks to 'enormous pressure from neighbors' on the authorities in Bucharest. 'If your democracy can be destroyed with a few hundred thousand dollars in digital advertising from a foreign country, then it wasn't very strong,' he declared before the European elite of diplomacy and defense. Abandoned by the American ally, the Twenty-Seven have an interest in closing ranks to best contain the rising pro-Russian wave. 'If the EU does not pursue a more active foreign policy, the Georgian scenario, that is, the rise to power of supposedly pacifist but actually collaborationist parties, could prevail in Europe,' warns Ms. Thom. Moreover, the Russian president has not lost any of his determination to weaken the EU, which he despises and whose leaders he refers to as "Washington's lapdogs." His capacity for mischief is unlimited. According to a political analyst who wishes to remain anonymous due to their proximity to the Russian presidency: "Vladimir Putin knows that, during his lifetime, there will be no turning back with Western Europe. Even if he does not want to expand territorially, he can create multiple areas of friction to sow and maintain disorder." From this perspective, Donald Trump's outstretched hand comes at the right time. It reinforces the Russian president's belief that Russia is only equal to the United States, while Europe is just a bargaining chip. Like his predecessors, Vladimir Putin sees the world as a Moscow-Washington condominium, divisible into spheres of influence – a recurring thought among the Kremlin's tenants. "I ask one thing of you: give Europe to Russia (...) you can take all the other states under your wing, I will take the European states and ensure their security," reformist President Boris Yeltsin said to his American counterpart, Bill Clinton, in a private conversation on the sidelines of the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe summit in Istanbul, Turkey, in 1999, the stenographic content of which has since been made public. At the time, Mr. Clinton rebuffed his interlocutor. It is unlikely that Donald Trump will do the same when he meets Vladimir Putin.
lead: The stalemate of the conflict, public opinion fatigue, and fear of escalation are playing in favor of Moscow and to the detriment of Europe, at a time when the continent is being abandoned by its American ally. A fracture line is forming on the continent between states determined to protect themselves from the Russian predator's appetites and those who advocate for appeasement towards Vladimir Putin's regime.

title: Donald Trump lance, avec Vladimir Poutine, des négociations de paix en Ukraine et brise l’unité des alliés
text: In diplomacy, choreography is never a secondary matter. It allows for the staging of power dynamics. The way Donald Trump approached the war in Ukraine on Wednesday, February 12, is significant: he sees it as a bilateral issue with Moscow, with the sole purpose being peace, however fragile. The American president had previously refused to comment on his phone calls with Vladimir Putin since the transition period. This time, he detailed their morning exchange in a post on his Truth Social network. Only after that did Donald Trump call Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. Washington thus declares the peace negotiations open, which have already been the focus of confidential exchanges for several weeks. The conversation between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin lasted about ninety minutes according to the Kremlin; it is the first bilateral contact of this level since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. The American president mentioned a few topics discussed: Ukraine, as well as the Middle East, artificial intelligence, energy, the "power of the dollar," and inevitably identified his interlocutor's vanity as a vulnerability. "He used my very strong campaign slogan, that of 'COMMON SENSE'," he rejoiced on Truth Social. The two leaders have decided to "work very closely together," including visiting each other's countries, as Donald Trump specified. In the afternoon, the American president revealed that he could meet his Russian counterpart in Saudi Arabia at an unknown date. The overall tone of the message was very warm towards the Russian president, who is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court for war crimes. "I want to thank President Putin for his time and efforts for this call, and for the release, yesterday, of Marc Fogel," Donald Trump concluded. Sentenced to fourteen years in prison in Russia for drug possession, Marc Fogel was released by the Russian authorities before being triumphantly received at the White House on Tuesday evening. In return, the United States agreed to release Alexander Vinnik, a Russian convicted of cryptocurrency-related crimes. Donald Trump sees this exchange as the possible beginning of a virtuous bilateral cycle. Vladimir Putin, on the other hand, demonstrates his skill here, ready to compromise on the trivial while focusing on the essential: preventing a democratic and liberal development of Ukraine, tied to the West. Unlike his American counterpart, the Russian president does not need a positive outcome in these negotiations. But the holding of these talks contributes to his diplomatic detoxification. American red lines, all to the disadvantage of Kiev. After his meeting with Volodymyr Zelensky, Donald Trump made a shorter comment. "The conversation went very well," he said. Like President Putin, he wants to make peace. For now, the American president has not publicly expressed clear support for the Ukrainians. He has emphasized the need to somehow repay the efforts already made by Washington, by using Ukrainian rare earth lands as collateral, rich in metals. The first important stage of negotiations will take place in Munich (Germany) on February 14. The Ukrainian president will meet with Vice President J.D. Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio on the sidelines of the annual security conference. The Secretary of State is part of the team designated by Donald Trump to negotiate with Moscow. The president also mentioned his national security advisor, Mike Waltz, CIA director John Ratcliffe, but not General Keith Kellogg, who was appointed as a special envoy on this issue. This demotion is accompanied by a promotion for Steven Witkoff, a real estate entrepreneur and golf partner of Donald Trump. He, who was involved in negotiations on Gaza, is now also taking charge of negotiations on Ukraine, a subject he knows little about. According to conservative Fox News host Sean Hannity, the chief propagandist of Trumpism on the network, Steven Witkoff reportedly met with Vladimir Putin for over three hours in Moscow, on the sidelines of his trip to retrieve Marc Fogel. This meeting has not been officially confirmed. In Brussels, U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth bluntly outlined the Trump administration's red lines, all unfavorable to Kiev. "The United States does not believe that Ukraine's accession to NATO is a realistic outcome for a negotiated settlement," he said. This is a complete continuity with the Biden administration. The Democratic president, influenced by the Cold War, feared above all a nuclear escalation with Russia. In an interview with Time magazine in June 2024, he said the following about resolving the conflict: "Peace looks like making sure that Russia never, ever, ever, ever occupies Ukraine again. And that doesn't mean NATO, that they become part of NATO, it means we have a relationship with them like with other countries, where we provide weapons so they can defend themselves in the future." Sense of urgency from the United States The second red line outlined by Mr. Hegseth is the refusal of any American deployment in Ukraine. He mentioned "European and non-European troops" sent as part of a "non-NATO mission." The third red line is to consider it an "unrealistic goal" to return to the pre-2014 Ukrainian borders. Once again, these two points were shared by the Biden administration. The huge difference lies in their statements at the start of the negotiations. The United States immediately forfeits any leverage by stating what will not happen, without obtaining any concessions from the Russian aggressor. The only remaining argument is the net of sanctions still in place against Moscow. This hasty approach gives a sense of rush on the part of the United States. Donald Trump believes it is possible to achieve a ceasefire in the "not too distant future." He does not question the principle of supporting Ukraine, but rather the financial conditions. "We will continue as much as we need to," said the president. Behind these supposedly balanced statements, Wednesday represents a major turning point. "Any agreement negotiated from a position of weakness will be exploited by the Kremlin," notes Maria Snegovaya, an expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies in Washington. It will not hold in the long term, as Putin's essential goals remain unchanged: total control over Ukraine and a revision of post-Cold War borders. Such an agreement could increase the risk of a broader war in the future. No room for Europeans in the negotiations Through this individual approach, Donald Trump breaks the united front presented by Western countries since the beginning of the war. The new American president does not foresee any role for Europeans in the negotiations: he treats them like a master with a metal ruler, correcting them for their insufficient level of security spending, inventing fictitious amounts. But the most serious rupture is with a cardinal principle of the Biden administration: "Nothing about Ukraine without Ukraine." The Democratic president did not want an agreement behind the victim's back, but he also refused to publicly discuss a realistic end to the conflict, giving the impression of a war without a solution. Donald Trump, on the other hand, abruptly discards this collective approach, hostile to the aggressor. He takes the risk of replacing it with a dictate in Russian, or a parody of a temporary ceasefire.
lead: Without consulting with European leaders, the American president held lengthy discussions on Wednesday with the Kremlin leader. He also briefly called Volodymyr Zelensky, who is set to meet on Friday in Munich with an American delegation led by Vice President J.D. Vance.

title: La Roumanie tente de surmonter sa « marée brune »
text: Concerned about the rise of the far right in Romania, a few thousand protesters gathered on Saturday, March 15, in Victory Square in Bucharest to express their support for the European Union (EU) and their rejection of the "brown tide." Champions of extremism and hateful rhetoric, far-right parties have gained ground in recent years, increasing from 9% of the votes in the 2020 legislative elections to 32% in 2024. Holding a star-spangled flag, 28-year-old Andra said she felt "a great relief" since the far-right candidate Calin Georgescu, a fervent critic of the EU and NATO and an admirer of Russian President Vladimir Putin, was excluded from the upcoming presidential election scheduled for May 4. Razvan, 21, shares her sentiment. "The invalidation of Georgescu's candidacy is justified because he supports the fascist Legionnaire movement, which is modeled after the one affiliated with the Nazis during World War II in Romania. Moreover, the funding of his campaign was opaque, and he is beholden to Moscow. This man is dangerous." "The rise of the far right does not mean that voters are rejecting the EU; it is more of an internal problem. Our politicians are corrupt, people no longer trust them, they want them out, and to achieve that, they are willing to vote for anyone. It's the fault of the traditional centrist parties that we are in this situation," emphasized Mihaela Brebinaru Gheorghe, an activist from the anti-corruption NGO Déclic, one of the organizers of the protest. The unexpected success of Calin Georgescu, who garnered 22.9% of the votes in the first round of the presidential election on November 24, 2024, and the subsequent annulment of the election by the Constitutional Court, plunged Romania into a serious political crisis from which it hopes to emerge through the May presidential election. "TikTok Candidate" At first glance, the threat posed by Mr. Georgescu has been averted, as his candidacy was invalidated on March 11 by the Constitutional Court. However, another figure from the local far right has taken up the mantle. George Simion, 38, leader of the Alliance for the Unity of Romanians (AUR), is now seen as the favorite for the first round, according to polls crediting him with around 30% of the vote intentions. Speaking to Le Monde in his Parliament office, where 28 of his party's deputies sit, Mr. Simion insisted that the interview be filmed to be posted on TikTok afterward. It should be noted that the social network has become the new darling of Romanian politicians since the electoral success of Calin Georgescu, who extensively used it during his campaign last fall, to the point of being dubbed "the TikTok candidate." "It was phenomenal; in the last three days of his campaign, he accumulated 85 million views, ranking as the 8th most tagged account on the network, ahead of Lionel Messi!" Barbu Mateescu, author of a sociological study that analyzes in detail the discontent of Romanians, which was published in January by the Konrad-Adenauer Foundation, explains, "Another absurdity of this campaign was that some voters who had given their vote to Georgescu were unable to tell their acquaintances the day after the vote the name of the candidate they had voted for. Others confused Calin Georgescu with George Calinescu, a Romanian writer who had nothing to do with the election," continues the sociologist. "Corrupt politicians" Ana, 66 years old, a retired florist, knows who she voted for on November 24, 2024. She speaks highly of Calin Georgescu, whom she sees as "the messiah," the one who will rescue the country "from the clutches of the traditional political system and the corrupt politicians who have been governing for thirty-five years and mocking us." She believes she is experiencing "a historic turning point" that will see the replacement of the liberal system by sovereigntists. Because NATO, like the EU, are "destined to disappear in the near future, just like the USSR and the Soviet socialist bloc," of which Romania was once a part, collapsed in the early 1990s. "You will meet the same fate!" she predicts, tapping on her kitchen table with her ringed hand. The war in Ukraine does not worry her much. "Russia is not the aggressor. The problem is that Ukraine has embraced the Western enemy. Moscow punished it for that! It's all Kiev's fault, they shouldn't have provoked the Russian bear. We, in Romania, don't care about Putin, he has never harmed us." It doesn't matter to her that her hero, Calin Georgescu, cannot run; she will give her vote "to the sovereigntist candidate who defends the same ideas." George Simion (left), leader of Romania's second largest party, the Alliance for the Unity of Romanians (AUR), and former Polish Prime Minister Mateusz Morawiecki, in Bucharest, on March 14, 2025. ANDREEA CAMPEANU/REUTERS George Simion, the new candidate of the far right, hopes to attract the electorate of the "TikTok candidate," who has recently been placed under judicial control. "Some of his supporters will support me, others not, depending on the voting instructions he will give. Above all, I am a moderate, I want to prevent my country from repeating the mistakes of the past, from moving towards totalitarianism," explains this tall man with a booming voice. "Incredible rise" With sweeping gestures and a loud voice, the man likes to pose for the cameras. Formerly a member of a football fan group, he emerged on the political scene in 2020 when his party, AUR, created a year earlier, advocating Christian values, family, and Greater Romania, entered Parliament for the first time. "An incredible rise achieved in a few weeks, thanks to the handling of traditional themes of the far right, religion, nostalgia for lost greatness, and the non-recognition of the country's current borders. In the midst of the Covid-19 pandemic, the AUR party opposed sanitary measures, claiming that the vaccine was a poison," recalls Cristian Preda, a professor at the University of Bucharest. According to him, the most incredible thing is how "these extremist political currents manage to reverse reality." Mr. Preda, a former Member of the European Parliament, can't believe it. "Look at this reversal of values! Lately, President Macron has become their scapegoat, they make us believe that he is the one who wants war and that he will drag all of Europe into conflict!" In Mr. Simion's office, on the 3rd floor of the Parliament, a building so vast that each political group has its own floor, television screens continuously broadcast the party's channel, Realitatea Plus, which criticizes Emmanuel Macron and praises the American president, Donald Trump. However, George Simion wants to show that he is not as anti-European and pro-Russian as his reputation suggests. Bankruptcy of traditional centrist parties He is certain that Romania, "with its gas reserves in the Black Sea," will contribute to replacing Russian gas in European households. The war in nearby Ukraine does not affect the country, but it is far from over. "Westerners believe in a ceasefire, they forget that Russia will never stop, war is its main sustenance. Ukraine will have to accept an unfair peace, it will have to temporarily cede territories. Personally, I have no sympathy for Ukraine, which does not respect its Romanian minority, but I am in favor of it maintaining its territorial integrity," he says, recalling the loss of Romanian lands during the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact in August 1939: "We too were victims of a dismemberment against which we could do nothing." Surprisingly, he presents himself as "a staunch supporter of NATO, which must remain a defensive alliance." Election poster of candidate Nicusor Dan, in Bucharest, March 12, 2025. ANDREEA CAMPEANU/REUTERS The other prominent candidate for the May 4th election is Nicusor Dan, 55 years old, the mayor of Bucharest, a mathematician trained in France, described by some of the Romanian youth as "the cleanest of all politicians." Receiving Le Monde in his office, the youthful-looking and soft-spoken mayor strives to describe his country in a positive light. "The private sector is developing well, the country is catching up with its neighbors in Central and Eastern Europe, and our universities are producing excellent specialists," he explains in perfect French. Among these institutions is the Bucharest University of Advanced Studies, which he helped create. Also caught up in the TikTok fever, its communicators are also insisting that the interview be filmed to be later posted on this social network, which has 9 million users out of 19 million Romanians. According to Mr. Dan, two major problems darken the prospects of his country: the rise of extremist parties and the bankruptcy of traditional centrist parties, which, "mired in bureaucracy and corruption," have lost the trust of voters. According to a survey published on March 17 by AtlasIntel, Mr. Dan is in a good position. Initially predicted to lose in the first round on May 4 against George Simion, he would win in the second round, in a potential runoff vote against the far-right. This estimation should be taken with caution, as more than a fifth of voters are undecided or tempted by abstention.
lead: After the cancellation of the presidential election in November 2024, the Romanians vote again at the beginning of May, in a climate marked by mistrust towards traditional parties.

title: « Ukraine. Sur les traces des bourreaux », sur Arte : chronique d’une horreur mûrement planifiée par la Russie
text: ART – TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 18 AT 9:00 PM – DOCUMENTARY Summary executions, torture, civilian abductions, deportation of children... Ksenia Bolchakova and Manon Loizeau have collected evidence of war crimes committed by the Russians on the ground, as part of the "special military operation" launched in February 2022. Years of horrors and suffering that are part of a vast project carefully planned by Vladimir Putin for about ten years, since the annexation of Crimea in 2014. A project that aims not only to "denazify" the country, as Russian propaganda claims, but also to annihilate a culture, a language. Behind the war and the atrocious fighting, there is also a vast project of forced assimilation. Even if it means deporting children, so that young Ukrainians become good little Russians. Ksenia Bolchakova, born in Moscow, co-winner of the Albert-Londres audiovisual award in 2022 for a documentary on the Wagner Group, and Manon Loizeau, a French-British journalist and filmmaker, conducted the investigation in the heart of areas that have become sadly famous: Bucha, Izyum, Kherson... Chilling testimony Several witnesses agreed to speak on camera. A former soldier recruited by the Wagner Group from a Russian prison, who is now in hiding in Russia, recounts the "cleansing" he participated in Bakhmut: "We shot 250 or 300 people that day." Nikita, another Russian soldier, witness to horrors, deserted after two months on the front lines and sought refuge in Spain. Another chilling testimony: Victoria, a young math teacher in Izyum, was abducted, tortured, and imprisoned for six months in Russia before being released following a prisoner exchange. Through interviews and revisiting places used for imprisonment and torture (such as schools and police stations in Izyum), the evidence accumulates and demonstrates that these war crimes were planned well in advance. Preparation facilitated by the presence of infiltrators in many cities and "collaborators," especially in the eastern part of the country. The evidence? Detailed lists of individuals to be arrested dating back to January 2022 and bearing the signature of the FSB, the successor of the KGB in the Russian Federation. Or the presence on the ground of "specialists" in torture methods from private companies, specially brought from Russia to extract information and break witnesses. Finally, mention these thousands of ready-to-use textbooks to instill in the minds of young Ukrainians the good principles of Russian education. From start to finish, this thorough on-the-ground investigation proves to be relentless. And necessary.
lead: Ksenia Bolchakova and Manon Loizeau collected evidence of war crimes committed by the Russians in the field. A long and relentless investigation. And necessary.

title: Les Européens cherchent à empêcher un veto hongrois sur les sanctions contre la Russie
text: Since the invasion of Ukraine by Russia, Hungary has regularly threatened to block European Union (EU) aid to Kiev or to oppose sanctions against Russia. The return of Donald Trump to the White House, with whom Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban shares, among other things, the desire to quickly end the war in Ukraine, has further hardened his positions, and today, Europeans are concerned about it. So far, the crisis has been avoided each time at the last minute. In mid-March, Hungary finally approved the renewal of some sanctions against Russia - those targeting President Vladimir Putin, Sergey Lavrov, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, and around 2,400 Kremlin associates. At the end of January, it had already joined at the last minute in extending sectoral restrictive measures, which allowed the EU to freeze 200 billion euros of assets of the Russian Central Bank, impose import and export bans, and cut off Russia from Western financial circuits. In Brussels, some imagine that Donald Trump asked his friend Viktor Orban not to exercise his veto right. While the American president is trying to convince Moscow to engage in a ceasefire agreement with Kiev and is himself threatening to tighten US sanctions against Russia, he had no interest in Europeans easing the pressure. Toughening the stance What will happen next time the sanctions against Moscow need to be renewed? "We do not want [them] to become an obstacle to negotiations on a ceasefire or peace," reminded Hungarian Minister of European Affairs Janos Boka on March 18. The Twenty-Seven have thus obtained a respite, but it could be short-lived. The renewal of restrictive measures, which requires unanimity, is done every six months, and the next deadline is July 31. Europeans fear that Hungary will cancel the sectoral sanctions that weaken the Russian economy and give Europeans, with the 200 billion euros of frozen Russian assets, a significant leverage in future peace negotiations in Ukraine. This money, the interests of which fund the 18 billion euros in aid they plan to provide to Kiev in 2025, could also one day pay for the reconstruction of Ukraine. In recent weeks, in Brussels and in the capitals, efforts are being made to protect the sixteen packages of sanctions, and more specifically the 200 billion euros of Russian assets. "France and Germany should already toughen their stance towards Hungary," says a European diplomat, who is counting on future Chancellor Friedrich Merz to take a stand. "So far," he continues, "we have all been accommodating with Orban. At the Council table, everyone gives him big hugs." "Olaf Scholz [the outgoing German chancellor] says nothing, Emmanuel Macron seems to appreciate him." "Ongoing opposition on Ukraine" Beyond political pressure, Europeans have tools to twist Viktor Orban's arm. A minority of them, supporters of the stick approach, like Belgium, advocate for the use of Article 7, which allows for a member state to be deprived of its voting rights in case of systematic violations of the rule of law. They believe that the new Hungarian law against the Gay Pride gives them arguments. "Hungary's ongoing opposition on Ukraine must eventually lead to a conclusion on Hungary's status in the EU," declared German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock on March 17. However, to activate Article 7, unanimity of the member states (minus one vote) is required. The vote of Italy under Giorgia Meloni is not guaranteed. As for Slovakia, its Prime Minister Robert Fico assured on March 20 that they will "never" support sanctions against Hungary. Pro-Russian, "Slovakia does not act in Brussels as it speaks in Bratislava," emphasizes a European diplomat. During the summits on March 20 and March 6, Slovakia sided with the twenty-six European heads of state and government who reaffirmed their support for Kiev, while Hungary distanced itself. Among those in favor of a showdown with Viktor Orban, new financial sanctions are also advocated. In 2024, Hungary has already been deprived of one billion euros in structural funds, and to date, 20 billion euros in European funds that it could claim remain frozen due to its rule of law violations. France under Emmanuel Macron, who is pleased to have some influence over Viktor Orban, and other member states prefer to focus on dialogue. Once and for all, argued by proponents of the soft approach, it could be possible to buy off Hungary, so that all twenty-seven can decide to "protect" the frozen Russian assets, for example in a separate legal entity. The Commission is working on all scenarios. It is true that Europeans can find arrangements, as when, on December 14, 2023, on an idea from Olaf Scholz, Viktor Orban left the Council room to let his counterparts decide, without him, on the opening of accession negotiations for Ukraine to the EU. The day before, the Commission had announced the release of just over 10 billion euros for Hungary... There remains a last resort, if Viktor Orban is not convinced: national measures. Belgium, in particular, could take action. It hosts the majority of the frozen Russian assets, managed by the company Euroclear, based on its soil. However, in this case, it warns that it would require its partners to share the economic, financial, and legal risks. The Commission, for its part, is working on all scenarios and reminds Budapest, whenever possible, that Brussels can harm it. It is thus considering opening an investigation into the illegal Chinese subsidies that BYD may have received to assemble electric vehicles in Hungary. While China is one of the main investors in Hungary, this could be detrimental to Budapest's relations with Beijing.
lead: The European Union is considering how to counter the Hungarian Prime Minister who opposes restrictive measures against Moscow, particularly the freezing of the 200 billion euros in assets.

title: « L’Europe refuse de voir que Vladimir Poutine mène en réalité une guerre contre elle et contre l’Occident »
text: What is Europe still waiting for? Ten years ago, when Russian troops occupied Crimea and launched an insurgency of criminals and separatists in eastern Ukraine, one could still afford to be "shocked" and "stunned". Even on the eve of February 24, 2022, after months of deploying hundreds of thousands of Russian soldiers, people still refused to believe that a full-scale territorial conflict against a European country would actually happen. For three years now, Europe has almost daily seen on the news what is happening less than two hours away by flight: the relentless bombing of cities, the fleeing of millions of people from war zones and across borders, countless crimes committed against the civilian population, missile strikes near nuclear facilities - in short, the destruction of the Ukrainian nation's livelihood. This is about plunging Ukraine into cold and darkness and forcing it to surrender. All of this is not happening somewhere unknown but right in the heart of Europe. It is Kiev, a thousand-year-old city with its cathedrals and monasteries, that is being affected. In Kharkiv, a metropolis with modernist skyscrapers from the 1920s, entire neighborhoods are in ruins. Even the "little Vienna" (Lviv, the former Habsburg Lemberg) has not been spared by Russian missiles. Odessa, known for its promenade and the Potemkin Stairs thanks to Sergei Eisenstein's film (1898-1948), Battleship Potemkin (1927), is under attack by Iranian drones. How would Europeans react if bombs were falling on Trieste in Italy or Marseille? A dangerous illusion It is a dangerous illusion to assume that this is just a Russo-Ukrainian conflict. From the beginning, Russian leaders have left no doubt, neither through their statements nor their actions, about their objectives: to regain control of Central and Eastern Europe and restore the Yalta Europe, which Milan Kundera (1929-2023) once called the "kidnapped West" in an article published in 1983 in the journal Le Débat (Gallimard). A taste of this is seen in the occupied territories of eastern Ukraine, with prison camps and torture chambers. Europe, turning a blind eye, refuses to see that Vladimir Putin is actually waging a war against Europe and the West. This is openly expressed in a language that combines the Pan-Slavic myths of the 19th-century theorist, Nikolai Danilevsky (1822-1885), with the diatribes of current Moscow propagandists against the "Gayrope", the decadence of the West, and "totalitarian liberalism". Putin's ideologues understand the vulnerability of the transatlantic world, they analyze the rivalries within the European Union as well as the social conflicts specific to each state. Poutine has become a master in stoking fears and resentments, but he is especially skilled in leveraging the fear that Russia, a nuclear power, uses at all times to blackmail - not without success, as evidenced by the decline in solidarity towards Ukraine in many European countries. Putin's war aims not only to exhaust Ukrainians after over a thousand days of fighting, but also to undermine the willingness of the West to help, to generate weariness and resignation in a society overwhelmed by crises. It is not just about targeted disinformation or the destruction of credibility through a widespread relativism of the type "Nothing Is True and Everything Is Possible," as described by British journalist Peter Pomerantsev, but also concrete actions of subversion and sabotage, which have recently targeted military infrastructures. Putin's Russia seeks to become the center of all nationalist and anti-European currents. "Breaking the backbone of Europe," as formulated by Sergei Karaganov, even if it requires the use of nuclear weapons, but the most advantageous option would be an agreement with the American president, without Ukraine or Europeans having a say. Europeans, who have so far relied on the military protection of the United States, must now learn to finally rely only on themselves, as demonstrated by the arrogant and provocative speech delivered on February 14 by Vice President J.D. Vance at the Munich Security Conference. The capitulation of Ukraine to Russian conditions - the cession of occupied territories, disarmament, neutrality without security guarantees - would mean delivering a European nation to the appetites of a neo-imperialist power with fascist tendencies. Erosion Manifestations It is impossible to know if and how Europeans, abandoned by a MAGA version of Donald Trump, will manage to assert themselves. It is not just about being able to defend oneself and wage war, the issue is also the ideological battle against Putinism and its worldview. A new frontier and a new front are crossing the continent today. It is mainly Ukrainians, Poles, and Balts who are on the front line defending Europe, soon to be followed, hopefully, by an "alliance of volunteers" who understand what is at stake. Erosion manifestations are visible everywhere today, whether it be the dispute over immigration, the cost of defense, the growing gap between the rich and the poor, the conflict between particular national interests and the common good of Europe. To meet this challenge, it is useful to delve into the history of the great intellectual debates of 20th-century Europe. I am thinking of "The Treason of the Intellectuals" (Grasset, 1927) by the philosopher Julien Benda (1867-1956), written during the moral crisis of the 1920s. But I am also thinking of the lifelong battle fought by Raymond Aron (1905-1983) against the totalitarian temptation, and especially of the hopeful message he had formulated nearly half a century ago, albeit in a completely different historical context, in his book "In Defense of Decadent Europe" (Robert Laffont, 1977): "The crises of Europe are not only signs of exhaustion, resignation, and anxiety about the future, but also signs of the persistent vitality of a civilization that, through self-criticism and self-correction, is able to reinvent itself." No one can say - and historians have learned that all history is open - how Europe will emerge from its crisis at the end of a long post-war period. (Translated from German by Pierre Deshusses)
lead: Faced with the totalitarian pressure that the Russian president is exerting on Europe, the winner of the 2024 Jacques Delors Prize for European Book, Karl Schlögel, reminds, in an article in "Le Monde", of the European identity of Ukraine and expresses concern about a return of the "kidnapped West" mentioned by Milan Kundera.

title: A Pékin, Jean-Noël Barrot demande à la Chine d’influencer la Russie sur le dossier ukrainien
text: Lately, Chinese officials have been whispering things in the ears of their European counterparts that they want to hear. The defense of an international standards framework is among the elements they hold dear, but it has been eroding since Donald Trump came to power in the United States. China positions itself as an alternative, constantly denouncing the shortcomings of American power. This argument has been echoed, both by its Foreign Minister, Wang Yi, and by its Prime Minister, Li Qiang, when they each received the French Foreign Minister, Jean-Noël Barrot, in Beijing on Thursday, March 27. "Faced with profound changes in the international context (...), we must defend multilateralism and oppose unilateralism," Mr. Wang declared at the Diaoyutai State Guesthouse. "As we all know, the world is not at peace today, and instability and uncertainty are rising," Li Qiang lamented, welcoming the French Foreign Minister at the Great Hall of the People. The EU is waiting for concrete assurances. China is counting on the turmoil caused by the attitude of the American president and his entourage towards Europe to try to achieve a relaxation of relations with the latter. This would help turn the page on three years of complicated relations due to two major issues: Beijing's support for Moscow despite the war in Ukraine, and Chinese factory production threatening to undermine European industries. However, Europeans are not giving a blank check; they are waiting for concrete assurances. This issue is particularly relevant to France because, in retaliation for the EU's adoption of tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles in October 2024, China targeted French spirits. In January, it initiated an anti-dumping investigation affecting cognac and armagnac, resulting in respective tariffs of 34% and 39%, significantly impacting the French sector, as Paris had supported the barriers to entry for its vehicles. The French minister was able to announce, on Friday evening in Shanghai at the end of his visit, that the final adoption of these measures, originally scheduled for April 5, had been postponed by three months. "This is a first step that will need to be followed by others for us to definitively put this dispute behind us," Mr. Barrot said. Stocks that had already been shipped to China for duty-free sale but had been blocked by authorities are also expected to be released. Another stumbling block is the war in Ukraine. While the closeness between Moscow and Beijing has continued to strengthen despite the Russian invasion, the two capitals share a common interest in weakening Western powers.
