President Xi Jinping is expected to visit Moscow in May for the celebrations of the 80th anniversary of the Soviet victory over Nazi Germany, and Vladimir Putin is set to visit China at the end of the summer for the anniversary of Japan's defeat. Moving towards a "ceasefire logic" For Europeans, who have been urging China for three years to use its influence on Moscow but have seen growing Sino-Russian exchanges and China supplying components to keep Russian arms factories running, a weariness has set in regarding China's claims of neutrality. However, the fact that the new American president largely aligns with the Russian discourse on the conflict in Ukraine leaves Europeans alone in pressuring China on its Russian policy. "I have invited my counterparts to exert their influence on Vladimir Putin's Russia so that it genuinely enters into a ceasefire logic allowing for negotiations leading to a just and lasting peace in Europe," summarized Jean-Noël Barrot on Friday. The day before in Paris, Emmanuel Macron expressed his hope that Xi Jinping "could play a very active role" in favor of peace in Ukraine. On the other hand, China is aware that Europe is struggling with being sidelined in the discussion on the future of Ukraine between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin. Foreign Minister Wang Yi emphasized that he believes the EU should have a place in these negotiations, although it remains unclear if China is willing to use its presumed influence on Moscow to help in this regard. For China, which sees the US military alliance network as a barrier to its own rise in power, the apparent abandonment of Europe by the United States is a godsend. During the American election campaign, China may have feared that American diplomacy would turn largely against it in the future, but in fact, much of Donald Trump's and his circle's discourse is focused against Europe, even though Beijing is also targeted in the trade war. China, therefore, has every reason to emphasize to Europeans the unreliability of America and speak highly of the potential progress of European autonomy.
lead: China hopes to attract more European states abandoned by the decisions of the new American diplomacy. During his visit to Beijing and Shanghai, the French Minister of Foreign Affairs obtained a guarantee regarding the cognac issue.

title: « Les dépenses européennes en matière de défense devront augmenter considérablement »
text: Facing the threat posed by Russia to the European continent, the rearmament of Europe is an urgent and vital priority. This awareness is already being translated into bold announcements by the European Union (EU) and, in Germany, by the historic decision of the Christian Democratic Union, its Bavarian allies, and the Social Democratic Party to exclude defense and infrastructure (roads, railways, etc.) from the budgetary discipline rules enshrined in the Constitution (the "debt brake"). The political will in Europe is stronger than ever, with efforts underway to assess the ground forces needed to deter Vladimir Putin from aggression. The war has already been costly for Russia. Its army is now larger, more experienced, and better equipped than the force that invaded Ukraine in 2022. By the end of 2024, around 700,000 Russian soldiers were deployed on the front lines. The Russian defense industry has significantly increased its production, producing or refurbishing around 1,550 tanks, 5,700 armored vehicles, and 450 artillery pieces per year, as well as thousands of drones and long-range missiles. Production has increased by at least 150% for all categories of weapon systems. At this rate, the Kremlin would be capable of launching an attack on Europe if it so desired. According to assessments by the intelligence services of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), Russia will be ready within three years. In these circumstances, Europe's top priority is to support Ukraine. The Ukrainian army is one of the most experienced in the world and represents the most effective deterrent against Russian aggression. Europe can effectively support the country, even though it is still challenging to do without the United States for certain essential capabilities (satellites, military intelligence, long-range air defense, missiles). Need for 300,000 European soldiers Financially, Europe can replace the United States: it has collectively provided as much military aid. The EU would only need to spend an additional 0.12% of its gross domestic product (GDP) to replace the American big brother, which is not beyond reach. In the event of a conflict on the eastern flank, NATO planning foresees that the 90,000 American soldiers in Europe could be reinforced by up to 200,000 soldiers, particularly for American ground forces. Therefore, the realistic estimate of European needs to replace American combat power would be 300,000 soldiers, focusing on 50 mechanized and armored brigades. These would supplement the 1.47 million European soldiers currently in service. Equipping these 50 new brigades would indeed be a major challenge for Europe. During Chancellor Helmut Schmidt's term (1974-1982), West Germany quickly modernized the Bundeswehr in response to the threat posed by Soviet forces. These brigades require 1,400 tanks, 2,000 infantry fighting vehicles, and 700 artillery pieces (howitzers, multiple rocket launchers), at least 1 million 155-millimeter artillery shells, and thousands of drones. Europe should also develop aviation, transport, communication, and intelligence capabilities for missile and drone warfare. As military equipment spending represents only about 0.7% of European GDP, it must be significantly increased to meet these objectives. More competition is needed. European-wide procurement will be crucial, costs could be reduced if orders are pooled, and competition is encouraged. The European Sky Shield air defense project, crucial as well, should focus on European products, such as drones or missiles. There should be more competition among European companies for large contracts and less government intervention in the companies themselves. Unused industrial capacities, for example in the automotive industry, suggest that additional demand could be met quickly. European defense spending, currently around 2% of GDP, will need to increase significantly from the current level. An initial assessment suggests an increase of 250 billion euros per year would be necessary to reach around 3.5% of GDP. This effort could be evenly split between the EU and member states, facilitating both significant joint purchases and substantial national military spending. Politically, this arrangement should suit countries most exposed to the Russian threat and those with significant military shortcomings. In the short term, these spending increases should be financed through debt for political and economic reasons. This financing should boost European economic activity at a time when external demand could be undermined by the trade war initiated by Donald Trump. Defense spending can also contribute to long-term growth through innovation. However, annual budgets must also increase regularly. Germany alone has already announced a major military spending program, financed by debt through a relaxation of budget discipline rules. But a true European mechanism is lacking to promote common purchases and European investment programs such as in military satellites. Finally, to ensure the necessary common direction for Europe's security, coordination between France, Germany, the United Kingdom, and Poland will be essential.
lead: Militarily, Europe still cannot do without the United States for certain essential capabilities: satellites, military intelligence, long-range air defense, missiles. However, experts Alexandr Burilkov and Guntram Wolff believe that these gaps can be filled, as they argue in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".

title: Françoise Thom, historienne : « La liste des services rendus par Trump à Moscou s’allonge chaque jour »
text: Historian, emeritus lecturer at Sorbonne University, Françoise Thom is the author of numerous works on Russia, including "Poutine ou l’obsession de la puissance" (Litos, 2022). She analyzes the evolution of Russo-American relations, which she believes is marked by a "Putinization" of the United States, orchestrated by the Kremlin and accepted by Trump. How do the American-Russian negotiations aimed at bringing peace to Ukraine illustrate the "Putinization" of the United States that you diagnose? We cannot truly speak of negotiations when the American side unilaterally abandoned its pressure instruments on Moscow even before diplomatic exchanges officially began: Ukraine's accession to NATO, non-recognition of Russian territorial annexations, and the gradual lifting of sanctions. Vladimir Putin's tactic is simple: pretend to negotiate, concede nothing, and wait for new concessions from his American counterparts. It is noteworthy that Putin refused to negotiate with the initial candidate chosen by the American president, Donald Trump, as a special envoy for Russia and Ukraine, General Keith Kellogg, because he knew Russia and had expressed the opinion that one should be wary of the Russians. Trump promptly acceded to the desires of the Russian president and chose Steve Witkoff, a real estate developer and his golf partner, as his interlocutor, who was approved by Putin due to his profound ignorance of the issues and propensity to swallow even the most outrageous Kremlin propaganda. The "Putinization" of the Trump administration is evident in its indifference to ethics, leading to a clouding of intelligence. Trump is incredibly easy to manipulate, and Putin demonstrates every day that he knows how to push the right buttons to get the desired reactions. What does Trump's attitude owe to his personal admiration for Putin? Trump is primarily characterized by his partisan fanaticism and vindictive temperament. Putin has convinced him that they have the same enemies: the "globalists" lurking in the "deep state": Hillary Clinton, Joe Biden... Trump feels connected to Putin by a strange solidarity since the U.S. intelligence report on Russian interference in the 2016 elections, which Trump dismisses as "fake news." Putin has linked the Ukrainian cause to the Democrats, and since Trump systematically wants to do the opposite of Biden, it is easy to manipulate him from the Kremlin by suggesting that a certain measure will enrage the Democrats. Trump admires how Putin has dealt with Russian liberals and his mastery of staying in power. In an article published on the "Desk Russie" website, you write about the public humiliation of Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House by Trump and Vice President J. D. Vance, on February 28, "has all the marks of a special operation devised within the walls of the Kremlin." Why? For a long time, the Kremlin has dreamed of having the West impose surrender on the Ukrainians. The Russians want to break the morale of the Ukrainians. To do this, they must send the message that they have been abandoned by their Western allies and that their only option is to join their Russian brothers. After the 2008 Russo-Georgian war, the main argument of pro-Russian forces in Georgia was that the West had abandoned President Mikheil Saakashvili, who had relied entirely on the support of democracies. The exemplary punishment of the latter, who has been languishing in detention for over three years, is a demonstrative revenge by Putin: "This is what happens to those who oppose me." It is this maneuver that the Kremlin wants to repeat by tasking the Americans with toppling President Zelensky. Furthermore, Zelensky's public flogging strikingly resembled Stalinist conclaves of denunciation of fallen communists where everyone was forced to demonstrate their zeal by condemning the unfortunate "enemy of the people" – an expression that is becoming common in the United States. Senator Lindsey Graham, who had assured Zelensky in the morning that he stood in solidarity with Ukraine, loudly demanded his resignation in the evening. This kind of behavior is reminiscent of Stalinism. The Trump regime more closely resembles Stalinism than fascism. How so? For me, the most telling criterion is the pervasive lying, an impudent lie that one is forced to adhere to if they want to advance in the administration. The lie is not there to persuade, but to humiliate the one repeating it, to make them feel their insignificance in the face of a dictator who can demand that they affirm that the 2020 election was stolen, that Ukraine is responsible for the war. There is a typically totalitarian mechanism at play. Just like the abrupt shifting of alliances, with the United States voting at the United Nations alongside Russia and North Korea. Another striking feature for those familiar with the Soviet system is the emulation in servility, the cult of personality, which takes grotesque forms. One can also note the systematic appeal to the basest passions, the cult of strength. Is there a connection between the Trump administration's stated desire to destroy the federal state and this relationship with Russia? It is said that Trump is sowing chaos in the United States. But there is a logic in this madness: most of the measures adopted are in Moscow's interest. The first decision of Kash Patel, the new head of the FBI, was to close the Washington office responsible for counter-espionage. Attorney General Pam Bondi closed investigations into Russian oligarchs and their assets. Tulsi Gabbard, chosen by Trump to lead the intelligence services, was criticized by Senator Mitt Romney who said that she "simply parrots Russian propaganda. Her deceitful lies could cost lives." On February 24, 2022, Tulsi Gabbard echoed verbatim in a tweet the Kremlin's justification for the Russian aggression against Ukraine: "This war and suffering could have been avoided if the Biden administration and NATO had simply taken into account Russia's legitimate concerns about a possible entry of Ukraine into NATO." Also noteworthy is the closure of Usaid, the American soft power tool that Moscow feared the most, partly due to the support provided by this organization to Russian and Belarusian dissidents, not to mention aid to Ukraine. Additionally, there is the sabotage of transatlantic relations, daily attacks on America's allies, promises to lift sanctions, and the dramatic abandonment of Ukraine... the list of favors to Moscow grows longer each day. Is the connection between Russia and the Trump administration now acknowledged? From the Russian side, it is. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov stated that the foreign policies of Washington and Moscow have been largely aligned since Donald Trump came to power: "The new American administration is rapidly changing all the configurations of foreign policy. This largely coincides with our vision." On the American side, it is acknowledged in a different way. The Trump administration regurgitates all the clichés of Kremlin propaganda. Trump seems to have internalized Russian fabrications. His rhetoric faithfully echoes Kremlin's allegations and the shifts in the Putinist party line. How do you compare the two classes of oligarchs: the Russians and the American tech giants? Can they have common goals? The interests of Russian oligarchs converge with those of Big Tech magnates. Putin and the major oligarchs, Russian and American, believe they are above the law. Putin's contempt for the law goes so far that he questions the very notion of the state, precisely because a state is based on a legal framework and has borders, which he dislikes. Trumpism aims to replace the state with a "power vertical" akin to Putin's, in which officials are chosen for their loyalty rather than competence. The dismantling of the state is accompanied by deregulation that benefits the major oligarchs seeking to control financial flows. This is why Kremlin strategists early on understood the services that Western libertarians could provide them. Russian foreign policy is characterized by its extraordinary persistence. This new phase in Russo-American relations is sometimes referred to as a Russian revenge for the end of the Cold War... In the KGB and GRU [the Russian military intelligence service], some have not accepted the end of the communist bloc and the collapse of the USSR, which they attributed to an American conspiracy. This core group of die-hards found a theorist in Alexander Dugin. As early as the "Yeltsin years" [president from 1991 to 1999], he brought back to the forefront the irreconcilable antagonism between Russia and the United States, resurrecting it through a geopolitical lens. Dugin proclaims that there is a total opposition between "continental" land powers such as Russia and maritime merchant powers like the United States and Great Britain. The former adopt authoritarian conservative regimes, while the latter embody liberalism. Just as Lenin believed that there could be no coexistence between socialism and capitalism – with socialism inevitably prevailing – Dugin argues that there is an existential antagonism between continental powers and maritime powers. Dugin's theories have permeated the Putin regime and have inspired the policy of undermining the United States that has been implemented over the past twenty years. The goal is to symmetrically inflict on the United States the same blows that they allegedly dealt to the USSR under Gorbachev [who led the Soviet Union from 1985 to 1991]: deprive them of their allies, unilaterally disarm them, ruin their economy, push them to the brink of civil war and dismantlement. All of which the Trump administration is currently carrying out. When Deputy Andrei Lugovoi declares on the show of propagandist Vladimir Soloviev: "Today, we have achieved total revenge, erasing our setback from the end of the Cold War," he reflects the triumphalist mindset of the Kremlin elites.
lead: The Russia specialist explains in an interview with "Le Monde" how the Russian president manages to manipulate his American counterpart, who has aligned his speech and actions with the interests of the Kremlin.

title: Andrii Sybiha, ministre des affaires étrangères ukrainien : « Rien ne doit être discuté sur l’Ukraine sans l’Ukraine »
text: Andrii Sybiha has been the Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs since September 2024. He was in Paris on Wednesday, February 12, to attend a meeting with his counterparts from the main European states: France, Germany, Poland, Italy, Spain, and the UK. He advocates for a lasting peace agreement negotiated with the participation of his country and the Europeans, in exchange for security guarantees. Were you surprised by the phone call between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin? Such a conversation had been announced several times by the American administration. President Zelensky spoke with President Trump after that call. He had already met with him in Paris, along with Emmanuel Macron [on December 7, for the reopening of Notre-Dame Cathedral]. He then had the opportunity to explain his vision for achieving a complete, just, and lasting peace and preventing Russia from aggressing against us again in a few years. It is in all of our interests to join efforts to end the war in 2025. We don't just want the fighting to stop. Nothing should be discussed about Ukraine without Ukraine. Nothing should be discussed about Europe without Europe. Are you concerned about a too hasty compromise? We want a comprehensive agreement. This is also in the interest of the United States. It should be the greatest diplomatic achievement since the end of World War II. Under President Trump's leadership, with strong commitment and unity among Europeans, we have a chance to inject new momentum into this process. But, to my knowledge, our American allies have not yet finalized their plan. Are you not afraid that Trump will rush into negotiations with Putin? We are interested in accelerating the process. Ukraine is the country most affected by a just peace. We suffer every day, as 20% of our territory is occupied. Ukraine managed to liberate half of the invaded territories in 2022, including the major city of Kherson, to restrict the capabilities of the Russian navy in the Black Sea to establish a grain corridor. But our economy is functioning. This demonstrates, after three years of war, the strength of our people and our army, even though we have not always received timely strong decisions. We have not received enough weapons. We have always lagged behind the most appropriate choices. Diplomacy now has a chance to play an active role in bringing us closer to the strategic goal of peace. Do you understand what Trump wants to do to reach an agreement with Russia? We are in contact with his administration, and we will be especially during the Munich Security Conference [from Friday, February 14 to Sunday, February 16]. President Zelensky is preparing to meet with President Trump soon. I hope to have contact soon with the Secretary of State, Marco Rubio. It is the best way to exchange our views and unify our efforts for a just peace. The U.S. Secretary of Defense, Pete Hegseth, stated in Brussels on Wednesday that Ukraine's accession to NATO was "not realistic." How do you react? I only comment on facts. At the last summit in Washington in July 2024, all our allies stated that Ukraine's path to NATO was irreversible. This perspective of accession is enshrined in our Constitution. It is in our strategic interest. Over 90% of our population supports this project. Before the war, support was around 50%, and society was divided. This is not about charity. We will contribute to transatlantic security with our over 110 brigades, war experience, and technologies. We are developing sophisticated production, which we immediately test on the battlefield. We have increased the size of our defense industry sixfold to be more self-sufficient. This is also part of the security guarantees we seek to obtain. We had a bad experience with the Budapest Memorandum [signed in December 1994, following the dismantling of the USSR, in exchange for Ukraine giving up its nuclear weapons]. The best security guarantee remains joining NATO. It is the cheapest way for the Alliance to ensure its own security. The transatlantic family needs our contribution. That is the reality. What is the status of the idea of deploying European soldiers in Ukraine in the event of a ceasefire? This is one of the security guarantees we are considering. It is important to know the details, scope, and timeline. Before that, of course, we must force Russia to negotiate. We rely on the support of our partners to stabilize the situation on the battlefield, which is very tough. It is about defending our country and preventing further advances by Russian troops. Is a just peace compatible with territorial concessions? There should be no compromise on Ukrainian territorial integrity and sovereignty. Putin wants to extend the post-Yalta reality, where he could divide the world into spheres of influence and create gray areas. It is important to understand that Ukrainian security and transatlantic security are indivisible. Are you willing to exchange your rare earth minerals for American support, as Trump demands? Ukraine is indeed very rich in these resources. We are willing to exploit these raw materials and use them with our allies, including the United States. They have the appropriate technologies. This would require additional investments. This is a form of security guarantee for the business. We also need to protect these resources, otherwise, Russia could take advantage of them along with North Korea and Iran. President Zelensky was able to discuss this with Trump during their meeting before the election. This will be one of the benefits that our allies can gain from our victory.
lead: While in Paris, the Ukrainian foreign minister reacts to the initiatives of the new American administration regarding the conflict with Russia.

title: En Italie, Matteo Salvini se veut plus trumpiste que Giorgia Meloni et déséquilibre la coalition d’extrême droite au pouvoir
text: The Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, faces a balancing act, both abroad and at home. On the international stage, the leader of the far-right executive walks a narrow path between her affinity with the Trump administration and her relationships with European partners, while in Rome, she must manage a coalition influenced by conflicting movements. On one side, Forza Italia (center-right), a party founded by Silvio Berlusconi, remains firmly committed to Italy's European integration; on the other, her turbulent ally from the League (far-right), Matteo Salvini, has engaged in a Trumpist escalation by demonstrating his alignment with Washington, driven by the current reactionary wave. Having distinguished himself as a fervent admirer of Vladimir Putin before the invasion of Ukraine, Salvini, who became Vice President of the Council and Minister of Transport under Ms. Meloni in October 2022, even had a fifteen-minute phone call on March 21 with the American Vice President, J.D. Vance, a vocal opponent of the European democratic model. "I've read everything about you," Salvini reportedly said to Vance, author of a bestseller on peripheral America, his background, and how he became a wealthy financier after attending Yale. Trump, "the only one capable of bringing peace" On March 3, Salvini also denounced a Europe that is "collapsing," "marginal," and led by "Ursula [von der Leyen], Berlin, and Paris," asserting that Italy had a "historic opportunity (…) to be central on the international stage." For Salvini, who has frequently repeated this since the American administration's shift on the Ukrainian issue, President Donald Trump is "the only leader capable of finally bringing peace, unlike Europe." According to him, the American billionaire had done "more in two months than others in three years." Another sign of allegiance, Matteo Salvini supports Italy's use of systems from SpaceX, Elon Musk's company, the oligarch and advisor to Donald Trump, for Italian government and military communications. In Rome, the topic has been controversial since December 2024 following the publication of information on negotiations between Rome and the American company. While Giorgia Meloni has established excellent relations with Mr. Musk, the prospect of Italy adopting these technologies raises questions regarding sovereignty and challenges the relevance of European projects, albeit in their early stages, concerning satellite constellations. The issue has become political: The positioning of representatives of the Italian political world on the issue of the Starlink satellite constellation is now dependent on their stance towards the billionaire's person and actions, the soul and model of the new reactionary international. Endorsement of European rearmament efforts While expressing their alignment with the views of the American administration and opposing any inclination to consider Europe's security outside the strict transatlantic framework, the president of the council holds more nuanced positions. Having also welcomed Mr. Vance's Munich speech on February 14, she supports Donald Trump's efforts on Ukraine; however, she has given her endorsement to European rearmament efforts while displaying extreme caution regarding their implementation. Matteo Salvini, who had previously appeared on Moscow's Red Square wearing a t-shirt with Vladimir Putin's image in 2022, rejects them on principle. The divergences that appear between the parties of the majority dominated by the far right fuel political chronicles, with the vice president of the council and minister of foreign affairs, Antonio Tajani (Forza Italia), often finding himself at odds with Mr. Salvini, as seen in the recent unorthodox phone call with J.D. Vance. On Wednesday, on the eve of the Paris summit, Giorgia Meloni, her two allies, and the defense minister, Guido Crosetto, founder of Fratelli d'Italia, the president of the council's party, met in hopes of ending these dissensions. Tensions had peaked when the League's number two, Claudio Durigon, described Mr. Tajani as "in difficulty" in an interview with La Repubblica published on Sunday, offering his party's help to engage with the Trump administration, as if he enjoyed a privileged position in this regard. Since Giorgia Meloni took office in October 2022, international imbalances have never weighed so heavily on Italian politics. The divisions on the right are reflected in the opposition, between supporters and opponents of European rearmament, with a cross-cutting pacifist current also shaping public opinion. Matteo Salvini relies on this, while trying to position himself as the closest to the Trump administration within the ruling coalition, even conducting his own foreign policy, whereas Giorgia Meloni's approach seems less clear.
lead: Matteo Salvini's repeated overtures to the Trump administration are challenging the delicate balance that the Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, is trying to maintain between Europe and the United States.

title: Le mois de mars en dessins : paix lointaine en Ukraine, réarmement européen, talibans, pollution marine…
text: This month, the news was once again dominated by the American president, Donald Trump, and the war in Ukraine. The fate of Afghan women, the prison sentence imposed on the Franco-Algerian writer Boualem Sansal, and marine pollution also marked the month of March. In eleven drawings, "La Matinale" looks back on these highlights. Spectacular clash between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky Drawing published on the cover of "Le Monde" dated March 5, 2025, signed by the French cartoonist Chappatte. CHAPPATTE The meeting between Donald Trump, his vice president, J. D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28, in the Oval Office of the White House, turned into an altercation, calling into question American support for Kiev. The President of the United States notably demanded, raising his voice, that his Ukrainian counterpart show "gratitude." The confrontation began with J. D. Vance, who accused Mr. Zelensky, who had come seeking Washington's support after three years of war against Russia, of "disrespecting" Americans. Ukraine pushed to accept a ceasefire... Drawing published on the cover of "Le Monde" dated March 18, 2025, signed by the Swiss cartoonist Herrmann. HERRMANN Ukraine accepted, on Tuesday, March 11, a U.S. proposal for a thirty-day ceasefire with Russia, following an American-Ukrainian meeting in Saudi Arabia. In return, Washington announced the "immediate" lifting of the suspension of military aid to Ukraine and the sharing of intelligence with Kiev. Moscow, absent from the talks in Jeddah, simply stated that it "did not rule out contacts" with Washington. ...but Trump also fails to convince Vladimir Putin Drawing published on the cover of "Le Monde" dated March 25, 2025, signed by the French cartoonist Chappatte. CHAPPATTE The highly anticipated phone call between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin on Tuesday, March 18, produced very limited results. The ceasefire in Ukraine discussed by the American and Russian presidents is limited to energy infrastructure, which is less crucial with the end of winter. While Kiev, under American pressure, had accepted the principle of a complete cessation of hostilities, Moscow refused. Europe adopts a rearmament plan Drawing published on the cover of "Le Monde" dated March 11, 2025, signed by the Algerian cartoonist Dilem. DILEM During an extraordinary European Council meeting on Thursday, March 6, the Twenty-Seven adopted the "Rearming Europe" plan, providing for a budget of 800 billion euros; a strengthening of their spending capacities that should also benefit Ukraine. However, while some argue for "strategic autonomy," and while French President Emmanuel Macron has revived discussions on expanding France's nuclear umbrella to other European countries, many still rely on American protection. A "gender apartheid" in Afghanistan Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 19, 2025, signed by Tanzanian cartoonist Meddy. MEDDY They can no longer go out alone, speak out loud, study, work... The situation of Afghan women, the main victims of the reign of terror imposed by the Taliban in August 2021, is only getting worse. Some laws even directly endanger their health and lives, such as the ban on being treated by men. This dramatic situation has been described as a "gender apartheid" by the United Nations. Boualem Sansal sentenced to five years in prison in Algeria Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 27, 2025, signed by Malagasy cartoonist Pov. POV The 80-year-old French-Algerian writer, arrested on November 16, 2024 in Algeria and placed in provisional detention, was sentenced on Thursday, March 27, to a five-year prison term. Suffering from prostate cancer, Boualem Sansal was charged with endangering state security, territorial integrity, and institutional stability for statements regarding the belonging of Western Algeria to Morocco. This legal case has fueled diplomatic tensions between Algeria and France for several months. The fall of Tesla in the stock market Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 4, 2025, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIN The Trump effect on the stock market is fading, and Elon Musk is the first victim. By the end of February, Tesla's valuation had already fallen by 38% compared to its peak of $488 reached in December 2024. The stock had surged due to the close relationship between Trump and Musk, with traders hoping that the car company would quickly obtain approvals to operate its driverless vehicles. This has not happened yet, while Tesla's sales are declining, partly due to Musk's extremism damaging the brand's image. The trade war is expected to fuel inflation Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 7, 2025, signed by Taiwanese cartoonist Stellina. STELLINA As the new high tariffs imposed by Donald Trump - against Canada, China, Mexico, and the European Union - come into effect, most economists and analysts expect prices to start rising in the American domestic market, to the detriment of household purchasing power. European countries less concerned about ship pollution Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 13, 2025, signed by Tanzanian cartoonist Meddy. MEDDY Oil spills in the water, lost containers and fishing gear at sea... While the state of the marine world is severely degraded, European countries still have much to do in terms of pollution from ships, as confirmed by a report from the European Court of Auditors released on Tuesday, March 4. The identification and sanctioning of polluters remain important areas for improvement. Donald Trump is targeting the Department of Education. Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated March 26, 2025, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIM Donald Trump signed a presidential decree on Thursday, March 20, organizing the sabotage - but not the disappearance - of the Department of Education. Its complete elimination would indeed require a supermajority (60 out of 100 votes) in the Senate. For now, the White House has begun to weaken this administration, with half of its staff already eliminated through departure plans, by transferring a large part of its responsibilities to the states.
lead: Once a month, "La Matinale" offers you a review of the news with illustrations signed by members of the international collective of cartoonists Cartooning for Peace and published on the front page of "Le Monde".

title: Après les attaques virulentes de Donald Trump contre Volodymyr Zelensky, les Ukrainiens sous le choc
text: It only took one week for everything to change. A week during which the efforts of American diplomacy to get closer to Russia and the thunderous statements of President Donald Trump put Ukraine in a state of shock. The mechanism suddenly started, between the increasing American pressures, bitter statements from Ukrainian authorities, and finally, on Wednesday, February 19, a outburst from Donald Trump, president of the most crucial ally for Kiev, accusing the Ukrainian head of state of being a "dictator without election." This new development completely overshadowed the visit, awaited for weeks, of General Keith Kellogg, the U.S. special envoy for Ukraine and Russia, who arrived Wednesday morning at the Kiev train station. He is expected to meet with the Ukrainian president on Thursday. "It is crucial for us that this meeting and our work with America in general be constructive," said Volodymyr Zelensky on Wednesday, without mentioning Donald Trump. "With America and Europe, peace can be safer and that is our goal." The tension between the Ukrainian and American heads of state escalated a week after a first confirmed call between Donald Trump and his Russian counterpart. Since then, not a day has passed without a statement or a meeting hinting at the possibility of an agreement unfavorable to Kiev. After spending months trying to please Donald Trump, President Volodymyr Zelensky simply made the mistake of defending the interests of his country. First, by announcing that Ukraine would not accept a peace agreement resulting from discussions to which it had not been invited. Then, by refusing to sign a preliminary contract delivering Ukrainian natural resources to the United States. Terribly damaging for Kiev and devoid of security guarantees, it was presented by Donald Trump as a "repayment" of the financial aid granted to Ukraine by the United States. An acrid and violent statement This access to certain essential minerals, notably for new technologies, had been highlighted by the Zelensky administration a few months earlier, in the hope of convincing Donald Trump to support Ukraine and obtain security guarantees, but without specifying a legal framework. Wednesday morning, during a press conference, the Ukrainian president said he was ready for a new "serious" agreement, including security guarantees. "I defend Ukraine, I cannot sell our country. That's it," he said. But the Ukrainian president's refusal to sign provoked the fury of the American president: "Think about it, a moderately successful actor, Volodymyr Zelensky, convinced the United States of America to spend 350 billion dollars [approximately 335 billion euros] to engage in a war that could not be won, that should never have started, but a war that he will never be able to resolve without the United States and 'TRUMP'," he said in a virulent statement filled with false information, starting with the amount of American aid, actually estimated at 114.2 billion dollars since 2022 by the Kiel Institute for the World Economy (IfW). "A dictator without elections, Zelensky would do well to react quickly, otherwise he will no longer have a country," continued the American president. This statement adds to the one from the day before, when he falsely claimed that the Ukrainian leader was only at "4%" popularity in the polls. Flags of the European Union, Ukraine, and the United States in Kiev on February 18, 2025. RAFAEL YAGHOBZADEH FOR 'LE MONDE' These statements about the alleged illegitimacy of Volodymyr Zelensky, combined with the beginning of a rapprochement between Russia and the United States, have imposed on the country the feeling that there will be a before and an after, giving the impression of entering an even more uncertain period just a few days before the 3rd anniversary of the invasion of Ukraine by Russia on February 24, 2022. Ukrainians now fear the worst, as the dependence on Washington in terms of military support and intelligence sharing is considered crucial for the army. "We will resist," simply commented the head of Ukrainian military intelligence service (HUR), Kyrylo Boudanov, on Telegram. "No one can force Ukraine to give up. We will defend our right to exist," said the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Andrii Sybiha, on the X social network. The attack by the White House tenant against his counterpart in Kiev has also sparked outrage among part of the population. While the mandate of the head of state, supposed to end in May 2024, has indeed been indefinitely extended and criticisms of him are becoming increasingly harsh within the country, few Ukrainians question his legitimacy. The vast majority accepts the postponement of elections until the end of the war given the current stakes, that of the survival of the nation, especially in a country divided by a front line, with millions of displaced people. Russian talking points "Only Ukraine will decide when and how to hold elections and whether or not to re-elect Zelensky. All other countries can either respectfully accept it or go to hell," reacted Max Kolesnikov, a veteran of the armed forces, on Facebook. "I have only one explanation for Donald Trump's statements," commented Ukrainian journalist Yaroslava Gres with irony." "Trump wants the Ukrainians to stop fighting amongst themselves, to feel the unity of February 2022 again, and for Zelensky to win the elections with 73%. There has been incredible progress on these three points." The recent events have seriously tarnished the image that Ukrainians had of the Trump administration. Just after his election in November 2024, many were counting on the supposedly unpredictable nature of the Republican, hoping that frustration caused by Vladimir Putin would push him to strengthen his support for Ukraine. However, his recent statements, in line with Russian talking points, have caused a shock. Since the beginning of the week, experts and public figures have been appearing on television and in the media, trying to decipher the latest news with headlines that were unimaginable just a week ago: "Trump has handed Europe to Putin"; "Trump is falling into the Russian trap"; "New Trump slogan: 'Make Russia Great Again'?" "Putin doesn't need to go to the United States. He is already present in the White House," commented the highly respected journalist Sergiy Sydorenko, editor-in-chief of European Truth. "We are all a bit disturbed," euphemized political scientist Oleksiy Haran. "We can see that Trump wants to quickly end the war. But why is Trump making such concessions even before the start of negotiations? We understand that we will not regain our 1991 borders or join NATO for the time being. But isn't negotiating about keeping all the cards on your side to adjust later? Trump, on the other hand, excludes everything, right away, publicly." On Wednesday evening, the American president declared that the Russians "hold the cards." While there is palpable concern in the statements of everyone involved, it is not felt in the streets of Kiev and more broadly in the country, which is being hit day and night by Russian missiles and drones. On the front line as well, soldiers continue to fight tirelessly. It has now been three years that the daily life of Ukrainians has been punctuated by bombings, funerals, infrastructure destruction, nuclear threats... The fear of a forced peace On Independence Square in Kiev, bustling with afternoon traffic, as yet another air raid siren sounded, several passersby confessed on Wednesday that they had not been following the latest news from Washington. Like this young soldier on convalescence, accompanied by his wife, who awkwardly admits to not having an opinion on Donald Trump. "Sorry, I have to go find some guys," he finally says slightly annoyed, gesturing towards the nearby war memorial, adorned with thousands of flags in tribute to each fallen soldier. The same goes for Maria, 35 years old, who arrived from the city of Dnipro so that her young son, who has a heart condition, could receive treatment in a hospital in the capital. "Everyone I know says that Trump will not do anything good for Ukraine," she finally says. She adds that in her opinion, "the war will continue for another year or two." On the sidewalk in front of a music conservatory where groups of students are smoking cigarettes, only one teenager, Kyrylo, shares his opinion: "The United States want our metals, but they don't give anything in return," he timidly says, referring to the pre-contract proposed by Donald Trump on natural resources. "They are our partners, even today, because they support us with their weapons, but everything can change for the worse," he continues. Not far away, Iryna, a cashier in a supermarket in her fifties, waits for the reopening of the doors of the shopping center where she works, closed during the alert. Does she think the war will stop? "Anything is possible, but we don't know what to do after all these deaths, all these lost territories... Everyone wants the war to end. But, on the other hand, where are the guarantees that Russia will stop?" A target of the President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin, in a shooting range in Kiev, on February 17, 2025. RAFAEL YAGHOBZADEH FOR "LE MONDE" Beyond Donald Trump's boastful statements, the rapprochement between Americans and Russians mainly raises fears of a forced peace, without security guarantees for the country. Ukrainians, like former Foreign Minister Dmytro Kuleba, in an interview with the BBC, believe that Moscow's objective is not just to take over a few regions: "[Putin] did not come for a simple piece of land, he came for Ukraine itself." The announcement made on Tuesday by the Kremlin spokesperson that Vladimir Putin would be ready to negotiate directly with Volodymyr Zelensky makes Valeriy Pekar, a former businessman and university teacher, shrug. "It doesn't mean anything," he says. "Why would the Russians stop fighting when they are in a position of strength?" "Putin wants to dismember Ukraine. And his objective has not changed," also states Vitaliy Deynega, former Deputy Minister of Defense. "It is likely that Putin and Trump will meet and agree on something. But it will never be more than a new Minsk," he adds, referring to the Minsk agreements signed in 2014 and 2015, supposed to end the conflict in the Donbass, but which were violated multiple times. Like many Ukrainians, Vitaliy Deynega mentions the importance of the reaction of European countries. "I have the impression that they are motivated enough to increase their support for Ukraine and their military budgets," he believes. But I don't think this support will be enough to cover all of the American aid.
lead: The President of the United States accused Kiev of being the source of the war and called his Ukrainian counterpart a "dictator." This escalation has stunned both the population and the authorities, further plunging the country into uncertainty.

title: Giorgia Meloni, prise dans ses contradictions sur la crise de la relation transatlantique, bénéficie des divisions de l’opposition
text: Caught in a delicate position on European defense and the transatlantic crisis, the Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, escaped through a controversial statement on Wednesday, March 19, attacking a founding text of anti-fascism and the European idea. While presenting to the Chamber of Deputies the position Italy will defend at a European Council dominated by defense issues on Thursday, the far-right executive leader harshly criticized the supposed support of the opposition for the "Ventotene Manifesto" for European unity. "I don't know if this is your vision of Europe, but it certainly isn't mine," Giorgia Meloni concluded her speech as chaotic outcries came from the left benches before a suspension of the session. The executive leader was referring to a text written in 1941 by the anti-fascists Ernesto Rossi and Altiero Spinelli while they were under house arrest by the Mussolini regime on the small island that gave its name to the manifesto. Previously, the Prime Minister had quoted passages out of context, implying that the European unity advocated in Ventotene and presented as a reference by her opponents had an authoritarian content and was hostile to private property. By accusing her adversaries of aligning themselves with dogmatic and liberty-restricting Europeanism, she omitted to mention the parts of the manifesto dedicated to criticizing totalitarianism, doctrinaire communism, economic oligarchies, and racism. These were all issues that, according to her, should be banned from a united Europe with a common armed force that its authors hoped would emerge after the war and the defeat of fascist and Nazi regimes. One of the two authors, Altiero Spinelli (1907-1986), who was a member of the European Commission from 1970 to 1976, is still considered one of the great European figures offered by Italy to the continent, although his resolutely federalist vision was sidelined. "Giorgia Meloni's statements represent the new guise of Euroscepticism: saying that one is for a Europe that is not that of the founders," accused her fellow countryman, the MEP (Renew Europe) Sandro Gozi. The Prime Minister thus chose to discredit two of the early supporters of European unity at the end of an intervention dedicated precisely to Europe, which echoed the substance of her speech the day before in the Senate. She had then endeavored to defend, on a tightrope, her positioning in an international power field disrupted by the arrival of the American President, Donald Trump. Rejecting any decoupling with Washington First, Giorgia Meloni reiterated her support for Ukraine, albeit in a more discreet manner since the deterioration of relations between Kiev and Washington. She also defended in a very nuanced way her support for the European Commission's €800 billion rearmament plan, describing it as "grandiose compared to the nature of what is proposed." Her solution for Ukraine's security also involves a hypothetical extension of Article 5 of the North Atlantic Treaty without integrating Kiev into the Atlantic Alliance. This provision states that an attack on one of the signatories of the treaty should be considered as targeting all others. However, the Italian leader once again praised the efforts of the Trump administration for peace in Ukraine, rejecting any tendency towards transatlantic decoupling and repeating her criticisms of the possible deployment of European troops in the country. While emphasizing the necessary unity of the West. Giorgia Meloni, the only European leader invited to the inauguration of the American president on January 20, declared her adherence to the speech given in Munich by Vice President J.D. Vance, denounced as a direct attack on the European democratic model. The council president also cultivates her friendship with the oligarch Elon Musk, a key figure in Trumpism in Washington, with whom she shares some ideas. As she reiterated on Wednesday, she still believes in her role as a bridge between the two sides of the Atlantic, refraining from any criticism of Washington, whether on the plans for the annexation of Greenland or on tariffs. However, this positioning has not yet allowed her to be highlighted by Donald Trump, despite the promising start of their relationship. "Giorgia Meloni continues to see Donald Trump as a better ally than anyone in Europe. She wants to maintain her privileged relationship with him and believes that over time other countries will rally to her line, and above all, she wants to give reassurances to her League allies who try to be more Trumpist than her," summarizes Lorenzo Castellani, a political science researcher at Luiss University in Rome. The council's vice president, Matteo Salvini, leader of the League, an extreme right-wing party and a pillar of the coalition, and his troops maintain a constant escalation in favor of Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, portrayed as working alone for peace against a Europe they consider bellicose and firmly reject rearmament. His stance is close to that of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, whom he met on Wednesday in Brussels along with the French representatives of the National Rally, Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella. In Italy, a pacifist rhetoric rooted in public opinion. However, Matteo Salvini's stance contrasts with the more pro-European positions of Antonio Tajani, the leader of another party in the ruling coalition, Forza Italia (center-right). Tajani aims to stay close to the line of the European People's Party, currently dominated by the Christian Democratic Union (CDU) of Friedrich Merz, the likely future German chancellor, who advocates for the "independence" of the European Union (EU) from the United States. Tajani does align, however, with some of the opposition's positions, which are against European rearmament, drawing from a deeply ingrained pacifist rhetoric in public opinion that sees war as a scourge that the production of weapons would only perpetuate at the expense of public services. This cross-cutting peace party includes former anti-establishment populists from the Five Star Movement and a faction of the Democratic Party (PD, center-left), which includes the PD's own general secretary, Elly Schlein. Schlein has opposed the EU's rearmament plan, in disagreement with key figures in the movement such as former prime ministers Enrico Letta and Paolo Gentiloni. On March 12, ten out of the 21 PD members of the European Parliament abstained from voting on the Commission's defense policy, distancing themselves from the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament group. "A large part of Italians, especially on the left, cannot see Russia as a strategic threat and view the situation in Ukraine through the lens of pacifist idealism," explains PD deputy Lia Quartapelle. The international situation is therefore dividing the opposition. In the absence of a united front from opponents, the majority can still afford to show its own divergences.
lead: The President of the Italian Council is providing measured support to Ukraine and the European rearmament plan while also expressing support for the Trump administration. In her country, she must deal with a pacifist movement that affects different parties, including those in her coalition.

title: Volodymyr Zelensky consolé à Londres, c’est peut-être un détail pour vous…
text: London embraces history in motion, literally. In this image, taken in London on Sunday, March 2nd, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky strides confidently towards Lancaster House, the seat of British diplomacy and a key location for many historic negotiations. He is awaited by all European leaders, gathered for a summit of utmost importance. Together, they will heal the wounds of the Ukrainian president, who was mistreated by Donald Trump and his cronies at the White House, and, incidentally, contemplate a strategy for a new world. Solidarity mode For this London rendezvous, Volodymyr Zelensky did not pull out all the stops. No, as has been his habit since the invasion of Ukraine by Russia, he wore his "tactical" pants and sweatshirt, showing his real involvement on the ground with his troops. While in terms of style research, this may not match the siren suit, the one-piece tailored suit fabric worn by Winston Churchill during World War II, in terms of communication, it is just as effective. The last poilus Facing the Ukrainian president, the Grenadier Guards, members of the oldest of the five infantry regiments of the sovereign's household guard, stand at attention. On their heads, the traditional and spectacular bearskin hat. This hat first appeared in Napoleon I's Imperial Guard before spreading worldwide. Today, fourteen armies still wear the bearskin hat. It is worth noting that while the Swedes and Italians now wear models made of synthetic fur, other countries have not given up real fur. Winter collection In addition to their bearskin hats, the Grenadier Guards are dressed in their traditional winter coats, made of a gray atoll-colored wool, adorned with brass buttons and fastened with a white parade belt displaying their regiment number. Do you find it chic? Enjoy it: on the last Sunday of March, according to an unchanging calendar that disregards actual temperatures, the Grenadier Guards will remove their winter coats to don their summer wardrobe and iconic red tunics. The coats will not be worn again until the last Sunday of October. Gallant collar Also noteworthy is the uniqueness of the coat's collar, particularly round, high, and enveloping. What is it called? In French, this collar is referred to as the mysterious "chevalière collar." Similar to a shirt collar, and unlike a blazer collar, the chevalière collar is designed to protect the neck and upper chest. In fact, this collar was particularly popular on moleskin jackets worn by French workers precisely because it reduced the risk of injuries for laborers carrying heavy loads on their shoulders.
lead: but not for Marc Beaugé. After the ambush suffered in Washington, the Ukrainian president went to the temple of British diplomacy on March 2, where he received comfort from European heads of state.

title: Les Etats-Unis de Donald Trump engagent un dialogue cordial avec la Russie, au détriment de l’Ukraine
text: Ukraine has sacrificed a lot since the Russian invasion of February 2022 to defend its sovereignty and the right to choose its own future. The United States, which has long been its main supporter in this struggle, now treats it as a secondary player in the conflict resolution, under the authority of their president, Donald Trump. This is the drama that has emerged in the background during the resumption of diplomatic exchanges between the United States and Russia on Tuesday, February 18. Held in a palace in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, this meeting between two high-level delegations followed a phone call between Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin on February 12. It seals the acceleration of the timeline and a reconfiguration entirely favorable to Moscow. On the American side, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, National Security Advisor Mike Waltz, and President's Special Envoy Steve Witkoff participated. A common trait among these men is the lack of any experience in dealing with Russian officials. Facing them, however, were two men experienced in bilateral negotiations: Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov and Vladimir Putin's diplomatic advisor Yuri Ushakov. Along with them was another close associate of the Russian president: Kirill Dmitriev, who heads the RDIF sovereign fund. The meeting confirms the shared willingness to restore traditional diplomatic relations, suspended since February 2022. The United States and Russia commit to restoring all missions in their embassies. Negotiating teams will be formed to address the conditions for a ceasefire in Ukraine, with conflict resolution being the next step. It is unclear in what capacity the authorities in Kiev will be involved. Washington also hints at the possibility of productive cooperation with Russia on geopolitical matters - such as disarmament and security in Europe - and especially on economic investments. The gradual lifting of American sanctions is intended to serve as a major incentive to push Moscow towards peace, in line with the White House's thinking. A reversal of American logic. However, the Kremlin knows that opening up Russian oil and gas fields to American investments would be a welcomed move in the Trumpian language. "Talking about trade and investments seems very premature at this stage," says David Kramer, former Deputy Secretary of State (2008-2009). "Instead, we should talk about the $300 billion that Russia has stupidly left in Western financial institutions and use it to help rebuild and rehabilitate Ukraine." "Putin wants peace," Donald Trump has been repeating for weeks. This willingness to give credit to the good intentions and sincerity of his Russian counterpart is reminiscent of another. Twenty-four years later, there is a strange echo of a statement made by George W. Bush, who claimed to have "gotten a sense of his soul [that of Vladimir Putin]" by looking into the Russian president's eyes. Donald Trump goes far beyond naivety. Speaking on Tuesday from his residence in Mar-a-Lago, Florida, he confirmed a reversal of American logic, making the victim - Ukraine - responsible for its own misfortunes, and the aggressor - Russia - a respectable interlocutor whose demands must be heard and even satisfied. The Russians "want to put an end to the savage barbarism" in Ukraine, even dared to say the American president, who never mentions international law. When asked about the feelings of the Ukrainians, in the absence of an invitation to Riyadh, Donald Trump retorted: "You should have ended [the war] three years ago - you should never have started it. You should have reached an agreement." The American president claimed that the money given to Ukraine was not rigorously accounted for. He also once again called for elections in the country, falsely claiming that "Zelensky is at 4%" in the polls. Solitary maneuvers of the American administration It is difficult to predict the outcome of the negotiations, but at this stage, victory is already clear for Moscow. Russia is rehabilitated by this American revisionism that many members of Congress disapprove of. In Riyadh, participants were cautious in their assessment of this moment. In a statement, the State Department reminded on Tuesday that "a phone call followed by a meeting is not enough to establish lasting peace. We must act, and today, we have taken an important step forward." When Europeans seek a reassuring glimmer in the solitary maneuvers of the American administration, they turn to Secretary of State Marco Rubio. But his influence seems marginal in Donald Trump's personal diplomacy, in a short-circuit where his special envoy, Steve Witkoff - already dispatched to the Middle East - plays a key role. In an interview with CNN and the Associated Press, Marco Rubio defended the idea of a peace agreement "acceptable to all parties involved." He also assured that, by necessity, European voices would be heard in the process, "because they have also imposed sanctions" against Russia. Allies "are consulted almost literally every day," said National Security Advisor Mike Waltz, while acknowledging that "President Trump is determined to move forward very quickly," as if one should expect a meeting between the two heads of state on a tight schedule. In June 2021, Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin met in Geneva, eight months before the start of the war. Focused on the rivalry with China, the Democratic president wanted to then secure the bilateral relationship with Moscow to make it more predictable. This serious and uneventful meeting was intended, from the American side, to contrast with the one between the Russian president and Donald Trump in Helsinki in 2018. The two men had a long exchange with no witnesses other than the interpreter, before the billionaire criticized the media and American intelligence services regarding their handling of Russian interference in the 2016 presidential campaign. A recognition of Russian influence zone. Of very different nature, these two meetings in 2018 and 2021 offered the Kremlin what it has always demanded: a status as an indispensable power, guaranteed notably by its nuclear arsenal. The very act of negotiating in the old-fashioned way, on a range of security issues, as in Soviet times, is a revenge. For Moscow, a statement made by Barack Obama in 2014 remained unforgivable. He had described Russia as a "regional power that threatens some of its immediate neighbors, not by strength but by weakness." What Donald Trump is proposing today is a recognition of the Russian sphere of influence and a historical reparation of this snub, as if the massive war crimes committed by the aggressor since 2022 were mere peripheral circumstances. Publicly, Donald Trump has already ruled out any deployment of American forces in Ukraine and any entry of this country into NATO. Insufficient, clarified Russian Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova on Tuesday. According to her, the Atlantic Alliance should "renounce the promises made in Bucharest in 2008." At that time, the principle of Ukraine and Georgia's membership - without a timetable or roadmap - was approved. Today, there is talk of a possible major withdrawal of American forces deployed on the European continent. "Russia wants to return to the situation of 1999 in terms of American troops," notes David Kramer, which would actually mean an absence of American soldiers in any country that was not a NATO member before that date [such as Poland, the Czech Republic, and Hungary]. This is simply unacceptable. Such a withdrawal could weaken the three Baltic countries. But it is the entire European continent that seems subject to the desire for an American-Russian reset, due to lack of unity, clarity, and foresight.
lead: Moscow emerged as the clear winner from the initial talks that took place on Tuesday in Riyadh. Washington hinted at the possibility of productive cooperation in geopolitics and, most importantly, economic investments.

title: La Turquie, partenaire autrefois « encombrant », est devenue incontournable pour une Union européenne fragilisée
text: On February 17, President Emmanuel Macron gathered at the Elysée Palace seven European leaders, the heads of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), and community officials to reaffirm their support for Ukraine. Several European Union (EU) officials had expressed their frustration at not being invited. However, one country was notably absent: Turkey, the second military power in NATO. It took a spectacular altercation in front of the cameras eleven days later, on February 28, at the White House between the American president, his vice president, J.D. Vance, and the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, for things to change. Turkey's absence from Paris, immediately labeled a "strategic miscalculation" by Middle East specialist at the American think tank Hudson Institute, Zineb Riboua, was compensated for in London two days later, on March 2, by British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Invited to this crisis summit among about fifteen leaders, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan did not say much after the meeting, like the other participants, but he emphasized the importance of his country's participation in the new security architecture being discussed for Europe. And that is already a lot. The day before, he had a phone call with his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov. After years of distancing from Brussels, increasingly visible diplomatic tensions with Paris and Berlin, Ankara, which was an enthusiastic EU candidate just a quarter of a century ago, now embodies a country with multiple and complex diplomatic facets. Considered by its peers at best as "troublesome," at worst as "disruptive" and "unreliable" within the Atlantic Alliance, Turkey is now unavoidable, even essential, for a Europe more than ever weakened by Washington's changing stance. Displaying a cordial understanding with Putin. Why? The country led by Recep Tayyip Erdogan for twenty-two years is first and foremost one of the few regional powers capable of both cooperating with Russia and limiting its influence, as it has shown in Syria. Before the fall of Bashar al-Assad, Moscow defined the parameters and contours of Turkish influence in northern Syria. Today, it is the other way around. This seriously encourages EU countries to establish mechanisms to involve Turkish leaders more frequently. But that's not all, of course. The day after the meeting at the Elysée, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Sergey Lavrov met in Riyadh to start a "normalization" of their relations and discuss Ukraine. At the same time, Volodymyr Zelensky visited Ankara to meet with the Turkish head of state. By hosting the Ukrainian president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan sent a clear message that Turkey remained a key player in shaping the future of Ukraine. President Zelensky himself reinforced this idea by declaring that "the European Union, Turkey, and the United Kingdom should participate in discussions and the development of necessary security guarantees with America regarding the fate of our region." Despite the friendly relationship displayed with his "friend" Putin, Mr. Erdogan remains one of Ukraine's strongest supporters, firmly committed to defending Kiev's territorial integrity and countering Moscow's ambitions. Turkey cannot help but see Russia's aggressive expansionism in the Black Sea region as a threat to its geopolitical and energy security. The encroachment around the Black Sea, the annexation of Abkhazia in 2008, the annexation of Crimea in 2014, and the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 are all perceived in Ankara as destabilizing the region. Not to mention the symbolic significance of Crimea, the mythical homeland of the Turkic-speaking Tatars, a former Ottoman protectorate. Strategic position It is worth noting that, unlike many European nations that hesitated to support Kiev in the early days of the Russian invasion, Ankara acted decisively. Turkey invoked the Montreux Convention, closing the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits to Russian warships – a measure that, although presented as neutral, significantly limited Moscow's ability to strengthen its Black Sea fleet. Supporting Ukraine effectively limits Russian expansion in the region and strengthens Turkey's strategic position. From Ankara's perspective, a resilient and sovereign Ukraine is a crucial barrier to Russian dominance in the region. On the battlefield, Turkey provided Kiev with one of its most effective weapon systems: the Bayraktar TB2 drone, which had also proven decisive in Libya, where Ankara exposed Russian vulnerabilities. These drones helped Ukraine destroy enemy armored columns and ships, tipping the balance in key battles. By arming Ukraine on one hand and engaging with Russia on the other without applying Western sanctions against Moscow, Turkey is walking a fine line. This is not the first time. It is up to the Europeans to accept this Turkish moment. Recep Tayyip Erdogan has not ruled out contributing to a peacekeeping force in Ukraine – in the event of a ceasefire. The country has no shortage of manpower or technological expertise on the battlefield, whether in the air or on the ground. "Contradictory power," according to the formula of European law researcher Alexis Coskun, Turkey would have no trouble flexing its muscles in the midst of the continent's strategic awakening.
lead: By arming Ukraine with one hand and negotiating with Russia with the other, without applying Western sanctions against Moscow, Ankara is playing a delicate balancing act.

title: A Séries Mania, François Hollande décrypte les présidents de fiction
text: In forty-five minutes flat and in front of a captivated audience, François Hollande reminded those who needed it, on Sunday, March 23, of his undeniable quick wit, his mastery of meaningful silences, and his unquestionable sense of humor. Invited as part of the Séries Mania festival, taking place in Lille until Friday, March 28, the former president spoke to journalist Sonia Devillers about his relationship with the portrayal of presidents in TV series. The conference, punctuated by brief but well-chosen TV show clips, provided the socialist politician with an opportunity to reflect, through the lens of fiction, on his own legacy and to gauge how much the times had changed between the end of his presidency and today. The France Inter journalist playfully began the conference by going through the "mandatory scenes" in fiction when depicting a sitting president. The passing of nuclear codes from one president to their successor prompted François Hollande to point out that the "red button," jokingly referred to by Sonia Devillers as "the fictional place, since no one has ever seen it," does not actually exist. This segment, illustrated by scenes from the series Designated Survivor, allowed the former president to emphasize that foreign policy often remains neglected in electoral campaigns. Another recurring scene in TV series (here exemplified by the humorous handshake between Jean-Pascal Zadi and Alain Chabat in En place) is the transfer of power on the steps of the Elysée Palace and its derivative, the anticipation of a late-arriving guest. "A trial," noted François Hollande, sparking laughter in the audience. The back of limousines where serious matters are discussed, the presence of the tabloid press, and the ever-present threat of embarrassing revelations – the former president smiled when his interviewer mentioned the scooter and croissant incident – the corridors where the president Bartlet's "walk and talk" scenes from The West Wing were filmed ("it's very American to think that decisions are made in hallways...," he quipped), the spokesperson's press conference... All these moments fueled a discussion in which the former president, who admitted to not having seen much more than Aaron Sorkin's series in his life, reflected on his own experience. Praise for democracy The conversation took on a more serious tone when Sonia Devillers discussed the portrayal, already outdated in some aspects, of populists and autocrats in TV series, from Boris Johnson (This England) to Donald Trump (The Comey Rule). After pointing out that these individuals are "always styled in a certain way," François Hollande highlighted the tendency of dictators to present themselves as larger-than-life characters, whose reality now far exceeds their on-screen representation. The room fell silent when it came time to discuss the very particular position of Volodymyr Zelensky, a former actor known for portraying the Ukrainian president in a satirical series, who was elected to lead a country that entered into war shortly after. François Hollande also noted that series often depict the laborious work of Parliament, and in that sense, they are a form of praise for democracy – "they can show everything, even if it's to mock or denounce," he observed. "I would like to know how things are at the Kremlin, in Xi Jinping's palace, in North Korea...," he added, in a discreet yet powerful appeal for the preservation of our institutions.
lead: In front of the audience at the Lille festival, the former head of state discussed with journalist Sonia Devillers about his counterparts on screen, from President Bartlet in "The West Wing" to the one played by Jean-Pascal Zadi in "En place".

title: Derrière les récentes insultes de Donald Trump, le refus de Volodymyr Zelensky de céder l’exploitation de minerais en Ukraine
text: The scene, as reported by a well-informed source, is of rare symbolic violence. It depicts the shift caused by Donald Trump's return to the White House. Arriving in Kyiv by train, the U.S. Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, met with the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, on February 12th. The guest came with a dictate, a brief document in hand. Either his host signed on the spot an agreement on Ukrainian strategic minerals - in other words, rare earth minerals - agreeing to give half to the United States, or Washington, according to Scott Bessent, would end its economic and military support to Ukraine, ongoing for three years. These highly coveted rare earth minerals are rich in lithium for phone batteries, uranium for nuclear power plants, graphite for electric car batteries, and titanium, valued in the aerospace sector. In addition, there is oil and gas. The potential for exploiting these resources is very theoretical at this stage, due to security and industrial capacity reasons. However, it has sparked urgent interest from the White House, especially since Volodymyr Zelensky himself suggested a joint exploitation in the fall. On that day, faced with Scott Bessent's ultimatum, Volodymyr Zelensky buys time, promises to study the proposal, and already envisions making corrections. He points out that such an agreement must be approved by the Parliament (the Rada). His guest does not hide his frustration, heavily insisting that the president signs. However, according to the same source, the American document lacks any details on the investments envisaged by Washington in the local industrial structures, which are already very outdated. It also stipulates that the agreement can only be challenged in New York courts. Respect for Putin It is in the light of this "confrontational" meeting that one must understand Donald Trump's outburst against the Ukrainian president. On Thursday, February 20th, in the White House press room, Mike Waltz did not want to say who started the war in Ukraine. However, the U.S. National Security Advisor emphasized that Donald Trump "is clearly very frustrated with President Zelensky." Mike Waltz denounced the "unacceptable" insults supposedly coming from Kiev towards the American administration. Yet, the opposite conclusion is evident. A cascade of contemptuous remarks, threats, and lies has been observed in Washington against the Ukrainian leader. Donald Trump even went as far as calling him a "dictator." This verbal violence contrasts with the respect shown towards Vladimir Putin, at a time when diplomatic relations were being restored with Russia. To the point that, according to the Financial Times, Washington would oppose labeling Moscow as an "aggressor" in the statement prepared among G7 members, ahead of the third anniversary of the war. Donald Trump's anger towards Volodymyr Zelensky surely has multiple reasons. The Ukrainian leader is associated with the first impeachment procedure against him, at the end of 2019. Furthermore, the American president respects authoritarian figures like Vladimir Putin or Chinese Xi Jinping more than his Ukrainian counterpart. The Ukrainian president has shown personal courage in defending his country and Western values. Donald Trump, on the other hand, believes in the logic of strength and in the distinction between hunters and prey. "It takes two to tango," said the billionaire, still a candidate, when meeting the Ukrainian leader at Trump Tower in October 2024. In tango, only one leads. Not invited to Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, for the resumption of contact between Americans and Russians, Volodymyr Zelensky expressed his bad mood. He also reacted to Donald Trump's remarks, stating that the latter lived in a "disinformation space." According to advisors of the American president, this expression would have exasperated him. But the most determining reason in the offensive launched by the billionaire lies in the Ukrainian underground. "Historical opportunity" In Trump's logic, Ukraine has taken advantage of the United States since the beginning of the war, receiving free military and financial aid. Donald Trump presents false figures: $350 billion on the American side (€334 billion), compared to $100 billion for the Europeans. In reality, the United States has allocated a total amount of around $183 billion, including $65 billion in military aid, mostly representing a subsidy to the national military-industrial complex. But, in this logic maintained by Donald Trump, the time for the bill has come for Kiev, in the form of guarantees. The United States has therefore demanded half of the minerals contained in these rare earths, a share estimated at $500 billion by the American president, representing an unprecedented attempt to extort the strategic resources of a country extremely destabilized by the war.
