Mike Waltz stated on Thursday that a "historical opportunity" was presented to Volodymyr Zelensky, that of "expanding the pie" of these resources, thanks to American investments. He specifically mentioned a poorly maintained foundry, which, if restored, "would represent the equivalent of the total aluminum imports to America for an entire year." For Donald Trump, his counterpart's refusal to sign is an affront. On the plane returning from Mar-a-Lago to Washington on February 19, he revisited the conditions of this mission. Scott Bessent, according to him, "was treated quite roughly, because basically, they [the Ukrainians] told him no, and Zelensky was sleeping and not available to see him." This is obviously false. A photo captured the handshake between the two men. Donald Trump also emphasized that the train journey from Warsaw was "a dangerous trip," even though his predecessor, Joe Biden, had made the same journey. To understand Donald Trump's maximalist approach, one only needs to consult his seminal book, The Art of the Deal, published in 1987. The real estate mogul wrote: "My style of deal-making is quite simple and direct. I aim very high, and then I just keep pushing and pushing to get what I'm after. Sometimes I settle for less than I sought, but in most cases, I still end up with what I want." According to his own words, Donald Trump intends to "resurrect" this rare earths deal, in one form or another, despite Kiev's initial refusal. He makes it a precondition for any future military aid, while continuing to attack the legitimacy of Volodymyr Zelensky, using language favored in Moscow. The American president demands elections in Ukraine and promises an investigation into the alleged diversion of part of the funds allocated by the United States.
lead: The cascade of contemptuous remarks, threats, and lies from the American president this week is largely due to the Ukrainian underground: the Ukrainian president refused to sign a draft agreement on these minerals, which was seen as an affront to the White House.

title: Gérard Depardieu, soupçonné d’être faussement domicilié en Belgique, visé par une enquête pour fraude fiscale aggravée et blanchiment
text: New legal case for Gérard Depardieu. The actor, targeted by several accusations of sexual violence which he denies, is now the subject of a preliminary investigation for aggravated tax fraud and money laundering, a judicial source informed Le Monde on Monday, February 24, confirming information from Mediapart. He is suspected of having claimed fiscal residency in Belgium since 2013, while continuing to live in France without paying taxes there. Searches were carried out on February 11 in France and Belgium by officers from the Central Office for the Fight against Corruption and Financial and Tax Crimes and Belgian judicial authorities as part of a European investigation decision, the same judicial source specified, adding that the 76-year-old actor had not been questioned at this stage. The investigation opened by the National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF) in February 2024 aims to determine the reality of his residency in Belgium, and, if it proves to be fictitious, the amount of tax he would have evaded from France since 2013. This procedure follows several reports from the tax authorities. A "rather despicable" departure "The information from Mediapart and the PNF is once again inaccurate," replied Gérard Depardieu's lawyer, Jérémie Assous. "The tax authorities have been trying for nine years to establish fraud. Without success, they are now trying to achieve this through a criminal procedure. Unfortunately for them, and to the great despair of those who dream of being substitutes, the result will be the same. Some might be tempted to describe these legal methods as despicable, but they are legal and customary." In December 2012, criticizing the tax policy of taxing high incomes desired by the socialist president François Hollande, Gérard Depardieu announced he was leaving France to settle in Belgium, specifically in Néchin, a border town known for hosting wealthy expatriates. A heated controversy followed, with the then Prime Minister, Jean-Marc Ayrault, notably calling his departure "rather despicable," and the actor threatening to return his French passport. In January 2013, he became a Russian citizen, showing his closeness to President Vladimir Putin before distancing himself at the time of the invasion of Ukraine, nine years later. Faced with the announcement of a new judicial investigation against his client, Jérémie Assous denounces a "schedule that fools no one, exactly one month before the trial of Les Volets verts." The actor is set to be judged on March 24 and 25 by the Paris Criminal Court for sexual assaults on two women that allegedly occurred during the filming of the film Les Volets verts, by Jean Becker, in 2021. The case, which was supposed to be tried in October 2024, had indeed been postponed due to Gérard Depardieu's absence for "health reasons." The public prosecutor also requested in August 2024 his referral to the criminal court for rape against the actress Charlotte Arnould, the first woman to have filed a complaint against him in 2018. Whether or not a trial will take place now depends on the decision of the investigating judge in charge of this case. A complaint for "destruction of property" Gérard Depardieu's lawyer also denounces the methods used during the search of his private mansion in the 6th arrondissement of Paris on February 11, and announces to Le Monde that he has received a written mandate from the actor to file a complaint for "damage, theft, and destruction of property": "This concerns notably art objects, but also, and above all, books and sacred texts, including the Quran, which were intentionally trampled and torn, while there was no doubt that they had no connection, neither direct nor indirect, with the object of the search." He adds that he has "testimonies and videos attesting to the destruction of the server as well as the disappearance of the hard drives containing the video recordings made that day." Contacted, the National Financial Prosecutor's Office did not react.
lead: Since February 2024, the National Financial Prosecutor's Office has been trying to determine the truth about his residency in Néchin, a border town known for hosting wealthy expatriates.

title: « Giorgia Meloni, prise entre deux feux sur la guerre en Ukraine, marche désormais sur une corde raide »
text: The heated argument in the Oval Office of the White House between the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, and the American President, Donald Trump, and, by extension, between Washington and Europe, put the Italian Prime Minister in a delicate position. Several hours after the altercation, Giorgia Meloni was the only one among European leaders to abstain from openly supporting Volodymyr Zelensky. In fact, the situation was complicated. Taking the side of the Ukrainian leader would have compromised the special relationship she maintains with Donald Trump, as she desires to become his preferred European interlocutor. However, at the same time, siding with the American President would have definitively labeled her as a nationalist with weak European commitment and little determination to defend Ukraine. At the London summit on Ukraine in early March, Giorgia Meloni could not hide her discomfort. The demonstration of unity among European leaders strengthens the positions of those who have long advocated for a strategic Europe. It also weakens Giorgia Meloni's plans to rely on her bilateral relations with the Trump administration to better secure her position in Europe – even if it means fragmenting her unity. Meanwhile, in Italy itself, her coalition partner, Matteo Salvini, openly sided with Donald Trump. Alongside Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, he may try to both court Washington by presenting himself as an alternative to Giorgia Meloni's right-wing and also appeal to the Italian public opinion that wants an end to the war in Ukraine. Facilitator of transatlantic exchanges Caught between two fires, Giorgia Meloni is now walking a tightrope. The only real card she can play is to carve out a role as a mediator between Europe and Washington on the issue of Ukraine and, beyond that, a role as a facilitator of transatlantic exchanges. In fact, Donald Trump picks up the phone to talk to her more easily than he does with other European leaders. And yet, all that glitters is not gold. Giorgia Meloni is undoubtedly a skilled head of government, but she may be playing for stakes that are too high for her. In January, the Italian Prime Minister shone brightly. As the only leader of the European Union invited to the inauguration ceremony of the new American President in Washington, she touted her trip to the United States as a great success, both domestically and in Europe. It should come as no surprise that Giorgia Meloni is appreciated in Washington. During her two years at the helm of Italy, she adeptly alternated between moderate statements and radical positions – embodying in one way or another a new global model for the right in power. Its foreign policy is based on a set of very clear hierarchies. Interests take precedence over values, national interest comes before multilateral consensus, personal relationships are prioritized over institutional channels, and economic growth outweighs ecological imperatives. Short-term agreements with little impact on domestic policy are favored over long-term solutions. This list could go on. The rather vague term "pragmatism," which can mean anything and its opposite, will summarize this approach here. This is a model that does not provide global solutions but works just enough to maintain power. Softness of economic growth Giorgia Meloni's "pragmatic radicalism" can certainly influence leadership and government practices beyond Italy, within the emerging global network of radical right-wing leaders in positions of power. But at the same time, she may also succumb to the same model she is trying to build. Italy's structural problems, decades old, are numerous and destined to worsen. It is the second most indebted country in the European Union. Its economic growth is very weak and solely due to the massive investments (€194.4 billion) the country received through the European Union's Recovery and Resilience Plan. Over the next ten or fifteen years, Italy, with its aging population, will be forced to increase its public spending. Compared to France, Germany, and the United Kingdom, its diplomatic network is less extensive in terms of foreign offices and personnel. This lack of resources complicates the task of translating ambitious visions into a feasible strategy at the political level. Similarly, Italy's defense spending in 2024 amounted to 1.49% of GDP, far from the 2% threshold set by the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in 2014. This puts Rome in a delicate situation at a time when the White House is preparing to ask its allies to increase their respective commitments to collective security. Strengthening the country's prestige It is certain that the special relationship Giorgia Meloni has managed to establish with Donald Trump may lead him to show some leniency towards her in the short term on specific issues such as Ukraine. However, these immediate benefits will do little to address Italy's structural problems. Aligning at any cost with the United States and their radical initiatives against the multilateral system would sooner or later harm Rome in decline more than it would benefit it. Since the end of the Second World War, Italy has fervently supported multilateralism, and not by chance. Successive Italian governments have consistently supported multilateral institutions such as the United Nations, the European Union, and NATO, with the aim of consolidating the country's prestige while trying to overcome its internal problems. Giorgia Meloni faces a very weighty alternative: to engage in dialogue with Washington for the benefit of Europe or to bet on her ideological proximity with Donald Trump and Elon Musk and thereby threaten the cohesion of the European Union; to establish herself as a star of the international radical right or to recognize that Italy has much to lose from a global (dis)order based solely on force. After all, it is a choice between Giorgia Meloni and Italy: asserting her stature or defending the interests of her country. A very delicate mission for a self-proclaimed nationalist. Translated from English by Frédéric Joly.
lead: Close to Donald Trump but also pragmatic with the European Union, the Italian Prime Minister is in a delicate position, especially since her country is in debt and aging, analyze three Italian political scientists in an article in "Le Monde."

title: L’Ukraine salue des « discussions productives et ciblées » avec les Etats-Unis, à la veille de pourparlers américano-russes
text: "Productive and targeted discussions," that's how the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, Rustem Umerov, leading his country's delegation, described the exchanges with American officials that took place on Sunday, March 23, in Riyadh. On Monday, it's the Russians' turn to sit at the negotiating table with the United States in Saudi Arabia. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called for pushing his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin, to "give a real order to stop the strikes" in a message on social media on Sunday. "The one who started this war must end it," he said. "It is clear to everyone that Russia is the only one prolonging this war," and "without pressure, they will continue in Moscow to scorn real diplomacy and destroy lives." Following the discussions with the Americans, Rustem Umerov stated that "key points, including energy," had been addressed, reiterating Ukraine's goal of achieving a "just and lasting peace." Commenting on the talks scheduled with the Russians on Monday, Donald Trump's special envoy, Steve Witkoff, expressed optimism, expecting "real progress," "particularly regarding a ceasefire in the Black Sea on ships between the two countries." "And from that, we will naturally move towards a total ceasefire," the American envoy said. Ukraine says it is "ready" for a "general" and unconditional ceasefire. However, Vladimir Putin, whose army is advancing on the ground despite heavy losses, seems to be playing for time until his troops have expelled Ukrainian forces from the Russian border region of Kursk. At this stage, the Kremlin claims to have only agreed with Washington on a moratorium concerning the bombing of energy infrastructure. Its spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, has already tempered expectations for the Monday discussions. "This is a very complex issue and there is much to be done," Mr. Peskov emphasized, stating that the negotiations would be "difficult." "We are only at the beginning." Ukraine has retaken a village in the East Despite efforts to bring the warring parties closer on how to achieve a ceasefire in the war triggered by the Russian invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, fighting continues with deadly strikes in Ukraine and Russia. Ukrainian authorities reported strikes hitting the regions of Kiev, Kharkiv (East), and Zaporizhia (East) overnight from Sunday to Monday, causing several injuries, following deadly bombings on the capital the day before. The Ukrainian army announced on Sunday that they had retaken the small village of Nadiya in the eastern region of Luhansk, a rare success for Ukrainian military in this area almost entirely controlled by Russia. The Russian forces, on the other hand, have declared that they have taken control of the town of Sribne, also in eastern Ukraine. Symbolizing the differences to be bridged to achieve a ceasefire, the Ukrainian delegation is led by the Minister of Defense, while the Russian delegation consists of a former career diplomat and senator, Grigori Karassine, and Sergei Besseda, a senior official from the FSB, the security services. Another notable difference: Dmitry Peskov stated that the "main" topic of discussion with the Americans would be the "resumption" of the Black Sea grain agreement, omitting any mention of a potential commitment regarding the suspension of fighting, whether limited or unconditional. This agreement, in effect from July 2022 to July 2023, had allowed Ukraine to export its vital cereals for global food supply, despite the presence of the Russian fleet in the area. Russia later withdrew from the agreement, accusing Western countries of not honoring their commitments to ease sanctions on Russian agricultural exports and fertilizers. Since returning to power in January, Donald Trump has reestablished contact with Vladimir Putin, breaking away from the Western policy of isolating the Russian president.
lead: A delegation led by the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, Rustem Umerov, met with American officials on Sunday in Riyadh to discuss a potential ceasefire. On Monday, it will be the Russians' turn to sit at the negotiating table.

title: Guerre en Ukraine : « Une victoire de la Russie serait une défaite des Etats-Unis »
text: Russia regularly holds an important place at the Munich Security Conference. In 2007, Russian President Vladimir Putin presented his vision of the world, as well as his foreign and security policy, with cold determination. It was a shock for those who knew little about Russia. He then systematically implemented his agenda until the invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022. In terms of history, the 2025 edition lived up to expectations. American President Donald Trump had aimed to quickly resolve the Ukrainian conflict, and his vice president's speech was highly anticipated. However, J.D. Vance barely mentioned the crisis on Friday, February 14, preferring to give a lesson in democracy to European leaders. Once the shock passed, it is important to focus operationally on the negotiation equation that has been initiated. First and foremost, it is crucial to understand Russia's demands based on the objectives Moscow has highlighted since the beginning of the war. These demands can be grouped into three blocks. The territorial block, first: accepting a ceasefire implies the withdrawal of Ukrainian troops from the Donetsk, Luhansk, Kherson, and Zaporizhia regions within their administrative boundaries, meaning a Ukrainian withdrawal from areas that Russia has, in some cases, never controlled, like Zaporizhia. An agreement will then require the recognition of the "new territorial realities," meaning the incorporation of Crimea and Sevastopol into the Russian Federation and the Donetsk, Luhansk, Kherson, and Zaporizhia regions, which have already been annexed. The security block, next: Ukraine's "demilitarization" entails renouncing NATO membership and accepting a neutral, non-aligned, and non-nuclear status. The political block, finally: "denazification" begins with refusing to negotiate with President Zelensky, whose legitimacy has been denied by Moscow since the official end of his term in May 2024. Respect for the rights, freedoms, and "interests" of Russian speakers in Ukraine is also mentioned. Complexity of the equation. In addition, all sanctions must be lifted. These are starting positions for negotiation. Moscow will adjust them based on developments on the ground, particularly in the Kursk pocket. Russia will likely have additional demands, as indicated by the Kremlin's statement after the phone call between the two presidents, to "address the root causes of the conflict," meaning the issue of security order in Europe and strategic stability. In this regard, Moscow had put on the table, in December 2021, two treaty projects, one with the United States on security guarantees, the other with NATO on measures to "ensure the security of the Russian Federation and NATO member states," calling for the development of legally binding security guarantees. The Russians knew that their proposals, aiming to bring the Alliance back to its pre-1997 enlargement configuration, were inherently unacceptable. It was a staged ultimatum, a prelude to war. This reminder shows the complexity of the equation and the need to distinguish between different stages: a ceasefire followed by the search for a sustainable settlement taking into account European and international security issues. It is important to then approach negotiations from a position of strength. While developments on the ground obviously matter, there is a fundamental rule in the perfect negotiator's manual: do not give anything away before the start of negotiations. Without ignoring the complexity of the debate and the divisions among allies, it is difficult to understand why NATO membership is immediately ruled out. Similarly, unilaterally accepting losses of internationally recognized territories, questioning the legitimacy of Volodymyr Zelensky, who could be replaced by a power aligned with Belarus or radical nationalists, is also meeting Moscow's expectations before discussions have even begun. Crisis in the form of Russian nesting dolls Finally, the extent of the confrontation must be well understood. Russia spreads its conflict on three fronts: the military operations field, the domestic front with the political lockdown of the country, and the external field, in its diplomatic dimension and under the dark side of interference and destabilization actions. It is a crisis in the form of Russian nesting dolls: the war against Ukraine represents the smallest doll; the struggle against the United States, NATO, and the European Union the intermediate figurine; the largest one, the fight for a multipolar world – in other words, the recognition of spheres of influence of a Russia seeking to reposition itself in contrast to the United States, as in the good old days. Today, developments are accelerating. But we are only at the beginning of a new sequence. It will extend over the long term as long as a serious solution is sought, and not just a quick fix. It is up to the Europeans to pull themselves together, to assume their responsibilities for their security and not just for Ukraine, and to take their place in the emerging configuration. The informal summit in Paris, on Monday, February 17, at the initiative of France, is part of this mobilization, which is expected to grow. It is up to the Americans to understand that they are in the crosshairs of Russia: To de-westernize the world, de-dollarize the global economy, act in complicity with Iran, North Korea, and China, and leverage the BRICS [Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa] are the strategic axes of Moscow to end the "hegemony" of the United States, followed in a clear and consistent manner. A just and lasting peace is a major condition for transatlantic security. A victory for Russia would be a defeat for the United States. The repercussions would be global, particularly in East Asia. Beijing is in the wings. How could President Trump accept such a crisis resolution?
lead: If the Munich Security Conference, held from February 14 to 16, saw the relationship between the United States and Europe fracture, the negotiation of a just and lasting peace in Ukraine remains a major condition for transatlantic security, analyzes Pierre Lévy, former French ambassador to Russia, in an article for "Le Monde".

title: La Corée du Nord annonce avoir tiré des missiles de croisière stratégiques, quelques jours après le retour de Donald Trump
text: North Korea tested, on Saturday, January 25, strategic cruise missiles launched from the sea, announced the state news agency KCNA on Sunday, January 26, adding that the missiles had hit their target with "precision." "The deterrent capabilities of the armed forces of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea [DPRK] are being further perfected," said North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, who attended the tests on Saturday, according to the agency. The missiles reached their targets after following an elliptical trajectory of 1,500 kilometers, the same source reported, adding that there was "no negative impact on the security of neighboring countries." This test of North Korean strategic weapons is the first since Donald Trump returned to the U.S. presidency on Monday. Shortly before the inauguration ceremony, North Korea had fired several short-range ballistic missiles. "Opposing the United States" Donald Trump, who had several meetings with the North Korean leader during his first presidential term, stated in an interview aired on Thursday that he would try again to make contact with Kim Jong-un, calling him a "smart guy." Relations between North Korea and South Korea, which technically remain at war since their armed conflict of 1950-1953, are currently at their lowest point in years. KCNA released a statement issued by the North Korean Ministry of Foreign Affairs, in which Seoul and Washington are criticized for conducting joint military exercises in January. These exercises "highlight how the DPRK must oppose the United States with the most serious countermeasures," stated the North Korean ministry. "This is the best way to deal with the United States," added Pyongyang's diplomacy. Such exercises regularly provoke the anger of North Korea, a nuclear-armed state, which sees them as preparations for an invasion. In October 2024, North Korea tested what it described as its most advanced intercontinental ballistic missile. According to U.S. and South Korean intelligence services, Pyongyang has also sent thousands of soldiers to Russia to support the war led by Vladimir Putin against Ukraine.
lead: North Korean leader Kim Jong-un attended the tests on Saturday, according to the state news agency KCNA. This is the first test of strategic weapons by Pyongyang since the return of the Republican to the White House on Monday.

title: Emmanuel Macron qualifie Vladimir Poutine d’« impérialiste révisionniste »
text:
French President Emmanuel Macron described his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin, as a "revisionist imperialist" on Thursday, March 6, after the Russian president indirectly compared him to Napoleon. "Napoleon led conquests. The only imperial power I see in Europe today is called Russia," Macron told the press following an exceptional summit in Brussels where the Twenty-Seven agreed on a plan to significantly strengthen their defense capabilities. "Vladimir Putin is a revisionist imperialist of history and the identity of peoples," he added. Emphasizing a "historical misunderstanding," Emmanuel Macron suggested that the Russian president was likely "stung by the fact that [Europe had] exposed his game." He stated that Moscow wanted a ceasefire in Ukraine in the context of talks with the United States under Donald Trump not for "lasting peace" but "to resume the war more effectively." 

Russian President Vladimir Putin expressed regret on Thursday that "there are still people who want to return to the time of Napoleon, forgetting how it ended," in an apparent jab at Emmanuel Macron, whose speech on Wednesday evening was labeled a "threat" by Russia. During a solemn address on Ukraine and European defense, the French President expressed readiness for discussions on protecting Europe under the French nuclear umbrella and accused Russia of wanting the war to continue. Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte invaded the Russian Empire in 1812, capturing Moscow, but his campaign ended in a disastrous retreat and ultimately a Russian victory. The character of Napoleon and his Russian campaign, with Moscow in flames, are often invoked to bolster Russian patriotism.

On Friday, the spokesperson for the Russian presidency, Dmitry Peskov, reacting to the exceptional summit in Brussels, denounced a "confrontational rhetoric" that opposes "the search for a resolution" to the conflict in Ukraine. "We see that the EU is actively discussing militarization, and we are closely monitoring this process because the EU is positioning Russia as the main enemy," he stated. "This could potentially be a matter of deep concern for us and may raise the need to take appropriate retaliatory measures to ensure our security," he continued. On Thursday, Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov drew a parallel between Emmanuel Macron and Hitler and Napoleon. "Unlike his predecessors, who also wanted to go to war with Russia – Napoleon, Hitler – Mr. Macron is not acting with much finesse," he said during a press conference. He added, "Because they were frank about it:..." "We must conquer Russia, we must defeat Russia." And he [Emmanuel Macron], apparently, wants the same thing (...), but he says that it is necessary to go to war with Russia [because it] poses threats to France and Europe."
lead: Responding to the French head of state's speech on Ukraine and European defense, the Russian president regretted that "there are still people who want to return to the times of Napoleon."

title: Ukraine : « Ni Donald Trump ni Vladimir Poutine n’ont encore fait la moindre promesse pour garantir une place aux Européens »
text: The formula is repeated like a mantra by the European allies of Kiev: there can be no negotiations on Ukraine without Ukraine, and no discussions on Europe without Europe. After nearly three years of conflict, both sides are therefore doing everything they can to be at the negotiating table that Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin agreed to launch "immediately" on Wednesday, February 12, over the phone. "Whatever the 'deal,' it cannot be done without Europe. The war is in Europe, which is why we must be at the table and cannot be left out," emphasizes Kaja Kallas in an interview with Le Monde. The High Representative of the European Union (EU) for Foreign Affairs was in Paris that evening to meet with her counterparts from the main European states, along with the Ukrainian Foreign Minister, Andrii Sybiha, at a time when pressure from the Trump administration is increasing to push for negotiations with Vladimir Putin's Russia. At the same time, the big maneuvers have begun between Moscow and Washington. Indeed, it seems natural that Ukraine should be involved in the negotiations, since... it is about Ukraine. If Vladimir Putin contests the legitimacy of the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky is set to meet with American Vice President J.D. Vance on Friday at the Munich Security Conference in Germany. A visit by the Ukrainian leader to the White House to meet with Donald Trump is also being considered. Painful memory Access to rare earths, exchange of territories, the leader of the Ukrainian resistance is making multiple gestures to win the favor of the unpredictable Republican president. Because, ultimately, he may fear that Donald Trump will make peace on the terms of his Russian counterpart, if he were to accept, even if it means forcing Kiev's hand, a sort of freezing of the conflict based on significant territorial concessions, or even a neutralization of Ukraine to prevent it from joining the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) one day. Less existential, the concern of the Europeans to have a seat at the negotiating table is of a different nature. Representatives of the Trump administration, including J.D. Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio, will indeed be in Munich, where they will meet their European counterparts, but they are vague about their intentions. Neither Donald Trump nor Vladimir Putin, who shows no sign of goodwill to end the fighting, have yet made any promises to guarantee a place for the Europeans. "There will be no just and lasting peace in Ukraine without the participation of Europeans," warned French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot after the announcement of the phone call between Trump and Putin. Continental leaders have a painful memory of the Kremlin's behavior on the eve of the large-scale invasion of Ukraine. In December 2021, Moscow had sent its conditions - a draft agreement not to expand NATO to Ukraine, in particular - to the United States and the Atlantic Alliance. Vladimir Putin had, in passing, bypassed European leaders, whom he had then not hesitated to treat as negligible, as shown by his prolonged and futile exchanges with Emmanuel Macron or Olaf Scholz after February 24, 2022. Taking their responsibilities Now, all Europeans are convinced: the security of the continent is at stake in Ukraine, seen as a first line of defense against Vladimir Putin's expansionism. In the event of a Ukrainian defeat, continental leaders fear that the Kremlin leader will target other countries, including, in the worst case, the Baltic States, former Soviet republics now members of the Atlantic Alliance. Conversely, an uncertain compromise with Moscow must therefore, in their minds, help restore the architecture of continental security, hence the need to contribute to discussions without relying solely on the Trump administration, which, deep down, they distrust. Kaja Kallas reminds a fact that Donald Trump seems to ignore when he urges Europeans to spend more on Kiev. "All combined aid, Europe is the most important supporter of Ukraine since 2022, although the United States are slightly ahead of us in terms of military aid," said the former Prime Minister of Estonia. "We have supported Ukraine to the tune of 134 billion euros, including 50 billion in military aid, and we will continue in the hope that the Americans will continue their efforts." In order not to be passive, and to have some influence, Europeans promise to take their responsibilities, in the still distant prospect of a ceasefire. They are discussing "security guarantees" that could prevent any new Russian offensive. The most willing states, starting with France and the United Kingdom, are discussing the deployment of ground troops to monitor compliance with a possible agreement. "Whatever the scenario, it is certain that European countries will have to play a role, especially if European troops are deployed in Ukraine to supervise the ceasefire. The outcome of the negotiations will affect Ukraine and Russia, as well as Europe," said Finnish Prime Minister Petteri Orpo during a visit to Paris on February 10 and 11. Finland is one of the few countries considering the possibility of sending soldiers to ensure Ukrainian security, and therefore that of Europe. Negotiations are difficult, but should accelerate as pressure from the United States increases.
lead: Europeans are jostling to be involved in the negotiations, convinced that the security of the Old Continent is at stake in the country under attack, says journalist Philippe Ricard in his column for "Le Monde".

title: Gérard Depardieu visé par une enquête pour fraude fiscale aggravée et blanchiment
text: Gérard Depardieu has been the subject of an investigation for aggravated tax fraud and money laundering since February 2024, a judicial source told Le Monde on Monday, February 24, confirming information from Mediapart. He is suspected of having fictitiously domiciled himself in Belgium starting in 2013. The investigation concerns acts of tax fraud, aggravated tax fraud, and money laundering of aggravated tax fraud committed since 2013; searches and hearings took place in mid-February 2025 in France and Belgium, according to the same source. This preliminary investigation, opened by the National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF), has been entrusted to the police officers of the national brigade for the repression of tax fraud of the central office for the fight against corruption and financial and tax offenses. Trial in March for sexual assaults The 76-year-old actor had announced in December 2012 that he was domiciling himself in Belgium for tax reasons. He intended to protest against the taxation on large fortunes desired by the then President of the Republic, François Hollande. He had settled in Néchin, a Belgian village bordering France and known for hosting wealthy expatriates. This move had sparked a heated controversy. Criticized notably by political leaders - his departure had been described as "rather mean" by the then Prime Minister, Jean-Marc Ayrault - he had threatened to return his French passport. He had acquired Russian citizenship through President Vladimir Putin shortly after, in January 2013. The actor is scheduled to be tried in March by the Paris Criminal Court for sexual assaults on two women during the filming of Jean Becker's film "Les Volets verts" in 2021. The public prosecutor has also ordered him to stand trial for rape of actress Charlotte Arnould in 2018 - which the actor disputes. The investigating judge in charge of this case has not yet ruled on whether or not a trial will be held.
lead: Searches and hearings took place in France and Belgium, according to a judicial source at "Le Monde".

title: Sur la défense et sur l’Ukraine, les Européens encore en ordre dispersé
text: The statements are ambitious, but the facts are more ambiguous. While Europeans are pleased to have started taking control of their destiny in terms of defense and to remain united behind Ukraine, they are not yet entirely credible. On the eve of the meeting of European heads of state and government, scheduled in Brussels on Thursday, March 20, the Twenty-Seven are still seeking a compromise that will allow them to hold their place in the new world order taking shape since Donald Trump's return to the White House. They now assert – in a major shift – that they need to work together to better protect themselves in a world where the United States may no longer finance their security and where Russia's imperialistic ambitions are concerning. However, the European Commission's "Rearm Europe" plan, which is meant to support the war effort of the Twenty-Seven, is just a "first step," as stated by French President Emmanuel Macron on March 6. It allows member states to activate a "national derogation clause," exempting their defense spending from stability pact rules and theoretically freeing up 650 billion euros from budget constraints. It also foresees the Commission borrowing 150 billion euros, which it will then lend to them on favorable terms, and organizing joint purchases of military equipment. Nevertheless, it is unlikely that the Twenty-Seven will invest 800 billion euros in their rearmament. "No room for maneuver" For budgetary, geographical, cultural, or political reasons, Europeans are effectively approaching the issue of their security, as well as providing new aid to Ukraine, in a fragmented manner. Some want to support Kiev and increase their military efforts at all costs. Others are willing but lack the means. There are also those whose public opinions do not see the need for rearmament. Not to mention the countries that, due to a certain ideological proximity with Donald Trump or Vladimir Putin, could distance themselves from Ukraine. Today, Poland, the Baltic and Scandinavian states are urging their partners like the European Union (EU) to increase their war efforts and aid to Kiev. The Netherlands are also very supportive, but they refuse to allow the EU, which is not within its competence, to meddle in defense. The Hague, in fact, will not make use of the "Rearm Europe" plan. Heavily indebted countries like France, Italy, and even Spain cannot continue to borrow indefinitely without risking soaring interest rates, and from this perspective, the "Rearm Europe" plan does not suit them. They would prefer a common European loan with subsidies for defense. "We will not activate the safeguard clause, because we do not have the leeway to do so," Eric Lombard, the Minister of Finance, announced on March 11. In this context, Paris, Rome, and Madrid do not support the new €40 billion aid plan for Kiev proposed by the EU High Representative, Kaja Kallas. However, in Italy, budget constraints do not explain everything. Since the reelection of Donald Trump, whom she is ideologically close to and hopes will grant favorable treatment if he were to impose new tariffs on Europeans, the President of the Council, Giorgia Meloni, has stayed out of debates on supporting Kiev. "Italy is the weak link." In fact, on March 12, for the first time since coming to power, her post-fascist party Fratelli d'Italia did not vote for a resolution in the European Parliament reaffirming the EU's "unconditional and unwavering" support for Kiev. "We do not vote for something that goes against the Americans," argued Defense Minister Guido Crosetto the next day. On March 17, Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani stated he wanted to wait for the call scheduled the following day between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin before taking a stance on the Kallas plan. "Italy is the weak link," according to a European diplomat. Far from the Union's eastern border, Italians, Spaniards, or Portuguese feel little concern about the Russian threat and are not willing to make financial sacrifices for Ukraine or rearmament. "The only Russians they have ever seen are tourists," another European diplomat quipped. In Spain, "the threat is not seeing Russian troops crossing the Pyrenees," Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez told the Financial Times on March 13. Therefore, Madrid and Rome advocate for an extensive definition of defense spending to benefit from budgetary favor without increasing their military efforts strictly speaking: Pedro Sanchez wants to include everything related to cybersecurity and climate security, while Giorgia Meloni includes the salaries of coast guards or investments in competitiveness. In Hungary, Viktor Orban, close to both Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, is increasingly distancing himself from his partners on the Ukrainian issue. During the European Council summit on March 6, the Hungarian Prime Minister, for the first time, disagreed with his counterparts who reaffirmed their support for Kiev. "The Brussels empire does not help Ukraine, it colonizes it," he declared on March 15. For now, European action relies more on coalitions of volunteers, with the support of the United Kingdom, Norway, and even Turkey, extending beyond the Union's borders. Moreover, in Berlin, the likely future Chancellor Friedrich Merz is still negotiating the government's coalition agreement, and Germany is not yet in the game. Aware of this fragmented landscape, Ursula von der Leyen is proceeding with great caution. Concerned about not deepening the divisions among member states, the President of the Commission does not want to risk pushing them to the breaking point. She is also careful not to influence the debate in Germany, where the Bundestag voted on Tuesday, March 18, for the reform of the debt brake. Germany has already indicated that it wants a more substantial reform of the stability pact, while Mr. Merz plans to spend 100 billion euros per year on defense and invest 500 billion euros in infrastructure. However, before making new proposals, Mrs. von der Leyen is waiting for the next government to be established in Berlin. In the meantime, Mr. Macron hopes to convince Mr. Merz, who is eager to revitalize the Franco-German relationship and have an impact on the European stage, of the need for a large European loan for defense. Warsaw, which shares this objective and also wants to get closer to Berlin, is also working on it. The future Chancellor "is not closing the door," says a close aide to the Elysée. "Friedrich Merz is disruptive," adds Benjamin Haddad, the Minister Delegate for Europe.
lead: The leaders of the twenty-seven EU member states, who are meeting on Thursday in Brussels, are seeking to reach a compromise despite their differences in resources, ideological orientation, and geographical location.

title: Aux 80 ans de l’ouverture du camp d’Auschwitz, la mise en garde des derniers survivants
text: The Kaddish rises to the sky. Then, a handful of survivors from the Auschwitz-Birkenau camp, frail and hesitant silhouettes, advance towards the entrance of the camp, illuminated by a blood-red artificial light. Accompanied, sometimes supported, by young Poles, they place a candle in front of a wagon, placed on the tracks that led the deportees to the gas chambers, most of them upon their arrival. Gathered on Monday, January 27 in Oswiecim (Poland), 70 kilometers from Krakow, for the 80th anniversary of the camp's discovery by the Soviets, some wear a scarf or a striped canvas cap, reminiscent of the deportees' pajamas. Dressed in black, one of the survivors stops for a moment in front of the wagon to cry softly. Behind the tent flaps, under which several dozen heads of state and government mingle with the last survivors, barbed wire topped with watchtowers and red brick barracks stretch as far as the eye can see... Symbol of the genocide of the Jews perpetrated by Nazi Germany, Auschwitz-Birkenau, the largest and deadliest extermination camp - over 1.1 million people, including 1 million Jews, were murdered there - was discovered on January 27, 1945 by the Red Army on its way to Berlin. The place had been emptied ten days earlier. Upon leaving, the Nazis dynamited the gas chambers and crematoria before pushing nearly 60,000 deportees westward in grim "death marches." Only about 9,000 survivors were left behind because they were sick or too weak to walk; they were dying or wandering in dilapidated barracks amidst frozen corpses. "An antisemitism that shocks our children" Ten years ago, for the celebration of the 70th anniversary, there were 300 survivors present. Five years ago, there were 100. This time, there are only 50 who have made the trip, some at the end of their strength, most of them nearly a hundred years old. "We are witnessing a huge rise in antisemitism today, and it is precisely antisemitism that led to the Holocaust," alarmed one of the survivors, 98-year-old Marian Turski, in front of Charles III, the monarch of the United Kingdom, French President Emmanuel Macron, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, and German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier. "War and chaos can happen anywhere," added 95-year-old Janina Iwanska, addressing "those who have their whole lives ahead of them." Tova Friedman represents the deported children. She recounts her arrival on a "dark Sunday" in this camp where a "terrible stench" hung in the air. From her hiding place, she sees other children being dragged to their deaths, hears the heart-wrenching cries of their parents. "I thought to myself: 'Am I the only Jewish child left in the world?'" Seeing "the sky darkened by smoke," she "knew, at five and a half years old, what that meant." "I remember thinking: "I will never let [the Nazis] know how much they hurt me," she continues. Eighty years later, the world is facing "an antisemitism that shocks our children and grandchildren," she adds. "Israel, the only democracy in the Middle East, is fighting for its survival, for its way of life," says the woman who wants to "proclaim that we will never, ever allow history to repeat itself!" "Preventing evil from prevailing," Leon Weintraub, 99 years old, finally shares his "terrible pain to see, in many European countries, Nazi-style uniforms appearing in parades, without any consequences." "I implore all of you to fight against these ideas that led to genocide," adds the survivor, who warns against these "nationalist heroes" who are espousing "the same hateful ideas" as before. He recalls that the world "did not take the Nazi regime seriously" and urges the "new generation" to remain "vigilant against expressions of intolerance or resentment." Heads of state and government listened to these words in silence. Earlier, in a statement, Mr. Zelensky, who met with Mr. Macron after the ceremony, called on the world to "prevent evil from prevailing," while Mr. Putin, who is subject to an international arrest warrant, was not invited. "The memory of the Holocaust is fading (...) and the evil that seeks to destroy the lives of entire peoples still exists," he added in a transparent reference to Russia. The French delegation, led by Emmanuel Macron, in front of one of the entrances to the Auschwitz camp, on the occasion of the 80th anniversary of the camp's discovery by the Red Army, in Oswiecim (Poland), on January 27, 2025. LUDOVIC MARIN / AFP Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, also under an international arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court, was represented by his Minister of Education, Yoav Kisch. As for Donald Trump, who has been staging the expulsion of undocumented migrants since taking office, he chose to send the new U.S. ambassador to France, Charles Kushner, to the ceremony. "There is no longer a common memory." In 2005, the 60th anniversary of the discovery of Auschwitz, of unprecedented magnitude, was marked by optimism and the belief that democracy had definitively triumphed. The Eastern European countries had just joined the European Union and, as historian Annette Wieviorka explains in La Croix on Monday, "'never again' seemed possible." "But what has become of the Russian liberators who discovered Auschwitz, when Vladimir Putin claims that the invasion of Ukraine extends the war against Nazism?" continues the Holocaust specialist, who has just published "Itinérances" (Albin Michel, 592 pages, 25.90 euros). "There is no longer a common memory," she observes. The 70th anniversary took place a few days after the Charlie Hebdo and Hyper Cacher killings in Paris in January 2015. "Jews are targeted because they are Jews," denounced the president of the World Jewish Congress, American Ronald Lauder, who, on Monday at Auschwitz, said he wanted to prevent "our past from becoming the future of our children." For him, the deadly Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, and the Holocaust have "more than one thing in common: the hatred of Jews." If celebrations also serve as lessons for the present, what will be remembered from those of January 2025? Undoubtedly, it is the last major anniversary in the presence of survivors with this underlying, haunting question: what happens to memory as time passes, when there are no more living witnesses? This commemoration also took place in a serious context: a resurgence of anti-Semitism and a rise in nationalism. A few days before the ceremony, American billionaire Elon Musk, close to Donald Trump, participated via video conference in a meeting of Alternative für Deutschland, the German far-right party, which was launching its campaign for the legislative elections on February 23. He stated that too much emphasis was being placed on past mistakes: "Children should not be guilty for the sins of their grandparents. We need to move beyond all of that!" "Telling everything we endured" Before flying to Poland, Emmanuel Macron visited the Shoah Memorial in Paris, where he assured that the French "will not give in to anti-Semitism." Then, he listened to the testimony of Esther Sénot, who recounted being deported at 15 years old and returning at 17 years old "to total indifference." The 97-year-old survivor visited Birkenau in December 2024 to accompany high school students. In 1944, she saw her sister Fanny die in the camp. "You, you are young, you seem to be holding up, promise me, if you ever have the happiness to return to France, to tell everything we endured: the abuse, the dehumanization, the beatings, all those people patiently waiting for the gas chamber..." Fanny had asked her. "Yes, I promise you," Esther replied before returning to work. On January 19, speaking at the Shoah Memorial, Esther Sénot noted that they were now only "a handful of survivors of Nazi crimes." "Children!," she called out, turning to the young people who had come to listen, "I count on you so that when we are no longer here, you become our witnesses, as we have witnessed what we experienced."
lead: Emmanuel Macron attended on Monday the commemorations of the discovery of the Auschwitz-Birkenau camp by the Red Army on January 27, 1945. Former deportees have raised the alarm about the "significant rise of anti-Semitism."

title: En Turquie, 78 morts lors de l’incendie d’un hôtel d’une station de ski
text: Seventy-eight people died in the fire that ravaged their hotel in the middle of the night on Tuesday in a ski resort in central Turkey, according to a new toll announced by the Ministry of Justice on Thursday, January 23, marking the end of the victim identification process at the hospital in Bolu, the regional capital. Following the tragedy, Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan announced that a national day of mourning would take place on Wednesday. An earlier count reported 66 deaths and 51 injuries, as stated by the Minister of the Interior, Ali Yerlikaya, who visited the scene. Entire families perished in the fire at the Grand Kartal hotel in Kartalkaya, which broke out on Tuesday just before 3:30 a.m. (1:30 a.m. in Paris), according to authorities. Accusations of negligence have been mounting since then. Survivors and experts have pointed out that the twelve-story hotel's fire alarm system did not work. Derya Basyilmaz, the president of the Ankara Chamber of Architecture who visited the site, also told Agence France-Presse that the establishment had only one staircase serving as an emergency exit, which was not up to standards. Eleven people, including a deputy mayor of Bolu, the city's fire chief, the owner of the Grand Kartal, its general manager, and its chief electrician, have been arrested since Tuesday as part of an official investigation. According to Tanju Ozcan, the mayor of Bolu, where the Kartalkaya resort is located, the fire was brought under control late Tuesday morning, and local authorities now fear the collapse of the ten-story building. Kartalkaya resort, situated 170 kilometers northwest of Ankara, is backed by a cliff, making it difficult for firefighters to intervene. Nearly 270 firefighters and rescuers were mobilized to extinguish the fire that started on the upper floors for reasons still unknown, explained Turkish Minister of Justice Yilmaz Tunç. It quickly spread to the rest of the building due to the wooden cladding, as reported by Turkish media, which showed large flames coming out of the upper windows and roof, along with plumes of black smoke. Over 230 guests were in the hotel during the national winter holidays, according to the private television channel NTV, which stated that three people died by jumping out of windows. Hotel staff at the Grand Kartal assisted in evacuating guests, but witnesses and media on site denounced the lack of alarm and smoke detectors. "No alarms went off, there were no smoke detectors or emergency exits, even though there was smoke up to the tenth floor," lamented a tearful man in his fifties on television, who had been staying with his family at the establishment since Sunday. "People were calling for help, asking for blankets to be able to escape through the windows," testified Baris Salgur, an employee of a nearby hotel. "We brought them whatever we could find, ropes, pillows, and even a sofa... When the flames got closer to them, some jumped out the windows." The Minister of Justice announced the opening of an investigation entrusted to "six prosecutors" and the formation of an expert committee to support them. Addressing his party, the Justice and Development Party, gathered at a congress, Recep Tayyip Erdogan also assured that "everything will be done to shed light on all aspects of the tragedy and to hold those responsible accountable." Many countries, including Germany, Greece, Ukraine, Pakistan, Azerbaijan, officials from the European Union, and Russian President Vladimir Putin, have offered their condolences and expressed solidarity after this tragedy.
lead: The disaster occurred for a still unknown reason on the upper floors of the building, in the middle of the night on Tuesday.

title: Sur l’Ukraine et la défense européenne, Jean-Luc Mélenchon et LFI s’éloignent encore du reste de la gauche
text: Taking a higher perspective, assuming a presidential posture, and addressing the geopolitical issues related to the war in Ukraine is the ambition of Jean-Luc Mélenchon, who has always placed international issues at the forefront of his ideological framework. On Monday, March 10, on the eve of Volodymyr Zelensky's arrival in Saudi Arabia, where the conditions for a ceasefire were to be negotiated, the three-time presidential candidate outlined in a lengthy blog post titled "If you want peace, organize it!" the path he would take if in power. "It is useful to recall what our response would be to the situation if we were governing our country," he wrote, marking his fifth text since the confrontation between Donald Trump and the Ukrainian president on Friday, February 28, at the White House. To resolve the crisis and despite the ongoing realignment of alliances between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, Jean-Luc Mélenchon is not willing to align with French political leaders and strengthen Europe, which he has always detested. "Making war economy and a European defense the new European foundation is a disaster waiting to happen," he had already expressed. On Tuesday, March 11, on Franceinfo, the coordinator of La France insoumise (LFI), Manuel Bompard, advocated for "a policy of disarmament for all parties," a way to put Ukraine and Russia on an equal footing. These speeches go against those of the socialists, ecologists, and Macronists, who call for strengthening an autonomous European defense. The leader of Place publique, Raphaël Glucksmann, said on BFM-TV on Sunday that he is "in absolute confrontation" with Jean-Luc Mélenchon, whose "defeatist discourse" on Ukraine borders on "surrender." "At this point, the war in Ukraine is lost for the Europeans," wrote the "insubordinate" leader on March 1. Green MEP David Cormand sees "an increasingly significant dissonance with reality." "The situation vindicates those who have long defended European federalism," adds the elected official. "Alter-globalist diplomacy" However, LFI's positions could find some resonance in a public opinion already impacted by inflation, not inclined to make new sacrifices, and also harboring "distrust towards the elites," as political scientist Rémi Lefebvre points out. "Will public opinion support European defense? It's not certain at all. Jean-Luc Mélenchon is therefore cautious," he adds. In the meantime, Jean-Luc Mélenchon continues to advocate for a doctrine of "non-alignment," which seems to come from another era, that of the Cold War, when part of the world rejected imperialist powers. "The non-alignment is 'neither Trump nor Putin' while the National Rally is 'both Trump and Putin'," explained Manuel Bompard, eager to distance himself from the far-right party, which is also opposed to a European defense. "The expression of 'non-alignment' made sense in the 1950s or 1970s, before the fall of the Soviet empire, and thus in a bipolar world. But the European Union did not exist, and China was not the great power it is today," analyzed Frédéric Sawicki, professor of political science at the University of Paris-I. "Today, it no longer means anything." In fact, in this context, who would be the new partners of France, if we exclude Europe and NATO, which LFI advocates leaving? The former socialist senator, who has been advocating for cooperation with China for fifteen years, refrains from outlining a path, simply mentioning an 'alternative global diplomacy' that would lead to finding partners on 'common causes', as opposed to a lasting military cooperation, which the movement does not want. "Our vision cannot be Eurocentric," added LFI deputy for Seine-Saint-Denis Bastien Lachaud on Tuesday, pointing out France's presence in the overseas territories. "Border Conference" But how to convince Vladimir Putin to lower arms and abandon any imperialist project? While emphasizing that the 'unsubmissive' were 'the first' to condemn the Russian invasion in Ukraine on February 24, 2022, Jean-Luc Mélenchon continues to downplay Russia's responsibility. He, who had explained that Crimea was 'given' to Ukraine one evening when the former leader of the USSR Nikita Khrushchev 'was drunk', first recommends establishing 'mutual security guarantees' that would benefit both nations, establishing the idea that Russia also needs to be protected. Another nod to Moscow, Jean-Luc Mélenchon also criticized former socialist President François Hollande, who had 'signed in Washington' 'the installation of anti-missile missiles in Poland', threatening, according to him, '75% of Russia's territory', even though these are defensive weapons. But the leader of LFI has mainly brought back his famous 'border conference' which would consist of reviewing the demarcations established after the fall of the USSR. For the 'unsubmissive' leader, this would be a 'preventive way to take into account existing conflicts and resolve those that are festering', citing Georgia or Moldova, two territories that the Russians are encroaching on and disputing with Europe, before 'the method of the Russians, Netanyahu [the Israeli prime minister], Rwanda, Azerbaijan becomes the norm'. "Jean-Luc Mélenchon has not come to terms with the way the Soviet bloc was dismantled since the fall of the Berlin Wall. This is Putin's discourse." "What was signed during the dismantling was done under duress, so it needs to be revised," analyzes the ecologist David Cormand, who believes that borders result from a "layering of scars." "Once they are what they are, we don't play around with them," he adds. Such positions are likely to deepen the rift on the left.
lead: Like the National Rally, France Unbowed opposes a European defense and advocates for France's "non-alignment," without outlining the details of a new partnership.

title: Sanctions prises contre la CPI : le cadeau de Donald Trump à Benyamin Nétanyahou
text: On Thursday, February 6, the American President, Donald Trump, signed an executive order activating sanctions against the International Criminal Court (ICC). These retaliatory measures were expected after the ICC issued an arrest warrant on November 21, 2024, for crimes against humanity and war crimes against Benjamin Netanyahu. The Israeli Prime Minister, who is a suspect and fugitive in the eyes of the ICC, was able to conclude his visit to Washington by taking this executive order with him as a souvenir. In effusive thanks, Benjamin Netanyahu praised the "bold" executive order as a defense of the United States and Israel against "this anti-Semitic and corrupt court." This action marks another step in a war against the jurisdiction that Netanyahu initiated in mid-April 2024, after being informed of imminent arrest warrants against himself and his former defense minister, Yoav Gallant. At that time, Netanyahu called on all governments to act against the court, accusing it of "waging a ruthless campaign against Israel." Trump's executive order states that the United States will impose "tangible and significant consequences" on those responsible for ICC "transgressions." Currently, the list of targeted individuals has not been made public. Officials subject to economic sanctions will also be banned from entering the United States, as will their immediate family members. Additionally, any person involved in the investigation into crimes committed in Gaza, who "aids, sponsors, provides financial, material, or technological support," or even "goods and services," may also be sanctioned. This last aspect of the sanctions could significantly complicate the work of NGOs, law firms, suppliers, and officials from ICC member states, who are obligated to cooperate with the court, especially in its investigations and arrests. "Being under sanctions means no longer having a bank card and using a phone disconnected from before 1995," said a source at the Court who wishes to remain anonymous. "It's a curious role reversal. We are supposed to prosecute, and we are being treated as criminals." Since indicting Russian President Vladimir Putin in March 2023, several ICC judges have been subject to arrest warrants from Moscow. Many legal experts and diplomats criticize the United States for mirroring their response to that of their adversary and, in doing so, inadvertently favoring Vladimir Putin by reinforcing the perception that the West practices double standards on an international scale. With the ICC, Washington advocates for a selective international justice system, serving its national interests, and has never hidden this fact. According to Donald Trump, investigations targeting American or allied nationals who have not joined the Court would constitute an "abuse of power." Israel, like the United States, rejects any jurisdiction of this court, as they have not ratified the founding treaty. However, by joining the ICC, some states hope, beyond their adherence to the values it promotes, to deter anyone from committing war crimes on their own territory. With this decree, the United States "expect their allies to oppose" the Court's prosecution of Israeli officials. But a few hours after the announcement of sanctions, France, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Germany, traditional allies of the United States, were among the 79 signatories of a statement in support of the ICC. The sanctions could "seriously compromise all situations currently under investigation," they write, committing to "ensure the continuity of the ICC's activities." In The Hague, for several weeks, the Court has been trying to anticipate these sanctions. Some employees have received salary advances, and states have helped their own nationals, closely or remotely linked to the investigation into crimes committed in Gaza, and potentially targeted by these sanctions. Among the countermeasures considered, the ICC could ask the European Commission to activate its blocking statute, a law that allows for mitigating sanctions from outside the Union if they were to affect the institution itself. A law even more radical than the decree issued by Donald Trump on Thursday is still being considered by the U.S. Senate. If adopted, "it would be even more serious than the decree, as it would amount to engraving the sanctions in stone," explains a Court official who wishes to remain anonymous, "it would be much more difficult to revoke them one day." The ICC's expertise on sanctions has evolved over time, as in 2020, the Trump administration had already sanctioned the then-prosecutor, Fatou Bensouda, for wanting to investigate crimes committed by U.S. forces in Afghanistan and looking into crimes committed in the occupied Palestinian territory. At that time, the Netherlands, the host country of the Court, which is based in The Hague, had already been approached. The Netherlands exposed to American backlash On Thursday, Dutch Prime Minister Dick Schoof assured that his government would continue to "ensure the unimpeded functioning of the International Criminal Court." The Netherlands have long been exposed to American backlash against this court. In 2002, the United States passed a law giving the American president full power to release a person detained in the ICC prison in Scheveningen, near the Court's headquarters. A law ironically named the "Hague Invasion Act" by NGOs. The ICC "must be able to freely pursue the fight against impunity in the world," wrote the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, on X. "Europe will always defend justice and respect for international law," she added. Conflict of values? Tomoko Akane, a Japanese judge and president of a court built to preserve "the fragile mosaic of peoples," as her status says, called on "all those who share the values enshrined in the [Rome] Statute to remain united in defending the Court." Faced with sanctions, will the ICC choose to use its own weapons? According to its texts, it can prosecute and punish with up to five years of imprisonment any obstruction of justice. In The Hague, many wonder if the Court will survive American pressures and the defiance of certain States. Several attempts to arrest suspects have recently failed, as in Italy. On January 21, Rome facilitated the escape of a Libyan official pursued for crimes against humanity committed against migrants, further eroding the authority of the Court.
lead: The American president signed an executive order on February 6, imposing sanctions on the International Criminal Court, imposing "tangible and significant consequences" on those responsible for "violations" of the ICC.

title: A Washington, Emmanuel Macron met en garde Donald Trump contre une « capitulation » forcée de l’Ukraine
text: Despite significant differences that persist on the core issues, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Monday, February 24th, that he was "convinced there was a way" with Donald Trump to end the war in Ukraine, while also warning his American counterpart that a peace agreement could not mean a "surrender" by Kiev. During a joint press conference with his host at the White House, the French President also emphasized the need to provide "security guarantees" to prevent Russian President Vladimir Putin from launching another attack. "We want a quick deal but not an agreement that is fragile," said Emmanuel Macron, as Europeans have been concerned for several weeks about a deal that may be too favorable to Russia after being excluded from the Russo-American rapprochement initiated by Donald Trump since his inauguration on January 20th. "This peace cannot mean the capitulation of Ukraine, this peace cannot be a ceasefire without guarantees, this peace must provide for the conditions of Ukrainian sovereignty," emphasized Emmanuel Macron, stating that "for us, Europeans, it is an existential question." Volodymyr Zelensky in Washington? The French President also suggested a ceasefire during an interview in English with Fox News after the meeting. "The plan should be as follows: negotiations between the United States and Russia and between the United States and Ukraine," explained Macron, praising Donald Trump's announcement of a possible upcoming visit by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the White House. "First, a ceasefire is needed. I think it could be concluded in the coming weeks," continued the French President in English, who has recently spoken with almost all European leaders. "If it is not respected, it will be the best proof that Russia is not serious," Emmanuel Macron added. Just before his discussion with the French leader, Donald Trump, during a question-and-answer session with journalists in the Oval Office, praised his "special" relationship with Macron and claimed to be able to end hostilities in "a few weeks." The American President also stated that the signing of an agreement with Ukraine on American access to the country's minerals was "very close," and even mentioned a visit by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the White House to ratify it, "this week or the next." He sees this as a way for the United States to recoup the expenses incurred in supporting Kiev militarily since the Russian invasion in 2022. The Republican, who relies primarily on his dialogue with Russian President Vladimir Putin to stop the fighting, also stated that the latter would agree to the future deployment of European troops in Ukraine, but remained vague on the security guarantees that Washington might provide for these soldiers. The Russian president, for his part, stated on Monday that Europeans could "participate" in the conflict resolution process. "Europeans are ready to go as far as sending troops" to verify that "peace is being respected," said Emmanuel Macron, also asserting that Europe was ready to "strengthen" its defense – a constant demand from his American counterpart. Gulf French President and Donald Trump resumed their diplomatic camaraderie on Monday, already well-established during the Republican's first term (2017-2021), exchanging vigorous handshakes, as well as jokes and compliments. But behind this cordial atmosphere, a real gulf has emerged between Donald Trump and the Europeans regarding Ukraine. While Emmanuel Macron spoke to Donald Trump about Russia as an "aggressor" in Ukraine, the American president continues to equate the two countries – when he is not holding Volodymyr Zelensky responsible for the conflict, as he recently did. In parallel to the meeting in Washington, the United States, joining Russia and North Korea on this occasion, voted against a resolution adopted by the UN General Assembly reaffirming its support for Ukraine and its territorial integrity. "This year should be the beginning of a real and lasting peace," said Volodymyr Zelensky, presiding over a meeting in Kyiv with a dozen Western leaders who came to express their support for Ukraine. "Deals" Three years after unsuccessfully trying to dissuade Vladimir Putin from attacking, Emmanuel Macron has embarked on an equally uncertain mission with the American president. His American counterpart, who has never hidden his fascination for authoritarian leaders and is bored by lengthy multilateral negotiations, adopts a purely transactional approach to diplomacy. On Monday, he once again hinted on his social media platform at "major" economic agreements with Russia, which has been the target of numerous American sanctions until now. "I make deals. All my life, it's about making deals. That's all I know, making deals," declared the former real estate developer. Donald Trump also claimed to have made a "clear break" with the United States' foreign policy "of the past," which, after 1945, positioned itself as a guarantor of democratic values and as a protector of Europe through NATO and its nuclear arsenal. The Russian president, echoing Donald Trump, stated that American and Russian companies were already "in contact." He also supported American investments in the occupied territories of Ukraine. During a televised interview, the Russian president also suggested that Volodymyr Zelensky, who was recently harshly criticized by Donald Trump, was on the verge of becoming a "toxic" figure.
lead: "The peace 'cannot be a ceasefire without guarantees,' warned the French president during a joint press conference on Monday at the White House, stating that 'for us Europeans, it is an existential issue'."

title: En Ukraine, devenir parent dans la guerre
text: The stroller always remains in the hallway, next to the front door. When the sirens sound in Kiev - on average twice a night this winter - Sasha and Dima leave their bed, gently lift Marian from his crib, and place him in the pram in case they need to escape quickly. The infant rarely wakes up. The parents sit near him with blankets and pillows, waiting for the end of the alert. Hoping that the Russian drones and missiles fall elsewhere or are intercepted by the Ukrainian air defense. Hoping that the MIG planes, those carrying ballistic missiles, cease their frightening flight and return to their base in Russia. In the hallway of their small apartment, they feel vaguely protected, with two walls separating them from the windows. In the distance, explosions are often heard, and there have been times when the walls trembled during attacks. The nearest shelter is in the basement of a school at the corner of the street, but it is cold and not equipped. Dima visited it before the birth: it was unthinkable to go down there every night with a baby. So, during the air alerts, near the stroller, Sasha Kurovska and Dima (who, as a precaution, does not wish to give his last name) follow real-time information on Telegram chats, chat a little, doze off. Until, after several hours, a notification on their phones announces the end of the danger. The routine. Since the birth of their little boy on October 17, 2024, peaceful nights have been rarer than restless ones. In the capital and throughout the country, Russian weapons crisscross the sky, disrupt sleep, and instill anxiety drop by drop. The recent phone call between Vladimir Putin and Donald Trump only worries them more, as does the Russo-American meeting on February 18 in Saudi Arabia, without the Ukrainians. Angers and small joys Since the first day of the Russian invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022, Sasha Kurovska has been chronicling her disrupted daily life with her sister Olga Kurovska for M Le Magazine du Monde. The two sisters speak and write French fluently, having studied it at university, as did their parents and maternal grandmother. Sasha is 35 years old, a French teacher who has always lived in Kiev, while Olga, 37, a sommelier who also teaches French, settled in the Paris region in 2015. Over the past three years, one in Ukraine and the other in France, they have recounted this conflict that has infiltrated every aspect of their lives. Month after month, they have shared their fears, angers, and small joys. They have shared their country and its culture. But in 2024, their lives were transformed by something other than war: Olga and Sasha both became mothers. On February 20, 2024, Olga gave birth in France to a boy whom she named Zakary with her partner Yanis. In Kiev, eight months later, Sasha gave birth to Marian, whom she had with Dima, describing him as "a child of love, hope, and war." Becoming parents changed everything for them. It dramatizes every moment of their existence, every choice they make. In early January, Sasha and Dima welcomed us into this new life. Their daily family life is both ordinary and upended, set against a conflict that has been ongoing for three years now. "Having a baby was not the goal of my life, I would have been happy without one, but I am delighted that Marian is here, delighted to have a child with Dima. He is the best father imaginable," explains Sasha one morning while breastfeeding her baby. Her long blonde hair is tied up in a bun, her gentle face barely marked by fatigue. The room is still decorated with fairy lights and fir branches, remnants of the small New Year celebrations she had with her mother, Dima, and Marian. "When will the war end? With what outcome? No idea. I have no trust in either Trump or Putin, they will decide our future without us. But we cannot live constantly in this pain and remain paralyzed. So we also laugh, have a drink, have a child." The baby babbles in his bouncer. He is 3 months old, in perfect health, and cherished by his parents. Sasha teaches most of her French classes online from home. Dima, a video editor, is looking for work. The three of them are always together. This cocoon reassures them. Taking care of a baby, tending to his basic needs, changing him, breastfeeding him, giving him a bath, talking to him, taking him for walks. An appearance of normalcy and simple joys, even though dark thoughts assail them: the responsibility of bringing a child into such a world, the immense anxiety of seeing her father go to the front, of raising him alone, the idea that one day her son will also have to fight. Sasha constantly oscillates between her desire to believe in a better future and the sadness that nothing will ever improve. Small shadows crossing the war So far, in the chronicles of Sasha and Olga, children were like small shadows crossing the war. At the very beginning, in February and March 2022, Olga recounted how they fled in whole trains with their mothers and grandmothers – men aged 18 to 60 are forbidden to leave the country. In September 2022, Sasha noticed schoolchildren carrying two bags on the first day of school: their school bag and the "worry suitcase," containing essentials in case of an attack. The two sisters cried for these small coffins whose images sometimes appear on social media. They worried about exhausted mothers, disabled fathers, and thousands of children forcibly transferred to Russia.
