They wondered how all these war-traumatized adults were going to be able to raise children. Until the summer of 2023, the idea of having a baby was inconceivable for both of them: everything was so dark, so uncertain. However, one day, Olga announced her pregnancy to her loved ones. Shortly after, it was her sister's turn. Sasha gave birth in a private facility in western Kiev. Her best friend, Yana, who holds a good position in a telecommunications company, helped her pay the very high price of 100,000 hryvnias, around 2,300 euros. "I preferred this to the public hospital, where the service is random and bribes are common," explains Sasha. She stayed for three days with Marian in this modern clinic, staffed with well-trained personnel and a basement set up in case of an attack. She has good memories of it, giving birth to the sound of The Beatles, accompanied by attentive midwives. She even managed to work, correcting some of her students' papers after giving birth. The Ukrainian maternity leave lasts four months (there is no paternity leave), and Sasha received a total of 650 euros in state benefits. She quickly resumed her distance learning courses, needing money. With Dima, they planned to move to a larger, more expensive new apartment at the end of January. The building with thick walls even has an elevator and a shelter in the basement. Moving for more space and above all more security is what all the couples around them who have had a baby are doing. A matter of time In their daily lives, Dima and Sasha share everything, from waking up to changing diapers, shopping, cleaning, and cooking. Even in their generation, this is very rare. Dima wants to make the most of his time with his child. He is unemployed, but there is the looming threat of mobilization. At 39 years old, in good health, he knows he will be called up. If the war continues, it's just a matter of time. "For months, I haven't dreamed, I haven't planned anything, we've learned to live day by day," he says softly. He runs his hand through his shoulder-length hair, a grimace darkening his thin face. "In six months, I don't know where I'll be. I prefer not to think about it." This is their biggest fear, with Sasha: that he will have to go to the army. They give no credence to any potential negotiations with Russia under the auspices of the United States. On the ground, Ukrainian forces are retreating in the face of the Russian army, troops are weakening, some soldiers have been on the front lines since the beginning of the war, they need to be relieved. Dima avoids going out and going further than the supermarket or the square below their apartment. In December 2024, he also did not attend his father's funeral, which took place in a city north of Kiev. On social media and in the media, there are all these stories of men being rounded up in the streets to be taken directly to training centers, and then to combat. Recently, a friend of Dima was caught right in the heart of Kiev, outside a metro station. In the capital, they say that men like him have been "bussed," because the agents put them in a small police bus to take them away. It's 3 p.m. on this Wednesday, January 1st, the winter sun will soon be setting. Sasha meets up with his girlfriend Anna at the park. They got takeaway cappuccinos and walk together with their strollers under the bare trees. The cold is dry and biting, the babies are sleeping, bundled up. Anna is an architect, she gave birth to a baby boy, Nestor, in the summer. Her partner signed a fixed-term contract with the army as a drone pilot, just before their son was born. Like him, more and more men have decided in recent months to enlist in the army under contract, to avoid living in fear of being mobilized or arrested in the street. At the time of Anna's childbirth, the future father was assigned to Kiev. Since then, he has had two ten-day leaves in six months. Anna smiles, she spends a lot of time alone with her baby. "I knew... but I didn't really know what it meant. Being afraid, waking up for alerts, going down to the metro to take shelter, alone with a child, it's hard." But Anna doesn't want to complain. There are worse things, always worse. "Family Illusion" This fall, a friend of Sasha's died on the front lines, his partner was pregnant. She has since given birth to a baby girl. This is Sasha's worst fear: living with a child whose father died in the war. "This idea takes my breath away," she wrote in November 2024, in one of her letters to the readers of M Le magazine du Monde. When this thought overwhelms her, she pushes it away to hold on to what she calls her "family illusion." Bath time, errands, reflux, smiles. The days go by. It's Sunday, she's not working today. Marian is sleeping. Dima is out with Rom, their jealous bulldog, while Sasha grinds coffee and serves a slice of fruitcake, Yana with beautiful brown curls rings the doorbell. While Sasha pours coffee and serves a slice of fruitcake, Yana with beautiful brown curls rings the doorbell. She doesn't have a child but she would like to. She is 35 years old. "Of course, with the war, you worry more, you think more about the danger, but, on the other hand, a birth gives you landmarks and you need to find new ones to keep living," she says. "A child forces you to project yourself, to prepare for the future." Olha, a friend and colleague of Sasha, also a French teacher, whom she met later, will explain that she hesitated for a long time to decide whether or not to have a child, before finally making a decision, recently: "I wanted to wait until the end of the war, but life goes by so quickly... Russia has already taken so much from us, I don't want it to take away my chance to be a mother." In Ukraine, in recent years, the postponement of births is a growing phenomenon. At the Ptoukha Institute for Demography and Social Studies, office 610, they closely monitor birth statistics. "Ukraine is facing a demographic crisis, but what worries us the most is the duration of the war," explains Oleksandr Gladun, its deputy director. "Births have been postponed for too long: there was the economic crisis in 2010, political instability, the war in Donbass since 2014, the crisis related to Covid-19, and then the large-scale invasion. For a whole generation of women, their fertile age is passing." The latest figures from the institute report 87,655 births for the first half of the year 2024. For the same period in 2021, 133,457 children were born. The fertility rate, already one of the lowest in Europe before the war at 1.1 child per woman in 2021, has dropped to 0.9. Headache These numbers should be taken with caution, warn Oleksandr Gladun and his colleague, birth specialist Svitlana Aksyonova. With men in the army, women and children abroad, population movements within the country, likely underestimated death toll, and the lack of data from territories occupied by Russia, providing reliable statistics is now a real headache. "In September 2024, the Ministry of Social Policy launched a 'plan for improving the demographic situation.' It's a first step, but it remains very vague: there is no dedicated budget, and very few of our proposals have been accepted," says Svitlana Aksyonova. "Family policy is weak in Ukraine, when we should absolutely be thinking about the future. Because after the war, children, the elderly, those returning from the front line, all of this will weigh heavily on women." The statistician laments the lack of childcare options, assistance with household tasks, measures to facilitate remote work. She smiles slightly and adds that the government does offer a birth grant and a newborn package with baby clothes and essential products. Sasha received this grant of 235 euros (followed by 19 euros per month for three years), but she didn't need the government package. Her sister supported her as best she could from France, with advice and care packages. Olga sent six shipments. Clothes, bibs, hygiene products, baby carriers, toys, books, but also a baby bouncer and a crib, everything was sent in vans from Boulevard Vincent-Auriol, in the 13th arrondissement of Paris. Ukrainian carriers transported them to Chernivtsi, a city near the border with Hungary, and a private Ukrainian postal service took over. Sasha received the packages in three days. Olga also organized an online fundraiser through which she was able to raise 2,426 euros for her nephew's baby gift: a powerful electric generator to cope with the regular blackouts. The device is now in place in Sasha and Dima's hallway. "I wanted to take as much of this off my little sister as possible, I had given birth eight months earlier, I knew what she would need," Olga recalls. "I wanted so much to take care of her." In their daily lives, the two sisters, who have not seen each other for a year and a half, exchange messages and short videos throughout the day. They show each other their respective sons, trying to build a relationship with them from a distance. "Milk mama club" They discovered that they both diligently follow the Instagram account of a Ukrainian doctor specializing in maternity and breastfeeding, Khrystyna Mazurok. Originally, this energetic blonde woman wanted to create a health center in Kiev dedicated to a new approach to maternity. But with the Russian invasion, she had to change her plans and came up with the "Milk mama club" on social media. Her chats on Telegram and her Instagram account are followed by more than 20,000 women, 20% of whom live abroad. Khrystyna Mazurok offers online appointments, workshops, and advice for young mothers. She has gathered a team of eight female specialists around her: a gynecologist, pediatrician, psychotherapist, allergist, sleep specialist, etc. Since the start of the war, the doctor has observed a shift in the concerns of Ukrainian women. "In 2022, the requests were focused on their baby's survival: how to manage without water? Without electricity? If supermarkets close? Since 2023, women are wondering whether to have a child, they worry about their safety, about their partners. We need to be very attentive to their mental health after childbirth, they often need psychological help because they have more difficulty realizing that they are mothers." Throughout this long conflict, Ukraine is undergoing profound changes. The country is experiencing the definitive departure from a Soviet world, including in parenting. "If someone had asked me five years ago what the role of a father was," Dima notes, "I would probably have said to go to work and only see your children briefly in the morning and on weekends. I believe our generation does things differently. At least, Sun and I want to do things differently." Sun is the nickname he gave to Sasha since they have known each other. Together, they read articles, listen to podcasts, discuss, but above all, follow their desires. And they still project themselves, even if it brings them as much joy as pain. How else could it be done? "Later," Sasha continues, "I want us to be very close, all three of us. I want Marian to be able to come to us if he is suffering or if he encounters a problem. I want us to be able to talk about his mental health, his spiritual life, intimacy, sex. All of that was forbidden or taboo for our parents in the Soviet world. There was a kind of distance between parents and children, the adults managed the children. Love, affection were... modest." She pauses and adds, "Then, when you have to wait in line for three hours to find toilet paper or food, as was the case in the 1980s, you have less time to think about all that." Maryna Marchenko, director of the NGO Golada, raises her hazel eyes to the sky when she talks about the Soviet legacy and the condition of women that results from it. This lawyer by profession has three children with her husband, Serhiy Marchenko, the country's finance minister. She receives at the GNRTR, a trendy coworking space, where the NGO has offices. "The strong and independent woman was not the goal of the patriarchal Soviet government: the woman was not equal to the man, she was a labor force, subjected to all kinds of injunctions," she says in a soft voice. "And if there were nurseries in the factories, it was just so that the mothers could work." "Invisible and poorly supported" Founded in 2018, Golada aims to help women better experience motherhood and early childhood, periods when they are "invisible and poorly supported by society," says Maryna Marchenko. With the war, the actions of the NGO (whose name is inspired by a Slavic goddess, Lada, who takes care of young mothers) have gained momentum: psychological support, information campaigns, chatbot to understand emotions, conferences and monthly meetings... Sasha has already attended one of these "mum's day" events and plans to return in March. Women come with their babies to listen to professionals on various topics (body, work, sex...), babysitters are present, a stroller garage is available, and the conference room is close to a metro station, to take shelter in case of an air raid. Since February 2022, more than 10,000 women have received help from Golada and nearly 6,000 have participated in their events, according to Maryna Marchenko. "The trend is there, there are places that create better conditions for women, if they wish, to become mothers. This is even more crucial given the demographic crisis we are in." Another huge difference with the generation of their parents and an obvious consequence of the war: many young parents intend to give their children a 100% Ukrainian education. For Olga and Sasha, it is out of the question for Marian and Zakary's mother tongue to be Russian, as it was for them, or for Russian culture to be the reference. "Today, my children do not speak Russian, do not listen to Russian music, do not watch anything Russian, they are Russophobic even though originally my family is Russophone," notes Iryna Vyhovska. The thirty-year-old, mother of a 6-year-old daughter and a 10-year-old son, is a writer of tales and an editor. In April 2024, with one of her acquaintances, Olena Balova, they launched a podcast of testimonies on parenting. The title of this podcast, "And there will be people...", takes a verse from the national poet Taras Shevchenko. Sasha has devoured the 12 episodes already online. Others are in preparation but the sound engineer has just been mobilized. The listens are still quite confidential (20,000), but the topics addressed reflect the concerns and experiences of many Ukrainian parents. One episode addresses the management of children's trips, mother, grandmother, a very common situation in the last three years, as men are being drafted into the army or avoiding travel. Another gives voice to a soldier dad on the front lines, who recounts how he communicates from a distance with his daughter whom he hasn't seen in two years and tries to explain the situation to her. "The children quickly realized that we were at war," continues Iryna Vyhovska, "but making them understand why the Russians are attacking us is more complicated. I bought books that helped me talk to them about it, and in the morning, on the way to school, we listen to history podcasts. But they need that Russophobia I mentioned to understand why, every day, they have to go down to the shelter at their school and why, every night, they have to sleep in a hallway. How else can I explain to them other than with the notions of good and evil, as in tales?" A serious issue At 4 and 12 months, Sasha and Olga's babies are of course too young for them to explain the conflict, but Olga admits she has already thought about it. "Who attacked whom, why, how Ukraine has been fighting for its independence for years, I will tell Zakary as soon as possible in simple words." Similarly, it was very important for her son to have Ukrainian nationality, in addition to French nationality. She fought with the consulate administration, and on January 8, Zakary obtained his nationality certificate. In daily life, Olga speaks exclusively Ukrainian to him, sings him Ukrainian lullabies, and makes him listen to Suspilne, a Ukrainian web radio for children. "His dad Yanis laughs, saying that Zakary is already a little Ukrainian because he is starting his complementary feeding with beetroot - an essential element of borscht, which, I remind you, the Ukrainian preparation has been inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list!" The whole family also enjoys Zakary's long strand of hair on his forehead, which reminds them of an oseledets, the typical hairstyle of a Ukrainian Cossack. Olga smiles, but behind it all, the stakes are serious: "In a world where Putin wants to eliminate our country, our people, it is crucial that I pass on Ukrainian culture and history to him. If the Russians teach their children to hate us as they do with their propaganda in schools, where will these kids be in ten or twenty years? What will they want to do? Kill Ukraine and Ukrainians." She pauses: "The current discussions with Putin disgust me. I feel like the West is about to abandon Ukraine..." In March, Olga plans to take Zakary to Kiev, she has bought her tickets. Twice since her child's birth, she has canceled such a trip because the conditions did not seem right to her. She hopes to make it there for good this time. "The war adds fear, of course, but it won't stop me from going," she says as if to give herself courage. Sasha has never seen Zakary, and I also want to introduce him to my grandmother. She is 88 years old, she is sick. And then, I don't know my nephew, Marian, and neither does Zakary." Olga dreams that, later on, the two cousins will be close, spend holidays together in France, go to camp in Ukraine. "In Ukrainian, we don't have the word 'cousin,' we say 'brother.' Sasha and I are first-generation sisters. Marian and Zakary are dvoyuridnyy brat, which means second-generation brothers." Sasha would also like to spend a few weeks in France with Marian to rest, but she cannot imagine leaving Dima. She does not want to deprive him of his child when their days together seem numbered. In Kiev, it has been dark for a long time, the day falls very early in January. With her sleeping baby close to her, Sasha sits in a chair covered with a blue and yellow shawl printed with "Be brave like Ukraine." With a weary voice and a veiled look, she thinks out loud: "What is our future here? Should Dima go to war? Should I also go to war if they ever mobilize women? And Marian later as well? I am tired. I would like to say that I have confidence, but that would be false. Anxiety has taken over me, and it is getting stronger. More than the war, I fear the post-war period that awaits us. It will take us decades before we become a livable country. Do I have the right to want something else for my child?" Her question lingers. Sasha refuses to think about what she would do if Dima were mobilized; she forbids herself from imagining leaving the country one day with her child. Today, she cannot believe in a real peace that Putin would accept. A peace that would allow Ukraine to grow and flourish. Without a desirable future, she has only the present.
lead: For the past three years, Sasha Kurovska, from Kiev, and her sister Olga, in Paris, have been sharing their lives disrupted by the Russian invasion. In 2024, they each gave birth to a son, despite a future that remains bleak. Today, Sasha is distressed to see Russia and the United States discussing the fate of Ukraine without Ukrainian involvement, and she lives with an unbearable anxiety: the mobilization of her partner.

title: En Hongrie, un documentaire sur l’enrichissement de la famille de Viktor Orban attire des millions de vues
text: LETTER FROM CENTRAL EUROPE Although it has already been extensively documented by the written press, the spectacular enrichment of Viktor Orban's family and friends is even more eloquent when brought to the screen. Entitled "The Dynasty," a documentary produced by the Hungarian investigative site Direkt36 has been a resounding success in Hungary since it was released on YouTube on February 7, with nearly three million views by February 20. "We knew the subject would generate some interest, but we certainly did not expect millions of views," rejoices Andras Pethö, director of Direkt36 and producer of the film, which, on paper, has already been seen by a third of the inhabitants of this Central European country. While Direkt36 is one of the news sites that publish the most in-depth investigations into the affairs of the nationalist prime minister, this film attracted an unprecedented audience in just two weeks. This success was partly fueled by the unintentional publicity from the government, which accused the documentary, even before its release and without providing evidence, of "being part of a campaign by Ukrainian intelligence to discredit the prime minister," in line with Mr. Orban's pro-Russian obsessions, who has made Kiev and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky his new enemy of the moment. For a little over an hour, the film effectively traces how Mr. Orban's close associates miraculously became wealthy parallel to his political career. In power for the first time between 1998 and 2002, and continuously since 2010, the leader has favored their businesses each time. Their grip on the economy and their power now reach a level reminiscent of monarchical times, inspiring the title chosen by Direkt36. The film first focuses on the fabulous destinies of Mr. Orban's two friends - Lajos Simicska and Lörinc Meszaros - who became the country's wealthiest individuals in just a few years, seizing control of all sectors of the Hungarian economy dependent on public contracts, before Mr. Simicska eventually lost a large part of his businesses after falling out with the head of government in 2015. The documentary also briefly mentions the fate of Mr. Orban's father, Gyözo, a successful owner of a quarry that supplies numerous public construction projects. A son-in-law who wins many tenders But the most intriguing part is dedicated to Mr. Orban's eldest daughter, Rahel, and her husband, Istvan Tiborcz. At just 38 years old, Mr. Orban's son-in-law has become the fifteenth wealthiest individual in Hungary, according to Forbes magazine's ranking. While his company BDPST operates in several sectors, Mr. Tiborcz particularly enjoys luxury hotels, which he acquires one after the other in the capital, before renovating them with the help of Rahel Orban, the group's "artistic director." In a hidden camera investigation, Direkt36 managed to film for the first time the Botaniq Budai Klub, the very exclusive club that Mr. Tiborcz opened in 2023 in the upscale neighborhoods on the right bank of the Danube. Tennis courts, luxurious lounges, and game rooms are reserved for carefully selected members willing to pay an entrance fee of several thousand euros. This luxury sharply contrasts with the modest standard of living of Hungarians, who have been severely affected in recent years by record inflation. Even though Istvan Tiborcz insists that his fortune is solely the result of his hard work, Direkt36 meticulously demonstrates that "the reality is quite different." The son-in-law of the prime minister's first business venture was actually a street lighting company that miraculously started winning all the tenders issued by municipalities controlled by Fidesz, the ruling party. Financed by the European Union, these contracts even prompted an investigation by the European anti-fraud office, which concluded there were irregularities. Comparison with Ben Ali's Tunisia After his bright start in public lighting, Mr. Tiborcz reappeared in 2018 at the helm of BDPST, a company that has since been expanding its activities in transportation, tourism, and real estate. Once again, benefiting from remarkable government generosity in Hungary, as shown in the documentary, which lists the tax advantages and subsidies accumulated by BDPST. "A politically controlled capitalism," the film concludes by comparing Hungary to Ben Ali's Tunisia, the autocrat who ruled his country from 1987 to 2011. Although the film is subtitled in English, it can sometimes be a bit unclear for foreign viewers due to the abundance of details. However, Direkt36 cleverly juxtaposes the prime minister's statements, who consistently promises to fight corruption while expressing outrage when asked about the wealth of his relatives, repeatedly stating that he does not interfere in the private affairs of his father, son-in-law, or friends.
lead: "The Dynasty" effectively recounts how the close associates of the prime minister have miraculously become wealthy alongside his political career for at least the past fifteen years.

title: « Les bouleversements provoqués par le retour de Donald Trump pèsent sur l’équation politique française et pourraient modifier les enjeux de 2027 »
text: The movements of tectonic plates triggered by Donald Trump's return to the White House continue to have repercussions around the world. In Canada, the balance of power between the Liberal Party of the new Prime Minister, Mark Carney – who succeeded Justin Trudeau – and the leader of the Conservative Party, Pierre Poilievre, has abruptly shifted in the weeks leading up to the federal elections. The populist and libertarian candidate, who is anti-vaccine, anti-elites, and anti-media, and who has taken Trump as a model, was the clear favorite in the polls in January (with a 20-point lead over his opponent). However, he has been caught up by the former Governor of the Bank of Canada, who has a more reassuring profile, as tensions between Canada and its American neighbor escalate. The bluster of the American president, who is imposing 25% tariffs on Canada and threatening to make it the 51st American state, has caused the intentions to vote for the Trumpist candidate to collapse. "A drastic change in the political landscape," observes Roland Lescure, deputy Renaissance of the French abroad (North America), who does not rule out a victory for Mark Carney, which seemed "impossible" at the beginning of the year. "The unpredictability and excesses of Trump, which have sparked a national backlash in Canada, are impacting people's daily lives and therefore the political landscape," believes the former minister. The upheavals caused by Mr. Trump's return, his rapprochement with Russia, and his distrust of Europe, from which he is abruptly disengaging, could similarly affect the French political equation, and even change the stakes for 2027. "The price of the ticket for the presidential election has just increased significantly," analyzed political scientist Bruno Cautrès on March 11, speaking to Agence France-Presse. The return of the "old hands" Far from the anti-establishment wave generated in 2017 by the emergence of a president under 40, promoter of the "start-up nation" and architect of depoliticization, the geopolitical turmoil and the concerns it raises are bringing back into favor the "old hands," experienced and knowledgeable about the global stage. This is evidenced by the success enjoyed since the beginning of the year by former Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin, invited everywhere to decipher the world's disorders, hailed for his historic speech at the UN in 2003. On the left, François Hollande is also credited for his lucidity towards Vladimir Putin during his term. As for the former Brexit negotiator and short-lived Prime Minister Michel Barnier, he also believes that his experience could be an asset in the presidential competition. All three have their sights set on the 2027 deadline. Overshadowed by international news, the political scene continues to evolve. Engrossed in the preparation of their congresses, members of the Les Républicains party, the Socialist Party, and the Ecologists are divided internally. While declared or potential candidates, such as Edouard Philippe or Raphaël Glucksmann, are organizing themselves. However, they are acting under the radar, not very visible, not very audible, while international crises have become the main concerns of the French people. If the spotlight has shifted, the change in paradigm forces the actors to position themselves. "The new international situation serves as a revealer for the parties," summarizes the deputy general director of Ipsos, Brice Teinturier, according to whom each party's positions can "have serious consequences for the presidential election." "Those who downplay the Russian threat will look foolish if tomorrow proves otherwise," he quips. "Marine Le Pen is stumbling." In fact, the new Trump-Putin axis goes against the far right, as well as the radical left, both trapped by their past positions. The National Rally (RN) is paying the price for its old complacency towards Moscow and its fascination with Mr. Trump, a populist leader with protectionist tendencies, whom they credit with wanting to defend American interests. Today, Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella are trying to keep more or less at a distance from both. But while they support Ukraine, they struggle to define a clear position on the threat that Russia poses to the European continent and oppose the European defense. These conflicting demands make them difficult to hear. "For the first time in a long time, Marine Le Pen is stumbling," observes Brice Teinturier. Jean-Luc Mélenchon faces the same difficulty, as his anti-Americanism has often been accused of showing complacency towards Russia. The "unsubmissive" leader also claims to defend a position of "neither Trump nor Putin," but his criticisms are more focused on the former, "who physically threatens us (...), who suffocates part of our economy," as he said on Sunday, March 16, on France 3, rather than the latter. More broadly, while the return of Donald Trump - and his dramatic positions - could embolden some of the far-right voters, riding the populist wave, his excesses and the new regime he is shaping could scare another segment of this electorate, who supported the RN on the grounds that "we had tried everything except him." The damages already caused in the United States (mass layoffs in the public sector, budget cuts for science...) visible to all, and the climate of fear instilled by the American president could paradoxically weaken the positions of those in France who also criticize the rule of law. "The brutality of Trump, which destabilizes the planet and scares the French, does not serve those who were in this ideological vein," argues Brice Teinturier. Macron is regaining popularity. On the contrary, Emmanuel Macron is one of the main beneficiaries of this new international situation. Destined for a slow and painful end to his reign, the president finds himself in a central position, on the verge of saving the end of his term. While his failed dissolution had discredited him in the eyes of the French, he is regaining their favor, gaining ground in the polls. According to political scientist Vincent Martigny, periods of crisis always favor strong leadership and hyper-presidency, disqualifying parliamentary time, which is no longer suitable. The head of state, who had endured with some annoyance the cohabitation with Michel Barnier, appointed nine weeks after the legislative elections, has therefore regained control over some of the lost levers. The international configuration allows him to regain influence over the government, as evidenced by his summoning of key ministers, gathered on March 13 at the Elysée, to reflect on the "defense effort" mentioned in his last televised address. The events that have restored his legitimacy seem to have made everyone forget that the president and his camp suffered a heavy defeat in the European elections and legislative elections in June and July 2024. On the other hand, Prime Minister François Bayrou is criticized for using geopolitical circumstances to interfere in the meeting of social partners on pensions, deemed "out of touch" by Edouard Philippe and part of the "central bloc." On Sunday, in the program "Political Questions" on France Inter, in partnership with France Télévisions and Le Monde, Mr. Bayrou firmly ruled out a return to the legal retirement age of 62, provoking the anger of the unions currently discussing the issue. This "conclave" was a pledge given to the left by Mr. Bayrou, in the hope of avoiding censorship. An excess of regained confidence could backfire on the executive, as the political equation remains infinitely fragile and unstable. Because while the new disorders of the world have an impact on the national political equation, a number of constants remain. Faced with immense challenges, particularly in terms of defense and sovereignty, the nation remains heavily indebted. And Messrs. Macron and Bayrou still do not have a majority.
lead: The Trump-Putin axis goes against both the far right and the radical left, but it strengthens the President of the Republic as international crises become the top concern for the French, as explained by Solenn de Royer in her column.

title: A Paris, les alliés se lancent dans la « planification » de garanties de sécurité pour l’Ukraine
text: Faced with the increasingly urgent question of "security guarantees" for Ukraine, the meeting held in Paris, at the former military hospital of Val-de-Grâce, on Wednesday, March 12, between the defense ministers of the main European military powers - France, the United Kingdom, Germany, Italy, and Poland - made "planning" its main focus. A term that, in military language, carries significant weight, and signifies the desire to move beyond mere declarations of intent to the implementation of concrete objectives. While Europeans are currently marginalized in the discussions initiated between the United States, Russia, and Ukraine for a ceasefire, this third meeting of the so-called "E5" format, created in the aftermath of the election of U.S. President Donald Trump, aimed to signal the determination of some allies to have an impact, no matter the cost. "Now, we need to start having a pragmatic agenda, a bit rough (...), but it is in the details that we will manage to change the geopolitical paradigm," emphasized the Minister of the Armed Forces, Sébastien Lecornu, on Wednesday. To demonstrate the seriousness of their intentions, the allies heavily relied on the tools of strategic communication. They notably aligned strongly with the conclusions reached the day before in Paris at the meeting of the chiefs of staff of the armed forces of 34 countries, including all NATO members except the United States, under the leadership of the only two states in Europe with nuclear deterrence: France and the United Kingdom. "Considering all scenarios" A meeting in a unique and historic format that took place at the Museum of the Navy, a symbolic location: it was indeed on this hill of Chaillot that the allies met between 1952 and 1959 when the Atlantic Alliance was still in its early stages and Paris was its headquarters. At the end of this meeting, the military chiefs agreed, in a statement, to "present to their political authorities" "strong options" to "support Ukraine and deter Russia." A new meeting is scheduled for the week of March 17 with countries that wish to participate, about fifteen, as specified by Mr. Lecornu on Wednesday. However, the exercise is far from simple, as the willing countries are dependent on a schedule and negotiations beyond their control. "Defense ministers cannot only consider the best-case scenarios; we must think about all possible and imaginable scenarios, from the best to the worst," bluntly reminded the Italian Defense Minister, Guido Crosetto, during a press conference alongside his counterparts on Wednesday at Val-de-Grâce. If the principle of sending troops to Ukraine, once a ceasefire is declared, appears to be supported by a relatively large number of allies, many details still need to be discussed. "The final decision will depend on very clear conditions regarding the mission, objective, and mandate of this force," said the Minister of Defense of the Netherlands, Ruben Brekelmans, cautiously, on Tuesday at the Paris Defence and Strategy Forum, an annual meeting of defense circles held in parallel with political meetings at the Military School in Paris. "A demilitarized Ukraine does not exist." However, European allies of Ukraine took advantage of these two meetings to support other demands. They expressed concerns about security in the Black Sea, the safety of Ukrainian nuclear power plants, and their wish to avoid Moscow demanding the "demilitarization" of Ukraine as a condition for validating the ceasefire. "The primary security guarantee for Ukraine is the Ukrainian army itself, its capabilities, armament, equipment, training, and structure," emphasized Mr. Lecornu. "A demilitarized Ukraine does not exist... It's like a man missing a leg," added his Italian counterpart. These carefully chosen words echo the allies' fears that Russia may seek to halt or contain European military aid to Ukraine, as well as limit various soldier training programs supported since the beginning of the war. This includes activities outside Ukraine, notably in Poland, and in some cases, more discreetly, on Ukrainian territory. Similar concerns have also emerged regarding demands towards Kiev in the past two days. Some allies are now considering revisiting a request pushed by the Biden administration in late 2024: a further lowering of the conscription age. This measure is highly unpopular in Ukraine, as Kiev has always sought to protect its youth from war. Under pressure, Ukrainian authorities had already lowered this age limit from 27 to 25 in mid-2024. However, in the fall, Washington began demanding that it be set at 18. These meetings in Paris have also provided an opportunity to make progress on defense cooperation projects among Europeans, as the European Commission presented new financing options on March 4 as part of its Rearming Europe plan. This plan is considered an integral part of Ukraine's protection. "These are decisive days," concluded British Secretary of State for Defense John Healey. "I say to President Putin: the ball is in your court." You said you wanted to talk, prove it. Accept the ceasefire (…). Do not make mistakes.
lead: Excluded for now from the negotiations between Washington, Moscow, and Kiev on a ceasefire, the Europeans are trying to influence the process from afar.

title: Donald Trump tente de rééquilibrer son message prorusse sur la guerre en Ukraine, puis revient en arrière
text: Here is a cosmetic turnaround. In a message posted on his Truth Social network on Friday, March 7, Donald Trump threatened Russia with additional banking sanctions and new large-scale tariffs "until a ceasefire and a definitive peace agreement are reached" in Ukraine. This statement came at the same time as Moscow launched a massive attack of drones and missiles on Ukraine's energy infrastructure. It also comes at the end of a week where the White House has increased pressure on President Volodymyr Zelensky, following the failed signing of the agreement on Ukrainian minerals on February 28. Donald Trump continues to express confidence in Russia's good faith, portraying the Ukrainian leader as the main obstacle to a quick ceasefire. He questioned his democratic legitimacy and decided to interrupt arms and ammunition deliveries, as well as the sharing of American intelligence. When asked on Friday in the Oval Office, Donald Trump seemed to already contradict his own reversal. "I think he's doing what anyone would do," he said, regarding Vladimir Putin and the intense Russian bombings. According to the American president, it would be "easier" to deal with Russia. "I think he will be more generous than he needs to be," he added about his counterpart in the Kremlin. However, he still seems to doubt Ukrainian motivation. "If they don't want to agree, we'll leave," he warned again. The day before, also in the Oval Office, Donald Trump told journalists that Ukraine had "no choice" and would want to make peace. "I also think that Russia wants to reach an agreement because in a different way - a different way that I am the only one to know, the only one - it has no choice." Cryptic statements. Nuances within the administration The White House is facing widespread rejection of its pro-Russian stance in public opinion and among traditional Republican lawmakers in Congress. In a Reuters-Ipsos poll, 70% of Americans (62% among Republicans) reject the idea that Ukraine bears primary responsibility for the war. In another CBS News-YouGov study, published before the altercation between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House, 52% said they supported Ukraine. There are nuances in approach within the administration. There are those who claim to quickly turn the page on the war, with little consideration for Ukraine's fate or European security. And then there are officials who are more wary of Russia. The special envoy and friend of the president, Steve Witkoff, has no knowledge of this country, nor familiarity with its diplomatic practices. It was he, however, who spent three hours one-on-one with Vladimir Putin in Moscow, without a representative from the State Department. A direct channel that also signifies vulnerability, facing a cunning leader who has been meeting with his Western counterparts for a quarter of a century. Speaking on Thursday, March 6, in front of the Council on Foreign Relations think tank in Washington, General Keith Kellogg, Donald Trump's special envoy on the Ukrainian side, tried to provide a summary. The general represents the realist wing among the advisors. He took care to correct the false figure repeated by Donald Trump – $350 billion – regarding the amount of American aid to Ukraine. Keith Kellogg mentioned $170 billion and also described the conflict as a "global issue," not just a "European one." But he mainly defended Donald Trump's approach, which is the desire to "reset" the relationship with Russia. To bring both parties to the negotiating table, one must use "carrots and sticks," explained the military man. According to Keith Kellogg, on a scale of one to ten, the sanctions are at seven, but their implementation is only at three. In addition to the $200 billion in Russian assets seized in Europe, he emphasized the energy sector to prevent Russia's "ghost fleet," which exports hydrocarbons, from continuing its routes; "70% of this oil passes through the Baltic Sea," he noted, citing the American action in 2018-2019 against the export of North Korean coal to China as an example. However, the real influence of General Kellogg seems questionable. The Witkoff line dominates, reinforcing Donald Trump in his magical thinking: Vladimir Putin and he are destined to do great things together.
lead: In the span of a few hours on Friday, the American president threatened Russia with additional sanctions and new tariffs, before praising Vladimir Putin for his "generosity."

title: L’Ukraine et l’Europe seules face à la Russie
text: Vladimir Putin achieved a remarkable success in his war against Ukraine on Friday, February 28. He achieved this without firing a single missile, without even having to say a word. This success was offered to him by Donald Trump in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. The Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, who came to sign an agreement allowing the United States to exploit the mineral resources of his country in exchange for their military support since the Russian invasion of February 24, 2022, was able to witness the hostility of the new administration. This hostility was illustrated by the unprecedented aggressiveness of an American vice president, J.D. Vance, towards the leader of a democratic country at war with a dictatorship. Bitter exchanges, accusations, threats - never in the history of the United States had differences been so bluntly and publicly exposed between two allies who are drifting further apart. The hasty departure of the Ukrainian president from the White House, apparently asked to leave by his hosts, indicated that a breaking point had been reached. It is difficult to see how this rupture can now be avoided. Since the new direction taken by U.S. diplomacy, which aims to negotiate directly with Moscow for a resolution to this war on terms dictated by Russia, a dispute has been growing between Washington and Kiev, with the main European countries supporting the latter on this issue. This concerns the security guarantees requested from Washington to prevent a fragile peace from turning into a mere respite for the Russian war machine, tested by a conflict it thought it would win in three days, before new offensives aimed at reconstituting what Moscow considers its sphere of influence on European soil. By refusing any commitment on this point, Mr. Trump is increasingly playing into the hands of the Kremlin, adopting, down to the last comma, Moscow's view of the ongoing conflict, including its origins, as shown by the dramatic votes at the United Nations on February 24. During his first term from 2017 to 2021, the Republican consistently embraced the views of the Russian president, regularly praising him. His alignment with Moscow's denials of proven interference in American elections at the Helsinki summit in July 2018 caused consternation, even within his party, which has since become completely silent. Reversal of alliances Outraged by the brutality of Mr. Trump and Mr. Vance on Friday, European leaders quickly expressed their support for Mr. Zelensky, with the notable exception of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who thanked the American president. However, the ambush set for the Ukrainian leader in the Oval Office only confirms their fears: The transatlantic rift is deep, and the alignment of Mr. Trump's discourse with that of Mr. Putin suggests a shift in alliances. Europe is now alone facing Russia. This is a huge challenge, which will dominate discussions when these European leaders meet in London on Sunday, March 2, with British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. He initially invited them to report on his meetings at the White House on Thursday, February 27, three days after President Emmanuel Macron's, and to draw lessons from the new transatlantic situation. But the tone changed radically between the lavish praise Mr. Trump showered on Thursday, apparently eager to divide the British and Europeans, and the public humiliation of the Ukrainian president the following day. For both Mr. Starmer and Mr. Macron, the time is no longer for charm offensives to try to appease Washington, which has yielded little results. It is necessary to stand together, accelerate the schedule for building a strong European defense, and prepare public opinion for the abrupt shift towards a different world.
lead: The violent altercation that occurred between Donald Trump, J.D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House on Friday, February 28, illustrates the hostility of the new American administration towards Ukraine and marks a breaking point in the relationship between the allies.

title: L’Allemagne sous le choc de la perte de l’ami américain
text: Within the grounds of the former Tempelhof airport in Berlin, an old propeller plane commemorates a crucial moment in German history. It recalls the Berlin Airlift (1948-1949), eleven months during which American planes of this type supplied West Berlin, whose communication routes were blocked by the Soviets. These aircraft were affectionately nicknamed Rosinenbomber ("Raisin Bombers") by Berliners, as they dropped thousands of candies over the city for the children until the blockade was lifted. For many Germans, these planes symbolize the friendship and freedom guaranteed by the United States against dictatorship. Did the elders think back to this moving event when they heard J.D. Vance's speech at the Munich Security Conference on February 14, nine days before the legislative elections where the far-right party Alternative for Germany (AfD) is credited with 20% of the votes? In a few sentences, the American vice president trampled on one of the founding pillars of the Federal Republic of Germany: the transatlantic relationship. "The threat that concerns me most regarding Europe is not Russia, not China, not an external actor. It is the threat from within," he declared, accusing Europe of abandoning the values of freedom of expression and democracy, and calling for the abandonment of any cordon sanitaire against far-right groups. In Munich, he met not with the chancellor, Olaf Scholz, but with the AfD president, Alice Weidel, who had already been endorsed in January by Donald Trump's billionaire advisor, Elon Musk. The violent attack by the American president on his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, on Wednesday, February 19, sealed the rupture. For Germany, this represents a deeper turning point than the Russian attack on Ukraine in February 2022, which marked the return of war to Europe and shattered its conception of international trade as a guarantor of stability. The link with the United States is of a different nature. It is a fundamental marker of modern Germany, one that imposed democracy and the rule of law against the Nazi horror. Any leading politician, especially within the Christian Democratic Union (CDU-CSU) party, must reaffirm their commitment to the transatlantic relationship. They may even have been close to one of the associations that embody it, such as the influential Atlantik-Brücke ("Atlantic Bridge"), founded in 1952 and chaired between 2009 and 2019 by a certain Friedrich Merz (CDU), a favorite for the position of chancellor after the elections. The Americans played a major role in the political, economic, and institutional reconstruction of Germany after World War II. They organized the denazification process of institutions and imposed the country's attachment to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). They inspired the major principles of the Basic Law (1949), such as respect for human rights protected by a Supreme Court, the separation and balance of powers, and federalism. They made a significant contribution to the monetary reform of 1948, which introduced the Deutsche Mark. The German "economic miracle" would have been inconceivable without the monetary stability on which the Marshall Plan was implemented. Discredited in terms of military power, West German identity was largely built on the economic and monetary strength resulting from these transformations. Neither a friend nor a model, this identity is marked by very strong memories and bilateral ties. On the town hall of the Berlin district of Schöneberg, a plaque commemorates the day in June 1963 when President John F. Kennedy shouted, "Ich bin ein Berliner" ("I am a Berliner"), in support of the population. These words echo Ronald Reagan's speech in 1987, when he addressed Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev with, "Tear down this wall!", considered a herald of the end of the Cold War and reunification. American intellectual influence on economic theory, especially in the 1990s, is considerable. The prestigious University of Bonn, the Free University of Berlin, and those in Munich developed based on the model of major universities in the United States, which are essential for many high-level careers in research and higher education in Germany. "What we are experiencing is an absolute break. The entire post-World War II order is being buried," notes economist Jens Südekum, a professor at the University of Düsseldorf. "Without America?" read the headline of Die Zeit weekly on its Thursday, February 20 edition. "The Western Schism," declared one of the directors of the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung, the major Frankfurt newspaper, in an editorial on the same day. Germany, which had delegated its defense to Washington and greatly benefited from the "peace dividends" after 1990, is now in dire straits. Chancellor Scholz's restraint on the Ukrainian issue, justified by respect for American precedence on security matters within NATO, is no longer sustainable. In Berlin, the government resulting from the elections on Sunday, February 23, will have to react very quickly. The debt brake, a constitutional rule limiting the public deficit to 0.35% of the country's gross domestic product, on which the outgoing coalition foundered, seems outdated given the enormous expenses required for security. But the most significant change is likely cultural. For the first time in eighty years, America can no longer be considered either a friend or a model. Berlin will have to urgently redefine, with European partners who wish to do so, France at the forefront, the foundations of its strategic autonomy.
lead: Foundation of the reconstruction of West Germany after the Second World War, the country's alliance with the United States had become an almost identitary element for many Germans, who view the geopolitical shifts of the Trump administration as a seismic event.

title: Les partis pro-européens unis sur l’aide à l’Ukraine et la défense européenne, mais divisés sur les moyens
text: Just three months ago, most people imagined him finishing his term in a twilight atmosphere, alone and unloved. Emmanuel Macron's loyal supporters could be counted on one hand, the page seemed almost turned. The geopolitical crisis has reshuffled the deck. At the Palais-Bourbon, now, except in the ranks of La France insoumise (LFI) or the Rassemblement national (RN), few, in mid-March, dare to speak ill of the President of the Republic, even behind the scenes. Macron's troops, proud, praise the accuracy of their leader's European insights when socialist opponents admit that, fundamentally, the president was right. At a time when the world order is being disrupted by the Trump administration, a sort of "sacred union" has formed in the National Assembly to defend, alongside the head of state, a powerful Europe capable of arming and defending itself, alone, without the American ally. "Emmanuel Macron is respected on the international stage. It is fortunate that political forces are coming together to support Ukraine!" comments Roland Lescure, deputy of the Renaissance for French citizens abroad. The gravity of the moment has put a pause on the petty wars within pro-European parties. For how long? Emmanuel Macron's martial speech on March 5, although praised by these temporary allies, brings out nuances. All say they are ready to make the war effort deemed necessary by the president to defend Kyiv and Europe. A European defense must be built, and the military budget in France must be significantly increased. But the question arises of how. Emmanuel Macron, who is not unaware of the state of public finances, took on Churchillian tones during his speech, promising "reforms, choices, courage," all "without increasing taxes," charging the government to make proposals. Five days later, Gilbert Cette, the president of the Retirement Orientation Council, explained in an article published on the site Telos-eu.com that "the gradual, more or less explicit, entry into a wartime economy will make the current debates on the legal retirement age of 64 secondary, if not trivial." For this close associate of the head of state, "the question will rather become (...) how to quickly increase this [age] beyond 64." Citizens' concerns It didn't take much for minds on the left to heat up. "We are not fooled by the way this issue can be instrumentalized by the government," grumbles Laurent Baumel, a socialist deputy. "Our financing proposals involve a wealth tax, taxation, and not a questioning of social spending!" pushes the elected representative from Indre-et-Loire. Despite François Bayrou's words promising, on March 6, to "not abandon any part of the social model that is part of the French identity," the left remains suspicious. "There is a kind of inconsistency on Emmanuel Macron's part in highlighting existential issues to make the French accept - implicitly - cuts in social spending. It would be a moral mistake to make the poorest bear the burden of war finances, but it would also be a golden gift to Putin. This will elect the far right!" exclaims David Cormand, a Green Party MEP. Macronist officials, like those from Horizons, do not hide their desire to restore public finances and to forget about the reconsideration of raising the retirement age to 64, a reform painfully pushed by Emmanuel Macron in 2023. "Whether it's for war efforts or not, we need to work more!" insists Mathieu Lefèvre, a Renaissance deputy from Val-de-Marne, who envisions quickly conducting a strategic review of state spending. "The French are ready to prioritize buying cannons over recycling aids," he suggests. But central bloc officials observe the citizens' concerns in their constituencies. The French support Ukraine, but if the effort becomes too painful, could public opinion not shift? It is important to proceed cautiously to prevent the country from tearing apart. "National unity is not just a slogan, it's a path," thinks Roland Lescure. For many Macronist officials, as well as representatives from Horizons, it is crucial to distinguish between the issue of restoring public finances and that of the war effort. "We must not fall into the trap of the National Rally and France Unbowed by letting them believe that Ukraine is responsible for our deficit," says David Amiel, a Renaissance official from Paris. According to the Macronist, "to build a European defense, European funding is needed," he says, mentioning the Rearming Europe plan proposed by the President of the European Commission, Ursula Von der Leyen, who has put forward a figure of around 800 billion euros. To complement this plan, the idea of involving the wealthiest individuals is ruled out, but why not tap into the savings of the French, for example, suggest some. "The issue of Russian assets will have to be resolved!" Above all, the officials from the "central bloc" want, for a large part of them, to make the main culprit pay: Russia. Following Gabriel Attal's lead, they advocate for seizing Russian assets. A windfall of around 200 billion euros. "This solution would make Russia pay for the war rather than Europe and Europeans, and thus the French," argues the former Prime Minister in a column published in Le Monde on March 5, aware of "the fears and legal reservations" associated with this idea. Initially reluctant to this option, "contrary to international agreements," the executive seems to be softening its position. "It can only be considered within the framework of the European Union," François Bayrou clarified on Tuesday, March 11, during questions to the government in the Assembly without rejecting this idea, while the next day a resolution proposal was to be examined to strengthen aid to Ukraine, inviting to "use" the frozen Russian assets. "The issue of Russian assets will have to be resolved!" pushes Roland Lescure. "People are worried. When the time comes, we will have to be clear and show that national unity is not just words," concludes Frédéric Valletoux, deputy of Horizons de Seine-et-Marne.
lead: The Socialist Party and the central bloc support Emmanuel Macron's proposals for a European defense and support for Kiev. However, while the head of state rules out any tax increases, the issue of defense clashes with that of public finances.

title: Main basse sur le Groenland
text: Donald Trump's interest in Greenland is not new, but it has become an obsession. In a matter of weeks, the American president's claims on this Arctic territory under Danish sovereignty have become one of the most acute points of tension between the United States and Europe, shedding light on Washington's disregard for international law, Europeans, and the transatlantic alliance. Tensions escalated further on Tuesday, March 25, with the announcement of the visit on Friday, March 28, by Vice President J.D. Vance and his wife to the Pituffik air base, maintained by the U.S. forces in Greenland for many years. In a video message posted on the social network X, Mr. Vance stated that the announcement of his wife, Usha's visit, "had generated such excitement" that he decided to join her "to be briefed on Arctic security issues" and "see the state of Greenland's security." In reality, Washington altered the nature of the originally planned visit in response to the backlash it had caused in Denmark and Europe. Mrs. Vance, the vice president's wife, was supposed to go with her son to attend a sled dog championship on Saturday, March 29. Interestingly, she was supposed to be accompanied by the national security adviser, Mike Waltz, and the energy secretary, Chris Wright. Two U.S. military transport planes had already landed in Greenland on Monday to disembark security personnel. The problem is that, contradicting President Trump's claim that this visit was at the request of the Greenland authorities, they quickly made it known that not only had they not invited anyone, as they were busy forming a government following the recent elections, but they also deemed the American visit "very aggressive." Protests were being organized among the population of 56,500 inhabitants. Memories of Crimea Finally, the civilian part of the visit was canceled, Mrs. Vance will not see "this monumental sled dog race" she had looked forward to in a statement announcing her trip and will not "celebrate the unity and culture" of Greenland. However, the delegation's level is high, with the vice president himself participating. This is a way to spare Mrs. Vance and American voters the discomfort of a hostile reception, while maintaining the U.S. claim to control over this territory dependent on the Danish crown. Because that is the crux of the matter. President Trump's regularly used phrasing regarding Greenland leaves little doubt about his intentions: "We really need it for global security," he recently said, "and I think we will get it. One way or another, we will have it." Without any basis, Mr. Vance also refers to Denmark as a "bad ally." The Prime Minister of Denmark, Mette Frederiksen, considers these pressures "unacceptable," and she is right. If Europeans do not understand what Donald Trump means by "one way or another," the memory of the "little green men" sent by Russian President Vladimir Putin in 2014 to take control of Crimea is fresh enough for these threats to be taken seriously. European solidarity with Denmark must be unwavering: allowing Donald Trump to seize control of Greenland would be a fundamental mistake.
lead: The visit of the American Vice President, J.D. Vance, to the Danish territory on Friday, March 18, will be limited to the American military base of Pituffik, but President Donald Trump's declared ambition to take control of Greenland is unacceptable.

title: En Allemagne, la question de l’aide à l’Ukraine s’invite dans la campagne électorale
text: While it had been at the heart of the regional elections in September 2024 in eastern Germany, the war in Ukraine had so far been relegated to the background of the campaign for the legislative elections on February 23, overshadowed by two other themes: immigration and the economy. The issue has resurfaced a month before the election, highlighting the depth of the divisions it generates, including within the government. A persistent quarrel pits Social Democratic Chancellor Olaf Scholz (SPD) against his defense minister, Boris Pistorius, and foreign affairs minister, Annalena Baerbock (Greens), over a budget increase of 3 billion euros destined for Ukraine. Requested by the two ministers, this funding is blocked by the chancellery. "Germany is not currently seen as a driving force for peace in Europe, and honestly, it saddens me," Ms. Baerbock told Politico on Friday, January 17. "In the context of the campaign, some prioritize a national perspective, wondering how to quickly gain votes in the legislative elections, rather than assuming real responsibility to ensure peace and freedom in Europe," she added, without naming the chancellor. "The 'chancellor of peace'" On his part, Olaf Scholz justified his position by the risks posed by an "unfunded budget deficit of 25 billion euros," unless the constitutional rule restricting debt is lifted. A reform in favor of which he pleaded and which led to the rupture of his coalition with the departure of the liberal-democrat ministers in November 2024. "The orders of magnitude are not the same," argues Christian Mölling, director of the program on the future of Europe at the Bertelsmann Foundation. "It's about 3 billion euros for Ukraine, compared to several hundred billion for social benefits." The conservative candidate (CDU-CSU) for the chancellorship, Friedrich Merz, indicated that Ms. Baerbock and Mr. Pistorius had called him to ask for the CDU's support in the Bundestag to push through this issue. They now accuse each other of campaigning at the expense of Ukraine. Running for re-election, Olaf Scholz has centered his campaign on the promise of not sacrificing any of his priorities despite constrained budgetary means, facing a CDU-CSU that, on the contrary, intends to finance defense, aid to Ukraine, or investments in infrastructure by cutting social expenses or "bureaucracy." Presenting himself as the "chancellor of peace," the only one capable of avoiding a military escalation in Ukraine, facing a Friedrich Merz portrayed as a warmonger, Mr. Scholz counters the criticisms by pointing out that Germany is the second supporter of Kiev behind the United States. According to the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, Germany has spent nearly 15.7 billion euros to support Ukraine since 2022, which effectively places it as the second largest contributor globally, but only ranks 17th when this amount is converted into a share of its national wealth, 0.39% of its gross domestic product (GDP). "Olaf Scholz is doing the opposite of what he said, namely playing aid to Ukraine against issues such as funding for municipalities or pensions," comments Claudia Major, a security expert at the German Marshall Fund, pointing to the complex historical relationship that the SPD (Social Democratic Party) has with Russia, still seen as the power that liberated the country from Nazism. "Putin uses this idea of historical debt very well, when he mentions the potential presence of German tanks in his speeches," emphasizes the researcher. It seems that the idea that Russia could lose is considered by the Chancellery as a very great risk to the stability of Europe, greater than the risks associated with a Ukrainian defeat. In the minds of many Social Democrats, Russia will always be there. It cannot be defeated, so we must come to terms with it." "Raising the voice against Putin" The country itself is divided on the subject: opinion polls show that a majority of Germans support aid to Ukraine, but not the delivery of Taurus cruise missiles, which would allow Kiev to strike deep into Russian territory, and to which Olaf Scholz has so far always been opposed. The far-right party Alternative for Germany (AfD), credited with 21% of the vote intentions, as well as the new left-wing party Alliance Sahra Wagenknecht (between 4% and 7%), both well-established in the Eastern states, call for the interruption of arms deliveries to Ukraine. The Greens, on the other hand, do not hesitate to go against their historically pacifist doctrine, and advocate for increased support to Kiev, as well as for the delivery of Taurus missiles. The Green Party's candidate for the Chancellery and current Minister of Economy, Robert Habeck, goes even further than the CDU-CSU on defense matters, advocating for Germany to increase its contribution to NATO to 3.5% of its GDP, up from the current 2.1%. Such an ambition would require a considerable budgetary effort. Today, Germany meets NATO's 2% target for its members thanks to a special fund of 100 billion euros adopted at Olaf Scholz's initiative following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. However, this fund will expire in 2027, so the annual defense budget, which currently stands at around 50 billion euros, will need to increase by at least 30 billion per year for Germany to continue to meet NATO's threshold after that date. "The figure of 3.5% includes security in a broad sense, both internal and external, and not just military aspects, explains the co-president of the Greens, Franziska Brantner, to Le Monde. With accidents on undersea cables and drone flyovers, hybrid warfare is already very real for all of us." The Greens are the party of human rights, she continues, which sometimes requires "raising our voices against people like Vladimir Putin." Regarding Ukraine, there are real convergences between the Greens and the CDU-CSU. Could this alignment lead these two political forces to form a government after the elections on February 23? Such a scenario is regularly discussed. However, it must be arithmetically possible. In terms of voting intentions, the conservatives are credited with 30% to 31% of the votes, but the Greens with only 13% to 15%, which seems a bit insufficient to form a majority. With one month left until the election, the CDU-CSU might lean towards the SPD, which fluctuates between 14% and 18% in the latest polls, to form a coalition.
lead: Running for re-election, the Social Democrat Olaf Scholz wants to avoid any military escalation with Russia. Members of his coalition, the Greens, on the other hand, advocate for increased support for the forces in Kiev. These positions are also shared by the conservatives of the CDU-CSU and their leader, Friedrich Merz, who is leading in the polls.

title: Dix-neuf projets français choisis pour aider l’Ukraine à panser ses plaies
text: History repeats itself. In 1915, Marie Curie went on a tour of wealthy Parisian women she knew and, with a smile, convinced them to donate their cars in order to equip them with X-ray machines. The objective was to locate shrapnel without disturbing the wounded too much, as close as possible to the trenches. Eighteen limousines were transformed into radiological ambulances. Today, one hundred and ten years later, a similar operation is being prepared between France and Ukraine. DMS, a large French SME, will be sending 120 mobile radiology units to Kiev. "It's the digital version of the 'Little Curies'," notes its CEO, Samuel Sancerni. These small devices can be used in trucks, hospitals near or far from the front lines, schools, for emergency radiography. They will be manufactured in our factory in Gard, in Gallargues-le-Montueux. We are currently setting up the assembly line." First deliveries are expected this summer. This time, it is not wealthy socialites like Princess Murat making the venture possible, but the French government, through a donation of 11 million euros. This is not a unique case. In order to help Ukraine heal its wounds and assist French companies in establishing themselves in a country where much needs to be done, France announced in June 2024 the establishment of a fund with 200 million euros, and opened a call for applications. "I was told, 'No company will want to go to a war zone!'" recounts Pierre Heilbronn, the special envoy of the French President, Emmanuel Macron, for the reconstruction of Ukraine. In fact, we had over 70 applicants." After a review of the applications by French authorities and a selection of the most useful projects in the short term by the government of Kiev, 19 projects were selected and officially announced on Friday, March 7th, during a ceremony at Bercy. They align with Ukraine's current priorities: health, demining, energy, water, and infrastructure. Each project will receive public investment ranging from 3 million to 15 million euros. Promoting French presence on site Among the winners are several projects led by national champions. EDF will modernize electricity distribution substations, Schneider will renovate public electrical equipment in Kharkiv, Saint-Gobain will install a pipeline to supply water to Kryvy Rih, the hometown of President Volodymyr Zelensky. However, most of the projects come from smaller companies, some of which had never set foot in Ukraine, such as DMS, as well as Shark Robotics (firefighting robots) and Exail (demining equipment). The list also includes the American company General Electric, but for products that will be manufactured in France. In each case, four criteria had to be met: A direct interest in the reconstruction of Ukraine, a deployment within a maximum of sixteen months, a French grant capped at 15 million euros, a minimum added value of 50% to be achieved on French territory. "It is an unprecedented initiative that meets the needs of a country at war," says Pierre Heilbronn. "Usually, we rely on loans. Here, it involves grants." A well-organized charity. It must help Ukraine stand firm despite daily Russian strikes and is part of European support for the country. But the objective also aims to promote French presence on the ground, beyond major projects such as that of Xavier Niel (an individual shareholder of Le Monde Group) in the telecom sector or that of Orano (formerly Areva), revealed on Thursday, to provide uranium enrichment services to the nuclear operator Energoatom. "Ukraine has a functioning economy, and in the long term, it will be a huge market," promises Pierre Heilbronn.
lead: Paris will subsidize the installation of water pipes, demining equipment, and various infrastructure projects to the tune of 200 million euros. Among the selected companies are EDF, Saint-Gobain, small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as General Electric.

title: « L’Europe doit exploiter les divisions latentes à l’intérieur de la triple alliance qui se dessine entre les Etats-Unis, la Russie et la Chine »
text: The international system is currently marked by such instability that it is difficult to discern the exact direction in which the upheavals affecting it are pointing. Nevertheless, two scenarios stand out above the others. The first favors the hypothesis of a temporary coalition between the United States and Russia. This "duumvirate" would allow the former to settle the European question in such a way that they could then concentrate their efforts on the power they consider to be the most threatening, China. Consequently, China would have "nothing to lose by waiting": the concessions made by the United States to Russia would have the effect of detaching Moscow from its current dependence on Beijing, and would enable Washington to better target its main adversary. The second scenario is that of a "tripartite", an alliance between the three major powers, which would last long enough to satisfy the primary objectives of each partner: Greenland and Panama for you, maybe even Canada; Ukraine, the Baltic countries, control of Hungary, and a few others for me; Taiwan and the South China Sea for them... This is now referred to in Washington as "diplomatic realism", or even "hard realism". The meeting of the "three emperors" in Moscow, which Trump has mentioned as a possibility for May 9, would formalize the birth of a new "triple alliance". Of course, the second scenario is so risky for each of the actors that one hesitates to believe in its credibility. But several factors compel us to take it very seriously. Irredentist obsession The three powers demonstrate a surprising ideological consensus. It is not only the Trump team that promotes masculinism and advocates for a moral counter-revolution: their Russian and Chinese counterparts do as well. The disdain expressed for the democratic model is also shared, as is the model of a "scientific" policy entrusted to a meritocracy selected by business, relationships, the rigorous selective process developed by the party-state, or simply by a mix of cynicism and audacity. Finally, each one emphasizes the threat that the other (any other) represents to the political order. Furthermore, the territories coveted by these empires are of such importance to them that they are willing to make concessions, the value of which they would better assess if the irredentist obsession or simply expansionist tendencies did not blind them to such an extent. The division of the world that would emerge if this triple alliance were to materialize would be extraordinarily fragile. The issue of Taiwan raises questions about control over the entire Pacific. Similarly, allowing Russia to control part of its former empire would only encourage it to seek control over the entirety. In the American case, consenting to the appetites that are now crudely expressed can only lead to an immediate intensification of those appetites. Furthermore, the interests of potential partners in the Middle East, Africa, and even Latin America are immediately contradictory, even if a diplomatic logic based on transactions from start to finish could extend these operations of division on a global scale. In any case, Europe is the primary target: both Russia and the United States spare no disdainful expression to refer to it as "the sick man of the global system," so to speak. Europe is not only the primary target but also the first to be divided on how to react. Let us summarize what should now be seen as obvious: the countries of the European Union (EU), along with the United Kingdom, must isolate those among them who hinder their determination; they must arm themselves economically, militarily, and psychologically to ensure their survival, which depends on that of their neighbors; any complacency towards the divisions fostered by Moscow and now by Washington is akin to a form of collaboration; the fundamentals, both productive and strategic, must be reestablished following the model of a wartime economy. Strategy of circumvention It is also up to Europe to exploit the latent divisions within the emerging triad. The contradictions resulting from any agreement between Russia and the United States must be brought to light, and the consequences of surrendering, even partially, to Putin's Russia must be made explicit. In contrast, a negotiation space is most clearly opening up with China. The latter would have much to fear if the triad were ultimately revealed to be a duad, once an agreement between the United States and Russia is reached. Furthermore, while China has regularly attempted to divide the European front, it has refrained, for example, from courting far-right parties, in contrast to Russian and American interference. Finally, Beijing has proven to be a more reliable actor than the other two on global issues, particularly climate-related ones. There is a negotiation space, a possibility to identify common interests that are still largely theoretical. From China's perspective, this requires making the EU its primary partner rather than addressing each individual country within it first. It is necessary to prioritize the dangers, with the primary one now being the formation of the Washington-Moscow axis. Therefore, it is essential to pursue a strategy of circumvention, combined with a policy of redefining European strategies with India, Africa, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. The defensive position in which Europe finds itself calls for a very extensive diplomatic offensive. Faced with a rapidly developing triple alliance, we must outline a new agreement to counter a coalition whose potential consolidation threatens our interests, our values, and even our political framework.
