lead: United by a common rejection of democracy and their expansionist ambitions, the three powers could temporarily come to an agreement to carry out a world division that is as illegitimate as it is fragile, warns sinologist Benoît Vermander, a Jesuit particularly knowledgeable about the relations between the Vatican and Beijing, in an article in "Le Monde".

title: Le retour au front du soldat ukrainien Serhi Hnezdilov, entre amertume et satisfaction
text: I'm sorry, I couldn't detect the language you used. Could you please provide the text you'd like me to translate into English?
lead: I'm sorry, but it seems like the text you provided is not in a language that I can recognize or translate. If you can provide the text in a different language, I'd be happy to help translate it for you.

title: Au Royaume-Uni, les pilotes ukrainiens continuent de s’initier au pilotage des F-16 malgré l’incertitude sur les négociations
text: On this icy Monday in February, at this air base in the East Midlands, the dozen Grob Tutors, small German-made training aircraft used by the Royal Air Force (RAF) to train its pilots, remained neatly lined up on the ground: the cloud ceiling was too low to fly. The Ukrainian fighter pilot trainees being trained by the British are busy with other tasks. In a study room, young men with youthful faces and in pilot suits are fine-tuning flight plans on large topographic maps. In total, there are about ten of them, forming the fourth cohort of Ukrainian military personnel being trained by the RAF as part of the Interstorm program, an international effort launched in early 2023 by the British and the Dutch, organized at the NATO summit in Vilnius in July 2023, to train Ukrainians in flying American F-16 fighter jets. The Belgians, Danes, Romanians, Americans, French, and Portuguese are also involved. Since the British do not have F-16s, they have taken charge of the first phase of training: learning the basics of navigation and flying fighter aircraft, as well as learning English. Le Monde and four other European media outlets were granted access to these pilot trainees, but were unable to disclose their names or specify the base where they are being trained for security reasons. These young men were selected by the Ukrainian military after completing four years at their air school, and have already had some experience flying Soviet-type aircraft (Czech-made L-39s). For six months in the UK, they receive additional theoretical training to familiarize themselves with the standards of flying or air traffic control specific to the Atlantic Alliance. Their training also includes intensive English classes and sixty hours of flight on the Grob Tutor aircraft. "Soviet-era aircraft" "Compared to Soviet fighter aircraft, the instrument layout in the cockpit is different, and we don't read the altimeter the same way either. In Soviet aircraft, speeds are in meters per second, here they are in miles per second or knots," remarks Yakiv, one of the young pilot trainees (all names have been changed), in already fluent English. "Our aircraft are old, they are from the Soviet era [they are Russian Sukhoi Su-27s], it is becoming difficult to maintain them," explains his comrade Ivan. "We need to transition to the F-16s quickly because we have little time, and we also need to contribute to renewing our practices, keeping the best of the old systems and the best of the new." "Instruction is provided by voluntary reservists or serving instructors, at no additional cost to the RAF, but each flying hour for a Ukrainian student is one less flying hour for pilot students selected from British universities by the RAF. Already, 24 Ukrainians have gone through this initial training in the UK. The British military hopes to train 20 per year. The apprentice pilots will then travel to France in spring 2025, where they will continue their training for six months on faster aircraft (Alpha Jets, also used by the Patrouille de France). They will then go to Romania to undergo actual F-16 pilot training. The total duration of their training is eighteen months – shortened by nearly a year compared to the usual two and a half to three years for F-16 pilot training. The idea is obviously to prepare young pilots for combat as quickly as possible, as Ukraine has received nearly 70 F-16 aircraft (donated by Denmark, the Netherlands, or Greece). Further reducing training seems impossible, unless the annual visit of apprentice pilots to their families is eliminated. Additionally, the upcoming cohorts expected by the UK may have even less flight experience than those who have arrived so far, as the Ukrainian military struggles to train pilots. 'Better protect our borders' The student pilots are ready to defend their country: 'When we see in the videos what the Russians are doing to our people, it drives us crazy. We must protect our nation, our land, our culture,' says Ivan. 'The F-16s will allow us to hunt down enemy aircraft more effectively; they have better radars and will help stop long-range missiles. Our big problem is these missiles, drones that come from the sky and attack our power plants, our infrastructure. The F-16s will help better protect our borders, but that alone will not be enough to win the war.' What will happen to the Interstorm program if Donald Trump pushes for negotiations with President Putin? Its duration is not defined, and it could continue even after a ceasefire in Ukraine, as this training was also designed to bring the Ukrainian army up to NATO standards. The UK, which has also trained over 30,000 soldiers of the Ukrainian army (under Operation 'Interflex'), has spent a total of £12.8 billion (€15.35 billion) for Ukraine (£7.8 billion in military support and £5 billion in financial support). The British government, which allocates 2.3% of GDP to defense (and aims to increase to 2.5%), will support Ukraine 'for as long as necessary,' says John Healey, the defense minister of the Starmer government."
lead: For six months, the group of soldiers from Ukraine will be trained by the Royal Air Force, who will teach them the basics of navigation and flight on combat aircraft, as well as the fundamentals of English as part of the Interstorm program.

title: La Commission européenne dévoile de premières solutions pour financer le réarmement du continent
text: The 27 heads of state and government had been calling for it since the beginning of 2024, Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the European Commission, finally unveiled on Tuesday, March 4, two days before an extraordinary summit dedicated to the subject, her proposal to "rearm Europe." This plan should allow states to spend up to an additional 800 billion euros on their defense in the next five years, according to the very optimistic calculations of the Commission. These resources will help accelerate Europe's reinvestment in its military capabilities, but also support Ukraine in its fight against Vladimir Putin's Russia, especially as Donald Trump has announced the freezing of American military aid to Kiev and is now clearly and deliberately turning away from Europe. "Europe is facing a clear and immediate danger of a magnitude that none of us has experienced in our adult lives," Ursula von der Leyen assures in a letter sent to the leaders of the continent. From now on, "Europe must be responsible for its own deterrence and defense," she declares. This will require a lot of resources, as European leaders continue to emphasize, whose budgets are already difficult to balance. While the Twenty-Seven have already increased their military spending by 30% since 2021, allocating 320 billion euros to their defense in 2024, the Commission is proposing an additional 150 billion euros in aid in the coming years. The former German defense minister proposes the creation, through an emergency procedure provided for in the treaties, of a new fund, Rearm, to lend up to 150 billion euros to states for their military expenses. The EU budget will allow the necessary funds to be raised on the markets, guaranteed, and lent to states over the long term. They will then have to repay them. Thanks to this mechanism, around twenty EU countries will benefit from a much better interest rate than if they borrowed individually, according to the European executive. Poland would have preferred the implementation of subsidies, but most member states welcome this provision as consensual. "Finally, concrete measures are being taken to build an essential European defense," praised Antonio Tajani, the Italian Minister of Foreign Affairs. This is an "important first step," praised his German counterpart, Annalena Baerbock. The European Parliament's mixed reception In contrast to a common European loan, advocated by France but also by many Northern European states, this formula allows the EU to take the lead and does not require frugal states, notably Germany and the Netherlands, to pay for others. "We can live with this type of financing," confirms a representative of a frugal country. In any case, another diplomat adds, "as long as Friedrich Merz is not in charge in Berlin, the possibility of a loan could not have been on the table." In the European Parliament, the reception is more mixed. While conservatives welcome the initiative, social democrats regret the scale of the financing. "This plan is still very vague," notes French MEP (Place publique) Aurore Lalucq. "Instead of national loans, we need to move towards a common debt." "We must not (...) encourage fragmentation or buy American weapons. European money should go towards a truly European defense strategy," warns German MEP (Greens) Daniel Freund. While the European Defence Agency - as well as the Atlantic Alliance - has identified the "capability gaps" in European armies, the Commission proposes to use these diagnostics to guide the use of these 150 billion euros towards a dozen priority areas such as missiles, ammunition, drones, military mobility, or cyber. "The impact will be greater," explains Ursula von der Leyen, "if states make joint purchases, ensuring interoperability and predictability of equipment, reducing costs, and creating economies of scale to strengthen the European defense industrial base." But questions are already arising. Will this new instrument be reserved for the purchase of only European equipment, or will it be able to finance equipment from outside the EU? "Ursula von der Leyen did not mention it," worries French MEP (Renew Europe) Nathalie Loiseau, concerned about defending the European preference. The Commission assures that loans will be granted to those who intend to jointly purchase equipment - with at least three EU countries, or two EU countries and Ukraine. However, it has not yet established the exact eligibility criteria, a subject that has divided the French, Germans, Dutch, and Poles for over a year in the negotiation of another European program dedicated to defense. Using European savings to finance defense efforts Beyond loans, the Commission also allows EU member states to deviate from the stability pact for their defense expenses. Today, in case of exceeding the 3% of GDP deficit limit, states face excessive deficit procedures. Tomorrow, capitals will be able to exclude their defense investments and current expenses from the calculation of their budget deficit. This announcement was particularly awaited by the most lagging countries, such as Italy and Spain. "If member states were to increase their defense spending by 1.5 percentage points of GDP on average, this could create a budget margin of nearly 650 billion euros over a four-year period," explains the Commission president. Between the 150 billion euros in loans and the 650 billion euros in budget flexibility, Europe could theoretically invest an additional 800 billion euros in its defense. However, achieving these figures will depend on the ability of the Member States to allocate resources for their defense. In her Rearm plan, Ursula von der Leyen also proposes that EU Member States, if they wish, redirect a portion of their cohesion funds or use other recently launched instruments to invest in defense. Likewise, Brussels is calling on the European Investment Bank to play a larger role in supporting the defense industrial base by expanding its loans to industry players, and is asking Member States to improve the union of savings and capital in order to use European savings to finance defense efforts. By putting forward these consensus options, the Commission has played it safe and deliberately set aside more disruptive ideas. In addition to the proposed European joint borrowing, which many capitals are not yet ready for, the Commission has also avoided discussing the idea of confiscating the approximately 210 billion euros in frozen Russian assets, which is the subject of intense debate both in Brussels and in the capitals. This debate is progressing in the right direction, according to a diplomat who supports this confiscation. Similarly, the Commission has not taken a position on the proposed rearmament bank, advocated for example by Poland, which several researchers support to accompany the revival of military investments at the EU and wider European level, including the UK. "For now, the Commission wanted to propose quick solutions that can be implemented," says a diplomat. "Questions of longer-term financing can be addressed at a later stage."
lead: The President of the Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, has proposed 150 billion euros in subsidized loans to Member States to strengthen their defense and allows States to redirect European funds towards this priority, while giving them flexibility in terms of deficits.

title: En Ukraine, la colère et l’angoisse après « l’humiliation » subie par Volodymyr Zelensky à la Maison Blanche
text: Anger, anxiety, sadness. The remarks made by the American president towards Ukraine and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28, have caused outrage and horror in the war-torn country. A torrent of emotions that Ukrainians, whether civilians at home or soldiers on the front lines, shared massively on Telegram channels and social media throughout a night marked by numerous Russian bombings on several cities. The shock was even more significant as many, in recent days, had hoped for a rapprochement between Volodymyr Zelensky and Donald Trump, even though the latter had announced on February 12 the opening of negotiations with Russia to end the conflict, without including Kiev. The relationship between the two men seemed to be heading towards a reconciliation, after the promise of an agreement on the joint exploitation of Ukrainian natural resources, the initial version of which, very disadvantageous for Kiev, had been rejected at the risk of frustrating the American president. On the eve of this meeting with his counterpart, Donald Trump had even used kind words towards the Ukrainian president on Thursday, February 27, denying ever calling him a "dictator" - which he had done a week earlier - and expressing his "respect" for "the best president of Ukraine." And then everything changed. Suddenly, abruptly, violently, from the first handshake between Volodymyr Zelensky and Donald Trump, with the latter making an ironic comment about the Ukrainian president's black military attire worn during the three-year war. Ukrainian television channels obviously shared the astonishing images of the altercation between Donald Trump, his vice president J.D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky. The scene caused a shockwave. "While we mourn our dead, we watch American leaders humiliate Ukraine through our president by treating him as a subordinate rather than an equal partner," activist Mariam Naiem lamented on Instagram. "I didn't vote for Zelensky, but I fully support him today," former deputy defense minister Vitaliy Deynega wrote. "Today, I was not ashamed of the president and the country. I'm not sure Americans can say the same." Many similar messages of support for the head of state were posted on social media. However, political scientist Igor Chalenko expects that some of the opposition will denounce Volodymyr Zelensky's "lack of diplomatic finesse" during this crucial meeting for the war-torn country. "Allies do not behave like this." The burial of an American soldier who fought with the Ukrainian army on February 28, 2025 in Kiev. EFREM LUKATSKY / AP "What do you want me to tell you?" says an officer who has been fighting since 2014 and the start of the Donbass war on the phone. Yes, it's very tough, and it's a tragedy for the whole world. But that won't stop us from continuing to fight to defend our children, our homes, and our country." "American leaders have shown disrespect to Ukrainians and their fight for freedom," says Igor Chalenko. That's how the situation is perceived in Ukraine." "Allies don't behave like that, especially not in public," writes Vitaly Sych, the editor-in-chief of Nv.ua, on Facebook. "It was a disgusting attempt to strike at the president of a country at war using all possible weaknesses. It's simply unworthy of America." "They provoked Zelensky and Zelensky couldn't not react," says political expert and teacher Oleksiy Haran. "It was a trap," says former diplomat and international relations expert at the Center for Defense Strategies, Oleksandr Khara. "Ukraine is doomed to be blamed for its 'lack of willingness' to seek peace," adds Mr. Khara, certain that this latest meeting is just confirmation of the White House's line to achieve a ceasefire in the country. In recent weeks, while sidelining Kiev, the U.S. administration has continued to increase contacts with the Kremlin, aligning with Russian talking points, even voting alongside Russia, Belarus, and North Korea against a resolution prepared by Ukraine at the UN on Monday, February 24. The Ukrainian president had gone to Washington hoping to get clarification on American aid and to discuss security guarantees. He leaves with even greater uncertainty about American support. "We will fight and seek solutions," said Vitaly Sych, editor-in-chief of Nv.ua, on Facebook. "That's what we've been doing for three years now. We will find these paths with the Americans, or we will find them with someone else."
lead: Social networks and Ukrainian media support the president, but the opposition could benefit from the failure of his trip to Washington.

title: Pourquoi le gel par Trump de l’aide internationale sidère le monde et provoque déjà des dégâts dévastateurs
text: Among the flurry of decrees that Donald Trump signed on January 20, the day of his arrival at the White House, one is already causing a devastating shockwave. The American president ordered the suspension of all U.S. foreign aid programs for a period of 90 days, with the exception of emergency food aid, as well as military assistance to Israel and Egypt. This decision, as radical as it is controversial, has three objectives: the reassessment of current programs, the alignment of personnel according to ideological criteria, and the drastic reduction of expenses. Since coming into effect, the presidential decree has sown chaos and panic worldwide, especially in the most vulnerable countries, which are heavily dependent on U.S. assistance, and among American personnel employed in numerous missions, who are facing the threat of dismissal. The shock is commensurate with the weight of the United States in terms of foreign aid. Present in 158 countries through USAID, the U.S. agency responsible for development assistance, they are by far the largest global provider. In 2023, the total amount of U.S. foreign aid reached $64.7 billion (€62.4 billion), according to the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), representing approximately 1% of the U.S. federal budget. Humanitarian aid is at the forefront (25%), followed by capacity building for governments and civil societies (20%), and then health (12%). Nathaniel Raymond, executive director of the Humanitarian Research Lab at the Yale School of Public Health, operating in conflict-affected countries, stated, "The global aid system collapsed on Monday, January 20, because of this decree. What is happening is unprecedented. Various programs had already been defunded during Trump's first term [2017-2021], but never had the entire system been interrupted as it is today." The impact of the decree was immediate: multiple aid operations in crisis zones, from Ukraine to Afghanistan to Sudan, were halted. On January 24, the U.S. State Department sent a threatening letter to all recipients of American aid. "Upon receipt of this suspension notice, the recipient must cease all work under the grants and incur no new expenses," reads the letter seen by Le Monde. "Any use of funds to promote diversity, equity, and inclusion must stop," orders the State Department. An internal memo sent to USAID employees shortly after, also obtained by Le Monde, is equally blunt: "Our president is committed to pursuing an 'America First' policy." The suspension of all foreign assistance means a complete halt (...). Failure to comply with this directive or any directive sent this week and in the coming weeks will result in disciplinary procedures." Faced with the outcry within humanitarian organizations, the new U.S. Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, had to urgently expand the list of exemptions on Tuesday, January 28. From now on, the freeze no longer applies to "essential medicines for survival, medical services, food, shelter, and livelihood support." However, the wording and scope of these exemptions are so vague that their ambiguity has only increased confusion. Everywhere, organizations and operations funded by the United States must already accelerate the pace of layoffs, furloughs, and program closures. "Millions of people are threatened and will be affected in different ways," warns Mr. Raymond from the Humanitarian Research Lab. Many people will lose their jobs. Organizations will be poor and weakened. But, above all, in the meantime, the most vulnerable people on the planet will suffer even more." Despite the magnitude of the fallout, an eerie silence prevails. The thousands of non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and structures affected by the freeze of U.S. aid are keeping a low profile, refusing to respond or communicating sparingly, careful not to publicly criticize Donald Trump's decision for fear of reprisals. "Everyone is working behind the scenes to plead their case with the Americans and obtain exemptions. We maintain a cautious silence to avoid jeopardizing negotiations," explains a member of a large international NGO, speaking anonymously. Ukraine and Africa on the front line Testimonies gathered by Le Monde describe a toxic atmosphere marked by fear of a "witch hunt." When some dare to express their concerns, it is almost always under the cloak of anonymity. "Organizations believe there is still a chance to continue receiving funds after the 90-day suspension. They are afraid to come out, to become a target and lose everything," explains another well-informed source close to these structures. Ukraine is particularly concerned as it is by far the largest beneficiary of U.S. assistance due to the war with Russia. While military aid provided by the Pentagon does not seem affected, this is not the case for programs funded by the State Department. Numerous projects, support for veterans or media assistance, are already at risk. Even demining activities are threatened, despite Ukraine being the most mined country in the world today. However, so far, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has downplayed the impact of Donald Trump's announcements in order not to compromise the working relationship between the two leaders. Africa is also on the front line. Six countries - Ethiopia, South Sudan, Nigeria, Uganda, Kenya, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) - are among the top ten recipients of US aid. For each of them, American assistance makes up more than half of the foreign aid received. In 2022, it accounted for 35% of the public development assistance received by the entire African continent, according to the latest available figures published by the OECD. Puzzle of programs The response to humanitarian crises in Africa absorbs a large portion of American funds, granted in the form of grants to UN agencies and often American NGOs. These emergency programs are supposed to be, at least in part, exempt from the 90-day freeze, in accordance with exemptions outlined by Mr. Rubio on January 28. Yet, uncertainty persists. On the ground, organizations struggle even more to see clearly as the Trump administration has placed around sixty senior USAID officials on leave, suspected of helping aid recipients bypass the freeze. The complexity of funding and the multiplicity of programs, often hybrid themselves, make the task even more challenging. "The crises we are trying to address are becoming increasingly complex. It is difficult to draw a clear line between humanitarian and development aid. The decision partly lies with the person in Washington responsible for sorting through the expenses," notes Anne Bideau, director of the French office of the NGO Plan International, dedicated to promoting children's rights. For example, food assistance, which is supposed to be exempt, is also a puzzle. Nearly 40% of operations by the World Food Programme involve payments through mobile applications, rather than physical food delivery. In other cases, it may involve complementary foods or sometimes vitamins. Does the exemption apply to all these aids or only a portion of them? The actors are in suspense. "We have not received any information yet," testifies (anonymously) the representative of Rebuild Hope for Africa, which, since October 2024 and thanks to exclusive funding from USAID, distributes equipment to provide clean water to populations in eastern DRC displaced by conflicts. The program targets 100,000 beneficiaries. The early warning platform for famine risks, Fews Net, an essential tool used by all humanitarians and funded by USAID, is currently inactive, even though it aggregates all information regarding food security. "Worse than the Taliban's anti-women decrees" South Sudan, which receives 62% of its foreign aid from the United States, is also plunged into uncertainty. The fate of one million Sudanese who have fled the civil war could suffer from the American freeze. Several hundred thousand South Sudanese returnees and Sudanese refugees are still settled in the Renk area, in Upper Nile. The French NGO Solidarités International has suspended 80% to 90% of its Usaid-funded water and sanitation assistance operations in the area, but continues to provide clean water to over 187,000 people in Renk. "We cannot stop, not even for a few days," says Kevin Goldberg, general director of Solidarités International. Despite the latest exemptions, he finds the situation still confusing and does not know if the activities not suspended will be covered by American funds. In Afghanistan, the third largest beneficiary of American aid, the announcement of the suspension of American aid has shocked the humanitarian world, on which the Taliban regime relies to run the country. Authorities have stated that 31 Afghan and foreign NGOs have already had to cease their activities, calling for "not politicizing humanitarian aid." These organizations worked in the fields of health, agriculture, education, social services, disaster response, food, and refugee support. The World Food Programme stated that such a reduction in support could push a third of the Afghan population into a severe famine situation. Programs launched after the ban on Afghan women over 12 years old accessing education, imposed in 2022, are directly affected by this decision. The Usaid's Girl's Education Challenge program alone had, in 2023, enrolled 5,100 students in 188 private community schools and accelerated learning classes, a precarious system dependent on foreign financial aid. According to the NGO #AfghanEvac, the American decree also affects over 40,000 Afghan applicants for departure, including former soldiers and officials whose applications had already been approved, and others whose requests were under review. "This decision by Donald Trump is even more harmful than the Taliban leader's anti-women decrees," says one of the officials from a major international NGO, speaking anonymously from Kabul. Back to the 1980s-1990s, the activities of NGOs running programs to combat gender-based violence, promote racial or sexual minority inclusion, or more broadly, promote diversity are in the sights of the Trump administration. In Haiti, "we had to lay off 40% of our staff who were working directly thanks to this funding," said Nathalie Vilgrain, general coordinator of the feminist organization Marijàn. "The choices being made at the moment are of extreme seriousness for generations of girls," denounced Mrs. Bideau, from the NGO Plan International, describing a general context marked by a decline in development aid from most donors, starting with France. The fight against AIDS is also at risk due to Donald Trump's decree. According to the Amfar, an American foundation against AIDS, more than 20 million people worldwide depend on treatments funded by the Pepfar program established by the United States in 2003. This represents over 222,000 people treated each day, including 7,745 children under 15 years old. However, the latest exemptions on life-saving drugs remain unclear and do not explicitly mention the Pepfar program to date. The freeze, "if prolonged, could lead to an increase in new infections and deaths, undoing decades of progress and risking a return to the 1980s-1990s, when millions of people were dying each year from HIV worldwide," including in the United States, warned Amfar. Aid, a symbol of waste The consequences of the suspension of American aid are also of a security nature. In Syria, Mr. Trump's decision destabilized the organization of prisons and camps holding former jihadists of the Islamic State organization and their families, totaling 50,000 people, settled in Rojava, a territory located in the northeast of the country and administered by the Kurds. The situation was chaotic there for several days. Some guards initially stopped working. According to information from Le Monde, they have since returned to their posts on Tuesday, January 28, after NGOs active in the area obtained an extension of funding for two weeks. However, essential services within the camps remain affected, according to a security source from the Al-Hol refugee camp (North). "International NGOs now have two weeks to justify why they should continue to secure the detention of around 50,000 ISIS [Arabic acronym for the Islamic State organization] fighters and their families," the same source warned. In the United States, among conservative circles, foreign aid has long been considered a symbol of waste and unnecessary programs. On January 22, Marco Rubio defined his country's foreign policy in this way. "Every dollar we spend, every program we fund, every policy we implement, must be justified by an answer to three simple questions. Does it make America safer? "Does it make America stronger? Does it make America more prosperous?" Preventing diseases, fighting malnutrition, or clearing landmines in conflict zones do not, in themselves, make America more "prosperous." But these actions contribute to its status as a great power, to its famous soft power. "If the president claims to want to serve the interests of the United States, he should understand that such an approach is really not in the country's interest," denounces Sarah Yager, director of the Human Rights Watch office in Washington. "This suspension is causing so much suffering and anger around the world!" However, "these programs give the United States a lot of influence, while also bringing stability to ease conflicts and minimize their impact on populations. Suspending them, or even stopping them permanently, is very bad for the image and credibility of the United States," she continues. In this way, the decree signed by Donald Trump also plays into the hands of authoritarian regimes, such as Russia or China, which could fill the void left by the withdrawal of American aid.
lead: The American president has ordered, by decree, the suspension of all programs for 90 days, except for a few exceptions. A radical measure with immense effects: the total amount of aid in 2023 had amounted to 64.7 billion dollars.
